title: 00 · Kiselev — Geometry, Book II (Stereometry)
text:
Source
Givental’s English translation (free, authoritative): Kiselev’s Geometry, Book II — Stereometry, Sumizdat. The PDF stays external; the pointers in each lesson cite Givental’s chapter and section numbers.






Citation, not transcription. Givental’s translation is copyrighted. Each lesson adapts Kiselev’s exposition and exercises in its own words and points the learner at the source PDF for the canonical proofs. Do not copy Givental’s prose directly.

title: 00 · Kiselev — Geometry, Book II (Stereometry)
text:
Lessons
The full Stereometry track, in book order:

01 · Lines and Planes in Space (Ch 1) — coplanar / parallel / skew positions; perpendicular line to a plane; dihedral angles; the three-perpendiculars theorem.
02 · Polyhedra: Prisms and Pyramids (Ch 2) — volume of a prism (Ah); volume of a pyramid (\tfrac{1}{3}Ah); Cavalieri’s principle.
03 · Cylinder and Cone (Ch 3) — the lateral surface as an unrolled rectangle / sector; Archimedes’ 3 : 2 : 1 ratio.
04 · The Sphere (Ch 4) — Archimedes’ theorems: surface 4\pi R^2 via projection, volume \tfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3 via Cavalieri.
05 · Regular Polyhedra (Ch 5) — Euler’s formula; the five Platonic solids and the proof that no sixth exists; similarity scaling.

Track complete. Together with the nine Planimetry chapters, this is the full Kiselev geometry track in Russian-classical style.

title: 00 · Kiselev — Geometry, Book II (Stereometry)
text:
Sister synthesis tiers
The synthesis lessons covering the same ground sit in Tier 1 Foundations (area, perimeter, volume — the Touch layer) and the CBSE Class 9 and Class 10 tracks (surface areas and volumes, mensuration). For the proof-heavy treatment of three-dimensional geometry as a coordinate problem, see Tier 4 (vectors in 3D) and CBSE Class 12 (lines and planes in 3D).

title: Ch 3 — Cylinder and Cone (Round Solids)
text:
Do you know the area of a circle is \pi r^2 and its circumference is 2 \pi r? See t1.area-perimeter-volume.
Can you compute the volume of a prism as \text{base area} \times \text{height}?
Are you comfortable with the formula \text{volume of pyramid} = \tfrac{1}{3} \times \text{base} \times
\text{height}? See cbse9.surface-areas-and-volumes.
Do you accept that a cylinder is a “circular prism” and a cone is a “circular pyramid” — i.e. the limits of regular prisms / pyramids as the number of sides n \to \infty?

If three yeses, proceed.

title: Ch 3 — Cylinder and Cone (Round Solids)
Ch 3 — Cylinder and Cone (Round Solids)
text:
Wonder

We just unrolled the cylinder. What about a cone — what shape do you get if you slit it from apex to base and unroll?
A cylinder and a cone share the same circular base and the same height. By how much do their volumes differ? Eyeball it first; the answer is famously simple.
Drop a sphere of radius r inside a cylinder that just contains it (radius r, height 2r). What fraction of the cylinder does the sphere fill?
Can you imagine slicing a cone parallel to its base? At height y from the apex, what is the radius of the cross-section?







TipWhat you might notice






A circular sector (pie-slice). (2) The cone is exactly one-third of the cylinder — the same factor that turned a prism into a pyramid. (3) Two-thirds — Archimedes’ favourite theorem.
Linearly: at height y the radius is \frac{y}{h} r. This is why cones scale by y^3 in volume.

title: Ch 3 — Cylinder and Cone (Round Solids)
text:
Discover

Problem 1 — The lateral surface of a cylinder
Show that the lateral surface area of a cylinder of base radius r and height h is 2 \pi r h.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Cut the cylinder along one vertical line and unroll it.



Full solution

The unrolled surface is a rectangle. Its width equals the circumference of the base (2 \pi r); its height equals h. So the lateral area is 2 \pi r \cdot h. Total surface (including two circular caps) is 2 \pi r h + 2 \pi r^2. (Planimetry-style proof — unroll, measure, multiply.)



Problem 2 — Volume of a cylinder
Show that the volume of a cylinder of base radius r and height h is \pi r^2 h.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Approximate the cylinder by a regular n-gon prism, then take n \to \infty.



Full solution

For each n \ge 3, inscribe a regular n-gon in the base circle. The resulting prism has volume A_n \cdot h, where A_n is the area of the inscribed n-gon. As n \to \infty, A_n \to \pi r^2 (definition of \pi from area). So the cylinder’s volume — sandwiched between inscribed and circumscribed prisms — is \pi r^2 \cdot h. QED.



Problem 3 — Lateral surface of a cone
A cone has base radius r and slant height \ell (the distance from apex to base-circle along the surface). Unroll its lateral surface. What shape and what dimensions?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Every point on the surface is at distance \ell from the apex — so the unrolled shape is part of a circle of radius \ell.



Full solution

The unrolled shape is a circular sector of radius \ell. Its arc length must equal the base circumference 2 \pi r (the rim). A full circle of radius \ell has circumference 2 \pi \ell, so the sector is the fraction \frac{2 \pi r}{2 \pi \ell} =
\frac{r}{\ell} of the disc. The disc has area \pi \ell^2, so the sector — the lateral area of the cone — is \pi \ell^2 \cdot \frac{r}{\ell} = \pi r \ell.



Problem 4 — Volume of a cone
Show that the volume of a cone of base radius r and height h is \dfrac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h.


Hint 1 — a nudge

The cone is the limit of regular pyramids over regular n-gons. A pyramid has volume \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot \text{base} \cdot
\text{height}.



Full solution

Inscribe a regular n-gon in the base; build a regular pyramid over it sharing the cone’s apex. Its volume is \tfrac{1}{3} A_n \cdot h. As n \to \infty, A_n \to \pi r^2, so the cone’s volume is \tfrac{1}{3} \pi r^2 \cdot h. Same trick as Problem 2 — inscribe, increase n, take the limit. Kiselev uses the prism / pyramid relation as the load-bearing axiom; the cone / cylinder relation falls out.



Problem 5 — Cone fits inside cylinder
Place a cone (base radius r, height h) inside a cylinder of the same base and height, sharing the base. What fraction of the cylinder does the cone occupy?


Full solution

\dfrac{\frac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h}{\pi r^2 h} = \dfrac{1}{3}. One third. (The same one-third factor that took prism to pyramid.)



Problem 6 — Archimedes’ theorem (read-off)
Inside a cylinder of radius r and height 2r (so it just contains a sphere of radius r), place the sphere and a double cone (two cones apex-to-apex, each of height r, sharing the cylinder’s axis). Compute the three volumes and check Archimedes’ famous relation cylinder : sphere : double cone = 3 : 2 : 1.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Cylinder volume: \pi r^2 \cdot 2r = 2 \pi r^3. Sphere volume: take \tfrac{4}{3} \pi r^3 as known. Double cone: two cones of base r and height r.



Full solution

Cylinder: 2 \pi r^3. Sphere: \tfrac{4}{3} \pi r^3. Double cone: 2 \cdot \tfrac{1}{3} \pi r^2 \cdot r = \tfrac{2}{3} \pi r^3. Ratios (divide each by \tfrac{2}{3} \pi r^3): \quad \dfrac{2}{2/3} : \dfrac{4/3}{2/3} : \dfrac{2/3}{2/3}
= 3 : 2 : 1. Archimedes asked for this ratio to be carved on his tombstone, and it was.


Archimedes of Syracuse (~250 BCE) considered the cylinder–sphere ratio of Problem 6 — the simple 3 : 2 : 1 — his greatest result. In his treatise On the Sphere and the Cylinder, he proved both the volume and the surface formulas via the method of exhaustion (the ancestor of integral calculus). He requested that a sphere inscribed in a cylinder be carved on his tombstone. A century and a half later, the Roman politician Cicero, serving as quaestor in Sicily, hunted through the overgrown cemetery at Syracuse and found the gravestone — identifying it by the carved figure. The geometry had outlived the inscription.


A cylinder is a limit of regular prisms and a cone is a limit of regular pyramids — so the polygon-area limit that defined \pi r^2 in 2D becomes the volume formulas \pi r^2 h and \tfrac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h in 3D, for free. The same one-third factor that turned a parallelogram into a triangle turns a cylinder into a cone.

title: Ch 3 — Cylinder and Cone (Round Solids)
text:
Name

A cylinder of base radius r and height h has:

\text{lateral area} = 2 \pi r h, \quad
\text{total surface} = 2 \pi r h + 2 \pi r^2, \quad
\text{volume} = \pi r^2 h.

A cone of base radius r, height h, slant height \ell = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2} has:

\text{lateral area} = \pi r \ell, \quad
\text{total surface} = \pi r \ell + \pi r^2, \quad
\text{volume} = \tfrac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h.

A sphere of radius r has:

\text{surface area} = 4 \pi r^2, \quad
\text{volume} = \tfrac{4}{3} \pi r^3.

The Archimedes relation: for the smallest enclosing cylinder of a sphere of radius r (and the inscribed double cone of the same height), the volumes are in the ratio 3 : 2 : 1.

A verse:

Unroll the cylinder — a rectangle, two pi r and h. Unroll the cone — a sector, slant by arc, the rim’s path. One-third for the cone, like one-third for the pyramid before. And cylinder, sphere, cone — three, two, one — Archimedes’ law.

title: Ch 3 — Cylinder and Cone (Round Solids)
text:
Apply

Worked example

A cone has base radius 6 cm and slant height 10 cm. Find its volume and total surface area.

Slant height \ell = 10, base radius r = 6, so the height is h = \sqrt{\ell^2 - r^2} = \sqrt{100 - 36} = \sqrt{64} = 8 cm.
Volume: V = \tfrac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h = \tfrac{1}{3} \pi \cdot 36
\cdot 8 = 96 \pi \approx 301.6 cm³.
Lateral area: \pi r \ell = 60 \pi. Plus base \pi r^2 = 36 \pi.
Total surface: 96 \pi \approx 301.6 cm². Volume: 96 \pi \approx 301.6 cm³.
(A pleasing accident: same number, same units of \pi — but different physical quantities.)


Practice — Warm
W1. A cylindrical can has base radius 3 cm and height 10 cm. Find its volume and lateral area.


Full solution

V = \pi \cdot 9 \cdot 10 = 90 \pi cm³. Lateral = 2 \pi \cdot 3 \cdot 10 = 60 \pi cm².

W2. A cone has volume 48 \pi cm³ and base radius 4 cm. Find its height.


Full solution

\tfrac{1}{3} \pi \cdot 16 \cdot h = 48 \pi, so h = 9 cm.

W3. A sphere has volume \tfrac{32}{3} \pi cm³. Find its radius.


Full solution

\tfrac{4}{3} \pi r^3 = \tfrac{32}{3} \pi, so r^3 = 8, r = 2 cm.



Practice — Working
P1. A cone is cut by a plane parallel to its base, halfway up. What fraction of the original cone’s volume is the upper small cone?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Linear scale halves; volume scales by the cube.



Full solution

The upper cone is similar to the whole, with linear ratio \tfrac{1}{2}. So volume ratio = (\tfrac{1}{2})^3 =
\tfrac{1}{8}. The frustum below has \tfrac{7}{8} of the original volume.

P2. A solid is composed of a cylinder (r = 3, h = 7) with a hemisphere on top (r = 3). Find its total volume.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Volume = cylinder + half-sphere.



Full solution

Cylinder = \pi \cdot 9 \cdot 7 = 63 \pi. Hemisphere = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \tfrac{4}{3} \pi \cdot 27 = 18 \pi. Total: 81 \pi cubic units.

P3. An ice-cream cone (open cone, no base) has slant height 12 cm and lateral area 60 \pi cm². Find its base radius.


Full solution

\pi r \ell = 60 \pi with \ell = 12, so r = 5 cm.



Practice — Stretch
S1. A cylinder, a sphere, and a cone share base circle (radius r) and height h where h = 2r. Show that the sum of the volumes of the cone and the sphere equals the cylinder’s volume.


Hint 1 — a nudge

This is Problem 6 in disguise. Add cone + sphere; compare with cylinder.



Full solution

Cone (height 2r): \tfrac{1}{3} \pi r^2 \cdot 2r = \tfrac{2}{3}
\pi r^3. Sphere: \tfrac{4}{3} \pi r^3. Sum = 2 \pi r^3 = cylinder. QED. (This is the equivalent statement of Archimedes’ 3:2:1 — Kiselev gives both.)

S2. A right circular cone has the property that its lateral surface area equals the area of its base. Show that its slant height is exactly the radius — and conclude that no such non-trivial cone exists (because then the height would be zero, i.e. the cone is degenerate).


Hint 1 — a nudge

\pi r \ell = \pi r^2, so \ell = r. But \ell^2 = r^2 + h^2.



Full solution

\pi r \ell = \pi r^2 \Rightarrow \ell = r (assuming r > 0). Then \ell^2 = r^2 + h^2 gives h^2 = 0, so h = 0. The “cone” collapses to a flat disc. Boundary case; no genuine cone satisfies the condition.



Kiselev-style problems
Three problems in Kiselev’s classical style, adapted from Givental, Stereometry, §§430–460.
K1. A cone of slant height 10 and base radius 6 is unrolled flat into a sector. Find the angle at the apex of the unrolled sector.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Sector arc = base circumference = 2\pi r. Sector full circle of radius \ell has circumference 2\pi\ell. The fraction is the angle as a fraction of 2\pi (or 360°).



Full solution

Fraction = \dfrac{r}{\ell} = \dfrac{6}{10} = 0.6. So the angle is 0.6 \cdot 360° = 216°. (Or in radians, 0.6 \cdot 2\pi = 1.2\pi.)

K2. A sphere is inscribed in a cone whose axial cross-section is an equilateral triangle of side 2a. Express the sphere’s radius in terms of a.


Hint 1 — a nudge

The axial cross-section of the inscribed sphere is the incircle of the equilateral triangle.



Full solution

The incircle of an equilateral triangle of side s has radius \dfrac{s}{2\sqrt 3} = \dfrac{s\sqrt 3}{6}. With s = 2a, r_{\text{sphere}} = \dfrac{a\sqrt 3}{3} = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt 3}.

K3. Two right circular cones share the same axis and apex. The inner cone has base radius r_1 and height h_1; the outer cone has base radius r_2 > r_1 and height h_2 > h_1. Show that the frustum-like region between them — bounded by the inner cone’s lateral surface, the outer cone’s lateral surface, and the two base discs at heights h_1 and h_2 from the apex — has volume

V = \tfrac{\pi}{3}\bigl(r_2^2 h_2 - r_1^2 h_1\bigr).



Hint 1 — a nudge

Subtract one cone’s volume from the other.



Full solution

V = V_{\text{outer cone}} - V_{\text{inner cone}}
= \tfrac{1}{3} \pi r_2^2 h_2 - \tfrac{1}{3} \pi r_1^2 h_1
= \tfrac{\pi}{3}(r_2^2 h_2 - r_1^2 h_1). (Kiselev uses this kind of difference-of-cones to derive the volume of a conical frustum; this is the simpler co-axial version.)







Source pointer. Adapted from Givental, Kiselev’s Geometry, Book II — Stereometry, §§430–460 (Sumizdat 2008). Free PDF on cimat.mx. K3 is the cleanest entry into Kiselev’s frustum chapter — adapt freely; do not paste Givental’s prose.





Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this chapter, remember these:

The lateral surface of a cylinder is a rectangle — unroll it — of size 2\pi r \times h; its volume is \pi r^2 h.
The lateral surface of a cone is a circular sector; its volume is \tfrac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h — the same one-third factor as in pyramids.
Archimedes’ 3 : 2 : 1. Cylinder, sphere, double cone in the smallest enclosing cylinder — volumes split as 3 : 2 : 1. The fact carved on his tombstone.



Common confusions

“Slant height equals height.” No. Height is the perpendicular from apex to base; slant height is the distance along the surface, \ell = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2}. Use \ell for surface area, h for volume.
“The lateral area of a cylinder is \pi r h.” Half the truth. The full circumference is 2 \pi r, so lateral area = 2 \pi r h. The factor of 2 comes from going around the circle.
“Volume scales linearly with size.” It does not. Doubling every linear dimension multiplies volume by 2^3 = 8 — see Problem P1. This is why a giant ant cannot exist (its weight scales by 8, its leg cross-section only by 4).



Self-Assessment

Could you re-derive both V_{\text{cylinder}} = \pi r^2 h and V_{\text{cone}} = \tfrac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h from scratch using the inscribed-prism / inscribed-pyramid limit?
Could you state Archimedes’ 3 : 2 : 1 in your own words and sketch the configuration that proves it?
Can you spot in a real-world object (an ice-cream cone, a tin can, a basketball) which of these formulas applies — and which dimension to measure first?


3 yes: move on.
2 yes: redo Problem 6 — the Archimedes computation. It is the most important payoff of the chapter.
0–1 yes: the unroll Touch widget did not land. Replay it with paper and scissors — cut a paper cylinder and unroll it.

title: Ch 3 — Cylinder and Cone (Round Solids)
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

t1.area-perimeter-volume — \pi r^2, 2 \pi r, and the area-as-counting intuition.
cbse9.surface-areas-and-volumes — the prism and pyramid formulas, in particular the \tfrac{1}{3}-pyramid factor.



What this unlocks

kg2.sphere (planned) — the surface-area 4 \pi r^2 proof by the cylindrical-projection argument (Archimedes again).
kg2.frustums (planned) — generalise Problem K3 to any axial frustum.
t4.definite-integral — the limit-of-prisms / limit-of-pyramids argument is the seed of Riemann integration.
cbse10.surface-areas-and-volumes — combined-solid mensuration in board-exam idiom.



Kiselev chapter alignment

Geometry, Book II — Chapter 3: Round bodies (§§430–460). Givental translation, Sumizdat 2008. Free PDF on cimat.mx.
Sister synthesis lessons: cbse9.surface-areas-and-volumes — same material, computational style; and cbse10.surface-areas-and-volumes — combined solids.
Read alongside the source PDF for Kiselev’s full development including the spherical-zone surface-area argument, which this pilot defers to a future lesson.



Where this matters

Game development. Cone, cylinder, capsule, and sphere are the canonical collision primitives in physics engines. The volume and surface formulas here are exactly the ones used to compute mass, inertia tensors, and intersection volumes in real-time. The unroll-into-rectangle trick is also how UV mapping a cylindrical texture works.


Research. The “inscribe a polygon, take n \to \infty” argument is Eudoxus’ method of exhaustion, the historical seed of integral calculus. Archimedes’ 3:2:1 is the first non-trivial theorem proved this way — and the line from this problem to the fundamental theorem of calculus is short.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones: Kiselev (the unroll-and-measure proof of lateral area; the inscribed-prism limit for volume); Archimedes (the 3 : 2 : 1 relation in Discover, Problem 6); 3Blue1Brown (the Touch widget framing of unrolling).
Source citation: §§430–460 of Kiselev’s Stereometry cover cylinder and cone in this order — surfaces first, volumes second, Archimedes’ relations last.
What this lesson does not do: prove the surface-area formula for the sphere (4 \pi r^2). That belongs in kg2.sphere, which builds the cylindrical-projection trick (Archimedes’ “On the Sphere and Cylinder”) in full.

title: 15 · Sequences (Intro)
text:
Have you done T1 L11 — Variables and 07 · Exponents?
Can you compute 2^5 = 32?
Have you ever heard “compound interest” or “Moore’s Law”? They are geometric sequences — the topic of this lesson.

title: 15 · Sequences (Intro)
15 · Sequences (Intro)
text:
Wonder

Pick “arithmetic (add d)” with start 2 and d = 3. Read the terms aloud. Each is bigger than the last by how much?
Now switch to “geometric (multiply r)” with start 2 and r = 3. Read the terms. How fast do they grow?
Set arithmetic with d = 0.5. Now geometric with r = 0.5. What happens?

title: 15 · Sequences (Intro)
text:
Discover

Problem 1
The sequence 3, 7, 11, 15, \ldots — what kind of sequence is it, and what’s the next term?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Each step, what is added?



Full solution

Common difference d = 4. Arithmetic sequence. Next term: 19.
Formula: a_n = a_1 + (n - 1)d = 3 + (n - 1) \cdot 4. The 10th term: a_{10} = 3 + 9 \cdot 4 = 39.



Problem 2
The sequence 2, 6, 18, 54, \ldots — what kind, and what’s the next?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Each step, what is multiplied?



Full solution

Common ratio r = 3. Geometric sequence. Next term: 162.
Formula: a_n = a_1 \cdot r^{n-1} = 2 \cdot 3^{n-1}.



Problem 3
You deposit Rs. 10000 in a savings account at 5% interest, compounded annually. After year 1: Rs. 10500. After year 2: 5% on the new balance, so Rs. 10500 × 1.05 = Rs. 11025. After year 3: 11025 × 1.05.
Is the balance over years an arithmetic or geometric sequence?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Each year, you multiply by the same factor (1.05).



Full solution

Geometric, with r = 1.05. After n years: balance =
10000 \cdot 1.05^n.
After 10 years: 10000 \cdot 1.05^{10} \approx 16289 rupees. After 30 years: 10000 \cdot 1.05^{30} \approx 43219 rupees.
Compound interest is exponential, not linear. Doubling time at 5%: about 14 years. (The “Rule of 72” estimates doubling time as 72/rate.)



Problem 4
A geometric sequence with r > 1 grows fast. With r < 1 (but positive), it shrinks. Compute the first 5 terms of a geometric sequence with a_1 = 100 and r = 0.5.


Full solution

100, 50, 25, 12.5, 6.25, \ldots Each term is half the previous.
This is the pattern of radioactive decay (half the substance remains after each “half-life”), of drug elimination from the body (approximately), and of any damped oscillation.
If you continue forever, the terms approach 0 — they have a limit. The infinite sum is bounded too: 100 + 50 + 25 + \cdots =
200. We meet infinite series properly in Tier 3.



Problem 5
An arithmetic sequence has a_3 = 11 and a_7 = 23. Find a_1 and d.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Going from a_3 to a_7 is 4 steps, each of size d. So a_7 - a_3
= 4d.



Full solution

23 - 11 = 12 = 4d \Rightarrow d = 3. a_3 = a_1 + 2d \Rightarrow 11 = a_1 + 6 \Rightarrow a_1 = 5.
Check: sequence is 5, 8, 11, 14, 17, 20, 23, \ldots. a_3 = 11 ✓, a_7 = 23 ✓.
Two pieces of information about an arithmetic (or geometric) sequence are enough to pin down all terms. This is the same “two equations, two unknowns” idea from Lesson 6.


The famous Fibonacci sequence (1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, \ldots) debuted in Europe in 1202 in the Liber Abaci of Leonardo of Pisa — known to history as Fibonacci. He posed a deliberately playful problem: if a pair of newborn rabbits matures in one month, then breeds a new pair every month after, and no rabbit ever dies, how many pairs are alive after a year? The month-by-month answer is the sequence that bears his name. The same book also championed the Hindu–Arabic digits (0–9) over Roman numerals — arguably its more consequential contribution.


An arithmetic sequence walks; a geometric sequence runs. Walking adds the same step every time — distance grows in proportion to time. Running multiplies — distance explodes (or collapses) at a rate that quickly dwarfs anything walking can do. That single difference is why compound interest looks like a miracle and why exponential growth ends every “this can continue forever” intuition.

title: 15 · Sequences (Intro)
text:
Name

A sequence is an ordered list of numbers a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots. The number in position n is a_n, the n-th term.
Arithmetic sequence: common difference d. Each term is the previous plus d. a_n = a_1 + (n - 1) d.
Geometric sequence: common ratio r. Each term is the previous times r. a_n = a_1 \cdot r^{n - 1}.
Sum of an arithmetic sequence (n terms): S_n = \dfrac{n}{2}(a_1 + a_n) = \dfrac{n}{2}(2 a_1 + (n-1)d).
Sum of a geometric sequence (n terms, r \neq 1): S_n = a_1 \cdot \dfrac{r^n - 1}{r - 1}.


Arithmetic — add a fixed step; the formula is linear in n. Geometric — multiply by a fixed factor; the formula is exponential in n. Two terms determine the whole sequence; sums have closed forms.

title: 15 · Sequences (Intro)
text:
Apply

Worked example

Find the sum of the first 100 positive integers: 1 + 2 + 3 +
\cdots + 100.

A competent solver recognises this as an arithmetic sequence with a_1 = 1, d = 1, n = 100, a_n = 100.
S = \dfrac{100}{2}(1 + 100) = 50 \cdot 101 = 5050.
Answer: 5050. Legend has it that the young Carl Friedrich Gauss (later one of the greatest mathematicians) was set this exact problem by a teacher who hoped to keep him quiet for an hour. Gauss returned the answer in minutes by pairing terms (1 + 100, 2 + 99, \ldots, 50
+ 51 — fifty pairs each summing to 101) and computing 50 \cdot 101 =
5050. The same trick yields the general formula.


Practice — Warm
W1. Identify each as arithmetic or geometric, and give the next two terms:

5, 8, 11, 14, \ldots
3, 6, 12, 24, \ldots
20, 17, 14, 11, \ldots



Full solution


Arithmetic, d = 3: 17, 20.
Geometric, r = 2: 48, 96.
Arithmetic, d = -3: 8, 5.


W2. For arithmetic sequence a_1 = 4, d = 5, find a_{10}.


Full solution

a_{10} = 4 + 9 \cdot 5 = 49.

W3. For geometric sequence a_1 = 3, r = 2, find a_5.


Full solution

a_5 = 3 \cdot 2^4 = 48.



Practice — Working
P1. Sum of the first 50 positive even integers (2 + 4 + 6 + \cdots
+ 100).


Full solution

Arithmetic with a_1 = 2, d = 2, n = 50, a_n = 100. S = \dfrac{50}{2}(2 + 100) = 25 \cdot 102 = 2550.

P2. Find the 8th term of the sequence 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, \ldots.


Full solution

Geometric, a_1 = 1, r = 2. a_8 = 1 \cdot 2^7 = 128.
This is the doubling sequence — the same one from the chess-board problem (T2 L07 Stretch S1).

P3. A car worth Rs. 800,000 depreciates by 20% each year. What is its value after 5 years?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Each year multiplies by (1 - 0.20) = 0.80. Geometric sequence with r = 0.8.



Full solution

a_5 = 800000 \cdot 0.8^5 = 800000 \cdot 0.32768 \approx 262{,}144 rupees.
Depreciation, drug elimination, atmospheric absorption — many real-world quantities decay geometrically.



Practice — Stretch
S1. A neural network’s learning rate is often decayed geometrically: each epoch, the rate is multiplied by 0.95 (say). Starting at 0.01, what is the learning rate after 50 epochs? After 100 epochs?


Hint 1 — a nudge

\text{rate}_n = 0.01 \cdot 0.95^n.



Full solution

After 50: 0.01 \cdot 0.95^{50} \approx 0.01 \cdot 0.0769 \approx
7.7 \times 10^{-4}. After 100: 0.01 \cdot 0.95^{100} \approx 0.01 \cdot 0.0059 \approx
5.9 \times 10^{-5}.
By epoch 100 the rate has shrunk to about 0.6% of its starting value — still positive, but very small. This annealing of the learning rate is one of the most common training tricks in deep learning, and it is exactly a geometric sequence.

S2. The Fibonacci sequence is neither arithmetic nor geometric: each term is the sum of the two previous. Starting from 1, 1: 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21, \ldots. Compute the next 5 terms. What is unusual about this rule compared to arithmetic/geometric?


Full solution

Continuing: 34, 55, 89, 144, 233, \ldots.
Unusual: the rule is recursive: a_n = a_{n-1} + a_{n-2} — each term depends on two previous terms, not just one. This places Fibonacci outside the simple arithmetic/geometric world.
Yet a closed form does exist: Binet’s formula a_n = \dfrac{\phi^n - (1 - \phi)^n}{\sqrt{5}}, \qquad \phi = \dfrac{1 + \sqrt{5}}{2}.
The number \phi \approx 1.618 is the golden ratio — and a_{n+1}
/ a_n \to \phi as n grows. So Fibonacci is asymptotically geometric with ratio \phi, even though it isn’t exactly so.
The Fibonacci sequence appears in nature (sunflower spirals, pinecone patterns), in art (the golden rectangle), in algorithms (Fibonacci heaps, Euclidean algorithm complexity), and in finance (technical analysis “Fibonacci retracements”). A whole journal, The Fibonacci Quarterly, is dedicated to it. Recursive sequences are a major topic of Tier 5+ discrete mathematics.



Common confusions

“Arithmetic = numbers; geometric = shapes.” No — both are types of sequence. Arithmetic adds a fixed amount; geometric multiplies by a fixed factor. The names are historical.
“a_1 is term zero.” Convention varies. Most school texts start with a_1; computer scientists often use a_0. Be consistent within one problem.
“All sequences are arithmetic or geometric.” No — most sequences are neither (e.g., Fibonacci, the prime numbers, the sequence of perfect squares 1, 4, 9, 16, \ldots — which is quadratic).



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A sequence is an ordered list of numbers — and most of the world’s interesting growth follows one of two simple rules.
An arithmetic sequence adds a fixed step; a geometric sequence multiplies by a fixed factor. Linear vs exponential, in one distinction.
Two terms are enough to pin the whole sequence down — and both types have closed-form sum formulas, so you never need to add term by term.



Self-Assessment

Could you explain the difference between arithmetic and geometric sequences using “step” vs “factor”?
Without scrolling back, can you find the 10th term of an arithmetic sequence with a_1 = 7, d = 4?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: a_{10} = 7 + 9 \cdot 4 = 43.)

3 yes: You have completed Tier 2! Begin Tier 3 (planned) or consult an applied bridge.
2 yes: redo S1 (geometric LR decay) — common in modern ML.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 (compound interest) and the worked example.

title: 15 · Sequences (Intro)
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

T1 L11 — Variables
07 · Exponents and Powers — needed for geometric sequences.



What this unlocks

Tier 3 — Sequences and Series — infinite sums, convergence, the geometric series formula.
Tier 4 — Calculus — limits of sequences are the foundation of calculus.



Where this matters

AI / Machine Learning. Learning-rate annealing (S1), exponential moving averages, batch-size warmup schedules — all are sequences in the training loop. Many model-architecture choices (hidden layer widths, attention head counts) are also sequences.


Mathematical research. Recursive sequences, Fibonacci, the prime counting function — sequences are a major topic of analytic number theory and combinatorics.


Finance. Compound interest is the most consequential geometric sequence in human life — it underlies retirement planning, debt accumulation, mortgage amortisation, inflation, and economic growth.


Physics & biology. Radioactive decay, population growth (under unlimited resources), pharmacokinetics, sound attenuation — geometric sequences and their continuous counterpart, the exponential function (Tier 3+), are everywhere.

title: 15 · Sequences (Intro)
text:
You have finished Tier 2
This lesson closes Tier 2 — Pre-Algebra & Algebra I. Across 15 lessons you have:

Met functions and the input-output picture.
Solved linear equations and inequalities.
Mapped points on the coordinate plane, drawn linear functions as lines, and solved systems of two equations.
Mastered exponents, scientific notation, and met the irrational \sqrt{2}.
Proved the Pythagorean theorem and the distance formula.
Multiplied and factored polynomials, solved quadratics by factoring.
Reasoned about similarity and the square-cube law.
Met arithmetic and geometric sequences — the two simplest patterns governing growth.

You are now ready for Tier 3 — Algebra II / Geometry / Trigonometry (planned), or for any of the applied bridges.
Congratulations.







NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones honoured: Pestalozzi (the live-terms widget), Boole (Wonder questions distinguish add-vs-multiply step), Russian school (P5 forces “two terms determine all” thinking), Bhāskara (single-box Name + verse — and Indian mathematicians including Bhāskara I and II contributed extensively to arithmetic and geometric series formulas), Gauss (the worked-example anecdote).
Self-study guarantees all present.

title: 11 · Polynomials — Adding and Multiplying
text:
Have you done T1 L11 — Variables and 07 · Exponents?
Can you compute 5x^2 for x = 3 and get 45?
Can you multiply two single-digit numbers in your head?

title: 11 · Polynomials — Adding and Multiplying
11 · Polynomials — Adding and Multiplying
text:
Wonder

What kind of expression is 3x^2 + 5x - 7? How many terms?
Is 3x + 2 + 5x the same as 8x + 2? Why?
What does (x + 1)(x + 1) give?

title: 11 · Polynomials — Adding and Multiplying
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Add: (2x^2 + 3x - 5) + (x^2 - 2x + 8).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Combine like terms: x^2 with x^2, x with x, constants with constants.



Full solution

x^2: 2 + 1 = 3. x: 3 - 2 = 1. Constant: -5 + 8 = 3. 3x^2 + x + 3.
Adding polynomials is just adding the coefficients of matching powers — like sorting items by category before counting.



Problem 2
Multiply: 3x \cdot (2x + 5).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Distribute the 3x across both terms inside the bracket.



Full solution

3x \cdot 2x + 3x \cdot 5 = 6x^2 + 15x.
Recall the exponent rule: x \cdot x = x^2. Multiplying a number by the same variable adds 1 to the variable’s exponent.



Problem 3
Multiply: (x + 2)(x + 3).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Picture a rectangle with sides (x + 2) and (x + 3). Split into 4 sub-rectangles.



Full solution

The four sub-rectangles have areas x^2, 3x, 2x, 6. Sum: x^2 + 5x +
6.
Equivalently, distribute: (x + 2)(x + 3) = x(x + 3) + 2(x + 3) = x^2
+ 3x + 2x + 6 = x^2 + 5x + 6.
The widget shows the same result: x^2 coefficient = 1, x coefficient = 1 \cdot 3 + 2 \cdot 1 = 5, constant = 2 \cdot 3 = 6.
This pattern generalises into the FOIL mnemonic (First, Outer, Inner, Last) — but the rectangle picture is more honest and generalises beyond binomials.



Problem 4
Multiply: (x + 5)(x - 5).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Use the rectangle picture or distribute carefully.



Full solution

x^2 - 5x + 5x - 25 = x^2 - 25. The middle terms cancel.
This is the difference of squares identity: (x + a)(x - a) = x^2 - a^2. A pattern worth memorising — it appears constantly when factoring (Lesson 12) and in higher algebra.



Problem 5
Multiply: (x + 3)^2 — that is, (x + 3)(x + 3).


Full solution

x^2 + 3x + 3x + 9 = x^2 + 6x + 9.
This is the square of a binomial identity: (x + a)^2 = x^2 + 2ax + a^2.
Common error alert: (x + a)^2 \neq x^2 + a^2. The 2ax in the middle is essential. We met this gotcha briefly in Tier 2 L07; it returns to bite anyone who forgets it.


The first systematic study of polynomial-like expressions appears in the Arithmetica of Diophantus of Alexandria (c. 250 CE) — a 13-book treasure-house of algebraic problems. Diophantus had no modern symbol for the unknown; he used Greek abbreviations, written in prose. It was his book, scribbled in the margin a thousand years later, that prompted Pierre de Fermat in 1637 to claim he had a “truly marvellous proof” of what we now call Fermat’s Last Theorem — a proof finally completed by Andrew Wiles in 1994.


A polynomial is just a tidy list of how much of each power of x you have. Adding two polynomials sorts the powers and adds in columns; multiplying them is the rectangle picture, with one side per polynomial. Every algebraic identity you will ever meet is some pattern hiding in those columns.

title: 11 · Polynomials — Adding and Multiplying
text:
Name

A polynomial in one variable is a sum of terms, each of the form c \cdot x^k where c is a constant and k is a non-negative integer.
The degree of a polynomial is the highest exponent of x present.



Degree
Name




0
constant (5)


1
linear (3x + 2)


2
quadratic (x^2 - 4x + 7)


3
cubic (x^3 + 2x - 1)


4
quartic


5
quintic



Adding polynomials: combine like terms (same power of x).
Multiplying polynomials: distribute every term in the first across every term in the second; then collect like terms.
Two key identities:

Difference of squares: (x + a)(x - a) = x^2 - a^2.
Square of a binomial: (x + a)^2 = x^2 + 2ax + a^2.



Same powers add as like terms; different powers stand alone; To multiply: every term in one across every term in the other. Difference of squares cancels the middle; square of a binomial doubles it.

title: 11 · Polynomials — Adding and Multiplying
text:
Apply

Worked example

Multiply (2x - 3)(x + 4).

A competent solver distributes systematically:

2x \cdot x = 2x^2.
2x \cdot 4 = 8x.
-3 \cdot x = -3x.
-3 \cdot 4 = -12.
Combine: 2x^2 + (8 - 3)x - 12 = 2x^2 + 5x - 12.

Answer: 2x^2 + 5x - 12. Check on the widget: a = 2, b = -3, c =
1, d = 4 → 2x^2 + 5x - 12 ✓.


Practice — Warm
W1. Add (3x + 5) + (4x - 2).


Full solution

7x + 3.

W2. Multiply 4x \cdot (x + 7).


Full solution

4x^2 + 28x.

W3. Multiply (x + 4)(x - 4).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Difference of squares.



Full solution

x^2 - 16.



Practice — Working
P1. Multiply (x + 2)^2.


Full solution

x^2 + 4x + 4.

P2. Multiply (2x + 1)(3x - 5).


Full solution

6x^2 - 10x + 3x - 5 = 6x^2 - 7x - 5.

P3. Subtract (3x^2 + 4x - 1) - (x^2 - 2x + 5).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Distribute the negative sign across the second polynomial.



Full solution

(3x^2 - x^2) + (4x - (-2x)) + (-1 - 5) = 2x^2 + 6x - 6.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Multiply (x + 1)(x^2 - x + 1).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Distribute carefully — three terms in the second polynomial.



Full solution

x \cdot (x^2 - x + 1) + 1 \cdot (x^2 - x + 1) = x^3 - x^2 + x + x^2 -
x + 1 = x^3 + 1.
This is another famous identity: sum of cubes — x^3 + 1 = (x +
1)(x^2 - x + 1). (And generally x^3 + a^3 = (x + a)(x^2 - ax + a^2).)
Polynomials have many such identities — they are the alphabet of algebraic manipulation. Memorising the most common ones (difference of squares, square of a binomial, sum/difference of cubes) saves time.

S2. Polynomials are everywhere in encryption. RSA encryption treats messages as numbers and polynomials over modular arithmetic appear in advanced schemes. Lattice-based post-quantum cryptography literally lives in polynomial rings: \mathbb{Z}[x] /
(x^n + 1) — polynomials modulo another polynomial. Why might polynomials be useful for cryptography?


Full solution

Polynomials provide structured complexity. They are large enough (many coefficients) to carry significant information; they have rich algebraic structure (addition, multiplication, identities); and operations on them are well-studied and efficient.
Polynomial rings allow constructions like error-correcting codes (Reed-Solomon — used in CDs, DVDs, satellite communication) and lattice cryptography (NTRU, Kyber — currently being standardised as post-quantum cryptography by NIST).
The humble polynomial of Tier 2 is the entry point to topics that secure modern digital infrastructure. Tier 6 (abstract algebra) builds the theory; Tier 7 (encryption track) applies it.



Common confusions

“(x + a)^2 = x^2 + a^2.” False. The correct expansion is x^2 + 2ax + a^2. The cross-term is essential.
“Like terms are terms with the same number coefficient.” No — like terms have the same variable raised to the same power. 3x and 7x are like terms; 3x and 7x^2 are not.
“Multiplying always increases the degree.” Yes — multiplying a degree-m polynomial by a degree-n polynomial gives degree m + n. Adding two polynomials of the same degree, however, can sometimes reduce the degree (e.g., x^2 + (-x^2 + 3) = 3, a constant).



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A polynomial is a sum of powers of x, each scaled by a number. Adding combines like powers; multiplying distributes every term across every term.
The degree of a polynomial is its highest power of x. Multiplying adds degrees; that one fact predicts the shape of the answer before you compute.
Two patterns are worth burning into memory: the difference of squares (x+a)(x-a) = x^2 - a^2 and the square of a binomial (x+a)^2 = x^2 + 2ax + a^2.



Self-Assessment

Could you derive the difference-of-squares identity (x + a)(x - a)
= x^2 - a^2 in your own words?
Without scrolling back, can you multiply (x + 3)(x - 7)?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: x^2 - 4x - 21.)

3 yes: ready for 12 · Factoring Polynomials.
2 yes: redo S1 (sum of cubes) — it appears in many later contexts.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3, P4, P5 — the three identities are the bedrock.

title: 11 · Polynomials — Adding and Multiplying
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

T1 L11 — Variables
T1 L05 — Multiplication as Area
07 · Exponents and Powers



What this unlocks

12 · Factoring Polynomials — the inverse of multiplication.
14 · Quadratic Equations — solving x^2 + bx + c = 0.



Where this matters

Encryption. Polynomial rings underpin lattice-based post-quantum cryptography (NIST has standardised CRYSTALS-Kyber, an NTRU-style scheme). Polynomial codes (Reed-Solomon) protect every CD, DVD, QR code, and deep-space satellite signal.


Mathematical research. Polynomial rings are the central object of commutative algebra and algebraic geometry — entire branches of modern mathematics. The Hilbert Nullstellensatz, the Gröbner basis algorithm, the Riemann hypothesis (in its function-field analogue) — all live in polynomial-ring language.


Engineering & physics. Polynomial fitting is the simplest curve- fit method (extending the linear regression of Lesson 5). Splines — piecewise polynomials — model smooth curves in CAD, fonts, and animation.


Everywhere else. Polynomial models appear in finance (yield curves), economics (marginal cost = derivative of a polynomial), statistics (regression with quadratic and higher terms), and computer graphics (Bezier and B-spline curves are polynomial in nature).

title: 12 · Factoring Polynomials
text:
Have you done 11 · Polynomials?
Comfortable with (x + 2)(x + 3) = x^2 + 5x + 6?
Can you list the divisors of 12 (1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12) — recall T1 L15 — Primes?

title: 12 · Factoring Polynomials
12 · Factoring Polynomials
text:
Wonder

To factor x^2 + 5x + 6: find two numbers that add to 5 and multiply to 6. Can you guess them?
What about x^2 + 7x + 12? x^2 - 7x + 12? x^2 + x - 12?
What if the constant is prime — say x^2 + px + 7?

title: 12 · Factoring Polynomials
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Factor x^2 + 5x + 6.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Two numbers, sum 5, product 6.



Full solution

2 and 3: 2 + 3 = 5 ✓, 2 \cdot 3 = 6 ✓. So x^2 + 5x + 6 = (x + 2)
(x + 3).
Check: expand (x+2)(x+3) = x^2 + 5x + 6 ✓.
This is the inverse of multiplication from Lesson 11. The Tier 1 inverse pairs (add ↔︎ subtract, multiply ↔︎ divide) get a polynomial- level companion (multiply ↔︎ factor).



Problem 2
Factor x^2 - 7x + 12.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Sum -7, product +12 → both factors negative.



Full solution

-3 and -4: -3 + -4 = -7 ✓, (-3)(-4) = 12 ✓.
x^2 - 7x + 12 = (x - 3)(x - 4).
When the constant is positive but the middle coefficient is negative: both factors are negative.



Problem 3
Factor x^2 + x - 12.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Sum +1, product -12 → one positive, one negative; positive larger.



Full solution

+4 and -3: +4 + (-3) = +1 ✓, (+4)(-3) = -12 ✓.
x^2 + x - 12 = (x + 4)(x - 3).
When the constant is negative: factors have opposite signs. Sign of the middle coefficient tells you which is bigger.



Problem 4
Factor x^2 - 9.


Hint 1 — a nudge

This is a difference of squares (Lesson 11): x^2 - a^2 = (x + a)(x
- a).



Full solution

x^2 - 9 = x^2 - 3^2 = (x + 3)(x - 3).
The difference of squares is one of the most-used factoring patterns. Whenever you see \text{something}^2 - \text{something}^2, factor it.



Problem 5
Try to factor x^2 + 1.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Looking for r and s with r + s = 0 and r s = 1. So s = -r, and -r^2 = 1 ⇒ r^2 = -1. Is there a real r?



Full solution

Cannot be factored over the real numbers. x^2 + 1 is irreducible over \mathbb{R}.
To factor it, we’d need a number whose square is -1 — which leads to complex numbers i = \sqrt{-1}. Over the complex numbers, x^2 + 1 = (x + i)(x - i). We meet complex numbers in Tier 6.
Until then: not every polynomial factors with real numbers. The Fundamental Theorem of Algebra (Tier 6) says every polynomial of degree n has exactly n roots — once you allow complex numbers.


The first general method for solving the cubic equation x^3 + px + q = 0 was discovered in 16th-century Italy under truly operatic circumstances. Scipione del Ferro found it around 1515 but kept it secret. Niccolò Tartaglia rediscovered it independently and won a public mathematical duel against del Ferro’s student. Gerolamo Cardano then coaxed the formula out of Tartaglia under an oath of secrecy — and promptly published it in his 1545 book Ars Magna. Tartaglia spent the rest of his life denouncing Cardano. The formula bears Cardano’s name today.


Factoring is multiplication run in reverse — and reverse-engineering is always harder than building forward. The two-numbers trick (sum to p, product to q) turns the search into a tiny puzzle you can solve in your head; everything harder is just this puzzle with extra scaffolding.

title: 12 · Factoring Polynomials
text:
Name

Factoring a polynomial means writing it as a product of simpler polynomials.
For a quadratic x^2 + px + q:

Find integers r, s such that r + s = p and r \cdot s = q. Then x^2 + px + q = (x + r)(x + s).

If no such integers exist, the polynomial is irreducible over the integers.
Common patterns to recognise instantly:



Pattern
Factored form




x^2 - a^2 (difference of squares)
(x + a)(x - a)


x^2 + 2ax + a^2 (square of a binomial)
(x + a)^2


x^2 - 2ax + a^2
(x - a)^2


x^3 + a^3 (sum of cubes)
(x + a)(x^2 - ax + a^2)


x^3 - a^3 (difference of cubes)
(x - a)(x^2 + ax + a^2)



Always check by expanding.


Find two numbers that add to the middle and multiply to the end; Difference of squares: factor as (x + a)(x - a); Always check by multiplying back.

title: 12 · Factoring Polynomials
text:
Apply

Worked example

Factor x^2 - 5x - 14.

A competent solver looks for two numbers with sum -5 and product -14.

Pairs of integers with product -14: (1, -14), (-1, 14), (2, -7),
(-2, 7).
Of these, sum -5? (2, -7): 2 + (-7) = -5 ✓.
So x^2 - 5x - 14 = (x + 2)(x - 7).
Check: expand (x + 2)(x - 7) = x^2 - 7x + 2x - 14 = x^2 - 5x -
14 ✓.

Answer: (x + 2)(x - 7).


Practice — Warm
W1. Factor x^2 + 7x + 12.


Full solution

(x + 3)(x + 4).

W2. Factor x^2 - 16.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Difference of squares.



Full solution

(x + 4)(x - 4).

W3. Factor x^2 + 6x + 9.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Recognise the perfect square: (x + 3)^2.



Full solution

(x + 3)^2. (Sum 6, product 9, and 3+3=6, 3·3=9 — both factors equal.)



Practice — Working
P1. Factor x^2 - 11x + 28.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Sum -11, product +28 → both negative.



Full solution

-4 and -7. (x - 4)(x - 7).

P2. Factor 2x^2 + 6x. (Pull out the greatest common factor first.)


Hint 1 — a nudge

GCF of 2x^2 and 6x is 2x.



Full solution

2x^2 + 6x = 2x(x + 3).
Always look for a GCF first — it simplifies what’s left to factor. This is the same idea as factoring a number into primes (Tier 1 Lesson 15) extended to polynomials.

P3. Factor x^2 + 2x - 15.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Sum 2, product −15 → opposite signs, positive a bit larger.



Full solution

+5 and -3. (x + 5)(x - 3).



Practice — Stretch
S1. Factor x^2 - 4x + 4 - y^2. (Hint: group the first three terms; recognise as a square; then difference of squares with the y^2.)


Hint 1 — a nudge

x^2 - 4x + 4 = (x - 2)^2. Then (x - 2)^2 - y^2 is a difference of squares.



Full solution

x^2 - 4x + 4 - y^2 = (x - 2)^2 - y^2 = ((x - 2) + y)((x - 2) - y) =
(x - 2 + y)(x - 2 - y).
Combining factoring patterns is one of the keys to advanced algebraic manipulation. Recognising “x^2 - 4x + 4” as a perfect square is a pattern-recognition skill that develops with practice.

S2. Why does the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic (T1 L15) have a polynomial cousin? In words: every polynomial can be written essentially uniquely as a product of irreducible polynomial factors. This is true for polynomials over the rationals, the reals, and the complex numbers. Why is uniqueness important?


Full solution

Uniqueness of factorisation makes algorithms predictable and provable. If a polynomial could be factored in two genuinely different ways, equations would have ambiguous solutions and many algorithms (simplification, root-finding, integration in calculus) would have no canonical form to work with.
The polynomial-ring analogue of the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic is the reason commutative algebra (Tier 6) and algebraic geometry work as cleanly as they do. The integers and the polynomial ring \mathbb{R}[x] share the property of being unique factorisation domains (UFDs).
(Not every ring is a UFD. \mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{-5}] is a famous counter-example, where 6 = 2 \cdot 3 = (1 + \sqrt{-5})(1 - \sqrt{-5}) in two genuinely different ways. Recovering uniqueness for such rings required ideal theory, which transformed 19th-century algebra. This is graduate-level material — Tier 7.)



Common confusions

“Every polynomial factors over the integers.” False. x^2 + 1 doesn’t (Discover P5). x^2 + x + 1 doesn’t either. Many quadratics are irreducible over \mathbb{Z} but factor over \mathbb{R} or \mathbb{C}.
“FOIL is the universal multiplication method.” No — FOIL works only for two binomials. The general method is distribute every term across every term, which generalises to any polynomials.
“To factor, just guess.” Guessing helps for simple cases. For harder cases, use the systematic search (r + s = p and r s = q), the GCF method first, and recognise the standard patterns.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

Factoring is multiplication played backwards. You are unbuilding a polynomial into the pieces that would multiply to it.
For x^2 + px + q, hunt for two integers that add to p and multiply to q. That single drill unlocks every basic factorisation.
Patterns save thought. Spot a difference of squares, a perfect square, or a sum/difference of cubes, and the answer is already written before you reach for paper.



Self-Assessment

Could you explain why finding r, s with r + s = p and rs = q factors x^2 + px + q, by expanding (x+r)(x+s)?
Without scrolling back, can you factor x^2 + 8x + 15?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: (x + 3)(x + 5).)

3 yes: ready for 13 · Similarity and Scale.
2 yes: redo S1 — combining patterns is where factoring becomes art.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P5.

title: 12 · Factoring Polynomials
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

11 · Polynomials — multiplication; factoring is its inverse.
T1 L15 — Divisibility and Primes — the analogous integer idea.



What this unlocks

14 · Quadratic Equations — if (x - r)(x - s) = 0, the solutions are x = r or x = s.



Where this matters

Encryption & error correction. Factoring polynomials is the heart of Reed-Solomon error correction (used in QR codes, CD/DVD storage, deep-space communication). Polynomial factoring algorithms over finite fields are central to coding theory.


Mathematical research. Polynomial rings as unique factorisation domains are foundational to commutative algebra. The failure of unique factorisation in some number rings led to the invention of ideals — one of the central concepts of modern algebra.


Computer algebra systems. SymPy, Mathematica, and Maple all use sophisticated polynomial-factoring algorithms (Berlekamp-Zassenhaus, LLL) — directly applying these ideas at industrial scale.

title: 13 · Similarity and Scale
text:
Have you done T1 L09 — Ratio and Proportion and T1 L12 — Shapes and Angles?
Recall: doubling a square’s side quadruples its area? (T1 L13 Stretch S1 — the square-cube law.)
Comfortable with the idea that photographs and maps are scaled versions of the real thing?

title: 13 · Similarity and Scale
13 · Similarity and Scale
text:
Wonder

Set k = 2. The new triangle has all sides twice as long. By how much does its area multiply?
The two triangles look the same but at different sizes. What property survives scaling? What changes?
Could a square and a rectangle ever be similar?

title: 13 · Similarity and Scale
text:
Discover

Problem 1
A photograph is 8 cm wide and 6 cm tall. To enlarge it to 12 cm wide while keeping its shape, what should the new height be?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Scale factor: k = 12/8 = 1.5. Apply to height too.



Full solution

k = 1.5. New height = 6 \times 1.5 = 9 cm.
The width:height ratio stays 4:3. Scaling preserves aspect ratio — same shape, different size.



Problem 2
Two triangles have angles (40°, 60°, 80°). Are they similar?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Same angles → same shape → similar (by definition).



Full solution

Yes, similar. Two triangles are similar iff they have the same angles. (A condition called AAA — angle, angle, angle. Just AA is enough since angles sum to 180°.)
This is one of the most useful facts in geometry. If you can show two triangles share two angles, all corresponding sides are proportional — and you can solve for unknown lengths.



Problem 3
A 1.8 m tall person casts a 2.4 m shadow. At the same time, a tree casts an 8 m shadow. How tall is the tree?


Hint 1 — a nudge

The triangles formed by sun-person-shadow and sun-tree-shadow are similar (same sun angle).



Full solution

\dfrac{\text{height}}{\text{shadow}} = \dfrac{1.8}{2.4} = \dfrac{T}{8}. Cross-multiply: T = \dfrac{1.8 \times 8}{2.4} = \dfrac{14.4}{2.4} = 6 m.
Tree is 6 m tall. This is the classic shadow problem, used by the ancient Greeks (Thales) to estimate the height of pyramids — the first recorded use of similarity for measurement.



Problem 4
A model car is 1:18 scale (every length is 1/18 of the real car). The real car is 4.5 m long and 2 m wide. What are the dimensions of the model? What is the ratio of areas (real : model)?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Lengths scale by 1/18. What about areas?



Full solution

Model length: 4.5/18 = 0.25 m = 25 cm. Model width: 2/18 \approx
0.111 m = 11.1 cm.
Ratio of areas: (18)^2 = 324. The real car’s surface area is 324 times the model’s. The model’s area is 1/324 of the real area.



Problem 5
If the model car (Problem 4) is solid — same density as the real car — how does its volume compare to the real car’s? What about its mass?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Volume scales by k^3.



Full solution

Real volume = (18)^3 = 5832 × model volume. The real car has 5832 × the volume.
Mass is proportional to volume (for same material and density), so the real car has 5832 × the mass of the solid model. If the model weighs 100 g, the real car would weigh 583 kg — close to a real small car.
The square-cube law again (T1 L13 Stretch S1): scaling lengths by k scales areas by k^2 and volumes by k^3. This is the reason elephant legs are proportionally thicker than mouse legs, why scaling buildings up requires new structural ideas, and why scaling a recipe 2× doesn’t always work for a cake (heat penetrates by surface area but must reach interior volume).


Around 240 BCE, the Greek scholar Eratosthenes measured the circumference of the Earth using nothing but similar triangles, a stick, and a pair of cities. At noon on the summer solstice, the sun shone straight down a deep well in Syene (modern Aswan) — but in Alexandria, 800 km north, a vertical stick cast a small shadow at the same moment. The angle of that shadow (about 7.2°, or one-fiftieth of a circle) had to equal the angle subtended at Earth’s centre between the two cities. So Earth’s circumference was 50 \times 800 \approx
40{,}000 km — within a few percent of the modern value.


Shape is preserved by scaling — but size, area, and volume are not. Doubling a model doubles its lengths, quadruples its surfaces, and octuples its insides. That mismatch is why a scaled-up ant collapses, a scaled-up bridge needs reinforcement, and a shadow can measure a planet.

title: 13 · Similarity and Scale
text:
Name

Two figures are similar if one can be obtained from the other by scaling — multiplying every length by the same constant k (possibly with rotation or reflection).
Equivalently, two figures are similar iff:

All corresponding angles are equal.
All corresponding sides are in the same ratio k.

For two triangles, similarity is determined by angles alone:

AA (Angle-Angle): if two angles match, the third must (since angles sum to 180°), so the triangles are similar.
SAS: two sides in ratio with the angle between them equal.
SSS: all three sides in ratio.

Scaling laws (the square-cube law):



Quantity
Scales by




Lengths (sides, perimeter)
k


Areas (surface, cross-section)
k^2


Volumes (mass, capacity)
k^3





Same angles, sides in ratio — that is similarity; Lengths × k; areas × k²; volumes × k³.

title: 13 · Similarity and Scale
text:
Apply

Worked example

A miniature toy is 1:50 scale of a real bus. The real bus is 12 m long and weighs 15 tonnes. What is the length of the toy? If the toy is solid (same density as the real bus), how much would it weigh?

A competent solver applies the scaling laws:

Length: 12 \div 50 = 0.24 m = 24 cm.
Mass (proportional to volume): scales by (1/50)^3 = 1/125000. Toy mass = 15{,}000{,}000 \div 125{,}000 = 120 g.

Toy is 24 cm long and (if solid) about 120 g.
(Real toys are usually hollow plastic, much lighter than this — a deliberate departure from solid scaling.)


Practice — Warm
W1. Two similar triangles. The smaller has sides 3, 4, 5. The larger is scaled by k = 4. What are its sides?


Full solution

12, 16, 20.

W2. Scale factor 3. By how much does area multiply? Volume?


Full solution

Area \times 9, volume \times 27.

W3. A scale model is 1:200. A 50 m flagpole has model height of…?


Full solution

50 / 200 = 0.25 m = 25 cm.



Practice — Working
P1. Two photos are similar: small photo 4 \times 6, large photo 10 \times 15. Confirm they are similar (same aspect ratio). What is the scale factor of areas?


Full solution

4:6 = 2:3 and 10:15 = 2:3. Same aspect ratio — similar. Length scale: 10/4 = 2.5. Area scale: (2.5)^2 = 6.25.
Check: small area = 24, large area = 150. 150/24 = 6.25 ✓.

P2. A pizza place sells two pizza sizes: 10-inch (Rs. 200) and 20-inch (Rs. 600). Which gives more pizza per rupee?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Pizza area scales like the square of the diameter.



Full solution

Area scale (20-inch vs 10-inch): (20/10)^2 = 4. The big pizza has 4 times the area of the small one.
Cost-per-area: small = Rs. 200/area; big = Rs. 600/(4·area) = Rs. 150/area. The big pizza gives 33% more pizza per rupee.
This square-scaling is why doubling pizza diameter way more than doubles the food. Many fast-food chains exploit this — “small” pizzas are deliberately less efficient per rupee.

P3. A small triangle has sides 5, 12, 13 (a Pythagorean triple). A similar triangle has its longest side equal to 39. Find its other two sides.


Full solution

Scale factor: 39/13 = 3. Other sides: 5 \times 3 = 15 and 12
\times 3 = 36. So 15, 36, 39 — itself a Pythagorean triple (15^2 +
36^2 = 225 + 1296 = 1521 = 39^2 ✓).



Practice — Stretch
S1. Why a giant ant cannot exist. An ordinary ant carries about 50× its own weight. If you scaled an ant up to the size of a person (say, 1000× longer), what happens to its strength-to-weight ratio?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Strength scales like cross-sectional muscle area (k^2). Weight scales like volume (k^3). The ratio strength/weight scales like k^2 / k^3 = 1/k.



Full solution

Strength: scales by k^2 = 10^6. Weight: scales by k^3 = 10^9. Strength-to-weight ratio: scales by 1/k = 1/1000.
The giant ant would be 1000× weaker per unit weight than its small cousin. Where the small ant carries 50× its weight, the giant ant could carry only 50/1000 = 0.05 of its own weight — i.e., it couldn’t even support itself.
The square-cube law sets physical limits on biology: this is why big land animals (elephants) have proportionally massive legs, why whales must live in water (where buoyancy supports their weight), and why insects cannot be scaled up. Galileo wrote about exactly this in 1638 — and modern biology still finds the principle indispensable.

S2. A computer simulation models a virtual world at 1:1000 scale. To realistically simulate gravity, what should the simulation do with the time scale (frame rate, physics step) so that “scaled seconds” feel right?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Falling time under gravity scales with \sqrt{\text{height}}. So if heights scale by 1/1000, times scale by \sqrt{1/1000} = 1/\sqrt{1000}
\approx 1/31.6.



Full solution

For physical realism: time should scale by \sqrt{1/1000} \approx
1/31.6. So the simulation should run 31.6× slower than wall-clock time to look right. (Or, equivalently, gravity should be reduced.)
This is why miniature movie sets (1980s practical effects era — Star Wars, Lord of the Rings) had to be filmed at very high frame rates, then played back normally — the physics of falling at miniature scale looks unnatural at normal speed. Modern CGI handles this correctly by explicit physical simulation in the right units.



Common confusions

“Similar means identical.” No — similar shapes have the same shape (angles) but possibly different size. Identical = similar and same scale.
“Doubling dimensions doubles everything.” No — doubles lengths, quadruples areas, octuples volumes. The square-cube law catches everyone.
“AAA is the most general similarity test.” For triangles, AAA (or AA) suffices. For quadrilaterals, AAA is not enough — you need additional information about side lengths. (Two parallelograms with the same angles can have different shapes.)



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

Two figures are similar when one is a scaled copy of the other — same angles, sides in the same ratio.
For triangles, two equal angles are enough. That single shortcut (AA) lets you measure shadows, model trains, pyramids, and distant stars.
The square-cube law governs all scaling. Lengths scale by k, areas by k^2, volumes by k^3 — and biology, engineering, and cooking all bend to it.



Self-Assessment

Could you explain why doubling all sides of a square quadruples its area, using the rectangle picture?
Without scrolling back, can you find the scale factor between triangles with sides 3,4,5 and 12,16,20?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: k = 4.)

3 yes: ready for 14 · Quadratic Equations.
2 yes: redo S1 (giant ant) — the strength-to-weight scaling is one of the most consequential ideas in physics and biology.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P4 and P5.

title: 13 · Similarity and Scale
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

T1 L09 — Ratio and Proportion
T1 L12 — Shapes and Angles



What this unlocks

Tier 3 — Trigonometry — trigonometric ratios depend only on the angles of a right triangle, by similarity.
Tier 4 — Calculus & physics — scaling arguments are everywhere in dimensional analysis, fluid dynamics, biomechanics.



Where this matters

Game development & graphics. Sprite scaling, level-of-detail techniques (smaller models when far away), perspective rendering — all rest on similarity. The graphics card scales mesh data on the fly using the same proportionality you used for the toy car.


Mathematical research. Scaling arguments and dimensional analysis are some of the most powerful tools in applied mathematics and theoretical physics — including in fluid dynamics, finance (option pricing scales with \sqrt{T}), and statistical mechanics.


Biology & medicine. Allometry — the study of how biological quantities scale with body size — uses these ideas constantly. Drug doses scale with body mass, but metabolic rate scales with mass to the 3/4 power (Kleiber’s law) — surprising and important.


Everywhere else. Maps and blueprints, model trains, scaled prototypes (engineering, architecture), photography, recipe adjustment, taxes (proportional vs progressive scaling).

title: 09 · Square Roots and Irrationals
text:
Have you done 07 · Exponents?
Can you compute 7^2 = 49 and 12^2 = 144?
Comfortable with the idea of an inverse operation (subtraction undoes addition, division undoes multiplication)?

title: 09 · Square Roots and Irrationals
09 · Square Roots and Irrationals
text:
Wonder

What is \sqrt{16}? \sqrt{25}? \sqrt{100}?
What is \sqrt{2}? Roughly?
Could \sqrt{2} be written as a fraction \frac{p}{q}?

title: 09 · Square Roots and Irrationals
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Compute (without a calculator): \sqrt{1}, \sqrt{4}, \sqrt{9}, \sqrt{36}, \sqrt{81}.


Full solution

1, 2, 3, 6, 9. The “perfect squares” are the squares of the natural numbers; their square roots are whole.



Problem 2
Estimate \sqrt{50} without a calculator.


Hint 1 — a nudge

50 is between 49 = 7^2 and 64 = 8^2. So \sqrt{50} is between 7 and 8. Closer to which?



Full solution

\sqrt{50} is just slightly above 7 — closer to 7 than to 8. Try 7.07: 7.07^2 \approx 49.98. So \sqrt{50} \approx 7.07.
Estimating by bracketing between perfect squares is a useful technique that survives all the way into high-end engineering — quick sanity checks on calculator output.



Problem 3
Use exponent rules to simplify \sqrt{a^2} for a \ge 0. (Recall \sqrt{a} = a^{1/2} — the square root is the half-power.)


Hint 1 — a nudge

\sqrt{a^2} = (a^2)^{1/2} = a^{2 \cdot 1/2} = a^1 = a.



Full solution

\sqrt{a^2} = a (for a \ge 0). And \sqrt{a} \cdot \sqrt{a} = a — that’s the definition.
Square root and squaring are inverse operations, just like addition/subtraction and multiplication/division. The notation a^{1/2} for \sqrt{a} extends the exponent laws to fractional exponents — we just discovered fractional exponents implicitly.



Problem 4
Compute \sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{8}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\sqrt{a} \cdot \sqrt{b} = \sqrt{ab} (since both sides squared equal ab).



Full solution

\sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{8} = \sqrt{16} = 4.
The product of two irrationals can be rational. Often, simplifying a product of square roots reveals a clean answer.



Problem 5
Suppose \sqrt{2} = \dfrac{p}{q} for some integers p, q in lowest terms (no common factor). Squaring: 2 = \dfrac{p^2}{q^2}, so p^2 =
2 q^2.
This means p^2 is even, so p must be even. Write p = 2k: (2k)^2 = 2q^2, i.e. 4k^2 = 2q^2, i.e. 2k^2 = q^2. So q^2 is also even, so q is even.
But then p and q are both even — contradicting “lowest terms”.
What does this prove?


Full solution

\sqrt{2} cannot be written as a fraction — it is irrational.
This is the famous proof, attributed to the school of Pythagoras (c. 500 BCE). Legend has it that the Pythagoreans had built their philosophy on the belief that all numbers are rational, and the discovery of irrationals was so disturbing that they tried to keep it secret — and supposedly drowned the discoverer (Hippasus) at sea. The truth of the legend is debated; the truth of the proof is not.
Since then we know that most numbers are irrational. Beyond \sqrt{2}: \sqrt{3}, \sqrt{5}, \sqrt{6}, \ldots are all irrational (unless the input is a perfect square). \pi is irrational. e (the calculus constant) is irrational.
The number system extends from naturals → integers → rationals → reals. The reals contain both rational and irrational numbers. We will build the reals carefully in Tier 5.


The Babylonians were computing \sqrt{2} to astonishing accuracy nearly four thousand years ago. A clay tablet known as YBC 7289 (c. 1800–1600 BCE), now at Yale, shows \sqrt{2} written in sexagesimal (base 60) as 1; 24, 51, 10 — equal to 1.41421296\ldots, correct to six decimal places. They found it without calculators, without algebra, and roughly 1300 years before the Pythagoreans proved it irrational.


A square root is a length the eye can see but the digits can never finish writing. The diagonal of a unit square exists — you can draw it — yet no fraction names it. Geometry hands us numbers that arithmetic alone could never have invented.

title: 09 · Square Roots and Irrationals
text:
Name

The square root \sqrt{a} of a non-negative number a is the non-negative number whose square is a: \sqrt{a} \cdot \sqrt{a} = a. Equivalently, \sqrt{a} = a^{1/2} — square root is the half-power.
For a < 0, \sqrt{a} is not a real number (we will meet complex numbers in Tier 6).
A real number is rational if it can be written as a fraction \dfrac{p}{q} of integers (q \neq 0); otherwise it is irrational.
Key facts:

\sqrt{ab} = \sqrt{a} \cdot \sqrt{b}.
\sqrt{a/b} = \sqrt{a} / \sqrt{b}.
\sqrt{2}, \sqrt{3}, \sqrt{5}, \pi, e, \ldots are all irrational.
The set of rationals + irrationals = the real numbers, \mathbb{R}.



Square root undoes squaring — the inverse operation; Half-power notation: \sqrt{a} = a^{1/2}; Most numbers are irrational — including \sqrt{2}, proved by Pythagoras.

title: 09 · Square Roots and Irrationals
text:
Apply

Worked example

Simplify \sqrt{72}.

A competent solver factors out perfect squares:

72 = 36 \times 2 = 6^2 \times 2.
\sqrt{72} = \sqrt{36 \cdot 2} = \sqrt{36} \cdot \sqrt{2} = 6\sqrt{2}.

Answer: 6\sqrt{2}. This is the simplified radical form — pull out perfect-square factors. The remaining \sqrt{2} is irrational and can’t be reduced further.


Practice — Warm
W1. Compute: \sqrt{49}, \sqrt{121}, \sqrt{0.25}.


Full solution

7, 11, 0.5.

W2. Estimate: \sqrt{30}, \sqrt{200} (give whole-number bounds).


Full solution

\sqrt{30} is between 5 (5^2=25) and 6 (6^2 = 36); closer to 5. \sqrt{200} is between 14 (14^2 = 196) and 15 (15^2 = 225); closer to 14.

W3. Simplify: \sqrt{20}, \sqrt{50}.


Full solution

\sqrt{20} = \sqrt{4 \cdot 5} = 2\sqrt{5}. \sqrt{50} = \sqrt{25 \cdot 2} = 5\sqrt{2}.



Practice — Working
P1. Simplify \sqrt{75} + \sqrt{12}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Simplify each first; then combine like radicals.



Full solution

\sqrt{75} = 5\sqrt{3}. \sqrt{12} = 2\sqrt{3}. Sum: 7\sqrt{3}. Like radicals (same number under the root) add like algebraic terms.

P2. Solve x^2 = 25.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Two numbers square to 25.



Full solution

x = 5 or x = -5. Both square to 25. The positive root is \sqrt{25} = 5 by convention; but the equation x^2 = 25 has two solutions, written x = \pm \sqrt{25} = \pm 5.

P3. Solve x^2 = 18. Give the answer in simplified radical form.


Full solution

x = \pm \sqrt{18} = \pm \sqrt{9 \cdot 2} = \pm 3\sqrt{2}.
The solutions are irrational — exact values that no decimal fully captures. Solving quadratic equations (Lesson 14) often produces such irrational solutions.



Practice — Stretch
S1. A right triangle has both shorter sides of length 1. What is the length of its hypotenuse? (Hint: Pythagorean theorem, which we prove formally next lesson, says hypotenuse² = leg₁² + leg₂².)


Full solution

h^2 = 1^2 + 1^2 = 2, so h = \sqrt{2}.
The hypotenuse of a unit right-isoceles triangle is \sqrt{2}. This is the original geometric appearance of the irrational number \sqrt{2} — and is what made the Pythagoreans realise irrationals exist (Discover P5). Geometry built the question; algebra answered it.

S2. Many encryption schemes (notably Diffie–Hellman key exchange) work modulo a prime p but use exponents that themselves have square-root operations on the side. Why might irrational numbers be inconvenient for cryptography?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Computers store finite digits. Irrationals have infinite, non- repeating decimals.



Full solution

Cryptography needs exact arithmetic — the slightest rounding error can break a key or a signature. Irrationals can only be stored approximately. So cryptography uses integers and modular arithmetic exclusively, where everything is exact.
Yet square roots do appear in crypto, in the form of “modular square roots” — solutions of x^2 \equiv a \pmod p. These are integer solutions, computed exactly, and are central to schemes like Rabin’s cryptosystem.
The takeaway: applied maths often avoids irrationals not for philosophical reasons but for engineering ones (exact storage). This shapes how cryptography is built. (Tier 7 elaborates.)



Common confusions

“\sqrt{a + b} = \sqrt{a} + \sqrt{b}.” False. \sqrt{9 +
16} = \sqrt{25} = 5, but \sqrt{9} + \sqrt{16} = 3 + 4 = 7. Square root does not distribute over addition.
“Every decimal is rational.” No. Decimals that terminate or eventually repeat are rational. Decimals that go on forever without repeating (like \pi or \sqrt{2}) are irrational.
“\sqrt{x^2} = x.” Only if x \ge 0. For x < 0, \sqrt{x^2}
= |x|. (E.g. \sqrt{(-3)^2} = \sqrt{9} = 3, not -3.)



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A square root undoes squaring. It asks “what number, multiplied by itself, gives this?”
Pull perfect-square factors out from under the radical. That is the simplification move — \sqrt{72} = 6\sqrt{2}, never left as \sqrt{72}.
Most numbers cannot be written as fractions. The irrationals — \sqrt{2}, \pi, e — live on the number line right next to the rationals, and there are far more of them.



Self-Assessment

Could you walk through the proof that \sqrt{2} is irrational, in your own words?
Without scrolling back, can you simplify \sqrt{48}?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: \sqrt{48} = \sqrt{16 \cdot 3} = 4\sqrt{3}.)

3 yes: ready for 10 · The Pythagorean Theorem.
2 yes: redo S1 (hypotenuse of unit right triangle).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P5 (the irrationality proof).

title: 09 · Square Roots and Irrationals
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

07 · Exponents and Powers — \sqrt{a} = a^{1/2} extends the exponent laws to fractional exponents.



What this unlocks

10 · The Pythagorean Theorem — needs square roots to extract side lengths from squared lengths.
Tier 5 — Real Analysis — careful construction of the real numbers, including the proof that \sqrt{2} exists as a real number (not obvious!).



Where this matters

Mathematical research. Irrationals are the bulk of the real numbers (in a measure-theoretic sense, the rationals have measure zero). Most of analysis lives in the irrational realm.


Encryption. Stretch S2 explains why crypto avoids irrationals in practice — and uses modular analogues like quadratic residues.


Game development & physics. Distance computations (Pythagorean theorem next lesson) routinely produce irrational results. Game engines compute \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2} countless times per frame.


Everywhere else. Standard paper sizes (A4, A3) are scaled by \sqrt{2} — so each step preserves aspect ratio. Musical intervals (the 12-tone equal temperament) are built on \sqrt[12]{2}. Architecture and design routinely use irrational ratios.

title: 00 · Introduction
text:
Tier 1 gave you the operations and the number systems. Tier 2 gives you the symbolic language — functions, equations, inequalities, graphs, exponents, polynomials — that lets you state and solve problems involving unknown quantities and changing relationships.
By the end of Tier 2 you can:

Read and write functions, evaluate them, and graph linear functions on the coordinate plane.
Solve linear equations and inequalities; solve systems of two linear equations.
Use exponents and scientific notation for very large or very small numbers.
Apply the Pythagorean theorem and reason about square roots and irrational numbers.
Add, multiply, and factor polynomials; solve quadratic equations by factoring.
Reason about similar shapes, scale factors, and how dimensions combine.
Recognise arithmetic and geometric sequences as the simplest forms of pattern.
