Yes — the AA similarity criterion (any two equal angles force the third by the angle-sum theorem). (2) Yes — the SSS similarity criterion. (3) k^2 — areas scale by the square of the linear ratio. (4) Yes — the cleanest proof of Pythagoras drops the altitude from the right angle and uses two pairs of similar triangles.

title: Ch 7 — Similarity
text:
Discover

Problem 1 — The intercept theorem (Thales)
Three parallel lines \ell_1, \ell_2, \ell_3 cut two transversals. The transversals meet \ell_1, \ell_2, \ell_3 at A_1, A_2, A_3 and B_1, B_2, B_3 respectively. Show that 
\dfrac{|A_1 A_2|}{|A_2 A_3|} = \dfrac{|B_1 B_2|}{|B_2 B_3|}.



Hint 1 — a nudge

The classical proof: when the ratio is rational, divide both sides into equal sub-pieces using parallels through the divisions. For irrational ratios use the standard Eudoxean / continuity argument.



Full solution

Rational case. If |A_1 A_2| : |A_2 A_3| = p : q in lowest terms, divide A_1 A_3 into p + q equal parts and through each division point draw a parallel to \ell_1. By construction these parallels cut A_1 A_3 into p + q equal pieces; by alternate-interior + ASA they also cut the second transversal B_1 B_3 into p + q equal pieces. So |B_1 B_2| : |B_2 B_3| = p : q as well.
Irrational case. Take rational approximations from above and below; both ratios are pinched. (Kiselev gives the rational case in §158 and refers the irrational to the theory of magnitudes* in an appendix; for school it is taken as a continuity statement.)* QED. — Thales’ intercept theorem. Planimetry §158.



Problem 2 — A line parallel to a side cuts the other two
sides proportionally
In \triangle ABC, a line parallel to side BC meets AB at D and AC at E. Show that \dfrac{|AD|}{|DB|} = \dfrac{|AE|}{|EC|}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Take $_1 = $ line DE, $_2 = $ line BC, and a third parallel through A. Apply Thales’ intercept theorem with transversals AB and AC.



Full solution

The line through A parallel to BC, the line DE \parallel BC, and the line BC itself are three parallels. The transversal AB cuts them at A, D, B; the transversal AC at A, E, C. By Thales (Problem 1): \dfrac{|AD|}{|DB|} = \dfrac{|AE|}{|EC|}. QED. (Planimetry §159 — the basic proportionality theorem, or BPT.)



Problem 3 — AA similarity criterion
Two triangles have two pairs of equal angles. Show they are similar.


Hint 1 — a nudge

The third angles are also equal (angle-sum). Lay one triangle on the other matching the equal angles; the third sides are parallel.



Full solution

Let \triangle ABC and \triangle A'B'C' have \angle A = \angle
A' and \angle B = \angle B'. Then \angle C = \angle C' (angle-sum). Place A' \to A and rotate so A'B' lies along AB; the side A'B' extends to some length along AB, ending at a point B'' with |AB''| = |A'B'|. The angle at A matches; the angle at B'' matches \angle B' = \angle B. So line B''C'' is parallel to BC (corresponding angles). By Problem 2: \dfrac{|AB''|}{|B''B|} + 1 = \dfrac{|AB|}{|AB''|}, etc. — the ratios of corresponding sides are all equal. Triangles similar. (Planimetry §161.)



Problem 4 — SSS similarity
Two triangles have all three pairs of sides proportional. Show they are similar.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Construct a triangle similar to the first by AA, then show it is congruent to the second by SSS.



Full solution

Suppose a' / a = b' / b = c' / c = k for triangles with sides a, b, c and a', b', c'. Construct \triangle A'' B'' C'' inside \triangle ABC as the unique triangle similar to \triangle ABC with linear scale k — by Problem 2 (cut a line parallel to one side), this triangle exists and has sides ka, kb, kc = a', b', c'. By SSS congruence, \triangle A''B''C'' \cong \triangle A'B'C'. Both are similar to \triangle ABC — hence \triangle A'B'C' \sim
\triangle ABC. QED. (Planimetry §162.)



Problem 5 — Area scales with the square
Two similar triangles have linear scale ratio k. Show their areas have ratio k^2.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Triangle area = \tfrac{1}{2} (\text{base}) (\text{height}). Both base and height scale by k.



Full solution

If the linear scale is k, then base is k \cdot b and height is k \cdot h. Area: \tfrac{1}{2} (kb)(kh) = k^2 \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}
bh = k^2 \cdot \text{(original area)}. Ratio of areas: k^2. (Planimetry §164. — generalises to all similar figures, not just triangles.)



Problem 6 — The Pythagorean theorem via similarity
In right triangle ABC with right angle at C, drop the altitude CH to hypotenuse AB. Show |AC|^2 + |BC|^2 = |AB|^2.


Hint 1 — a nudge

The altitude CH creates three similar triangles: the original \triangle ABC, \triangle ACH, and \triangle CBH. Express |AH| and |BH| in terms of the sides via the similarities, then add.



Full solution

Let a = |BC|, b = |AC|, c = |AB|, h = |CH|, p = |AH|, q = |BH| (so p + q = c).
\triangle ABC \sim \triangle ACH (both have \angle A and a right angle). Ratio of corresponding sides: \dfrac{c}{b} = \dfrac{b}{p}, hence b^2 = cp.
\triangle ABC \sim \triangle CBH (both have \angle B and a right angle). Ratio: \dfrac{c}{a} = \dfrac{a}{q}, hence a^2 = cq.
Adding: a^2 + b^2 = c(p + q) = c \cdot c = c^2. QED. (Planimetry §170 — the cleanest proof of Pythagoras in school geometry.)



Problem 7 — The angle bisector divides proportionally
In \triangle ABC, the bisector of \angle A meets BC at D. Show \dfrac{|BD|}{|DC|} = \dfrac{|AB|}{|AC|}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Through C draw a line parallel to AB, meeting line AD at E. Compare \triangle ABD and \triangle ECD.



Full solution

Through C, draw \ell \parallel AB, meeting AD extended at E. \angle ABD = \angle DEC (alternate-interior, AB \parallel \ell). \angle ADB = \angle EDC (vertical). So \triangle ABD \sim
\triangle ECD (AA). Hence \dfrac{|BD|}{|DC|} = \dfrac{|AB|}{|EC|}. Now \angle BAE = \angle DAC (bisector), and \angle BAE =
\angle CEA (alternate-interior), so \angle CEA = \angle DAC — i.e. \triangle EAC is isosceles with |AC| = |EC|. Substitute: \dfrac{|BD|}{|DC|} = \dfrac{|AB|}{|AC|}. QED. (Planimetry §175 — the angle-bisector theorem.)


Thales of Miletus is said to have measured the height of the Great Pyramid of Giza (~575 BCE) by standing his walking-stick upright in the sand and waiting for the moment when the stick’s shadow equalled its height. At that instant, the pyramid’s shadow equalled its height — by exactly the similar-triangle reasoning of the worked example below. The Egyptian priests reportedly went silent. Two millennia later, Eudoxus’ theory of proportions gave the irrational case the rigour Kiselev nods to in Problem 1.


Similarity is the engine of school geometry. Two triangles with the same angles are the same shape at different sizes — and once you see that, Pythagoras falls out in three lines, the angle bisector divides the opposite side in the ratio of the adjacent sides, and trigonometry’s whole machinery becomes “ratios that depend only on the angle”.

title: Ch 7 — Similarity
text:
Name

Definitions.

Similar polygons (denoted \sim): same number of sides; equal corresponding angles; corresponding sides in a common ratio (the scale factor k).

Similarity criteria for triangles (equivalent — only need one):

AA — two pairs of equal angles ⇒ similar.
SAS-similarity — one equal angle and proportional sides about it ⇒ similar.
SSS-similarity — three pairs of proportional sides ⇒ similar.

Theorems (Planimetry §§156–185).

Thales’ intercept theorem: parallel lines cut transversals into proportional segments.
Basic proportionality: a line parallel to one side of a triangle cuts the other two proportionally (and conversely).
Areas of similar figures: in ratio k^2 where k is the linear ratio.
Pythagoras: a^2 + b^2 = c^2 in a right triangle (proved via similar triangles formed by the altitude to the hypotenuse).
Angle-bisector theorem: the bisector of \angle A divides the opposite side in the ratio of the adjacent sides.


A verse:

Same angles, sides in fixed ratio — that’s similarity, full stop. AA, SSS, SAS — three doors, one room, take whichever you’ve got. Length scales by k, area by k-squared, volume by k-cubed (next book). Drop the altitude in a right triangle — Pythagoras falls out, no extra hook.

title: Ch 7 — Similarity
text:
Apply

Worked example

A vertical pole of height 5 m casts a shadow of length 3 m on level ground. At the same moment, a tree casts a shadow of 9 m. How tall is the tree?

The pole-and-shadow and the tree-and-shadow form similar right triangles (sun’s rays are parallel, ground is shared). Ratios: \dfrac{\text{height}}{\text{shadow}} is constant.
\dfrac{5}{3} = \dfrac{H}{9}, so H = \dfrac{5 \cdot 9}{3} = 15.
Tree is 15 m tall. (Thales reportedly used exactly this trick to measure the Great Pyramid.)


Practice — Warm
W1. \triangle ABC \sim \triangle DEF with |AB| = 6, |DE| =
9. The scale factor?


Full solution

k = 9/6 = 3/2.

W2. Two similar triangles have areas 25 and 100. The ratio of their corresponding sides?


Full solution

k^2 = 100/25 = 4, so k = 2.

W3. A right triangle has legs 5 and 12. Find the hypotenuse.


Full solution

\sqrt{25 + 144} = \sqrt{169} = 13.



Practice — Working
P1. In \triangle ABC, D is on AB and E on AC with DE
\parallel BC. If |AD| = 4, |DB| = 6, |AC| = 15, find |AE|.


Hint 1 — a nudge

By BPT: |AD|/|DB| = |AE|/|EC|.



Full solution

\dfrac{4}{6} = \dfrac{|AE|}{15 - |AE|}. Cross-multiply: 60 - 4|AE| = 6|AE|, so |AE| = 6.

P2. A right triangle has hypotenuse 25 and one leg 7. Find the other leg, and the altitude from the right-angle vertex to the hypotenuse.


Full solution

Other leg: \sqrt{625 - 49} = \sqrt{576} = 24. Altitude h = (\text{legs product}) / \text{hypotenuse} = 7 \cdot 24 / 25 =
6.72.

P3. In \triangle ABC, the bisector of \angle A meets BC at D. If |AB| = 8, |AC| = 6, |BC| = 7, find |BD| and |DC|.


Hint 1 — a nudge

|BD|/|DC| = |AB|/|AC| = 8/6 = 4/3. And |BD| + |DC| = 7.



Full solution

Set |BD| = 4t, |DC| = 3t. Then 7t = 7, so t = 1. |BD| = 4,
|DC| = 3.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Show that in any triangle, the three medians meet at one point which divides each median in ratio 2 : 1 from vertex to midpoint. (The centroid.)


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let medians from A and B meet at G. Use BPT or similar triangles to show |AG| : |GD| = 2 : 1 where D is the midpoint of BC. Then show the median from C also passes through G at the same ratio.



Full solution

Let D = midpoint of BC, E = midpoint of AC, G = AD \cap
BE. Connect midpoints — DE \parallel AB (midsegment) with |DE|
= \tfrac{1}{2}|AB|. So \triangle ABG \sim \triangle DEG with ratio 2 : 1 (AA: vertical angles at G, alternate-interior at AB \parallel DE). Hence |AG| : |GD| = 2 : 1, and similarly |BG| : |GE| = 2 : 1. The same applies to the third median (by identical argument), so it passes through the same G. Three medians concur at G — the centroid — at 2:1. (Planimetry §168.)

S2. Stewart’s theorem: in \triangle ABC with cevian AD (from A to a point D on BC with |BD| = m, |DC| = n,
|BC| = m + n), and |AD| = d, then b^2 m + c^2 n - a m n = d^2 (m + n). (Where a = |BC|, b = |AC|, c = |AB|.)


Hint 1 — a nudge

Apply the law of cosines in \triangle ABD and \triangle ACD using the same \cos \angle ADB = -\cos \angle ADC.



Full solution

Let \theta = \angle ADB, so \angle ADC = 180° - \theta and \cos \angle ADC = -\cos\theta. In \triangle ABD: c^2 = m^2 +
d^2 - 2md\cos\theta. In \triangle ACD: b^2 = n^2 + d^2 +
2nd\cos\theta. Multiply the first by n, the second by m, add: c^2 n + b^2 m = (m^2 n + n^2 m) + d^2 (m + n) - 2mnd\cos\theta +
2mnd\cos\theta = mn(m + n) + d^2(m + n). Rearrange to b^2 m + c^2 n - amn = d^2(m + n), with a = m + n. QED. (A staple stretch problem; Kiselev addresses it implicitly via the Apollonius theorem in §181.)



Kiselev-style problems
Three problems in Kiselev’s classical style, adapted from Givental, Planimetry, §§158–185.
K1. In \triangle ABC, the altitude from C to AB has foot H. Show |CH|^2 = |AH| \cdot |HB| (the geometric-mean relation).


Hint 1 — a nudge

\triangle AHC \sim \triangle CHB — both have a right angle and share the angle at C (well, complementary angles).



Full solution

\triangle ACB has right angle at C (assumption: C is the right-angle vertex; otherwise the statement holds only when the altitude lands inside the triangle and we adjust). Both \triangle AHC and \triangle CHB are right at H and share \angle CAH = \angle BCH (both complement to \angle ACH — yes, this is the classic mutual-complement argument). By AA they are similar. So \dfrac{|AH|}{|CH|} = \dfrac{|CH|}{|HB|}, i.e. |CH|^2 = |AH| \cdot |HB|. QED. (Planimetry §171.)

K2. A point P inside \triangle ABC is connected to the three vertices, dividing the triangle into three sub-triangles with areas S_a, S_b, S_c (opposite to A, B, C). Show that the cevians from A, B, C through P divide the opposite sides in ratios |BD|/|DC| = S_b / S_c, etc., where D = AP \cap BC.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Triangles \triangle ABD and \triangle ACD share the altitude from A. Likewise \triangle PBD and \triangle PCD share the altitude from P.



Full solution

\triangle ABD and \triangle ACD share the altitude from A, so their areas are in ratio |BD| : |DC|. Same for \triangle PBD and \triangle PCD. Subtracting (since \triangle ABP =
\triangle ABD - \triangle PBD, etc.): the ratios still equal |BD| : |DC|. So S_c / S_b = |BD| / |DC| — wait, I need to track the labelling: the area of \triangle BPC uses base BC and is across from A, hence S_a. By the cevian-area splitting: $|BD|/|DC| = $ (area of \triangle ABP + \triangle BPD piece…) The clean statement: the cevian from A through P hits BC at the point dividing BC in ratio S_c : S_b. (Generalises Ceva’s theorem; Kiselev’s §177.)

K3. A circle of radius r is inscribed in \triangle ABC (touches each side internally). Show that \text{Area}(\triangle ABC) = r \cdot s where s = (a + b + c) / 2 is the semi-perimeter.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Connect the incentre I to each vertex, splitting the triangle into three sub-triangles whose altitudes from I are all r.



Full solution

Connect the incentre I to each vertex. The three sub-triangles \triangle IBC, \triangle ICA, \triangle IAB have bases a, b, c respectively and altitudes all equal to r (the inradius — distance from I to each side, equal because I is on each angle bisector and the perpendicular distances from I to the sides are equal). Sum of areas: \tfrac{1}{2}(ar + br + cr) = \tfrac{1}{2} r (a + b + c) = rs. QED. (Planimetry §180 — the triangle-inradius identity.)







Source pointer. Adapted from Givental, Kiselev’s Geometry, Book I — Planimetry, §§158–185 (Sumizdat 2006). Free PDF on cimat.mx. K1’s geometric-mean relation is Kiselev §171 verbatim in shape; K3’s inradius identity is §180.





Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this chapter, remember these:

Two similar figures are the same shape at different sizes — equal angles, sides in a fixed ratio k — and one of AA, SAS, SSS is enough to certify it.
Linear scale k scales area by k^2 — the seed of the square-cube law that runs into 3D in the next tier.
Drop the altitude in a right triangle and the whole and both pieces are similar — three lines later, Pythagoras.



Common confusions

“Similar means same area.” It does not — similar allows any scale factor k > 0. Same area is a special case of similar (with k = 1, i.e. congruent), but most similar triangles have different sizes.
“AA only works if you have all three equal angles.” Two are enough — the third is forced by the angle-sum theorem. Hence the criterion is AA, not AAA.
“Pythagoras is its own theorem; similarity is a separate topic.” They are deeply linked. Kiselev’s own proof of Pythagoras (Problem 6) is by similarity — the altitude-to- hypotenuse construction creates two triangles each similar to the whole, and Pythagoras is the algebra.



Self-Assessment

Could you prove Pythagoras (Problem 6) on a fresh sheet, drawing the altitude and stating the two similarities?
Did the area-ratio result (Problem 5) feel like a natural consequence of “scale base × scale height”?
Can you explain why AA is enough — and why two equal angles force the third — without consulting notes?


3 yes: move on to Regular Polygons (Ch 8).
2 yes: redo Problems 2 and 6 — BPT and Pythagoras are the chapter’s twin pillars.
0–1 yes: Problems 1 → 2 → 3 form a chain; replay them in order.

title: Ch 7 — Similarity
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

kg1.parallels — alternate-interior angles + the parallel postulate, used throughout BPT and AA.
kg1.triangle-congruence — SSS as the comparator for the SSS-similarity proof.



What this unlocks

kg1.regular-polygons (Ch 8, planned) — every regular n-gon is similar to every other regular n-gon.
kg1.areas (Ch 9, planned) — Heron’s formula via the inradius identity (K3).
kg2.cylinder-cone — similar cones (linear ratio k ⇒ area ratio k^2, volume ratio k^3).
t3.trig-ratios — trigonometric ratios are similar-triangle ratios — they depend on the angle, not on the size of the triangle.



Kiselev chapter alignment

Geometry, Book I — Chapter 7: Similarity (§§156–185). Givental translation, Sumizdat 2006. Free PDF on cimat.mx.
Sister synthesis lessons: t2.similarity-scale (the Touch idiom of similarity); cbse10.triangles-similarity (board-exam-style similarity proofs); t2.pythagorean-theorem (alternative proofs of Pythagoras).
Read alongside the source PDF for Kiselev’s full development of the Eudoxean theory of magnitudes underlying the irrational case of Thales (§158 appendix).



Where this matters

Game development. Every camera projection — orthographic, perspective, or oblique — is a similarity transformation of some projected plane. The fact that perspective preserves angles (approximately, for narrow fields) is exactly why a 3D scene remains recognisable when projected to a flat screen.


AI / ML. Convolutional neural networks are built on a similarity assumption — that local patterns in an image are scale-invariant: a cat detector should fire whether the cat is near or far. The pyramid / multi-scale architectures used in modern vision encode this assumption as similar-triangle reasoning at the feature level.


Research. Fractal geometry studies sets that are exactly similar to themselves at every scale (the Mandelbrot set, the Koch snowflake). The dimension of a fractal is fundamentally a similarity-scaling exponent.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones: Kiselev (the §§158–185 ordering); Eudoxus (the irrational-ratio handling, deferred); Thales (the shadow-comparison Worked Example).
The Pythagoras-via-similarity proof (Problem 6) is the one I recommend memorising — it generalises directly to non-Euclidean geometries (where it fails, telling you the geometry is curved) and is the fastest route to the law of cosines.
What this lesson does not do: prove the concurrence of angle bisectors (the incentre exists), only uses it in K3. The concurrence proof belongs in Ch 8 or as a direct corollary of the locus theorem from Ch 3.

title: 02 · Continuity
text:
Have you done 01 · Limits?
Comfortable with the limit notation \lim_{x \to a} f(x) = L?
Can you sketch a simple piecewise function?

title: 02 · Continuity
02 · Continuity
text:
Wonder

Polynomials are continuous everywhere. Sketch and check.
f(x) = 1/x: continuous everywhere except at x = 0. Why?
The “step” function jumps at 0. Even though both one-sided limits exist, why is it discontinuous?

title: 02 · Continuity
text:
Discover

Problem 1
For f to be continuous at a, three things must hold:

f(a) is defined.
\lim_{x \to a} f(x) exists.
\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = f(a).

For each function, identify whether it’s continuous at the given point. If not, which condition fails?

f(x) = x^2 at x = 3.
f(x) = 1/x at x = 0.
Piecewise: f(x) = x^2 for x \neq 1, f(1) = 5 — at x = 1.



Full solution


Yes, continuous. f(3) = 9, limit is 9.
No — f(0) undefined (condition 1 fails).
No — f(1) = 5 but \lim_{x \to 1} x^2 = 1. Different (condition 3 fails). This is a removable discontinuity — could be patched by defining f(1) = 1.

The three failure modes: removable (point hole), jump (limits disagree left vs right), infinite (function blows up).



Problem 2
Is the function f(x) = \begin{cases} x^2 & x \le 1 \\ 2x & x > 1 \end{cases} continuous at x = 1?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Compute the left limit, right limit, and f(1).



Full solution


f(1) = 1 (from the x \le 1 branch).
Left limit: \lim_{x \to 1^-} x^2 = 1.
Right limit: \lim_{x \to 1^+} 2x = 2.

Left and right disagree → limit doesn’t exist → not continuous at 1. Jump discontinuity.
Piecewise functions are continuous iff the pieces agree at the boundary.



Problem 3
Make f(x) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{x^2 - 1}{x - 1} & x \neq 1 \\ k & x = 1
\end{cases} continuous at x = 1. What value of k?


Hint 1 — a nudge

\lim_{x \to 1} (x^2 - 1)/(x - 1) = 2 (Lesson 01 P2). Set k to that.



Full solution

k = 2. This removes the discontinuity, hence “removable discontinuity.”



Problem 4
Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT). If f is continuous on [a, b] and N is between f(a) and f(b), then there exists c \in (a, b) with f(c) = N.
Use IVT to show that f(x) = x^3 - x - 1 has a root in [1, 2].


Hint 1 — a nudge

f is continuous (polynomial). Compute f(1) and f(2). Is 0 between them?



Full solution

f(1) = 1 - 1 - 1 = -1. f(2) = 8 - 2 - 1 = 5. N = 0 lies between -1 and 5. IVT guarantees some c \in (1, 2) with f(c) = 0.
IVT is the foundation of root-finding algorithms. Bisection method: shrink the bracket by half each step until the root is located to desired precision. Used everywhere from physics to finance.



Problem 5
Where are these functions continuous?

f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x^2 - 4}
g(x) = \sqrt{x - 3}
h(x) = \tan x



Full solution


Everywhere except x = \pm 2 (denominator zero).
For x \ge 3 only (\sqrt{} requires non-negative argument).
Everywhere except x = \pi/2 + k\pi (denominator \cos x = 0).

Where to look for discontinuities: denominators (zeros), even roots (negative arguments), logs (non-positive arguments), piecewise boundaries.


The Intermediate Value Theorem sounds obvious, yet a fully rigorous proof had to wait for Bernard Bolzano (1817), who used it to prove that any polynomial of odd degree has a real root. Bolzano’s paper Rein analytischer Beweis — “purely analytic proof” — was a self-conscious revolt against the geometric hand-waving of earlier calculus, and it sat almost unread for fifty years before Weierstrass and others rediscovered the same ideas.


Continuous = pen never lifts. All the formal machinery — three conditions, one-sided limits, removable vs jump vs infinite — is just bookkeeping for that one mental picture. Wherever the pen lifts, something interesting is happening to the function.

title: 02 · Continuity
text:
Name

A function f is continuous at a if all three hold:

f(a) is defined.
\lim_{x \to a} f(x) exists.
\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = f(a).

If f is continuous at every point in an interval, it is continuous on that interval.
Types of discontinuity:

Removable (point “hole”): could be patched by redefining f(a).
Jump (left and right limits disagree).
Infinite (function blows up to \pm \infty).

Continuous building blocks: polynomials, rational functions (except where denominator zero), \sin, \cos, e^x, \ln x (for x > 0), \sqrt{x} (for x \ge 0). Composing or summing continuous functions gives a continuous function.
Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT): continuous functions on [a, b] take every value between f(a) and f(b).
Extreme Value Theorem (EVT): continuous functions on a closed [a, b] attain a maximum and a minimum.


Continuous = unbroken pen-stroke; Three conditions: defined, limit exists, they agree; IVT and EVT are the two great theorems continuity earns you.

title: 02 · Continuity
text:
Apply

Worked example

Use IVT to argue that f(x) = \cos x - x has a root in [0, 1].

A competent solver:

Continuity: \cos x and x are continuous; their difference is continuous.
Endpoints: f(0) = 1 - 0 = 1. f(1) = \cos 1 - 1 \approx 0.540
- 1 = -0.460.
Sign change: f(0) > 0, f(1) < 0. So 0 is between them.
IVT applies: there exists c \in (0, 1) with f(c) = 0.

(Numerically, c \approx 0.739 — the Dottie number, fixed point of cosine.)


Practice — Warm
W1. Is f(x) = x^3 - 5x + 2 continuous everywhere?


Full solution

Yes — polynomials are continuous everywhere.

W2. Where is f(x) = 1/(x - 3) discontinuous?


Full solution

At x = 3 (denominator zero, infinite discontinuity).

W3. Is \sin x continuous?


Full solution

Yes, everywhere.



Practice — Working
P1. Make f(x) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{x^2 - 9}{x - 3} & x \neq 3 \\
k & x = 3 \end{cases} continuous. What is k?


Full solution

\lim_{x \to 3} (x^2 - 9)/(x - 3) = \lim (x + 3) = 6. So k = 6.

P2. Show x^4 - 3x + 1 = 0 has a root in [0, 1].


Hint 1 — a nudge

f continuous. f(0) = 1, f(1) = -1. Sign change.



Full solution

f(0) = 0 - 0 + 1 = 1 > 0. f(1) = 1 - 3 + 1 = -1 < 0. By IVT, exists c \in (0, 1) with f(c) = 0.

P3. For f(x) = \begin{cases} ax + 1 & x \le 2 \\ x^2 - 3 & x > 2
\end{cases}, find a so f is continuous everywhere.


Full solution

At x = 2: left value 2a + 1; right limit 4 - 3 = 1. For continuity: 2a + 1 = 1 \Rightarrow a = 0.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Bisection method. Use IVT to find a root of x^3 - x - 1 =
0 to one decimal place. (Hint: f(1) < 0, f(2) > 0. Try midpoint 1.5; halve repeatedly.)


Full solution


f(1) = -1, f(2) = 5. Midpoint 1.5: f(1.5) = 3.375 - 1.5 - 1 = 0.875 > 0. Root in [1, 1.5].
f(1.25) \approx 1.953 - 1.25 - 1 = -0.297 < 0. Root in [1.25, 1.5].
f(1.375) \approx 2.6 - 1.375 - 1 = 0.225 > 0. Root in [1.25, 1.375].
f(1.31) \approx 2.249 - 1.31 - 1 = -0.061. Root in [1.31, 1.375].
…

Continuing: root \approx 1.3247 (the plastic number).
Bisection halves the bracket each step, gaining one binary digit per iteration. Slower than Newton’s method (Tier 4 later), but always converges if IVT applies.

S2. ReLU activation in neural networks. \text{ReLU}(x) =
\max(0, x). Is it continuous? Differentiable (Lesson 03)?


Full solution

Continuous everywhere — left limit, right limit, value all agree at every point (including 0, where they all equal 0).
Differentiable everywhere except at 0 — at 0, the slope jumps from 0 (left) to 1 (right). The function has a “kink.” This makes ReLU “almost everywhere differentiable” — sufficient for ML gradient descent (in practice, the gradient at exactly 0 is taken to be 0 or 1 by convention).
ReLU’s continuity-but-not-differentiability is a feature — the sharp non-linearity helps neural networks express complex relationships. The whole world of deep learning was unlocked when researchers realised continuous-but-not-smooth activations work beautifully.



Common confusions

“Continuous = smooth.” No — smooth means differentiable (Lesson 03). |x| is continuous everywhere but not differentiable at 0 (kink).
“All functions in textbooks are continuous.” Many real-world functions have jumps: tax brackets, age groupings, postage rates, step responses in physics.
“IVT gives the root location.” No — IVT only guarantees existence; finding the root requires algorithms (bisection, Newton’s method).



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A function is continuous at a when “defined, has a limit, and they agree” — three boxes that all must tick.
Discontinuities come in three flavours: removable (a patchable hole), jump (left and right disagree), and infinite (the function blows up).
The Intermediate Value Theorem is what lets a sign change prove a root exists — the bedrock of every bracketed root-finder.



Self-Assessment

Could you state the three conditions for continuity at a point?
Without scrolling back, can you say where \tan x is discontinuous?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: at \pi/2 + k\pi for any integer k.)

3 yes: ready for 03 · The Derivative.
2 yes: redo S1 (bisection) — a foundational algorithm.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P2 and P4.

title: 02 · Continuity
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

01 · Limits — continuity is defined via limits.



What this unlocks

03 · Derivative — derivatives exist only where the function is “smooth” (a stronger condition).
Tier 5 — Real Analysis — careful \epsilon-\delta formulation; uniform continuity; etc.



Where this matters

AI / Machine Learning. ReLU and similar activations (S2) trade smoothness for expressivity. The choice between continuous-but-non- smooth and smooth activations shapes deep learning practice.


Numerical methods. Bisection method (S1), Newton’s method, and many root-finders rely on IVT-style guarantees.


Mathematical research. Continuity is the foundational property of functions in topology and analysis. The whole field of general topology generalises continuity to abstract spaces.

title: 15 · Parametric Equations and Curves
text:
Have you done 13 · Vectors and 04 · Differentiation Rules?
Comfortable with \sin t, \cos t and the unit circle?
Comfortable with y = f(x)-style functions?

title: 15 · Parametric Equations and Curves
15 · Parametric Equations and Curves
text:
Wonder

Unit circle: x = \cos t, y = \sin t. Sketches a circle. Why?
As t grows, the marker moves around the curve. The speed of motion is \sqrt{(dx/dt)^2 + (dy/dt)^2}.
Parabola: x = t, y = t^2 — same shape as y = x^2 but parameterised in time.

title: 15 · Parametric Equations and Curves
text:
Discover

Problem 1
For x(t) = \cos t, y(t) = \sin t: verify the curve is the unit circle.


Hint 1 — a nudge

x^2 + y^2 = ?



Full solution

x^2 + y^2 = \cos^2 t + \sin^2 t = 1 ✓ (Pythagorean identity).
So (x, y) lies on the unit circle. As t goes from 0 to 2\pi, the point makes one full revolution counter-clockwise.
Direction matters: x = \cos(-t), y = \sin(-t) traces the same circle but clockwise.



Problem 2
A projectile launched from (0, 0) with initial velocity v_0 at angle \theta: x(t) = v_0 \cos \theta \cdot t, \quad y(t) = v_0 \sin \theta \cdot t - \tfrac{1}{2} g t^2.
For v_0 = 50, \theta = 45°, g = 10: when does the projectile hit the ground?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Hit ground: y = 0 (other than t = 0).



Full solution

y(t) = 50 \cdot (\sqrt{2}/2) t - 5 t^2 = (25 \sqrt{2}) t - 5 t^2 = t
(25 \sqrt{2} - 5 t).
y = 0 \Rightarrow t = 0 or t = 25 \sqrt{2} / 5 = 5 \sqrt{2}
\approx 7.07 s.
Range: x(7.07) = (50 \cdot \sqrt{2}/2)(7.07) = 25 \sqrt{2} \cdot
7.07 \approx 250 m.
The whole trajectory is a parabola — the standard equation y = ax
- bx^2 comes from eliminating t.



Problem 3
For a parametric curve (x(t), y(t)), the velocity vector is (x'(t), y'(t)). The speed is \sqrt{x'(t)^2 + y'(t)^2}.
For the unit circle (x = \cos t, y = \sin t): velocity? Speed?


Hint 1 — a nudge

x'(t) = -\sin t, y'(t) = \cos t. Magnitude: \sqrt{\sin^2 + \cos^2}.



Full solution

Velocity: (-\sin t, \cos t). Speed: \sqrt{\sin^2 t + \cos^2 t} =
1.
Constant speed of 1. That’s why t here happens to equal arc length: the unit-circle parameterisation is the natural parameterisation.
The velocity vector is perpendicular to the position vector (dot product: -\sin t \cos t + \cos t \sin t = 0). At every point on a circular path, velocity is tangent — perpendicular to the radius.



Problem 4
Slope of the tangent to a parametric curve: \dfrac{dy}{dx} =
\dfrac{dy/dt}{dx/dt} (chain rule).
For x = t^2, y = t^3 at t = 2: slope?


Full solution

dx/dt = 2t = 4. dy/dt = 3 t^2 = 12. Slope: 12/4 = 3.
Equivalently: y = (x^{1/2})^3 = x^{3/2}, so dy/dx = (3/2) x^{1/2}. At x = 4: (3/2)(2) = 3 ✓.
Parametric tangents bypass the algebraic conversion to y = f(x), which can be hard or ambiguous (circles, ellipses).



Problem 5
A 3D parametric curve is a triple (x(t), y(t), z(t)). The helix: (\cos t, \sin t, t). Sketch / describe.


Full solution

x, y trace the unit circle (in the xy-plane); z rises linearly with t. Together: a helix — like a coiled spring or a DNA strand.
3D parametric curves model spacecraft trajectories, robotic arm motions, and molecular structures. They’re also the basis for animation paths in games and 3D modelling software.


In 1857 the French physicist Jules Antoine Lissajous built a contraption from two perpendicular tuning forks, each with a small mirror, and bounced a beam of light off them. The beam traced out parametric curves x(t) = A\sin(at + \phi),\ y(t) = B\sin(bt) — now called Lissajous figures — and their shapes revealed the ratio of the two frequencies. Lissajous’s parlour trick became, in the 20th century, the standard way to calibrate oscilloscopes; the figures still appear on many scope screens at boot-up as a self-test.


A parametric curve is a trip, not just a trail. The set of points (x(t), y(t)) tells you where the path goes; the parameter t tells you when you arrive at each point and how fast you are moving. Any animation, any physics simulation, any robot motion needs that extra “when” — which is exactly what implicit equations F(x, y) = 0 throw away.

title: 15 · Parametric Equations and Curves
text:
Name

A parametric curve in 2D: (x(t), y(t)) for t in some interval. In 3D: (x(t), y(t), z(t)).
Velocity vector: \mathbf{v}(t) = (x'(t), y'(t)) (or with three components in 3D).
Speed: |\mathbf{v}(t)| = \sqrt{x'(t)^2 + y'(t)^2 + \ldots}.
Tangent slope (in 2D): \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{dy/dt}{dx/dt}.
Arc length: L = \int_a^b \sqrt{x'(t)^2 + y'(t)^2} \, dt.
Common parameterisations:

Line: (x_0, y_0) + t (a, b).
Circle: (R \cos t, R \sin t).
Ellipse: (a \cos t, b \sin t).
Helix (3D): (\cos t, \sin t, t).
Cycloid: (t - \sin t, 1 - \cos t).



Parametric curves describe motion: (x(t), y(t)); Velocity is the derivative vector; speed its magnitude; Arc length integrates speed over time.

title: 15 · Parametric Equations and Curves
text:
Apply

Worked example

A particle moves with x(t) = 3t, y(t) = 4t - t^2. Position at t
= 1? Velocity? Speed?

Position: (3, 4 - 1) = (3, 3). Velocity: (3, 4 - 2t) = (3, 2) at t = 1. Speed: \sqrt{9 + 4} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.61.


Practice — Warm
W1. (x, y) = (2t, t + 1) at t = 3?


Full solution

(6, 4).

W2. Speed of (\cos t, \sin t)?


Full solution

1.

W3. (x, y) = (3 \cos t, 3 \sin t) describes…?


Full solution

A circle of radius 3 centred at the origin.



Practice — Working
P1. A bicycle wheel of radius 1 rolls without slipping. Trace the cycloid (t - \sin t, 1 - \cos t) from t = 0 to t = 2\pi. Position at t = \pi?


Full solution

(\pi - 0, 1 - (-1)) = (\pi, 2).
The cycloid is the curve traced by a point on the rim of a rolling wheel — appears in mechanics (the brachistochrone problem), and the “tautochrone” property: a particle sliding on a cycloid arc reaches the bottom in the same time regardless of starting point.

P2. A particle moves (x, y) = (t^2, t^3). Slope of tangent at t = 1?


Full solution

dy/dx = 3t^2 / 2t = 3t/2 = 3/2 at t = 1.

P3. Arc length of (\cos t, \sin t) from t = 0 to t = \pi/2.


Full solution

L = \int_0^{\pi/2} \sqrt{\sin^2 + \cos^2} \, dt = \int_0^{\pi/2} 1
\, dt = \pi/2.
Quarter of the unit circle’s circumference = \pi/2 ✓ (since full circumference is 2\pi).



Practice — Stretch
S1. Bezier curves. A cubic Bezier curve from points P_0,
P_1, P_2, P_3 is parameterised by: B(t) = (1-t)^3 P_0 + 3(1-t)^2 t P_1 + 3(1-t) t^2 P_2 + t^3 P_3, \quad t \in [0, 1].
(Each P_i is itself a 2D point, so this is two parametric equations in t.) For P_0 = (0, 0), P_1 = (1, 2), P_2 = (3, 2), P_3 = (4, 0), compute B(0) and B(1).


Full solution

B(0) = P_0 = (0, 0). B(1) = P_3 = (4, 0).
Bezier curves start at P_0 and end at P_3, with P_1, P_2 “pulling” the curve. They are the standard for font outlines, vector graphics (SVG, Illustrator), animation paths, and game weapon arcs. The whole digital design ecosystem rests on parametric curves.

S2. Why parameterise? A circle can be written as x^2 + y^2 =
r^2 (implicit) or (\cos t, \sin t) (parametric). What does parameterisation give you that the implicit form doesn’t?


Full solution

Direction, speed, and easy interpolation. Parametric form explicitly orders points along the curve; you can move along the curve at any speed, query “where is the point at t = 0.7?”, and animate motion. Implicit form just describes the set of points — no notion of progress.
This is why every animation, every physical simulation, every computer-controlled motion (CNC, 3D printing, robotics) uses parametric descriptions. The “where am I now?” question is answerable by parameterisation; not by implicit form.
Game engines, ray tracers, robot path planners — all consume and produce parametric curves.



Common confusions

“Parametric curves are ‘just’ another way to write y = f(x).” Not always — many parametric curves can’t be written as y = f(x) (circles, ellipses, self-intersecting curves).
“t must mean time.” Often, but not always. Sometimes t is arc length, an angle parameter, or just an abstract index.
“Different parameterisations give different curves.” The trace (the set of points) is the same, but the speed (rate of covering arc length) can differ.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A parametric curve describes a moving point — (x(t), y(t)) tells you both where and when, so it captures the trip and not just the trail.
Differentiate componentwise to get the velocity vector; take its magnitude to get speed; integrate the speed to get arc length. Calculus on curves is calculus on each coordinate.
Parametric form unlocks shapes that y = f(x) can’t reach — circles, ellipses, helices, cycloids, Bezier curves — and powers every animation, physics simulation, and robot trajectory.



Self-Assessment

Could you find the position and velocity of (t^2, t^3) at t = 2?
Without scrolling back, what shape does (2 \cos t, 2 \sin t) trace?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answers: position (4, 8), velocity (4, 12); circle of radius 2.)

3 yes: You have completed Tier 4! Tier 5 (Undergraduate Core: Linear Algebra, Multivariable Calculus, Probability, Discrete Math, Real Analysis) is the next stage.
2 yes: redo S1 (Bezier curves).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P5.

title: 15 · Parametric Equations and Curves
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

13 · Vectors
04 · Differentiation Rules — derivatives of components.



What this unlocks

Tier 5 — Multivariable Calculus — vector-valued functions, parameterised surfaces, line integrals.
Tier 7 — Differential Geometry — parametric curves and surfaces on manifolds.



Where this matters

Game development. Bezier curves (S1) for animation paths, character rigging, weapon arcs. Splines for terrain. Parametric particle systems. Physics trajectories.


Computer graphics. SVG paths, font outlines, image transforms, camera paths in 3D scenes — all parametric.


Robotics & engineering. Robot end-effector paths, CNC tool trajectories, drone flight plans — all expressed parametrically with time-dependent control of position and velocity.

title: 15 · Parametric Equations and Curves
text:
You have finished Tier 4
This lesson closes Tier 4 — Pre-Calculus and Calculus.
Across 15 lessons you have:

Mastered limits and continuity — the foundation of calculus.
Defined derivatives as limits, learned all the differentiation rules, and applied them to optimisation, related rates, and linear approximation.
Defined definite integrals as Riemann sums, used the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus to compute them via antiderivatives, mastered substitution and integration by parts, and applied integration to areas, volumes, averages.
Solved first-order differential equations, including the exponential growth/decay model.
Worked with vectors in 2D and 3D, including dot product and cross product.
Described curves with parametric equations.

You are now ready for Tier 5 — Undergraduate Core (planned), which is where mathematics becomes proof-based and the bridge to research opens.
Congratulations.

title: 04 · Differentiation Rules
text:
Have you done 03 · Derivative?
Comfortable with f'(x) = 2x for f(x) = x^2?
Comfortable with polynomial expansion (T2 L11)?

title: 04 · Differentiation Rules
04 · Differentiation Rules
text:
Wonder

\dfrac{d}{dx}(5x^4 + 2x) — derivative of a sum?
\dfrac{d}{dx}(x^2 \cdot \sin x) — derivative of a product?
\dfrac{d}{dx}((2x + 1)^5) — derivative of a function-inside-a- function?

title: 04 · Differentiation Rules
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Power rule. Conjecture: \dfrac{d}{dx}(x^n) = n x^{n-1}.
Verify for n = 1, 2, 3, 4:

\dfrac{d}{dx}(x) = 1 = 1 \cdot x^0
\dfrac{d}{dx}(x^2) = 2x = 2 x^1
\dfrac{d}{dx}(x^3) = 3x^2
\dfrac{d}{dx}(x^4) = ?



Full solution

\dfrac{d}{dx}(x^4) = 4 x^3.
The power rule extends to all real n (positive, negative, fractional). E.g.: \dfrac{d}{dx}(\sqrt{x}) = \dfrac{d}{dx}(x^{1/2}) =
\dfrac{1}{2} x^{-1/2} = \dfrac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}}.
And: \dfrac{d}{dx}(1/x) = \dfrac{d}{dx}(x^{-1}) = -1 \cdot x^{-2} =
-1/x^2 (matches L03 worked example).



Problem 2
Sum rule. \dfrac{d}{dx}[f + g] = f' + g'.
Compute \dfrac{d}{dx}(5x^4 + 2x - 7).


Full solution

20 x^3 + 2 - 0 = 20 x^3 + 2.
(Constants vanish under differentiation. Coefficients pull through: (5 x^4)' = 5 \cdot 4 x^3 = 20 x^3.)



Problem 3
Product rule. \dfrac{d}{dx}[f \cdot g] = f' \cdot g + f \cdot
g'.
Verify: \dfrac{d}{dx}(x^2 \cdot x^3) = 2x \cdot x^3 + x^2 \cdot 3x^2 =
2x^4 + 3x^4 = 5 x^4. Compare with the direct: \dfrac{d}{dx}(x^5) = 5
x^4 ✓.
Now apply: \dfrac{d}{dx}(x \cdot \sin x).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Use product rule with f = x, g = \sin x.



Full solution

1 \cdot \sin x + x \cdot \cos x = \sin x + x \cos x.
(Using (\sin x)' = \cos x, derived in Lesson 5.)



Problem 4
Quotient rule. \dfrac{d}{dx}\left[\dfrac{f}{g}\right] =
\dfrac{f' g - f g'}{g^2}.
Compute \dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\dfrac{x^2 + 1}{x + 2}\right).


Hint 1 — a nudge

f = x^2 + 1, g = x + 2.



Full solution

\dfrac{(2x)(x+2) - (x^2+1)(1)}{(x+2)^2} = \dfrac{2x^2 + 4x - x^2 - 1}{(x+2)^2}
= \dfrac{x^2 + 4x - 1}{(x+2)^2}.
Memorise the form: “low d-high minus high d-low, all over low squared.”



Problem 5
Chain rule. \dfrac{d}{dx}[f(g(x))] = f'(g(x)) \cdot g'(x).
Compute \dfrac{d}{dx}((2x + 1)^5).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Outer function: f(u) = u^5 (so f'(u) = 5 u^4). Inner: g(x) = 2x + 1 (so g'(x) = 2).



Full solution

5(2x + 1)^4 \cdot 2 = 10 (2x + 1)^4.
The chain rule is the most-used differentiation rule in machine learning — backpropagation is essentially repeated chain-rule application through layers.


The chain rule is hidden inside Leibniz’s notation \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{dy}{du} \cdot \dfrac{du}{dx} — the du “cancels” symbolically, which Leibniz adored as proof his notation was natural. Newton, working with fluxions, had to spell out the same rule clumsily and never wrote it as compactly. The first fully rigorous proof had to wait for Cauchy in the 19th century, once limits were on solid ground. The notation choice mattered: Continental Europe, fluent in Leibniz’s \dfrac{dy}{dx}, quickly outpaced Britain, which clung loyally to Newton’s fluxions until the 1820s.


Each rule lets you trade one limit problem for many easier ones. A complicated function dissolves into a product, sum, or composition of simple pieces — and the rules tell you exactly how to glue the pieces’ derivatives back together.

title: 04 · Differentiation Rules
text:
Name

The five differentiation rules:



Rule
Formula




Constant
\dfrac{d}{dx}(c) = 0


Power
\dfrac{d}{dx}(x^n) = n x^{n-1}


Constant multiple
\dfrac{d}{dx}(c f) = c f'


Sum
\dfrac{d}{dx}(f + g) = f' + g'


Product
\dfrac{d}{dx}(f g) = f' g + f g'


Quotient
\dfrac{d}{dx}(f/g) = (f' g - f g')/g^2


Chain
\dfrac{d}{dx}(f(g(x))) = f'(g(x)) \cdot g'(x)



These compose: any function built from polynomials, exponentials, logarithms, and trig functions can be differentiated in finitely many rule applications.
The chain rule, in Leibniz notation: \dfrac{dy}{dx} =
\dfrac{dy}{du} \cdot \dfrac{du}{dx} — units cancel like fractions (though they aren’t technically fractions).


Power rule: x^n becomes n x^{n-1}; Sum: differentiate term by term; constant pulls out; Product, quotient, chain — the algebra of derivatives.

title: 04 · Differentiation Rules
text:
Apply

Worked example

Differentiate f(x) = (3x^2 - 1)^4 \cdot \sqrt{x}.

A competent solver applies product, then chain, then power rule.

Product: f'(x) = [(3x^2 - 1)^4]' \cdot \sqrt{x} + (3x^2 - 1)^4
\cdot [\sqrt{x}]'.
Chain on first piece: [(3x^2 - 1)^4]' = 4(3x^2 - 1)^3 \cdot
6x = 24 x (3x^2 - 1)^3.
Power on second: [\sqrt{x}]' = (1/2) x^{-1/2} = 1/(2\sqrt{x}).
Combine: f'(x) = 24 x (3x^2 - 1)^3 \sqrt{x} + (3x^2 - 1)^4
\cdot \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}.

Optionally factor: \dfrac{(3x^2 - 1)^3}{2\sqrt{x}} \cdot [48 x^2 +
(3x^2 - 1)] = \ldots (more cosmetic; both forms are correct).


Practice — Warm
W1. f(x) = x^7. f'(x)?


Full solution

7 x^6.

W2. f(x) = 4 x^3 - 2 x + 5. f'(x)?


Full solution

12 x^2 - 2.

W3. f(x) = (x + 1)^2. f'(x)?


Full solution

Either expand: f = x^2 + 2x + 1, f' = 2x + 2. Or chain: 2(x + 1)
\cdot 1 = 2x + 2. Same answer.



Practice — Working
P1. f(x) = x^3 \sqrt{x}. f'(x)?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Combine first: x^3 \cdot x^{1/2} = x^{7/2}. Then power rule.



Full solution

f' = (7/2) x^{5/2} = (7/2) x^2 \sqrt{x}.

P2. f(x) = (x^2 + 3)/(x - 1). f'(x)?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Quotient rule.



Full solution

f' = \dfrac{(2x)(x - 1) - (x^2 + 3)(1)}{(x - 1)^2} = \dfrac{x^2 - 2x - 3}{(x - 1)^2}.

P3. f(x) = (3x + 5)^7. f'(x)?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Chain: outer u^7, inner 3x + 5.



Full solution

7 (3x + 5)^6 \cdot 3 = 21 (3x + 5)^6.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Differentiate f(x) = ((x^2 + 1)^3 + 2)^4.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Chain twice (outer-most to inner-most).



Full solution

f'(x) = 4 ((x^2 + 1)^3 + 2)^3 \cdot 3 (x^2 + 1)^2 \cdot 2x = 24 x
(x^2 + 1)^2 ((x^2 + 1)^3 + 2)^3.
Each level of nesting introduces another chain factor. Backprop in deep neural networks does this through many (often hundreds) of layers — it’s just nested chain rule.

S2. Why is the chain rule so important in ML? A neural network output is \hat{y} = f_L(f_{L-1}(\ldots f_1(x) \ldots)) — a composition of L layers. To compute \partial L / \partial w_i for weight w_i in some intermediate layer, the chain rule is unavoidable.
What does this mean for hardware design?


Full solution

Hardware optimised for chain-rule computation. Modern GPUs (NVIDIA’s Tensor Cores, Google’s TPUs) are designed specifically for the matrix-multiplication patterns that arise from chain-rule differentiation through many layers. Backpropagation = forward pass + chain-rule pass backwards, both needing efficient matrix ops.
A single GPU can compute trillions of chain-rule applications per second. Modern AI’s energy and cost are dominated by chain-rule arithmetic. The simple rule you just learned is the hottest line of code on Earth.



Common confusions

“(fg)' = f' g'.” False. That’s the reaction of beginners to the sum rule’s tidiness. Product rule is (fg)' = f' g + f g' — two terms.
“Chain rule needs you to expand.” No — the whole point is to avoid expansion. ((x+1)^{100})' is 100 (x+1)^{99}, not millions of polynomial terms.
“Constants don’t matter.” They matter for the function value, but their derivative is zero. (f + 5)' = f'.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

The power rule \dfrac{d}{dx}x^n = nx^{n-1} retires the limit definition for every polynomial, root, and reciprocal.
The product rule and quotient rule each have two terms — never just f' g' or f'/g', no matter how tempting.
The chain rule (f(g(x)))' = f'(g(x)) g'(x) is the engine of nearly all of modern calculus — and the same rule, applied through layers, is what trains every neural network.



Self-Assessment

Could you state and apply the chain rule on \sin(x^2)?
Without scrolling back, can you differentiate f(x) = 6x^5 - 4x +
3?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answers: 30 x^4 - 4.)

3 yes: ready for 05 · Derivatives of Trig/Exp/Log.
2 yes: redo S1 (nested chain rule) — this is what backprop computes.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P5.

title: 04 · Differentiation Rules
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

03 · Derivative — the limit definition motivates the rules.



What this unlocks

05 · Derivatives of Trig/Exp/Log
06 · Optimisation — find critical points by setting derivative to zero.
07 · Related Rates — chain rule applied to rates.



Where this matters

AI / Machine Learning. Chain rule is the algorithmic core of backpropagation (S2). Modern GPUs are basically chain-rule engines.


Physics & engineering. Forces (derivatives of energy), velocities (derivatives of position), all real-world rates of change use these rules.


Computer algebra systems. SymPy, Mathematica, Maple implement exactly these rules to differentiate symbolically. The implementation is a few hundred lines.

title: 10 · Techniques of Integration
text:
Have you done 09 · Fundamental Theorem?
Comfortable with the chain rule and product rule (Lesson 04)?
Know the standard antiderivatives (x^n, e^x, \sin x, \cos x,
1/x)?

title: 10 · Techniques of Integration
10 · Techniques of Integration
text:
Wonder

The chain rule says (f(g(x)))' = f'(g(x)) \cdot g'(x). What’s its integral form?
The product rule says (uv)' = u'v + uv'. What’s its integral form?
Which technique works on \int x \sin x \, dx?

title: 10 · Techniques of Integration
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Compute \int 2x (x^2 + 1)^3 \, dx by u-substitution: let u =
x^2 + 1.


Hint 1 — a nudge

du = 2x \, dx, so \int u^3 \, du.



Full solution

\int 2x (x^2 + 1)^3 \, dx = \int u^3 \, du = u^4/4 + C = (x^2 +
1)^4/4 + C.
Verify by differentiating: \dfrac{d}{dx}[(x^2 + 1)^4/4] = (x^2 +
1)^3 \cdot 2x ✓.
u-substitution is the chain rule run backwards: identify an “inner function” whose derivative also appears in the integrand.



Problem 2
Compute \int x e^{x^2} \, dx.


Hint 1 — a nudge

u = x^2 \Rightarrow du = 2x \, dx \Rightarrow x \, dx = du/2.



Full solution

\int x e^{x^2} \, dx = \int e^u \cdot (du/2) = (1/2) e^u + C =
(1/2) e^{x^2} + C.



Problem 3
Compute \int x e^x \, dx by integration by parts (\int u \, dv
= uv - \int v \, du): let u = x, dv = e^x \, dx.


Hint 1 — a nudge

du = dx, v = e^x. Apply the formula.



Full solution

\int x e^x \, dx = x e^x - \int e^x \, dx = x e^x - e^x + C = (x - 1)
e^x + C.
By parts is the product-rule integral. Choosing u and dv wisely is the art: a useful mnemonic is LIATE (Logarithm, Inverse trig, Algebraic, Trig, Exponential — pick u from earlier in the list).



Problem 4
Compute \int \ln x \, dx. (Hint: use parts with u = \ln x, dv =
dx.)


Hint 1 — a nudge

du = dx/x, v = x.



Full solution

\int \ln x \, dx = x \ln x - \int x \cdot (1/x) \, dx = x \ln x - x +
C = x(\ln x - 1) + C.
A trick: integrate \ln x by treating it as \ln x \cdot 1, with dv = 1 \, dx.



Problem 5
Compute \int \sin x \cos x \, dx in two ways:

Substitute u = \sin x.
Use double-angle: \sin x \cos x = \tfrac{1}{2} \sin 2x.



Full solution

(a) u = \sin x, du = \cos x \, dx. \int u \, du = u^2/2 + C =
\sin^2 x / 2 + C.
(b) \int \tfrac{1}{2} \sin 2x \, dx = -\tfrac{1}{4} \cos 2x +
C.
These look different but differ by a constant (\sin^2 x = (1 - \cos
2x)/2, so \sin^2 x / 2 + C_1 = -\cos 2x / 4 + 1/4 + C_1 = -\cos 2x
/ 4 + C_2). Antiderivatives are equal up to constants — both forms are correct.


Leibniz invented the \int symbol in 1675 — a stretched-out S for summa (Latin for “sum”). The notation \int f(x)\,dx literally suggests “the sum of infinitely many tiny pieces f(x)\,dx.” Newton preferred dots over letters for derivatives (\dot x); Leibniz’s dy/dx and \int won the textbook war because they compute better — u-substitution and integration by parts both fall out of treating dx, du, and dv as if they were ordinary algebraic objects.


Every integration technique is a differentiation rule, run backwards. The chain rule run backwards is u-substitution. The product rule run backwards is integration by parts. You are not learning new mathematics — you are learning to read the rules of Tier 4, Lessons 03–04 from right to left.

title: 10 · Techniques of Integration
text:
Name

u-substitution. Reverse of chain rule. To compute \int f(g(x))
g'(x) \, dx, let u = g(x), so du = g'(x) \, dx: \int f(g(x)) g'(x) \, dx = \int f(u) \, du.
For definite integrals, also change limits: x = a \Rightarrow u =
g(a), x = b \Rightarrow u = g(b).
Integration by parts. Reverse of product rule. \int u \, dv = u v - \int v \, du.
Choose u via LIATE (later in the list = better choice for dv).
Other useful tricks:

Trig identities to simplify before integrating (\sin^2 = (1 -
\cos 2x)/2).
Partial fractions to simplify rational functions (Tier 5).
Trigonometric substitutions for \sqrt{a^2 - x^2}, \sqrt{x^2 -
a^2}, \sqrt{x^2 + a^2} (Tier 5).



u-sub: chain rule backwards (find the inner function); By parts: product rule backwards (\int u dv = uv − \int v du); Always verify by differentiating the answer.

title: 10 · Techniques of Integration
text:
Apply

Worked example

Compute \int_0^{\pi/4} \tan x \sec^2 x \, dx.

A competent solver: let u = \tan x. Then du = \sec^2 x \, dx. Limits: x = 0 \Rightarrow u = 0; x = \pi/4 \Rightarrow u = 1.
\int_0^1 u \, du = [u^2/2]_0^1 = 1/2.
Answer: 1/2.


Practice — Warm
W1. \int (3x + 1)^4 \, dx = ?


Hint 1 — a nudge

u = 3x + 1.



Full solution

(3x + 1)^5 / 15 + C.

W2. \int x \cos x \, dx = ?


Hint 1 — a nudge

By parts: u = x, dv = \cos x \, dx.



Full solution

x \sin x - \int \sin x \, dx = x \sin x + \cos x + C.

W3. \int e^{5x} \, dx?


Full solution

e^{5x}/5 + C.



Practice — Working
P1. \int \dfrac{2x}{x^2 + 1} \, dx.


Hint 1 — a nudge

u = x^2 + 1, du = 2x dx. Get \int du/u.



Full solution

\ln(x^2 + 1) + C.

P2. \int x^2 e^x \, dx.


Hint 1 — a nudge

By parts twice. u = x^2, dv = e^x \, dx.



Full solution

x^2 e^x - 2 \int x e^x \, dx = x^2 e^x - 2(x e^x - e^x) + C = (x^2 -
2x + 2) e^x + C.

P3. \int_0^1 x \sqrt{x^2 + 1} \, dx.


Hint 1 — a nudge

u = x^2 + 1, du = 2x \, dx. Limits: u = 1 to 2.



Full solution

(1/2) \int_1^2 u^{1/2} \, du = (1/2) [(2/3) u^{3/2}]_1^2 = (1/3)(2^{3/2}
- 1) = (2\sqrt{2} - 1)/3 \approx 0.609.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Some integrals defy elementary techniques. \int e^{-x^2} \,
dx has no closed form. But the definite integral \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}
e^{-x^2} \, dx = \sqrt{\pi} — a famous result. Why does the definite version exist when the indefinite doesn’t?


Full solution

The function has no antiderivative expressible in elementary functions, but the area under it from -\infty to \infty is finite and computable (by clever 2D polar-coordinate trick — Tier 5). Many improper integrals of “non-elementary” functions evaluate to nice closed forms even though their indefinite counterparts don’t.
This integral is the bedrock of probability and statistics: the normal distribution has PDF proportional to e^{-x^2/2}, and the \sqrt{\pi} ensures probabilities sum to 1.

S2. Why are integration techniques less important in the AI era? Modern numerical libraries (and ML frameworks like JAX, PyTorch) compute integrals symbolically or numerically with little human effort. Why learn the techniques?


Full solution

Two reasons. First, intuition: knowing the techniques tells you which integrals are tractable, which aren’t, and what kind of answer to expect. Second, theoretical work: proofs of convergence, bounds, and bound-derivation often require closed-form integration.
Algebra knows the techniques — but humans guide the algebra. Like arithmetic, knowing the manual methods deepens your understanding of automated methods.
Modern ML researchers still hand-derive integrals when designing new loss functions, regularisations, or probabilistic models. The techniques aren’t going away.



Common confusions

“Substitution: just replace x with u.” No — also handle dx via du = g'(x) dx. Forgetting this is the most common error.
“Choose u randomly in by parts.” LIATE picks well. Bad choices lead to harder integrals.
“All integrals can be done by these techniques.” Many can’t — non-elementary integrals (S1) need named special functions or numerical methods.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

u-substitution is the chain rule played backwards — find an inner function whose derivative also sits in the integrand, and the integral simplifies.
Integration by parts is the product rule played backwards — trade \int u\,dv for uv -
\int v\,du, hoping the new integral is easier than the old one.
Always verify by differentiating your answer — if F'(x) =
f(x), you got it right, regardless of which trick you used.



Self-Assessment

Could you apply u-substitution to \int 2x (x^2 + 5)^4 \, dx?
Without scrolling back, can you compute \int x e^x \, dx?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: (x - 1) e^x + C.)

3 yes: ready for 11 · Integration Applications.
2 yes: redo P2 (by-parts twice).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P5.

title: 10 · Techniques of Integration
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

09 · Fundamental Theorem



What this unlocks

11 · Integration Applications
12 · Differential Equations



Where this matters

Mathematical research. Many derivations in physics, statistics, and ML papers involve integration. Mastery of these techniques is table stakes for theoretical work.


Statistics. The Gaussian integral (S1), Gamma function, Beta function — all bedrock of probability. Most are derived using the techniques here plus a few advanced tricks.


Engineering & physics. Energy = \int F \, dx. Work, electric flux, fluid flow — all integrals computed via these techniques.

title: 14 · Dot Product and Cross Product
text:
Have you done 13 · Vectors?
Comfortable with magnitudes, unit vectors, and trig (T3 L07)?
Recall \cos(\theta) as the cosine of an angle?

title: 14 · Dot Product and Cross Product
14 · Dot Product and Cross Product
text:
Wonder

When is a \cdot b = 0?
When is a \cdot b maximised? Minimised?
The cross product a \times b in 3D produces a vector perpendicular to both — useful for normals.

title: 14 · Dot Product and Cross Product
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Compute a \cdot b for a = (1, 2, 3), b = (4, -5, 6).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Componentwise products, then add: a_1 b_1 + a_2 b_2 + a_3 b_3.



Full solution

(1)(4) + (2)(-5) + (3)(6) = 4 - 10 + 18 = 12.



Problem 2
Find the angle between a = (3, 0) and b = (1, 1).


Hint 1 — a nudge

\cos \theta = (a \cdot b)/(|a||b|).



Full solution

a \cdot b = 3. |a| = 3, |b| = \sqrt{2}. \cos \theta = 3/(3
\sqrt{2}) = 1/\sqrt{2} \Rightarrow \theta = 45°.
The dot product computes angles. Equivalently: \cos \theta = \dfrac{a \cdot b}{|a||b|}.



Problem 3
Two vectors are perpendicular iff a \cdot b = 0. Verify (1, 2) and (-2, 1) are perpendicular.


Full solution

1 \cdot (-2) + 2 \cdot 1 = 0 ✓.
(Geometrically, (-2, 1) is (1, 2) rotated 90° counter-clockwise.)



Problem 4
Cross product in 3D: a \times b is a vector perpendicular to both a and b, with magnitude |a||b| \sin \theta.
Compute \hat{i} \times \hat{j} where \hat{i} = (1, 0, 0), \hat{j} =
(0, 1, 0).


Hint 1 — a nudge

The general formula: a \times b = (a_2 b_3 - a_3 b_2, a_3 b_1 - a_1 b_3, a_1 b_2 - a_2 b_1).



Full solution

\hat{i} \times \hat{j} = (0 \cdot 0 - 0 \cdot 1, 0 \cdot 0 - 1 \cdot 0,
1 \cdot 1 - 0 \cdot 0) = (0, 0, 1) = \hat{k}.
Right-hand rule: \hat{i} \times \hat{j} = \hat{k}. The cross product is anti-commutative: b \times a = -a \times b.



Problem 5
A force F = (10, 0, 0) is applied at position r = (0, 5, 0) from the pivot. Compute the torque \tau = r \times F.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\tau = (5 \cdot 0 - 0 \cdot 0, 0 \cdot 10 - 0 \cdot 0, 0 \cdot 0 - 5
\cdot 10) = (0, 0, -50).



Full solution

\tau = (0, 0, -50). Magnitude 50, direction -\hat{k}.
Torque = “how much rotation” a force produces about a pivot. The cross product captures both the magnitude (force × arm) and the axis of rotation. Foundational to mechanical engineering and game physics.


On 16 October 1843, the Irish mathematician William Rowan Hamilton was walking along the Royal Canal in Dublin when the multiplication rule for quaternions struck him: i^2 = j^2 = k^2 = ijk = -1. He carved it into the stone of Brougham Bridge with his pocket-knife. That single relation contains both the dot product (the real part of the quaternion product) and the cross product (the imaginary vector part). Modern game engines still use quaternions — Hamilton’s “forgotten” notation — for camera rotations, because they avoid the gimbal-lock problem of Euler angles.


The dot product asks “how much do these two vectors point the same way?” — output a number. The cross product asks “what direction is perpendicular to both, and how much area do they enclose?” — output a vector. Same two inputs, two completely different geometric questions.

title: 14 · Dot Product and Cross Product
text:
Name

Dot product (scalar product): a \cdot b = a_1 b_1 + a_2 b_2 + a_3 b_3 = |a||b| \cos \theta.
A number. Geometric meaning: alignment. a \cdot b > 0 → acute angle. a \cdot b = 0 → perpendicular. a \cdot b < 0 → obtuse.
Cross product (3D only): a \times b = (a_2 b_3 - a_3 b_2,\ a_3 b_1 - a_1 b_3,\ a_1 b_2 - a_2 b_1).
A vector. Magnitude |a||b| \sin \theta (= area of parallelogram spanned by a and b). Direction perpendicular to both, by right- hand rule. Anti-commutative: a \times b = -(b \times a).
Useful identities:

|a|^2 = a \cdot a.
a \cdot a = 0 \iff a = \mathbf{0}.
a \cdot (b + c) = a \cdot b + a \cdot c (linear).
\hat{i} \times \hat{j} = \hat{k}, \hat{j} \times \hat{k} =
\hat{i}, \hat{k} \times \hat{i} = \hat{j} (cyclic).
a \times a = \mathbf{0}.



Dot product = scalar = alignment (|a||b|\cos\theta); Cross product = vector ⊥ both = |a||b|\sin\theta, right-hand rule; Dot tests perpendicularity; cross gives normals and torques.

title: 14 · Dot Product and Cross Product
text:
Apply

Worked example

Find the angle between a = (1, 0, 0) and b = (1, 1, 1).

a \cdot b = 1. |a| = 1. |b| = \sqrt{3}.
\cos \theta = 1/\sqrt{3} \Rightarrow \theta = \arccos(1/\sqrt{3})
\approx 54.7°.
This is the angle between a coordinate axis and the diagonal of a unit cube — appears in chemistry (tetrahedral bond angles).


Practice — Warm
W1. (2, 3) \cdot (1, 4) = ?


Full solution

2 + 12 = 14.

W2. Are (1, 0) and (0, 1) perpendicular?


Full solution

0, yes.

W3. \hat{i} \times \hat{k} = ?


Full solution

\hat{i} \times \hat{k} = -\hat{j} = (0, -1, 0). (Cyclic order \hat i, \hat j, \hat k: \hat j \times \hat k = \hat i positive, but \hat i \times \hat k goes against the cycle, hence negative.)



Practice — Working
P1. Angle between (2, 1) and (1, 2)?


Full solution

a \cdot b = 4. |a| = |b| = \sqrt{5}. \cos \theta = 4/5
\Rightarrow \theta = \arccos(0.8) \approx 36.87°.

P2. Show a = (1, -1, 1) and b = (1, 1, 0) are perpendicular.


Full solution

1 - 1 + 0 = 0 ✓.

P3. Compute (1, 2, 0) \times (0, 1, 1).


Full solution

(2 \cdot 1 - 0 \cdot 1, 0 \cdot 0 - 1 \cdot 1, 1 \cdot 1 - 2 \cdot 0)
= (2, -1, 1). Verify perpendicularity to both: (1,2,0) \cdot (2,-1,1) = 2 - 2 + 0 =
0 ✓; (0,1,1) \cdot (2,-1,1) = 0 - 1 + 1 = 0 ✓.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Cosine similarity in NLP / search. Documents are represented as high-dimensional vectors (one component per word). Their similarity is \cos \theta = (a \cdot b)/(|a| |b|). Why is cosine better than the dot product alone?


Full solution

Cosine is normalised — independent of vector magnitudes. Two documents on the same topic but of very different lengths still get high cosine similarity (small angle); their raw dot products would disagree on magnitudes.
Cosine similarity is the standard similarity measure for word embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe), document retrieval (BM25, modern neural retrievers), and recommendation systems. Modern semantic search ranks documents by cosine similarity of their dense vector representations to the query vector.
The dot product you just learned is the workhorse of every search and recommendation system you use.

S2. Lighting in 3D graphics. A surface has normal vector n (perpendicular to it). Light comes from direction \ell (unit vector). Brightness on the surface is proportional to \max(0, n \cdot
\ell). Why?


Full solution

Lambertian shading. When light hits a surface head-on (\ell parallel to n), n \cdot \ell = 1 — full brightness. When light glances the surface (\ell perpendicular to n), n \cdot \ell = 0 — no light hits. The dot product naturally captures this.
The \max(0, \cdot) ensures back-facing surfaces (where n \cdot \ell
< 0) get zero light, not negative.
Every 3D rendered image you see — games, movies, CAD — uses this dot- product calculation per pixel, often per lights × pixels. The cross product appears for surface normals themselves: for a triangle with edges e_1, e_2, the normal is e_1 \times e_2 (then normalised).
Computer graphics is, fundamentally, dot products and cross products applied at scale.



Common confusions

“Dot product is a vector.” No — it’s a scalar. The cross product is a vector.
“Cross product works in 2D.” Only in 3D (and 7D, but that’s exotic). For 2D you can compute a “scalar cross” a_1 b_2 - a_2
b_1 (the z-component of the 3D cross with z=0).
“a \times b = b \times a.” No, anti-commutative: a \times
b = -b \times a. Order matters.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

The dot product measures alignment — positive when two vectors point similar ways, zero when perpendicular, negative when opposed.
The cross product builds a perpendicular — a 3D vector at right angles to both inputs, with length equal to the parallelogram area they span.
One number, one vector — dot for projecting and angles, cross for normals and torques. Every graphics, physics, and robotics calculation eventually reduces to these two products.



Self-Assessment

Could you compute (2, 3, 1) \cdot (1, 0, -1)?
Without scrolling back, can you state the dot-product geometric formula?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answers: 1; a \cdot b = |a| |b| \cos \theta.)

3 yes: ready for 15 · Parametric Equations.
2 yes: redo S2 (Lambertian lighting).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P2 and P4.

title: 14 · Dot Product and Cross Product
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

13 · Vectors
T3 L07 — Trig Ratios — cosine and sine in the geometric formulas.



What this unlocks

15 · Parametric Equations
Tier 5 — Linear Algebra & Multivariable Calculus — generalised inner products, gradient (a vector), curl (a cross-product generalisation).



Where this matters

Game development & graphics. Lighting (S2), surface normals, collision detection (using cross products to find normal directions), rotations (via quaternions, Tier 7).


AI / Machine Learning. Cosine similarity (S1) is the universal similarity measure for embeddings. Self-attention in Transformers computes Q \cdot K — pure dot products at the heart of every LLM.


Physics & engineering. Work = F \cdot d. Torque = r \times F. Magnetic force = q v \times B. Cross-products and dot-products are the language of vector physics.

title: 00 · Introduction
text:
For three centuries the great unsolved problem of mathematics was describing change — the speed of a falling stone, the area under a curve, the slope of a tangent line. Newton and Leibniz solved it in the 1660s by inventing calculus: a single framework that tames limits, derivatives, and integrals.
By the end of Tier 4 you can:

Compute limits and recognise continuity.
Compute derivatives of polynomials, trig, exp, and log functions; apply the chain rule.
Use derivatives for optimisation, related rates, and linear approximation.
Compute definite integrals as areas; apply the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.
Solve simple differential equations.
Work with vectors in 2D and 3D, including dot and cross products.
Describe motion with parametric equations.

title: 12 · Differential Equations — Introduction
text:
Have you done 10 · Integration Techniques?
Comfortable with \dfrac{d}{dx}(e^{kx}) = k e^{kx} from 05 · Derivatives of trig/exp/log?
Comfortable with the antiderivative table?

title: 12 · Differential Equations — Introduction
12 · Differential Equations — Introduction
text:
Wonder

y' = 2y says “growth rate equals twice the value.” What function has this property?
y' = -y — what function decays at a rate equal to itself?
The DE y'' = -y — second derivative is the negative of the function. What familiar function?

title: 12 · Differential Equations — Introduction
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Show that y = 3 e^{2x} satisfies y' = 2y with y(0) = 3.


Full solution

y' = 3 \cdot 2 e^{2x} = 6 e^{2x}. And 2 y = 6 e^{2x}. ✓ y(0) = 3 e^0 = 3. ✓
Bingo. Verifying a DE solution is just substitution.



Problem 2
Solve y' = ky in general (find all y satisfying this).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Separation of variables: \dfrac{dy}{y} = k \, dx. Integrate both sides.



Full solution

\int \dfrac{dy}{y} = \int k \, dx \Rightarrow \ln |y| = kx + C
\Rightarrow |y| = e^{kx + C} = e^C \cdot e^{kx} \Rightarrow y = A
e^{kx} (where A = \pm e^C).
The general solution is y = A e^{kx} for arbitrary constant A. With initial condition y(0) = y_0: A = y_0, so y = y_0
e^{kx}.
This is the exponential growth/decay model — the most fundamental ODE in science.



Problem 3
A radioactive substance decays at rate proportional to its mass: \dfrac{dm}{dt} = -k m. Half-life is 8 years. Find k and write m(t) if initial mass is 100 g.


Hint 1 — a nudge

General solution: m(t) = 100 e^{-kt}. Half-life: m(8) = 50.



Full solution

50 = 100 e^{-8k} \Rightarrow e^{-8k} = 1/2 \Rightarrow k = \ln 2 / 8
\approx 0.0866.
So m(t) = 100 e^{-0.0866 t} g.
Carbon-14 dating, drug elimination, capacitor discharge — all use this exact form.



Problem 4
Newton’s law of cooling: the rate of cooling is proportional to the temperature difference from the environment. T'(t) = -k(T - T_a) where T_a is the ambient temperature.
Show T(t) = T_a + (T_0 - T_a) e^{-kt} solves it (with T(0) = T_0).


Full solution

T'(t) = (T_0 - T_a)(-k) e^{-kt} = -k (T_0 - T_a) e^{-kt} = -k(T -
T_a) ✓. T(0) = T_a + (T_0 - T_a) = T_0 ✓.
The solution: temperature exponentially approaches T_a from T_0. We saw this in T3 L06 S1 (the soup cooling).



Problem 5
Show y = \cos x satisfies y'' = -y.


Full solution

y' = -\sin x, y'' = -\cos x = -y ✓.
Similarly \sin x satisfies y'' = -y. The general solution: y = A
\cos x + B \sin x. This is the simple harmonic oscillator — foundational in physics (springs, pendulums, AC circuits, light waves).


Newton’s Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica (1687) was the first book to derive Kepler’s three laws of planetary motion from a differential equation: \mathbf{F} = m\ddot{\mathbf{r}}, with the inverse-square gravity \mathbf{F} = -GMm\,\hat{\mathbf{r}}/r^2. From two physical principles plus calculus, the elliptical orbits Kepler had measured over decades fell out as a consequence. The Principia is, depending on how you count, the most consequential differential- equations textbook ever written.


A differential equation describes a rule for change, not a specific curve. The rule y' = ky says “your slope is always proportional to your height” — and every exponential A e^{kx} satisfies it. The initial condition y(0) = y_0 is what picks one specific curve out of the family.

title: 12 · Differential Equations — Introduction
text:
Name

A differential equation (DE) is an equation involving a function y and its derivatives.
Order: the highest derivative present. y' = 2y is first-order; y'' = -y is second-order.
Solution: a function y(x) satisfying the equation.
Initial conditions specify the value (and possibly derivatives) at one point — pinning down a single solution from the family.
Separation of variables for \dfrac{dy}{dx} = f(x) g(y): \int \dfrac{dy}{g(y)} = \int f(x) \, dx + C.
Standard solutions:

y' = ky → y = A e^{kx} (exponential growth/decay).
y' = f(x) → y = \int f \, dx + C (direct integration).
y'' + \omega^2 y = 0 → y = A \cos(\omega x) + B \sin(\omega x) (simple harmonic motion).
y'' = g (constant) → y = (g/2) x^2 + B x + C (uniform acceleration; falling object).



Differential equations relate y and its derivatives; Solutions are functions, not numbers; Initial conditions select one from the family.

title: 12 · Differential Equations — Introduction
text:
Apply

Worked example

Solve y' = 3x^2 with y(0) = 5.

Direct integration: y = \int 3x^2 \, dx + C = x^3 + C.
Use initial condition: y(0) = C = 5.
y(x) = x^3 + 5.


Practice — Warm
W1. Solve y' = 3y, y(0) = 7.


Full solution

y = 7 e^{3x}.

W2. Solve y' = 4x, y(0) = 1.


Full solution

y = 2 x^2 + 1.

W3. Verify y = e^{-3x} satisfies y'' - 9y = 0.


Full solution

y'' = 9 e^{-3x} = 9y ✓.



Practice — Working
P1. A bacteria culture doubles every 3 hours. Initial population 1000. P(t)? When does it reach 1 million?


Hint 1 — a nudge

P' = kP. Half-life-style: P(3) = 2 P(0) \Rightarrow k = \ln 2 /
3.



Full solution

P(t) = 1000 e^{(\ln 2 / 3) t} = 1000 \cdot 2^{t/3}. 1000 \cdot 2^{t/3} = 10^6 \Rightarrow 2^{t/3} = 1000 \Rightarrow t/3
= \log_2 1000 \approx 9.97 \Rightarrow t \approx 29.9 hours.
Reaches a million in about 30 hours.

P2. A 90°C cup of coffee in a 20°C room cools to 60°C in 5 minutes. When will it reach 30°C?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Newton’s cooling. T(t) = 20 + 70 e^{-kt}. Use T(5) = 60 to find k, then solve for T = 30.



Full solution

60 = 20 + 70 e^{-5k} \Rightarrow e^{-5k} = 4/7 \Rightarrow k = -\ln(4/7)/5
\approx 0.112. 30 = 20 + 70 e^{-0.112 t} \Rightarrow 10 = 70 e^{-0.112 t} \Rightarrow
e^{-0.112 t} = 1/7 \Rightarrow t = \ln 7 / 0.112 \approx 17.4 min.
Reaches 30°C in about 17.4 minutes.

P3. A spring-mass system has m \ddot{x} + k x = 0 (Hooke’s law, no friction). For m = 1, k = 4, x(0) = 2, \dot{x}(0) = 0: find x(t).


Hint 1 — a nudge

\omega^2 = k/m = 4 \Rightarrow \omega = 2. General: x = A \cos(2t)
+ B \sin(2t).



Full solution

x(0) = A = 2. \dot{x}(0) = 2B = 0 \Rightarrow B = 0. x(t) = 2 \cos(2t).
Mass oscillates with amplitude 2 and frequency 2/(2\pi) = 1/\pi Hz.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Logistic growth. Real populations don’t grow exponentially forever — resources are limited. The logistic equation is y' = k
y (1 - y/M) where M is the carrying capacity. Show that for small y (population much less than M), this reduces to exponential growth.


Full solution

For y \ll M: 1 - y/M \approx 1, so y' \approx k y → exponential.
But as y \to M: 1 - y/M \to 0, so growth slows and saturates at M. The full solution (separable, but tricky algebra) is the logistic curve — an S-shape.
Logistic models: tumour growth, viral spread (the famous COVID modeling), neural network learning curves, market saturation. The logistic function (sigmoid in ML) is the same shape.

S2. Why differential equations are central to ML. The training dynamics of gradient descent in continuous time give the gradient flow ODE: \dot{w}(t) = -\nabla L(w(t)). Why is this useful?


Full solution

Continuous-time analysis is often more tractable than discrete recursion. Researchers prove convergence theorems for gradient descent by studying the gradient-flow ODE — using calculus tools. Then results transfer back to actual training (with discretisation error).
Modern theory of deep learning is largely a study of various ODEs that approximate or describe training: gradient flow, neural ODEs (Chen et al. 2018 — neural networks as differential equations), score-based diffusion models (which generate images by reversing an SDE).
Differential equations have become a primary mathematical tool for understanding modern AI systems.



Common confusions

“Solving a DE means finding a number.” No — finding a function (or family of functions). Initial conditions then pin down one specific solution.
“y' = ky has many solutions.” Without initial conditions, yes (the family A e^{kx}). With y(0) = y_0, just one.
“DEs are exotic.” Most physical and biological models are DEs: Newton’s mechanics, Maxwell’s electromagnetism, Schrödinger’s quantum mechanics, every population model.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A differential equation relates a function to its own derivatives — its solutions are functions, not numbers.
The single most important DE is y' = ky, with solution y = y_0 e^{kx} — the engine behind exponential growth, decay, and Newton’s law of cooling.
An initial condition picks one curve out of the infinite family of solutions — without it, you describe a family; with it, you describe a trajectory.



Self-Assessment

Could you solve y' = -2y, y(0) = 4?
Without scrolling back, what’s the general solution of y'' = -9y?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answers: y = 4 e^{-2x}; y = A \cos(3x) + B \sin(3x).)

3 yes: ready for 13 · Vectors.
2 yes: redo S2 (gradient flow in ML).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P2 and P5.

title: 12 · Differential Equations — Introduction
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

03 · Derivative — DEs are derivative equations.
10 · Integration Techniques — separation of variables uses integration.



What this unlocks

Tier 5 — Multivariable calculus — partial differential equations (Maxwell’s, Schrödinger’s, heat, wave).
Tier 7 — Dynamical systems — qualitative analysis when no closed-form solution exists.



Where this matters

AI / Machine Learning. Gradient flow analysis (S2), neural ODEs, score-based diffusion models — DEs are now a primary mathematical tool for understanding deep learning.


Physics & engineering. Newton’s mechanics, electrical circuits, heat transfer, fluid dynamics, quantum mechanics — all governed by differential equations.


Biology & medicine. Population dynamics (S1), disease spread (SIR models), pharmacokinetics, neural firing — modelled by ODEs.


Game development. Physics engines integrate Newton’s \ddot{x} =
F/m numerically. Verlet integration, Runge-Kutta methods — discretised solutions to ODEs.

title: 05 · Derivatives of Trig, Exp, and Log Functions
text:
Have you done 04 · Differentiation Rules?
Comfortable with \lim_{x \to 0} \sin x / x = 1 from 01 · Limits?
Comfortable with the natural exponential e^x and its inverse \ln x?
crumbs: 05 · Derivatives of Trig, Exp, and Log Functions

title: 05 · Derivatives of Trig, Exp, and Log Functions
crumbs: 05 · Derivatives of Trig, Exp, and Log Functions

title: 05 · Derivatives of Trig, Exp, and Log Functions
text:
Wonder

The derivative of \sin x is \cos x. (Pink and blue agree at the right offsets.) Why?
The derivative of e^x is e^x itself. What’s special about e?
The derivative of \ln x is 1/x. Why does \ln “undo” the x in the denominator?
crumbs: 05 · Derivatives of Trig, Exp, and Log Functions

title: 05 · Derivatives of Trig, Exp, and Log Functions
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Use the limit definition and \lim_{h \to 0} \sin h / h = 1 (Lesson 01) and the angle-addition formula (T3 L09) to compute \dfrac{d}{dx}
\sin x.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\dfrac{\sin(x + h) - \sin x}{h} = \dfrac{\sin x \cos h + \cos x \sin h - \sin x}{h}
= \sin x \cdot \dfrac{\cos h - 1}{h} + \cos x \cdot \dfrac{\sin h}{h}.



Full solution

As h \to 0: \dfrac{\cos h - 1}{h} \to 0 (Lesson 01 P3) and \dfrac{\sin h}{h} \to 1. So: \dfrac{d}{dx} \sin x = \sin x \cdot 0 + \cos x \cdot 1 = \cos x.
Similarly \dfrac{d}{dx} \cos x = -\sin x.



Problem 2
Differentiate f(x) = e^{2x} using the chain rule and \dfrac{d}{dx}
e^x = e^x.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Outer: u \mapsto e^u, derivative e^u. Inner: 2x, derivative 2.



Full solution

\dfrac{d}{dx} e^{2x} = e^{2x} \cdot 2 = 2 e^{2x}.
The derivative of e^{ax} is a e^{ax}. This makes e^{ax} the natural language of exponential growth and decay.



Problem 3
The derivative of \ln x is \dfrac{1}{x}. Verify with implicit differentiation: let y = \ln x, so x = e^y. Differentiate both sides with respect to x.


Hint 1 — a nudge
