This “method of exhaustion” is the direct ancestor of integral calculus, invented two thousand years later by Newton and Leibniz. Archimedes was so proud of his result on the sphere (V = \tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3) that he asked for a sphere inscribed in a cylinder to be carved on his tombstone.


Length, area, and volume aren’t different amounts of the same thing — they live in different worlds (1D, 2D, 3D), measured in different units, and they scale by different factors. Mix them up and your answers come out in the wrong universe.

title: 13 · Area, Perimeter, Volume
text:
Name

Perimeter is the total distance around the boundary of a 2D shape, measured in linear units (m, cm, km).
Area is the surface inside a 2D shape, measured in square units (m², cm²).
Volume is the space inside a 3D shape, measured in cubic units (m³, cm³).
Key formulas (Tier 1 set):









Shape
Perimeter
Area
(Volume, if 3D)




Rectangle (w × h)
2(w + h)
w \times h
—


Square (side s)
4s
s^2
—


Circle (radius r)
2\pi r (circumference)
\pi r^2
—


Triangle (base b, height h)
sum of 3 sides
\tfrac{1}{2}\,b\,h
—


Cuboid (\ell \times w \times h)
—
(surface area, later)
\ell \times w \times h


Cube (side s)
—
—
s^3





Around the edge — perimeter, in length; Inside the edge — area, in length squared; Inside the box — volume, in length cubed.

title: 13 · Area, Perimeter, Volume
text:
Apply

Worked example

A rectangular swimming pool is 25 m long, 10 m wide, and 2 m deep. A tile costs Rs. 50 per square metre to cover the floor. What is the total tile cost? How much water (in cubic metres) does the pool hold?

A competent solver attacks each measurement separately:

Floor area: 25 \times 10 = 250 m².
Tile cost: 250 \times 50 = 12{,}500 rupees.
Volume of water: 25 \times 10 \times 2 = 500 m³.

Tile cost: Rs. 12,500. Water: 500 m³. Notice each calculation uses a different multiplication: 2D for floor, 3D for water.


Practice — Warm
W1. A rectangle is 7 m by 4 m. Perimeter? Area?


Full solution

P = 2(7+4) = 22 m. A = 7 \times 4 = 28 m².

W2. A square has side 9 cm. Perimeter? Area?


Full solution

P = 36 cm. A = 81 cm².

W3. A box is 5 × 4 × 3 m. Volume?


Full solution

V = 60 m³.



Practice — Working
P1. A rectangular field is 80 m by 50 m. Find the cost of fencing it at Rs. 25 per metre.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Fencing follows the perimeter, not the area.



Full solution

P = 2(80+50) = 260 m. Cost = 260 \times 25 = 6{,}500 rupees.

P2. A circular garden has radius 7 m. Find the area covered. (Use \pi = \tfrac{22}{7} for an exact-looking answer.)


Full solution

A = \tfrac{22}{7} \times 7^2 = \tfrac{22}{7} \times 49 = 22 \times 7 =
154 m². The choice of \tfrac{22}{7} as the approximation makes arithmetic clean when the radius is a multiple of 7.

P3. A triangular sail has base 4 m and height 3 m. What is its area?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Triangle area = \tfrac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}.



Full solution

A = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 4 \times 3 = 6 m².
The factor of \tfrac{1}{2} has a beautiful explanation: any triangle is exactly half of the rectangle that would enclose it (with the same base and height). So a triangle’s area is half of base × height. We will prove this carefully in Tier 3.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Two cubes: one with side 2 m, the other with side 4 m. The second cube has a side twice that of the first. By how much does the volume multiply?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Compute both volumes.



Full solution

First cube: V = 2^3 = 8 m³. Second cube: V = 4^3 = 64 m³. The volume multiplies by 8 — that is, 2^3.
General rule: if you scale every dimension of a shape by a factor k, then perimeter scales by k, area by k^2, and volume by k^3.
This is why a baby elephant is short and stout, while an adult elephant needs proportionally thicker legs: doubling the elephant’s height makes it 8× heavier (volume), but only 4× stronger (cross-sectional area of muscle and bone, which scales like area). At some scale, the legs can no longer hold up the body — the square-cube law sets a ceiling on how big land animals can grow. Galileo first wrote about this in 1638.

S2. A 3D model in a video game is a mesh of many tiny triangles. A single character might have 20,000 triangles. The graphics card computes the area of each (for lighting and rendering). What does this say about the importance of the triangle area formula in real-time graphics?


Full solution

Every frame of a game (60 frames per second on most modern hardware) has to compute area-related quantities for millions of triangles. The simple \tfrac{1}{2} b h formula is run as a vector cross-product on a GPU, but the underlying idea is exactly what you used in Practice P3.
The area, perimeter, and volume formulas you’ve just met are the foundational primitives of computer graphics. Tier 4 (vectors) and Tier 6 (computational geometry) build the full toolkit; Tier 1 sets the language.



Common confusions

“Area and perimeter are the same kind of measurement.” No — perimeter is a length (1D, m), area is a square length (2D, m²). They have different units and different behaviours under scaling (Stretch S1).
“Volume = side × side.” That’s the area of a square’s face. Volume needs three multiplications, one per dimension.
“Doubling the dimensions doubles the area.” No — it quadruples the area, and octuples the volume. The square-cube law (Stretch S1) surprises everyone the first time.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

Perimeter measures around, area measures inside, and volume measures the space within — three different worlds, three different units (m, m², m³).
For rectangles, area is width × height; for boxes, volume is length × width × height — and once you have \pi r^2 in your pocket you can measure circles too.
Scale every dimension by k and area scales by k^2, volume by k^3 — the square-cube law** explains everything from elephant legs to packaging design.**



Self-Assessment

Could you explain to a friend why a 3×4 rectangle and a 2×6 rectangle have the same area but different perimeters?
Without scrolling back, can you compute the volume of a 5 × 6 × 2 box?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer to #2: V = 60 cubic units.)

3 yes: ready for Lesson 14 — Data and the Average.
2 yes: redo S1 (the square-cube law) — that result is genuinely surprising and worth feeling.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and the worked example.

title: 13 · Area, Perimeter, Volume
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

Lesson 12 — Shapes and Angles — we needed names for shapes (rectangle, square, triangle, circle) before measuring them.
Lesson 5 — Multiplication as Area — the rectangle picture is the basis for both area and (extended) volume.



What this unlocks

Tier 2 — Pythagoras’ theorem — a beautiful relationship between the areas of squares built on the sides of a right-angled triangle.
Tier 3 — Trigonometry & coordinate geometry — once positions are numbers, areas become integrals.
Tier 4 — Calculus — area under any curve, not just polygons.



Where this matters

Game development & graphics. Stretch S2 makes the case: every rendered triangle area is computed from this formula. Volumes appear in collision detection (“does this cube intersect that one?”), particle systems, and physics. The Tier 1 measurements are the alphabet.


Engineering & architecture. Floor space, paint coverage, water tank capacity, beam strength (cross-section), heat loss (surface area). Every building decision is some combination of perimeter, area, and volume measurements.


Mathematical research. The isoperimetric problem (Discover P3) — “what shape encloses the most area for a given perimeter?” — is the ancestor of the calculus of variations, a major branch of analysis.


Everywhere else. Carpet purchasing, garden planning, packaging design, fuel-tank capacity, room ventilation, agriculture (area yields), real estate (price per square metre).








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones honoured: Pestalozzi (the cell-grid widget makes area literally countable), Boole (the can two rectangles have the same area but different perimeters? question is a classic Boolean question), Russian school (Discover P3 derives the isoperimetric intuition from concrete cases), Bhāskara (single-box Name + verse; Bhāskara’s Līlāvatī contains many area problems set in vivid scenes — moats, ponds, gardens), 3Blue1Brown (the square-cube law in S1 is exactly the kind of dimension-shifting moment 3B1B excels at).
Self-study guarantees all present.

title: 08 · Decimals
text:
Have you done Lesson 3 — Place Value and Lesson 7 — Fractions?
Can you say what each digit of 357 is worth?
Have you ever read a price like “Rs. 24.50” or a measurement like “1.6 metres”?

title: 08 · Decimals
08 · Decimals
text:
Wonder

What does the digit “7” in 0.7 mean? What does “7” mean in 0.07?
Why does 2.50 mean the same as 2.5? What if you wrote 2.5000?
The fraction \tfrac{1}{4} is also 0.25. The fraction \tfrac{1}{3} is…?

title: 08 · Decimals
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Read aloud: 3.7, 0.42, 2.05.
What is each digit worth?


Full solution


3.7 = “three and seven tenths” = 3 + \tfrac{7}{10}.
0.42 = “zero and forty-two hundredths” = \tfrac{4}{10} + \tfrac{2}{100}.
2.05 = “two and five hundredths” = 2 + \tfrac{0}{10} + \tfrac{5}{100}.

The 0 in the tenths column of “2.05” is doing the same placeholder duty as the 0 in 305 (Lesson 3). Without it, you’d read “2.5”, which is ten times bigger.



Problem 2
Convert these fractions to decimals: \dfrac{1}{2}, \dfrac{3}{4}, \dfrac{1}{5}, \dfrac{1}{8}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

A fraction with denominator 10, 100, 1000, … is read directly as a decimal. Rewrite each fraction with such a denominator.



Full solution


\tfrac{1}{2} = \tfrac{5}{10} = 0.5
\tfrac{3}{4} = \tfrac{75}{100} = 0.75
\tfrac{1}{5} = \tfrac{2}{10} = 0.2
\tfrac{1}{8} = \tfrac{125}{1000} = 0.125

Every fraction whose denominator is a product of 2s and 5s converts to a finite decimal.



Problem 3
Try \dfrac{1}{3}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Long-divide 1.000\ldots by 3.



Full solution

1 \div 3 = 0.3333\ldots — the 3s never stop.
We write this as 0.\overline{3} — the bar over the 3 means “repeats forever”. Some fractions (those whose denominator has prime factors other than 2 or 5) give repeating decimals. The decimal expansion is infinite but periodic. It’s still a perfectly good number — just one that needs infinitely many digits to write in decimal form.
Both 0.\overline{3} and \tfrac{1}{3} are exactly the same number, in two different costumes.



Problem 4
Compute 2.4 + 1.7 by treating the decimal point as the alignment guide.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Add column by column from the right, just like whole-number addition. Carry across the dot if needed.



Full solution

\begin{array}{r} 2.4 \\ +\,1.7 \\ \hline 4.1 \end{array} Tenths: 4 + 7 = 11 → write 1, carry 1. Ones: 2 + 1 + 1 = 4.
Answer: 4.1. Decimal arithmetic is just place-value arithmetic with columns extending to the right of the dot.



Problem 5
What is 0.1 × 0.1? (Picture two strips, each one-tenth of a unit square.)


Hint 1 — a nudge

The rectangle picture from Lesson 5 still applies. Width 0.1, height 0.1. What fraction of a unit square is that?



Full solution

0.1 \times 0.1 = 0.01 — one hundredth. Picture: a tiny square, one-tenth wide and one-tenth tall, fits exactly 100 times into a 1×1 square (10 across, 10 down). Each tiny square is \tfrac{1}{100} of the whole.
So multiplying two decimals less than 1 gives a smaller result. This contradicts the whole-number intuition “multiplication makes things bigger” — and is the source of much confusion. The rectangle picture explains it cleanly.


The Flemish engineer Simon Stevin published De Thiende (“The Tenth”) in 1585 — a 36-page pamphlet arguing that decimal fractions should replace the clumsy mixed units everyone used (rods and feet, pounds and shillings, hours and minutes). He proposed the decimal point itself as a notation. His pamphlet is one of the great “small books that changed the world”: within a century, decimals were standard for science and commerce, and three hundred years later most of the world’s measurements went metric — the long shadow of Stevin’s argument.


The decimal point isn’t a wall — it’s a marker on the same place-value road. Walking left, each column is ten times bigger; walking right, each column is ten times smaller. The dot just tells you where the ones are. Every decimal is a fraction in different clothes; every fraction has a decimal twin.

title: 08 · Decimals
text:
Name

A decimal is a number written in base-10 place value, with columns continuing to the right of a decimal point — but each step right divides the value by 10 (instead of multiplying by 10 as we go left).
The columns are: \ldots \mid \text{hundreds} \mid \text{tens} \mid \text{ones} \mathbf{\ .\ } \text{tenths} \mid \text{hundredths} \mid \text{thousandths} \mid \ldots
A decimal is the same as a fraction: 0.d_1 d_2 d_3 = \frac{d_1}{10} + \frac{d_2}{100} + \frac{d_3}{1000}.
A fraction whose reduced denominator only has prime factors 2 and 5 terminates as a decimal; otherwise the decimal repeats forever.


To the right of the dot, divide instead of multiply; Each new column, ten times smaller than the last. Every fraction has a decimal — Some end; some sing forever.

title: 08 · Decimals
text:
Apply

Worked example

Compute 2.45 + 1.6.

A competent solver aligns the decimal points, padding with a zero where needed: \begin{array}{r} 2.45 \\ +\,1.60 \\ \hline 4.05 \end{array} Hundredths: 5 + 0 = 5. Tenths: 4 + 6 = 10 — write 0, carry 1. Ones: 2 + 1 + 1 = 4. Answer: 4.05.
Padding 1.6 to “1.60” makes the place-value alignment explicit. The trailing zero adds nothing, but it makes the column structure visible.


Practice — Warm
W1. Convert: \tfrac{2}{5}, \tfrac{7}{10}, \tfrac{9}{100} to decimals.


Full solution

0.4, 0.7, 0.09.

W2. Convert: 0.6, 0.25, 0.125 to fractions in their simplest form.


Full solution

0.6 = \tfrac{6}{10} = \tfrac{3}{5}. \ 0.25 = \tfrac{25}{100} =
\tfrac{1}{4}. \ 0.125 = \tfrac{125}{1000} = \tfrac{1}{8}.

W3. Compute 3.2 - 1.7.


Full solution

Tenths: 2 - 7 underflows; borrow 1 from the ones column. Tenths: 12 -
7 = 5. Ones: (3-1) - 1 = 1. Answer: 1.5.



Practice — Working
P1. Compute 0.4 \times 0.5.


Hint 1 — a nudge

0.4 \times 0.5 = \tfrac{4}{10} \times \tfrac{5}{10}. Multiply across.



Full solution

\tfrac{4 \times 5}{10 \times 10} = \tfrac{20}{100} = 0.2.
A useful shortcut: count the digits after the decimal point in each factor (here, 1 and 1 — total 2). The product has the same total of decimal digits. 4 \times 5 = 20; place the decimal so the result has 2 decimal digits: 0.20 = 0.2. ✓

P2. Order from smallest to largest: 0.5, \ 0.05, \ 0.55, \ 0.005.


Hint 1 — a nudge

The widest fraction is the smallest. Pad with zeros to make all the same length: 0.500, 0.050, 0.550, 0.005. Now compare like whole numbers.



Full solution

0.005 < 0.05 < 0.5 < 0.55.
A surprisingly common error: assuming “more digits = bigger”. With the same digits but different decimal-point positions, place value rules.

P3. A petrol pump charges Rs. 102.40 per litre. You pump 12.5 litres. What’s the bill?


Hint 1 — a nudge

102.40 \times 12.5. Use round-number splitting: 102.40 \times 10 =
1024; 102.40 \times 2 = 204.80; 102.40 \times 0.5 = 51.20.



Full solution

102.40 \times 12.5 = 1024 + 204.80 + 51.20 = 1280. Rs. 1,280. Decimals turn “12 and a half litres at 102 rupees and 40 paise” into a single tidy multiplication.



Practice — Stretch
S1. A computer stores numbers using a fixed number of bits — say, 32 bits. The decimal 0.1 cannot be represented exactly in binary. Why might that be? (Hint: the binary place values to the right of the dot are \tfrac{1}{2}, \tfrac{1}{4}, \tfrac{1}{8}, \tfrac{1}{16}, \ldots — all powers of 2.)


Hint 1 — a nudge

Recall Discover P3: a fraction terminates in base 10 iff its denominator’s prime factors are 2 and 5. What is the analogous condition for base 2?



Full solution

In base 2, only fractions whose denominators are powers of 2 terminate. 0.1 = \tfrac{1}{10} = \tfrac{1}{2 \times 5} — the prime factor 5 makes it non-terminating in binary. So 0.1 in binary is a repeating binary fraction, like \tfrac{1}{3} in our decimal system.
Computers store this as a truncated approximation. That tiny rounding error is the source of famous bugs like 0.1 + 0.2 \neq 0.3 in nearly every programming language. The error is small (about 10^{-17}), but it accumulates in long calculations and matters for financial software, scientific simulation, and ML training.
This is one reason decimals (base 10) and binary (base 2) coexist in computing — currency code uses fixed-point decimal arithmetic to avoid the rounding issue.

S2. What number does 0.\overline{9} (zero point nine recurring, forever) equal?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Try this: let x = 0.\overline{9}. Compute 10x. Then compute 10x - x.



Full solution

Let x = 0.9999\ldots. Then 10x = 9.9999\ldots. Subtract: 10x - x = 9.9999\ldots - 0.9999\ldots = 9 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 9x = 9 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = 1.
0.\overline{9} = 1 exactly. Same number, two costumes.
This is a famously uncomfortable result for many learners — but the algebra is irrefutable. Acceptance of 0.\overline{9} = 1 is acceptance that the rationals (Lesson 7) extend continuously into the real numbers — a story we tell properly in Tier 5.



Common confusions

“More decimal digits means a bigger number.” 0.5 > 0.45, even though 0.5 has fewer digits. Compare column by column from the left, not by length.
“Multiplying decimals always makes them smaller.” Only when both are less than 1. 1.5 \times 1.5 = 2.25, which is bigger than either factor.
“0.\overline{9} is just almost 1.” No — it equals 1 exactly, as Stretch S2 proved.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A decimal is just place value extended right of a decimal point — each step right divides by ten, mirroring each step left multiplying by ten.
Every fraction has a decimal twin: it terminates if the reduced denominator’s only primes are 2 and 5; otherwise it repeats forever.
Computers store decimals in binary, so 0.1 becomes a tiny rounding error — the source of countless real-world software bugs.



Self-Assessment

Could you explain why 0.05 < 0.5 < 0.55 to a friend, using “column” and “place value”?
Without scrolling back, can you compute 1.4 + 0.85?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?


3 yes: ready for Lesson 9 — Ratio and Proportion.
2 yes: redo S1 (binary 0.1) — that’s the deepest connection to systems work.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P5.

title: 08 · Decimals
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

Lesson 7 — Fractions — decimals are a special form of fraction (denominators are powers of 10).
Lesson 3 — Place Value — the dot just extends the columns rightward.



What this unlocks

Ratio and Proportion — percentages are decimals in a different costume (per hundred).
Tier 2 — Scientific Notation — when the same number is huge or tiny, decimal place value combined with powers of 10 gives a compact form.



Where this matters

Systems. Stretch S1 is the most important takeaway: every floating-point number on every computer is a binary approximation. The rounding error inherent in this is the source of countless real-world bugs — and is why financial systems use decimal arithmetic libraries even when their programming language defaults to binary.


AI / Machine Learning. Almost every weight, activation, gradient, and learning rate in a neural network is a floating-point decimal. The precision of those decimals (32-bit, 16-bit, 8-bit) is now a first-class design parameter for training large models.


Mathematical research. 0.\overline{9} = 1 is the doorway to limits and the structure of the real numbers — central topics of Tier 5 real analysis.


Everywhere else. Money, measurement, time (clocks have moved from “hours and minutes” toward decimal hours), GPS coordinates, scientific data — decimals are the world’s lingua franca for non-whole quantities.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones honoured: Pestalozzi (the number-line widget makes decimals literally movable), Boole (the trailing-zero question surfaces equivalence intuitively), Russian school (the long-division result for 1/3 surprises and gets named, not skipped over), Bhāskara (Name + verse), 3Blue1Brown (the 0.\overline{9}=1 proof is a classic visual-algebraic moment).
Self-study guarantees all present.

title: 10 · Negative Numbers
text:
Have you done Lesson 2 — The Number Line and Lesson 4 — Addition and Subtraction?
When you saw 5 - 12 in Lesson 4 and got -7, did that feel reasonable, or strange?
Have you ever seen a thermometer dip below zero, or a bank account go into overdraft?

title: 10 · Negative Numbers
10 · Negative Numbers
text:
Wonder

Why is 3 − 5 = −2 but 5 − 3 = 2? What changes when you reverse the order?
What does − (−3) mean? Why might subtracting a negative have the opposite effect of subtracting a positive?
What is (−1) × (−1)? (Most learners are taught this is +1 but never shown why.)

title: 10 · Negative Numbers
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Compute, by walking the number line: 4 - 7, \quad -3 - 5, \quad -8 + 11.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Same rule as before: - hops left, + hops right. The starting point or the result can be negative.



Full solution

4 - 7 = -3 (start at 4, hop left 7). -3 - 5 = -8 (start at −3, hop left 5). -8 + 11 = 3 (start at −8, hop right 11 — past zero into the positives).
The number line did all the work. Negative numbers are not a special case — they are just positions to the left of zero.



Problem 2
A diver is 5 metres above the surface (call this +5). She dives down 8 metres. Where is she relative to the surface?


Hint 1 — a nudge

“Down” means subtract. 5 - 8 = ?



Full solution

5 - 8 = -3. 3 metres below the surface. The negative sign records the direction: below, debt, deficit, before zero — context picks the meaning.



Problem 3
A bank account is overdrawn by Rs. 200. Write the balance as a signed number. After a deposit of Rs. 350, what is the new balance?


Full solution

Old balance: -200. After deposit: -200 + 350 = +150. The account is now in the black, with Rs. 150.
The negative number was useful because it carried the sign. Banks quote your balance directly with a “−” or “(OD)” marker. The arithmetic is exactly the same as for positive numbers.



Problem 4
Compute 5 − (−3). Then compute 5 + 3. What do you notice?


Hint 1 — a nudge

“Subtract” means hop left. “Subtract a negative” reverses that.



Full solution

5 - (-3) = 8. And 5 + 3 = 8. They are equal.
The rule: subtracting a negative is the same as adding the positive. a - (-b) = a + b.
A picture: subtracting “-3” means “remove 3 of the negative stuff”, which is the same as “add 3 of the positive stuff”. Or, on the line: “hop left by -3” reverses the hop direction (left becomes right) and moves you 3 to the right.
This is the rule that lets us reduce all subtraction to addition: just add the opposite of the second number. Computers compute subtraction this way internally.



Problem 5
What is (−1) × (−1)? You should get +1.


Hint 1 — a nudge

This one needs a consistency argument, not a picture argument. Think about what (-1) \times 0 has to be, and split 0 = 1 + (-1).



Full solution

We know (-1) \times 0 = 0 (multiplying by zero gives zero, Lesson 5). And 0 = 1 + (-1). So 0 = (-1) \times 0 = (-1) \times (1 + (-1)) = (-1) \times 1 + (-1) \times (-1). Now (-1) \times 1 = -1 (multiplying by 1 leaves things unchanged in sign, Lesson 5). So: 0 = -1 + (-1) \times (-1) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad (-1) \times (-1) = +1.
This is forced — not an arbitrary rule. To keep the distributive law working, (-1) \times (-1) must equal +1. The general rule:

Positive × positive = positive.
Positive × negative = negative.
Negative × negative = positive.

This is one of those facts that everyone “knows” but few are shown why.


The first systematic rules for arithmetic with negative numbers were written by the Indian mathematician Brahmagupta in his Brāhmasphuṭasiddhānta (628 CE). He called positives “fortunes” and negatives “debts,” and stated all the sign rules — including debt × debt = fortune — more than a millennium before European mathematicians were comfortable with them. As late as the 18th century, Western mathematicians like d’Alembert still doubted whether negative numbers were “real.”


A negative number is a direction, not a deficiency. Once you accept the line runs both ways from zero, every operation you already knew works exactly the same — you just allow the answer to land on the other side.

title: 10 · Negative Numbers
text:
Name

A negative number is a number to the left of zero on the number line: \ldots, -4, -3, -2, -1, \ 0, \ 1, 2, 3, \ldots The whole numbers (positive, negative, and zero) are the integers, written \mathbb{Z}.
For any number a, the negation -a is its mirror across zero. The sum a + (-a) = 0 — they cancel.
Subtraction is addition of the opposite: a - b = a + (-b).
Sign rules for multiplication: \text{(+)} \times \text{(+)} = \text{(+)}, \quad \text{(+)} \times \text{(−)} = \text{(−)}, \quad \text{(−)} \times \text{(−)} = \text{(+)}.


Mirror across zero — that is negation; Subtract a negative — that is addition of its opposite; Two negatives multiplied — they cancel back to positive.

title: 10 · Negative Numbers
text:
Apply

Worked example

Compute -7 + 4 - (-3) - 8.

A competent solver rewrites every subtraction as addition of the opposite, then groups:

-7 + 4 - (-3) - 8 = -7 + 4 + 3 + (-8).
Group the positives and negatives:

Positives: 4 + 3 = 7.
Negatives: -7 + (-8) = -15.

Combine: 7 + (-15) = -8.

Answer: −8. Once subtractions are converted to additions of opposites, order doesn’t matter — addition is commutative for all numbers, including negatives.


Practice — Warm
W1. Compute: -3 + 8, \ 6 - 9, \ -4 - 5.


Full solution

5, \ -3, \ -9.

W2. Compute: 7 - (-2), \ -5 + (-3), \ -10 - (-4).


Full solution

9, \ -8, \ -6. Adding a negative is the same as subtracting; subtracting a negative is the same as adding.

W3. Compute: (-3) \times 4, \ (-2) \times (-7), \ (-1) \times
(-1) \times (-1).


Full solution

-12, \ 14, \ -1.
For the third: an odd number of negatives gives negative; an even number of negatives gives positive. Pair them up and the rest decides the sign.



Practice — Working
P1. A submarine is at depth −45 m (45 metres below the surface). It rises 18 m. What is its new depth?


Full solution

-45 + 18 = -27. 27 metres below the surface.

P2. The temperature on Monday morning is −4 °C. By afternoon it has risen by 9 °C. By evening it has fallen by 6 °C. What is the evening temperature?


Full solution

Morning: -4. Afternoon: -4 + 9 = 5. Evening: 5 - 6 = -1. −1 °C.
Three hops on the line — start cold, warm up, cool down a little.

P3. Compute: \dfrac{(-12) \times 5}{-3}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Numerator: negative × positive = negative. Then negative ÷ negative.



Full solution

(-12) \times 5 = -60. Then -60 \div -3 = +20. 20.
The same sign rules apply to division: same signs → positive, opposite signs → negative. (Division is just multiplication by a reciprocal — Lesson 7.)



Practice — Stretch
S1. A computer stores integers using a fixed number of bits. With 8 bits, it can store 2^8 = 256 distinct values. Signed 8-bit integers typically run from −128 to +127. What happens when you compute 127 + 1?


Hint 1 — a nudge

The value tries to become 128 — but 128 isn’t representable. What value can the computer store, and what is its sign?



Full solution

The result wraps around to −128. The bit pattern just below 128 (in “two’s complement” representation, the standard scheme) is the same as the bit pattern for −128, so the value flips sign.
This is signed integer overflow — distinct from the unsigned overflow we met in Lesson 4 Stretch S2. The infamous Civilization “Gandhi nuclear aggression” bug arose from this exact mechanism: Gandhi’s aggression rating, set to a small positive value and decreased by Indian democracy, underflowed to a very large positive value (or in some accounts, wrapped from −1 to a huge unsigned number).
Sign-aware integer arithmetic on real hardware is a leaky abstraction. Understanding negatives as wrapping behaviour is the entry point to careful systems programming.

S2. ML models often use a signed loss that can be positive or negative — but typically losses are non-negative (squared error, cross-entropy). When can a loss become negative? Why might that be a warning sign?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Some losses (rewards in RL, or negative log-likelihoods of probabilities > 1) can go negative. What does a negative reward signal?



Full solution

In reinforcement learning, “loss” and “reward” are often signed: a positive reward signals a good action, a negative reward signals a bad one. Negative rewards are a normal part of training — the model learns to avoid states that produce them.
In supervised learning, however, loss should be non-negative. A negative cross-entropy loss usually signals a bug — typically a probability greater than 1 (impossible) or a sign error in the loss formula. Practising with negatives makes spotting these errors second nature.



Common confusions

“Two negatives make a negative.” No — for multiplication and division, two negatives cancel to a positive (-3 \times -2 = 6). For addition and subtraction, two negatives stay negative (-3 + -2
= -5). Operation matters.
“Negative numbers are smaller than positives — but I can’t tell which negative is bigger.” -2 is bigger than -7 (further right on the line). The minus sign flips your intuition; the line restores it.
“-x is always negative.” False. -x is the opposite of x. If x = -5, then -x = 5 (positive). The minus sign means “negation”, not “negative”.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A negative number is a position to the left of zero — a direction, not a missing thing.
Subtraction is just addition of the opposite, so every problem reduces to adding signed numbers.
For multiplication, two like signs give positive and two unlike signs give negative — forced by the rules of arithmetic, not by convention.



Self-Assessment

Could you derive (-1) \times (-1) = +1 from the distributive law and (-1) \times 0 = 0, without notes?
Without scrolling back, can you compute -3 + 7 - (-4) - 9?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?


3 yes: ready for Lesson 11 — Variables.
2 yes: redo S1 (signed overflow) — that’s a topic many programmers don’t really understand.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P4 and P5.

title: 10 · Negative Numbers
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

Lesson 2 — The Number Line — negatives are positions to the left of zero. The line is the picture.
Lesson 4 — Addition & Subtraction — the operations themselves are the same; we just allow the operands to go below zero.



What this unlocks

Variables — once we have negative numbers, equations like x + 5 = 2 have solutions (x = -3), where before they did not.
Tier 2 — Linear equations — every line on a graph requires negatives to describe direction.



Where this matters

Systems. Signed integer overflow (S1) is one of the most notorious sources of real-world bugs. The Patriot missile failure of 1991 (a tracking timer overflow), the Ariane 5 rocket explosion (an unsigned-to-signed cast that overflowed), and many security vulnerabilities trace back to misunderstood signed arithmetic.


Mathematical research. Extending the natural numbers to include negatives gives the integers \mathbb{Z} — the second great number-system extension. The integers are the prototypical example of a ring in abstract algebra (Tier 6).


AI / Machine Learning. Gradients, signed errors, RL rewards, contrastive losses — every training signal carries a sign. The direction of weight update is the sign of the gradient.


Everywhere else. Temperature, elevation (above/below sea level), banking (credit/debit), accounting (assets/liabilities), time (BCE/CE), velocity (forward/backward) — wherever there is a “reference zero”, negatives express the other side.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones honoured: Pestalozzi (the operation widget makes subtracting a negative visible as a sign-flip), Boole (the (−1) × (−1) question is exactly the question Boole would have asked — “why?”), Russian school (the consistency argument in P5 is not hand-waved away), Bhāskara (single-box Name + verse — and Brahmagupta in 628 CE was the first to write down the rules of negative arithmetic systematically, including the rule for negative × negative).
Self-study guarantees all present.

title: 05 · Complex Functions and Cauchy’s Theorem
text:
Have you done 04 · Complex Numbers and T5 L05 — Partial Derivatives?
Comfortable with z = a + bi, polar form, and Euler?
Recall path integrals from vector calculus.

title: 05 · Complex Functions and Cauchy’s Theorem
05 · Complex Functions and Cauchy’s Theorem
text:
Wonder

Why is complex differentiability so much more restrictive than real?
Cauchy’s theorem says contour integrals of analytic functions around closed loops are zero. Why is this profound?
The residue theorem computes integrals via singular points. How?

title: 05 · Complex Functions and Cauchy’s Theorem
text:
Discover

Problem 1
A function f: \mathbb{C} \to \mathbb{C} is differentiable at z_0 if \lim_{h \to 0} (f(z_0 + h) - f(z_0))/h exists as h \to
0 in \mathbb{C} (any direction).
This is much stronger than real differentiability — the limit must agree from all 2D directions.
For f(z) = z^2, derive f'(z) by the limit.


Full solution

\dfrac{(z + h)^2 - z^2}{h} = \dfrac{2 z h + h^2}{h} = 2z + h \to 2z.
Same as real case: f'(z) = 2z. Polynomial functions of z are analytic everywhere with the obvious derivatives.



Problem 2
The Cauchy-Riemann equations. Write f(z) = u(x, y) + i v(x, y) where z = x + iy. Then f is analytic iff \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y}, \quad \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} = -\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x}.
Verify for f(z) = z^2: u = x^2 - y^2, v = 2xy.


Full solution

u_x = 2x, v_y = 2x ✓. u_y = -2y, v_x = 2y, so u_y = -v_x ✓.
Cauchy-Riemann captures the direction-independence of complex differentiability.



Problem 3
Show f(z) = \bar{z} (conjugate) is not analytic.


Hint 1 — a nudge

f(z) = x - iy. u = x, v = -y. Cauchy-Riemann?



Full solution

u_x = 1, v_y = -1. u_x \neq v_y. Cauchy-Riemann fails. \bar{z} is not analytic anywhere.
(Even though \bar{z} is smooth in (x, y). Complex differentiability is a strict subset of real-vector differentiability.)



Problem 4
Cauchy’s theorem. If f is analytic in a simply connected region, then \oint_C f(z) \, dz = 0 for any closed contour C in the region.
Why is this remarkable?


Full solution

For real functions, \int f(x) dx over a “loop” is just zero trivially. But for complex line integrals, the path matters in general — and Cauchy’s theorem says it doesn’t for analytic functions. This is much stronger.
Consequences include path-independence, existence of antiderivatives, the Cauchy integral formula (f(a) =
\dfrac{1}{2\pi i} \oint \dfrac{f(z)}{z - a} dz), and the proof that analytic functions are infinitely differentiable.
This is the miracle of complex analysis.



Problem 5
The residue theorem computes contour integrals via the singularities (places where f blows up) inside the contour. Compute \oint_C \dfrac{1}{z} dz where C is the unit circle traced counter-clockwise.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Parametrise: z = e^{i\theta}, dz = i e^{i\theta} d\theta. Then \dfrac{1}{z} dz = \dfrac{i e^{i\theta}}{e^{i\theta}} d\theta = i \,
d\theta. Integrate \theta from 0 to 2\pi.



Full solution

\int_0^{2\pi} i \, d\theta = 2\pi i.
So \oint \dfrac{1}{z} dz = 2 \pi i around the unit circle.
The residue of 1/z at 0 is 1. The residue theorem says: \oint_C f(z) \, dz = 2 \pi i \sum \text{residues inside } C.
This lets you compute many real integrals (via cleverly chosen contours) — the superpower of complex analysis.


Augustin-Louis Cauchy delivered the integral theorem you just met to the Académie des Sciences in 1825, in a memoir titled Mémoire sur les intégrales définies prises entre des limites imaginaires. Three decades later, Bernhard Riemann’s 1851 doctoral thesis recast the same circle of ideas in geometric language and introduced what we now call Riemann surfaces — multi-sheeted complex domains that tame multi-valued functions like \sqrt{z} and \log z. Together, Cauchy’s analytic backbone and Riemann’s geometric vision made complex analysis the most unreasonably powerful tool in 19th-century mathematics.


Demanding that f'(z) exist and not depend on the direction h \to 0 in the plane is a far stricter condition than ordinary real differentiability — and from that single extra demand cascades a miracle: analytic functions are automatically infinitely differentiable, equal to their Taylor series, and have closed contour integrals that vanish. The Cauchy–Riemann equations are the price of admission; everything else is the prize.

title: 05 · Complex Functions and Cauchy’s Theorem
text:
Name

A complex function f: D \subseteq \mathbb{C} \to \mathbb{C}.
Analytic at z_0: f'(z_0) exists as a complex limit (independent of direction of approach).
Cauchy-Riemann equations: f = u + iv analytic iff u_x = v_y and u_y = -v_x.
Cauchy’s theorem: \oint_C f(z) \, dz = 0 for f analytic in a simply-connected region containing C.
Cauchy integral formula: for analytic f inside / on contour C enclosing a: f(a) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi i} \oint_C \dfrac{f(z)}{z - a} \, dz.
Residue theorem: for f with isolated singularities a_k inside C: \oint_C f(z) \, dz = 2 \pi i \sum_k \text{Res}(f; a_k).
Magic facts:

Analytic ⇒ infinitely differentiable.
Analytic functions equal their Taylor series in their convergence disc.
Liouville: bounded entire functions are constant.
Maximum principle: |f| attains max only on the boundary (interior maxima impossible for non-constant analytic f).



Complex differentiability requires direction-independence (Cauchy-Riemann); Cauchy’s theorem: contour integrals of analytic functions vanish; Residues compute many otherwise-difficult integrals.

title: 05 · Complex Functions and Cauchy’s Theorem
text:
Apply

Worked example

Show \sin z is analytic. Find \sin'(z).

\sin z = \sin(x + iy) = \sin x \cosh y + i \cos x \sinh y.
u = \sin x \cosh y, v = \cos x \sinh y.
u_x = \cos x \cosh y, v_y = \cos x \cosh y ✓. u_y = \sin x \sinh y, v_x = -\sin x \sinh y, so u_y = -v_x ✓.
Analytic everywhere. \sin'(z) = \cos z (same as real).


Practice — Warm
W1. Is f(z) = 3z + 5 analytic?


Full solution

Yes (linear in z).

W2. Is f(z) = \text{Re}(z) = x analytic?


Full solution

u = x, v = 0. u_x = 1, v_y = 0. Cauchy-Riemann fails. No.

W3. \oint_C 1 \, dz for closed C?


Full solution

0 (constant is analytic, Cauchy’s theorem).



Practice — Working
P1. Compute \oint_C \dfrac{1}{z - 2} dz where C is the unit circle.


Full solution

z = 2 is outside C. By Cauchy’s theorem (no singularities inside), integral is 0.

P2. Compute \oint_C \dfrac{2z}{z^2 + 1} dz where C is the circle |z| = 2.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Singularities: z^2 = -1 \Rightarrow z = \pm i, both inside. Sum of residues × 2\pi i.



Full solution

Residues at \pm i are each 1 (compute via \lim_{z \to i} (z - i)
\cdot 2z/((z-i)(z+i))). Sum 2. Integral = 2 \cdot 2\pi i = 4\pi i.

P3. \sin z analytic? \cos z?


Full solution

Both yes. They satisfy Cauchy-Riemann.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Compute \int_{-\infty}^\infty \dfrac{1}{1 + x^2} dx using contour integration.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Use a semi-circular contour in the upper half plane. Singularity: z = i (residue 1/(2i)).



Full solution

On the semi-circle: \int_{-R}^{R} 1/(1+x^2) dx + \int_{\text{arc}}
\to 0 as R \to \infty. Total integral = 2\pi i \cdot \text{Res} =
2\pi i \cdot 1/(2i) = \pi.
\int_{-\infty}^\infty 1/(1+x^2) dx = \pi.
(Direct: \arctan x from -\infty to \infty is \pi ✓.)
Contour integration computes integrals that are difficult or impossible by elementary methods. This is one of the most beautiful techniques in mathematics.

S2. Why is the Riemann zeta function \zeta(s) = \sum 1/n^s so important? It has analytic continuation to \mathbb{C} \setminus
\{1\}. The Riemann hypothesis claims its non-trivial zeros lie on \text{Re}(s) = 1/2.


Full solution

The zeta function encodes information about the prime numbers (Euler product: \zeta(s) = \prod_p (1 - p^{-s})^{-1}). Its zeros control the fluctuations of the prime-counting function.
The Riemann Hypothesis (1859) — still unsolved, $1M Clay Millennium Prize — would imply the strongest possible bounds on prime distribution.
Complex analysis is the natural setting for analytic number theory: real-valued problems about primes resolved via complex- function methods. Tier 7 (research track) develops this.



Common confusions

“Analytic = differentiable as a real function.” Stronger: complex differentiability + Cauchy-Riemann.
“Cauchy’s theorem applies to any closed contour.” Needs f analytic in the region. Singularities ruin it.
“Residues are mysterious.” Just the coefficient of 1/(z - a) in the Laurent series at a. Computable via formulas.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

An analytic function on \mathbb{C} is one whose derivative exists independently of direction — captured exactly by the Cauchy–Riemann equations.
Cauchy’s theorem: for analytic f, \oint_C f(z)\, dz = 0 around any closed loop in a simply-connected region — and that one identity unlocks path-independence, the integral formula, and the residue calculus.
The residue theorem reduces contour integrals to a sum over singularities — and lets you evaluate real integrals like \int_{-\infty}^\infty 1/(1+x^2)\,dx that brute-force methods can’t reach.



Self-Assessment

Could you state the Cauchy-Riemann equations?
Without scrolling back, what’s \oint 1/z \, dz around the unit circle?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: 2\pi i.)

3 yes: ready for 06 · Second-Order ODEs.
2 yes: redo S1 (contour integration of a real integral).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P4 and P5.

title: 05 · Complex Functions and Cauchy’s Theorem
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

04 · Complex Numbers
T5 L05 — Partial Derivatives — Cauchy-Riemann uses partial derivatives.



What this unlocks

Tier 7 — Algebraic Geometry, Modular Forms — extensions of complex analysis.



Where this matters

Mathematical research. The Riemann hypothesis (S2). Whole fields (analytic number theory, conformal field theory) live in complex-analytic territory.


Engineering. Conformal mapping for fluid dynamics and electrostatics. Z-transform in signal processing (discrete-time Fourier).

title: 09 · Convex Optimisation
text:
Have you done T5 L05 — Partial Derivatives and T5 L04 — Eigenvalues?
Comfortable with gradients and the Hessian matrix (T4 L06 S1)?
Recall positive-definite matrices (eigenvalues > 0)?

title: 09 · Convex Optimisation
09 · Convex Optimisation
text:
Wonder

Why is “convex” so important for optimisation?
Quadratic functions f(x) = (1/2) x^T A x + b^T x + c — when are they convex?
Are sums of convex functions convex?

title: 09 · Convex Optimisation
text:
Discover

Problem 1
A function f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R} is convex iff f''(x) \ge 0 everywhere (for twice-differentiable f).
Verify f(x) = x^4 is convex.


Full solution

f''(x) = 12 x^2 \ge 0 ✓. Convex.
(Strictly convex except at x = 0.)



Problem 2
For multivariate f, convex iff Hessian \nabla^2 f is positive semi-definite (eigenvalues \ge 0).
Determine: is f(x, y) = x^2 + 2 x y + 3 y^2 convex?


Hint 1 — a nudge

H = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 6 \end{pmatrix}. Eigenvalues?



Full solution

\det(H - \lambda I) = (2 - \lambda)(6 - \lambda) - 4 = \lambda^2 - 8
\lambda + 8 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = 4 \pm 2 \sqrt{2} \approx 6.83,
1.17. Both positive ⇒ Hessian PD ⇒ strictly convex.



Problem 3
Why convexity matters: every local minimum of a convex function is a global minimum.
Sketch: if x^* is a local min and y has f(y) < f(x^*), then on the segment from x^* to y, by convexity, f stays \le f(y) <
f(x^*) near x^* — contradicting local min.
Why is this practically huge?


Full solution

No “saddle” or “local minimum” traps. Gradient descent (and many other algorithms) on convex functions reach the global optimum reliably. For non-convex problems (most real ML), you might get stuck.
This is why much of machine learning theory focuses on convex problems first: results there are clean, then extended (with caveats) to non-convex.



Problem 4
Operations preserving convexity:

Sum of convex functions is convex.
\max\{f_1, f_2\} of convex functions is convex.
Composition g \circ f: convex if g is convex non-decreasing and f is convex (e.g.).

Show: f(x) = \max(0, x) (ReLU) is convex.


Full solution

ReLU is the max of two convex functions: 0 (constant — convex) and x (linear — convex). Max of convex is convex.
ReLU is convex but not strictly convex (flat for x < 0). Still, it’s the standard activation in deep networks.



Problem 5
Convex optimisation problems have well-developed theory:

Linear Programming (LP): linear objective, linear constraints. Polynomial-time algorithms.
Quadratic Programming (QP): quadratic objective, linear constraints. Solved efficiently.
Convex programming (general): convex objective, convex constraints. Polynomial-time interior-point methods.

Why is “non-convex” a hard line in computer science?


Full solution

Non-convex problems may have many local minima, requiring exponential time in the worst case. Many NP-hard problems are non-convex.
But many practically important problems (LP, semidefinite, certain ML losses for shallow models) are convex. Modern ML is mostly non- convex — yet works well in practice via stochastic gradient methods and architectural tricks.
Understanding why non-convex deep learning works is one of the biggest open theoretical questions in AI.


Linear programming was born in wartime. Soviet mathematician Leonid Kantorovich invented LP in 1939 while consulting for the Leningrad plywood trust on how to allocate machines and raw timber optimally — convex optimisation, decades before the West had a name for it. His pamphlet circulated quietly through Soviet planning offices for years. In 1947 the American George Dantzig, working on US Air Force logistics, independently invented the simplex method for the same problem. Then in 1984, Narendra Karmarkar (at Bell Labs) shocked the field with the first polynomial-time interior-point algorithm, beating simplex’s exponential worst case and triggering the modern convex-optimisation renaissance. Kantorovich shared the 1975 Nobel Prize in Economics for his work.


A convex landscape is one where every “downhill” step is a step toward the global bottom. There are no false valleys, no traps, no surprises. This is the entire reason convex optimisation is clean: walking downhill always works, because there is only one “down.”

title: 09 · Convex Optimisation
text:
Name

A set C \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n is convex if for x, y \in C and t \in [0, 1]: (1 - t) x + t y \in C.
A function f is convex on C if for x, y \in C, t \in [0, 1]: f((1-t) x + t y) \le (1-t) f(x) + t f(y).
For twice-differentiable f:

1D: convex iff f'' \ge 0.
\mathbb{R}^n: convex iff Hessian \nabla^2 f is positive semi-definite.

Strict convexity: strict inequality for t \in (0, 1), x \neq y.
Properties:

Local min = global min for convex f.
Sum, max, non-negative-scaling of convex are convex.
Composition with monotone functions sometimes preserves convexity.

Convex optimisation problem: \min_{x \in C} f(x), \quad C \text{ convex}, f \text{ convex}.
Common classes: linear (LP), quadratic (QP), semidefinite (SDP), general convex (interior-point methods).
Duality: primal problem ↔︎ dual problem; under regularity, equal optima.


Convex = lies below chords; convex set = closed under midpoints; Convex optimisation: local min = global min; tractable; Non-convex: hard, but ML works in practice via SGD.

title: 09 · Convex Optimisation
text:
Apply

Worked example

Show f(x) = e^x + x^2 is convex.

f''(x) = e^x + 2 > 0 for all x. Strictly convex.
(Sum of convex functions e^x and x^2 is convex.)


Practice — Warm
W1. Is f(x) = -x^2 convex?


Full solution

f''(x) = -2 < 0. No (concave).

W2. Convex set: \{x : x \ge 0\}?


Full solution

Yes (half-line).

W3. Convex set: union of two intervals?


Full solution

Generally no (midpoint between intervals isn’t included).



Practice — Working
P1. Is the unit ball \{x : \|x\| \le 1\} convex?


Full solution

Yes. By triangle inequality: \|t x + (1-t) y\| \le t \|x\| + (1-t)
\|y\| \le 1.

P2. Convex Hessian for f(x, y) = 5 x^2 + 4 x y + 3 y^2?


Full solution

H = \begin{pmatrix} 10 & 4 \\ 4 & 6 \end{pmatrix}. Det = 60 - 16 = 44 > 0; trace 16 > 0 ⇒ both eigenvalues positive ⇒ convex.

P3. Is the function f(x) = -\log x (for x > 0) convex?


Full solution

f''(x) = 1/x^2 > 0. Convex. (Used in interior-point methods as a “barrier function” enforcing x > 0.)



Practice — Stretch
S1. Why is least squares regression convex? L(\beta) = \|y -
X\beta\|^2.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Quadratic in \beta: \beta^T X^T X \beta - 2 y^T X \beta + y^T y.



Full solution

L(\beta) = \beta^T (X^T X) \beta - 2 (X^T y)^T \beta + y^T y.
Hessian: 2 X^T X, which is positive semi-definite (Gram matrix). So L is convex.
Hence linear regression has a unique (or affine subspace of) minima — solvable in closed form (normal equations T5 L02 S2). This is why classical regression is so well-behaved.

S2. In modern ML, neural network losses are NOT convex. Why does training still work?


Full solution

Several theoretical reasons (active research):

Over-parameterisation: enough capacity → loss landscape becomes “benign” (saddle points easy to escape, minima are wide and equivalent).
Stochastic gradient noise: SGD’s noise helps escape saddles.
Implicit bias: gradient descent finds particular nice minima (e.g., low-norm in linear models).
Architecture: skip connections (ResNets) and normalisation (BatchNorm) shape the loss landscape favourably.

Despite non-convexity, modern deep nets reach (essentially) global minima reliably. Why is one of the biggest open theoretical questions in modern ML.



Common confusions

“Convex set and convex function are the same.” Different things. A function is convex; its epigraph (region above its graph) is a convex set.
“Convex = bowl-shaped only.” Higher dimensions: convex shapes include simplices, polyhedra, balls, ellipsoids — many forms.
“All ML is non-convex.” Linear regression, logistic regression, SVM, many generative models are convex. Deep nets are not.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A convex function lies below every chord — bowl-shaped, no internal bumps; every local minimum is automatically the global minimum.
The Hessian PSD test (eigenvalues \ge 0, via the eigenvalues of \nabla^2 f) is the workhorse check for convexity in higher dimensions.
Convex problems are tractable; non-convex problems are not — yet modern deep learning works empirically because over- parameterisation, SGD noise, and architecture flatten the landscape.



Self-Assessment

Could you state when a 1D function is convex?
Without scrolling back, is ReLU convex?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: yes.)

3 yes: ready for 10 · Gradient Methods.
2 yes: redo S1 (least squares).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P5.

title: 09 · Convex Optimisation
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

T5 L05 — Partial Derivatives
T5 L04 — Eigenvalues — for Hessian PD test.



What this unlocks

10 · Gradient Methods



Where this matters

AI / Machine Learning. Classical convex methods (linear/logistic regression, SVM); modern (non-convex) deep learning that works “empirically convex” via tricks (S2).


Operations research. LP, QP, network flow, scheduling, portfolio optimisation — all rest on convex optimisation theory.


Mathematical research. Convex analysis is a polished subject (Rockafellar’s Convex Analysis). Deep ties to functional analysis and game theory.

title: 01 · Groups — Symmetry and Structure
text:
Have you done T5 L11 — Sets, Logic, Proof?
Comfortable with set notation and basic proof techniques?
Recall modular arithmetic (\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}) and rotation matrices?

title: 01 · Groups — Symmetry and Structure
01 · Groups — Symmetry and Structure
text:
Wonder

Why do four axioms (closure, associativity, identity, inverses) capture so much?
Some groups are abelian (a \cdot b = b \cdot a); some aren’t. Why does this matter?
The integers \mathbb{Z} under multiplication: is this a group? (Hint: inverse of 3 is 1/3 — not an integer.)

title: 01 · Groups — Symmetry and Structure
text:
Discover

Problem 1
A group (G, \cdot) satisfies:
(G1) Closure: a \cdot b \in G for all a, b \in G. (G2) Associativity: (a \cdot b) \cdot c = a \cdot (b \cdot c). (G3) Identity: there exists e \in G with e \cdot a = a \cdot e = a. (G4) Inverses: for each a, there exists a^{-1} with a \cdot
a^{-1} = a^{-1} \cdot a = e.
A group is abelian if also a \cdot b = b \cdot a.
Verify (\mathbb{Z}, +) is a group. Identity? Inverse of 5?


Full solution

Closure ✓. Associative ✓. Identity 0 (0 + a = a). Inverse of a: -a (since a + (-a) = 0). Inverse of 5: -5.
(\mathbb{Z}, +) is an abelian group of infinite order.



Problem 2
Show (\mathbb{Z}, \cdot) (integers under multiplication) is not a group.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Identity is 1. Find the inverse of 5.



Full solution

5 \cdot ? = 1 \Rightarrow ? = 1/5 \notin \mathbb{Z}. No inverse in \mathbb{Z}. So G4 fails.
(\mathbb{Q} \setminus \{0\}, \cdot) is a group: every non-zero rational has an inverse in \mathbb{Q}.



Problem 3
The symmetric group S_3: all permutations of \{1, 2, 3\} under composition. There are 3! = 6 elements. Show S_3 is non-abelian.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Two permutations: \sigma = (1 2) (swap 1, 2) and \tau = (2 3) (swap 2, 3). Compute \sigma \tau vs \tau \sigma.



Full solution

\sigma \tau: first swap 2, 3, then swap 1, 2. 1 \to 1 \to 2, 2
\to 3 \to 3, 3 \to 2 \to 1. Net: 1 \to 2, 2 \to 3, 3 \to 1 — a 3-cycle.
\tau \sigma: first swap 1, 2, then swap 2, 3. 1 \to 2 \to 3, 2
\to 1 \to 1, 3 \to 3 \to 2. Net: 1 \to 3, 2 \to 1, 3 \to 2 — the other 3-cycle.
Different! So S_3 is non-abelian.
The symmetric groups S_n are central: every finite group embeds in some S_n (Cayley’s theorem).



Problem 4
The order of G is |G| (number of elements). The order of an element a is the smallest positive n with a^n = e.
For \mathbb{Z}/6 under +, what’s the order of element 2?


Hint 1 — a nudge

2 + 2 = 4, 4 + 2 = 0 \pmod 6. So 2 has order 3.



Full solution

Order 3.
Lagrange’s theorem: in a finite group, the order of every element divides |G|. Here 3 | 6 ✓.
Lagrange constrains the possible structures of finite groups.



Problem 5
A subgroup H \le G is a subset that is itself a group under the restricted operation.
Find all subgroups of \mathbb{Z}/6.


Full solution

\{0\}, \{0, 3\} (order 2), \{0, 2, 4\} (order 3), and the whole group (order 6).
These are all divisors of 6 (1, 2, 3, 6). For cyclic groups, the subgroups correspond exactly to divisors of the order — a complete classification.


Évariste Galois died in a duel in Paris on 31 May 1832, at age twenty. The night before, he stayed up scribbling letters to his friend Auguste Chevalier, sketching the group-theoretic ideas that would explain which polynomial equations are solvable by radicals — the foundations of what we now call Galois theory. The manuscripts sat unread for fourteen years before Joseph Liouville finally published them in 1846. Earlier permutation work by Lagrange (1770) and Cauchy (1815) had laid the ground; Galois saw what it was for.


A group is the algebra of reversible moves. The four axioms are the bare minimum required for “do this, then undo it” to make sense — and from that single demand, the entire landscape of symmetry, from clock arithmetic to Rubik’s cube to elliptic-curve cryptography, falls out.

title: 01 · Groups — Symmetry and Structure
text:
Name

A group (G, \cdot) is a set with operation satisfying: closure, associativity, identity e, inverses a^{-1}.
Abelian: a \cdot b = b \cdot a for all a, b.
Order of G: |G|. Order of element a: smallest n > 0 with a^n = e (or \infty).
Subgroup H \le G: subset that is a group on its own.
Lagrange’s theorem: in a finite group, |H| divides |G|. So order of every element divides |G|.
Examples:

(\mathbb{Z}, +) — infinite abelian.
(\mathbb{Z}/n, +) — cyclic of order n.
((\mathbb{Z}/p)^*, \cdot) for prime p — cyclic of order p - 1 (used in cryptography).
S_n — permutations of n items, order n!, non-abelian for n \ge
3.
D_n — dihedral group, symmetries of a regular n-gon, order 2n.
GL(n, \mathbb{R}) — invertible matrices, non-abelian.

A homomorphism \phi: G \to H preserves the operation: \phi(a
\cdot b) = \phi(a) \cdot \phi(b). An isomorphism is an invertible homomorphism.


Group = closure + associativity + identity + inverses; Abelian or not — that’s the central distinction; Lagrange constrains finite groups; subgroups divide the order.

title: 01 · Groups — Symmetry and Structure
text:
Apply

Worked example

Show \mathbb{Z}/4 and \mathbb{Z}/2 \times \mathbb{Z}/2 are both groups of order 4 but are not isomorphic.

In \mathbb{Z}/4: element 1 has order 4 (cyclic). In \mathbb{Z}/2
\times \mathbb{Z}/2: every non-identity element has order 2.
So no isomorphism preserving order can exist.
There are exactly two groups of order 4 (up to isomorphism): cyclic \mathbb{Z}/4 and Klein four-group V = \mathbb{Z}/2 \times
\mathbb{Z}/2.


Practice — Warm
W1. Identity element of (\mathbb{Q}, \cdot)? (Note: this fails G4 at 0.)


Full solution

1. (Need to remove 0 to get a group: (\mathbb{Q} \setminus \{0\},
\cdot).)

W2. Is (\mathbb{N}, +) a group?


Full solution

No — no inverses (no negatives).

W3. Order of S_4?


Full solution

4! = 24.



Practice — Working
P1. Is (\mathbb{R}, \cdot) a group?


Full solution

No — 0 has no inverse. (\mathbb{R} \setminus \{0\}, \cdot) is.

P2. Find the inverse of 3 in \mathbb{Z}/7 (mult. group of units).


Full solution

3 \cdot 5 = 15 \equiv 1 \pmod 7. So 3^{-1} = 5.

P3. Subgroups of \mathbb{Z}/12?


Full solution

Cyclic, so subgroups correspond to divisors of 12: orders 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12. Six subgroups.



Practice — Stretch
S1. The group of symmetries of a square (D_4) has order 8. List its elements (rotations and reflections).


Full solution

4 rotations: r_0 (identity), r_{90}, r_{180}, r_{270}. 4 reflections: across horizontal, vertical, two diagonals.
Total 8. Non-abelian (rotation then reflection ≠ reflection then rotation).
This is the dihedral group D_4. Crystals, molecular symmetries, and Rubik’s cube all involve dihedral or symmetric groups. Group theory captures all possible symmetries.

S2. Groups in cryptography. RSA uses (\mathbb{Z}/n)^*. Diffie- Hellman uses any cyclic group with hard discrete logarithm. Why does group structure matter for security?


Full solution

Cryptographic schemes need: (a) operations that are easy one way but hard to reverse, (b) algebraic structure that supports encoding and verification.
Cyclic group structure ensures that exponentiation g^x is well- defined and efficient. The discrete log problem (“given g, g^x, find x”) is hard in well-chosen groups (e.g., (\mathbb{Z}/p)^* for large prime p, or elliptic curve groups).
Quantum computers break some of these (Shor’s algorithm), motivating post-quantum cryptography that uses different group structures (lattices).
Group theory is the backbone of modern cryptography.



Common confusions

“All groups are abelian.” Most are not. S_n for n \ge 3, D_n, GL(n, \mathbb{R}) are non-abelian.
“Subgroups have any order.” No — Lagrange forces |H| \mid
|G|.
“Group = set with operation.” Need all four axioms. (\mathbb{N}, +) has 3 of 4 (no inverses) — not a group.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A group is a set with one operation plus four axioms — closure, associativity, identity, inverses — and that minimal package captures every notion of symmetry.
Most groups are non-commutative: in S_n (n \ge 3), in D_n, and in invertible matrices, a \cdot b \ne b \cdot a, and that single fact drives most of the subject’s depth.
Lagrange’s theorem constrains what subgroups can exist — the size of any subgroup must divide the size of the whole group.



Self-Assessment

Could you state the four group axioms?
Without scrolling back, what’s the order of S_5?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: 5! = 120.)

3 yes: ready for 02 · Rings & Fields.
2 yes: redo S2 (groups in cryptography).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P5.

title: 01 · Groups — Symmetry and Structure

Connect

What this unlocks

03 · Group Actions — groups acting on sets.
Tier 7 — Galois Theory, Lie Groups — deeper abstract algebra.



Where this matters

Encryption. RSA, Diffie-Hellman, ECDSA — every classical cryptographic scheme uses group theory.


Physics & chemistry. Symmetry groups classify molecular structures, crystal lattices, and elementary particles. The Standard Model is a gauge theory of certain Lie groups.


Mathematical research. Groups are the algebraic side of symmetry. Galois theory (Tier 7) uses groups to determine which polynomial equations are solvable by radicals.

title: 10 · Gradient Methods and SGD Variants
text:
Have you done 09 · Convex Optimisation?
Comfortable with the gradient \nabla f as direction of steepest ascent?
Recall T5 L09 stretch on stochastic gradient descent.

title: 10 · Gradient Methods and SGD Variants
10 · Gradient Methods and SGD Variants
text:
Wonder

Why use stochastic gradients (one sample) instead of full-batch?
Why does momentum help convergence?
Why is the learning rate \eta such a critical hyperparameter?

title: 10 · Gradient Methods and SGD Variants
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Gradient descent on f(w) = (1/2) w^2. Update: w \leftarrow w -
\eta \cdot w. Starting at w_0 = 1, \eta = 0.1, run 5 steps.


Full solution

w_{n+1} = (1 - \eta) w_n = 0.9 w_n.
w_1 = 0.9, w_2 = 0.81, w_3 = 0.729, w_4 = 0.6561, w_5 = 0.59.
Geometric convergence to 0 (the minimum). Rate 1 - \eta = 0.9.



Problem 2
For f(w) = (1/2) w^2, what’s the optimal step size \eta? What happens if \eta > 2?


Hint 1 — a nudge

w_{n+1} = (1 - \eta) w_n. Converges iff |1 - \eta| < 1, i.e., 0 < \eta < 2.



Full solution

Optimal: \eta = 1 (single-step convergence: w_1 = 0). \eta > 2: divergence (|1 - \eta| > 1, w_n explodes).
For general convex quadratic with Hessian H, optimal \eta = 2/(\lambda_{\min}(H) + \lambda_{\max}(H)).
Step size matters enormously. Too small: slow. Too large: divergence.



Problem 3
Stochastic gradient descent (SGD). For L(w) = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum
\ell_i(w), SGD picks one (or a mini-batch of) \ell_i per step: w \leftarrow w - \eta \nabla \ell_i(w).
Why does this work?


Full solution

E[\nabla \ell_i] = \nabla L — the stochastic gradient is an unbiased estimator of the true gradient.
Each step is noisy but on average correct. Over many steps, the trajectory follows the true gradient (with some noise).
Benefits:

Cheap: one example per step instead of all N.
Noise can help escape saddle points and shallow local minima.

Modern training: SGD with mini-batches of 32-1024 samples per step.



Problem 4
Momentum. Update: v \leftarrow \beta v - \eta \nabla L; \; w
\leftarrow w + v. With \beta \approx 0.9, accumulates past gradients (exponentially decaying weight).
Why does this help in ill-conditioned problems (Hessian eigenvalues of very different magnitudes)?


Full solution

In ill-conditioned valleys (long, narrow), plain GD oscillates across the narrow direction while progressing slowly along the long direction. Momentum smooths the oscillations and accelerates the slow direction.
Equivalent to a heavy ball rolling downhill: built-up momentum carries it through ridges and along long valleys.
Nesterov’s accelerated gradient (1983) achieves the optimal O(1/T^2) convergence rate for convex problems — much better than GD’s O(1/T).



Problem 5
Adam. Maintains running averages of:

m = \beta_1 m + (1-\beta_1) g (mean of gradient, “first moment”).
v = \beta_2 v + (1-\beta_2) g^2 (mean of squared gradient, “second moment”).

Update: w \leftarrow w - \eta \cdot m / (\sqrt{v} + \epsilon).
Why divide by \sqrt{v}?


Full solution

Adapts per-parameter learning rates. Parameters with consistently large gradients get a smaller effective rate (divided by \sqrt{v}); those with tiny gradients get a larger one. Self-tuning.
Adam combines momentum (via m) with adaptive scaling (via v). Default in nearly every modern deep learning framework. Hyperparameter defaults (\beta_1 = 0.9, \beta_2 = 0.999) work well across tasks.
Variants: AdamW (decoupled weight decay), LAMB (large-batch training), Lion (sign-based, more memory-efficient).


Cauchy proposed gradient descent in 1847 — in a two-page note to the Comptes Rendus of the French Academy, while trying to solve systems of nonlinear equations in celestial mechanics. He called it the méthode générale pour la résolution des systèmes d’équations simultanées. It then sat almost unused for a century. Robbins and Monro (1951) gave it a stochastic twist for online estimation; Rumelhart, Hinton and Williams (1986) wired it into neural networks via backpropagation; and Adam was only published in 2014 (Kingma and Ba). The optimiser that trains every deep network you have ever heard of is one Cauchy idea, two twists, and 167 years of hardware.


Every modern optimiser is the same loop — look at the slope, take a step downhill — with different definitions of “look at” and “step.” Vanilla GD looks at one gradient; momentum looks at a running history; Adam looks at the running mean and the running variance and uses them to re-scale each coordinate’s step. The math is small; the practical difference is everything.

title: 10 · Gradient Methods and SGD Variants
text:
Name

Gradient descent (GD): w \leftarrow w - \eta \nabla L(w).
Stochastic gradient descent (SGD): uses single example or mini-batch per step. Unbiased estimator of true gradient.
Momentum: v \leftarrow \beta v - \eta \nabla L; w \leftarrow w +
v. Smooths and accelerates.
Nesterov accelerated gradient (NAG): “look ahead” before gradient — O(1/T^2) for convex.
Adam: adaptive learning rate per parameter via running averages of g and g^2. Default for deep learning.
Convergence rates for convex L:



Method
Rate (convex)
Rate (strongly convex)




GD
O(1/T)
O((1 - \kappa^{-1})^T) exponential


Nesterov
O(1/T^2)
O((1 - \kappa^{-1/2})^T)


SGD
O(1/\sqrt{T})
O(1/T)



(Where \kappa = condition number.)
Practical tips:

Choose \eta via grid search or schedule (cosine decay).
Use Adam unless you have reason otherwise.
Mini-batch size: 32-512 typical.
Mix gradient clipping for stability.



GD: full-batch, deterministic; SGD: stochastic, scalable; Momentum: smooths and accelerates; Adam: per-parameter adaptive learning rate — default for deep learning.

title: 10 · Gradient Methods and SGD Variants
text:
Apply

Worked example

Manually run 3 steps of Adam on f(w) = w^2 from w_0 = 4, with \eta = 0.5, \beta_1 = 0.9, \beta_2 = 0.999, \epsilon = 10^{-8}.

\nabla = 2w. Step 1: g = 8. m = 0.8, v = 0.064. \hat{m} = m/(1-0.9^1) = 8 (bias correction). \hat{v} = v/(1-0.999) =
64. w_1 = 4 - 0.5 \cdot 8/\sqrt{64} = 4 - 0.5 = 3.5.
(Adam with bias correction gives a step similar in magnitude to GD on the first step.)
Subsequent steps progress similarly. The full algorithm is more nuanced; libraries handle it.


Practice — Warm
W1. GD update for f(w) = w^2 at w = 3, \eta = 0.1?


Full solution

w_{\text{new}} = 3 - 0.1 \cdot 6 = 2.4.

W2. What does \eta stand for in optimisation?


Full solution

Learning rate / step size.

W3. What does \beta = 0.9 mean in momentum?


Full solution

The velocity retains 90% of its previous value each step.



Practice — Working
P1. GD on f(w_1, w_2) = w_1^2 + 100 w_2^2 from (1, 1) with \eta = 0.01. Compute one step.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\nabla = (2 w_1, 200 w_2) = (2, 200). Step: (1 - 0.02, 1 - 2) = (0.98,
-1).



Full solution

(0.98, -1). The w_2 direction overshoots dramatically due to ill-conditioning (\kappa = 100). \eta = 0.01 is too large for w_2.
This is why adaptive methods (Adam) help: they would scale down the w_2 step.

P2. Why might SGD with batch size 1 be unstable?


Full solution

Single-sample gradients are very noisy. A pathological example can knock the weights far from optimum. Mini-batches (e.g., 32-256) average noise out, retaining stochastic benefits while reducing variance.

P3. Run 2 steps of momentum on f(w) = w^2 from w_0 = 4, \eta =
0.1, \beta = 0.9.


Full solution

Step 1: v_0 = 0. g = 8. v_1 = 0.9 \cdot 0 - 0.1 \cdot 8 = -0.8. w_1 = 4 - 0.8 = 3.2.
Step 2: g = 6.4. v_2 = 0.9 \cdot -0.8 - 0.1 \cdot 6.4 = -1.36. w_2 = 3.2 - 1.36 = 1.84.
Faster than plain GD (which would give w_2 = 4 \cdot 0.8^2 =
2.56). Momentum builds.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Why is Adam so popular but theoretically not always convergent? Adam has been shown (Reddi et al. 2018) to fail to converge on some convex problems. Why is it still used?


Full solution

Adam works empirically on most deep learning problems, even if not provably convergent on all convex ones. The non-convergence issue requires unusual gradient distributions; for typical neural network losses, Adam converges fine.
Variants like AMSGrad (uses the max of past v) restore theoretical convergence. AdamW (decoupled weight decay) is the practical default for transformer training.
Theoretical study of optimisers continues; in practice, what works for your model is the right answer.

S2. Learning rate schedules. Modern training rarely uses a constant \eta. Cosine decay, warmup, cyclic — why?


Full solution

Different stages of training need different rates:

Warmup: small \eta at start to avoid early instability.
Plateau then decay: high \eta for fast progress, then low for fine-tuning.
Cosine decay: smooth schedule from peak to near-zero, often with restarts.
Cyclic: periodic up/down — escapes shallow minima.

In ML, schedule design is a substantial fraction of the hyperparameter tuning effort. Modern recipes include warmup + cosine for transformers, often with a “1cycle” policy.



Common confusions

“Gradient descent always converges.” Only with appropriate step size. Too large → divergence. Too small → too slow.
“SGD = randomness.” SGD is deterministic given the data sample — randomness comes from sampling.
“Adam fixes everything.” Adam is a great default but can be suboptimal for some tasks (large-batch training favours LAMB, image generation sometimes prefers SGD).



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

Gradient descent is the rule “step against the gradient” — the simplest possible way to walk downhill on a loss landscape.
The learning rate \eta is the single most consequential hyperparameter — too small wastes compute, too large diverges; modern training schedules it on purpose.
SGD adds noise (cheap, escapes saddles); momentum adds memory (smooths and accelerates); Adam adds per-parameter scaling — each variant fixes a specific failure mode of the one before it.



Self-Assessment

Could you write the GD update rule?
Without scrolling back, what does momentum do?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: smooths past gradients into a velocity-like quantity.)

3 yes: ready for 11 · Information Theory.
2 yes: redo S2 (LR schedules).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P5.
