The rigorous definition: \lim_{n \to \infty} a_n = L means: for every \epsilon > 0, there exists N \in \mathbb{N} such that |a_n - L| < \epsilon for all n \ge N.
Verify \lim 1/n = 0.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Given \epsilon > 0. Need 1/n < \epsilon for n \ge N. So pick N > 1/\epsilon (any integer works).



Full solution

Given \epsilon. Choose N > 1/\epsilon (e.g., N = \lceil
1/\epsilon \rceil + 1). For n \ge N: 1/n \le 1/N < \epsilon ✓.
This is the archetypal \epsilon-N proof — the foundation of real analysis.



Problem 2
A series \sum a_n converges if its partial sums S_n = \sum_{k
\le n} a_k converge. We saw geometric series converge for |r| < 1 (T3 L04).
n-th term test: if \sum a_n converges, then a_n \to 0. Contrapositive: if a_n \not\to 0, the series diverges.
Apply: does \sum (1 + 1/n) converge?


Full solution

a_n = 1 + 1/n \to 1 \neq 0. Diverges.
(Note: a_n \to 0 is necessary but not sufficient — harmonic series has a_n = 1/n \to 0 yet diverges.)



Problem 3
Comparison test: if 0 \le a_n \le b_n and \sum b_n converges, then \sum a_n converges. Conversely, if \sum a_n diverges, so does \sum b_n.
Use to determine: does \sum 1/(n^2 + 1) converge?


Hint 1 — a nudge

1/(n^2 + 1) \le 1/n^2. And \sum 1/n^2 converges.



Full solution

\sum 1/n^2 = \pi^2/6 (a famous result by Euler). By comparison, \sum 1/(n^2 + 1) also converges (smaller positive terms).
The p-series \sum 1/n^p converges iff p > 1. (Comparison with \int 1/x^p dx.)



Problem 4
Ratio test: for \sum a_n with a_n > 0, if \lim a_{n+1}/a_n =
L:

L < 1: converges.
L > 1: diverges.
L = 1: inconclusive.

Apply to \sum 1/n!.


Full solution

a_{n+1}/a_n = n!/(n+1)! = 1/(n+1) \to 0 < 1. Converges.
(The sum \sum_{n=0}^\infty 1/n! = e, the natural exponential at 1.)



Problem 5
The Taylor series of e^x is \sum_{n=0}^\infty x^n / n!. Show this converges for all real x.


Full solution

Apply ratio test: |a_{n+1}/a_n| = |x|/(n+1) \to 0 for any fixed x. So converges for all x.
The radius of convergence of this power series is \infty. (For some power series like \sum x^n, radius is finite — here, 1.)
Power series let you define functions (\sin, \cos, e^x) as infinite polynomials — a major theme in analysis.


For a century after Newton and Leibniz, mathematicians manipulated infinite series with reckless confidence — Euler famously summed 1 - 1 + 1 - 1 + \cdots to \tfrac12. Augustin-Louis Cauchy’s Cours d’analyse (1821) introduced the modern notion of limit of partial sums as the meaning of a convergent series, and laid down the comparison and ratio tests in something close to today’s form. Karl Weierstrass then built the full \epsilon–N machinery in mid-century lectures at Berlin (never published in his lifetime; spread through students’ notebooks). The lesson of that century: a series is not just a formula — whether it sums to anything at all is a question you have to settle first, and the answer can be delicate.


A series is just the limit of its partial sums. The terms going to zero is necessary for convergence but not sufficient — the harmonic series is the canonical reminder. The convergence tests (ratio, root, comparison, integral, p-series) are not magic; they are different ways of comparing your series to one whose behaviour you already know.

title: 14 · Sequences, Series, and Convergence
text:
Name

Sequence convergence: a_n \to L iff for every \epsilon > 0, there exists N such that n \ge N \Rightarrow |a_n - L| < \epsilon.
Series convergence: \sum a_n converges iff partial sums converge.
Tests for series convergence:







Test
Statement




n-th term
If \sum a_n converges, a_n \to 0. (Necessary, not sufficient.)


Geometric
\sum r^n converges iff |r| < 1.


p-series
\sum 1/n^p converges iff p > 1.


Comparison
0 \le a_n \le b_n, \sum b_n converges ⇒ \sum a_n converges.


Ratio
If \lim |a_{n+1}/a_n| = L: < 1 converge, > 1 diverge.


Root
If \lim |a_n|^{1/n} = L: < 1 converge, > 1 diverge.


Integral
f positive decreasing, \sum f(n) and \int f converge together.



Absolute convergence: \sum |a_n| converges. Implies convergence of \sum a_n (and rearrangements behave nicely).
Conditional convergence: \sum a_n converges but \sum |a_n| diverges (e.g., \sum (-1)^n / n). Rearrangements can change the sum (Riemann series theorem).
Power series: \sum a_n x^n. Converges in some interval (radius of convergence R, possibly 0 or \infty).


ε-N: limits made rigorous; Tests for convergence: ratio, comparison, integral, p-series; Power series define functions analytically.

title: 14 · Sequences, Series, and Convergence
text:
Apply

Worked example

Does \sum n / 2^n converge?

Ratio: a_{n+1}/a_n = ((n+1)/2^{n+1}) / (n/2^n) = (n+1)/(2n) \to 1/2
< 1. Converges.


Practice — Warm
W1. Does \sum 1/n converge?


Full solution

No — harmonic series, diverges.

W2. Does \sum 1/n^2 converge?


Full solution

Yes (p-series with p = 2 > 1).

W3. Does \sum (-1)^n converge?


Full solution

a_n \not\to 0. Diverges.



Practice — Working
P1. Does \sum n^2 / 2^n converge?


Full solution

Ratio: ((n+1)^2/2^{n+1}) / (n^2/2^n) = (n+1)^2/(2 n^2) \to 1/2 < 1. Converges.

P2. Radius of convergence of \sum n! \cdot x^n.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Ratio: |(n+1)! x^{n+1}/(n! x^n)| = (n+1) |x| \to \infty for any x
\neq 0.



Full solution

Radius 0. Converges only at x = 0.

P3. Does \sum 1/n^{1.001} converge?


Full solution

p = 1.001 > 1. Yes, p-series.
(Just barely above 1; sums very slowly.)



Practice — Stretch
S1. Why does the harmonic series diverge but \sum 1/n^{1.01} converge? They look almost the same — what’s the difference?


Full solution

The terms decay just barely fast enough. The integral test: \sum 1/n^p \sim \int_1^\infty x^{-p} dx = 1/(p - 1) if p > 1, which is finite. For p = 1: \int 1/x dx = \ln x \to \infty.
The transition at p = 1 is sharp. \sum 1/(n \ln n) also diverges (just barely); \sum 1/(n (\ln n)^2) converges.
Convergence/divergence is a quantitative question with fascinating boundary cases.

S2. Why are power series (Taylor series) so important in science? Most useful functions are analytic — equal to their Taylor series in some region. Why is this practical?


Full solution

Polynomials are easy to compute. A truncated Taylor series gives a polynomial approximation of f(x) — easy to evaluate, differentiate, integrate. Calculators and computers compute \sin x, e^x, \ln x this way internally.
Properties transfer. Solutions of differential equations are often analytic; you can find them as power series (Frobenius method).
Theory. Analytic functions have remarkable rigidity properties (if they agree on a small interval, they agree everywhere). Foundation of complex analysis (Tier 6).
The infinite series you’re learning to test for convergence are the representation of every smooth function in mathematics.



Common confusions

“a_n \to 0 ⇒ \sum a_n converges.” Necessary, not sufficient. Harmonic counterexample.
“Power series have radius 1 always.” Different series have different radii: 0, finite, or \infty.
“Conditionally convergent = convergent enough.” Only unconditionally convergent series can be rearranged freely (Riemann’s rearrangement theorem).



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A sequence converges when, for every tolerance you demand, its terms eventually stay inside that tolerance — the \epsilon–N definition is just that promise written in symbols.
A series is the limit of its partial sums. The terms going to zero is necessary for convergence but not sufficient — the harmonic series demonstrates this in one line.
Convergence tests compare to a series you already understand (\sum r^n, \sum 1/n^p, \sum 1/n!); the ratio and root tests are how you spot the comparison automatically.



Self-Assessment

Could you state the \epsilon-N definition of a_n \to L?
Without scrolling back, does \sum 1/n^3 converge?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: yes, p = 3 > 1.)

3 yes: ready for 15 · Real Analysis Foundation.
2 yes: redo S1 (harmonic boundary).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P4.

title: 14 · Sequences, Series, and Convergence
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

T4 L01 — Limits
T3 L04 — Sequences & Series



What this unlocks

15 · Real Analysis Foundation — the rigorous foundation.
Tier 6 — Complex Analysis & Functional Analysis — power series in \mathbb{C}; Fourier series; Hilbert spaces.



Where this matters

AI / Machine Learning. Convergence guarantees for optimisation algorithms (e.g., SGD with proper learning-rate schedule). Power series approximations of activation functions for theoretical analysis.


Mathematical research. Series and convergence tests are foundational to all of analysis. Series solutions to differential equations, asymptotic series, generating functions in combinatorics.

title: 06 · Multiple Integrals
text:
Have you done T4 L08 — Definite Integral and 05 · Partial Derivatives?
Comfortable with \int_a^b f(x) \, dx and the FTC?
Recall area, volume formulas?

title: 06 · Multiple Integrals
06 · Multiple Integrals
text:
Wonder

Why is \iint_R 1 \, dA = area of R? \iiint_V 1 \, dV = volume?
Order of integration: does \int \int f \, dx \, dy = \int \int f \,
dy \, dx?
How would you integrate over a circle, not a rectangle?

title: 06 · Multiple Integrals
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Compute \int_0^1 \int_0^1 xy \, dy \, dx (inner: y from 0 to 1, treating x as a constant).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Inner: \int_0^1 xy \, dy = x [y^2/2]_0^1 = x/2. Outer: \int_0^1
x/2 \, dx = 1/4.



Full solution

Result: 1/4.
Iterated: \int \int = \int [\int \, dy] dx. Inner first treats outer variable as constant; outer integrates the resulting 1D function.



Problem 2
Compute \int_0^2 \int_0^3 (x + y) \, dx \, dy.


Full solution

Inner: \int_0^3 (x + y) \, dx = [x^2/2 + xy]_0^3 = 9/2 + 3y.
Outer: \int_0^2 (9/2 + 3y) \, dy = 9 + 6 = 15.
Order can usually be swapped (Fubini’s theorem): $(x + y) , dy , dx = $ same.



Problem 3
Compute the area of the unit disk x^2 + y^2 \le 1 using a double integral.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\iint_D 1 \, dA. Use polar coordinates: x = r \cos \theta, y = r
\sin \theta, dA = r \, dr \, d\theta.



Full solution

\iint_D 1 \, dA = \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^1 r \, dr \, d\theta =
\int_0^{2\pi} (1/2) \, d\theta = \pi.
Confirms the circle area formula.
In polar, r goes from 0 to 1 and \theta from 0 to 2\pi. The extra r factor in dA = r \, dr \, d\theta comes from the Jacobian of the change of variables.



Problem 4
A triple integral \iiint_V f \, dV over a 3D region. Compute \iiint_V 1 \, dV where V is the box [0, 2] \times [0, 3] \times
[0, 4].


Full solution

\int_0^4 \int_0^3 \int_0^2 1 \, dx \, dy \, dz = 2 \cdot 3 \cdot 4 =
24. (The volume of the box.)
For a constant integrand, \iiint 1 \, dV = volume of V. For varying f, you get a weighted volume — useful for mass (density), charge (charge density), expected values (probability density).



Problem 5
The expected value of a 2D random variable with joint density f(x, y) is E[X] = \iint x f(x, y) \, dA. Compute E[X] for f(x,
y) = 4 x y on [0, 1]^2.


Hint 1 — a nudge

E[X] = \int_0^1 \int_0^1 x \cdot 4 x y \, dy \, dx.



Full solution

\int_0^1 \int_0^1 4 x^2 y \, dy \, dx = \int_0^1 4 x^2 \cdot 1/2 \, dx
= \int_0^1 2 x^2 \, dx = 2/3.
E[X] = 2/3. Expectations of multivariate distributions are multiple integrals — foundational in probability.


The fact that you can compute a double integral by doing two single integrals — and in either order — feels obvious, but it is not. Guido Fubini proved the rigorous version in 1907, building on Lebesgue’s new theory of integration: for any function whose absolute value has a finite integral over the rectangle, the iterated integrals exist and agree. Without Fubini’s theorem, the move from \iint to \int \int is a leap of faith. With it, every multivariate calculation in physics, statistics, and ML rests on solid ground.


A multiple integral is just adding up little pieces of a region — and you compute it by sweeping one direction at a time. The choice of coordinates (rectangular, polar, spherical) is the choice of how to slice the region; the Jacobian factor is the bookkeeping that keeps each slice the right size. Pick coordinates that match your region’s symmetry, and intractable integrals become one-liners.

title: 06 · Multiple Integrals
text:
Name

Double integral over region R: \iint_R f(x, y) \, dA = \int \int f(x, y) \, dy \, dx (iterated; order swappable for nice f, by Fubini’s theorem).
Triple integral over V: \iiint_V f \, dV, three iterated integrals.
Geometric meaning: $ , dA = $ area of R. $ , dV = $ volume of V. With weighting f: total of “f per unit area / volume” — mass, probability, charge.
Polar coordinates in 2D: x = r \cos \theta, y = r \sin \theta, dA = r \, dr \, d\theta.
Cylindrical (3D): like polar plus z. Spherical: (\rho,
\theta, \phi), dV = \rho^2 \sin \phi \, d\rho \, d\theta \, d\phi.
Change of variables: \iint f \, dA = \iint f \cdot |J| \, du \, dv where J is the Jacobian of the coordinate change.


Iterated integration: inner first, outer last; Polar / cylindrical / spherical for symmetric regions; Multiple integrals compute mass, probability, expected values.

title: 06 · Multiple Integrals
text:
Apply

Worked example

Compute \iint_R xy \, dA where R is the triangle with vertices (0, 0), (1, 0), (1, 1).

Triangle: 0 \le x \le 1, 0 \le y \le x.
\int_0^1 \int_0^x xy \, dy \, dx = \int_0^1 x [y^2/2]_0^x \, dx =
\int_0^1 x^3 / 2 \, dx = 1/8.


Practice — Warm
W1. \int_0^1 \int_0^2 1 \, dx \, dy?


Full solution

2.

W2. \int_0^1 \int_0^1 x \, dy \, dx?


Full solution

\int_0^1 x \cdot 1 \, dx = 1/2.

W3. Polar area element?


Full solution

dA = r \, dr \, d\theta.



Practice — Working
P1. \int_0^2 \int_0^3 xy \, dy \, dx.


Full solution

Inner: 9x/2. Outer: 9.

P2. Volume of the upper half of the unit ball (x^2 + y^2 + z^2
\le 1, z \ge 0).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Spherical: \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi/2} \int_0^1 \rho^2 \sin \phi
\, d\rho \, d\phi \, d\theta.



Full solution

2\pi \cdot 1 \cdot 1/3 = 2\pi/3.
(Half of the full ball volume 4\pi/3.)

P3. \iint_R 1 \, dA over the rectangle [0, 5] \times [0, 3].


Full solution

15 (area).



Practice — Stretch
S1. The Gaussian integral. \int_{-\infty}^\infty e^{-x^2} \,
dx = \sqrt{\pi}. The trick: square it as a 2D integral, switch to polar.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\left(\int e^{-x^2} dx\right)^2 = \iint e^{-(x^2 + y^2)} \, dA. In polar: \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^\infty e^{-r^2} r \, dr \, d\theta.



Full solution

Polar inner: \int_0^\infty e^{-r^2} r \, dr. Substitute u = r^2: \int_0^\infty e^{-u}/2 \, du = 1/2.
Outer: \int_0^{2\pi} 1/2 \, d\theta = \pi.
So \left(\int e^{-x^2} dx\right)^2 = \pi \Rightarrow \int e^{-x^2} dx
= \sqrt{\pi} ✓.
This trick — squaring the integral as a 2D integral and using polar — is one of the most beautiful results in calculus, due to Poisson. Foundational to probability (normal distribution).

S2. Bayesian inference. A posterior probability p(\theta |
D) \propto p(D | \theta) p(\theta) — the normalising constant is a high-dimensional integral \int p(D | \theta) p(\theta) \, d\theta. Why are these often intractable?


Full solution

For high-dimensional \theta (modern ML models have millions of parameters), the integral has no closed form and grid-based numerical integration scales as N^d — exponentially expensive in dimension. Hence ML uses:

Monte Carlo (sample-based estimation; T4 L08 S2).
Variational inference (replace p(\theta | D) with tractable approximation).
Markov Chain Monte Carlo (generate samples to approximate the integral).

The whole subfield of probabilistic ML exists to deal with intractable multivariate integrals.



Common confusions

“Order of integration always matters.” For nice (continuous, absolutely integrable) functions on rectangles, Fubini says it doesn’t. For pathological cases or non-rectangular regions, be careful with limits.
“Polar always uses r.” r is the radius. The Jacobian factor r in dA = r \, dr \, d\theta is essential — easy to forget.
“Multiple integrals are just lots of 1D integrals.” Mostly yes for nice regions, but coordinate changes (polar, spherical) and complex regions complicate things.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A multiple integral is the higher-dimensional definite integral — it adds up f over an area or volume, slice by slice.
Iterate inner-first, outer-last, and Fubini’s theorem says the order can usually be swapped for nice integrands.
Change coordinates to match the region’s symmetry — polar for disks, spherical for balls — and don’t forget the Jacobian factor (the r in r \, dr \, d\theta).



Self-Assessment

Could you compute \int_0^1 \int_0^1 (x + y) \, dx \, dy?
Without scrolling back, what’s dA in polar coordinates?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answers: 1; r \, dr \, d\theta.)

3 yes: ready for 07 · Vector Fields.
2 yes: redo S1 (Gaussian integral).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P5.

title: 06 · Multiple Integrals
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

T4 L08 — Definite Integral
05 · Partial Derivatives — related machinery.



What this unlocks

07 · Vector Fields — line and surface integrals.
Tier 7 — Measure Theory — generalises multiple integrals.



Where this matters

AI / Machine Learning. Bayesian inference (S2) requires multivariate integration. Probabilistic generative models, Bayesian neural networks, and many statistical methods rest on these.


Physics & engineering. Mass, charge, energy, fluid flow are all multiple integrals over regions.


Statistics. The Gaussian integral (S1) gives the normalisation of the normal distribution; multivariate normal extends this with matrix inversions.

Comfortable with exponents (T2 L07)?
crumbs: 13 · Modular Arithmetic and the RSA Algorithm

title: 13 · Modular Arithmetic and the RSA Algorithm
crumbs: 13 · Modular Arithmetic and the RSA Algorithm

title: 13 · Modular Arithmetic and the RSA Algorithm
text:
Wonder

Clock arithmetic: 9 + 5 = 2 (mod 12). Why “2”?
Why is multiplication of large primes the basis of RSA?
Multiplying primes is easy; factoring back is hard. Why?
crumbs: 13 · Modular Arithmetic and the RSA Algorithm

title: 13 · Modular Arithmetic and the RSA Algorithm
text:
Discover

Problem 1
a \equiv b \pmod n iff n | (a - b). Compute 17 \pmod 5, -3
\pmod 7, 50 \pmod 9.


Full solution

17 \equiv 2 \pmod 5 (17 = 3 \cdot 5 + 2). -3 \equiv 4 \pmod 7 (-3 + 7 = 4). 50 \equiv 5 \pmod 9 (50 = 5 \cdot 9 + 5).
The “canonical” representative is in \{0, 1, \ldots, n-1\}.



Problem 2
Modular arithmetic respects +, -, \cdot. Compute (7 \cdot 8 + 5)
\pmod{11}.


Full solution

Reduce as you go: 7 \cdot 8 = 56 \equiv 1 \pmod{11} (since 56 = 5
\cdot 11 + 1). Then 1 + 5 = 6. Answer: 6.
This “reduce as you go” trick keeps numbers small.



Problem 3
Fermat’s Little Theorem. For prime p and a with \gcd(a, p) =
1: a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod p.
Verify for p = 7, a = 3.


Full solution

3^6 = 729 = 104 \cdot 7 + 1 \equiv 1 \pmod 7 ✓.
This gives a fast primality test (Fermat test) and underpins RSA.



Problem 4
The Euler totient \varphi(n) counts integers in \{1, \ldots,
n\} coprime to n. For prime p: \varphi(p) = p - 1. For n = pq (two distinct primes): \varphi(pq) = (p-1)(q-1).
Compute \varphi(15), \varphi(35).


Full solution

\varphi(15) = \varphi(3) \cdot \varphi(5) = 2 \cdot 4 = 8. \varphi(35) = \varphi(5) \cdot \varphi(7) = 4 \cdot 6 = 24.
Euler’s theorem generalises Fermat: a^{\varphi(n)} \equiv 1
\pmod n for \gcd(a, n) = 1.



Problem 5
RSA setup.

Pick two large primes p, q.
Compute n = pq and \varphi(n) = (p-1)(q-1).
Pick e coprime to \varphi(n) (often e = 65537).
Compute d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\varphi(n)} (extended Euclidean algorithm).
Public key: (n, e). Private key: d.

Encrypt message m: c = m^e \pmod n. Decrypt: m = c^d \pmod n.
For toy RSA p = 5, q = 11: n = 55, \varphi = 40. Pick e = 3 (coprime to 40). d = 27 (since 3 \cdot 27 = 81 \equiv 1 \pmod{40}).
Encrypt m = 4: c = 4^3 \pmod{55} = 64 \pmod{55} = 9. Decrypt: m = 9^{27} \pmod{55}. Verify this gives back 4.


Hint 1 — a nudge

9^{27} \pmod{55}: compute by squaring. 9^2 = 81 \equiv 26. 9^4
\equiv 26^2 = 676 \equiv 16. 9^8 \equiv 16^2 = 256 \equiv 36. 9^{16} \equiv 36^2 = 1296 \equiv 31. Then 9^{27} = 9^{16} \cdot
9^8 \cdot 9^2 \cdot 9 \equiv 31 \cdot 36 \cdot 26 \cdot 9. Reduce each step.



Full solution

31 \cdot 36 = 1116 \equiv 16 \pmod{55}. 16 \cdot 26 = 416 \equiv
31. 31 \cdot 9 = 279 \equiv 4 \pmod{55} ✓.
Decrypted: 4 — original message recovered. RSA works because of Euler’s theorem: m^{ed} = m^{1 + k\varphi(n)} \equiv m \pmod n (when \gcd(m, n) = 1).
Security: (n, e) is public. To decrypt, the attacker needs d, which requires \varphi(n) = (p-1)(q-1), which requires factoring n — intractable for n \approx 10^{600}.


For most of history “secret message” meant “shared secret key”: sender and receiver had to meet first. In 1976 Whitfield Diffie and Martin Hellman published New Directions in Cryptography, proposing the wild idea of public-key exchange — agreeing on a secret over an open channel, by exploiting the asymmetry between modular exponentiation (easy) and the discrete logarithm (hard). One year later, Rivest, Shamir, and Adleman at MIT published RSA, the first practical public-key encryption scheme — three 2,000-year-old facts (Euclid’s algorithm, Fermat’s little theorem, the difficulty of factoring) glued together with one breathtaking idea. The same year, Clifford Cocks at GCHQ revealed that he had discovered RSA four years earlier in 1973 — kept classified by British intelligence. The internet runs on a discovery that was secret for the first quarter of its life.


Modular arithmetic looks like a curiosity (“clock arithmetic, mod 12”) and turns out to be the structural bedrock of the internet. The single asymmetry — multiplying two large primes is easy, factoring the product is unreasonably hard — is what makes every HTTPS connection, every signed software update, every authenticated banking transaction possible. The mathematics is from antiquity; the application is from 1977.
crumbs: 13 · Modular Arithmetic and the RSA Algorithm

title: 13 · Modular Arithmetic and the RSA Algorithm
text:
Name

a \equiv b \pmod n iff n | (a - b).
The set \mathbb{Z}/n has elements \{0, 1, \ldots, n-1\} with +,
-, \cdot mod n.
Modular inverse: a^{-1} \pmod n exists iff \gcd(a, n) = 1. Found via the extended Euclidean algorithm.
Fermat’s Little Theorem: a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod p for prime p and \gcd(a, p) = 1.
Euler’s Theorem: a^{\varphi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod n for \gcd(a, n)
= 1.
Euler totient: $(n) = $ count of integers in [1, n] coprime to n. - \varphi(p) = p - 1 for prime p. - \varphi(p q) = (p - 1)(q - 1) for distinct primes. - General: multiplicative on coprime integers.
RSA:

Pick large primes p, q. n = pq, \varphi = (p-1)(q-1).
Choose e coprime to \varphi. Compute d = e^{-1} \pmod \varphi.
Public: (n, e). Private: d.
Encrypt: c = m^e \pmod n. Decrypt: m = c^d \pmod n.

Security: rests on the hardness of factoring large n.


Modular arithmetic: arithmetic with remainders; Fermat’s little: a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod p for prime p; RSA: easy to multiply primes, hard to factor; security follows.
crumbs: 13 · Modular Arithmetic and the RSA Algorithm

title: 13 · Modular Arithmetic and the RSA Algorithm
text:
Apply

Worked example

Compute 7^{100} \pmod{13} using Fermat.

By Fermat: 7^{12} \equiv 1 \pmod{13}.
100 = 12 \cdot 8 + 4. So 7^{100} = (7^{12})^8 \cdot 7^4 \equiv 1
\cdot 7^4 \pmod{13}.
7^2 = 49 \equiv 10. 7^4 = 10^2 = 100 \equiv 9.
Answer: 9.


Practice — Warm
W1. 25 \pmod 7 = ?


Full solution

25 = 3 \cdot 7 + 4, so 4.

W2. \varphi(7) = ?


Full solution

6.

W3. 2^{10} \pmod{11}?


Full solution

By Fermat: \equiv 1.



Practice — Working
P1. Compute 5^{50} \pmod{12}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\varphi(12) = 4. Euler’s theorem applies if \gcd(5, 12) = 1 ✓. 5^4 \equiv 1 \pmod{12}. So 5^{50} = (5^4)^{12} \cdot 5^2 \equiv
25 \equiv 1.



Full solution

1.

P2. Find \varphi(100).


Full solution

100 = 4 \cdot 25 = 2^2 \cdot 5^2. \varphi(100) = 100 (1 - 1/2)(1 -
1/5) = 100 \cdot 1/2 \cdot 4/5 = 40.

P3. RSA toy: p = 7, q = 13, e = 5. Find d.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\varphi = 72. Find 5 d \equiv 1 \pmod{72}.



Full solution

d = 29 (extended Euclidean / search). Check: 5 \cdot 29 = 145 = 2
\cdot 72 + 1 ✓.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Why is RSA secure if factoring is hard? Suppose someone knows n = pq but not p, q separately. Why can’t they compute d without factoring?


Full solution

d = e^{-1} \pmod{\varphi(n)}. To compute this, you need \varphi(n)
= (p-1)(q-1). Knowing only n, you don’t know \varphi(n) — unless you can find p, q, i.e., factor n.
In fact, computing \varphi(n) from n is equivalent to factoring n (knowing both gives a quadratic equation p, q).
So RSA’s security reduces to “factoring n = pq is hard for large pq.” This is the most studied computational problem in cryptography. No efficient classical algorithm is known. Shor’s algorithm (1994) factors in polynomial time on a quantum computer — motivating post-quantum cryptography (Tier 7).

S2. Why e = 65537? This is the most common choice for the RSA public exponent. Why?


Full solution

65537 = 2^{16} + 1 is a Fermat prime. Special properties:

Coprime to \varphi(n) for almost all n (since 65537 is prime).
Binary representation: 10000000000000001_2 — only two 1 bits. This makes m^e \pmod n fast: only ~17 squarings needed.
Large enough to prevent low-exponent attacks (e.g., naive cube root attacks for e = 3).

Standardised choice; supported by virtually every implementation.
The interplay of number theory (Fermat primes), bit representation (sparse binary for fast exponentiation), and security (avoiding small exponents) — all in one constant.



Common confusions

“Mod is just remainder.” Mod = equivalence relation. Multiple representatives (17 \equiv 2 \equiv -3 \equiv 22 \pmod
5).
“Modular inverse always exists.” Only if \gcd(a, n) = 1. E.g., 2 has no inverse mod 4.
“RSA is unbreakable.” RSA’s security depends on factoring being hard. Quantum computers may break it. Post-quantum alternatives (lattice-based) are being deployed.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

Modular arithmetic is arithmetic that wraps around — once you reduce mod n, the integers fold into a finite set \{0, 1, \ldots, n-1\} that still respects +, -, and \times.
Fermat’s little theorem and its generalisation (a^{\varphi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod n) make modular exponentiation predictable, which is exactly the lever RSA pulls.
RSA’s security rests on a single asymmetry — multiplying two primes is cheap, factoring the product is not. Most of internet encryption depends on that one gap holding.



Self-Assessment

Could you compute 3^{100} \pmod 7?
Without scrolling back, what’s \varphi(11)?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answers: 3^6 \equiv 1, 100 = 16 \cdot 6 + 4, so 3^4 = 81 \equiv
4. \varphi(11) = 10.)

3 yes: ready for 14 · Convergence.
2 yes: redo S1 (RSA security argument).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P5.
crumbs: 13 · Modular Arithmetic and the RSA Algorithm

title: 13 · Modular Arithmetic and the RSA Algorithm
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

T1 L15 — Divisibility & Primes
T3 L06 — Exp & Log Functions — exponentiation underlying modular powers.



What this unlocks

Tier 7 — Algebraic Number Theory & Encryption track — modular arithmetic generalised to rings of algebraic integers; elliptic curves over finite fields.



Where this matters

Encryption. RSA secures most internet traffic via TLS. Diffie- Hellman key exchange, ECDSA signatures all use modular arithmetic. Post-quantum schemes (Kyber, Dilithium) extend the toolkit.


Mathematical research. Number theory is one of the deepest subjects in mathematics. The Riemann Hypothesis, the Birch-Swinnerton-Dyer conjecture, and many other open problems connect deeply to modular arithmetic.

title: 08 · Continuous Distributions and the Normal
text:
Have you done T3 L15 — Discrete Distributions and T4 L08 — Definite Integral?
Comfortable with PMFs P(X = k) and expected values?
Recall \int e^{-x^2} dx is the Gaussian (T5 L06 S1)?

title: 08 · Continuous Distributions and the Normal
08 · Continuous Distributions and the Normal
text:
Wonder

Why is P(X = a) = 0 for any single point in continuous case?
Total area under a PDF must equal 1. Why?
Why does the normal distribution appear so often in nature?

title: 08 · Continuous Distributions and the Normal
text:
Discover

Problem 1
A uniform distribution on [a, b] has density f(x) = 1/(b - a) for x \in [a, b], else 0. Verify \int f = 1.


Full solution

\int_a^b 1/(b-a) \, dx = (b - a) / (b - a) = 1 ✓.
Uniform models “pick a random number in [a, b] with equal probability for any subinterval of equal length.”



Problem 2
The expected value of a continuous random variable is E[X] = \int x f(x) \, dx.
Compute E[X] for X \sim \text{Uniform}[0, 1].


Full solution

E[X] = \int_0^1 x \cdot 1 \, dx = 1/2.
Symmetry: the average of a uniform random variable is the midpoint.



Problem 3
The standard normal N(0, 1) has PDF \phi(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-x^2/2}. Show that \int \phi = 1 using the Gaussian integral \int e^{-u^2}
du = \sqrt{\pi} (T5 L06 S1).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Substitute u = x/\sqrt{2}, du = dx/\sqrt{2}.



Full solution

\int \phi(x) \, dx = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int e^{-x^2/2} \, dx
= \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{2} \int e^{-u^2} \, du
= \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{\pi} = 1 ✓.
The \sqrt{2\pi} in the formula is exactly what makes the area equal to 1.



Problem 4
The general normal N(\mu, \sigma^2) has PDF f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(x - \mu)^2 / (2 \sigma^2)}.
What is E[X]? (Hint: by symmetry around \mu.)


Full solution

E[X] = \mu. The PDF is symmetric around \mu, so the centre (mean) is \mu.
Standard deviation = \sigma (Lesson 09).



Problem 5
The exponential distribution with rate \lambda > 0: f(x) =
\lambda e^{-\lambda x} for x \ge 0. Verify \int f = 1 and compute E[X].


Full solution

\int_0^\infty \lambda e^{-\lambda x} \, dx = [-e^{-\lambda x}]_0^\infty
= 1 ✓.
E[X] = \int_0^\infty x \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda x} \, dx = 1/\lambda (by integration by parts).
The exponential models waiting times: time until the next email, the next radioactive decay, the next earthquake. E[X] = 1/\lambda is the average wait.


The bell curve was discovered by Abraham de Moivre in 1733 as an approximation to the binomial distribution, but its career really began with Carl Friedrich Gauss, who in 1809 derived it from the requirement that the arithmetic mean be the most likely value of a quantity measured many times — the lens through which he was reducing the messy astronomical observations of the asteroid Ceres. The Belgian astronomer-statistician Adolphe Quetelet then made the leap, in his 1835 Sur l’homme et le développement de ses facultés, that the same curve described human heights, chest sizes, and birth rates — turning statistics from astronomy’s housekeeping into a science of populations.


For a continuous random variable, the PDF is not a probability — it’s a density, like grams per centimetre. Probability is the area you collect when you sweep across an interval. That is why single points have zero probability (zero width) and why every PDF must enclose total area exactly 1.

title: 08 · Continuous Distributions and the Normal
text:
Name

A continuous random variable X has a PDF f(x) such that P(a \le X \le b) = \int_a^b f(x) \, dx.
Properties:

f(x) \ge 0 for all x.
\int_{-\infty}^\infty f(x) \, dx = 1.
P(X = a) = 0 for any single point.

Cumulative distribution function (CDF): F(x) = P(X \le x) = \int_{-\infty}^x f \, dt.
Expected value: E[X] = \int x f(x) \, dx.
Common continuous distributions:








Distribution
PDF
Mean




Uniform on [a, b]
1/(b-a)
(a + b)/2


Exponential rate \lambda
\lambda e^{-\lambda x}, x \ge 0
1/\lambda


Normal N(\mu, \sigma^2)
\frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(x-\mu)^2/(2\sigma^2)}
\mu



The normal distribution is uniquely characterised by being the limit of sums of many independent random variables (Central Limit Theorem, Lesson 09).


Continuous random variables: probabilities are integrals of PDFs; Total area = 1; single points have zero probability; Normal N(\mu, \sigma^2) is the central distribution of statistics.

title: 08 · Continuous Distributions and the Normal
text:
Apply

Worked example

X \sim \text{Uniform}[0, 4]. Compute P(1 \le X \le 3) and E[X].

PDF: f(x) = 1/4 on [0, 4].
P(1 \le X \le 3) = \int_1^3 1/4 \, dx = 2/4 = 1/2.
E[X] = (0 + 4)/2 = 2.


Practice — Warm
W1. f(x) = 2x on [0, 1]. Is this a PDF?


Full solution

\int_0^1 2x \, dx = 1 ✓ and f \ge 0 ✓. Yes.

W2. E[X] for the PDF in W1?


Full solution

\int_0^1 x \cdot 2x \, dx = \int 2x^2 \, dx = 2/3.

W3. X \sim N(5, 4) — what is \mu, \sigma?


Full solution

\mu = 5, \sigma = 2 (since \sigma^2 = 4).



Practice — Working
P1. X \sim \text{Exp}(2). P(X > 1)?


Full solution

P(X > 1) = \int_1^\infty 2 e^{-2x} dx = e^{-2} \approx 0.135.

P2. Find c so f(x) = c x^2 is a PDF on [0, 1].


Full solution

\int_0^1 c x^2 dx = c/3 = 1 \Rightarrow c = 3.

P3. X \sim N(0, 1). Approximate P(-1 \le X \le 1).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Standard normal table or 68-95-99.7 rule: about 68% within 1 SD.



Full solution

\approx 0.6827 (the famous “68% within 1 SD” rule).
The empirical rule: \approx 68\% within 1\sigma, \approx 95\% within 2\sigma, \approx 99.7\% within 3\sigma — used widely in quality control and statistical reasoning.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Why does the normal appear in so many contexts? Hint: the Central Limit Theorem (Lesson 09) says the sum (or average) of many independent random variables, regardless of their original distribution, tends to a normal distribution.
What does this say about real-world phenomena like heights, test scores, measurement errors?


Full solution

These quantities are sums of many small independent influences. Heights = many genetic + environmental contributions. Test scores = sum of correctness on many small questions. Measurement errors = accumulation of many tiny noise sources.
CLT says: regardless of the distribution of each contribution, their sum is approximately normal.
This is the single most important fact in statistics, and the reason the normal distribution dominates statistical practice. Without CLT, we’d need a different distribution for every situation.

S2. In ML: weight initialisation. Neural network weights are typically initialised from a normal distribution (or its variant). Why not uniform?


Full solution

Two reasons. (1) Activations through deep networks involve sums of many weighted inputs — by CLT, these sums are approximately normal regardless of the input distribution. Initialising weights to match this intermediate statistics (e.g., Xavier/Glorot init) keeps the activation magnitudes stable through the depth.

Symmetry breaking: random non-zero values prevent every neuron in a layer from learning the same thing.

Specific schemes: Xavier (variance 2/(n_{in} + n_{out})), He (variance 2/n_{in} for ReLU). All variants of normal initialisation designed to keep gradients from vanishing or exploding through depth.
The continuous normal distribution is the foundational building block of every well-trained neural network.



Common confusions

“P(X = 5) is small but non-zero.” For continuous X, it’s exactly zero. Only intervals have positive probability.
“PDF values are probabilities.” No — they’re densities. f(x) = 100 doesn’t mean “100% chance”; it means high density at x. Probabilities are areas, not heights.
“Normal = N(0, 1) only.” N(\mu, \sigma^2) has parameters. N(0, 1) is the standard normal; others scale and shift.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A continuous distribution is described by a PDF, not a PMF — probabilities are areas \int_a^b f(x)\,dx, not point masses.
The total area under any PDF equals 1, and the expected value is E[X] = \int x f(x)\,dx — a continuous version of the mean.
The normal distribution N(\mu, \sigma^2) is the universal bell curve — it appears wherever quantities are sums of many small independent influences (the Central Limit Theorem, next lesson).



Self-Assessment

Could you state the property \int f = 1 for any PDF?
Without scrolling back, what’s the mean of \text{Exp}(\lambda)?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: 1/\lambda.)

3 yes: ready for 09 · Variance & CLT.
2 yes: redo S2 (weight initialisation).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P5.

title: 08 · Continuous Distributions and the Normal
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

T3 L15 — Discrete Distributions — PMF generalises to PDF.
T4 L08 — Definite Integral — computes probabilities and expected values.



What this unlocks

09 · Variance & CLT — the spread, and the universality of the normal.
10 · Statistical Inference



Where this matters

AI / Machine Learning. Weight initialisation (S2). Gaussian processes (Bayesian models). Variational autoencoders (Gaussian latent space). Diffusion models (Gaussian noise → image).


Statistics & data science. The normal is the workhorse of modeling, hypothesis testing, confidence intervals.


Physics. Gaussians describe quantum wavefunctions of bound particles, equilibrium thermal distributions, measurement uncertainties.

title: 07 · Vector Fields, Divergence, Curl
text:
Have you done 05 · Partial Derivatives and T4 L14 — Dot/Cross?
Comfortable with \nabla f and partials?
Recall what a vector looks like in 3D?

title: 07 · Vector Fields, Divergence, Curl
07 · Vector Fields, Divergence, Curl
text:
Wonder

A pinwheel placed in (-y, x): would it spin? Why?
A sponge in field (x, y): would water flow into it or away?
The gradient \nabla f is a vector field — but a very special one. What’s special?

title: 07 · Vector Fields, Divergence, Curl
text:
Discover

Problem 1
A 2D vector field is \mathbf{F}(x, y) = (P(x, y), Q(x, y)).
For \mathbf{F} = (-y, x): compute \mathbf{F} at (1, 0), (0, 1),
(-1, 0), (0, -1).


Full solution

At (1, 0): \mathbf{F} = (0, 1). At (0, 1): (-1, 0). At (-1,
0): (0, -1). At (0, -1): (1, 0).
The vectors are tangent to circles, pointing counter-clockwise — this is a rotational field.



Problem 2
The divergence of \mathbf{F} = (P, Q) is \text{div } \mathbf{F} = \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F} = \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial Q}{\partial y}.
Compute div for \mathbf{F} = (x, y).


Full solution

\text{div } \mathbf{F} = 1 + 1 = 2.
Positive divergence means source — vectors emanate outward, like a fountain.



Problem 3
The curl of \mathbf{F} = (P, Q) in 2D is \text{curl } \mathbf{F} = \dfrac{\partial Q}{\partial x} - \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial y}.
Compute for \mathbf{F} = (-y, x).


Full solution

\text{curl } \mathbf{F} = 1 - (-1) = 2.
Positive curl = counter-clockwise rotation. A “paddle wheel” placed here would spin counter-clockwise.
In 3D, curl is a vector (\nabla \times \mathbf{F}) pointing along the axis of rotation by the right-hand rule.



Problem 4
Show that for any function f, curl of grad is zero: \nabla
\times \nabla f = 0.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\nabla f = (f_x, f_y). Curl: \partial / \partial x (f_y) - \partial / \partial y (f_x) =
f_{xy} - f_{yx}.



Full solution

f_{xy} = f_{yx} (mixed partials commute, by Clairaut’s theorem for nice f). So curl of grad = 0.
A vector field that is the gradient of some scalar function is called conservative — and it has zero curl. (Conservative fields arise from potentials in physics: gravity, electrostatics.)



Problem 5
Show that div of curl is zero: \nabla \cdot (\nabla \times
\mathbf{F}) = 0 in 3D.


Full solution

Compute (the components of \nabla \times F involve mixed second partials; their sum, after the divergence pattern, cancels by f_{xy} = f_{yx}).
This is a deep identity from vector calculus — the second of the two “\nabla of \nabla is zero” facts. Together with the curl-of-grad identity, these are the algebraic structure of vector calculus (forming the de Rham cohomology of \mathbb{R}^3, Tier 7 differential geometry).


The whole language of vector field, div, curl, and the \nabla symbol was forged by James Clerk Maxwell while writing his 1873 Treatise on Electricity and Magnetism. He needed a clean way to write the laws of electromagnetism that Michael Faraday had imagined geometrically (lines of force) but never put in equations. Maxwell borrowed Hamilton’s quaternion operator \nabla (“nabla,” named for an ancient Hebrew harp of similar shape), invented the modern div and curl notations, and produced the four equations that predicted light is an electromagnetic wave — confirmed by Hertz fifteen years later.


A vector field is a sea of arrows; div asks “is fluid being created or destroyed here?” and curl asks “would a paddle wheel spin here?” Together they decode any field’s local behaviour into two simple words: spreading and spinning. The two great identities — curl of grad is zero, div of curl is zero — say that “purely uphill” fields don’t spin and “purely rotational” fields don’t expand.

title: 07 · Vector Fields, Divergence, Curl
text:
Name

A vector field in 2D: \mathbf{F}(x, y) = (P(x, y), Q(x, y)). In 3D: \mathbf{F} = (P, Q, R).
Divergence: \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F} = P_x + Q_y + R_z (a scalar). Measures expansion / contraction.
Curl: in 2D: \nabla \times \mathbf{F} = Q_x - P_y (a scalar). In 3D: \nabla \times \mathbf{F} = (R_y - Q_z, P_z - R_x, Q_x - P_y) (a vector).
Identities:

\nabla \times (\nabla f) = 0 (curl of grad).
\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \mathbf{F}) = 0 (div of curl).

Conservative field: \mathbf{F} = \nabla f for some f. Has zero curl. Path integrals are path-independent.
Stokes’ theorem (preview): \iint_S (\nabla \times \mathbf{F})
\cdot d\mathbf{S} = \oint_{\partial S} \mathbf{F} \cdot d\mathbf{r}.
Divergence theorem (preview): \iiint_V \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}
\, dV = \iint_{\partial V} \mathbf{F} \cdot d\mathbf{S}.
These reduce volume/surface to boundary integrals.


Vector field = vector at every point; Div = expansion (source/sink), Curl = rotation; Curl of grad = 0; div of curl = 0 (the two great identities).

title: 07 · Vector Fields, Divergence, Curl
text:
Apply

Worked example

Compute div and curl of \mathbf{F}(x, y) = (x^2 y, y^2 - x).

P = x^2 y, Q = y^2 - x. P_x = 2xy, Q_y = 2y. \text{div} = 2xy + 2y. Q_x = -1, P_y = x^2. \text{curl} = -1 - x^2.


Practice — Warm
W1. Div of \mathbf{F} = (x, y)?


Full solution

2.

W2. Curl of \mathbf{F} = (x, y)?


Full solution

0.

W3. Curl of \mathbf{F} = (-y, x)?


Full solution

2.



Practice — Working
P1. Div and curl of \mathbf{F} = (\sin y, \cos x).


Full solution

P_x = 0, Q_y = 0 \Rightarrow \text{div} = 0. Q_x = -\sin x, P_y = \cos y \Rightarrow \text{curl} = -\sin x - \cos y.

P2. Is \mathbf{F} = (2xy, x^2 + 1) conservative? (Check curl.)


Hint 1 — a nudge

Curl: Q_x - P_y = 2x - 2x = 0. Zero curl — looks conservative.



Full solution

Yes, conservative. Find f with \nabla f = \mathbf{F}: f_x = 2xy \Rightarrow f = x^2 y + g(y). f_y = x^2 + g'(y) = x^2 + 1 \Rightarrow g'(y) = 1 \Rightarrow g(y) = y + C. f(x, y) = x^2 y + y + C.
(Path integrals of conservative fields equal f(end) - f(start).)

P3. Show \nabla f for f = x^2 + y^2 has zero curl.


Full solution

\nabla f = (2x, 2y). Curl: 0 - 0 = 0 ✓.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Maxwell’s equations in vacuum (in modern form): \nabla \cdot \mathbf{E} = 0, \quad \nabla \times \mathbf{E} = -\partial_t \mathbf{B}, \nabla \cdot \mathbf{B} = 0, \quad \nabla \times \mathbf{B} = \mu_0 \epsilon_0 \partial_t \mathbf{E}.
What does the whole of electromagnetism look like in this language?


Full solution

Four equations involving div and curl. They encode: - No magnetic monopoles (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{B} = 0). - Charge as source of E (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E} = \rho/\epsilon_0, with charges). - Faraday’s law (\nabla \times \mathbf{E} = -\partial_t \mathbf{B}). - Ampère-Maxwell law (the last).
From these, electromagnetic waves (light!) emerge as solutions where \mathbf{E} and \mathbf{B} wave with each other at speed c
= 1/\sqrt{\mu_0 \epsilon_0}.
The whole of classical EM, plus the prediction that light is an EM wave, is in those four lines of vector calculus. Maxwell’s synthesis in 1865 is one of the great achievements of physics.

S2. Smoke / fluid dynamics in games. Game engines often simulate fluid using Navier-Stokes equations, which heavily use div and curl. Why? What does \nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0 mean for incompressible fluid?


Full solution

\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0 means “no compression”: fluid that flows in must flow out at the same rate. Water is approximately incompressible.
Curl in fluids = vorticity (local rotation, e.g., eddies, smoke swirls). Modern realtime fluid simulations (smoke, water in games) solve simplified Navier-Stokes with operations on div and curl fields.
The “Smoke Plume” effect in CGI movies and games uses the vorticity confinement method — explicitly enhancing curl values to make smoke look turbulent and interesting. Vector calculus is the language of every physical simulation.



Common confusions

“Div is a vector.” Div is a scalar (a number for each point). Curl in 2D is also scalar; curl in 3D is a vector.
“Conservative = static.” Conservative just means “from a potential”. Time-varying conservative fields are common (e.g., gravity from a moving mass).
“Stokes and divergence theorems are exotic.” They generalise the FTC (“integral of derivative = boundary value”) to 2D / 3D — foundational, not exotic.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A vector field attaches a vector to every point — wind, water flow, gravitational pull, electric force.
Divergence measures spreading (sources and sinks) and curl measures spinning — built from partial derivatives via the \nabla operator.
Curl of grad is zero, div of curl is zero — the two identities that organise all of vector calculus and let “conservative” fields come from a potential.



Self-Assessment

Could you compute div and curl of \mathbf{F} = (xy, x + y)?
Without scrolling back, what is curl of grad?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answers: div = y + 1, curl = 1 - x; zero.)

3 yes: ready for 08 · Continuous Distributions.
2 yes: redo S1 (Maxwell).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P2 and P3.

title: 07 · Vector Fields, Divergence, Curl
text:
Connect

T4 L14 — Dot/Cross Product — div uses dot, curl uses cross.



What this unlocks

Tier 7 — Differential Geometry / Topology — generalises div / curl to manifolds and forms.



Where this matters

Physics. Maxwell’s equations (S1), Navier-Stokes (S2), Einstein’s general relativity — all built on vector / tensor calculus.


Game development. Fluid and smoke simulation. Realtime fluid-engine algorithms (Stable Fluids, FLIP/PIC) all manipulate div and curl.


Engineering. Computational fluid dynamics (aircraft, weather), electromagnetic simulation (antenna design, MRI).

title: 09 · Variance, Standard Deviation, and the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Have you done 08 · Continuous Distributions?
Comfortable with E[X] = \int x f(x) \, dx?
Recall the average from T1 L14?
crumbs: 09 · Variance, Standard Deviation, and the Central Limit Theorem

title: 09 · Variance, Standard Deviation, and the Central Limit Theorem
crumbs: 09 · Variance, Standard Deviation, and the Central Limit Theorem

title: 09 · Variance, Standard Deviation, and the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Wonder

The variance of one die roll is \approx 2.92. Of the average of 30 dice: 0.097. Why so much smaller?
The original distribution doesn’t look normal — but the average does. Why?
As n grows, the average converges to the mean (Law of Large Numbers). What’s CLT’s additional claim?
crumbs: 09 · Variance, Standard Deviation, and the Central Limit Theorem

title: 09 · Variance, Standard Deviation, and the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Discover

Problem 1
The variance of X is \text{Var}(X) = E[(X - \mu)^2] = E[X^2] - \mu^2.
Compute \text{Var}(X) for X \sim \text{Uniform}[0, 1].


Hint 1 — a nudge

\mu = 1/2. E[X^2] = \int_0^1 x^2 dx = 1/3. So \text{Var} = 1/3 -
1/4 = 1/12.



Full solution

\text{Var}(X) = 1/12. Standard deviation \sigma = 1/\sqrt{12}
\approx 0.289.



Problem 2
For independent X_1, \ldots, X_n with mean \mu and variance \sigma^2, the sample mean \bar X_n = (X_1 + \cdots + X_n)/n satisfies: E[\bar X_n] = \mu, \quad \text{Var}(\bar X_n) = \sigma^2/n.
Why does the variance shrink by 1/n?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Variance of sum (independent): \text{Var}(X_1 + \cdots + X_n) = n
\sigma^2. Then \bar X = \text{sum}/n scales variance by 1/n^2: n \sigma^2 / n^2 = \sigma^2/n.



Full solution

Variance of sum of independent = sum of variances. Then \text{Var}(c X) = c^2 \text{Var}(X), so dividing by n scales variance by 1/n^2.
This is the Law of Large Numbers intuition: \bar X_n tightens around \mu as n grows. Standard error: \sigma/\sqrt{n}.



Problem 3
The Central Limit Theorem (CLT): for i.i.d. X_i with finite mean \mu and variance \sigma^2, \dfrac{\bar X_n - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \xrightarrow{d} N(0, 1).
In words: the sample mean (suitably standardised) tends to a standard normal distribution, regardless of the original distribution.
Why is this remarkable?


Full solution

Universality. The original X_i could be anything — uniform, exponential, weird custom distribution — and the average still ends up normal.
This is why the normal distribution dominates statistics: most real-world quantities are averages (or sums) of many small random influences, and CLT makes them all normal.
The CLT’s proof uses characteristic functions (Fourier transforms of densities) — Tier 6 / Tier 7 material.



Problem 4
A coin is flipped 100 times. Number of heads X \sim \text{Bin}(100,
0.5). By CLT, X is approximately normal with mean np = 50 and variance np(1-p) = 25.
What’s the approximate probability of getting between 45 and 55 heads?


Hint 1 — a nudge

\sigma = 5. So 45-55 is within \pm 1\sigma. By 68-95-99.7 rule: \approx 68\%.



Full solution

\approx 68\%. The binomial \text{Bin}(100, 0.5) is well approximated by N(50, 25) for inference purposes.
CLT lets us use the normal distribution as a practical approximation for many discrete distributions when n is large.



Problem 5
An ML model trained on different random subsets of data gives different test accuracies. Each accuracy is a sample mean over the test set. What does CLT say about the variability of these accuracy estimates?


Full solution

The accuracy estimates are approximately normal, with standard error \sigma/\sqrt{n_{\text{test}}} where n_{\text{test}} is test- set size and \sigma depends on per-example accuracy variability.
This means: with a 10K-example test set, accuracies will vary by roughly \sqrt{p(1-p)/10000} \approx 0.005 (half a percentage point) at p = 0.5. Reporting “85.36%” implies precision the data doesn’t support.
Most ML papers’ accuracy claims should report confidence intervals based on this calculation.


The bell curve was first sketched in De Moivre’s 1733 essay Approximatio — he showed that the binomial distribution, for large n, hugs an exponential-of-a-square shape. Laplace then built it into his Théorie analytique des probabilités (1812), and a century later the Russian mathematician Lyapunov proved (1901) the first truly general central limit theorem — sums of independent variables satisfying mild conditions converge to the normal, no matter how weirdly distributed the summands. The “central” in CLT means foundational, not “middle.”


The normal distribution is not built into nature — it is built into averaging. Add or average enough independent random tugs of any shape, rescale by \sqrt{n}, and the bell curve appears as if from nowhere. That is why it pops up everywhere: heights, measurement errors, polling, sample means — they are all averages in disguise.
crumbs: 09 · Variance, Standard Deviation, and the Central Limit Theorem

title: 09 · Variance, Standard Deviation, and the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Name

Variance \sigma^2 = E[(X - \mu)^2] = E[X^2] - \mu^2.
Standard deviation \sigma = \sqrt{\text{Var}}.
Properties:

\text{Var}(c X) = c^2 \text{Var}(X).
\text{Var}(X + c) = \text{Var}(X) (shift invariant).
For independent X, Y: \text{Var}(X + Y) = \text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(Y).
\text{Var}(\bar X_n) = \sigma^2 / n (for i.i.d. sample mean).

Standard error of \bar X_n: \sigma / \sqrt{n} — measures typical deviation of sample mean from true mean.
Law of Large Numbers (LLN): \bar X_n \to \mu as n \to \infty (in probability).
Central Limit Theorem (CLT): \dfrac{\bar X_n - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \xrightarrow{d} N(0, 1). Sample mean is approximately normal with mean \mu and SD \sigma/\sqrt{n}, regardless of the original distribution (assuming finite variance).
Empirical rule (N(\mu, \sigma^2)):

\approx 68\% within \mu \pm \sigma.
\approx 95\% within \mu \pm 2\sigma.
\approx 99.7\% within \mu \pm 3\sigma.



Variance = expected squared deviation; SD has units of X; Sample mean has variance \sigma^2/n (LLN); CLT: sample mean is approximately normal — universally.
crumbs: 09 · Variance, Standard Deviation, and the Central Limit Theorem

title: 09 · Variance, Standard Deviation, and the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Apply

Worked example

A factory produces resistors with target resistance 100 Ω, SD 5 Ω. A sample of 25 resistors is tested. What’s the SD of the sample mean?

\sigma_{\bar X} = \sigma / \sqrt{n} = 5 / \sqrt{25} = 1 Ω.
So the sample mean of 25 resistors is approximately N(100, 1^2). By 68-95-99.7: about 68% chance the sample mean is in [99, 101].
This is the foundation of quality control.


Practice — Warm
W1. \text{Var}(\text{constant}) = ?


Full solution

0.

W2. SD of X \sim N(0, 9)?


Full solution

3.

W3. Sample mean of 100 i.i.d. with \sigma = 10. SD of sample mean?


Full solution

10/\sqrt{100} = 1.



Practice — Working
P1. X \sim \text{Bin}(100, 0.3). Mean and SD?


Full solution

\mu = np = 30, \sigma = \sqrt{np(1-p)} = \sqrt{21} \approx 4.58.

P2. Coin flipped 1000 times. Approximate P(\text{heads} > 540) using CLT.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\mu = 500, \sigma \approx 15.81. Compute z = (540 - 500)/15.81
\approx 2.53. Standard normal table: P(Z > 2.53) \approx 0.006.



Full solution

\approx 0.6\%. Significantly more than 540 heads in 1000 fair flips would be statistically very unusual.

P3. \text{Var}(2X + 3Y) for independent X, Y with \text{Var}(X) = 4, \text{Var}(Y) = 9.


Full solution

2^2 \cdot 4 + 3^2 \cdot 9 = 16 + 81 = 97.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Why does CLT need finite variance? The Cauchy distribution has infinite variance; the CLT fails for it. Sample means don’t narrow as n grows.
What practical implication does this have for outlier-prone data?


Full solution

Heavy-tailed distributions (Cauchy, Pareto, Lévy) violate CLT. For such data, sample means are unreliable: outliers dominate, and larger samples don’t help.
Practical examples: financial returns sometimes look like power-law tails; risk models that assume normality (and thus CLT) underestimate extreme losses (the fat tail problem). The 2008 financial crisis exposed widespread misuse of the normal-distribution assumption.
For heavy-tailed data, use median (robust to outliers) instead of mean, or trimmed / winsorised estimators.

S2. Bootstrap confidence intervals. Given a single dataset of size n, you can estimate the SD of the sample mean (= standard error) by resampling: draw n items with replacement many times, compute the sample mean each time, look at the SD of those many sample means.
Why does this work?


Full solution

Empirical distribution → bootstrap distribution → sampling distribution. The empirical distribution (your dataset’s frequencies) approximates the true distribution. Resampling from it generates “synthetic datasets” of the same size; their sample means approximate the sampling distribution of the true sample mean.
By CLT, this sampling distribution is approximately normal with SD = \sigma/\sqrt{n}. Bootstrap empirically estimates this SD without needing to know \sigma.
Bootstrap (Efron, 1979) is the dominant practical method for confidence intervals when no closed form exists. Used in nearly every modern data-analysis pipeline.



Common confusions

“Variance and SD are the same thing.” No — SD = \sqrt{\text{Var}}. SD has same units as X; variance has squared units.
“CLT applies to any sum.” Needs finite variance of the summands and (technically) some independence / identical distribution. Heavy-tailed (Cauchy) breaks it.
“Sample size n = 30 is enough for CLT.” Old rule of thumb; often true but can fail for very skewed distributions. Modern advice: check the actual distribution.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

Variance measures spread, and standard deviation puts that spread back in the original units of X.
Averaging shrinks variance by 1/n. The standard error \sigma/\sqrt{n} tells you how tightly the sample mean hugs the true mean.
The Central Limit Theorem is universal: averages of enough independent variables look normal, regardless of where they came from — provided variance is finite.



Self-Assessment

Could you derive \text{Var}(\bar X_n) = \sigma^2/n from independence?
Without scrolling back, what’s CLT’s claim?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: sample mean is approximately normal regardless of original distribution.)

3 yes: ready for 10 · Statistical Inference.
2 yes: redo S2 (bootstrap).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P5.
crumbs: 09 · Variance, Standard Deviation, and the Central Limit Theorem

title: 09 · Variance, Standard Deviation, and the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

08 · Continuous Distributions



What this unlocks

10 · Statistical Inference — applies CLT to confidence intervals.



Where this matters

AI / Machine Learning. Standard errors on accuracy / loss estimates (P3, S2). Stochastic gradient descent inherits CLT-style behaviour for batch averages. Many ML theorems assume CLT-like concentration.


Statistics. Hypothesis testing, confidence intervals, p-values — all rest on CLT.


Finance & risk. Heavy-tailed risks (S1) violate normality assumptions; sophisticated models account for this.
crumbs: 09 · Variance, Standard Deviation, and the Central Limit Theorem

title: 04 · Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors
text:
Have you done 02 · Linear Systems and 03 · Vector Spaces?
Comfortable with matrix-vector multiplication and determinants?
Recall solving polynomial equations (Tier 3)?

title: 04 · Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors
04 · Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors
text:
Wonder

Why might “directions a matrix only stretches” be useful?
Why does a rotation matrix have no real eigenvectors?
The product of eigenvalues = the determinant. Why?

title: 04 · Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors
text:
Discover

Problem 1
For A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix}, verify (1,
0) is an eigenvector with eigenvalue 2.


Full solution

A (1, 0)^T = (2, 0)^T = 2 \cdot (1, 0)^T ✓.
Diagonal matrices have the standard basis vectors as eigenvectors, with the diagonal entries as eigenvalues.



Problem 2
For general A, the equation A v = \lambda v rewrites as (A -
\lambda I) v = 0. For non-zero v, the matrix A - \lambda I must be singular, i.e., \det(A - \lambda I) = 0. This is the characteristic equation.
For A = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix}, compute the characteristic polynomial.


Hint 1 — a nudge

A - \lambda I = \begin{pmatrix} 4 - \lambda & 1 \\ 2 & 3 - \lambda
\end{pmatrix}. Determinant.



Full solution

\det = (4 - \lambda)(3 - \lambda) - 2 = \lambda^2 - 7 \lambda + 10.
Set to zero: (\lambda - 5)(\lambda - 2) = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = 5,
2. Eigenvalues 5 and 2.
To find eigenvectors: solve (A - \lambda I) v = 0 for each \lambda. For \lambda = 5: \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 2 & -2 \end{pmatrix} v
= 0 \Rightarrow v_1 = v_2. Eigenvector: (1, 1)^T (or any scalar multiple).



Problem 3
Find the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of A = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1
\\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} (90° rotation).


Full solution

\det(A - \lambda I) = \lambda^2 + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = \pm i. No real eigenvalues.
Geometrically: a 90° rotation has no direction preserved (every vector rotates). Real eigenvalues correspond to “preserved directions” — rotations have none unless trivial. (Complex eigenvalues describe rotations + scalings in the complex sense.)



Problem 4
Find eigenvalues and eigenvectors of A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1
& 1 \end{pmatrix}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Char eq: (1 - \lambda)^2 - 1 = \lambda^2 - 2\lambda = \lambda(\lambda
- 2) = 0.



Full solution

Eigenvalues: \lambda = 0, 2.
For \lambda = 0: \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} v =
0 \Rightarrow v_1 + v_2 = 0. Eigenvector: (1, -1)^T.
For \lambda = 2: \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix} v
= 0 \Rightarrow v_1 = v_2. Eigenvector: (1, 1)^T.
A zero eigenvalue means A has a null space — there’s a non-zero vector mapped to zero. The matrix is singular.



Problem 5
Diagonalisation. If a matrix A has n independent eigenvectors, A = P D P^{-1} where P is the matrix of eigenvectors and D is diagonal with eigenvalues. Then A^k = P D^k
P^{-1} — easy power computation.
What’s \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}^{10}?


Full solution

From P4: eigenvalues 0, 2. D = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2
\end{pmatrix}. D^{10} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1024 \end{pmatrix}.
P = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}, P^{-1} = \tfrac{1}{2}
\begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}.
A^{10} = P D^{10} P^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 512 & 512 \\ 512 & 512
\end{pmatrix}.
(Check: each step doubles the matrix entries since A^2 = 2A, so A^{10} = 2^9 A = 512 A ✓.)
Diagonalisation makes powers easy — a major reason eigenvalues matter.


The word eigenvalue is a half-translation. Cauchy worked with “valeurs caractéristiques” (characteristic values) in 1829, and the notion sat unnamed for decades. David Hilbert, in his 1904 papers on integral equations, called them Eigenwerte — German for “own values” or “proper values.” When the theory crossed into English, translators gave up on translating eigen- and just left it German, which is why we now happily say “eigenvalues,” “eigenvectors,” and “eigenspaces” with a straight face. Schrödinger’s 1926 wave equation made these words inescapable in physics.


Most directions get rotated and scaled by a matrix. Eigenvectors are the rare directions that only get scaled — the matrix’s own preferred axes. In an eigenbasis, a confusing transformation collapses into “stretch by these numbers along these directions,” and even raising it to the millionth power becomes a one-line computation.

title: 04 · Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors
text:
Name

For a square matrix A, a non-zero vector v is an eigenvector with eigenvalue \lambda if A v = \lambda v.
Characteristic equation: \det(A - \lambda I) = 0. Roots are the eigenvalues.
Eigenvectors for each \lambda are non-zero vectors in the null space of (A - \lambda I).
Properties:

Sum of eigenvalues = \text{trace}(A) (sum of diagonal).
Product of eigenvalues = \det(A).
Diagonal/triangular matrices have eigenvalues = diagonal entries.

Diagonalisation: if A has n linearly independent eigenvectors, A = P D P^{-1}. Then A^k = P D^k P^{-1}.
Real symmetric matrices are always diagonalisable, with real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors. (Spectral theorem.)


Eigenvectors: directions a matrix only stretches; Eigenvalues: the stretching factors; Diagonalisation = “find eigenvectors as a basis” — hugely useful for powers.

title: 04 · Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors
text:
Apply

Worked example

Find eigenvalues and eigenvectors of A = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 1 \\
0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}.

Triangular → eigenvalues are diagonal entries: 3, 2.
For \lambda = 3: (A - 3I) v = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -1
\end{pmatrix} v = 0 \Rightarrow v_2 = 0. Eigenvector: (1, 0)^T.
For \lambda = 2: (A - 2I) v = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0
\end{pmatrix} v = 0 \Rightarrow v_1 + v_2 = 0. Eigenvector: (1,
-1)^T.


Practice — Warm
W1. Eigenvalues of \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 7 \end{pmatrix}?


Full solution

5, 7.

W2. Trace of \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}?


Full solution

3 + 2 = 5.

W3. \det \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix}?


Full solution

6, also = product of eigenvalues.



Practice — Working
P1. Eigenvalues of \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix}?


Full solution

\lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 10 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = 5, 2. (Discover P2.)

P2. Eigenvectors for \lambda = 5 in P1?


Full solution

(A - 5I) v = 0 \Rightarrow v_1 = v_2. (1, 1)^T.

P3. Compute \det A via product of eigenvalues for the matrix in P1.


Full solution

5 \cdot 2 = 10. Direct: 4 \cdot 3 - 1 \cdot 2 = 10 ✓.



Practice — Stretch
S1. PCA in ML. Principal Component Analysis decomposes a data covariance matrix into eigenvectors (the principal components). Why are the eigenvectors with the largest eigenvalues the “most informative” directions?


Full solution

Eigenvalues of the covariance matrix = variances along the corresponding eigenvector directions. The eigenvector with largest eigenvalue is the direction of greatest variance — where the data spreads most. PCA projects high-dim data onto top-k eigenvectors, preserving as much variance as possible while reducing dimension.
PCA is the classic dimensionality reduction technique. Modern self-supervised representation learning (SimCLR, BYOL) often implicitly learns directions analogous to PCA components.

S2. Google’s PageRank algorithm assigns each web page an importance score given by the eigenvector of the link matrix corresponding to eigenvalue 1. Why does this work?


Full solution

The link matrix A_{ij} encodes “fraction of page j’s outgoing links that go to page i.” The eigenvector r with A r = r satisfies “score r_i equals weighted sum of scores of pages linking to i” — exactly the recursive definition of importance.
Existence is guaranteed by the Perron-Frobenius theorem for non- negative matrices. The eigenvector is computed iteratively (the power method).
Google’s $250B+ valuation initially rested on this eigenvalue calculation. Linear algebra you learned in 30 minutes built one of the most consequential companies of the modern era.



Common confusions

“Every matrix has real eigenvalues.” No — rotations have only complex (non-real) eigenvalues.
“Eigenvectors are unique.” Eigenvectors are unique up to scalar multiple. The whole eigenspace (1D for distinct eigenvalues, possibly higher for repeated) shares the same eigenvalue.
“All matrices diagonalise.” No — defective matrices have fewer eigenvectors than dimensions; they don’t diagonalise. (Jordan form handles them, Tier 6+.)



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

An eigenvector of a matrix is a direction the matrix only stretches, and the eigenvalue is the stretch factor.
Eigenvalues are roots of \det(A - \lambda I) = 0 — a polynomial equation in \lambda, the characteristic equation.
Diagonalisation A = P D P^{-1} turns matrix powers into number powers, the trick behind PageRank, PCA, and quantum mechanics.



Self-Assessment

Could you compute the characteristic polynomial of a 2×2 matrix?
Without scrolling back, what are the eigenvalues of \begin{pmatrix}
6 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{pmatrix}?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: 6, -2.)

3 yes: ready for 05 · Partial Derivatives.
2 yes: redo S2 (PageRank).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P2 and P5.

title: 04 · Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

02 · Linear Systems — to find eigenvectors.
03 · Vector Spaces — eigenspaces are subspaces.



What this unlocks

Tier 6 — Spectral Theorem — symmetric matrices fully diagonalise.
Tier 7 — Functional Analysis — operators on infinite-dimensional spaces (eigenvalues become spectra).



Where this matters

AI / Machine Learning. PCA (S1), spectral methods, attention in Transformers (computing eigenvalues for matrix decompositions), random matrix theory in deep learning theory.


Search & ranking. PageRank (S2), HITS, modern recommender systems all use eigenvector computations.


Physics. Quantum mechanics: observables are operators; eigenvalues are measured values; eigenvectors are energy states. Atomic and molecular structure is fundamentally an eigenvalue problem.

title: 10 · Statistical Inference
text:
Have you done 09 · Variance & CLT?
Comfortable with \sigma/\sqrt{n} as the SE of the mean?
Recall the empirical rule (68/95/99.7%)?

title: 10 · Statistical Inference
10 · Statistical Inference
text:
Wonder

What does “95% confidence” actually mean?
To halve a CI’s width, how many times more data do you need?
What does it mean to “reject” a null hypothesis?

title: 10 · Statistical Inference
text:
Discover

Problem 1
A 95% confidence interval for the mean (assuming known \sigma): \bar X \pm 1.96 \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}.
The “1.96” comes from z-score for 95% area in the standard normal.
If \sigma = 10, n = 25, \bar X = 50: CI?


Full solution

\text{SE} = 10/5 = 2. Margin of error = 1.96 \cdot 2 = 3.92. CI: [46.08, 53.92].



Problem 2
What does “95% confidence” mean? Common misconception: “there’s a 95% probability the true mean is in the interval.” That’s wrong (in the frequentist framework).
The correct interpretation:


Full solution

If we repeated the procedure many times (sample, compute CI), 95% of the resulting intervals would contain the true mean.
The true mean is fixed (unknown to us); the interval is random (depends on the sample). 95% of those random intervals catch the true value.
(In Bayesian inference, “95% probability” can be a valid statement about a credible interval. Frequentist CIs differ subtly.)



Problem 3
To shrink the CI by half: 1.96 \cdot \sigma/\sqrt{n_{\text{new}}} should be half of 1.96 \cdot \sigma/\sqrt{n}, so \sqrt{n_{\text{new}}}
= 2 \sqrt{n} \Rightarrow n_{\text{new}} = 4 n.
Quadrupling sample size halves the CI width.
If you want a 99% CI instead of 95%, the multiplier increases from 1.96 to 2.58. The CI is wider; more confidence requires a wider interval.


Full solution

Confidence level vs interval width is a fundamental tradeoff: more confidence requires wider intervals, more precision requires more data.
This single observation governs the cost of statistical knowledge: “How much data to make claims with confidence?”



Problem 4
A hypothesis test asks: is the data consistent with the null hypothesis H_0 (e.g., “the coin is fair”)?
Procedure:

Compute a test statistic under H_0.
Compute the p-value: probability of seeing data as extreme as yours, if H_0 is true.
If p-value < 0.05 (conventional threshold), reject H_0.

Toss a coin 100 times, get 65 heads. Test H_0: coin is fair (p =
0.5).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Under H_0: X \sim \text{Bin}(100, 0.5) \approx N(50, 25). SE = 5. z = (65 - 50)/5 = 3. P(|Z| > 3) \approx 0.0027.



Full solution

z = 3, p-value \approx 0.003. Less than 0.05 — reject H_0: the coin is biased.
(More carefully: 0.003 is the one-sided probability of Z > 3. Two-sided is twice that, still < 0.01.)



Problem 5
A/B testing. Two website variants: A converts 10% of 1000 visitors; B converts 12% of 1000. Is B significantly better?


Hint 1 — a nudge

SE of difference: \sqrt{p_A(1-p_A)/n_A + p_B(1-p_B)/n_B} \approx
\sqrt{0.0001 \cdot 9 + 0.000106 \cdot 8.8} \approx \sqrt{0.000189}
\approx 0.0137. z = (0.12 - 0.10)/0.0137 \approx 1.46. p-value two-sided \approx 0.144.



Full solution

p-value \approx 0.144 > 0.05. Cannot reject H_0 that the two are equal. Need more data to distinguish.
A/B testing in industry routinely uses this framework. Classical tests assume independence and binomial outcomes; Bayesian and sequential variants exist.
Common pitfall: p-hacking — running many tests, reporting only significant ones. Inflates false-positive rate. Pre-registration of hypotheses combats this.


The whole modern apparatus — null hypothesis, p-value, significance level, maximum likelihood — was assembled in roughly two decades by Ronald A. Fisher (working at Rothamsted on agricultural plots in the 1920s) and then formalised as a decision-theoretic framework by Jerzy Neyman and Egon Pearson in 1933. Fisher and Neyman disagreed bitterly for the rest of their lives over whether p-values were measures of evidence (Fisher) or long-run error rates (Neyman–Pearson). Today’s textbook fudge — using both vocabularies in the same paragraph — is a truce neither would have approved.


A 95% confidence interval is a statement about the procedure, not the parameter. The true mean is fixed; the interval is the random thing. Repeated honestly, the recipe catches the truth 95 times out of 100 — a guarantee about the method, not about any particular interval you draw.

title: 10 · Statistical Inference
text:
Name

A point estimate \hat \theta for parameter \theta is a single best guess (e.g., sample mean for population mean).
A (1 - \alpha) \cdot 100\% confidence interval for \theta is a random interval [\hat \theta - m, \hat \theta + m] such that, in repeated sampling, \theta falls in the interval with probability 1 - \alpha.
For the mean with known \sigma: \bar X \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot
\sigma / \sqrt{n} where z_{\alpha/2} is the standard normal quantile.



Confidence
z_{\alpha/2}




90%
1.645


95%
1.96


99%
2.576



Hypothesis test:

Set up H_0 (null) and H_1 (alternative).
Compute test statistic under H_0.
Compute p-value = P(\text{data this extreme or more} | H_0).
Reject H_0 if p < \alpha (significance level, often 0.05).

Two errors:

Type I: reject true H_0 (false positive). Probability \alpha.
Type II: accept false H_0 (false negative). Probability \beta.

Power = 1 - \beta = probability of correctly rejecting false H_0.


CI: range that catches the true parameter with stated probability; Hypothesis test: reject H₀ if p < α (typically 0.05); Two errors: false positive (α), false negative (β).

title: 10 · Statistical Inference
text:
Apply

Worked example

Sample of 100 students has mean test score 75, SD 12. 95% CI for the population mean?

SE = 12/\sqrt{100} = 1.2. Margin = 1.96 \cdot 1.2 = 2.35. CI: [72.65, 77.35].
(Strictly: when \sigma is unknown and estimated from the sample, the t-distribution replaces the normal — for n = 100, the difference is negligible.)


Practice — Warm
W1. z-score for 95% CI?


Full solution

1.96.

W2. SE of the sample mean if \sigma = 20, n = 400?


Full solution

1.

W3. To halve a CI width, multiply n by?


Full solution

4.



Practice — Working
P1. \sigma = 8, n = 64, \bar X = 50. 95% CI?


Full solution

SE = 1. Margin = 1.96. CI [48.04, 51.96].

P2. Test H_0: \mu = 100 vs H_1: \mu \neq 100. Sample: n =
100, \bar X = 102, \sigma = 10. z? p-value? Reject at \alpha =
0.05?


Full solution

z = (102 - 100)/(10/10) = 2. Two-sided p \approx 0.046. Reject.

P3. Sample size needed for 95% CI of width 2 when \sigma = 10?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Width = 2 \cdot 1.96 \cdot 10/\sqrt{n} \le 2. Solve for n.



Full solution

n \ge (1.96 \cdot 10)^2 = 384.16. At least 385.



Practice — Stretch
S1. The replication crisis. Many published scientific results fail to replicate. Reasons include p-hacking, low power, publication bias. Why do small samples and p < 0.05 produce so many false positives?


Full solution

With many studies, false positives accumulate. If 1000 papers test true H_0s with \alpha = 0.05, about 50 will reject (false positives). Combined with publication bias (significant results get published), much of the “literature” can be wrong.
Low power compounds this: small samples often miss true effects and erratically report extreme estimates when they do detect.
Modern practice: pre-register hypotheses, use larger samples, report effect sizes (not just p-values), require replication. Bayesian approaches reframe inference (in terms of posterior beliefs, not p-values).

S2. Statistical significance vs practical significance. A study finds a new drug increases recovery time by 0.2 days, p =
0.001. Is this important?


Full solution

Not necessarily. p-value tests whether an effect exists, not its size or practical importance. With huge samples, even tiny effects become statistically significant.
Always report:

Effect size (e.g., “0.2 days improvement”).
Confidence interval (e.g., “0.18–0.22 days, 95% CI”).
Practical context (clinically meaningful threshold?).

A good rule: p-value answers “is there evidence of any effect?”; magnitude and uncertainty answer “does it matter?”.
The p-value-only culture has been a major problem in clinical research, social science, and now ML benchmarking.



Common confusions

“95% CI = 95% probability the parameter is in the interval.” Frequentist CIs aren’t probability statements about parameters (which are fixed). The 95% refers to repeated procedures.
“p < 0.05 proves the effect is real.” No — it shows the data is unlikely under H_0. Could be: real effect; type I error; wrong test; biased sample.
“n = 30 is enough.” Sample sizes should match the problem. For tiny effects or skewed distributions, much larger samples are needed.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A confidence interval is a guarantee about the recipe, not about the particular interval — the parameter is fixed, the interval is random.
A p-value is the probability of seeing data this extreme if the null hypothesis were true; it does not tell you that an effect is real or important.
Quadrupling your sample size halves your interval width — so precision costs data quadratically, not linearly.



Self-Assessment

Could you state the meaning of a 95% confidence interval correctly?
Without scrolling back, what’s the z-multiplier for 99% CI?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: \approx 2.58.)

3 yes: ready for 11 · Sets, Logic, Proof.
2 yes: redo S2 (statistical vs practical significance).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P2 and P5.

title: 10 · Statistical Inference
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

09 · Variance & CLT — CLT enables CI / test computations.



What this unlocks

Tier 7 — Bayesian Inference — the alternative inferential paradigm.



Where this matters

AI / Machine Learning. A/B testing for ML systems (online learning, recommendation). Significance testing of model accuracy differences. Bandits and sequential testing.


Science & medicine. Clinical trials, replication, scientific methodology — all built on this framework. Replication crisis (S1) shows the consequences of misuse.


Business analytics. Marketing experiments, pricing changes, customer-segmentation tests — daily decisions hinge on statistical inference.

text:

Can you compute \binom{n}{k} (n choose k)?
