title: Ch 1 — Relations and Functions (Types)
text:
Can you list the elements of a Cartesian product A \times B when A and B are small finite sets?
Do you remember that a function from A to B assigns exactly one element of B to each element of A?
Do “domain”, “codomain”, and “range” feel familiar? (Class 11, Ch 2.)

If you said no to any, revisit Class 11 · Ch 2 — Relations and Functions.

title: Ch 1 — Relations and Functions (Types)
text:
Touch
Below is a tiny social network of three friends — Asha, Bina, Chetan — and the relation “is the same age as”.





Asha
Bina
Chetan




Asha
✓
✓
✗


Bina
✓
✓
✗


Chetan
✗
✗
✓




Toggle a few cells in your head:

Every person is the same age as themselves — the diagonal is all ✓.
If Asha is the same age as Bina, then Bina is the same age as Asha — the table is mirror-symmetric about the diagonal.
If Asha is the same age as Bina and Bina is the same age as Chetan, then Asha must be the same age as Chetan — the relation chains through.

Now redraw the table for “is taller than”. The diagonal is empty (you aren’t taller than yourself). The mirror symmetry is broken. The chaining still works.
That’s the whole lesson hiding in two tables.

title: Ch 1 — Relations and Functions (Types)
text:
Wonder

Which of the two tables looks “neater”? Why?
Could a relation be its own mirror image but fail to chain through?
If a relation chains and mirrors and has a full diagonal, what does the set get sliced into?
For a function f : A \to B, when do two different inputs produce the same output — and when does that not happen?

title: Ch 1 — Relations and Functions (Types)
text:
Discover
Let A = \{1, 2, 3\}. Treat each problem as a small puzzle.

Problem 1
Define R_1 = \{(a,b) : a = b\} on A. Write out every pair in R_1. Does the diagonal of the 3 \times 3 table have any blanks?


Hint 1 — a nudge

a = b means exactly the diagonal pairs.



Full solution

R_1 = \{(1,1), (2,2), (3,3)\}. Full diagonal, nothing off it. Every element relates to itself.



Problem 2
Define R_2 = \{(a,b) : a < b\} on A. Write out the pairs. Is (2,2) in there? Is (1,2) in but (2,1) out?


Hint 1 — a nudge

List all ordered pairs (a,b) from A \times A where a < b holds.



Full solution

R_2 = \{(1,2), (1,3), (2,3)\}. Diagonal empty. The table is not mirror-symmetric: (1,2) \in R_2 but (2,1) \notin R_2.



Problem 3
Define R_3 = \{(a,b) : a + b \text{ is even}\} on A = \{1,2,3\}. List the pairs. Check three things: is (a,a) always in? Is the table mirror-symmetric? If (a,b) and (b,c) are in, is (a,c) in?


Hint 1 — a nudge

a+b is even iff a and b are both odd or both even.



Full solution

R_3 = \{(1,1), (2,2), (3,3), (1,3), (3,1)\}. Diagonal full ✓. Mirror symmetric ✓ (because (1,3) and (3,1) both appear). Chaining: (1,3),
(3,1) gives (1,1) ✓; (3,1),(1,3) gives (3,3) ✓.
This relation slices A into two groups: \{1,3\} (odd) and \{2\} (even). Watch for that pattern in Name.



Problem 4
Define f : A \to A by f(1) = 2,\ f(2) = 3,\ f(3) = 1. Are any two different inputs sent to the same output? Does every element of A appear as some f(x)?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Read the three arrows. Note the outputs.



Full solution

Outputs are 2, 3, 1 — all different. So no two inputs share an output. And every element of A appears as an output. The arrow diagram is a permutation — a perfect pairing of inputs and outputs.



Problem 5
Define g : A \to A by g(1) = 2,\ g(2) = 2,\ g(3) = 3. Now answer the same two questions.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Look at the outputs again.



Full solution

Outputs 2, 2, 3. The element 1 never appears as g(x). And 1 and 2 both go to 2 — different inputs, same output. Two ways this g fails to be the “perfect pairing” you saw with f.

You have just felt the four important properties: a relation can be reflexive (full diagonal), symmetric (mirror), transitive (chaining); a function can be one-one (no two inputs share an output) and onto (every element of the codomain is hit).

The “two infinite sets are the same size when there is a bijection between them” idea was Cantor’s bombshell. In 1874 he showed the integers can be put into one-one correspondence with the rationals (a bijection \mathbb{N} \to
\mathbb{Q} exists) — so they’re the same “size” of infinity. Then in 1891 his diagonal argument proved no bijection \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{R} can exist — the reals are a strictly larger infinity. Two innocent words in this chapter, one-one and onto, are exactly the levers Cantor used to discover that infinity comes in different sizes.


The three relation properties and the two function properties are about the same question, asked twice: “do the arrows behave well?” An equivalence relation makes the arrows tile the set into clean batches; a bijection makes the arrows pair up perfectly with no leftovers and no doubles. Reflexive + symmetric + transitive = clean partition. One-one + onto = perfect pairing. Both are forms of no waste, no collision.

title: Ch 1 — Relations and Functions (Types)
text:
Name

Let R be a relation on a set A. Then R is

reflexive if (a,a) \in R for every a \in A,
symmetric if (a,b) \in R \Rightarrow (b,a) \in R,
transitive if (a,b),(b,c) \in R \Rightarrow (a,c) \in R.

A relation that is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive is called an equivalence relation. It partitions A into disjoint equivalence classes.
A function f : A \to B is

one-one (injective) if f(a_1) = f(a_2) \Rightarrow a_1 = a_2,
onto (surjective) if for every b \in B there exists a \in A with f(a) = b,
bijective if it is both one-one and onto.


A verse to memorise, in the spirit of Bhāskara’s Līlāvatī:

Diagonal full — call it reflexive; Mirror across — symmetric, alive; Chain a to c through b — transitive, true; Three together: one class for each crew. No two share — one-one is plain; Every target hit — onto’s domain; Both at once — bijection’s name.

title: Ch 1 — Relations and Functions (Types)
text:
Apply

Worked example

Let \mathbb{Z} be the set of integers. Define R on \mathbb{Z} by a R b \iff a - b \text{ is divisible by } 5. Show that R is an equivalence relation and find the equivalence class of 0.

A competent solver thinks like this:

Reflexive. a - a = 0, divisible by 5. ✓
Symmetric. If 5 \mid (a - b), then 5 \mid (b - a). ✓
Transitive. If 5 \mid (a-b) and 5 \mid (b-c), add the two: 5 \mid (a-c). ✓
Class of 0. Integers b with b - 0 divisible by 5, i.e. b \in \{\ldots, -10, -5, 0, 5, 10, \ldots\} — all multiples of 5.

Answer: R is an equivalence relation; [0] = 5\mathbb{Z}.


Practice — Warm
W1. On A = \{1, 2, 3\}, the relation R = \{(1,1), (2,2)\} — is it reflexive? symmetric? transitive?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Check each property in turn against the definition.



Full solution

Not reflexive — (3,3) is missing. Symmetric — every pair is its own mirror. Transitive — vacuously: there’s no (a,b),(b,c) to chain.

W2. Is f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R},\ f(x) = 2x + 3 one-one? onto?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Solve f(a) = f(b) for a, b. Then ask: for any y, does f(x) = y have a real solution?



Full solution

2a + 3 = 2b + 3 \Rightarrow a = b, so one-one. Given y \in
\mathbb{R}, set x = (y-3)/2 — real. So onto. Hence bijective.

W3. Is f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R},\ f(x) = x^2 one-one? onto?


Full solution

f(2) = f(-2) = 4 — not one-one. Outputs are \geq 0 — -1 is never hit, not onto.



Practice — Working
P1. Let T be the set of triangles in a plane. Define R on T by T_1 R T_2 \iff T_1 \text{ is similar to } T_2. Show R is an equivalence relation. (NCERT Ch 1.)


Hint 1 — a nudge

Check the three axioms: a triangle is similar to itself; if T_1 similar to T_2 then so back; chain through T_2.



Hint 2 — the technique

Each step rests on a basic fact about similarity.



Full solution

Reflexive. T \sim T via the identity correspondence. Symmetric. Similarity is symmetric — if angles match and sides are in proportion k, the reverse uses ratio 1/k. Transitive. Compose the two similarity ratios. ✓ All three. Hence equivalence relation.

P2. Let f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}, f(x) = x^3. Show f is bijective.


Hint 1 — a nudge

For one-one, suppose a^3 = b^3.



Full solution

One-one. a^3 = b^3 \Rightarrow a = b (cubing is strictly increasing on \mathbb{R}). Onto. For any y \in \mathbb{R}, x = y^{1/3} is real and f(x) = y. ✓

P3. Let A = \{1,2,3,4,5\}. The relation R = \{(a,b) : |a-b| =
2\}. Is R an equivalence relation? If not, which property fails?


Full solution

Not reflexive — |a-a| = 0 \ne 2. So R is not an equivalence relation. (Symmetric ✓; transitive fails: (1,3),(3,5) \in R but (1,5) \notin R.)



Practice — Stretch
S1. Let f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N} be defined by f(n) = n+1. Is f one-one? Is f onto?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Same domain and codomain — \mathbb{N} = \{1, 2, 3, \ldots\}. Which n \in \mathbb{N} is hit?



Hint 2 — the technique

Try to solve f(n) = 1.



Full solution

One-one. n_1 + 1 = n_2 + 1 \Rightarrow n_1 = n_2. ✓ Not onto. f(n) = 1 requires n = 0 \notin \mathbb{N}. So 1 is never hit. A function from an infinite set to itself can be one-one without being onto — a key point.

S2. Show that the number of equivalence relations on a set with 3 elements is 5. (Hint: count partitions.)


Full solution

Equivalence relations on A correspond to partitions of A. For A = \{a,b,c\}: 1. \{a\},\{b\},\{c\} (all singletons) 2. \{a,b\},\{c\} 3. \{a,c\},\{b\} 4. \{b,c\},\{a\} 5. \{a,b,c\} (one big block)
5 partitions, 5 equivalence relations.



Board-Pattern Practice
1-mark MCQ #1. Let R be a relation on \mathbb{Z} defined by aRb \iff a = -b. Then R is: (a) reflexive (b) symmetric (c) transitive (d) equivalence.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Check each axiom on a few integers.



Full solution

0R0 ✓, but 1R(-1) does not give 1R1 (since 1 \ne -1). Not reflexive in general — wait: aRa \iff a = -a \iff a = 0. So not reflexive. Symmetric ✓. Not transitive: 1R(-1), (-1)R1 but 1R1 would need 1=-1. (b) symmetric.

1-mark MCQ #2. f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}, f(x) = x^2 + 1. Then f is: (a) one-one onto (b) one-one not onto (c) not one-one onto (d) not one-one not onto.


Full solution

f(1) = f(-1) = 2, not one-one. Range [1, \infty), not all of \mathbb{R}, not onto. (d).

2-mark. Show that the relation R on \mathbb{R} defined by aRb \iff a \le b^2 is neither reflexive, nor symmetric, nor transitive.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Find a single counterexample for each.



Full solution

Not reflexive: a = 1/2 gives 1/2 \le 1/4? No. Not symmetric: -1 \le 0^2 = 0 ✓, but 0 \le (-1)^2 = 1 ✓ — both hold here, try harder: take a = 1, b = 2. 1 \le 4 ✓ but 2 \le 1 ✗. Not transitive: a=2, b=-2, c=1. 2 \le 4 ✓, -2 \le 1 ✓, but 2 \le 1? ✗.

2-mark. Let f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}, f(n) = 2n. Show f is one-one but not onto.


Full solution

2n_1 = 2n_2 \Rightarrow n_1 = n_2 — one-one. Range = \{2, 4, 6,
\ldots\}, the odd naturals are missed — not onto.

3-mark. Let A = \{1,2,3,\ldots,9\} and let R on A \times A be defined by (a,b)R(c,d) \iff a + d = b + c. Show R is an equivalence relation.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Reflexive: a + b = b + a ✓ trivially. Symmetric: swap. Transitive: add two equations and cancel.



Full solution

Reflexive: (a,b)R(a,b) since a+b = b+a ✓. Symmetric: (a,b)R(c,d) \Rightarrow a+d = b+c \Rightarrow c+b = d+a
\Rightarrow (c,d)R(a,b) ✓. Transitive: (a,b)R(c,d) and (c,d)R(e,f) give a+d = b+c and c+f = d+e. Add: a+d+c+f = b+c+d+e \Rightarrow a+f = b+e \Rightarrow
(a,b)R(e,f) ✓.

3-mark. Let f : \mathbb{R} - \{2\} \to \mathbb{R} - \{1\}, f(x) = \dfrac{x-3}{x-2}. Show f is bijective.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Solve y = (x-3)/(x-2) for x in terms of y.



Full solution

y(x-2) = x-3 \Rightarrow yx - 2y = x - 3 \Rightarrow x(y-1) = 2y - 3
\Rightarrow x = \frac{2y-3}{y-1}. Defined for y \ne 1, gives a unique x \ne 2. Hence one-one and onto.

5-mark long answer. Show that the function f : \mathbb{R} \to
\mathbb{R} defined by f(x) = \dfrac{x}{1+|x|} is one-one. What is its range?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Split into x \ge 0 and x < 0 to handle |x|.



Hint 2 — the technique

For one-one, examine each case and the boundary.



Full solution

For x \ge 0: f(x) = x/(1+x), increasing from 0 to (almost) 1. For x < 0: f(x) = x/(1-x), increasing from (almost) -1 to 0. f is strictly increasing on all of \mathbb{R}, hence one-one. Range = (-1, 1). (Not onto \mathbb{R}, but onto (-1,1).)

6-mark Case Study. Family relations as a relation on a set.
A school maintains a database of n students. Define R on the set of students by aRb \iff a and b have the same year of birth. Answer:

Show R is an equivalence relation. (2 marks)
If 5 students were born in 2007, 4 in 2008, and 3 in 2009, how many ordered pairs lie in R? (2 marks)
Why does this partition the school neatly into class batches? (2 marks)



Hint 1 — a nudge

Equivalence classes = birth-year groups. Pairs within each class.



Full solution

(i) Reflexive: same year as self. Symmetric: same year is symmetric. Transitive: same year chains through. ✓
(ii) Pairs within each class: 5^2 + 4^2 + 3^2 = 25 + 16 + 9 = 50 ordered pairs.
(iii) Every student belongs to exactly one birth-year class — the classes partition the school into disjoint batches that exhaust the roster. Equivalence classes are exactly what “batch” means.







NCERT Exemplar pointer: Ex 1.1 (full set), Ex 1.2 Q5, 7, 9. Previous-year pointer: CBSE 2023 Set 1 Q19 (similar to 3-mark (a,b)R(c,d) problem); CBSE 2022 Q21.





Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

An equivalence relation — reflexive, symmetric, transitive — slices a set into disjoint equivalence classes, and every “is the same as” rule in mathematics is one in disguise.
A function is one-one when no two inputs collide, onto when no output is missed; bijective means both — perfect pairing of inputs with outputs.
“Onto” is always relative to the declared codomain, so the same rule can be onto or not depending on which target set you write down.



Common confusions

“A relation that is symmetric and transitive must be reflexive.” No — there’s a famous trap. If R is symmetric and transitive, you can almost prove aRb \Rightarrow aRa, but only if there exists some b with aRb. An element with no partners has no diagonal pair forced. Example: A = \{1,2\}, R = \{(2,2)\} is symmetric and transitive but not reflexive.
“Onto means the function covers \mathbb{R}.” No — onto is relative to the codomain declared. f : \mathbb{R} \to [1,\infty), f(x) = x^2 + 1, is onto. It’s the same rule as before; the codomain changed.
“One-one functions are always invertible on their codomain.” Only when the function is also onto. Inverse needs bijection. We meet this carefully in the next lesson.



Self-Assessment
Be honest with yourself.

Could you state the three properties of an equivalence relation, and give a one-line counterexample for each, without notes?
Did you solve at least 5 of the 7 board-pattern problems with at most one hint?
Can you explain the difference between “one-one” and “onto” using a small arrow diagram?


3 yes: move on to Inverse Trigonometric Functions.
2 yes: redo the Stretch and 5-mark problems.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover and Apply.

title: Ch 1 — Relations and Functions (Types)
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

cbse11.relations-and-functions — Cartesian products, the idea of a function.



What this unlocks

cbse12.inverse-trig-functions — needs bijectivity to invert.
cbse12.matrices-intro — matrices are functions \{1,\ldots,m\} \times \{1,\ldots,n\} \to \mathbb{R}.



NCERT chapter alignment

Class 12, Chapter 1 — Relations and Functions (NCERT 2023 rationalised syllabus). Covers Sections 1.1–1.3: types of relations, types of functions.
Sister synthesis lessons: t2.functions-as-machines and t5.sets-logic-proofs.
Read alongside the NCERT textbook, which remains the canonical source for the board paper.



Where this matters

Databases. A primary key is exactly an injective function from rows to identifiers. Joining two tables on a key is composition of functions. SQL’s GROUP BY partitions a table into equivalence classes — the same mathematical move you just made.


AI/ML. Embedding a categorical feature is a function from a finite set of labels to a vector space; you want it injective so different labels stay distinguishable in the model.


Research. Equivalence classes appear everywhere — modular arithmetic in number theory, homotopy classes in topology, similarity classes of matrices in linear algebra. The pattern partition by an equivalence relation is a load-bearing tool.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones: Gelfand (problem-first Discover), Bhāskara (compact Name with verse), AoPS (graduated practice).
All forward references are to declared Unlocks.

title: Ch 6a — Application of Derivatives (Tangents, Normals, Rates)
text:
Can you compute \frac{dy}{dx} for a polynomial in 30 seconds?
Do you remember that slope of tangent = \frac{dy}{dx}|_{x = a}?
Have you done Ch 5b?
crumbs: Ch 6a — Application of Derivatives (Tangents, Normals, Rates)

title: Ch 6a — Application of Derivatives (Tangents, Normals, Rates)
text:
Touch
A balloon is inflated. Its radius r grows with time. At a moment when r = 5 cm and r is increasing at 2 cm/s, how fast is the surface area growing?

Surface area S = 4\pi r^2. The chain links two rates: \frac{dS}{dt} = \frac{dS}{dr} \cdot \frac{dr}{dt} = (8\pi r)(2) =
16\pi r. At r = 5: \frac{dS}{dt} = 80\pi \approx 251 cm²/s.

That’s a related-rates problem. Two quantities both depend on time; you know one rate and want the other.
Now think geometrically. The graph of y = x^2 at the point (2, 4) has tangent slope f'(2) = 4. The line through (2, 4) with slope 4 is the tangent. Its perpendicular through that same point is the normal.
Three uses of the derivative in this lesson: - slope of tangent and normal to a curve, - rate of change of one variable in terms of another, - whether a function is increasing or decreasing on an interval.
crumbs: Ch 6a — Application of Derivatives (Tangents, Normals, Rates)

title: Ch 6a — Application of Derivatives (Tangents, Normals, Rates)
text:
Wonder

If \frac{dy}{dx} at a point is positive, what does that say about the curve there?
The normal is perpendicular to the tangent — so its slope is…?
How does “the function is increasing on [a, b]” translate into a condition on f'?
crumbs: Ch 6a — Application of Derivatives (Tangents, Normals, Rates)

title: Ch 6a — Application of Derivatives (Tangents, Normals, Rates)
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Find the slope of the tangent to y = x^3 - 3x + 2 at x = 2.


Full solution

y' = 3x^2 - 3. At x = 2: y' = 9.



Problem 2
Find the equation of the tangent to y = x^2 at (1, 1).


Full solution

Slope = 2 at x = 1. Tangent: y - 1 = 2(x - 1) \Rightarrow y = 2x -
1.



Problem 3
Find the equation of the normal to the curve in Problem 2 at the same point.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Normal slope = -1/(\text{tangent slope}).



Full solution

Normal slope = -1/2. Normal: y - 1 = -\frac{1}{2}(x - 1) \Rightarrow
x + 2y = 3.



Problem 4
A spherical bubble has radius r = 3 cm growing at 0.5 cm/s. How fast is its volume changing?


Hint 1 — a nudge

V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3. Differentiate w.r.t. t.



Full solution

\frac{dV}{dt} = 4\pi r^2 \cdot \frac{dr}{dt} = 4\pi(9)(0.5) = 18\pi cm³/s.



Problem 5
For what values of x is f(x) = x^3 - 3x^2 increasing?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Increasing where f'(x) > 0.



Full solution

f'(x) = 3x^2 - 6x = 3x(x - 2). f' > 0 when x < 0 or x > 2. Increasing on (-\infty, 0) \cup (2, \infty), decreasing on (0, 2).


Newton derives Kepler’s laws — Principia, 1687. Isaac Newton’s Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica did the unthinkable: it took Kepler’s three observational laws of planetary motion (1609 and 1619) and derived them from a single rate-of-change postulate — F = ma with an inverse-square gravitational pull. The whole argument is a related-rates calculation. Position depends on time; velocity is the time-derivative of position; acceleration is the time-derivative of velocity; Newton showed that “centripetal acceleration toward the sun, varying as 1/r^2” reproduces the elliptical orbits Kepler had fitted to Tycho Brahe’s data. Every related-rates problem you solve in this lesson — balloon, ladder, conical sandpile — is a tiny version of the calculation that explained the solar system.


The derivative is a translator between rates. A tangent line carries “slope at a point”; the same number f'(a) also carries “how fast y changes per unit change in x.” When both x and y depend on time, the chain rule glues their rates: \dfrac{dy}{dt} = f'(x) \cdot \dfrac{dx}{dt}. Every related rates problem in the chapter is one application of that identity dressed in different words — balloon, ladder, sandpile, shadow.
crumbs: Ch 6a — Application of Derivatives (Tangents, Normals, Rates)

title: Ch 6a — Application of Derivatives (Tangents, Normals, Rates)
text:
Name

Let y = f(x) and let P = (a, f(a)).

Slope of tangent at P = f'(a). Tangent line: y - f(a) =
f'(a)(x - a).
Slope of normal at P = -1/f'(a) (when f'(a) \ne 0). Normal line: y - f(a) = -\frac{1}{f'(a)}(x - a).
If f'(a) = 0, tangent is horizontal; normal is vertical.

Rates of change. If y depends on t and x depends on t, the rates are linked by \dfrac{dy}{dt} = \dfrac{dy}{dx} \cdot \dfrac{dx}{
dt}.
Increasing / decreasing. f is increasing on [a, b] iff f'(x)
\ge 0 for x \in [a, b], decreasing iff f'(x) \le 0 there. Strictly increasing requires f'(x) > 0 except at isolated points.

A verse:

Slope of tangent — that’s f'(a); Negative reciprocal — the normal’s way. Linked rates by chain — from one stream to another; f' > 0 climbs, f' < 0 falls — that’s the rule.
crumbs: Ch 6a — Application of Derivatives (Tangents, Normals, Rates)

title: Ch 6a — Application of Derivatives (Tangents, Normals, Rates)
text:
Apply

Worked example

Find the equations of the tangent and normal to y = x^3 - 6x^2 +
13x - 10 at x = 1.

A competent solver:

f(1) = 1 - 6 + 13 - 10 = -2. Point: (1, -2).
f'(x) = 3x^2 - 12x + 13. f'(1) = 4.
Tangent: y + 2 = 4(x - 1) \Rightarrow y = 4x - 6.
Normal: y + 2 = -\frac{1}{4}(x - 1) \Rightarrow x + 4y + 7 = 0.



Practice — Warm
W1. Slope of tangent to y = \sin x at x = \pi/3.


Full solution

\cos(\pi/3) = 1/2.

W2. A ladder slides down a wall. Bottom moves outward at 2 m/s. When bottom is 3 m from wall and ladder is 5 m, how fast is top descending?


Hint 1 — a nudge

x^2 + y^2 = 25. \frac{dx}{dt} = 2. Find \frac{dy}{dt} at x = 3.



Full solution

At x = 3, y = 4. 2x x' + 2y y' = 0 \Rightarrow 6\cdot2 + 8 y' =
0 \Rightarrow y' = -3/2. Top descends at 1.5 m/s.

W3. Show f(x) = x^2 + 1 is increasing on (0, \infty).


Full solution

f'(x) = 2x > 0 for x > 0. ✓



Practice — Working
P1. Find points on y = x^3 - 11x + 5 where the tangent is y = x - 11.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Tangent slope is 1. Solve f'(x) = 1.



Full solution

3x^2 - 11 = 1 \Rightarrow x^2 = 4 \Rightarrow x = \pm 2. At x = 2: y = 8 - 22 + 5 = -9. Tangent at (2, -9) has slope 1: y + 9 = (x -
2) \Rightarrow y = x - 11 ✓. At x = -2: y = -8 + 22 + 5 = 19. Tangent: y = x + 21, not y = x - 11. Only (2, -9).

P2. Sand is being poured forming a conical pile. Height h equals \frac{1}{6} of base radius r. Sand falls at 12 cm³/s. How fast does h rise when h = 4 cm?


Hint 1 — a nudge

V = \frac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h with r = 6h. So V = 12\pi h^3.



Full solution

\frac{dV}{dt} = 36\pi h^2 \cdot \frac{dh}{dt}. At h = 4: 12 = 36\pi
\cdot 16 \cdot \frac{dh}{dt} \Rightarrow \frac{dh}{dt} = \frac{1}{
48\pi} cm/s.

P3. Find intervals on which f(x) = 2x^3 - 9x^2 + 12x + 30 is strictly increasing / decreasing.


Full solution

f'(x) = 6x^2 - 18x + 12 = 6(x-1)(x-2). f' > 0 on (-\infty, 1) \cup
(2, \infty) — increasing. f' < 0 on (1, 2) — decreasing.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Show that the tangent to y = bx^a at any point cuts the y-axis at a height that is -(a - 1) times the y-coordinate of the point.


Hint 1 — a nudge

General point (x_0, bx_0^a). Slope abx_0^{a-1}. Tangent equation; set x = 0.



Full solution

Tangent: y - bx_0^a = abx_0^{a-1}(x - x_0). At x = 0: y = bx_0^a - abx_0^a = bx_0^a(1 - a) = -(a-1) \cdot bx_0^a. ✓



Board-Pattern Practice
1-mark MCQ #1. Slope of tangent to y = e^x at (0, 1) is (a) 0 (b) 1 (c) e (d) 1/e.


Full solution

y' = e^x. At x = 0: 1. (b).

1-mark MCQ #2. f(x) = x^2 - 4 is decreasing on (a) (0, \infty) (b) (-\infty, 0) (c) \mathbb{R} (d) nowhere.


Full solution

f'(x) = 2x < 0 for x < 0. (b) (-\infty, 0).

2-mark. Find slope of normal to x = a\cos^3 t,\ y = a\sin^3 t at t = \pi/4.


Full solution

\frac{dy}{dx} = -\tan t. At t = \pi/4: slope of tangent = -1, so slope of normal = 1.

2-mark. A particle moves on y = x^2. When does \frac{dy}{dt} =
\frac{dx}{dt}?


Full solution

\frac{dy}{dt} = 2x \frac{dx}{dt}. So 2x = 1 \Rightarrow x = 1/2.

3-mark. Find the point on y = x^2 where the tangent is parallel to y = 4x + 5.


Full solution

y' = 2x = 4 \Rightarrow x = 2, y = 4. Point (2, 4).

3-mark. A water tank is in the shape of an inverted cone with semi- vertical angle 30°. Water flows in at 5 m³/min. Find the rate of rise of water-level when water is 2 m deep.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\tan 30° = r/h, so r = h/\sqrt{3}. V = \frac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h.



Full solution

V = \frac{1}{3}\pi \cdot h^2/3 \cdot h = \pi h^3/9. \frac{dV}{dt}
= \pi h^2/3 \cdot \frac{dh}{dt}. At h = 2: 5 = 4\pi/3 \cdot
\frac{dh}{dt} \Rightarrow \frac{dh}{dt} = 15/(4\pi) m/min.

5-mark long answer. A man 1.6 m tall walks away from a streetlight 5 m above ground at 1.2 m/s. (i) At what rate does the tip of his shadow move? (ii) At what rate does his shadow lengthen?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Similar triangles: tip of shadow s, his position x. Then s/(s -
x) = 5/1.6.



Full solution

Let x = distance from pole, s = distance of shadow-tip from pole. By similar triangles: \frac{1.6}{5} = \frac{s - x}{s}, so 1.6 s = 5
(s - x) \Rightarrow x = (3.4/5) s, equivalently s = (5/3.4)x = (25/17)
x. \frac{ds}{dt} = \frac{25}{17} \cdot 1.2 = \frac{30}{17} \approx 1.76 m/s. Tip moves at 30/17 m/s. Shadow length = s - x = (25/17 - 1)x = (8/17) x. Rate = 8/17 \cdot
1.2 = 9.6/17 \approx 0.56 m/s.

6-mark Case Study. Inflating a balloon for a campaign.
A spherical helium balloon is being inflated. Volume rises at V'(t) =
100\pi cm³/s.

Express radius r as a function of V. (1 mark)
Find \frac{dr}{dt} when V = 36\pi cm³. (3 marks)
The balloon’s surface is being printed at the same rate. Find \frac{dS}{dt} at the same moment. (2 marks)



Full solution

(i) V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \Rightarrow r = (3V/(4\pi))^{1/3}. (ii) V = 36\pi \Rightarrow r = (108\pi/(4\pi))^{1/3} = 27^{1/3} =
3 cm. \frac{dV}{dt} = 4\pi r^2 \frac{dr}{dt} \Rightarrow 100\pi =
36\pi \cdot \frac{dr}{dt} \Rightarrow \frac{dr}{dt} = 25/9 cm/s. (iii) S = 4\pi r^2, \frac{dS}{dt} = 8\pi r \frac{dr}{dt} = 8\pi
\cdot 3 \cdot 25/9 = 200\pi/3 cm²/s.







NCERT Exemplar pointer: Ex 6.1, 6.2, 6.3. Previous-year pointer: CBSE 2023 Set 2 Q27 (5-mark related rates); CBSE 2022 Q19.





Common confusions

“Tangent and normal at (a, b) have the same slope.” No — they are perpendicular. Slopes multiply to -1.
“f' = 0 means horizontal tangent and you’re done.” It only identifies a critical point — the next lesson tells you whether it’s a max, min, or neither.
“f is increasing iff f' \ge 0 at one point.” Need f' \ge 0 throughout the interval, not at one point.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

Tangent slope is f'(a); normal slope is -1/f'(a). Same point, perpendicular lines, slopes multiply to -1.
Related rates is the chain rule with t as the hidden parameter. Differentiate the geometric relation, plug in known rates, solve for the unknown one.
Sign of f' tells you the climb. Positive: increasing. Negative: decreasing. Zero only at isolated points: still strictly monotone there.



Self-Assessment

Could you find tangent and normal to a polynomial curve in 5 minutes?
Did you solve at least 5 of 7 board items unaided?
Can you set up a related-rates problem from a word description?


3 yes: move on.
2 yes: redo the 5-mark problem.
0–1 yes: redo Problem 4 with a fresh balloon example.
crumbs: Ch 6a — Application of Derivatives (Tangents, Normals, Rates)

title: Ch 6a — Application of Derivatives (Tangents, Normals, Rates)
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

cbse12.differentiability-and-rules — chain rule especially for related rates.



What this unlocks

cbse12.maxima-minima — what are the critical points good for?



NCERT chapter alignment

Class 12, Chapter 6 (NCERT 2023). Sections 6.1–6.3.
Sister synthesis lessons: t4.related-rates.
Read alongside the NCERT textbook.



Where this matters

Game development. Velocity vectors are tangent vectors; collision normals come from surface tangent planes. Both are derivatives in disguise.


Research / engineering. Newton’s second law F = m \frac{dv}{dt} is a related-rates statement. Heat-transfer rates, fluid flow, chemical kinetics — all use chain-of-rates.


AI/ML. Increasing/decreasing intervals correspond to monotone ranges of activation functions, which determine network expressivity.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones: Better Explained (“derivative as rate”), Bhāskara (verse), Gelfand (related rates problem-first).
crumbs: Ch 6a — Application of Derivatives (Tangents, Normals, Rates)

title: Ch 4b — Matrix Inverse and Cramer’s Rule
text:
Have you done Ch 4a — Determinants?
Comfortable expanding a 3\times3 determinant?
Have you solved a pair of linear equations by elimination at some point?
crumbs: Ch 4b — Matrix Inverse and Cramer's Rule

title: Ch 4b — Matrix Inverse and Cramer’s Rule
text:
Touch
A locker has a 3-digit combination (x, y, z). Three clues:


\begin{aligned}
2x + y + z &= 7 \\
x - y + 2z &= 6 \\
3x + 2y - z &= 4
\end{aligned}


You could solve by elimination — minutes of careful book-keeping. Or package the system as a matrix equation 
\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 & 2 \\ 3 & 2 & -1 \end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{pmatrix} =
\begin{pmatrix} 7 \\ 6 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix},
 or AX = B. If you had a magic operation A^{-1} such that A^{-1} A
= I, then X = A^{-1} B — one matrix multiplication and you’re done.
That magic operation is the inverse. It exists exactly when |A|
\ne 0.
This lesson: - builds A^{-1} from the adjoint (matrix of cofactors), - uses it to solve AX = B, - gives Cramer’s Rule as a determinant-only shortcut.
crumbs: Ch 4b — Matrix Inverse and Cramer's Rule

title: Ch 4b — Matrix Inverse and Cramer’s Rule
text:
Wonder

Why must |A| \ne 0 for an inverse to exist? (Hint: think about the geometry from Ch 4a.)
If |A| = 0, what does that say about the system AX = B? Could there be no solution? Many?
Cramer’s rule expresses each unknown as a ratio of determinants — why might that be slow for large systems but elegant for 2\times2?
crumbs: Ch 4b — Matrix Inverse and Cramer's Rule

title: Ch 4b — Matrix Inverse and Cramer’s Rule
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Let A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 5 & 3 \end{pmatrix}. Compute |A|, the cofactor matrix, the adjoint, and verify A \cdot \text{adj}(A) =
|A| \cdot I.


Hint 1 — a nudge

For a 2\times2 matrix, \text{adj}\begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d
\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{pmatrix}.



Full solution

|A| = 6 - 5 = 1. Cofactors: C_{11} = 3, C_{12} = -5, C_{21} = -1,
C_{22} = 2. Adjoint = transpose of cofactor matrix = \begin{pmatrix}
3 & -1 \\ -5 & 2 \end{pmatrix}. A \cdot \text{adj}(A) = \begin{pmatrix} 2\cdot3 + 1\cdot(-5) & 2\cdot(-1)
+ 1\cdot2 \\ 5\cdot3 + 3\cdot(-5) & 5\cdot(-1) + 3\cdot2 \end{pmatrix}
= \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = |A| \cdot I. ✓



Problem 2
Use Problem 1 to find A^{-1}.


Full solution

A^{-1} = \frac{1}{|A|} \text{adj}(A) = \frac{1}{1}\begin{pmatrix} 3 &
-1 \\ -5 & 2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & -1 \\ -5 & 2
\end{pmatrix}. Verify: A A^{-1} = I. ✓



Problem 3
Solve \begin{cases} 2x + 3y = 8 \\ 5x + 4y = 13 \end{cases} by writing it as AX = B and computing X = A^{-1} B.


Hint 1 — a nudge

A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 3 \\ 5 & 4 \end{pmatrix}. |A| = 8 - 15 =
-7.



Full solution

\text{adj}(A) = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & -3 \\ -5 & 2 \end{pmatrix}, A^{-1} = -\frac{1}{7}\begin{pmatrix} 4 & -3 \\ -5 & 2 \end{pmatrix}. X = A^{-1} B = -\frac{1}{7}\begin{pmatrix} 4 & -3 \\ -5 & 2
\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 8 \\ 13 \end{pmatrix} = -\frac{1}{7}
\begin{pmatrix} 32 - 39 \\ -40 + 26 \end{pmatrix} = -\frac{1}{7}
\begin{pmatrix} -7 \\ -14 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2
\end{pmatrix}. So x = 1, y = 2.



Problem 4
Try Cramer’s rule on the same system: x = \dfrac{D_x}{D},\ y = \dfrac{
D_y}{D} where D = |A|, D_x replaces column 1 of A with B, etc.


Full solution

D = -7. D_x = \begin{vmatrix} 8 & 3 \\ 13 & 4 \end{vmatrix} = 32 -
39 = -7. D_y = \begin{vmatrix} 2 & 8 \\ 5 & 13 \end{vmatrix} = 26 -
40 = -14. x = -7/-7 = 1, y = -14/-7 = 2. ✓ Same answer, faster.



Problem 5
Apply Cramer’s rule to \begin{cases} x + y = 5 \\ 2x + 2y = 10
\end{cases}. What goes wrong?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Compute D first.



Full solution

D = 2 - 2 = 0. Cramer’s rule fails. The two equations are proportional — one gives the same line as the other. Infinitely many solutions (every point on x + y = 5). When |A| = 0, the system has either no solution or infinitely many.


Gabriel Cramer published his rule in 1750 in Introduction à l’Analyse des Lignes Courbes Algébriques — but solving linear systems was not the book’s point. Cramer was studying which algebraic curves of degree n pass through a given set of points, and he ran into a puzzle now called Cramer’s paradox: two cubic curves can intersect in 9 points, yet a cubic is supposed to be determined by 9 points. While unwinding this, he needed a clean formula for “what coefficients make a system of linear equations have a unique solution,” and the rule fell out as a tool — buried in an appendix. The little determinant shortcut you use to solve 2 \times 2 systems was a side-effect of nineteen-year-old Cramer’s homework on cubic curves.


The inverse A^{-1} is the matrix that undoes A — it sends A’s columns back to the basis vectors e_1, \ldots, e_n. That undo exists exactly when A has not crushed any direction flat — i.e. when |A| \ne 0. Both methods in this lesson are then the same idea: “package the undoing as a matrix” (the adjoint approach) or “package the undoing as a ratio of determinants” (Cramer). When |A| = 0, A has flattened space — many points map to one — and no honest undoer can exist.
crumbs: Ch 4b — Matrix Inverse and Cramer's Rule

title: Ch 4b — Matrix Inverse and Cramer’s Rule
text:
Name

For a square matrix A, the adjoint \text{adj}(A) is the transpose of the cofactor matrix.
Key identity. A \cdot \text{adj}(A) = \text{adj}(A) \cdot A = |A|
\cdot I.
If |A| \ne 0, then A is invertible and A^{-1} = \frac{1}{|A|}\, \text{adj}(A).
Solving AX = B: X = A^{-1} B \quad \text{when } |A| \ne 0.
Cramer’s Rule. For AX = B with |A| = D \ne 0, x_i = \frac{D_i}{D}, where D_i is the determinant obtained by replacing column i of A with B.
Consistency cases for AX = B:

|A| \ne 0: unique solution.
|A| = 0 and (\text{adj } A)\,B \ne 0: no solution (inconsistent).
|A| = 0 and (\text{adj } A)\,B = 0: infinite solutions (or none — check by substitution; in CBSE practice, “infinite solutions”).


A verse:

Cofactor, transpose, divide by |A| — the inverse appears. Replace one column with B, ratio of dets — Cramer’s word. Determinant zero? Lines may merge or wander apart; Adjoint times B tells you which path the system charts.
crumbs: Ch 4b — Matrix Inverse and Cramer's Rule

title: Ch 4b — Matrix Inverse and Cramer’s Rule
text:
Apply

Worked example

Solve \begin{cases} x + y + z = 6 \\ \phantom{x +\,} y + 3z = 11 \\
x - 2y + z = 0 \end{cases} using A^{-1}.

A competent solver:

Set up. A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & -2 & 1
\end{pmatrix}, X = \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{pmatrix}, B = \begin{pmatrix} 6 \\ 11 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}. The matrix form is AX = B, and we want X = A^{-1} B.
Determinant. Expand along column 1: |A| = 1\,(1 + 6) - 0 + 1\,(3 - 1) = 7 + 2 = 9. Non-zero, so A^{-1} exists.
Cofactors of A. C_{11} = +(1 + 6) = 7,\ C_{12} = -(0 - 3) = 3,\ C_{13} = +(0 - 1) =
-1 C_{21} = -(1 + 2) = -3,\ C_{22} = +(1 - 1) = 0,\ C_{23} = -(-2 - 1)
= 3 C_{31} = +(3 - 1) = 2,\ C_{32} = -(3 - 0) = -3,\ C_{33} = +(1 - 0) =
1.
Adjoint $= $ transpose of the cofactor matrix: \text{adj}(A) = \begin{pmatrix} 7 & -3 & 2 \\ 3 & 0 & -3 \\ -1 & 3 &
1 \end{pmatrix}.
Inverse. A^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{|A|}\,\text{adj}(A) =
\dfrac{1}{9} \begin{pmatrix} 7 & -3 & 2 \\ 3 & 0 & -3 \\ -1 & 3 & 1
\end{pmatrix}.
Solve. X = A^{-1} B = \dfrac{1}{9}
\begin{pmatrix} 7(6) + (-3)(11) + 2(0) \\ 3(6) + 0(11) + (-3)(0) \\
(-1)(6) + 3(11) + 1(0) \end{pmatrix} = \dfrac{1}{9}
\begin{pmatrix} 9 \\ 18 \\ 27 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2
\\ 3 \end{pmatrix}.
Check. 1 + 2 + 3 = 6 ✓, \ 2 + 9 = 11 ✓, \ 1 - 4 + 3 = 0 ✓.

Answer: x = 1,\ y = 2,\ z = 3.


Practice — Warm
W1. Find A^{-1} for A = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 5 \\ 1 & 2
\end{pmatrix}.


Full solution

|A| = 8 - 5 = 3. \text{adj}(A) = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -5 \\ -1 & 4
\end{pmatrix}. A^{-1} = \frac{1}{3}\begin{pmatrix} 2 & -5 \\ -1 & 4
\end{pmatrix}.

W2. Verify A A^{-1} = I for the matrix in W1.


Full solution

\frac{1}{3}\begin{pmatrix} 4 & 5 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix} 2 & -5 \\ -1 & 4 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{3}
\begin{pmatrix} 8 - 5 & -20 + 20 \\ 2 - 2 & -5 + 8 \end{pmatrix} =
\frac{1}{3}\begin{pmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix} = I. ✓

W3. Use Cramer’s rule: \begin{cases} 3x - 2y = 4 \\ x + y = 3
\end{cases}.


Full solution

D = 3 + 2 = 5. D_x = \begin{vmatrix} 4 & -2 \\ 3 & 1 \end{vmatrix} =
4 + 6 = 10. D_y = \begin{vmatrix} 3 & 4 \\ 1 & 3 \end{vmatrix} = 9 -
4 = 5. x = 2, y = 1.



Practice — Working
P1. Solve by A^{-1}: \begin{cases} x + 2y + z = 8 \\ 2x + y - z = 1 \\ x - y + 2z = 5
\end{cases}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Form A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & -1 & 2
\end{pmatrix}, B = (8, 1, 5)^T, and apply X = A^{-1} B = \tfrac{1}{|A|}\,\text{adj}(A)\,B.



Hint 2 — the technique

Compute |A| first; if non-zero, the cofactor pattern follows the chess-board \begin{pmatrix} + & - & + \\ - & + & - \\ + & - & +
\end{pmatrix} of signs. Don’t forget to transpose the cofactor matrix to get the adjoint.



Full solution

Determinant (expand along row 1): |A| = 1\,(2 - 1) - 2\,(4 + 1) + 1\,(-2 - 1) = 1 - 10 - 3 = -12. Non-zero, so A^{-1} exists.
Cofactors. C_{11} = 2-1 = 1,\ C_{12} = -(4+1) = -5,\ C_{13} = -2-1 = -3 C_{21} = -(4+1) = -5,\ C_{22} = 2-1 = 1,\ C_{23} = -(-1-2) = 3 C_{31} = -2-1 = -3,\ C_{32} = -(-1-2) = 3,\ C_{33} = 1-4 = -3.
Adjoint = transpose of cofactor matrix: \text{adj}(A) = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -5 & -3 \\ -5 & 1 & 3 \\ -3 & 3 &
-3 \end{pmatrix}.
Solve. X = \dfrac{1}{-12}\,\text{adj}(A)\,B where B = (8, 1, 5)^T:
\text{adj}(A)\,B = \begin{pmatrix} 1(8) + (-5)(1) + (-3)(5) \\ -5(8) +
1(1) + 3(5) \\ -3(8) + 3(1) + (-3)(5) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -12
\\ -24 \\ -36 \end{pmatrix}.
X = \dfrac{1}{-12}\begin{pmatrix} -12 \\ -24 \\ -36 \end{pmatrix} =
\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}.
Answer: x = 1,\ y = 2,\ z = 3.
Check. 1 + 4 + 3 = 8 ✓, \ 2 + 2 - 3 = 1 ✓, \ 1 - 2 + 6 = 5 ✓.

P2. Use Cramer’s rule for \begin{cases} 2x + y + 3z = 9 \\ x +
y + z = 6 \\ x - y + z = 2 \end{cases}.


Full solution

D = \begin{vmatrix} 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 & 1 \end{vmatrix}
= 2(1+1) - 1(1-1) + 3(-1-1) = 4 - 0 - 6 = -2. D_x = \begin{vmatrix} 9 & 1 & 3 \\ 6 & 1 & 1 \\ 2 & -1 & 1 \end{vmatrix}
= 9(1+1) - 1(6-2) + 3(-6-2) = 18 - 4 - 24 = -10. D_y = \begin{vmatrix} 2 & 9 & 3 \\ 1 & 6 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \end{vmatrix}
= 2(6-2) - 9(1-1) + 3(2-6) = 8 - 0 - 12 = -4. D_z = \begin{vmatrix} 2 & 1 & 9 \\ 1 & 1 & 6 \\ 1 & -1 & 2 \end{vmatrix}
= 2(2+6) - 1(2-6) + 9(-1-1) = 16 + 4 - 18 = 2. x = 5, y = 2, z = -1. (Check: 10 + 2 - 3 = 9 ✓, 5 + 2 - 1 = 6 ✓, 5 - 2 - 1 = 2 ✓.)

P3. If A is a 3\times3 matrix with |A| = 2, find |\text{adj}(A)|.


Hint 1 — a nudge

|\text{adj}(A)| = |A|^{n-1} for n\times n matrices.



Full solution

|\text{adj}(A)| = |A|^{3-1} = 2^2 = 4.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Show that for invertible A, (A^{-1})^{-1} = A and (AB)^{-1}
= B^{-1} A^{-1}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

For (AB)^{-1}, multiply AB by B^{-1}A^{-1} on the right.



Full solution

(A^{-1})^{-1} = A: A^{-1}\cdot A = I, so A is the inverse of A^{-1}. (AB)(B^{-1}A^{-1}) = A(BB^{-1})A^{-1} = AA^{-1} = I, so (AB)^{-1} = B^{-1}A^{-1}. Note the order swap.



Board-Pattern Practice
1-mark MCQ #1. If A is a non-singular 3\times3 matrix, then |\text{adj}(\text{adj}(A))| = (a) |A| (b) |A|^2 (c) |A|^4 (d) |A|^9.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Apply |\text{adj} X| = |X|^{n-1} twice.



Full solution

|\text{adj}(A)| = |A|^2. |\text{adj}(\text{adj}(A))| =
(|A|^2)^{2} = |A|^4. (c).

1-mark MCQ #2. If A^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & -1 \\ -5 & 2
\end{pmatrix} then A = (a) \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 5 & 3 \end{pmatrix} (b) \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 5 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix} (c) \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1/3 \\ 1/5 & 1/2 \end{pmatrix} (d) none.


Full solution

A = (A^{-1})^{-1}. |A^{-1}| = 6 - 5 = 1. A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1
\\ 5 & 3 \end{pmatrix}. (a).

2-mark. Find inverse of A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 3 \\ 1 & 4
\end{pmatrix}.


Full solution

|A| = 8 - 3 = 5. A^{-1} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 4 & -3 \\ -1 &
2 \end{pmatrix}.

2-mark. For what value of k does the system \begin{cases} kx + 2y
= 5 \\ 3x + 4y = 7 \end{cases} have no unique solution?


Full solution

|A| = 4k - 6 = 0 \Rightarrow k = 3/2.

3-mark. Solve via Cramer: \begin{cases} x + 2y = 7 \\ 2x - y = 4
\end{cases}.


Full solution

D = -1 - 4 = -5. D_x = -7 - 8 = -15. D_y = 4 - 14 = -10. x = 3, y
= 2.

3-mark. If A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & -2 & 1
\end{pmatrix}, find A^{-1} and use it to solve AX = \begin{pmatrix}
6 \\ 11 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}.


Full solution

|A| = 1(1+6) - 1(0-3) + 1(0-1) = 7 + 3 - 1 = 9. Compute cofactors carefully (omitted for brevity); the inverse and the unique solution then come out to X = A^{-1} B. The problem is method-checking: examiner sees A^{-1} computed and then X substituted in.

5-mark long answer. Use the matrix method to solve \begin{cases} 3x - 2y + 3z = 8 \\ 2x + y - z = 1 \\ 4x - 3y + 2z = 4
\end{cases}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Compute |A| first; if non-zero, find A^{-1} via adjoint.



Full solution

A = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & -2 & 3 \\ 2 & 1 & -1 \\ 4 & -3 & 2
\end{pmatrix}. |A| = 3(2 - 3) - (-2)(4 + 4) + 3(-6 - 4) = -3 + 16 - 30 = -17. Cofactors \to adjoint \to A^{-1} = \frac{1}{-17}\text{adj}(A). Then X = A^{-1}B gives x = 1, y = 2, z = 3 (verify: 3 - 4 + 9 = 8 ✓, 2 + 2 - 3 = 1 ✓, 4 - 6 + 6 = 4 ✓).

6-mark Case Study. Tickets at three counters.
A theatre has counters offering Adult, Senior, and Student tickets. On three days, total tickets sold and total revenue (₹) were



Day
A
S
Stu
Revenue




1
2
3
5
1100


2
4
1
2
800


3
3
2
4
1100



Let prices (₹) of A, S, Stu be x, y, z.

Write the matrix equation. (1 mark)
Determine |A| and check the system is uniquely solvable. (2 marks)
Solve for x, y, z. (3 marks)



Full solution

(i) \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 3 & 5 \\ 4 & 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 2 & 4
\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{pmatrix} =
\begin{pmatrix} 1100 \\ 800 \\ 1100 \end{pmatrix}. (ii) |A| = 2(4 - 4) - 3(16 - 6) + 5(8 - 3) = 0 - 30 + 25 = -5 \ne
0 ✓. (iii) Cramer: D_x = \begin{vmatrix} 1100 & 3 & 5 \\ 800 & 1 & 2 \\ 1100 & 2 & 4
\end{vmatrix} = 1100(4-4) - 3(3200 - 2200) + 5(1600 - 1100) = -3000 +
2500 = -500. x = -500/-5 = 100. Similarly y = 150, z = 100. (Adult ₹100, Senior ₹150, Student ₹100.) Verify against the data.







NCERT Exemplar pointer: Ex 4.3, 4.4, 4.5. Previous-year pointer: CBSE 2023 Set 1 Q34 (5-mark inverse + system); CBSE 2022 Q33.





Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A square matrix has an inverse exactly when its determinant is non-zero, and the inverse is built as A^{-1} = \tfrac{1}{|A|}\,\text{adj}(A) — adjoint divided by determinant.
Every system of linear equations AX = B becomes one matrix multiplication X = A^{-1}B when |A| \ne 0 — the elimination steps you used in Class 10 are absorbed into building the inverse once.
Cramer’s rule expresses each unknown as a ratio of determinants: a single-line formula for small systems, but exponentially slow for large ones — practically, computers prefer Gaussian elimination, which scales as n^3 rather than n!.



Common confusions

“A^{-1} exists for every square matrix.” Only when |A| \ne 0.
“(AB)^{-1} = A^{-1} B^{-1}.” Wrong order. (AB)^{-1} = B^{-1}
A^{-1}.
“Cramer’s rule always works.” Only when D \ne 0. When D = 0 you must analyse separately (no solution or infinitely many).



Self-Assessment

Could you compute the inverse of a 3\times3 matrix in 10 minutes from scratch?
Did you solve at least 5 of 7 board-pattern items unaided?
Can you state when a linear system AX = B has unique / no / infinite solutions?


3 yes: move on.
2 yes: redo the 5-mark problem.
0–1 yes: re-read Apply worked example with paper.
crumbs: Ch 4b — Matrix Inverse and Cramer's Rule

title: Ch 4b — Matrix Inverse and Cramer’s Rule
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

cbse12.determinants — |A| enters every formula.
cbse10.pair-linear-equations — the elimination intuition.



What this unlocks

cbse12.continuity — the calculus thread begins.



NCERT chapter alignment

Class 12, Chapter 4 — Determinants (NCERT 2023 rationalised). Sections 4.5–4.6.
Sister synthesis lesson: t5.linear-systems-gaussian.
Read alongside the NCERT textbook.



Where this matters

AI/ML. Solving X^T X \beta = X^T y is the heart of linear regression. In practice you never form the inverse — you factor — but the existence of the inverse is what guarantees a unique fit when features are not collinear.


Game development. Camera and projection matrices are inverted to convert screen-space rays back to world-space rays for picking objects. The inverse must exist; near-singular matrices cause “lost click” bugs.


Research. Cramer’s rule is mostly historical, but the insight that solutions are ratios of polynomials in the data is structurally important — it’s used in symbolic computation and in proofs of existence.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones: Gelfand (problem-first), Bhāskara (verse), Khan Academy (cofactor-then-adjoint procedure).
crumbs: Ch 4b — Matrix Inverse and Cramer's Rule

title: Ch 4a — Determinants
text:
Have you done Ch 3b — Matrix Operations?
Comfortable computing 2\times2 and 3\times3 matrix products?
Do you know the area formula for a parallelogram?

title: Ch 4a — Determinants
text:
Touch
Two vectors \vec{u} = (3, 1) and \vec{v} = (1, 2) start from the origin. Together they span a parallelogram.

The parallelogram has corners O = (0,0), \vec{u} = (3,1), \vec{u}+\vec{v}
= (4,3), \vec{v} = (1,2). Sketch it on graph paper if you can.

You can compute its area in two ways:
Way 1 (geometry): signed area = u_1 v_2 - u_2 v_1 = 3\cdot2 -
1\cdot1 = 5.
Way 2 (formula): Form the matrix \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 2
\end{pmatrix} — vectors as columns. Its determinant is 3\cdot2 -
1\cdot1 = 5.
Same number. The determinant of a 2\times2 matrix is the signed area of the parallelogram its columns span (positive if the columns are oriented counter-clockwise, negative otherwise).
For a 3\times3 matrix, the determinant is the signed volume of the parallelepiped its columns span. For an n\times n matrix, signed n-dimensional volume.
That picture is the lesson. The formula is just a careful way to compute the number.

title: Ch 4a — Determinants
text:
Wonder

What if \vec u and \vec v are parallel — what’s the area? What does the determinant become?
Swap the two columns. Does the area change sign?
Multiply one column by 2. Does the area double?
Add a multiple of one column to the other. What happens to the area?

title: Ch 4a — Determinants
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Compute \begin{vmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \end{vmatrix}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

For \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}, determinant is ad
- bc.



Full solution

4\cdot3 - 2\cdot1 = 10.



Problem 2
Compute \begin{vmatrix} 2 & 4 \\ 3 & 6 \end{vmatrix}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Look at the rows. Are they related?



Full solution

2\cdot6 - 4\cdot3 = 0. Row 2 is 1.5\times row 1 — they’re proportional. Determinant is zero exactly when rows (or columns) are linearly dependent.



Problem 3
Compute \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \\ 7 & 8 & 10
\end{vmatrix} by expansion along the first row.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\det = a_{11} M_{11} - a_{12} M_{12} + a_{13} M_{13}, where M_{ij} is the 2\times2 determinant after deleting row i, column j.



Full solution

M_{11} = \begin{vmatrix} 5 & 6 \\ 8 & 10 \end{vmatrix} = 50 - 48 = 2. M_{12} = \begin{vmatrix} 4 & 6 \\ 7 & 10 \end{vmatrix} = 40 - 42 = -2. M_{13} = \begin{vmatrix} 4 & 5 \\ 7 & 8 \end{vmatrix} = 32 - 35 = -3. \det = 1\cdot2 - 2\cdot(-2) + 3\cdot(-3) = 2 + 4 - 9 = -3.



Problem 4
Compute \begin{vmatrix} 5 & 0 & 0 \\ 7 & 3 & 0 \\ 9 & 8 & 2
\end{vmatrix} — a lower-triangular matrix.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Expand along the first row — many zeros.



Full solution

\det = 5 \cdot \begin{vmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 8 & 2 \end{vmatrix} - 0 + 0 =
5 \cdot 6 = 30. Determinant of a triangular matrix is the product of the diagonal.



Problem 5
Without computing all three, decide which is zero and why: (a) \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \end{vmatrix} (b) \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 7 & 8 & 9 \end{vmatrix} (c) \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{vmatrix}.


Full solution

(a) zero — a row of zeros. (b) zero — two equal rows. (c) = 1 — diagonal product. Identity matrix has \det = 1.

You’ve now expanded along rows, used triangular structure, and seen that a zero row or a repeated row kills the determinant.

The schoolbook trick for 3\times3 determinants — write the matrix, copy the first two columns to the right, and add the three “diagonals down” while subtracting the three “diagonals up” — is Sarrus’s rule, published by Pierre Frédéric Sarrus in 1833. Sarrus was a quiet provincial professor in Strasbourg, and his rule worked only for 3
\times 3 matrices, but it gave generations of students a memorable mnemonic for a calculation that otherwise looks like book-keeping. The deeper formula — cofactor expansion — was already known to Vandermonde (1771) and Laplace (1772), and is what NCERT teaches because it generalises to every size. Sarrus’s rule is the picture; cofactor expansion is the proof.


A 2 \times 2 determinant is the signed area of a parallelogram; a 3 \times 3 determinant is the signed volume of a parallelepiped. Every property in Name — sign flip on row swap, scaling by k when a row is scaled, vanishing on equal rows — is a geometric fact in disguise. Two parallel rows give a flat (degenerate) parallelogram with zero area; that is exactly what “linearly dependent rows” means algebraically. The determinant is geometry written as arithmetic.

title: Ch 4a — Determinants
text:
Name

For a square matrix A = [a_{ij}]_{n \times n}, the determinant |A| = \det(A) is defined recursively. For n = 1, |[a]| = a. For larger n, the expansion along row i is \det(A) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} (-1)^{i+j} a_{ij} M_{ij}, where M_{ij} is the minor — the determinant of the (n-1)\times(n-1) matrix obtained by deleting row i, column j. The signed minor C_{ij} = (-1)^{i+j} M_{ij} is the cofactor.
Key properties (square A, B):

|A^T| = |A|.
Swap two rows (or columns): |A| flips sign.
Multiply one row by k: |A| multiplies by k.
Two equal rows (or columns): |A| = 0.
Add a multiple of one row to another: |A| unchanged.
|AB| = |A|\cdot|B|.
|kA| = k^n |A| for n\times n matrices.

A matrix is singular if |A| = 0, non-singular otherwise.

A verse:

Cofactors signed in checkerboard pattern; Expand by any row, the answer’s the same. Equal rows? Zero. Triangular? Diagonal product. Singular = no inverse — that’s the determinant’s name.

title: Ch 4a — Determinants
text:
Apply

Worked example

Find |A| for A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 & 2 \\ 3 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 4 &
5 \end{pmatrix}, expanding along column 2.

A competent solver thinks:

Column 2 entries: -1, 0, 4. The 0 kills one term.
Cofactor signs: -, +, - (column 2 of the checkerboard).
|A| = (-1)\cdot(-1)\cdot M_{12} + 0 + 4\cdot(-1)\cdot M_{32}.
M_{12} = \begin{vmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 \end{vmatrix} = 13. M_{32} = \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 1 \end{vmatrix} = -5.
|A| = 1 \cdot 13 + (-4)\cdot(-5) = 13 + 20 = 33.



Practice — Warm
W1. \begin{vmatrix} 7 & 2 \\ 5 & 3 \end{vmatrix}.


Full solution

21 - 10 = 11.

W2. \begin{vmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta &
\cos\theta \end{vmatrix}.


Full solution

\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1. (Rotation matrix has determinant 1 — it preserves area.)

W3. Expand along the first column: \begin{vmatrix} 2 & 3 & 1 \\ 0 & 4 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 5 \end{vmatrix}.


Full solution

Triangular: 2\cdot4\cdot5 = 40.



Practice — Working
P1. Find x if \begin{vmatrix} x & 2 \\ 18 & x \end{vmatrix} =
\begin{vmatrix} 6 & 2 \\ 18 & 6 \end{vmatrix}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Compute both determinants.



Full solution

LHS = x^2 - 36. RHS = 36 - 36 = 0. So x^2 = 36 \Rightarrow x = \pm
6.

P2. Show that \begin{vmatrix} 1 & a & a^2 \\ 1 & b & b^2 \\ 1 & c &
c^2 \end{vmatrix} = (b-a)(c-a)(c-b).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Apply R_2 \to R_2 - R_1 and R_3 \to R_3 - R_1. Then expand.



Full solution

\begin{vmatrix} 1 & a & a^2 \\ 0 & b-a & b^2-a^2 \\ 0 & c-a & c^2-a^2
\end{vmatrix}. Factor b-a, c-a from rows 2, 3: (b-a)(c-a)\begin{vmatrix} 1 & a & a^2 \\ 0 & 1 & b+a \\ 0 & 1 & c+a
\end{vmatrix}. Expand: (b-a)(c-a)\cdot1\cdot((c+a)-(b+a)) =
(b-a)(c-a)(c-b). ✓ This is the Vandermonde determinant.

P3. Find |3A| if |A| = 4 and A is 3\times3.


Hint 1 — a nudge

|kA| = k^n|A|.



Full solution

|3A| = 3^3 \cdot 4 = 108.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Without expansion, prove \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ a & b & c
\\ a^3 & b^3 & c^3 \end{vmatrix} = (a-b)(b-c)(c-a)(a+b+c).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Use column operations C_2 \to C_2 - C_1 and C_3 \to C_3 - C_1; factor; expand.



Full solution

After C_2 \to C_2 - C_1, C_3 \to C_3 - C_1: \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ a & b-a & c-a \\ a^3 & b^3-a^3 & c^3-a^3
\end{vmatrix}. Expand along row 1: = (b-a)(c^3 - a^3) - (c-a)(b^3 - a^3) = (b-a)(c-a)(c^2+ca+a^2) - (c-a)(b-a)(b^2+ba+a^2) = (b-a)(c-a)(c-b)(c+b+a) = (a-b)(b-c)(c-a)(a+b+c) after sign rearrangement. ✓



Board-Pattern Practice
1-mark MCQ #1. $
\begin{vmatrix} \cos15° & \sin15° \\ \sin75° &
\cos75° \end{vmatrix}
= $ (a) 0 (b) 1 (c) \cos60° (d) \sin60°.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\sin75° = \cos15°, \cos75° = \sin15°.



Full solution

\cos15° \cos75° - \sin15° \sin75° = \cos15°\sin15° - \sin15°\cos15° =
0. (a) 0.

1-mark MCQ #2. If |A| = 5 and A is 3\times3, then |2A^T| = (a) 10 (b) 40 (c) 80 (d) 25.


Full solution

|2A^T| = 2^3 |A^T| = 8\cdot5 = 40. (b).

Assertion–Reason. Assertion (A): If two rows of a 3\times3 matrix are identical, |A| = 0. Reason (R): The determinant changes sign under row swap; equal rows force |A| = -|A|.

Both A and R true; R correct explanation. (b) Both true; R not correct explanation. (c) A true, R false. (d) A false.



Full solution

Both true; R is exactly the reason. (a).

2-mark. Find values of x for which \begin{vmatrix} 3 & x \\ x & 1
\end{vmatrix} = \begin{vmatrix} 3 & 2 \\ 4 & 1 \end{vmatrix}.


Full solution

3 - x^2 = 3 - 8 = -5 \Rightarrow x^2 = 8 \Rightarrow x = \pm 2\sqrt{2}.

3-mark. Show that \begin{vmatrix} a-b-c & 2a & 2a \\ 2b & b-c-a &
2b \\ 2c & 2c & c-a-b \end{vmatrix} = (a+b+c)^3.


Hint 1 — a nudge

R_1 \to R_1 + R_2 + R_3. Each entry of the new R_1 becomes a+b+c.



Full solution

After R_1 \to R_1 + R_2 + R_3: each new R_1 entry equals a + b +
c. Factor out a+b+c: (a+b+c) \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 2b & b-c-a & 2b \\ 2c & 2c &
c-a-b \end{vmatrix}. Now C_2 \to C_2 - C_1 and C_3 \to C_3 - C_1: (a+b+c)\begin{vmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 2b & -b-c-a & 0 \\ 2c & 0 &
-a-b-c \end{vmatrix}. Triangular: = (a+b+c) \cdot 1 \cdot (-(a+b+c)) \cdot (-(a+b+c)) =
(a+b+c)^3. ✓

3-mark. Find the area of the triangle with vertices (2, 7), (1, 1),
(10, 8) using determinants.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Area = \frac{1}{2}|D| where D = \begin{vmatrix} x_1 & y_1 & 1 \\ x_2
& y_2 & 1 \\ x_3 & y_3 & 1 \end{vmatrix}.



Full solution

D = \begin{vmatrix} 2 & 7 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 10 & 8 & 1 \end{vmatrix}
= 2(1-8) - 7(1-10) + 1(8-10) = -14 + 63 - 2 = 47. Area = 47/2 = 23.5 sq units.

5-mark long answer. Using properties of determinants, show \begin{vmatrix} x+a & b & c \\ a & x+b & c \\ a & b & x+c \end{vmatrix}
= x^2(x + a + b + c).


Hint 1 — a nudge

C_1 \to C_1 + C_2 + C_3. Each entry of C_1 becomes x + a + b + c.



Full solution

C_1 \to C_1 + C_2 + C_3: (x+a+b+c)\begin{vmatrix} 1 & b & c \\ 1 & x+b & c \\ 1 & b & x+c
\end{vmatrix}. R_2 \to R_2 - R_1, R_3 \to R_3 - R_1: (x+a+b+c)\begin{vmatrix} 1 & b & c \\ 0 & x & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & x
\end{vmatrix}. Triangular: (x+a+b+c)\cdot 1\cdot x \cdot x = x^2(x+a+b+c). ✓

6-mark Case Study. Land area from GPS coordinates.
A surveyor records three corners of a triangular plot at coordinates A = (1, 5), B = (3, -1), C = (6, 4) (in some unit-grid).

Use the determinant formula to compute the plot’s area. (3 marks)
The next year, all three points have shifted by (+2, +3) due to re-surveying. Show that the area is unchanged. (1 mark)
If the same shape is scaled up by a factor of 4 (every coordinate multiplied by 4), what is the new area, and how does the determinant formula confirm it? (2 marks)



Full solution

(i) D = \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 5 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 & 1 \\ 6 & 4 & 1
\end{vmatrix} = 1(-1-4) - 5(3-6) + 1(12+6) = -5 + 15 + 18 = 28. Area = 14. (ii) Adding a constant to all three pairs of coordinates corresponds to translating; you can verify the determinant remains 28 by computing. Geometrically, translation preserves area. (iii) Scaling each coordinate by 4 multiplies each row’s first two entries by 4. The determinant scales by 4^2 = 16 (because it’s a 2D area; one factor of 4 from x, one from y). New area = 14\cdot16 =
224.







NCERT Exemplar pointer: Ex 4.1, 4.2 — properties Q4–10. Previous-year pointer: CBSE 2023 Set 3 Q26 (similar to 5-mark); CBSE 2022 Q2 (similar to MCQ #2).





Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

The determinant of a square matrix is one number that captures whether its rows (or columns) are linearly independent — non-zero means they span full dimension; zero means they collapse onto each other.
Cofactor expansion lets you expand along any row or column; pick the one with the most zeros to do the least work.
Row operations are the determinant’s friends: adding a multiple of one row to another leaves |A| unchanged, swapping rows flips its sign, and scaling a row by k multiplies |A| by k — these three rules turn brutal n \times n calculations into clean triangular ones.



Common confusions

“|A + B| = |A| + |B|.” False, almost always. Determinant is not linear in the matrix as a whole — only in each row separately.
“|kA| = k|A|.” Only when A is 1 \times 1. For n \times n, |kA| = k^n|A|.
“Row swap = same determinant.” No — row swap flips the sign. A common error in long determinant calculations.



Self-Assessment

Could you compute a 3\times3 determinant by row reduction in five minutes?
Did you solve at least 5 board-pattern items unaided?
Can you state when a matrix is singular and what that means geometrically?


3 yes: move on.
2 yes: redo the 5-mark long-answer problem.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover with paper.

title: Ch 4a — Determinants
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

cbse12.matrix-operations — matrix arithmetic.



What this unlocks

cbse12.matrix-inverse-cramers — inverse uses the determinant.



NCERT chapter alignment

Class 12, Chapter 4 — Determinants (NCERT 2023 rationalised). Sections 4.1–4.4.
Sister synthesis lesson: t5.matrices.
Read alongside the NCERT textbook.



Where this matters

AI/ML. The determinant of a covariance matrix appears in the multivariate normal density. Log-determinants are used in variational inference and normalising flows. A near-zero determinant warns of collinear features.


Research / engineering. Jacobian determinants govern the change-of- variables formula in multiple integrals. Stability analyses of physical systems hinge on the sign of a determinant.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones: 3Blue1Brown (“the determinant is the area scaling factor”), Bhāskara (verse), Gelfand (problem-first).

title: Ch 4 — Linear Equations in Two Variables
text:
Be honest before you begin.

Can you solve 3x - 7 = 8 for x? (If no, revisit cbse8.linear-equations-one-var.)
Can you plot (2, 3) and (-1, -2) on the coordinate plane? (If no, revisit cbse9.coordinate-geometry.)
Do you know that 2 \cdot 3 + 5 = 11 in your head?

Three yes? Read on.
crumbs: Ch 4 — Linear Equations in Two Variables

title: Ch 4 — Linear Equations in Two Variables
crumbs: Ch 4 — Linear Equations in Two Variables

title: Ch 4 — Linear Equations in Two Variables
text:
Wonder

How many pencil-and-pen combinations make ₹20 exactly? List a few.
If you allowed half-pencils (x can be a fraction), how many solutions would there be?
The combinations you wrote down land on a straight line. Why a line — why not a curve, or a scattered cloud?
If you change the price of a pen from ₹5 to ₹10, what changes about the picture you drew?







TipA whisper, no answer yet





Q1 already shows the lesson: infinitely many solutions, not one. Q3 is the deep question — the Discover sequence will force the answer.
crumbs: Ch 4 — Linear Equations in Two Variables

title: Ch 4 — Linear Equations in Two Variables
text:
Discover

Problem 1
The equation x + y = 7 has many solutions. Find six of them by listing x from 0 to 5 and computing y each time.


Hint 1 — a nudge

y = 7 - x. Plug in x = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5.



Full solution

(0, 7), (1, 6), (2, 5), (3, 4), (4, 3), (5, 2). There are infinitely many: x can be any real number.



Problem 2
Plot the six points from Problem 1 on a coordinate plane. What pattern do they form?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Use a ruler. Place the dots carefully.



Full solution

They lie on a single straight line. The line slopes downward (as x grows, y shrinks). Every solution of x + y = 7 corresponds to a point on this line; every point on this line is a solution.



Problem 3
For the equation 2x + 3y = 12, find three solutions and plot them. Verify by ruler that they are collinear (lie on one line).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Pick easy values: x = 0, then y = 0, then any third value.



Hint 2 — the technique

x = 0 \Rightarrow y = 4. y = 0 \Rightarrow x = 6. x = 3 \Rightarrow
y = 2.



Full solution

(0, 4), (6, 0), (3, 2). Place a ruler edge against any two — the third lands on it. So the three are collinear, and indeed every solution of 2x + 3y = 12 lies on this single line.



Problem 4
Find the solution of x + y = 7 for which x = y.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Substitute y = x.



Full solution

x + x = 7 \Rightarrow x = 3.5. Solution: (3.5, 3.5). The line y = x crosses x + y = 7 at this single point.



Problem 5
Sketch the graphs of y = 3 and x = -2 on the same plane. Where do they meet?


Hint 1 — a nudge

y = 3 means y is always 3 — every point at height 3. What does that look like?



Full solution

y = 3 is a horizontal line through height 3. x = -2 is a vertical line through x = -2. They meet at (-2, 3).



Problem 6
A toy seller sells two kinds of toys, x small and y big, with prices ₹3 and ₹4. Total revenue is ₹24. Write the equation; find three solutions in non-negative integers.


Hint 1 — a nudge

3x + 4y = 24.



Hint 2 — the technique

Try y = 0, 3, 6 (so that 4y is divisible into 24).



Full solution

3x + 4y = 24. Solutions: (8, 0), (4, 3), (0, 6). (Other rational solutions exist, but only these three are non-negative integers.)

You discovered: a linear equation in two variables has infinitely many solutions; the solutions form a straight line; horizontal and vertical lines y = c and x = c are the simplest cases.

The Chinese classic The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art (Jiǔzhāng Suànshù, c. 100 BCE) already contained methods for solving systems of linear equations — Chapter 8, Fāngchéng (“rectangular arrays”), lays out the procedure that the West would rediscover and call Gaussian elimination seventeen centuries later. The 3rd-century commentator Liu Hui even worked through the row-by-row reduction with negative numbers — almost a millennium before negatives were accepted in Europe.


One equation in two unknowns is under-determined — fix one variable and the other is forced. Slide the free variable, and the forced variable slides with it. The set of all such (free, forced) pairs is exactly a straight line. Algebra and geometry are the same picture from two angles.
crumbs: Ch 4 — Linear Equations in Two Variables

title: Ch 4 — Linear Equations in Two Variables
text:
Name

A linear equation in two variables is any equation that can be written ax + by + c = 0, where a, b, c are real numbers and a, b are not both zero.
A solution of this equation is a pair (x, y) that makes the equation true. There are infinitely many solutions.
The graph of every linear equation in two variables is a straight line, and conversely, every straight line is the graph of some linear equation. Each point on the line is a solution; each solution sits on the line.
Special cases:

y = c is the horizontal line at height c.
x = c is the vertical line at horizontal position c.


A short verse:

One equation, two unknowns — Pairs that fit form a line, no more, no less. Pick an x, the y is forced; Slide along the line, the equation never breaks.
crumbs: Ch 4 — Linear Equations in Two Variables

title: Ch 4 — Linear Equations in Two Variables
text:
Apply

Worked example

Draw the graph of 3x + 4y = 12. Find where it cuts the two axes.

A competent solver thinks:

Find the x-intercept (y = 0): 3x = 12 \Rightarrow x = 4. Point (4, 0).
Find the y-intercept (x = 0): 4y = 12 \Rightarrow y = 3. Point (0, 3).
A check point at x = 4 - 4 = 0? Already used. Try x = -4: 3(-4) + 4y = 12 \Rightarrow 4y = 24 \Rightarrow y = 6. Point (-4, 6).
Plot the three. Lay a ruler — they are collinear. Draw the line.

Answer: Line through (4, 0) and (0, 3). x-intercept = 4, y-intercept = 3.


Practice — Warm
W1. Write 5x = 3y - 7 in the standard form ax + by + c = 0.


Full solution

5x - 3y + 7 = 0. Here a = 5, b = -3, c = 7.

W2. Is (2, 1) a solution of 4x - 3y = 5?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Substitute and check.



Full solution

4(2) - 3(1) = 8 - 3 = 5 ✓. Yes.

W3. Find two solutions of x - 2y = 6.


Full solution

y = 0 \Rightarrow x = 6, so (6, 0). x = 0 \Rightarrow y = -3, so (0, -3).



Practice — Working
P1. Draw the graph of y = 2x - 1. Find y when x = 4.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Make a table for x = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4.



Full solution

Table: (0,-1), (1, 1), (2, 3), (3, 5), (4, 7). Points line up. At x = 4, y = 7.

P2. A two-digit number’s tens digit is twice its units digit. The sum of the digits is 9. Write a system of equations and solve it.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let tens digit = x, units digit = y. x = 2y and x + y = 9.



Full solution

Substituting: 2y + y = 9 \Rightarrow y = 3, x = 6. Number: 63.

P3. The cost of a notebook is twice the cost of a pen. Write an equation in two variables to represent this. Find three solutions.


Full solution

Let the pen cost ₹y and the notebook ₹x. Then x = 2y, i.e. x - 2y = 0. Solutions: (2, 1), (10, 5), (20, 10).



Practice — Stretch
S1. A line passes through (2, 5) and (4, 11). Find a linear equation that the line satisfies.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Try y = mx + c. Substitute both points to get two equations in m, c.



Hint 2 — the technique

5 = 2m + c and 11 = 4m + c. Subtract.



Full solution

11 - 5 = 4m - 2m \Rightarrow 6 = 2m \Rightarrow m = 3. From 5 = 2(3) +
c, c = -1. So y = 3x - 1, or 3x - y - 1 = 0.

S2. The taxi fare in a city is structured: ₹15 for the first km, then ₹8 per km. Let x be the total kilometres travelled and y the total fare. Write the equation relating x and y (for x \ge 1). Find the fare for x = 10 km.


Hint 1 — a nudge

First km is ₹15. Each km after that is ₹8. So total = 15 + 8(x - 1).



Full solution

y = 15 + 8(x - 1) = 8x + 7 for x \ge 1. Or 8x - y + 7 = 0. At x = 10: y = 8(10) + 7 = ₹87.



Board-Pattern Practice
1-mark (objective). The graph of x = 4 is a: (a) point (b) line parallel to x-axis (c) line parallel to y-axis (d) line through origin.


Full solution

x = 4 is the vertical line through x = 4. (c).

2-mark (short answer). Express y in terms of x for the equation 2x + 5y = 10, then find y when x = -5.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Solve for y: 5y = 10 - 2x.



Full solution

y = \tfrac{10 - 2x}{5} = 2 - \tfrac{2x}{5}. At x = -5: y = 2 - (-2) = 4.

3-mark (short answer II). The cost of 5 kg apples and 3 kg oranges is ₹240. Write the equation in two variables. If apples cost ₹x/kg and oranges cost ₹y/kg, find the cost of oranges when apples cost ₹30/kg.


Hint 1 — a nudge

5x + 3y = 240.



Full solution

5x + 3y = 240. With x = 30: 150 + 3y = 240 \Rightarrow y = 30. Oranges cost ₹30/kg.

5-mark (long answer). Draw the graph of the equation x + 2y = 6. From the graph, find the value of y when x = -2, and verify your answer by substitution.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Find at least three solutions, plot, draw the line.



Hint 2 — the technique

Easy choices: x = 0 \Rightarrow y = 3. x = 6 \Rightarrow y = 0. x = 2 \Rightarrow y = 2.



Full solution

Table. (0, 3), (6, 0), (2, 2). Plot and draw the straight line. From the graph, at x = -2, the line is at height y = 4. Verify: -2 + 2(4) = -2 + 8 = 6 ✓. Therefore y = 4.







NCERT Exemplar pointer: Class 9 Exemplar Ch. 4, Problem 4.3 (verifying solutions, similar to W2). Previous-year pointer: CBSE Class 9 sample paper “draw the graph of ax + by = c and read off a point” (similar to 5-mark above).





Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A linear equation in two variables has infinitely many solutions — one whole line’s worth.
Every solution is a point; every point on the line is a solution. The line and the equation are two names for the same object.
To plot, find two points and join. x-intercept (y = 0) and y-intercept (x = 0) are usually the easiest two.



Common confusions

“A linear equation in two variables has one solution.” No — it has infinitely many. A pair of linear equations (Class 10) is what typically pins down a single solution.
“y = 5 is just a number, not an equation.” It is an equation in two variables (the coefficient of x is zero). Its graph is a horizontal line.
“Equations like \tfrac{1}{x} + y = 2 are linear.” No — division by x is not linear. Linear means x and y each appear only to the first power, and not multiplied together.



Self-Assessment
Be honest with yourself.

Could you take any equation ax + by + c = 0 and plot its graph by finding three solutions?
Did you solve W1–P3 with at most one hint each?
Could you write a linear equation that fits a real-world situation (e.g. taxi fare) without prompting?


3 yes: move on to Euclid’s Geometry.
2 yes: redo S1 and S2.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover and Apply.

Connect

NCERT chapter alignment

Class 9, Chapter 4 — Linear Equations in Two Variables (NCERT 2023 rationalised syllabus). Covers Sections 4.1–4.3: linear equations, solutions, graph.
Sister synthesis lesson (different angle): t2.linear-functions-graphs.
Read alongside the NCERT textbook.



Where this matters

Research / Operations. “Linear programming” — the science of optimising under linear constraints — is built entirely on equations of this form. Modern logistics, scheduling, and supply chains run on these equations every minute of every day.


AI / ML. Linear regression fits the best line through a cloud of data points; that line is exactly the graph of a linear equation in two variables. The neural networks behind ChatGPT use the same affine maps as their fundamental building block, just stacked thousands deep.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones honoured: Pestalozzi (the pencil-and-pen Touch is a living equation), Russian problem-first (six Discover problems sweep from “list solutions” to “all solutions form a line”), Bhāskara (compact verse mnemonic).
