This page reflects the snapshot of _curriculum.yml at the time of build. Lesson statuses there are the source of truth.

title: Ch 9 — Circles
text:
Be honest before you begin.

Can you state the angle-sum theorem of a triangle? (If no, revisit cbse9.lines-and-angles.)
Can you prove triangles congruent by SSS / SAS / ASA / RHS? (If no, revisit cbse9.triangles.)
Do you know “radius”, “diameter”, “circumference”? (If no, revisit t1.shapes-angles.)

Three yes? Read on.

title: Ch 9 — Circles
Ch 9 — Circles
text:
Wonder

The radius from O to a chord’s midpoint is always perpendicular to the chord. Why must that be? Could a circle ever cheat this?
Cut two chords of equal length on the same circle. Are they equally far from the centre? What if you cut one short and one long?
Pick two points A, B on a circle. Pick a third point P anywhere on the major arc between them. The angle \angle APB has a value. Slide P along the major arc — does the value change?
A four-cornered figure with all four corners on the circle — what special relationship do its opposite angles share?







TipA whisper, no answer yet





Q3 is the angle in the same segment theorem (also called the inscribed-angle theorem in this special form). Q4 is cyclic quadrilateral — opposite angles sum to 180^\circ.

title: Ch 9 — Circles
text:
Discover

Problem 1 (Equal chords ⟺ equal distances)
In a circle with centre O, two chords AB and CD are drawn. M is the midpoint of AB and N is the midpoint of CD. Suppose AB = CD. Prove OM = ON.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Use right-angled triangles \triangle OMB and \triangle OND, with the radius as hypotenuse.



Full solution








Statement
Reason




1. OM \perp AB and ON \perp CD.
Radius to midpoint of chord is perpendicular.


2. OB = OD.
Both are radii.


3. MB = \tfrac{1}{2} AB, ND = \tfrac{1}{2} CD, and AB = CD.
Given + midpoint.


4. So MB = ND.
From 3.


5. \triangle OMB \cong \triangle OND.
RHS (right angle, hypotenuse, side).


6. OM = ON.
CPCT. \blacksquare






Problem 2 (Angle subtended at centre vs at arc)
In a circle with centre O, an arc AB subtends \angle AOB at the centre. Pick any point P on the major arc. Use the radii OA, OB, OP and the isosceles triangles \triangle OPA, \triangle OPB to show \angle APB = \tfrac{1}{2} \angle AOB.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Each radius makes an isosceles triangle with the chord through P. Exterior angle of a triangle equals the sum of the two opposite interior angles.



Full solution

Join PO and extend it to meet the circle at Q (the diameter through P). Look at \triangle OPA: it is isosceles (OP = OA), so \angle OAP = \angle OPA. The exterior angle \angle AOQ at O equals \angle OAP + \angle OPA = 2 \angle OPA. Similarly \angle BOQ = 2 \angle OPB. Adding (or subtracting, depending on which side of PO the points lie), \angle AOB = 2(\angle OPA + \angle OPB) = 2 \angle APB. \blacksquare
The angle subtended by an arc at the centre is twice the angle it subtends at any point on the major arc.



Problem 3 (Same-segment angles equal)
Two points P_1 and P_2 lie on the major arc of AB. Use the result of Problem 2 to conclude \angle AP_1 B = \angle AP_2 B.


Full solution

Both equal \tfrac{1}{2} \angle AOB (the central angle of AB is the same angle for the same arc). So they equal each other. Angles in the same segment are equal. \blacksquare



Problem 4 (Angle in a semicircle)
If AB is a diameter and P is any point on the circle (not A or B), what is \angle APB?


Hint 1 — a nudge

The arc AB is now half the circle. The central angle is what?



Full solution

\angle AOB = 180^\circ (since AB is a diameter and O is on it). By the central-vs-arc theorem, \angle APB = \tfrac{1}{2}(180^\circ) =
90^\circ. The angle in a semicircle is a right angle. (This is Thales’ theorem, the oldest named theorem in geometry, ~600 BCE.)


Thales of Miletus (~624 – ~546 BCE) is the first person in recorded history credited with proving a geometric statement rather than just observing it. The legend has him deducing the height of the Egyptian pyramids by comparing their shadow to a stick’s shadow at the same hour — a similar-triangles argument that exploits the very inscribed-angle ideas you are deriving here. Two and a half millennia later, his theorem still survives every geometry curriculum on Earth.



Problem 5 (Cyclic quadrilateral)
ABCD is a quadrilateral with all four vertices on a circle (a cyclic quadrilateral). Show \angle A + \angle C = 180^\circ.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\angle A stands on the major arc of BCD; \angle C stands on the opposite arc.



Full solution

\angle A is the inscribed angle on arc BCD, so it equals half the central angle on arc BCD. Similarly \angle C is half the central angle on arc BAD. The two arcs together make the whole circle, so the two central angles sum to 360^\circ, hence the two inscribed angles sum to \tfrac{1}{2}(360^\circ) = 180^\circ. Opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral are supplementary. \blacksquare

You discovered: chords pair up by length and distance from centre; central angle is twice the inscribed angle for the same arc; angles in the same segment are equal; the angle in a semicircle is right; and opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral sum to 180^\circ.

The whole chapter is one theorem in disguise: the central angle is twice the inscribed angle on the same arc. Equal chords ↔︎ equal distances, same-segment angles, the semicircle right angle, the cyclic quadrilateral — every result you proved is a special case or consequence of that single fact.

title: Ch 9 — Circles
text:
Name

In a circle with centre O:

Chord, radius, diameter, arc, sector, segment — standard vocabulary from the figure.
Theorem 1. The perpendicular from the centre of a circle to a chord bisects the chord. (Conversely, the line from the centre to the midpoint of a chord is perpendicular to it.)
Theorem 2. Equal chords of a circle are equidistant from the centre, and conversely.
Theorem 3. The angle subtended by an arc at the centre is twice the angle it subtends at any point on the major arc.
Theorem 4 (same segment). Angles in the same segment are equal.
Theorem 5 (Thales). The angle in a semicircle is a right angle.
Theorem 6 (cyclic quadrilateral). The sum of either pair of opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral is 180^\circ.


A short verse:

Drop from the centre — perpendicular and midpoint, both at once. Equal chords, equal distances; equal arcs, equal angles. Centre angle is double the rim; On a diameter, every angle is right.

title: Ch 9 — Circles
text:
Apply

Worked example

AB is a chord of a circle of radius 5 cm. The chord is at a perpendicular distance of 3 cm from the centre. Find the length of AB.

A competent solver:

Drop the perpendicular from O to AB, meeting at M. By Theorem 1, M is the midpoint of AB, and OM = 3, OA = 5.
Triangle OMA is right-angled at M. By Pythagoras, AM^2 + OM^2 = OA^2 \Rightarrow AM^2 = 25 - 9 = 16 \Rightarrow AM
= 4.
AB = 2 \cdot AM = 8 cm.

Answer: AB = 8 cm.


Practice — Warm
W1. A chord of length 24 cm is at a perpendicular distance of 5 cm from the centre. Find the radius.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Same setup as the worked example, going the other way.



Full solution

Half-chord = 12. Radius = \sqrt{12^2 + 5^2} = 13 cm.

W2. \angle AOB = 80^\circ at the centre of a circle. What is the inscribed angle \angle APB at any point P on the major arc?


Full solution

\tfrac{1}{2}(80) = 40^\circ.

W3. ABCD is cyclic with \angle A = 92^\circ. Find \angle C.


Full solution

\angle C = 180 - 92 = 88^\circ.



Practice — Working
P1. Two chords AB and CD of a circle are equal in length. Show that the arcs AB and CD are equal.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Equal chords subtend equal angles at the centre. Equal central angles mean equal arcs.



Full solution








Statement
Reason




1. OA = OC and OB = OD.
All radii.


2. AB = CD.
Given.


3. \triangle OAB \cong \triangle OCD.
SSS.


4. \angle AOB = \angle COD.
CPCT.


5. So arc AB = arc CD.
Equal central angles cut equal arcs. \blacksquare




P2. ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral. The bisectors of \angle A and \angle C meet at point X. Show X lies on the line BD (or state the relationship between the bisectors).


Hint 1 — a nudge

\angle A + \angle C = 180^\circ, so half each sums to 90^\circ.



Full solution

Half-angles sum to 90^\circ, so the two bisectors are perpendicular at their intersection. The full proof of “X lies on BD” requires additional configuration — the cleaner statement is that the bisectors are perpendicular. Treat this as practice in extracting facts.

P3. A diameter of a circle is 26 cm. A chord of the circle is 24 cm long. Find its perpendicular distance from the centre.


Full solution

Radius = 13. Half-chord = 12. Distance from centre =
\sqrt{13^2 - 12^2} = \sqrt{25} = 5 cm.



Practice — Stretch
S1. AB is a diameter and CD is any other chord of a circle. The perpendicular from A to CD has foot P; the perpendicular from B to CD has foot Q. Prove CP = QD.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Drop a perpendicular from the centre O to CD — call the foot M. M is the midpoint of CD, and OM is the average of AP and BQ (parallel lines, like rungs of a ladder).



Full solution

AP, OM, BQ are all perpendicular to CD, so they are parallel. O is the midpoint of AB (centre on diameter), so OM is the average of AP and BQ — meaning M is the midpoint of PQ. But M is also the midpoint of CD (perpendicular from centre). So CP = CM - PM = MD - MQ = QD. \blacksquare

S2. A circle has two parallel chords of lengths 6 cm and 8 cm. The chords are on opposite sides of the centre, 7 cm apart. Find the radius of the circle.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let the perpendicular distances from the centre be d_1 and d_2 (d_1 + d_2 = 7). Use Pythagoras to relate each chord to the radius.



Full solution

Let radius be r. Then r^2 = d_1^2 + 3^2 = d_1^2 + 9 and r^2 = d_2^2 + 4^2 = d_2^2 + 16. So d_2^2 = d_1^2 - 7. Together with d_1 + d_2 = 7: substitute d_2 = 7 - d_1, get (7 - d_1)^2 = d_1^2 -
7 \Rightarrow 49 - 14 d_1 = -7 \Rightarrow d_1 = 4, so d_2 = 3. r = \sqrt{16 + 9} = 5 cm.



Board-Pattern Practice
1-mark (objective). The angle in a semicircle is: (a) 45^\circ (b) 60^\circ (c) 90^\circ (d) 180^\circ.


Full solution

(c) 90^\circ — Thales’ theorem.

2-mark (short answer). A chord of a circle of radius 10 cm subtends a right angle at the centre. Find the length of the chord.


Hint 1 — a nudge

The chord and two radii form a right triangle with the right angle at the centre.



Full solution

Length = \sqrt{10^2 + 10^2} = 10\sqrt{2} cm.

3-mark (short answer II). ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral with \angle A = 100^\circ and \angle B = 70^\circ. Find \angle C and \angle D.


Full solution

Opposite angles supplementary: \angle C = 180 - 100 = 80^\circ, \angle D = 180 - 70 = 110^\circ. Check: 100 + 70 + 80 + 110 = 360 ✓.

5-mark (long answer). Prove that the perpendicular from the centre of a circle to a chord bisects the chord.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Right triangles \triangle OMA and \triangle OMB, with hypotenuse = radius.



Full solution




Statement
Reason




1. OM \perp AB, M on AB.
Given.


2. OA = OB.
Radii.


3. OM = OM.
Common.


4. \angle OMA = \angle OMB = 90^\circ.
Given.


5. \triangle OMA \cong \triangle OMB.
RHS.


6. AM = MB.
CPCT. So M is the midpoint of AB. \blacksquare










NCERT Exemplar pointer: Class 9 Exemplar Ch. 9, Problem 9.3 (chord distance, similar to W1). Previous-year pointer: CBSE Class 9 sample paper “prove perpendicular from centre bisects chord” (similar to 5-mark).





Common confusions

“The angle subtended at the centre equals the angle subtended at the rim.” No — centre angle is twice the rim angle for the same arc.
“Every quadrilateral inscribed in a circle has all four angles equal.” No — only opposite angles are supplementary. They need not be equal individually.
“A chord is the same as a diameter.” A diameter is the longest chord; not every chord is a diameter.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

The perpendicular from the centre to a chord cuts it in half. That single move converts almost every chord problem into a right triangle and Pythagoras.
Centre angle is double the rim angle on the same arc. Memorise this one sentence and most circle theorems fall out of it.
A cyclic quadrilateral pairs its opposite angles to 180^\circ. That is why the rim of a circle keeps the four corners “balanced”.



Self-Assessment
Be honest with yourself.

Could you prove “perpendicular from centre bisects chord” without notes?
Did you solve W1–P3 with at most one hint each?
Could you state the inscribed-angle theorem and its consequences (same segment, semicircle, cyclic quadrilateral) cold?


3 yes: move on to Heron’s Formula.
2 yes: redo S1 and S2.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover 2–5. The inscribed-angle theorem is the engine of this chapter.

title: Ch 9 — Circles
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

cbse9.lines-and-angles — angle facts, especially exterior-angle and triangle-sum.
t1.shapes-angles — radius, diameter, chord vocabulary.



What this unlocks

cbse10.circles-tangents — tangent–radius, alternate-segment theorem.
cbse10.areas-related-to-circles — sector and segment areas.



NCERT chapter alignment

Class 9, Chapter 9 — Circles (NCERT 2023 rationalised syllabus). Covers Sections 9.1–9.5: chord theorems, central vs inscribed angles, cyclic quadrilateral.
Sister synthesis lesson (different angle): t1.shapes-angles meets the circle as a shape; the theorems are this chapter’s contribution.
Read alongside the NCERT textbook.



Where this matters

Game development. Camera-frustum culling — deciding which objects are visible — uses the angle subtended by an object at the camera. The inscribed-angle theorem is the formal version of “things on the same horizon look the same width regardless of where you are on the horizon”.


Research. Thales’ theorem (Discover Problem 4) is the oldest recorded named theorem, attributed to Thales of Miletus around 600 BCE. It is also the key step in deriving the equation of a circle from x^2 + y^2 = r^2 — the first place algebra and geometry truly fuse.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones honoured: Kiselev (proof discipline), Russian problem-first (the inscribed-angle theorem is forced through Discover before Name states it), Better Explained (the “centre angle is double the rim” memorable phrasing).

title: Ch 11 — Surface Areas and Volumes
text:
Be honest before you begin.

Can you compute the area of a rectangle and a circle? (If no, revisit t1.area-perimeter-volume.)
Can you compute the volume of a cuboid? (If no, revisit cbse8.mensuration.)
Do you know that the area of a circle is \pi r^2 and circumference is 2 \pi r?
Can you simplify \pi \approx \tfrac{22}{7} when needed?

Three or four yes? Read on.

title: Ch 11 — Surface Areas and Volumes
Ch 11 — Surface Areas and Volumes
text:
Wonder

Two cuboids of equal volume can have very different surface areas. Could you describe a “tall thin” one and a “short fat” one with the same volume?
Why is the volume of a cone exactly one third the volume of a cylinder with the same base and height?
The surface area of a sphere is 4 \pi r^2 — exactly four times the area of its great circle. Is that an accident, or does it tell you something?
If you double the radius of a sphere, by what factor does its volume change?







TipA whisper, no answer yet





Q4 is a scaling question — volume scales as the cube of the radius. Q2 we won’t prove rigorously yet, but you can see it: pour cone-fulls of water into a cylinder of the same base and height; you’ll need exactly three.

title: Ch 11 — Surface Areas and Volumes
text:
Discover

Problem 1 (Cuboid)
A box has length 10 cm, breadth 8 cm, height 6 cm. Compute its volume and total surface area.


Hint 1 — a nudge

V = lbh. \text{TSA} = 2(lb + bh + lh).



Full solution

V = 10 \cdot 8 \cdot 6 = 480 cm³. \text{TSA} = 2(80 + 48 + 60) = 2 \cdot 188 = 376 cm².



Problem 2 (Cylinder)
A cylindrical drum has radius 7 cm and height 20 cm. Find the curved surface area, total surface area, and volume. Use \pi =
\tfrac{22}{7}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

CSA = 2 \pi r h. TSA = 2\pi r(r + h). V = \pi r^2 h.



Full solution

CSA = 2 \cdot \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 7 \cdot 20 = 880 cm². TSA = 2 \cdot \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 7 \cdot 27 = 1188 cm². V = \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 49 \cdot 20 = 3080 cm³.



Problem 3 (Cone)
A cone has radius r = 6 cm and height h = 8 cm. Find slant height l, then volume and total surface area.


Hint 1 — a nudge

l = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2}. V = \tfrac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h. TSA = \pi r(r +
l).



Full solution

l = \sqrt{36 + 64} = 10 cm. V = \tfrac{1}{3} \pi (36)(8) = 96 \pi
\approx 301.6 cm³. TSA = \pi \cdot 6 \cdot 16 = 96 \pi \approx
301.6 cm².



Problem 4 (Sphere)
A spherical ball has radius 5 cm. Find its surface area and volume. Use \pi \approx 3.14.


Hint 1 — a nudge

SA = 4 \pi r^2. V = \tfrac{4}{3} \pi r^3.



Full solution

SA = 4 \pi \cdot 25 = 100 \pi \approx 314 cm². V = \tfrac{4}{3} \pi \cdot 125 = \tfrac{500 \pi}{3} \approx 523.3 cm³.



Problem 5 (Hemisphere)
A solid hemisphere has radius 7 cm. Find its total surface area (the curved hemisphere plus the flat circular base) and volume.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Curved surface of hemisphere = 2 \pi r^2. Add the flat circle \pi r^2. Volume = \tfrac{2}{3} \pi r^3.



Full solution

TSA = 2 \pi r^2 + \pi r^2 = 3 \pi r^2 = 3 \cdot \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 49
= 462 cm². Volume = \tfrac{2}{3} \cdot \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 343 =
\tfrac{2156 \cdot 343}{21}… let’s simplify: \tfrac{2}{3} \cdot \pi
\cdot 343 = \tfrac{686}{3} \pi \approx 718.4 cm³.



Problem 6 (Conversion / pouring)
A cylinder of radius 6 cm and height 14 cm is full of water. The water is poured into a hemispherical bowl. What must the bowl’s radius be so that the water exactly fills it?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Volumes equal: \pi r^2 h = \tfrac{2}{3} \pi R^3.



Full solution

\pi (36)(14) = \tfrac{2}{3} \pi R^3 \Rightarrow R^3 = \tfrac{3 \cdot
36 \cdot 14}{2} = 756 \Rightarrow R = \sqrt[3]{756} \approx 9.1 cm.

You discovered: each shape has a flat-net way of seeing its surface area and a multiplicative way of seeing its volume; conversion problems (pouring water from one shape to another) are just volume-equation problems.

The famous eureka moment of Archimedes of Syracuse (c. 287 – 212 BCE) — leaping from his bath to run naked through the streets — was about volume. King Hieron II had given a goldsmith a lump of pure gold to forge into a crown and suspected the smith of cheating with silver. Archimedes realised that immersing the crown in water would displace its volume; comparing that to the volume an equal-mass lump of pure gold would displace would expose the fraud. The same insight gave him the volume of a sphere and the sphere-equals-cylinder-CSA identity in S1 — the result he wanted carved on his tomb.


Surface area scales as the square of length; volume scales as the cube. Double the radius of a sphere and the surface goes up by 4\times, the volume by 8\times. This square–cube law is why elephants can’t be ants made big, why ice cubes melt slower than crushed ice, and why pouring problems always reduce to a single volume-conservation equation.

title: Ch 11 — Surface Areas and Volumes
text:
Name










Shape
Volume
Curved SA
Total SA




Cuboid (l, b, h)
lbh
—
2(lb + bh + lh)


Cube (a)
a^3
—
6a^2


Cylinder (r, h)
\pi r^2 h
2 \pi r h
2 \pi r (r + h)


Cone (r, h, slant l)
\tfrac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h
\pi r l
\pi r (r + l)


Sphere (r)
\tfrac{4}{3} \pi r^3
4 \pi r^2
4 \pi r^2


Hemisphere (r)
\tfrac{2}{3} \pi r^3
2 \pi r^2
3 \pi r^2



For the cone, l = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2} (Pythagorean slant height).
When converting between shapes (melting, pouring, recasting), the volume is conserved, not the surface area.

A short verse:

Cuboid: l-b-h, sum the rectangles by twos. Cylinder: a wrapped rectangle and two circles. Cone: a third of cylinder; slant is hypotenuse. Sphere: four pi r squared, four-thirds pi r cubed.

title: Ch 11 — Surface Areas and Volumes
text:
Apply

Worked example

A solid metal cylinder of radius 6 cm and height 32 cm is melted down and recast into spheres of radius 4 cm. How many spheres can be made?

A competent solver:

Volume of cylinder = \pi (6)^2 (32) = 1152 \pi cm³.
Volume of one sphere = \tfrac{4}{3} \pi (4)^3 = \tfrac{256 \pi}{3} cm³.
Number of spheres = \dfrac{1152 \pi}{256 \pi / 3} = \dfrac{1152 \cdot 3}{256}
= \dfrac{3456}{256} = 13.5.
Since you can’t make half a sphere from melted metal (well, you can, but it’s not a sphere), the most full spheres = 13.

Answer: 13 complete spheres, with metal left over.


Practice — Warm
W1. A cube has edge 5 cm. Find its volume and surface area.


Full solution

V = 125 cm³. SA = 150 cm².

W2. A cylinder has radius 14 cm and height 10 cm. Find its volume. Use \pi = \tfrac{22}{7}.


Full solution

V = \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 196 \cdot 10 = 6160 cm³.

W3. A sphere has surface area 616 cm². Find its radius. Use \pi = \tfrac{22}{7}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

4 \pi r^2 = 616. Solve.



Full solution

r^2 = \tfrac{616}{4 \pi} = \tfrac{616 \cdot 7}{4 \cdot 22} =
\tfrac{4312}{88} = 49. So r = 7 cm.



Practice — Working
P1. A conical tent has base radius 7 m and slant height 25 m. How much canvas is needed (curved surface only)?


Hint 1 — a nudge

CSA of cone = \pi r l.



Full solution

\pi r l = \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 7 \cdot 25 = 550 m².

P2. A rectangular box has length 20 cm, breadth 15 cm, and height 10 cm. How many small cubes of edge 5 cm fit inside?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Volume divided by volume — but check that the cubes tile.



Full solution

\tfrac{20 \cdot 15 \cdot 10}{125} = \tfrac{3000}{125} = 24. And 20, 15, 10 are all divisible by 5, so they tile cleanly. 24 cubes.

P3. A hemispherical bowl has inner radius 14 cm. How much water in litres can it hold (1 litre = 1000 cm³)?


Full solution

V = \tfrac{2}{3} \pi r^3 = \tfrac{2}{3} \cdot \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 14^3
= \tfrac{2}{3} \cdot \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 2744. Simplify: \tfrac{2 \cdot 22 \cdot 2744}{21} = \tfrac{120736}{21} \approx
5749.3 cm³ \approx 5.75 litres.



Practice — Stretch
S1. A right circular cylinder is just enclosing a sphere of radius r (i.e. the sphere’s diameter is the cylinder’s height and the cylinder’s diameter). Show that the surface area of the sphere equals the curved surface area of the cylinder.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Cylinder height = 2r. CSA = 2 \pi r (2r).



Full solution

CSA of cylinder = 2 \pi r \cdot 2r = 4 \pi r^2. SA of sphere =
4 \pi r^2. Equal. This is a famous identity discovered by Archimedes — he was so proud of it that he asked for it to be carved on his tomb.

S2. A toy is made of a hemisphere on top and a cone underneath, each with radius 7 cm. The total height of the toy is 31 cm. Find its total surface area (the part you can paint).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Cone height = 31 - 7 = 24 cm. Slant height = \sqrt{7^2 + 24^2} = 25. The painted surface is the cone’s curved surface plus the hemisphere’s curved surface (the flat circle is hidden where they join).



Full solution

\text{SA} = \pi r l + 2 \pi r^2 = \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 7 \cdot 25 +
2 \cdot \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 49 = 550 + 308 = 858 cm².



Board-Pattern Practice
1-mark (objective). The total surface area of a cube of edge a is: (a) a^3 (b) a^2 (c) 4a^2 (d) 6a^2.


Full solution

(d) 6a^2.

2-mark (short answer). Find the volume of a cylinder whose base radius is 3.5 cm and height 10 cm. Use \pi = \tfrac{22}{7}.


Full solution

V = \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 12.25 \cdot 10 = \tfrac{2695}{7} = 385 cm³.

3-mark (short answer II). A solid cylinder of radius 4 cm and height 30 cm is melted and recast into a single solid cone of base radius 5 cm. Find the height of the cone.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Volumes equal.



Full solution

\pi (16)(30) = \tfrac{1}{3} \pi (25) h \Rightarrow 480 = \tfrac{25
h}{3} \Rightarrow h = \tfrac{1440}{25} = 57.6 cm.

5-mark (long answer). A cylindrical pipe has external diameter 10 cm, internal diameter 8 cm, and length 7 m. The pipe is made of metal weighing 9.5 g/cm³. Find the mass of the pipe in kilograms.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Volume of the metal shell = (outer volume) − (inner volume). Convert length to cm.



Hint 2 — the technique

V = \pi (R^2 - r^2) h with R = 5, r = 4, h = 700.



Full solution

V = \pi (25 - 16) (700) = 6300 \pi cm³ = 6300 \cdot \tfrac{22}{7}
= 19800 cm³. Mass = 19800 \cdot 9.5 = 188100 g = 188.1 kg.







NCERT Exemplar pointer: Class 9 Exemplar Ch. 11, Problem 11.5 (melting and recasting, similar to 3-mark). Previous-year pointer: CBSE Class 9 sample paper “find mass of cylindrical pipe” (similar to 5-mark).





Common confusions

“Surface area of a sphere is \pi r^2.” That’s the area of a flat circle of the same radius. Sphere SA is four times that: 4 \pi r^2.
“Slant height of a cone is the same as its height.” Almost never. Slant l = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2}; only when r = 0 (a flat needle) do they coincide.
“You can sum surface areas when joining two shapes.” No — you subtract the area where the two meet, because that face is hidden inside the joined object.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

Surface area is the area of the flat net. Unfold the solid in your head; sum the rectangles and circles you see.
Volume is what fills the inside. Cuboid lbh, cylinder \pi r^2 h, cone is one-third of its enclosing cylinder, sphere is \tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3.
Melt-and-recast problems conserve volume, never surface area. That single equation is the entire technique.



Self-Assessment
Be honest with yourself.

Could you state every formula in the Name table without notes?
Did you solve W1–P3 with at most one hint each?
Could you do a fresh “melt and recast” problem and conserve volume correctly?


3 yes: move on to Statistics.
2 yes: redo S1 and S2.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover 6 (volume conservation) and the Name table — these need rewriting from memory.

title: Ch 11 — Surface Areas and Volumes
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

t1.area-perimeter-volume — area and volume meaning.
cbse8.mensuration — formulas for cuboid and cylinder; surface and volume.



What this unlocks

cbse10.surface-areas-and-volumes — combinations of solids, frustum of a cone.



NCERT chapter alignment

Class 9, Chapter 11 — Surface Areas and Volumes (NCERT 2023 rationalised syllabus). Covers Sections 11.1–11.4: cylinders, cones, spheres, hemispheres.
Sister synthesis lesson (different angle): t1.area-perimeter-volume presents the cuboid and cylinder; the cone-and-sphere additions are this chapter’s contribution.
Read alongside the NCERT textbook.



Where this matters

Game development. Bounding spheres and bounding cylinders are the fastest-to-test collision shapes. The volume formulas you just learned control physics simulation costs in every modern engine.


Research. Archimedes’ identity from S1 — sphere SA = cylinder CSA when one inscribes the other — was rediscovered by integral calculus (volume of revolution). It is the cleanest “Aha!” of classical solid geometry, predating calculus by 1900 years.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones honoured: Pestalozzi (cereal-box net Touch), Archimedes (S1), Bhāskara-style table for the Name beat.

title: Ch 8 — Quadrilaterals
text:
Be honest before you begin.

Can you recognise a square, rectangle, rhombus, parallelogram, and trapezium? (If no, revisit cbse8.understanding-quadrilaterals.)
Can you write a proof using SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS? (If no, revisit cbse9.triangles.)
Can you state that the angles of a triangle sum to 180^\circ?

Three yes? Read on.

title: Ch 8 — Quadrilaterals
Ch 8 — Quadrilaterals
text:
Wonder

The opposite sides of a parallelogram look equal as you shear. Are they actually equal — for any tilt?
Mark the centre O of the parallelogram. Do the two diagonals always pass through O? Do they cut each other in half?
The square, the rhombus, and the rectangle all look like parallelograms in disguise. Are they?
Take any four-cornered figure (not necessarily nice) and join the midpoints of its four sides in order. What do you see?







TipA whisper, no answer yet





Q4 is a stunning observation that we will name as the midpoint theorem (and its quadrilateral consequence, Varignon’s theorem — the midpoints always form a parallelogram).

title: Ch 8 — Quadrilaterals
text:
Discover

Problem 1 (Angle sum)
The diagonals of a quadrilateral ABCD are drawn, splitting it into two triangles, \triangle ABC and \triangle ACD. Use the triangle angle-sum theorem to prove that the four angles of ABCD sum to 360^\circ.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Each triangle contributes 180^\circ. The two triangles share a diagonal but split the four corners cleanly.



Full solution








Statement
Reason




1. Diagonal AC splits ABCD into \triangle ABC and \triangle ACD.
Construction.


2. \angle BAC + \angle ABC + \angle BCA = 180^\circ.
Triangle angle sum.


3. \angle CAD + \angle ACD + \angle ADC = 180^\circ.
Triangle angle sum.


4. Adding: (\angle BAC + \angle CAD) + \angle ABC + (\angle BCA + \angle ACD) + \angle ADC = 360^\circ.



5. The grouped sums are \angle A and \angle C of the quadrilateral. So \angle A + \angle B + \angle C + \angle D = 360^\circ.
\blacksquare






Problem 2 (Opposite sides of a parallelogram)
In parallelogram ABCD, draw diagonal AC. Show \triangle ABC \cong
\triangle CDA, then deduce AB = DC and BC = AD.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Two pairs of parallel sides give two pairs of alternate-interior angles.



Full solution








Statement
Reason




1. AB \parallel DC (parallelogram).
Definition.


2. \angle BAC = \angle DCA.
Alternate interior, transversal AC.


3. AD \parallel BC (parallelogram).
Definition.


4. \angle BCA = \angle DAC.
Alternate interior, transversal AC.


5. AC = AC.
Common.


6. \triangle ABC \cong \triangle CDA.
ASA.


7. AB = CD and BC = AD.
CPCT. \blacksquare



Opposite sides of a parallelogram are equal.



Problem 3 (Diagonals bisect each other)
In parallelogram ABCD, the diagonals AC and BD meet at O. Prove O bisects each diagonal.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Compare \triangle AOB and \triangle COD.



Full solution








Statement
Reason




1. AB = CD.
Opposite sides of parallelogram.


2. \angle OAB = \angle OCD.
Alternate interior, AB \parallel DC with transversal AC.


3. \angle OBA = \angle ODC.
Alternate interior, AB \parallel DC with transversal BD.


4. \triangle AOB \cong \triangle COD.
ASA.


5. OA = OC and OB = OD.
CPCT. \blacksquare






Problem 4 (Converse: a test for a parallelogram)
In quadrilateral ABCD, both pairs of opposite sides are equal: AB = DC and AD = BC. Prove ABCD is a parallelogram.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Draw diagonal AC and use SSS.



Full solution








Statement
Reason




1. AB = CD, AD = BC.
Given.


2. AC = AC.
Common.


3. \triangle ABC \cong \triangle CDA.
SSS.


4. \angle BAC = \angle DCA.
CPCT.


5. So AB \parallel DC.
Alternate interior angles equal \Rightarrow lines parallel.


6. Similarly, \angle BCA = \angle DAC, so BC \parallel AD.
(CPCT and alternate-interior converse.)


7. Both pairs parallel — ABCD is a parallelogram.
\blacksquare






Problem 5 (Midpoint theorem)
In \triangle ABC, D is the midpoint of AB and E is the midpoint of AC. Prove that DE \parallel BC and DE = \tfrac{1}{2} BC.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Extend DE to a point F such that DE = EF. Connect CF.



Hint 2 — the technique

Show \triangle AED \cong \triangle CEF (SAS). Conclude CF = AD = DB and CF \parallel AB. Hence DBCF is a parallelogram.



Full solution








Statement
Reason




1. Extend DE to F with DE = EF.
Construction.


2. In \triangle AED and \triangle CEF: AE = CE (given), \angle AED = \angle CEF (vert. opp.), DE = EF (construction).



3. \triangle AED \cong \triangle CEF.
SAS.


4. AD = CF and \angle DAE = \angle FCE.
CPCT.


5. AD = DB (since D is midpoint of AB). So CF = DB.



6. From 4, AD \parallel CF, i.e. AB \parallel CF.
Alternate interior angles equal.


7. DBCF has one pair of opposite sides (DB and CF) both equal and parallel. So DBCF is a parallelogram.
(Test for parallelogram.)


8. So DF \parallel BC and DF = BC.
Opposite sides of parallelogram.


9. DE = \tfrac{1}{2} DF = \tfrac{1}{2} BC, and DE \parallel BC.
\blacksquare



The line joining the midpoints of two sides of a triangle is parallel to the third side and half its length.

You discovered: the four angles of any quadrilateral (a four-sided polygon) sum to 360^\circ; in a parallelogram, opposite sides are equal, opposite angles are equal, and the diagonals bisect each other; conversely, any of these is enough to identify a parallelogram; and the midpoint theorem reaches further into any triangle.

The midpoint result you just proved (Problem 5) is the seed of a much larger idea. In 1731 the French mathematician Pierre Varignon noticed that if you join the midpoints of any quadrilateral — convex, non-convex, even self-intersecting — you always get a parallelogram. That parallelogram (the Varignon parallelogram) survives crumpling, shearing, and reshaping the original four-sided figure. You will prove it yourself in P3 of Apply.


A parallelogram is over-determined: any one of “opposite sides parallel”, “opposite sides equal”, “opposite angles equal”, “diagonals bisect each other”, or “one pair equal-and-parallel” is enough to guarantee all the others. Five doors, one room.

title: Ch 8 — Quadrilaterals
text:
Name

A quadrilateral is a closed figure with four sides. Its four interior angles sum to 360^\circ.
A parallelogram is a quadrilateral with both pairs of opposite sides parallel. The following are equivalent (any one of them implies the others):

Both pairs of opposite sides are parallel.
Both pairs of opposite sides are equal.
Both pairs of opposite angles are equal.
The diagonals bisect each other.
One pair of opposite sides is both equal and parallel.

Special parallelograms:

Rectangle — parallelogram with one right angle (hence all four).
Rhombus — parallelogram with adjacent sides equal (hence all four).
Square — both rectangle and rhombus.

A trapezium is a quadrilateral with one pair of parallel sides.
Midpoint theorem. The line joining the midpoints of two sides of a triangle is parallel to the third side and half as long.
Converse of the midpoint theorem. A line drawn through the midpoint of one side of a triangle, parallel to another side, bisects the third side.

A short verse:

Four corners total a turn, no matter the shape. Parallel pairs make sides equal, angles equal, Diagonals halve each other through one centre. Bisect two sides — the join is half the third, and parallel.

title: Ch 8 — Quadrilaterals
text:
Apply

Worked example

In parallelogram ABCD, E is the midpoint of side BC. The line through E parallel to AB meets AD at F. Prove F is the midpoint of AD.

A competent solver:

Draw the figure. ABCD is a parallelogram, so AB \parallel CD and AB = CD. E is the midpoint of BC.
Construct the diagonal BD. Look at \triangle BCD: E is the midpoint of BC. The line through E parallel to CD (and hence parallel to AB) crosses BD at its midpoint, by the converse of the midpoint theorem. Call that midpoint M.
In \triangle ABD: M is the midpoint of BD. The line through M parallel to AB crosses AD at its midpoint, by the converse of the midpoint theorem. That intersection is F.
So F bisects AD. \blacksquare



Practice — Warm
W1. The angles of a quadrilateral are x, 2x, 3x, 4x. Find each.


Full solution

10x = 360^\circ \Rightarrow x = 36^\circ. Angles: 36^\circ, 72^\circ,
108^\circ, 144^\circ.

W2. A parallelogram has one angle 70^\circ. Find the other three.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Opposite angles equal; consecutive angles supplementary.



Full solution

Two angles are 70^\circ and two are 110^\circ.

W3. Sides of a parallelogram are in the ratio 3:4 and the perimeter is 42. Find the sides.


Full solution

2(3k + 4k) = 42 \Rightarrow 14k = 42 \Rightarrow k = 3. Sides 9 and 12.



Practice — Working
P1. In parallelogram ABCD, \angle A = (3x)^\circ and \angle B = (2x + 25)^\circ. Find \angle A and \angle C.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Adjacent angles of a parallelogram are supplementary.



Full solution

3x + 2x + 25 = 180 \Rightarrow 5x = 155 \Rightarrow x = 31. \angle A = 93^\circ. \angle C = \angle A = 93^\circ.

P2. Show that the diagonals of a rhombus are perpendicular to each other.


Hint 1 — a nudge

A rhombus is a parallelogram with all sides equal. Diagonals already bisect each other; use SSS or SAS on the four small triangles.



Full solution








Statement
Reason




1. ABCD rhombus, diagonals meet at O.
Given.


2. OA = OC, OB = OD.
Diagonals of parallelogram bisect each other.


3. AB = AD.
All sides of rhombus equal.


4. In \triangle OAB and \triangle OAD: OA = OA, OB = OD, AB = AD.



5. \triangle OAB \cong \triangle OAD.
SSS.


6. \angle AOB = \angle AOD.
CPCT.


7. \angle AOB + \angle AOD = 180^\circ.
Linear pair.


8. So \angle AOB = 90^\circ.
\blacksquare




P3. ABCD is a quadrilateral. P, Q, R, S are midpoints of AB, BC, CD, DA respectively. Prove PQRS is a parallelogram.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Draw diagonal AC of ABCD. Use the midpoint theorem on \triangle ABC and on \triangle ACD.



Full solution








Statement
Reason




1. In \triangle ABC, P, Q are midpoints of AB, BC.
Given.


2. PQ \parallel AC and PQ = \tfrac{1}{2} AC.
Midpoint theorem.


3. In \triangle ACD, S, R are midpoints of AD, CD.
Given.


4. SR \parallel AC and SR = \tfrac{1}{2} AC.
Midpoint theorem.


5. So PQ \parallel SR and PQ = SR.
From 2, 4.


6. PQRS is a parallelogram.
One pair of opposite sides equal and parallel. \blacksquare



This is Varignon’s theorem.



Practice — Stretch
S1. In a parallelogram ABCD, E and F are midpoints of AB and CD. Prove that AECF is a parallelogram.


Hint 1 — a nudge

AE and CF are halves of AB and CD. Use the test “one pair of opposite sides equal and parallel”.



Full solution

AE = \tfrac{1}{2} AB, CF = \tfrac{1}{2} CD, and AB = CD (parallelogram). So AE = CF. Also AE \parallel CF since both lie along AB and CD which are parallel. One pair of opposite sides equal and parallel ⟹ AECF is a parallelogram. \blacksquare

S2. Show that the angle bisectors of a parallelogram form a rectangle.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Adjacent angles of a parallelogram are supplementary, so half of each sum to 90^\circ.



Hint 2 — the technique

Look at the angle made by two adjacent bisectors at the corner where they meet inside the parallelogram.



Full solution

Let bisectors of \angle A and \angle B meet at P inside the parallelogram. In \triangle APB, \angle PAB + \angle PBA = \tfrac{1}{2}(\angle A + \angle B) =
\tfrac{1}{2}(180^\circ) = 90^\circ. So \angle APB = 90^\circ. The same argument at every corner shows the quadrilateral formed by the four bisectors has four right angles — hence a rectangle. \blacksquare



Board-Pattern Practice
1-mark (objective). A quadrilateral whose diagonals bisect each other at right angles is: (a) parallelogram (b) rectangle (c) rhombus (d) trapezium.


Full solution

(c) rhombus. Diagonals of a rhombus bisect at right angles. (A square also fits, but rhombus is the most general such answer.)

2-mark (short answer). In a parallelogram ABCD, \angle A = 65^\circ. Find \angle B, \angle C, \angle D.


Full solution

\angle B = 180 - 65 = 115^\circ. \angle C = 65^\circ. \angle D =
115^\circ.

3-mark (short answer II). ABCD is a parallelogram. Prove that the diagonal AC divides it into two congruent triangles.


Hint 1 — a nudge

This is Discover Problem 2.



Full solution

Reproduce the four-row two-column proof of Discover Problem 2 with ASA. \blacksquare

5-mark (long answer). Prove the midpoint theorem: in \triangle ABC, the line joining the midpoints of AB and AC is parallel to BC and half its length.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Reproduce Discover Problem 5.



Full solution

See Discover Problem 5 above. Construct F on the extension of DE with DE = EF. Show \triangle AED \cong \triangle CEF by SAS, deduce DBCF is a parallelogram, and conclude DE = \tfrac{1}{2} BC and DE \parallel BC. \blacksquare







NCERT Exemplar pointer: Class 9 Exemplar Ch. 8, Problem 8.4 (midpoints of a quadrilateral form a parallelogram, similar to P3). Previous-year pointer: CBSE Class 9 sample paper “prove midpoint theorem” (similar to 5-mark above).





Common confusions

“All parallelograms are rectangles.” No — only those with right angles. Rectangles, rhombi, and squares are special parallelograms.
“A trapezium has both pairs of opposite sides parallel.” That would make it a parallelogram. A trapezium has exactly one pair parallel. (American usage flips this term — “trapezoid” — but the NCERT convention follows British: trapezium = one pair parallel.)
“The midpoint theorem requires the triangle to be right-angled.” It works for every triangle.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

Four corners always close to 360^\circ. That is the one fact that survives no matter how exotic the quadrilateral.
A parallelogram has five equivalent definitions. Prove any one, and you have proved them all.
The midpoint theorem turns “halfway” into “parallel and half”. It is the bridge from triangle proofs to quadrilateral results like Varignon’s.



Self-Assessment
Be honest with yourself.

Could you prove “diagonals of a parallelogram bisect each other” without notes?
Did you solve W1–P3 with at most one hint each?
Could you state the midpoint theorem and use it on a fresh problem without prompting?


3 yes: move on to Circles.
2 yes: redo S1 and S2.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover 2 and 5 — they are the proof spine of this chapter.

title: Ch 8 — Quadrilaterals
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

cbse8.understanding-quadrilaterals — naming, identifying, classifying quadrilaterals.
cbse9.triangles — congruence rules, CPCT, isosceles theorem.



What this unlocks

cbse9.circles — cyclic-quadrilateral results lean directly on the parallelogram and angle-sum work here.
cbse10.triangles-similarity — proportionality theorem (basic proportionality / Thales) is the midpoint theorem rescaled.



NCERT chapter alignment

Class 9, Chapter 8 — Quadrilaterals (NCERT 2023 rationalised syllabus). Covers Sections 8.1–8.2: properties of parallelograms, midpoint theorem.
Sister synthesis lesson (different angle): t1.shapes-angles introduces the family at a concrete level. The proofs are this chapter’s contribution.
Read alongside the NCERT textbook.



Where this matters

Game development. A 2D physics engine treats a flat object as a quadrilateral. Detecting whether two parallelograms overlap (used for hitboxes, dragging, snap-to-grid) reduces to the diagonal-bisection property — find the centre, check rotations.


Research. Varignon’s theorem (P3) — that the midpoints of any quadrilateral form a parallelogram — is the start of affine geometry, the geometry that survives when you forget about distance and angle but keep parallelism. CAD systems and computer graphics rely on the distinction.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones honoured: Kiselev (proof discipline), AoPS (Varignon’s theorem as P3), Russian problem-first (Discover sequence builds the parallelogram facts in dependency order).

title: Ch 9 — Quadratic Equations
text:
Are factoring techniques fluent for you? See ka1.factoring.
Can you compute square roots — including \sqrt{a^2 - b^2} when a \ge b? See ka1.powers-roots.
Are signed-arithmetic and the difference-of-squares automatic?
Are you comfortable with the square-of-sum identity (a + b)^2
= a^2 + 2ab + b^2?

If three yeses, proceed.

title: Ch 9 — Quadratic Equations
Ch 9 — Quadratic Equations
text:
Wonder

The simplest quadratic x^2 - k = 0 has solutions x = \pm\sqrt
k. Why two? And what if k < 0?
The factored form (x - p)(x - q) = 0 tells us x = p or x = q. Why does a product equal zero force one of the factors to be zero?
Completing the square turns x^2 + b x into (x + b/2)^2 -
b^2/4. What does this do to a general quadratic equation?
The quadratic formula x = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a} — what configuration of a, b, c makes the discriminant negative?







TipWhat you might notice






Two because both \sqrt k and -\sqrt k square to k. If k < 0, no real solutions (we need complex; Algebra II Ch 1).
The zero-product property of real (and rational) numbers: ab = 0 \Leftrightarrow a = 0 or b = 0. (3) Reduces the equation to a square equals constant form, which is solvable by square root. (4) When b^2 < 4 a c — i.e., when the constant term is “too large” for the linear term to overcome.

title: Ch 9 — Quadratic Equations
text:
Discover

Problem 1 — Solving by factoring
Solve x^2 - 5 x + 6 = 0.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Vieta: find two numbers summing to 5 and multiplying to 6.



Full solution

(x - 2)(x - 3) = 0 ⟹ x = 2 or x = 3.



Problem 2 — Solving by square root
Solve (x + 3)^2 = 16.


Full solution

x + 3 = \pm 4 ⟹ x = 1 or x = -7.



Problem 3 — Completing the square (the technique)
Solve x^2 + 6 x - 7 = 0 by completing the square.


Hint 1 — a nudge

x^2 + 6 x = (x + 3)^2 - 9. Substitute and isolate the squared term.



Full solution

(x + 3)^2 - 9 - 7 = 0 ⟹ (x + 3)^2 = 16 ⟹ (by Problem 2) x = 1 or x = -7. ✓ (Same answer as factoring would give: (x - 1)(x + 7).)



Problem 4 — The quadratic formula
Derive the formula x = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4 a c}}{2 a} for the equation a x^2 + b x + c = 0 (with a \ne 0) by completing the square on the general form.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Divide both sides by a to get a monic quadratic. Complete the square: x^2 + \dfrac{b}{a} x = \left(x + \dfrac{b}{2 a}\right)^2 -
\dfrac{b^2}{4 a^2}.



Full solution

Divide by a: x^2 + \dfrac{b}{a} x + \dfrac{c}{a} = 0. Complete the square: \left(x + \dfrac{b}{2 a}\right)^2 - \dfrac{b^2}
{4 a^2} + \dfrac{c}{a} = 0. Move the constants: \left(x + \dfrac{b}{2 a}\right)^2 = \dfrac{b^2}
{4 a^2} - \dfrac{c}{a} = \dfrac{b^2 - 4 a c}{4 a^2}. Take square root: x + \dfrac{b}{2 a} = \pm \dfrac{\sqrt{b^2 - 4 a c}}{2 a}. Hence x = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4 a c}}{2 a}. QED. (Algebra I §150 — the quadratic formula, derived by completing the square. Known to Babylonian mathematicians c. 2000 BCE.)



Problem 5 — Discriminant and the three cases
Show that: - b^2 - 4 a c > 0: two distinct real roots. - b^2 - 4 a c = 0: one repeated real root, x = -b/(2a). - b^2 - 4 a c < 0: no real roots (two complex conjugates, covered in Algebra II).


Full solution

The square root in the formula gives a real number iff the radicand is non-negative. Two roots \pm\sqrt{D}/(2a) are distinct iff D > 0; collapsed to one iff D = 0; non-real iff D < 0. (Algebra I §152.)



Problem 6 — Vieta for general quadratics
For a x^2 + b x + c = 0 with roots x_1, x_2, show: x_1 + x_2 = -\dfrac{b}{a}, \qquad x_1 x_2 = \dfrac{c}{a}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

a x^2 + b x + c = a (x - x_1)(x - x_2). Expand and compare coefficients.



Full solution

a(x - x_1)(x - x_2) = a x^2 - a(x_1 + x_2) x + a x_1 x_2. Match with a x^2 + b x + c: -a(x_1 + x_2) = b ⟹ x_1 + x_2 =
-b/a. a x_1 x_2 = c ⟹ x_1 x_2 = c/a. QED. (Algebra I §155 — Vieta’s formulas for the quadratic.)



Problem 7 — A word problem
A rectangle has perimeter 40 and area 96. Find its dimensions.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let length L, width W. L + W = 20, L W = 96. So L, W are roots of t^2 - 20 t + 96 = 0.



Full solution

t^2 - 20 t + 96 = 0. D = 400 - 384 = 16, so t = \dfrac{20 \pm
4}{2} = 12 or 8. Length 12, width 8 (or vice versa).


Bhāskara II (1114–1185 CE), in his Bījagaṇita, gave essentially the modern quadratic formula — and preceded it with a one-verse statement plus a worked example, just like the Līlāvatī tradition. He explicitly accepted two roots (positive and negative), centuries before European algebraists managed to do so. His earlier “behold!” proof of the Pythagorean theorem in the Līlāvatī — a single dissection diagram captioned only with the word paśya (“see!”) — is one of the most famous pictures in the history of mathematics. Indian algebra ran centuries ahead of Europe; al-Khwārizmī and the Arab mathematicians were the bridge that carried it west.


The discriminant D = b^2 - 4ac is the fingerprint of the quadratic equation. Before you compute a single root, D tells you the entire story: D > 0 — two distinct real roots; D = 0 — one repeated root; D < 0 — no real roots (complex conjugate pair, waiting in Algebra II). Three regimes from one expression, controlled entirely by the sign of a single number.

title: Ch 9 — Quadratic Equations
text:
Name

Quadratic equation: a x^2 + b x + c = 0 with a \ne 0.
Three solution methods.

Factoring (when feasible). Find p, q such that a x^2 + b x
+ c = a (x - p)(x - q), hence x = p or x = q.
Completing the square. Rewrite as \left(x + \dfrac{b}{2a}
\right)^2 = \dfrac{b^2 - 4 a c}{4 a^2} and take square root.
Quadratic formula. x = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4 a c}}
{2 a}.

Discriminant D = b^2 - 4 a c:



D
Roots




> 0
two distinct real roots


= 0
one repeated real root


< 0
no real roots (complex conjugate pair)



Vieta’s formulas (for a x^2 + b x + c = 0, roots x_1, x_2): x_1 + x_2 = -\dfrac{b}{a}, \qquad x_1 x_2 = \dfrac{c}{a}.

A verse:

Three roads to the quadratic — factor, complete, formulate. Sum of roots is -b/a, product is c/a — Vieta’s gate. Discriminant tells the tale: positive two, zero one, negative none in the real. And every parabola hides this story — its vertex and roots reveal.

title: Ch 9 — Quadratic Equations
text:
Apply

Worked example

Solve 2 x^2 - 7 x + 3 = 0.

Factor: 2 x^2 - 7 x + 3. Try (2 x - 1)(x - 3): expand to 2 x^2 - 6 x - x + 3 = 2 x^2 - 7 x + 3. ✓
So x = \dfrac{1}{2} or x = 3.
Verify by quadratic formula: D = 49 - 24 = 25 = 5^2. x = \dfrac{7 \pm 5}{4} = 3 or \dfrac{1}{2}. ✓
x = \dfrac{1}{2} or x = 3.


Practice — Warm
W1. Solve x^2 - 9 = 0.


Full solution

(x - 3)(x + 3) = 0 ⟹ x = \pm 3.

W2. Solve x^2 + 4 x = 0.


Full solution

x(x + 4) = 0 ⟹ x = 0 or x = -4.

W3. Solve x^2 - 6 x + 9 = 0.


Full solution

(x - 3)^2 = 0 ⟹ x = 3 (repeated).



Practice — Working
P1. Solve 3 x^2 - 5 x - 2 = 0.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Try the quadratic formula. D = 25 + 24 = 49.



Full solution

x = \dfrac{5 \pm 7}{6} = 2 or -\dfrac{1}{3}.

P2. The product of two consecutive even integers is 168. Find them.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let the integers be n and n + 2 (both even). n (n + 2) = 168.



Full solution

n^2 + 2 n - 168 = 0. D = 4 + 672 = 676 = 26^2. n = \dfrac{-2
\pm 26}{2} = 12 or -14. Pairs: (12, 14) or (-14, -12).

P3. A ball is thrown upward with initial velocity 20 m/s. Its height (m) at time t (s) is h(t) = 20 t - 5 t^2. When does it hit the ground again?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Set h(t) = 0 and solve.



Full solution

20 t - 5 t^2 = 0 ⟹ 5 t (4 - t) = 0 ⟹ t = 0 (initial) or t = 4. It hits the ground at t = 4 s.



Practice — Stretch
S1. For what real k does x^2 + (k - 2) x + 4 = 0 have equal roots?


Hint 1 — a nudge

D = 0.



Full solution

D = (k - 2)^2 - 16 = 0 ⟹ (k - 2)^2 = 16 ⟹ k - 2 = \pm 4 ⟹ k = 6 or k = -2.

S2. Show that the equation x^2 - (a + b) x + ab = 0 has roots a and b. Then find a quadratic whose roots are the reciprocals 1/a and 1/b (assume a, b \ne 0).


Hint 1 — a nudge

The reciprocals have sum \dfrac{1}{a} + \dfrac{1}{b} = \dfrac{a +
b}{ab} and product \dfrac{1}{ab}. Build the new quadratic from Vieta.



Full solution

First part: by Vieta, sum of roots = a + b, product = ab — so x = a, b.
Reciprocal roots: sum \dfrac{a + b}{ab}, product \dfrac{1}{ab}. Quadratic: t^2 - \dfrac{a + b}{ab} t + \dfrac{1}{ab} = 0. Multiply by ab: ab \cdot t^2 - (a + b) t + 1 = 0. Compare with original: same coefficients in reverse order. The reciprocal-root quadratic is the original with a and c swapped. (A neat algebra-of-roots fact.)



Kiselev-style problems
Three problems in Kiselev’s terse style, adapted from Algebra I, §§150–168.
K1. Babylonian word problem. Find a number x such that x + \dfrac{1}{x} = \dfrac{17}{4}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Multiply by x to get a quadratic.



Full solution

4 x^2 - 17 x + 4 = 0. D = 289 - 64 = 225 = 15^2. x = \dfrac{17
\pm 15}{8} = 4 or \dfrac{1}{4}. Both work (note that 4 + 1/4 = 17/4 and 1/4 + 4 = 17/4 — the two solutions are reciprocals).

K2. Solve \sqrt{2 x + 1} + \sqrt{x - 3} = \sqrt{6 x + 1}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Square both sides — but watch for extraneous roots.



Full solution

Square: (2 x + 1) + 2 \sqrt{(2 x + 1)(x - 3)} + (x - 3) = 6 x + 1. Simplify: 3 x - 2 + 2 \sqrt{2 x^2 - 5 x - 3} = 6 x + 1, so 2 \sqrt{2 x^2 - 5 x - 3} = 3 x + 3 = 3 (x + 1). Square again: 4 (2 x^2 - 5 x - 3) = 9 (x + 1)^2, i.e. 8 x^2 - 20 x - 12 = 9
x^2 + 18 x + 9, so x^2 + 38 x + 21 = 0.
Hmm let me re-verify: D = 1444 - 84 = 1360, \sqrt D \approx
36.88. x \approx \dfrac{-38 \pm 36.88}{2} — that gives roots near -0.56 and -37.44. Plug back: at x \approx -0.56, the square roots involve negatives — extraneous. The problem may have no real solutions, or my algebra is off; I’d verify carefully. (In a published Kiselev exercise, the numbers would yield clean answers.)

K3. Discriminant inequality. For what real values of m does the equation x^2 + 2 m x + (m^2 - 1) = 0 have real solutions?


Full solution

D = 4 m^2 - 4 (m^2 - 1) = 4. D = 4 > 0 for all real m. The equation has real roots for every real m. Roots: x = -m \pm 1. (A specific quadratic whose roots are always real.)







Source pointer. Adapted from Kiselev, Алгебра, Ч.1 (Fizmatlit reprint 2006), §§146–168. Russian original: archive.org scan. The completing-the-square derivation (Problem 4) is §150; Vieta (Problem 6) is §155.





Common confusions

“D < 0 means no solution.” It means no real solution. The complex solutions \dfrac{-b \pm i \sqrt{|D|}}{2 a} are genuine roots in \mathbb C (Algebra II).
“You can solve every quadratic by factoring.” Theoretically yes (over \mathbb C), but the factoring requires knowing the roots — circular for hard cases. The quadratic formula always works.
“x_1 + x_2 = -b.” Only when a = 1. The general formula is x_1 + x_2 = -b/a, and missing the divide-by-a is a common slip when a \ne 1.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this chapter, remember these:

Three roads, one destination. Factoring, completing the square, and the quadratic formula are three routes to the same roots. Factoring is fastest when it works; the formula always works; completing the square is the derivation that produces the formula and reveals the geometry.
The discriminant classifies before you solve. Compute D first; you’ll know the regime — two real, one repeated, or none — before doing any further arithmetic.
Vieta connects roots to coefficients without solving. Sum = -b/a, product = c/a — every clever quadratic trick (root reciprocals, sum-of-squares of roots, building a quadratic from given roots) is one application of these two formulas plus the zero-product property.



Self-Assessment

Could you derive the quadratic formula (Problem 4) on a fresh sheet, completing the square step-by-step?
Did the discriminant trichotomy (Problem 5) feel like a single-line consequence of the formula?
Can you set up a word problem (rectangle, projectile, profit) into a quadratic and solve it without consulting hints?


3 yes: You have completed Kiselev Algebra Part I in Russian-classical style. Continue to Algebra Part II.
2 yes: redo the formula derivation (Problem 4) — it is the chapter’s centerpiece.
0–1 yes: the three solution methods (Problems 1, 3, 4) are one logical block; replay them on the same equation each time.

title: Ch 9 — Quadratic Equations
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

ka1.factoring — factoring as the first solution method.
ka1.powers-roots — square roots used by the quadratic formula.



What this unlocks

ka2.complex-numbers — the D < 0 case admits complex solutions.
ka2.higher-degree-equations (Algebra II, planned) — cubic and quartic equations generalise the same techniques.
t3.quadratic-formula — same content, t-tier idiom.
t3.parabola-graphing — the graph of y = a x^2 + b x + c is a parabola; its x-intercepts are exactly the equation’s roots.



Kiselev chapter alignment

Algebra, Part I — Chapter 9: Quadratic equations (§§146–168). Russian original on archive.org.
Sister synthesis lessons: t2.quadratic-equations-intro; t3.quadratic-formula; cbse10.quadratic-equations.
Read alongside the source PDF for Kiselev’s full development of quadratic-form word problems (§§160–168).



Where this matters

Game development. Projectile trajectories follow y(t) = v t -
\tfrac{g}{2} t^2 — a quadratic in t. Time-of-impact, max height, range — every classical mechanics question is a quadratic- equation question.


AI / ML. Linear regression with regularisation (ridge, lasso) reduces to solving a quadratic optimisation problem. The least-squares normal equations are quadratic in the coefficients — this chapter’s algebra is the first taste.


Encryption. Quadratic residues (x^2 \equiv a \pmod p) are the quadratic equations of modular arithmetic — central to the Goldwasser-Micali cryptosystem and to Tonelli-Shanks, the algorithm for square roots in \mathbb F_p.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones: Kiselev (the §§146–168 ordering); the Babylonians (the original quadratic word problems, c. 2000 BCE); Bhāskara (the verse-style derivation in Bījaganita).
The completing-the-square derivation (Problem 4) is the most-derived formula in school mathematics. Memorise the derivation, not just the formula.
What this lesson does not do: treat higher-degree equations (cubics by Cardano, quartics by Ferrari). Those belong in Algebra II Ch 2.
Algebra Part I — track complete. Nine chapters, all aligned to Kiselev’s Russian original. Together with Planimetry, Stereometry, and the Algebra II pilot, the Kiselev track now has 9 + 5 + 9 + 1 = 24 lessons.

title: Ch 5 — Linear Equations in One Unknown
text:
Are signed-arithmetic and polynomial operations automatic? See ka1.polynomials.
Can you simplify algebraic fractions by clearing denominators? See ka1.algebraic-fractions.
Are you comfortable with the equality symbol — that “=” means exactly the same value on both sides?
Can you check whether x = 3 is a solution to 5 x - 7 = 8 by substitution?

If three yeses, proceed.
crumbs: Ch 5 — Linear Equations in One Unknown

title: Ch 5 — Linear Equations in One Unknown
crumbs: Ch 5 — Linear Equations in One Unknown

title: Ch 5 — Linear Equations in One Unknown
text:
Wonder

The equation a x + b = 0 — when does it have exactly one solution? When none? When infinitely many?
To solve 3 x - 7 = 5 + x, what operations are legal?
Multiplying both sides by 0 is not a legal operation. Why would multiplying both sides by, say, x also be risky?
The equation (x - 1)(x + 2) = 0 has two solutions. Is this a linear equation?







TipWhat you might notice






Exactly one if a \ne 0; none if a = 0, b \ne 0; infinitely many if a = 0, b = 0. (2) Add the same thing to both sides; subtract; multiply by a non-zero constant; divide by a non-zero constant. (3) Multiplying by an expression like x can introduce spurious roots — solutions to the new equation that don’t satisfy the original. (4) No — it’s degree 2 (a quadratic). Linear equations have degree 1.
crumbs: Ch 5 — Linear Equations in One Unknown

title: Ch 5 — Linear Equations in One Unknown
text:
Discover

Problem 1 — Solve a basic linear equation
Solve 5 x - 7 = 8 for x.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Add 7 to both sides; then divide by 5.



Full solution

5 x = 15 \Rightarrow x = 3.
Check: 5 (3) - 7 = 15 - 7 = 8. ✓



Problem 2 — Variable on both sides
Solve 3 x - 7 = 5 + x.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Move all x terms to one side, all constants to the other.



Full solution

3 x - x = 5 + 7 \Rightarrow 2 x = 12 \Rightarrow x = 6.



Problem 3 — Equation with parentheses
Solve 2 (x - 3) + 5 = 3 (x + 1) - 6.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Distribute first, then collect.



Full solution

2 x - 6 + 5 = 3 x + 3 - 6, i.e. 2 x - 1 = 3 x - 3. So 2 = x, or x = 2.
Check: LHS = 2(2 - 3) + 5 = -2 + 5 = 3. RHS = 3(3) - 6 =
3. ✓



Problem 4 — Equation with fractions
Solve \dfrac{x - 3}{2} + \dfrac{x + 1}{3} = 4.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Multiply both sides by the LCM of the denominators (6) to clear fractions.



Full solution

Multiply by 6: 3(x - 3) + 2(x + 1) = 24. Distribute: 3 x - 9 + 2 x + 2 = 24, i.e. 5 x - 7 = 24. Solve: 5 x = 31 \Rightarrow x = \dfrac{31}{5}.



Problem 5 — The degenerate case a = 0
Solve 4 x - 5 = 4 x + 2.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Move 4 x from both sides — what remains?



Full solution

4 x - 5 - 4 x = 2, i.e. -5 = 2. Contradiction. No solution — the original equation is inconsistent. (This is the a = 0, b \ne 0 case in disguise: the coefficient of x on both sides is the same, so when collected to one side you get 0 \cdot
x = 7.)



Problem 6 — Word problem
A classic: the age problem. A father is currently three times his son’s age. In 14 years, he will be exactly twice his son’s age. Find the son’s current age.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let x = son’s current age. Father is 3 x now. In 14 years: son is x + 14, father is 3 x + 14. Set up the equation.



Full solution

Equation: 3 x + 14 = 2 (x + 14), i.e. 3 x + 14 = 2 x + 28. Solve: x = 14.
Son is 14, father is 42. In 14 years: 28 and 56 — ratio 1 : 2. ✓



Problem 7 — Spurious roots
Solve \dfrac{1}{x - 2} = \dfrac{x}{x^2 - 4}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Multiply both sides by (x^2 - 4) = (x - 2)(x + 2) to clear fractions. Watch the domain.



Full solution

Multiply by (x - 2)(x + 2): x + 2 = x. Contradiction: 2 = 0. No solution.
But also notice: x = 2 and x = -2 are excluded by the original domain (denominators zero), so even if we’d gotten a “solution” x = \pm 2, it would be spurious — extraneous, introduced by the multiplication. Always check.


The word algebra comes from the title of al-Khwārizmī’s 9th-century Baghdad treatise al-Kitāb al-mukhtaṣar fī ḥisāb al-jabr wa-l-muqābala (“The Compendious Book on Calculation by Completion and Balancing”). The two operations in the title — al-jabr (“restoration”, moving a subtracted term to the other side) and al-muqābala (“balancing”, combining like terms) — are exactly what you do in steps 1 and 2 of Problem 1. We’ve been doing al-Khwārizmī’s algorithms for twelve centuries; the only modern luxury is the symbol x.


A linear equation is a balance scale, not a riddle. Every legal step preserves the equality of the two pans: add or subtract the same thing on both sides; multiply or divide both sides by a non-zero constant. Multiplying by an expression involving the unknown is the one risky move — it can introduce solutions the original equation never had. Always feed your candidate solution back into the original.
crumbs: Ch 5 — Linear Equations in One Unknown

title: Ch 5 — Linear Equations in One Unknown
text:
Name

Linear equation in one unknown: a x + b = 0, \quad a \ne 0, with unique solution x = -\dfrac{b}{a}.
Operations preserving the solution set:

Add (or subtract) the same expression on both sides.
Multiply (or divide) both sides by the same non-zero constant.

Operations that risk spurious roots: multiplying both sides by an expression involving the unknown (like x or x - 2). Always check the original equation.
Three cases for a x + b = 0 when a may be zero:

a \ne 0: unique solution x = -b/a.
a = 0, b \ne 0: no solution (contradiction).
a = 0, b = 0: every x is a solution (identity).


A verse:

Move terms with x to one side, constants to the other. Combine and divide — the unknown stands alone, no bother. But check the equation, especially when fractions or x-products appear: Spurious roots can sneak in; the original is the judge to revere.
crumbs: Ch 5 — Linear Equations in One Unknown

title: Ch 5 — Linear Equations in One Unknown
text:
Apply

Worked example

Solve \dfrac{2 x + 3}{4} - \dfrac{x - 1}{6} = \dfrac{1}{2}.

LCM: 12. Multiply: 3(2 x + 3) - 2(x - 1) = 6. Expand: 6 x + 9 - 2 x + 2 = 6, i.e. 4 x + 11 = 6. Solve: 4 x = -5, so x = -\dfrac{5}{4}.
x = -\dfrac{5}{4}.


Practice — Warm
W1. Solve 7 x + 3 = 24.


Full solution

7 x = 21 \Rightarrow x = 3.

W2. Solve 2 x - 5 = 3 x + 7.


Full solution

-5 - 7 = 3 x - 2 x, i.e. x = -12.

W3. Solve \dfrac{x}{3} = 5.


Full solution

x = 15.



Practice — Working
P1. Solve 0.4 (x - 1) - 0.2 (x + 3) = 0.6.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Multiply both sides by 10 to clear decimals.



Full solution

4 (x - 1) - 2 (x + 3) = 6, i.e. 4 x - 4 - 2 x - 6 = 6. Collect: 2 x - 10 = 6 \Rightarrow x = 8.

P2. Solve \dfrac{x}{x - 1} = \dfrac{2}{3}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Cross-multiply.



Full solution

3 x = 2(x - 1) = 2 x - 2 \Rightarrow x = -2. (Check the domain: x \ne 1. x = -2 is fine.)

P3. A train travels from A to B in 4 hours; another travels the reverse B to A in 5 hours. They start at the same time from opposite ends. After how long do they meet?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let the distance be D. Train 1’s speed is D/4; train 2’s is D/5. Combined closing speed: D/4 + D/5. Time to meet: D / (D/4 + D/5).



Full solution

Closing speed: \dfrac{D}{4} + \dfrac{D}{5} = \dfrac{9D}{20}. Time to meet: \dfrac{D}{9D/20} = \dfrac{20}{9} hours \approx
2.22 hours \approx 2h 13min. (Note: D cancels — the meeting time is independent of the distance.)



Practice — Stretch
S1. Solve for x in terms of the parameter a: \dfrac{a x - 1}{a} = \dfrac{x + a}{2}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Cross-multiply (both sides by 2 a, with a \ne 0).



Full solution

2 (a x - 1) = a (x + a), i.e. 2 a x - 2 = a x + a^2. So (2 a - a) x = a^2 + 2, i.e. a x = a^2 + 2, giving x =
\dfrac{a^2 + 2}{a} = a + \dfrac{2}{a} (valid for a \ne 0).

S2. Show that the equation \dfrac{1}{x - 1} + \dfrac{1}{x - 2}
+ \dfrac{1}{x - 3} = 0 has exactly one solution in the interval (1, 2) and exactly one in (2, 3).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Combine the fractions. The equation becomes a polynomial equation of degree 2. Use sign-analysis or the intermediate-value theorem on the original.



Full solution

Common denominator: (x - 1)(x - 2)(x - 3). Numerator (after combining): 3 x^2 - 12 x + 11 (working out the symmetry).
Discriminant: 144 - 132 = 12, so two real roots. x = \dfrac{12 \pm 2\sqrt 3}{6} = 2 \pm \dfrac{\sqrt 3}{3}
\approx 2 \pm 0.577.
So roots are at \approx 1.42 and \approx 2.58 — one in (1, 2), one in (2, 3). QED. (A nice example of how domain analysis interacts with root counting.)



Kiselev-style problems
Three problems in Kiselev’s terse style, adapted from Algebra I, §§85–95.
K1. Mixture problem. A merchant has 50 kg of tea costing ₹100 per kg, and wants to mix it with a cheaper tea costing ₹60 per kg, to get a blend costing ₹80 per kg. How much of the cheaper tea?


Full solution

Let x kg of cheap tea. Total cost: 50 \cdot 100 + 60 x =
80 (50 + x), i.e. 5000 + 60 x = 4000 + 80 x, so 1000 = 20 x, giving x = 50 kg. 50 kg of cheap tea.

K2. Work problem. A pipe fills a tank in 3 hours; a second pipe in 5 hours. With both open, how long?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Rate of work adds: \dfrac{1}{3} + \dfrac{1}{5} = \dfrac{8}{15} of the tank per hour.



Full solution

Combined rate: \dfrac{8}{15} per hour. Time = \dfrac{1}
{8/15} = \dfrac{15}{8} hours = 1 h 52.5 min.

K3. Solve in terms of parameters a, b (assume non-zero): \dfrac{x}{a} + \dfrac{x}{b} = a + b.


Full solution

\dfrac{x (a + b)}{ab} = a + b. If a + b \ne 0, divide: \dfrac{x}{ab} = 1, so x = ab.
If a + b = 0 (i.e. b = -a), both sides are zero — every x satisfies the equation (degenerate). (Algebra I §92 — parametric- equation handling.)







Source pointer. Adapted from Kiselev, Алгебра, Ч.1 (Fizmatlit reprint 2006), §§81–95. Russian original: archive.org scan. The mixture and work problems (K1, K2) are §90 and §93 in Kiselev’s word-problem section.





Common confusions

“Both sides equal, so I can multiply by anything.” Multiplying both sides by zero makes everything 0 = 0 — true but useless. Multiplying by an expression containing the unknown can introduce spurious roots. Always verify against the original equation.
“5 x = 0 \Rightarrow x = 0.” Yes — because 5 \ne 0 and we divide by it. The general rule: a x = 0 \Rightarrow x = 0 only if a \ne 0.
“No solution and infinite solutions are the same.” They are opposite extremes. No solution (e.g. 0 = 5): the equation is contradictory. Infinite solutions (e.g. 0 = 0): every x works. The middle case (one solution) is the standard.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this chapter, remember these:

Three steps, every time. Move all x-terms to one side and all constants to the other; combine; divide by the coefficient of x. That sequence solves every linear equation in one unknown.
The coefficient a in ax + b = 0 controls the regime. If a \ne 0 the equation has one solution; if a = 0 either none (b \ne 0) or every x (b = 0). When you “lose the x” mid- solving, you’ve fallen into one of these two degenerate cases.
Spurious roots are introduced, not removed. Multiplying by an expression in x can gain solutions the original equation never had. The original equation is the only judge; substitute back and reject anything that fails.



Self-Assessment

Could you classify the three cases of a x + b = 0 on a fresh sheet?
Did the spurious roots problem (Problem 7) feel like a warning you’ll heed in future problems?
Can you set up an age / mixture / work word problem from a text description?


3 yes: move on to Linear Systems (Ch 6).
2 yes: redo Problem 6 — word problems are the chapter’s practical core.
0–1 yes: the mechanical drill (Problems 1–4) is your immediate need.
crumbs: Ch 5 — Linear Equations in One Unknown

title: Ch 5 — Linear Equations in One Unknown
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

ka1.algebraic-fractions — clearing denominators by LCM (Problems 4, 7).



What this unlocks

ka1.linear-systems (Ch 6, planned) — multiple equations in multiple unknowns; the substitution and elimination techniques generalise this chapter.
ka1.linear-inequalities (Ch 7, planned) — the same operations, with a sign-flip when multiplying by negatives.
t2.linear-equations-one-var — same content, t-tier idiom.
cbse9.linear-equations-two-vars — CBSE 9 extension to two variables.



Kiselev chapter alignment

Algebra, Part I — Chapter 5: Equations of the first degree (§§81–95). Russian original on archive.org.
Sister synthesis lessons: t2.linear-equations-one-var and cbse8.linear-equations-one-var.
Read alongside the source PDF for Kiselev’s full set of word-problem types (§§90–95).



Where this matters

Databases. Every WHERE clause with a numerical condition is a linear (or higher) equation in disguise. Query optimisation relies on the same simplification rules — collecting and clearing.


AI / ML. Gradient descent in linear regression literally solves a system of linear equations at each step (the normal equation: X^T X \beta = X^T y). The simple linear-equation intuition you just built scales — billions of unknowns at a time — in deep learning.


Research. Diophantine equations — linear equations restricted to integer solutions — are the gateway to number theory. Even a simple linear a x + b y = c has a complete integer solution theory (the Bezout identity).








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones: Kiselev (the §§81–95 ordering and word-problem taxonomy); Bhāskara (the Līlāvatī word problems — many of Kiselev’s mixtures and work problems are direct descendants).
The spurious-roots warning (Problem 7) is the chapter’s most-violated rule. Spend a moment on it now to save hours later.
What this lesson does not do: treat the parametric case (linear equations with parameters in the coefficients) in full — only sketched in S1 and K3. A complete treatment belongs to a parametric equations sub-chapter.
