title: 00 · Kiselev — Algebra, Part II
text:
Source
Russian original (public domain on archive.org): Алгебра. Часть вторая, А. П. Киселёв. The PDF stays external; the pointers in each lesson cite the relevant Russian sections.

title: 00 · Kiselev — Algebra, Part II
text:
Lessons
The full Algebra II track, in book order:

01 · Complex Numbers (Ch 1) — i^2 = -1, arithmetic on a + bi, modulus, conjugate.
02 · Higher-Degree Equations (Ch 2) — rational-root theorem, Cardano, biquadratic, Fundamental Theorem of Algebra.
03 · Higher-Degree Inequalities (Ch 3) — sign-charts, rational inequalities, AM-GM.
04 · Exponentials and Logarithms (Ch 4) — a^x, \log_a x, three log laws, change of base.
05 · Sequences and Progressions (Ch 5) — AP, GP, infinite GP convergence; means and AM-GM-HM.
06 · Combinatorics and the Binomial Theorem (Ch 6) — permutations, combinations, Pascal’s triangle, (a + b)^n.
07 · Limits and Continued Fractions (Ch 7) — sequence limits, the squeeze theorem, e as a limit, continued fractions.

Track complete. This closes the four-tier Kiselev geometry + algebra track.

title: 00 · Kiselev — Algebra, Part II
text:
Sister synthesis tiers
The synthesis lessons covering the same ground sit in Tier 3 Algebra II / Geometry / Trig (quadratic formula, sequences and series, logarithms, combinatorics, binomial theorem) and the more advanced Tier 6 Undergrad Advanced (complex numbers and complex functions). The CBSE Class 11 track (Class 11) covers the same material under board-exam discipline.

title: Ch 3 — Higher-Degree Inequalities
text:
Are linear-inequality manipulations (especially the flip on negative multiplication) automatic? See ka1.linear-inequalities.
Can you factor a polynomial into linear factors? See ka2.higher-degree-equations.
Are you comfortable with interval notation — open and closed intervals, unions?
Can you reason about the sign of a product? (Even number of negatives ⇒ positive product.)

If three yeses, proceed.

title: Ch 3 — Higher-Degree Inequalities
Ch 3 — Higher-Degree Inequalities
text:
Wonder

Each simple root flips the sign. What about a double root — does the sign flip there too?
The inequality p(x) > 0 — how do we describe its solution set in interval notation?
The expression \dfrac{x - 1}{x + 2} — its sign depends on both numerator and denominator. Where is it undefined?
The expression |x - 1| < 3 — what’s the geometric description?







TipWhat you might notice






A double root does not flip the sign — both factors of (x - r) contribute, and even-power factors keep the sign.
The solution is the union of intervals where p is positive.
Undefined where the denominator is zero (x = -2); sign-flips at both numerator and denominator zeros (one each side of the “undefined” point). (4) An interval of half-width 3 centred at 1, namely (-2, 4).

title: Ch 3 — Higher-Degree Inequalities
text:
Discover

Problem 1 — Sign-chart for a quadratic
Solve x^2 - 5 x + 6 < 0.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Factor: (x - 2)(x - 3). The product is negative when one factor is positive and the other negative.



Full solution

(x - 2)(x - 3) < 0 iff 2 < x < 3. Solution: (2, 3).



Problem 2 — Sign-chart for a cubic
Solve (x + 1)(x - 2)(x - 4) > 0.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Three roots split \mathbb R into four intervals. Test one point in each.



Full solution

Roots: -1, 2, 4. Intervals: (-\infty, -1), (-1, 2), (2, 4),
(4, \infty).
Sign at x = 0: (0 + 1)(0 - 2)(0 - 4) = 1 \cdot (-2) \cdot (-4) =
8 > 0. So in (-1, 2): positive.
Signs alternate across simple roots: (-\infty, -1) negative, (-1, 2) positive, (2, 4) negative, (4, \infty) positive.
Solution: (-1, 2) \cup (4, \infty).



Problem 3 — Double-root sign
Solve (x + 1)^2 (x - 2) > 0.


Hint 1 — a nudge

(x + 1)^2 \ge 0 always, with equality only at x = -1. So the sign of the product is the sign of (x - 2) — except at x = -1, where the product is 0.



Full solution

For x \ne -1: (x + 1)^2 > 0, so the product has the sign of (x - 2) — positive when x > 2, negative when x < 2.
At x = -1: product is 0 (not > 0).
Solution: x > 2, i.e. (2, \infty).
(Notice: the double root at -1 does not create a sign-flip; the sign is the same on both sides of x = -1.)



Problem 4 — Rational inequality
Solve \dfrac{x - 1}{x + 2} > 0.


Hint 1 — a nudge

A fraction is positive iff numerator and denominator have the same sign. Two roots / undefined points: x = 1 (numerator = 0) and x = -2 (denominator = 0).



Full solution

Both positive: x > 1. Both negative: x < -2. (Excluding the points where each is zero — particularly x = -2, where the fraction is undefined.)
Solution: (-\infty, -2) \cup (1, \infty).



Problem 5 — Combining strict and non-strict
Solve (x - 1)(x - 3) \le 0.


Full solution

The product is \le 0 in [1, 3] (negative inside; zero at endpoints). Solution: [1, 3].
(Strict < would have given (1, 3); the non-strict \le includes the endpoints where the product equals 0.)



Problem 6 — A biquadratic inequality
Solve x^4 - 5 x^2 + 4 \le 0.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Substitute u = x^2: u^2 - 5 u + 4 \le 0.



Full solution

u^2 - 5 u + 4 = (u - 1)(u - 4) \le 0 for u \in [1, 4].
Back-substitute: 1 \le x^2 \le 4, i.e. 1 \le |x| \le 2.
Solution: -2 \le x \le -1 or 1 \le x \le 2, i.e. [-2, -1]
\cup [1, 2].



Problem 7 — AM-GM inequality (general form)
Show that for non-negative a, b: \dfrac{a + b}{2} \ge \sqrt{ab}, with equality iff a = b.


Hint 1 — a nudge

(a - b)^2 \ge 0 always. Expand and rearrange.



Full solution

(a - b)^2 \ge 0 \Rightarrow a^2 - 2 a b + b^2 \ge 0 \Rightarrow
a^2 + b^2 \ge 2 a b \Rightarrow (a + b)^2 \ge 4 a b \Rightarrow
\dfrac{a + b}{2} \ge \sqrt{a b} (taking square roots; valid for a, b \ge 0).
Equality iff (a - b)^2 = 0 iff a = b. QED. (Algebra II §45 — arithmetic-geometric mean inequality in two variables. Generalises to n variables by induction or other arguments.)


Brahmagupta (598–668 CE), in his Brāhmasphuṭasiddhānta of 628 CE, was the first to write down systematic rules for arithmetic involving negative quantities — what he called “debts” (ṛṇa) as opposed to “fortunes” (dhana). The product of two debts is a fortune; a debt subtracted from zero is a fortune; the sign of a product flips with the parity of negatives. These are the same rules underlying the sign-chart machinery in this chapter — when you say “two negatives multiply to a positive, so the product on this interval is positive”, you are applying Brahmagupta’s 7th-century laws. Europe didn’t accept negative numbers until the 17th century, almost a thousand years later.


A polynomial’s sign is read off its factorisation: the roots split the line, and parity decides each interval. Once P(x) is factored as a (x - r_1)^{m_1} (x - r_2)^{m_2} \cdots, the roots are the only places where the sign can change — and the sign actually flips only at odd-multiplicity roots. Build a sign chart and the linear inequality routine of Algebra I generalises to every polynomial and rational expression.

title: Ch 3 — Higher-Degree Inequalities
text:
Name

Polynomial inequality: P(x) > 0, \ge 0, < 0, \le 0, where P is a polynomial.
Sign-chart method.

Factor P(x) over the reals as far as possible.
Find the real roots and their multiplicities.
Split \mathbb R into intervals at the roots.
Test one point in each interval (or note the alternation rule).
Read off the solution set.

Sign-flip rule. A simple root flips the sign. A double root does not flip the sign. A triple root flips the sign. (More generally, an odd-multiplicity root flips; even-multiplicity does not.)
Rational-inequality method. \dfrac{P(x)}{Q(x)} > 0 — combine into one rational expression, find roots of P and zeros of Q, build the sign-chart with both numerator and denominator zeros (remembering the undefined points where Q = 0).
AM-GM (two variables): \dfrac{a + b}{2} \ge \sqrt{a b}, with equality iff a = b. (Generalises to n variables.)

A verse:

Factor first, find the roots, walk the line. Each simple root flips the sign; the doubled ones decline. Test one point per interval, or use the alternation pattern. And AM-GM bounds the geometric mean from above by the arithmetic Saturn.

title: Ch 3 — Higher-Degree Inequalities
text:
Apply

Worked example

Solve x^3 - 6 x^2 + 11 x - 6 \le 0.

Factor (using the lesson’s earlier polynomial): (x - 1)(x - 2)
(x - 3) \le 0.
Roots 1, 2, 3. Sign-chart: - x < 1: all three factors negative ⟹ product negative ✓ - 1 < x < 2: one positive, two negative ⟹ positive ✗ - 2 < x < 3: two positive, one negative ⟹ negative ✓ - x > 3: all positive ⟹ positive ✗
Including the boundary roots: Solution: (-\infty, 1] \cup [2, 3].


Practice — Warm
W1. Solve (x - 2)(x + 5) > 0.


Full solution

Roots 2, -5. Outside the interval: x < -5 or x > 2. Solution: (-\infty, -5) \cup (2, \infty).

W2. Solve x^2 + 4 < 0.


Full solution

x^2 \ge 0 always, so x^2 + 4 \ge 4 > 0 always. No solution.

W3. Solve x^2 \ge 9.


Full solution

|x| \ge 3 ⟹ x \le -3 or x \ge 3. (-\infty, -3] \cup
[3, \infty).



Practice — Working
P1. Solve \dfrac{2 x - 3}{x + 1} \ge 1.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Move everything to one side: \dfrac{2 x - 3}{x + 1} - 1 \ge 0. Combine.



Full solution

\dfrac{2 x - 3 - (x + 1)}{x + 1} \ge 0 \Rightarrow \dfrac{x - 4}
{x + 1} \ge 0.
Sign-chart: zeros at x = 4 (numerator) and x = -1 (denominator, undefined).

x < -1: (x - 4) < 0, (x + 1) < 0, ratio positive ✓
-1 < x < 4: (x - 4) < 0, (x + 1) > 0, ratio negative ✗
x > 4: both positive ✓

Including x = 4 (numerator = 0, ratio = 0, OK) but excluding x = -1 (undefined). Solution: (-\infty, -1) \cup [4, \infty).

P2. Solve |2 x - 1| > 3.


Hint 1 — a nudge

|y| > 3 \Leftrightarrow y > 3 or y < -3.



Full solution

2 x - 1 > 3 (x > 2) or 2 x - 1 < -3 (x < -1). Solution: (-\infty, -1) \cup (2, \infty).

P3. Solve x^4 - 16 \ge 0.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Factor: x^4 - 16 = (x^2 - 4)(x^2 + 4) = (x - 2)(x + 2)(x^2 + 4). (x^2 + 4) > 0 always, so sign depends on (x - 2)(x + 2).



Full solution

(x - 2)(x + 2) \ge 0 outside (-2, 2). Solution: (-\infty,
-2] \cup [2, \infty).



Practice — Stretch
S1. Solve \dfrac{x^2 - 1}{x^2 - 4} \ge 0.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Factor: \dfrac{(x - 1)(x + 1)}{(x - 2)(x + 2)} \ge 0. Four critical points: x = -2, -1, 1, 2.



Full solution

Zeros: \pm 1. Undefined: \pm 2. Sign-chart on five intervals:

x < -2: positive
-2 < x < -1: negative
-1 < x < 1: positive
1 < x < 2: negative
x > 2: positive

Including roots \pm 1 but excluding \pm 2: Solution: (-\infty,
-2) \cup [-1, 1] \cup (2, \infty).

S2. Show that for any positive a, b, c: \dfrac{a + b + c}{3} \ge \sqrt[3]{a b c} (the three-variable AM-GM).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Reduces to two applications of the two-variable case, by clever choice. Or: use the identity a^3 + b^3 + c^3 - 3 a b c = (a + b
+ c)(a^2 + b^2 + c^2 - ab - bc - ca) from Algebra I Ch 3 S2 — and note that the second factor is \frac{1}{2} \cdot \text{(sum of
squares of differences)}.



Full solution

Use the identity a^3 + b^3 + c^3 - 3 a b c = (a + b + c) \cdot
\frac{1}{2} [(a - b)^2 + (b - c)^2 + (c - a)^2] \ge 0 (since positive sum of squares is non-negative).
So a^3 + b^3 + c^3 \ge 3 a b c. Substituting a = \alpha^{1/3},
b = \beta^{1/3}, c = \gamma^{1/3}: \alpha + \beta + \gamma \ge
3 (\alpha \beta \gamma)^{1/3}, i.e. \dfrac{\alpha + \beta +
\gamma}{3} \ge \sqrt[3]{\alpha \beta \gamma}. QED.
(The general n-variable AM-GM follows by an induction or a similar substitution; the proof here is one of Cauchy’s classical arguments.)



Kiselev-style problems
Three problems in Kiselev’s terse style, adapted from Algebra II, §§40–50.
K1. Find all real x such that \dfrac{x^2 - x - 6}{x^2 -
5 x + 6} > 0.


Full solution

Factor: \dfrac{(x - 3)(x + 2)}{(x - 2)(x - 3)} > 0.
For x \ne 3: cancel (x - 3), getting \dfrac{x + 2}{x - 2}
> 0. By P1’s logic: x < -2 or x > 2, excluding x = 3.
Solution: (-\infty, -2) \cup (2, 3) \cup (3, \infty).
(The cancelled (x - 3) creates a removable singularity* at x = 3 — undefined in the original, but otherwise sign-irrelevant.)*

K2. Show that for all real x: x^4 + 1 \ge 2 x^2.


Full solution

x^4 + 1 - 2 x^2 = (x^2 - 1)^2 \ge 0, with equality iff x = \pm
1. QED.

K3. Solve \sqrt{x + 7} > x - 1.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Two cases: (a) RHS negative (x < 1): inequality holds whenever LHS is defined; (b) RHS non-negative: square both sides, but be careful.



Full solution

Domain: x \ge -7.
Case (a): x < 1. RHS < 0 \le LHS. So inequality holds for -7 \le x < 1.
Case (b): x \ge 1. Both sides non-negative; square: x + 7 > (x - 1)^2 = x^2 - 2 x + 1. Rearrange: x^2 - 3 x - 6 <
0. Discriminant 9 + 24 = 33. Roots \dfrac{3 \pm \sqrt{33}}{2}
\approx \dfrac{3 \pm 5.745}{2}, so \approx -1.37, 4.37. Strict inequality holds for -1.37 < x < 4.37. Combined with x \ge 1: 1 \le x < (3 + \sqrt{33})/2.
Combined: -7 \le x < (3 + \sqrt{33})/2, approximately [-7,
4.37).







Source pointer. Adapted from Kiselev, Алгебра, Часть вторая (Fizmatlit reprint 2005), §§36–50. Russian original: archive.org scan. The AM-GM inequality (Problem 7 and S2) is §45.





Common confusions

“\dfrac{a}{b} > 0 \Leftrightarrow a > 0 and b > 0.” Wrong. Also when both are negative. The full statement: \dfrac{a}{b} > 0 iff a, b have the same sign.
“Sign flips at every root.” Only at odd-multiplicity roots. At even-multiplicity roots, the sign does not flip (Problem 3).
“Squaring an inequality preserves direction.” Only if both sides are non-negative. -3 < 1 but 9 \not< 1. Be careful.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this chapter, remember these:

Factor, mark the roots, walk the number line. The sign-chart method reduces every polynomial inequality to a single procedure: factor, find roots, test one point per interval (or alternate signs across odd- multiplicity roots).
Even multiplicity means no sign flip. A double root touches the axis without crossing; a triple root crosses; in general, only odd-multiplicity roots flip the sign. Forgetting this is the chapter’s most common error.
AM-GM is the universal bound. The arithmetic-mean– geometric-mean inequality \tfrac{a + b}{2} \ge \sqrt{ab} is the simplest of an entire family (Cauchy-Schwarz, Hölder, Minkowski) — but knowing this one and equality condition a = b already cracks dozens of competition problems.



Self-Assessment

Could you build a sign-chart from a factored polynomial without notes?
Did the double-root case (Problem 3) feel like a special-case exception, or like a natural consequence of the structure?
Can you prove the AM-GM in two variables (Problem 7) on a fresh sheet?


3 yes: move on to Exponentials and Logarithms (Ch 4).
2 yes: redo Problem 6 — the biquadratic substitution unifies Algebra I and II.
0–1 yes: practise W1, W2, W3 with several variations of signs and multiplicities.

title: Ch 3 — Higher-Degree Inequalities
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

ka1.linear-inequalities — the same operations + the new sign-chart machinery for products.
ka2.higher-degree-equations — factoring polynomials to apply the sign-chart.



What this unlocks

ka2.exponentials-logarithms (Ch 4, planned) — \log is monotone, so log-inequalities reduce to sign analysis on the argument.
t6.convex-optimization — inequality constraints define convex regions; sign-chart logic is the entry point.
cbse11.linear-inequalities — CBSE 11 board treatment.



Kiselev chapter alignment

Algebra, Part II — Chapter 3: Inequalities of higher degree (§§36–50). Russian original on archive.org.
Sister synthesis lessons: t2.linear-inequalities and cbse11.linear-inequalities.
Read alongside the source PDF for Kiselev’s full development of AM-GM and Cauchy-Schwarz (§§45–50).



Where this matters

AI / ML. Loss landscapes are usually inequality-bounded: predictions must lie in a probability simplex (each p_i \ge 0, \sum p_i = 1), gradient updates clip to a constraint set. Sign- chart logic at the boundary is exactly this lesson’s machinery.


Databases. Range queries like WHERE age BETWEEN 18 AND 65 AND status != 'inactive' are systems of polynomial-style inequalities (literal boolean polynomials over \{\text{TRUE},
\text{FALSE}\}). Query planners exploit sign-chart-like logic when choosing index strategies.


Research. The AM-GM inequality (Problem 7 and S2) is one of the most-used inequalities in mathematics. It generalises to Hölder, Minkowski, Cauchy-Schwarz — a whole web of named inequalities.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones: Kiselev (the §§36–50 ordering); Cauchy (the AM-GM induction proof); Minkowski (the inequality web).
The sign-chart method (Problem 2) is the most-used tool in this lesson — practise it until it’s second nature.
What this lesson does not do: treat Cauchy-Schwarz or Hölder in full; those belong to undergraduate analysis.

title: 03 · Conic Sections
text:
Have you done 02 · Parabolas and T2 L10 — Pythagoras?
Comfortable with the distance formula d = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 +
(y_2-y_1)^2}?
Can you square both sides of an equation when needed?

title: 03 · Conic Sections
03 · Conic Sections
text:
Wonder

The widget’s circle equation is x^2 + y^2 = r^2. Why? (Hint: distance formula.)
An ellipse looks like a “stretched circle.” What if a = b?
A parabola has one axis of symmetry. An ellipse has two. A hyperbola has two. A circle has infinitely many. Why?

title: 03 · Conic Sections
text:
Discover

Problem 1
A circle of radius r centred at the origin is the set of points (x, y) at distance r from (0, 0). Use the distance formula (\sqrt{x^2 + y^2} = r) to derive the equation.


Full solution

\sqrt{x^2 + y^2} = r \Rightarrow x^2 + y^2 = r^2.
That’s the standard equation of the circle of radius r centred at the origin. For a circle centred at (h, k): (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 =
r^2.



Problem 2
A circle has equation (x - 3)^2 + (y + 2)^2 = 25. What is its centre and radius?


Full solution

Centre (3, -2), radius 5. Match (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2 to read off h = 3, k = -2, r = \sqrt{25} = 5.



Problem 3
An ellipse has equation \dfrac{x^2}{a^2} + \dfrac{y^2}{b^2} = 1. What does a measure? What is special when a = b?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Set y = 0: x = \pm a. So a is the horizontal distance from centre to edge.



Full solution

a is the semi-major axis (horizontal extent), b the semi-minor axis (vertical extent). When a = b, the equation becomes x^2/a^2 + y^2/a^2 = 1, i.e., x^2 + y^2 = a^2 — a circle of radius a.
A circle is a special case of an ellipse where both axes are equal.



Problem 4
A parabola opening upward has equation x^2 = 4py, where the focus is at (0, p) and the directrix is the line y = -p. Verify: a point (x, y) on the parabola is equidistant from the focus and the directrix.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Distance from (x, y) to (0, p): \sqrt{x^2 + (y - p)^2}. Distance from (x, y) to line y = -p: y + p. Set equal, square, simplify.



Full solution

\sqrt{x^2 + (y - p)^2} = y + p. Square: x^2 + (y - p)^2 = (y +
p)^2. Expand: x^2 + y^2 - 2py + p^2 = y^2 + 2py + p^2. Simplify: x^2 = 4py ✓.
The focus-directrix property characterises a parabola. This is why parabolic dishes focus parallel rays — the geometry of the curve enforces it.



Problem 5
A hyperbola \dfrac{x^2}{a^2} - \dfrac{y^2}{b^2} = 1 has two branches — left and right. What value of x does it forbid? (Try x = 0.)


Full solution

x = 0: -y^2/b^2 = 1 \Rightarrow y^2 = -b^2, impossible. So the curve doesn’t cross the y-axis.
It does have x-intercepts (y = 0): x = \pm a. The curve has two disconnected branches at x \le -a and x \ge a.
The four conics — circle, ellipse, parabola, hyperbola — all come from slicing a cone at different angles. Apollonius (c. 200 BCE) wrote the foundational treatise on them.


Apollonius of Perga wrote his eight-book Conics around 200 BCE, naming all three new curves: ellipse (“falling short”), parabola (“alongside”), and hyperbola (“excess”) — words still in everyday English (an ellipsis leaves something out, a parable sets something beside, a hyperbole exaggerates). For 1,800 years they were a geometric curiosity. Then in 1609 Johannes Kepler announced that planets orbit the Sun in ellipses — and overnight Apollonius’s curves became the language of the cosmos.


Four shapes, one cone. Tilt the slicing plane and the same cone hands you a circle, then an ellipse, then a parabola, then a hyperbola — each a snapshot of how the plane is leaning relative to the cone’s side. The “different curves” are different views of one object.

title: 03 · Conic Sections
text:
Name

A conic section is a curve formed by intersecting a plane with a double cone.








Conic
Standard form (centred at origin)
Geometric definition




Circle
x^2 + y^2 = r^2
Points at distance r from centre


Ellipse
\dfrac{x^2}{a^2} + \dfrac{y^2}{b^2} = 1
Sum of distances to two foci is constant


Parabola
x^2 = 4py
Distance to focus = distance to directrix


Hyperbola
\dfrac{x^2}{a^2} - \dfrac{y^2}{b^2} = 1
Difference of distances to two foci is constant



The general equation Ax^2 + Bxy + Cy^2 + Dx + Ey + F = 0 with B = 0 classifies by the sign of AC:

AC > 0 (same sign): ellipse (or circle if A = C).
AC = 0 (one is zero): parabola.
AC < 0: hyperbola.



Slice a cone: circle, ellipse, parabola, hyperbola — all from one shape; Each has its own focus property — geometric and reflective; The sign of AC tells you which one you have.

title: 03 · Conic Sections
text:
Apply

Worked example

What kind of conic is 4x^2 + 9y^2 = 36? Sketch it.

Divide both sides by 36: \dfrac{x^2}{9} + \dfrac{y^2}{4} = 1. So a^2 = 9 \Rightarrow a = 3, b^2 = 4 \Rightarrow b = 2. Ellipse, horizontal semi-axis 3, vertical semi-axis 2.
Sketch: passes through (\pm 3, 0) and (0, \pm 2).


Practice — Warm
W1. Centre and radius of x^2 + y^2 = 49?


Full solution

Centre (0, 0), radius 7.

W2. Equation of circle centred at (2, -1) with radius 4?


Full solution

(x - 2)^2 + (y + 1)^2 = 16.

W3. What conic is x^2 - y^2 = 1?


Full solution

Hyperbola.



Practice — Working
P1. \dfrac{x^2}{16} + \dfrac{y^2}{25} = 1 — what kind of conic, which axis longer?


Full solution

Ellipse with semi-axes a = 4, b = 5. The vertical axis is longer (it’s b). This ellipse is “tall.”

P2. A satellite dish has parabolic cross-section x^2 = 8y (in cm). Where is the focus?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Compare x^2 = 4py. Here 4p = 8, so p = 2.



Full solution

Focus at (0, 2) — i.e., 2 cm above the bottom of the dish. The receiver should be placed there.

P3. A circle passes through (0, 0) and has centre (3, 4). What is its radius? Write its equation.


Full solution

Distance from (3, 4) to (0, 0) is \sqrt{9 + 16} = 5. Equation: (x - 3)^2 + (y - 4)^2 = 25.
Pythagoras returns: distance computation gave the radius.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Planetary orbits are ellipses with the Sun at one focus (Kepler’s First Law, 1609). Earth’s orbit has a \approx 1.496 \times
10^8 km (semi-major axis). The orbit is almost circular: b/a
\approx 0.99986. How close is the Earth’s path to a perfect circle?


Full solution

b/a \approx 0.99986 means the orbit is essentially a circle, but not quite. The difference between a and b is about 1.496 \times 10^8 \times (1 - 0.99986) \approx 21000 km — a sizeable distance, but tiny compared to a itself.
Kepler’s discovery that planetary orbits are ellipses, not circles was revolutionary. Newton later proved this follows from an inverse- square gravitational force. The conic-section equations of Tier 3 were essential to celestial mechanics.

S2. An ellipse has the whispering-gallery property: sound emitted at one focus reflects to the other focus. Why might this be useful?


Full solution

Eavesdropping or amplifying speech. The Whispering Gallery in St Paul’s Cathedral (London) and the National Statuary Hall in the US Capitol both use elliptical or near-elliptical domes. Whisper at one focus; hear clearly at the other. Lithotripsy machines (medical devices that break kidney stones with sound waves) are designed around this exact principle: place the patient’s stone at one focus, the sound source at the other.
Each conic has a beautiful reflective property:

Parabola: parallel rays converge at the focus.
Ellipse: rays from one focus converge at the other.
Hyperbola: rays aimed at one focus reflect as if coming from the other.

These properties are not accidents — they emerge from the distance-defining properties of each conic.



Common confusions

“Conic = cone-shaped.” No — conic section = curve from slicing a cone. The slice is two-dimensional, not three-.
“Circle is its own thing.” A circle is a special ellipse (a = b). All the ellipse properties degenerate elegantly.
“All conics have two foci.” Parabolas have one. Circles have one (centre). Ellipses and hyperbolas have two.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

Slice a double cone with a plane and you get one of four curves — circle, ellipse, parabola, or hyperbola — chosen by the angle of the slice.
Each conic has a focus property that defines it geometrically; each focus property powers a real engineering trick (satellite dishes, whispering galleries, lithotripsy).
Planetary orbits are ellipses (Kepler), projectile arcs are parabolas (Galileo), comet flybys are hyperbolas — three of the four conics are written across the sky.



Self-Assessment

Could you derive the circle equation from the distance formula?
Without scrolling back, classify x^2 - 4y^2 = 16.
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: hyperbola, a = 4, b = 2.)

3 yes: ready for 04 · Sequences and Series.
2 yes: redo S1 — Kepler’s First Law made the conics matter for physics.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P4 (focus-directrix derivation).

title: 03 · Conic Sections
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

02 · Parabolas
T2 L10 — Pythagoras — the distance formula derived the circle equation.



What this unlocks

Tier 4 — Calculus — area enclosed by an ellipse, length of an arc, parametric forms.
Tier 7 — Differential Geometry — generalised conics on curved surfaces.



Where this matters

Game development & graphics. Collision detection often uses bounding circles and bounding ellipses. Camera frustums are truncated cones (their cross-sections are conic sections). Lens optics use parabolic and elliptical mirrors.


Mathematical research. Conic sections were the first non-line curves studied algebraically. The 1637 unification by Descartes of geometry and algebra (his Géométrie) launched modern mathematics.


Astronomy & physics. Planetary orbits (ellipses), comet orbits (parabolas, hyperbolas), satellite trajectories — all conics, by Kepler & Newton.


Engineering. Whispering galleries, parabolic antennas, elliptical gears, hyperbolic cooling towers — conics in physical artefacts across centuries.

title: 11 · Combinatorics — Counting
text:
Comfortable with multiplication and division (T1 L05 and T1 L06)?
Have you played card games — and ever wondered “how many possible hands?”
Comfortable with the factorial: 5! = 5 \cdot 4 \cdot 3 \cdot 2 \cdot 1
= 120?

title: 11 · Combinatorics — Counting
11 · Combinatorics — Counting
text:
Wonder

From 4 letters, how many 3-letter “words” if letters can repeat? Cannot repeat?
Why are the combinations fewer than the permutations?
From 50 students, how many ways to pick a committee of 5?

title: 11 · Combinatorics — Counting
text:
Discover

Problem 1
A pizza shop offers 3 sizes, 5 crust types, and 8 toppings (pick one). How many distinct pizzas?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Multiplication principle: if step 1 has a options, step 2 has b, …, total is a \cdot b \cdot \ldots



Full solution

3 \cdot 5 \cdot 8 = 120 pizzas. The multiplication principle is the foundation of all counting.



Problem 2
How many ways to arrange 5 books on a shelf?


Hint 1 — a nudge

First slot: 5 choices. Second: 4. Third: 3. Etc.



Full solution

5 \cdot 4 \cdot 3 \cdot 2 \cdot 1 = 5! = 120 arrangements.
The number of arrangements of n distinct objects is n! (“n factorial”). For n = 10, that’s already 3.6 million.



Problem 3
A 3-digit PIN can use digits 0–9, repetition allowed. How many?


Full solution

10 \cdot 10 \cdot 10 = 10^3 = 1000. With repetition allowed: n^r arrangements (here n = 10, r = 3).



Problem 4
How many 3-digit codes using 0–9 with no repetition?


Full solution

10 \cdot 9 \cdot 8 = 720.
This is the permutation count: P(n, r) = \dfrac{n!}{(n-r)!}. P(10, 3) = 10!/7! = 720.



Problem 5
A committee of 3 people is chosen from a class of 10. How many committees? (Order doesn’t matter — Anil-Bina-Cara is the same committee as Cara-Anil-Bina.)


Hint 1 — a nudge

Permutations P(10, 3) = 720 over-count, because every committee is counted 3! = 6 times (once per ordering). So divide.



Full solution

\dfrac{P(10, 3)}{3!} = \dfrac{720}{6} = 120.
This is the combination count: C(n, r) = \binom{n}{r} =
\dfrac{n!}{r!(n - r)!}.
C(10, 3) = 120. 120 possible committees.
The “n choose r” notation \binom{n}{r} is read aloud as “n choose r” and is the number of r-element subsets of an n-set.


The Italian polymath Gerolamo Cardano, in his Liber de Ludo Aleae (“Book on Games of Chance,” written ~1564, published 1663), was the first European to count outcomes of dice rolls systematically — opening the door to probability. A century earlier in China, Yang Hui (杨辉, c. 1238–1298) tabulated the triangular array of binomial coefficients we now call Pascal’s triangle — over three hundred years before Pascal. Counting argued itself into mathematics from the gambling table and the court ledger long before it became a “branch.”


Two questions decide every counting problem: does order matter? can things repeat? Get those answers right and the formula \bigl( n^r,\ P(n,r),\ \binom{n}{r} \bigr) picks itself. Get them wrong and no amount of factorial gymnastics will save you.

title: 11 · Combinatorics — Counting
text:
Name

Multiplication principle: if a process has k independent steps with n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_k choices each, the total number of outcomes is n_1 \cdot n_2 \cdots n_k.
Factorial: n! = n(n-1)(n-2) \cdots 1. By convention 0! = 1.
Three counting formulas:









Question
Order matters?
Repetition?
Formula




Arrangements with rep
yes
yes
n^r


Permutations
yes
no
P(n, r) = \dfrac{n!}{(n-r)!}


Combinations
no
no
C(n, r) = \binom{n}{r} = \dfrac{n!}{r! (n-r)!}



Always ask: Does order matter? Can items repeat? The answer picks the formula.


Multiplication principle: choices multiply across independent steps; Permutations count ordered selections; combinations count unordered; Order matters? Repetition allowed? — those two questions decide.

title: 11 · Combinatorics — Counting
text:
Apply

Worked example

A 5-card poker hand is dealt from a standard 52-card deck. How many distinct hands?

Order doesn’t matter (the same five cards form the same hand regardless of dealing order); repetition impossible (each card is unique).
\binom{52}{5} = \dfrac{52!}{5! \cdot 47!} = \dfrac{52 \cdot 51 \cdot 50
\cdot 49 \cdot 48}{120} = 2{,}598{,}960 hands.


Practice — Warm
W1. How many ways to arrange the letters of “MATH”?


Full solution

4! = 24 (all letters distinct).

W2. C(7, 2) = ?


Full solution

\dfrac{7!}{2! \cdot 5!} = \dfrac{42}{2} = 21.

W3. A 4-digit PIN using digits 0–9, repetition allowed. How many?


Full solution

10^4 = 10{,}000.



Practice — Working
P1. A team of 4 players is chosen from 12 candidates. How many teams?


Full solution

\binom{12}{4} = \dfrac{12!}{4! \cdot 8!} = 495.

P2. How many distinct 5-letter “words” (real or made-up) using the 26 English letters, with no repeated letter?


Full solution

P(26, 5) = 26 \cdot 25 \cdot 24 \cdot 23 \cdot 22 = 7{,}893{,}600.

P3. From 8 different books, you choose 3 for a holiday. How many choices? Then: in how many orders can you read them?


Hint 1 — a nudge

First a combination, then a permutation of those 3.



Full solution

Choices: \binom{8}{3} = 56 sets. Orders for each: 3! = 6. Total order-and-choice combinations: 56 \cdot 6 = 336 = P(8, 3).
This shows: P(n, r) = C(n, r) \cdot r! — permutations = combinations times orderings of the chosen subset.



Practice — Stretch
S1. A password. Pick 8 characters from a–z (26), A–Z (26), 0–9 (10), and 10 special characters — total 72 distinct characters, repetition allowed. How many distinct passwords?


Full solution

72^8 \approx 7.2 \times 10^{14} — about 720 trillion passwords. At a billion guesses per second, brute-forcing through all of them takes ~8 days. (In reality, weak password choices reduce the effective space dramatically; that’s why diceware-style passphrases and password managers are recommended.)
The size of a password space is the measure of brute-force strength. The exponential function makes a small character-set expansion (62 → 72) much more dramatic than it sounds.

S2. Lottery odds. A lottery draws 6 numbers from 49 (no repetition, order doesn’t matter). What’s the chance a single ticket wins the jackpot?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Total combinations: \binom{49}{6}. One ticket = 1 favourable.



Full solution

\binom{49}{6} = \dfrac{49!}{6! \cdot 43!} = 13{,}983{,}816.
Chance per ticket: 1 / 13{,}983{,}816 \approx 7.15 \times 10^{-8} — roughly 1 in 14 million. To put it in perspective: you are several times more likely to be struck by lightning in a year (about 1 in a million in many places).
This is why lotteries are sometimes called “a tax on people who can’t do mathematics.”



Common confusions

“P(n, r) and C(n, r) are interchangeable.” No — order matters for P, doesn’t for C. Different problems need different formulas.
“n! for very small or very large n is weird.” 0! = 1 by convention (makes formulas work). 50! \approx 3 \times 10^{64} — astronomically large.
“\binom{n}{r} is just shorthand for some number.” It’s a genuine count — the number of r-element subsets of an n-set. Has rich combinatorial meaning.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

The multiplication principle is the engine of counting — independent steps multiply, full stop. Almost every counting formula is just this idea applied carefully.
Permutation cares about order; combination does not. Divide a permutation by r! to collapse the orderings of the chosen r.
The factorial explodes ferociously — 52! already exceeds the number of atoms in the observable universe, which is why “shuffle the deck” gives an arrangement no human has ever seen before.



Self-Assessment

Could you state the multiplication principle and apply it to a 3-step decision?
Without scrolling back, what is \binom{6}{2}?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: 15.)

3 yes: ready for 12 · Binomial Theorem.
2 yes: redo S1 (password space) — counting argument central to security.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P5 (the C = P/r! derivation).

title: 11 · Combinatorics — Counting
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

T1 L06 — Division as Fair Share — combination formula uses division of factorials.



What this unlocks

12 · Binomial Theorem — \binom{n}{r} is the coefficient.
13 · Basic Probability — probability often = (favourable count) / (total count), both combinatorial.



Where this matters

Encryption. Brute-force attack times depend on space sizes (passwords, key spaces). Modern crypto designs key spaces to be at least 2^{128} — beyond reach of any future computer.


AI / Machine Learning. Combinatorial explosion underlies why exhaustive search is intractable for many ML problems — driving the need for gradient-based and probabilistic methods.


Databases. Counting query optimisation, set sizes, join cardinalities, group-by aggregates — all combinatorics underneath.


Mathematical research. Combinatorics is a vast field — graph theory, design theory, partition theory, algebraic combinatorics — all built on the basic counting arguments here.

title: 10 · Solving Trigonometric Equations
text:
Have you done 09 · Trig Identities?
Recall the unit-circle picture and quadrant signs.
Comfortable with \arcsin, \arccos, \arctan as inverse functions (T2 L01)?

title: 10 · Solving Trigonometric Equations
10 · Solving Trigonometric Equations
text:
Wonder

For \sin\theta = 0.5, the widget shows multiple solutions. List them.
What if k > 1 or k < -1? Will there be any solutions?
The solutions are spaced by 360° within each “branch.” Why?

title: 10 · Solving Trigonometric Equations
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Solve \sin\theta = 1/2 for \theta in [0°, 360°].


Hint 1 — a nudge

\sin\theta > 0 in QI and QII. Reference angle: \arcsin(1/2) = 30°.



Full solution

QI: \theta = 30°. QII: \theta = 180° - 30° = 150°. Solutions in [0, 360°]: \theta = 30°, 150°.
For all \theta: \theta = 30° + 360° k or \theta = 150° + 360° k for any integer k.



Problem 2
Solve \cos\theta = -\sqrt{3}/2 in [0°, 360°].


Hint 1 — a nudge

\cos\theta < 0 in QII and QIII. Reference: \arccos(\sqrt{3}/2) =
30°.



Full solution

QII: \theta = 180° - 30° = 150°. QIII: \theta = 180° + 30° = 210°. \theta = 150°, 210°.



Problem 3
Solve \tan\theta = 1 in [0°, 360°].


Hint 1 — a nudge

\tan\theta > 0 in QI and QIII. Reference: 45°.



Full solution

\theta = 45°, 225°. (Tan repeats every 180°, so two solutions per 360° — half as many as sin and cos.)



Problem 4
Solve 2 \sin\theta + 1 = 0 in [0°, 360°].


Hint 1 — a nudge

Isolate sin first: \sin\theta = -1/2.



Full solution

\sin\theta = -1/2. QIII: 180° + 30° = 210°. QIV: 360° - 30° =
330°. \theta = 210°, 330°.
Treat trig equations like algebraic ones: isolate the trig function, then use the unit circle.



Problem 5
Solve \sin^2 \theta - \sin\theta = 0 in [0°, 360°].


Hint 1 — a nudge

Factor: \sin\theta (\sin\theta - 1) = 0.



Full solution

Either \sin\theta = 0 or \sin\theta = 1. \sin\theta = 0: \theta = 0°, 180°, 360°. \sin\theta = 1: \theta = 90°. \theta = 0°, 90°, 180°, 360° (using “0° and 360° are the same” or both depending on convention).
Trig equations often factor like polynomial equations. The same algebra (factoring, zero-product property) carries over.


The Greek astronomer Hipparchus (c. 150 BCE) compiled the first known table of chord lengths in order to solve a trig equation in disguise: “for what angle does the moon match this observed shadow length?” That problem — invert a trig function, find every angle that produces a given value — is exactly what you are doing here, two thousand years later, with a pocket calculator instead of a Babylonian astronomical tablet.


Trig equations almost never have a single answer. A sine value is shared by infinitely many angles — once per period, in two quadrants. If your solution sheet shows one angle and a full stop, you have probably forgotten its mirror twin.

title: 10 · Solving Trigonometric Equations
text:
Name

To solve a trig equation:

Isolate the trig function (algebra).
Find the reference angle (using \arcsin, \arccos, \arctan).
Use quadrant signs (Lesson 8) to find all solutions in one period.
Add multiples of the period for the general solution:

\sin, \cos: period 360° (or 2\pi).
\tan: period 180° (or \pi).


Standard solution counts in [0, 360°]:



Equation
Solutions




\sin\theta = k, |k| < 1
2 (QI/QII or QIII/QIV)


\sin\theta = \pm 1
1


\cos\theta = k, |k| < 1
2 (QI/QIV or QII/QIII)


\tan\theta = k
2 (QI/QIII or QII/QIV)


\sin\theta = k, |k| > 1
0





Isolate, find reference angle, apply quadrant signs; Add multiples of the period for the full family; Algebra of trig equations parallels polynomial equations.

title: 10 · Solving Trigonometric Equations
text:
Apply

Worked example

Solve 2 \cos\theta - 1 = 0 in [0, 2\pi] (radians).

A competent solver:

Isolate: \cos\theta = 1/2.
Reference: \arccos(1/2) = \pi/3 (60°).
Quadrants: \cos > 0 in QI and QIV.
Solutions: \theta = \pi/3 (QI) and \theta = 2\pi - \pi/3 = 5\pi/3 (QIV).

\theta = \pi/3, 5\pi/3.


Practice — Warm
W1. Solve \sin\theta = 0 in [0, 360°].


Full solution

\theta = 0°, 180°, 360°.

W2. Solve \cos\theta = 1 in [0, 360°].


Full solution

\theta = 0° (or 360°).

W3. Solve \tan\theta = -1 in [0, 360°].


Full solution

\tan < 0 in QII and QIV. \theta = 135°, 315°.



Practice — Working
P1. Solve \sqrt{2} \sin\theta = 1 in [0, 360°].


Full solution

\sin\theta = 1/\sqrt{2}. Reference: 45°. QI/QII. \theta = 45°,
135°.

P2. Solve 4 \cos^2\theta - 1 = 0 in [0, 360°].


Hint 1 — a nudge

\cos^2\theta = 1/4 \Rightarrow \cos\theta = \pm 1/2.



Full solution

\cos\theta = +1/2 \Rightarrow \theta = 60°, 300°. \cos\theta = -1/2 \Rightarrow \theta = 120°, 240°. Four solutions: 60°, 120°, 240°, 300°.

P3. Solve \sin 2\theta = 1 in [0, 360°].


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let \phi = 2\theta. Solve \sin\phi = 1 for \phi in [0, 720°], then divide by 2.



Full solution

\sin\phi = 1 \Rightarrow \phi = 90°, 450° in [0, 720°]. So \theta = 45°, 225°.
When the trig argument is n\theta, the period shrinks by n — giving more solutions in [0, 360°].



Practice — Stretch
S1. Solve \sin\theta = \cos\theta in [0, 360°].


Hint 1 — a nudge

Divide both sides by \cos\theta: \tan\theta = 1. Then solve.



Full solution

\tan\theta = 1 \Rightarrow \theta = 45°, 225°.
(Caveat: dividing by \cos\theta assumes \cos\theta \neq 0. Check the cases \theta = 90°, 270° separately: there \cos = 0 and \sin = \pm 1, so \sin \neq \cos — those aren’t solutions anyway.)

S2. A spring’s position satisfies x(t) = 5 \sin(2 \pi t) (amplitude 5, frequency 1 Hz). When does x(t) = 2.5?


Hint 1 — a nudge

\sin(2\pi t) = 0.5. Solve for 2\pi t, then t.



Full solution

\sin(2\pi t) = 0.5 \Rightarrow 2\pi t = \pi/6, 5\pi/6, 13\pi/6, \ldots.
So t = 1/12, 5/12, 13/12, 17/12, \ldots seconds — i.e., the spring is at x = 2.5 at infinitely many times, twice per period (1 second).
Trig equations as functions of time describe oscillations and periodic events — heart beats, traffic light cycles, planetary positions. Predicting “when next?” is a trig-equation question.



Common confusions

“There’s only one solution.” Almost never. Trig equations have 2 (or more) per period and infinitely many overall. Always state the interval of interest.
“\arcsin(0.5) = 30° is the only answer.” It’s the principal answer (in [-90°, 90°]). Use unit-circle reasoning to find the others.
“Squared equations like \sin^2 = 1/4 have one solution.” Two roots from the square root: \pm 1/2. Each gives multiple angles.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

Solve a trig equation by isolating the function, then unrolling the unit circle. Algebra first, geometry second.
Every solution lives in a family. Find one angle, find its quadrant twin, then add integer multiples of the period for the rest.
The same algebra you used on quadratics — factoring, substitution, zero-product — works on \sin\theta and \cos\theta too. Treat them as variables that happen to live on a circle.



Self-Assessment

Could you solve \sin\theta = 1/2 in [0, 360°] from memory?
Without scrolling back, can you solve 2\sin\theta + \sqrt{3} = 0 in [0, 360°]?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: \sin\theta = -\sqrt{3}/2 \Rightarrow \theta = 240°, 300°.)

3 yes: ready for 11 · Combinatorics — Counting.
2 yes: redo P3 (sin 2θ = 1) — the argument-substitution technique is essential.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P1 and P5.

title: 10 · Solving Trigonometric Equations
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

09 · Trig Identities — identities reduce complex equations to simpler standard forms.



What this unlocks

Tier 4 — Calculus — finding zeros of trigonometric functions appears in optimisation, root-finding, oscillator analysis.



Where this matters

Game development. “When does the projectile hit the ground at height 0?” “When does the rotating gear reach this angle?” — trig equations.


Engineering. AC circuits with phase shifts: solving when current or voltage equals a target value.


Physics. Pendulum clocks, planetary orbits, wave interference — all generate trig equations whose solutions are physically meaningful events.

title: 00 · Introduction
text:
Tier 1 gave you arithmetic. Tier 2 gave you the language of variables, equations, and lines. Tier 3 gives you curves (parabolas, circles, and the conic sections), the trigonometric functions of angles, the inverse of exponentials (logarithms), and the language of chance (combinatorics and probability).
By the end of Tier 3 you can:

Solve any quadratic equation by completing the square or the quadratic formula, and graph parabolas in vertex form.
Recognise the four conic sections as cross-sections of a cone and as algebraic curves.
Manipulate sequences and series, including the infinite geometric series.
Use logarithms to invert exponentials, and solve exponential equations.
Work with trigonometric ratios, the unit circle, fundamental identities, and basic trig equations.
Count outcomes systematically with combinatorics, and reason about chance with probability and discrete distributions.

title: 02 · Parabolas — Graphing Quadratics
text:
Have you done 01 · Quadratic Formula?
Comfortable plotting points and lines from T2 L05 — Linear Functions and Graphs?
Can you complete the square: x^2 + 6x + 4 = (x + 3)^2 - 5?

title: 02 · Parabolas — Graphing Quadratics
02 · Parabolas — Graphing Quadratics
text:
Wonder

Set a = 1, h = 0, k = 0. What is the parabola?
Now h = 3. What changed? What about k = -2?
Set a = -1. The parabola flips. Why?

title: 02 · Parabolas — Graphing Quadratics
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Sketch (mentally) y = x^2. What is its vertex? Axis of symmetry? Range?


Full solution

Vertex: (0, 0). Axis of symmetry: the y-axis (x = 0). Range: y \ge 0 (parabola opens upward, lowest point is the vertex).
Every other parabola is a transformation of this base parabola.



Problem 2
Convert y = x^2 - 6x + 11 to vertex form by completing the square.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Half of -6 is -3; squared is 9. So x^2 - 6x = (x-3)^2 - 9.



Full solution

y = (x-3)^2 - 9 + 11 = (x-3)^2 + 2.
Vertex form: y = (x - 3)^2 + 2. Vertex: (3, 2). Opens upward. Minimum value: 2 (the k).
The same completing-the-square move from Lesson 1 — now used to identify the vertex rather than to solve.



Problem 3
For y = ax^2 + bx + c, the axis of symmetry is x = -b/(2a) (midway between the two roots). Show this matches the vertex form formula.


Hint 1 — a nudge

In vertex form y = a(x - h)^2 + k, axis is x = h. Complete the square on ax^2 + bx + c — what is h?



Full solution

ax^2 + bx + c = a(x^2 + bx/a) + c = a(x + b/(2a))^2 - b^2/(4a) + c. So h = -b/(2a) ✓. The axis of symmetry sits at x = -b/(2a).
(And the minimum value — the k — is c - b^2/(4a).)



Problem 4
Find the vertex of y = -2x^2 + 8x + 1. Where is its maximum?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Use h = -b/(2a) — here a = -2, b = 8.



Full solution

h = -8/(2 \cdot -2) = -8 / -4 = 2. k = -2 \cdot 4 + 8 \cdot 2 + 1 =
-8 + 16 + 1 = 9.
Vertex: (2, 9). Since a = -2 < 0, the parabola opens down, so the vertex is the maximum. Maximum value: 9 at x = 2.
Maximum/minimum of a quadratic = vertex’s y-coordinate. The formula short-cuts the calculus we’ll meet in Tier 4.



Problem 5
A ball thrown upward has height h(t) = -5t^2 + 20t + 1. What is the maximum height and when is it reached?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Quadratic in t with a = -5, b = 20. Vertex at t = -b/(2a).



Full solution

t = -20 / (-10) = 2 s. Max height: h(2) = -20 + 40 + 1 = 21 m.
Max height 21 m at 2 seconds. Throwing physics: the trajectory is a parabola; the vertex is the peak.


The parabola was named by Apollonius of Perga around 200 BCE in his treatise Conics — the same eight-book classic that gave us ellipse and hyperbola (Lesson 03). The Greek word parabolē means “comparison” or “applied alongside” — Apollonius coined it because the curve arises when a plane is applied to a cone parallel to one of its sides. The trajectory of every thrown stone, every fountain jet, every cannonball under gravity has been tracing this 2,200-year-old shape.


A parabola is a quadratic with its mask off. Standard form (ax^2 + bx + c) hides the vertex; vertex form (a(x-h)^2 + k) shows it. Completing the square is just sliding from one mask to the other.

title: 02 · Parabolas — Graphing Quadratics
text:
Name

The graph of a quadratic y = ax^2 + bx + c is a parabola.
Standard form: y = ax^2 + bx + c. Vertex form: y = a(x - h)^2 + k, with vertex at (h, k). Factored form: y = a(x - r_1)(x - r_2), with x-intercepts r_1, r_2.
Key features (relating standard form to geometry):

Axis of symmetry: x = -\dfrac{b}{2a}.
Vertex: (h, k) = \left(-\dfrac{b}{2a}, \, c - \dfrac{b^2}{4a}\right).
Opens up if a > 0 (vertex is minimum).
Opens down if a < 0 (vertex is maximum).
y-intercept: (0, c).
x-intercepts: roots from the quadratic formula (if any).



Vertex form puts the lowest (or highest) point in plain sight; Axis of symmetry runs vertically through the vertex; Sign of a tells you whether the cup opens up or down.

title: 02 · Parabolas — Graphing Quadratics
text:
Apply

Worked example

Sketch y = 2x^2 - 12x + 13.

Steps:

Identify a: a = 2 > 0, so opens up.
Vertex x: -b/(2a) = 12/4 = 3. Vertex y: 2 \cdot 9 - 36 +
13 = -5. So vertex (3, -5), the minimum.
y-intercept: (0, 13).
x-intercepts: discriminant 144 - 104 = 40, so x = (12 \pm
\sqrt{40})/4 \approx 1.42, 4.58.
Sketch: opens up, vertex at (3, -5), crosses y-axis at 13, crosses x-axis at about 1.42 and 4.58.



Practice — Warm
W1. Vertex of y = (x - 4)^2 + 1?


Full solution

(4, 1).

W2. Does y = -3x^2 + 5 open up or down?


Full solution

Down (a < 0). Vertex is the max.

W3. Axis of symmetry of y = x^2 + 8x + 3?


Full solution

x = -8/2 = -4.



Practice — Working
P1. Convert y = x^2 - 4x + 7 to vertex form. State vertex.


Full solution

y = (x-2)^2 - 4 + 7 = (x-2)^2 + 3. Vertex (2, 3).

P2. Find the maximum of y = -x^2 + 6x - 5.


Full solution

x = -6/(-2) = 3. y(3) = -9 + 18 - 5 = 4. Max value: 4 at x = 3.

P3. A farmer has 100 m of fence to enclose a rectangular plot against a wall (so only 3 sides are fenced). What dimensions maximise area?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let width = w, length = \ell. 2w + \ell = 100 ⇒ \ell = 100 - 2w. Area A = w \ell = w(100 - 2w) = 100w - 2w^2. Maximum?



Full solution

A = -2w^2 + 100w. Vertex at w = -100/(-4) = 25. Then \ell = 50. 25 m × 50 m, area 1250 m².
This is optimisation by quadratic — a classic application before calculus arrives.



Practice — Stretch
S1. A neural network’s quadratic loss surface has a single minimum, found at the vertex. The MSE loss for a one-parameter model is exactly a parabola. Why does this make optimisation trivial mathematically?


Full solution

The minimum is at the vertex, computable in closed form: w^* =
-b/(2a). No iteration, no gradient descent — just a formula. This is the basis of closed-form linear regression (“normal equations” in statistics).
For multi-parameter models, the loss is a paraboloid (multi-D parabola) and the closed form is matrix algebra (Tier 5). For nonlinear models, no closed form exists and you must use iterative methods (Tier 6). The simplest case — the parabola — sits behind everything.

S2. A satellite dish is shaped like a parabola because parabolas have a focus — a single point where all incoming parallel rays reflect to. Why might this be useful?


Full solution

Concentrating signals (or sunlight, or sound). Parallel signals from a distant source (TV satellite, radio star, the Sun) all reflect off the parabolic dish and converge at the focus, where the receiver sits. Concentrates a faint signal into a strong one.
Reverse: a light at the focus produces parallel rays outward — which is why headlights, flashlights, and stage spotlights use parabolic mirrors.
The parabola is one of the most physically useful curves humans have discovered. The focus is a Lesson-3 idea (conic sections); the parabolic equation is what makes it computable.



Common confusions

“Vertex is always at the origin.” Only when there’s no horizontal or vertical shift. The vertex moves with h and k.
“Sign of a doesn’t matter for the vertex location.” Sign doesn’t shift the vertex’s coordinates, but it determines whether the vertex is min or max.
“Bigger |a| makes a wider parabola.” Reverse: bigger |a| means steeper (narrower) parabola. Smaller |a| → flatter (wider).



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

Every quadratic equation graphs as a parabola — a single, symmetric U (or upside-down U).
The vertex is the peak or trough; the axis of symmetry runs vertically through it; the sign of a chooses up or down.
Vertex form makes the geometry obvious; standard form makes the algebra easy — completing the square moves you between the two.



Self-Assessment

Could you sketch y = (x + 1)^2 - 4 from memory?
Without scrolling back, find the vertex of y = x^2 - 10x + 21.
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: vertex (5, -4).)

3 yes: ready for 03 · Conic Sections.
2 yes: redo P3 (fence) — quadratic optimisation is one of the workhorse applications.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P4.

title: 02 · Parabolas — Graphing Quadratics
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

01 · Quadratic Formula — completing the square gives vertex form.
T2 L05 — Linear Functions and Graphs — the coordinate plane and graph-reading.



What this unlocks

03 · Conic Sections — parabolas are one of four conic sections.
Tier 4 — Calculus — the derivative gives the slope at any point; setting it to zero finds vertex location for any function, not just quadratics.



Where this matters

Game development & physics. Projectile trajectories under uniform gravity are parabolas. Game physics engines compute parabolic arcs millions of times per frame.


AI / Machine Learning. Quadratic loss surfaces (S1) have closed- form minima; this is the basis of analytical regression and the foundation of optimisation theory.


Engineering. Parabolic mirrors and antennas (S2), suspension- bridge cables (parabolic shape under uniform load), parabolic arches in architecture.


Mathematical research. Parabolas are the simplest non-linear curves; understanding them lays the groundwork for algebraic geometry (Tier 6+), the study of curves defined by polynomial equations.

title: 06 · Exponential and Logarithmic Functions
text:
Have you done 05 · Logarithms and T2 L01 — Functions as Machines?
Comfortable with f(x) = 2x + 5 and evaluating f(3) = 11?
Comfortable with the log rules (product, quotient, power)?

title: 06 · Exponential and Logarithmic Functions
06 · Exponential and Logarithmic Functions
text:
Wonder

The exponential curve never touches the x-axis but gets close. Why?
The log curve never touches the y-axis but gets close (going to -\infty). Why?
They cross at the line y = x at exactly one point (for b > 1). What’s special about that point?

title: 06 · Exponential and Logarithmic Functions
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Evaluate f(x) = 3 \cdot 2^x at x = 0, 1, 2, 3.


Full solution

f(0) = 3 \cdot 1 = 3. f(1) = 6. f(2) = 12. f(3) = 24.
The values double each unit step in x. Exponential functions multiply by b for each unit increase.



Problem 2
The function f(x) = 2^x has domain all real x and range all positive y. Its inverse f^{-1}(x) = \log_2(x) has the swapped domain and range. What are they?


Full solution

f^{-1}: domain \{x : x > 0\}, range all real y.
When you invert a function, domain and range swap. The exponential 2^x never produces 0 or negative numbers, so the log of those is undefined.



Problem 3
Solve 5^x = 80.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Take \log_{10} of both sides; use power rule.



Full solution

x \log_{10}(5) = \log_{10}(80). x = \log_{10}(80) / \log_{10}(5) \approx 1.903 / 0.699 \approx 2.722.
Check: 5^{2.722} \approx 5^{2} \cdot 5^{0.722} \approx 25 \cdot 3.2
\approx 80 ✓.



Problem 4
Solve \log_3(x + 2) = 4.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\log_3(x + 2) = 4 \iff x + 2 = 3^4.



Full solution

x + 2 = 81 \Rightarrow x = 79. Check: \log_3(81) = 4 ✓.
Logarithmic equations: rewrite as exponential, then solve normally.



Problem 5
A bacteria culture doubles every hour. Starting with 100 bacteria, after how many hours will there be 1,000,000?


Hint 1 — a nudge

N(t) = 100 \cdot 2^t. Set N = 10^6 and solve.



Full solution

100 \cdot 2^t = 10^6 \Rightarrow 2^t = 10^4 \Rightarrow t = \log_2(10^4) =
4 \log_2(10) \approx 4 \cdot 3.32 = 13.29 hours.
About 13.3 hours to reach a million. Logarithms answer “how long until X reaches Y?” for any exponential process.


The number e \approx 2.71828 was discovered by Jacob Bernoulli around 1683 while studying continuous compound interest — what happens to (1 + 1/n)^n as n grows. Leonhard Euler named it e in 1727 (probably for exponential, certainly not for Euler), and proved it was irrational. Euler’s identity e^{i\pi} + 1 = 0 ties together the five most important constants in mathematics in seven symbols. Euler’s e is the most natural number for growth because e^x is the only function that is its own derivative — the rate of change equals the current value, exactly the rule underlying every population, every account balance, every decay.


Exponentials and logarithms are the same function looked at from opposite sides. One asks “raise the base to this exponent — what do you get?”; the other asks “to reach this value — what exponent?”. Reflecting one across the line y = x gives the other. Every exponential equation is a logarithm waiting to be read off, and vice versa.

title: 06 · Exponential and Logarithmic Functions
text:
Name

The exponential function with base b > 0, b \neq 1: f(x) = b^x. - Domain: all real x. Range: y > 0. - Increasing if b > 1, decreasing if 0 < b < 1. - Always passes through (0, 1).
Its inverse is the logarithm: f^{-1}(x) = \log_b(x). - Domain: x > 0. Range: all real y. - Always passes through (1, 0). - Mirror image of b^x across y = x.
The natural exponential e^x (with e \approx 2.71828) is the preferred base in calculus, statistics, and science. Its inverse is \ln(x) = \log_e(x).
Equation strategies:

Exponential equation (b^{f(x)} = c): take \log_b of both sides.
Log equation (\log_b(f(x)) = c): rewrite as f(x) = b^c.



Exponential and log are inverse functions — reflections across y = x; Solve exponentials by taking logs; solve logs by exponentiating; The natural base e is calculus’s favourite.

title: 06 · Exponential and Logarithmic Functions
text:
Apply

Worked example

The population of a town grows by 4% per year. If today’s population is 50,000, how many years until it doubles?

A competent solver:

Model: P(t) = 50000 \cdot (1.04)^t.
Equation: 50000 \cdot 1.04^t = 100000 \Rightarrow 1.04^t = 2.
Take log: t \log(1.04) = \log(2).
Solve: t = \log(2) / \log(1.04) \approx 0.301 / 0.0170 \approx
17.7 years.

Answer: ~17.7 years. This is one form of the famous Rule of 72 (doubling time ≈ 72/percentage rate, here 72/4 = 18 — close).


Practice — Warm
W1. Evaluate f(x) = 4 \cdot 3^x at x = 0, 2.


Full solution

f(0) = 4. f(2) = 36.

W2. Solve 2^x = 32.


Full solution

x = 5 (since 2^5 = 32).

W3. Solve \log_4(x) = 2.


Full solution

x = 4^2 = 16.



Practice — Working
P1. Solve 3^{x+1} = 27.


Hint 1 — a nudge

27 = 3^3. Equate exponents.



Full solution

3^{x+1} = 3^3 \Rightarrow x + 1 = 3 \Rightarrow x = 2.

P2. Solve \log_2(x) + \log_2(x - 2) = 3.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Combine using product rule: \log_2(x(x-2)) = 3. Then exponentiate.



Full solution

x(x - 2) = 8 \Rightarrow x^2 - 2x - 8 = 0 \Rightarrow (x - 4)(x + 2) =
0. So x = 4 or x = -2.
But check: \log_2(-2) is undefined, so reject x = -2. x = 4.
Always check log-equation solutions against the original equation’s domain. Spurious solutions can appear after combining logs.

P3. A radioactive substance decays with half-life 6 years: every 6 years half remains. Starting with 100 g, write M(t). After 20 years, how much remains?


Hint 1 — a nudge

M(t) = 100 \cdot (1/2)^{t/6}.



Full solution

M(20) = 100 \cdot (1/2)^{20/6} = 100 \cdot 0.5^{3.333} \approx 100
\cdot 0.0992 \approx 9.92 g.
Half-lives are exponential decay, the negative-exponent cousin of exponential growth. Carbon-14 dating, drug elimination from the body, and many physical processes follow this pattern.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Newton’s law of cooling — a hot object cools toward room temperature exponentially. A pot of soup at 90°C cools by half toward room temperature (20°C) every 10 minutes. So:
T(t) - 20 = (90 - 20) \cdot (1/2)^{t/10}.
When does the soup reach 30°C?


Full solution

T(t) - 20 = 10 \Rightarrow 70 \cdot (1/2)^{t/10} = 10 \Rightarrow
(1/2)^{t/10} = 1/7.
Take log: (t/10) \log(1/2) = \log(1/7) \Rightarrow t = 10 \cdot
\log(1/7) / \log(1/2) = 10 \cdot \log_2(7) \approx 10 \cdot 2.807
\approx 28.07 minutes.
About 28 minutes. Newton’s law (1701) is one of the oldest applications of exponential decay; modern thermal engineering uses it constantly.

S2. The “softmax” function in ML turns raw scores z_1, z_2,
\ldots, z_n into probabilities: p_i = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}. Why use e^{z} rather than just z itself?


Hint 1 — a nudge

What does e^z guarantee about its output? What about negative scores?



Full solution

Three reasons.

e^z > 0 always — so we get valid probabilities, even from negative scores.
e^z amplifies differences: a small advantage in scores (z_1 = 1, z_2 = 0) becomes a clear preference (p_1 \approx
0.73).
The natural base e has a clean derivative (\frac{d}{dz} e^z = e^z), making backpropagation efficient (Tier 4+).

Softmax is the bridge from neural-network scores to probabilities — and the universal output layer of every classification model. The exponential function you just met is doing this work trillions of times per second across the world’s deployed AI systems.



Common confusions

“2^x = -8 has a solution.” No — 2^x > 0 always. Some exponential equations simply have no real solution.
“\log(0) = 0.” \log(0) is undefined (or -\infty as a limit). \log(1) = 0.
“The natural log is just one of many.” True technically, but \ln has a special role: \frac{d}{dx} \ln(x) = 1/x, the simplest derivative. That’s why e is the base of choice in advanced maths.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

The exponential function b^x and the logarithm \log_b(x) are inverses — reflections of each other across the line y = x.
To solve an exponential equation, take a log; to solve a logarithmic equation, exponentiate — they undo each other.
The natural base e \approx 2.718 is the universal language of continuous growth and decay; \ln is its inverse, and most of science is written in this pair.



Self-Assessment

Could you sketch y = 2^x and y = \log_2(x) on the same axes from memory, identifying their reflective symmetry?
Without scrolling back, can you solve 3^x = 27?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: x = 3.)

3 yes: ready for 07 · Trig Ratios.
2 yes: redo S2 (softmax) — central to modern ML.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P5.

title: 06 · Exponential and Logarithmic Functions
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

05 · Logarithms
T2 L01 — Functions as Machines



What this unlocks

Tier 4 — Calculus — e^x is its own derivative; \ln is the primitive of 1/x. These two facts are calculus’s gravity.



Where this matters

AI / Machine Learning. Softmax (S2), sigmoid (a logistic function), exponential learning-rate decay, log-likelihood optimisation — exponentials and logs are everywhere.


Systems. Big-O complexity analysis often involves \log n (binary search, balanced trees) and 2^n (NP-hard problems). Algorithm choice is often “polynomial vs exponential.”


Mathematical research. e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta (Euler’s formula, Tier 6) unifies exponentials, trigonometry, and complex numbers. One of the most beautiful identities in mathematics.


Everywhere else. Population growth, compound interest, drug half-lives, radioactive dating, sound (decibels), earthquakes (Richter), pH, Newton’s cooling — exponentials and logs model the rate-of-change-proportional-to-current-amount situations that dominate physical, biological, and economic systems.

title: 12 · The Binomial Theorem
text:
Have you done 11 · Combinatorics?
Comfortable with \binom{n}{r} = n!/(r!(n-r)!)?
Comfortable with polynomial expansion: (x+1)^2 = x^2 + 2x + 1?

title: 12 · The Binomial Theorem
12 · The Binomial Theorem
text:
Wonder

(a + b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2. Coefficients: 1, 2, 1. Where did the 2 come from?
(a + b)^3 = a^3 + 3a^2 b + 3ab^2 + b^3. Coefficients: 1, 3, 3, 1. Pattern in Pascal’s triangle?
The sum of any row of Pascal’s triangle: 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, \ldots Why?

title: 12 · The Binomial Theorem
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Expand (a + b)^4 by direct multiplication: (a + b)^2 \cdot (a + b)^2.


Hint 1 — a nudge

(a + b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2. Multiply by itself.



Full solution

(a^2 + 2ab + b^2)(a^2 + 2ab + b^2) = a^4 + 2a^3b + a^2b^2 + 2a^3b
+ 4a^2b^2 + 2ab^3 + a^2b^2 + 2ab^3 + b^4.
Collect: a^4 + 4a^3 b + 6 a^2 b^2 + 4 a b^3 + b^4. Coefficients: 1, 4, 6, 4, 1.
Tedious. The binomial theorem gives the answer directly.



Problem 2
The coefficients of (a+b)^4 are 1, 4, 6, 4, 1. Verify: \binom{4}{0}, \binom{4}{1}, \binom{4}{2}, \binom{4}{3}, \binom{4}{4}.


Full solution

\binom{4}{0} = 1, \binom{4}{1} = 4, \binom{4}{2} = 6, \binom{4}{3} =
4, \binom{4}{4} = 1 ✓.
This is the binomial theorem: (a + b)^n = \sum_{r=0}^{n} \binom{n}{r} a^{n-r} b^r. The combinatorics from Lesson 11 is the polynomial expansion from Tier 2.



Problem 3
Why? When you expand (a+b)^n = (a+b)(a+b)\cdots(a+b) (n factors), each term is built by choosing a or b from each factor. The number of ways to choose b from exactly r of the n factors is \binom{n}{r}. Hence the coefficient.


Full solution

That’s the proof of the binomial theorem in one paragraph. Choosing “which factors contribute b” is a combination problem — \binom{n}{r} choices for r factors out of n. Each such choice gives a term a^{n-r} b^r.
This is why combinatorics and algebra are tied together: the polynomial coefficient counts something combinatorial.



Problem 4
Pascal’s triangle. Each entry is the sum of the two above it. Use this to compute row 5 from row 4 (1, 4, 6, 4, 1).


Full solution

Row 5: 1, (1+4), (4+6), (6+4), (4+1), 1 = 1, 5, 10, 10, 5, 1.
So (a+b)^5 = a^5 + 5a^4 b + 10 a^3 b^2 + 10 a^2 b^3 + 5 a b^4 + b^5.
The Pascal’s-triangle pattern is just the identity \binom{n}{r} =
\binom{n-1}{r-1} + \binom{n-1}{r} — provable directly from the formula or by combinatorial argument.



Problem 5
Compute the sum of all entries in row n of Pascal’s triangle.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Set a = b = 1 in (a+b)^n. Both sides become…



Full solution

(1 + 1)^n = 2^n on the left. On the right (binomial theorem): \sum_{r=0}^{n} \binom{n}{r}.
So \sum_{r=0}^{n} \binom{n}{r} = 2^n. The sum of any row n of Pascal’s triangle is 2^n. (Row 0: 1. Row 1: 2. Row 2: 4. Row 3: 8. Etc.)
This identity has a beautiful combinatorial interpretation: 2^n is the total number of subsets of an n-set; \binom{n}{r} counts subsets of each size; together they partition.


The triangular array on this page was published by Pascal in his Traité du triangle arithmétique (1665) — but it had been studied in China by Yang Hui around 1300 CE, in Persia by al-Karajī in the 11th century, and in India by Pingala as early as the 2nd century BCE. Newton, in the same year as Pascal’s treatise, generalised the theorem to fractional and negative exponents — the infinite series (1+x)^{1/2}, (1+x)^{-1}, \ldots — turning a finite algebraic identity into the gateway to calculus.


The coefficient is doing combinatorics. Every \binom{n}{r} in the expansion is silently asking: “in how many ways can I pick r of the n factors to contribute a b?” Algebra and counting are not two subjects — they are one subject that learned to wear two costumes.

title: 12 · The Binomial Theorem
text:
Name

The Binomial Theorem. For any non-negative integer n and any a, b: (a + b)^n = \sum_{r=0}^{n} \binom{n}{r} a^{n-r} b^r.
The coefficients \binom{n}{r} are the binomial coefficients — the same numbers that count r-subsets of an n-set.
Pascal’s triangle: rows are binomial coefficient rows. Each entry is the sum of the two above it (\binom{n}{r} = \binom{n-1}{r-1} +
\binom{n-1}{r}).
Useful identities:

\binom{n}{r} = \binom{n}{n-r} (symmetry — Pascal’s rows are palindromes).
\sum_{r=0}^{n} \binom{n}{r} = 2^n (set a = b = 1).
\sum_{r=0}^{n} (-1)^r \binom{n}{r} = 0 for n \ge 1 (set a = 1,
b = -1).



Coefficients of (a + b)^n are binomial coefficients; Pascal’s triangle: each entry is the sum of the two above; Combinatorics chooses which factors contribute b — that’s the proof.

title: 12 · The Binomial Theorem
text:
Apply

Worked example

Expand (2x + 3)^4.

Apply the binomial theorem with a = 2x, b = 3, n = 4:
\binom{4}{0} (2x)^4 + \binom{4}{1} (2x)^3 (3) + \binom{4}{2} (2x)^2
(3)^2 + \binom{4}{3} (2x)(3)^3 + \binom{4}{4} (3)^4
= 16 x^4 + 4 \cdot 8 x^3 \cdot 3 + 6 \cdot 4 x^2 \cdot 9 + 4 \cdot 2x
\cdot 27 + 81
= 16 x^4 + 96 x^3 + 216 x^2 + 216 x + 81.


Practice — Warm
W1. Row 4 of Pascal’s triangle?


Full solution

1, 4, 6, 4, 1.

W2. Coefficient of a^2 b^3 in (a + b)^5?


Full solution

\binom{5}{3} = 10. (3 factors contribute b.)

W3. (x + 1)^4 = ?


Full solution

x^4 + 4x^3 + 6x^2 + 4x + 1.



Practice — Working
P1. Find the coefficient of x^3 in (2x - 1)^5.


Hint 1 — a nudge

General term: \binom{5}{r} (2x)^{5-r} (-1)^r. Want x^3, so 5 - r = 3 \Rightarrow r = 2.



Full solution

\binom{5}{2} (2x)^3 (-1)^2 = 10 \cdot 8 x^3 \cdot 1 = 80 x^3. Coefficient: 80.

P2. Show that \binom{6}{2} = \binom{6}{4}.


Full solution

\binom{6}{2} = 15, \binom{6}{4} = 15 ✓. Both equal 6!/(2! \cdot 4!) since r and n - r swap symmetrically.

P3. Expand (1 - x)^4.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Treat as (1 + (-x))^4.



Full solution

1 - 4x + 6x^2 - 4x^3 + x^4. (Alternating signs because of the (-x)^r.)



Practice — Stretch
S1. Approximation by binomial expansion. (1 + x)^n \approx 1
+ nx when x is small. Use this to estimate (1.02)^{10}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

x = 0.02, n = 10. Linear approximation: 1 + 10 \cdot 0.02 = 1.2.



Full solution

1 + 10 \cdot 0.02 = 1.20. (Exact value: 1.219, so the linear estimate is close.)
This is the first-order Taylor approximation in disguise (Tier 4+). It powers the Rule of 72 (compound interest doubling time) and many quick mental-arithmetic tricks.

S2. Why does the binomial theorem matter for cryptography? The Frobenius endomorphism x \mapsto x^p on \mathbb{F}_p (a finite field of prime order p) has the surprising property that (a + b)^p = a^p + b^p in \mathbb{F}_p. Use the binomial theorem to explain.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Look at \binom{p}{r} for 1 \le r \le p - 1 when p is prime.



Full solution

\binom{p}{r} = \dfrac{p!}{r!(p-r)!} = \dfrac{p \cdot (p-1) \cdots (p-r+1)}{r!}. The numerator has a factor of p that the denominator (for 1 \le r
\le p - 1) does not (since p is prime, no smaller factorial contains it). So p divides \binom{p}{r}, which means in \mathbb{F}_p (arithmetic mod p), \binom{p}{r} \equiv 0.
Result: (a + b)^p = a^p + b^p in \mathbb{F}_p. The “freshman’s dream” identity, normally false, becomes true in finite fields of prime characteristic.
This identity underlies AES, Reed-Solomon codes, and most modern cryptography. The binomial theorem you just learned is doing load-bearing work in security at industrial scale.



Common confusions

“(a + b)^n = a^n + b^n.” False — that’s the freshman’s dream, true only in special algebraic settings (Stretch S2). The binomial theorem has cross-terms.
“Pascal’s triangle starts at row 1.” Convention is row 0 (with single entry 1, the case n = 0). Indexing matters: row n has n + 1 entries.
“Binomial coefficients are unrelated to combinations.” They are exactly combinations: \binom{n}{r} = C(n, r).



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

The binomial theorem writes (a+b)^n as one orderly sum — every term is \binom{n}{r}\,a^{n-r}b^r and nothing extra ever appears.
Each coefficient is a combination in disguise. \binom{n}{r} counts which of the n factors contributed a b; the algebra inherits the count.
Pascal’s triangle is the binomial theorem on your fingers — each row sums to 2^n, and each entry is the sum of the two above. No formula needed for small n.



Self-Assessment

Could you explain why (a + b)^n has \binom{n}{r} as the coefficient of a^{n-r} b^r?
Without scrolling back, can you write the row 5 of Pascal’s triangle?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: 1, 5, 10, 10, 5, 1.)

3 yes: ready for 13 · Basic Probability.
2 yes: redo S2 (Frobenius endomorphism) — surprising and consequential.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P5.

title: 12 · The Binomial Theorem
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

11 · Combinatorics — Counting — binomial coefficients = combinations.
T2 L11 — Polynomials — expanding products of binomials.



What this unlocks

Tier 4 — Calculus — Taylor series generalise binomial expansion to arbitrary functions.
Tier 5 — Probability — binomial distribution counts k successes in n trials.



Where this matters

Encryption. Frobenius / freshman’s dream identity (S2) underpins modern cryptography in finite fields.


Mathematical research. Generalised binomial coefficients (Newton’s generalised binomial theorem) extend to negative and fractional n, giving infinite series for (1 + x)^{-1}, (1 + x)^{1/2}, etc.


Statistics & ML. Binomial distribution = \binom{n}{k} p^k
(1-p)^{n-k} — the building block of many statistical models. Logistic regression and naive Bayes use this directly.

title: 09 · Trigonometric Identities
text:
Have you done 08 · Unit Circle?
Comfortable with \sin^2 \theta + \cos^2 \theta = 1?
Recall \tan \theta = \sin \theta / \cos \theta.

title: 09 · Trigonometric Identities
09 · Trigonometric Identities
text:
Wonder

The Pythagorean identity \sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1 holds for every angle. Where does that come from geometrically?
There’s a “double-angle” formula \sin(2\theta) = 2 \sin \theta
\cos \theta. How does it relate \sin 30° and \sin 60°?
Can you derive \sin(\alpha + \beta) from \sin \alpha, \cos
\alpha, \sin \beta, \cos \beta?

title: 09 · Trigonometric Identities
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Verify \sin 60° = 2 \sin 30° \cos 30° (the double-angle formula applied to \theta = 30°).


Full solution

2 \sin 30° \cos 30° = 2 \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} =
\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \sin 60° ✓.
The double-angle formula \sin(2\theta) = 2 \sin\theta \cos\theta is one of the most-used identities. We will derive it from the angle- addition formula.



Problem 2
Divide \sin^2 \theta + \cos^2 \theta = 1 by \cos^2 \theta. What identity do you get?


Full solution

\dfrac{\sin^2 \theta}{\cos^2 \theta} + 1 = \dfrac{1}{\cos^2 \theta}
\Rightarrow \tan^2 \theta + 1 = \sec^2 \theta.
Similarly dividing by \sin^2: 1 + \cot^2 \theta = \csc^2 \theta.
These are the three Pythagorean identities — all three follow from the basic one by dividing.



Problem 3
The angle-addition formula for sine: \sin(\alpha + \beta) = \sin \alpha \cos \beta + \cos \alpha \sin \beta.
Use it to compute \sin 75° as \sin(45° + 30°).


Hint 1 — a nudge

\sin 45° \cos 30° + \cos 45° \sin 30°.



Full solution

= \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \cdot \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} + \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} = \tfrac{\sqrt{6} + \sqrt{2}}{4}.
Decimal: \approx 0.966 — matches \sin 75°. The angle-addition formulas let you compute trig of any angle if you know the component pieces.



Problem 4
Derive the double-angle for sine from the angle-addition formula by setting \alpha = \beta = \theta.


Full solution

\sin(\theta + \theta) = \sin\theta \cos\theta + \cos\theta \sin\theta
= 2 \sin \theta \cos \theta ✓.
Similarly, the double-angle for cosine: \cos(2\theta) = \cos^2\theta -
\sin^2\theta, derivable from the cosine-addition formula \cos(\alpha + \beta) = \cos\alpha\cos\beta - \sin\alpha\sin\beta.
Three equivalent forms of \cos 2\theta: \cos^2 - \sin^2 = 2\cos^2 -
1 = 1 - 2\sin^2 (using sin² + cos² = 1).



Problem 5
Simplify \dfrac{\sin \theta}{1 + \cos \theta} + \dfrac{1 + \cos \theta}{\sin \theta} to a single fraction.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Common denominator \sin\theta (1 + \cos\theta). Combine, expand, use \sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1.



Full solution

= \dfrac{\sin^2 \theta + (1 + \cos\theta)^2}{\sin\theta (1 + \cos\theta)}
= \dfrac{\sin^2 \theta + 1 + 2\cos\theta + \cos^2\theta}{\sin\theta (1 + \cos\theta)}.
Use \sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1: numerator becomes 1 + 1 + 2\cos\theta = 2(1 + \cos\theta).
= \dfrac{2(1 + \cos\theta)}{\sin\theta (1 + \cos\theta)} = \dfrac{2}{\sin\theta} = 2 \csc\theta.
A messy expression collapses to 2 \csc \theta via two identities. This is the flavour of trig manipulation — pattern-matching to known identities.


The earliest “trig table” in history — Ptolemy’s chord table in the Almagest (c. 150 CE) — was built by repeated use of an identity now called Ptolemy’s theorem, the geometric ancestor of the angle-addition formulas. Centuries later, the Swiss mathematician Leonhard Euler in his Introductio in Analysin Infinitorum (1748) reframed sine and cosine as functions of a real number rather than tables of chord lengths — turning trig from a surveyor’s catalogue into the analytic toolkit you are using here.


An identity is a re-phrasing, not a discovery. The two sides of \sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1 are the same number wearing two outfits. All of trig manipulation is a wardrobe change: pull on the outfit that makes the next step obvious.

title: 09 · Trigonometric Identities
text:
Name

Pythagorean identities: \sin^2 \theta + \cos^2 \theta = 1, \quad 1 + \tan^2 \theta = \sec^2 \theta, \quad 1 + \cot^2 \theta = \csc^2 \theta.
Quotient identities: \tan \theta = \dfrac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}, \cot \theta = \dfrac{\cos\theta}{\sin\theta}.
Reciprocal identities: \sec \theta = \dfrac{1}{\cos\theta}, \csc \theta = \dfrac{1}{\sin\theta}, \cot \theta = \dfrac{1}{\tan\theta}.
Angle-addition formulas:

\sin(\alpha + \beta) = \sin\alpha \cos\beta + \cos\alpha \sin\beta.
\cos(\alpha + \beta) = \cos\alpha \cos\beta - \sin\alpha \sin\beta.
\tan(\alpha + \beta) = \dfrac{\tan\alpha + \tan\beta}{1 - \tan\alpha \tan\beta}.

Double-angle formulas (special case \alpha = \beta):

\sin 2\theta = 2 \sin\theta \cos\theta.
\cos 2\theta = \cos^2 \theta - \sin^2 \theta = 2 \cos^2 \theta - 1 = 1 - 2 \sin^2 \theta.

Co-function identities: \sin(\pi/2 - \theta) = \cos\theta.


Pythagorean identity is unit-circle-on-paper; Angle-addition formulas build any angle from pieces; Double-angle formulas are addition formulas with α = β.

title: 09 · Trigonometric Identities
text:
Apply

Worked example

Simplify \sin\theta \cos\theta \cdot \sec\theta.

A competent solver:

\sec\theta = 1/\cos\theta.
Substitute: \sin\theta \cos\theta \cdot \dfrac{1}{\cos\theta} =
\sin\theta.

Answer: \sin \theta. Trig manipulation often “cancels” cleanly once the reciprocals and quotients are in their fundamental forms.


Practice — Warm
W1. Verify \sin^2 45° + \cos^2 45° = 1.


Full solution

(\sqrt{2}/2)^2 + (\sqrt{2}/2)^2 = 1/2 + 1/2 = 1 ✓.

W2. Simplify \sin\theta / \cos\theta.


Full solution

\tan\theta.

W3. \sin 2\theta when \sin\theta = 0.6, \cos\theta = 0.8.


Full solution

2 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 0.8 = 0.96.



Practice — Working
P1. Use \cos(\alpha + \beta) = \cos\alpha\cos\beta - \sin\alpha\sin\beta to compute \cos 75° = \cos(45° + 30°).


Full solution

= \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \cdot \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} - \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} = \tfrac{\sqrt{6} - \sqrt{2}}{4} \approx 0.259.

P2. Show \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{\sin 2\theta} = \tan\theta.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Use \cos 2\theta = 1 - 2\sin^2\theta in the numerator; \sin 2\theta = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta in the denominator.



Full solution
