Do the equality cases (\le vs <) matter for the picture? Why does it become a dashed line for strict inequalities?







TipWhat you might notice





The dangerous step is multiplying or dividing by a negative — the inequality flips. Forgetting this is the single most common error in the chapter.

title: Ch 5 — Linear Inequalities
text:
Discover

Problem 1 — Solve a one-variable inequality
Solve 4x - 3 < 17 for x.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Treat it like an equation, but keep the inequality sign.



Full solution

4x < 20 \Rightarrow x < 5.
Solution set: (-\infty, 5). On the number line: open circle at 5, arrow to the left.



Problem 2 — Negative coefficient
Solve -2x + 5 > 1.


Hint 1 — a nudge

-2x > -4. Now divide by -2 — and remember to flip the inequality.



Full solution

-2x > -4 \Rightarrow x < 2 (dividing by -2 flips > to <).



Problem 3 — Combined inequality
Solve -3 \le 2x + 1 < 7.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Subtract 1 from all three parts; then divide by 2.



Full solution

-4 \le 2x < 6 \Rightarrow -2 \le x < 3.
Solution set [-2, 3).



Problem 4 — Graph in the plane
Sketch the half-plane y \ge 2x - 1.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Draw the line y = 2x - 1 first. Decide which side of the line satisfies the inequality by testing a sample point (e.g. the origin).



Full solution

Line passes through (0, -1) and (1, 1). Test (0, 0): 0 \ge -1? Yes. So (0, 0) is in the solution. Shade the side above the line. Boundary is solid because \ge includes equality.



Problem 5 — System of inequalities
Sketch the region defined by x + y \le 4,\ x \ge 0,\ y \ge 0.


Hint 1 — a nudge

x \ge 0 and y \ge 0 keep you in the first quadrant. Then bound from above by x + y = 4.



Full solution

A triangle with vertices (0,0), (4, 0), (0, 4), including the boundary.



Problem 6 — A word problem
A factory produces two products: each unit of A uses 2 kg of raw material; each unit of B uses 3 kg. The factory has 24 kg of raw material. Write the constraint.


Full solution

2x + 3y \le 24, plus x \ge 0, y \ge 0 (you can’t produce negative units).

You have just used flipping the sign on negative multiplication, combined inequalities, half-plane graphs, and the boundary line solid/dashed convention. Now we name them.

The symbols < and > are surprisingly young. They were introduced by Thomas Harriot in Artis Analyticae Praxis (printed posthumously in 1631), wedged into a manuscript of his alongside our modern equality. For two thousand years before that, mathematicians wrote inequalities in sentences — “minus thus is greater than” — until Harriot’s two strokes caught on. The next time you write x \le 5, remember it took the Renaissance to compress that idea into three keystrokes.


A linear inequality is almost a linear equation — same algebra, same graph — except the answer is a region, not a point. The single algebraic difference is rule 3: multiplying or dividing by a negative flips the sign. That one rule, plus “shade the half-plane that contains a test point you trust,” carries the entire chapter.

title: Ch 5 — Linear Inequalities
text:
Name

A linear inequality in one variable has the form ax + b \,\square\, 0 \quad \text{where } \square \in \{<, \le, >, \ge\}.
Operating rules (carry the inequality through unchanged, except rule 4):

Add or subtract the same value on both sides — sign unchanged.
Multiply or divide both sides by a positive number — sign unchanged.
Multiply or divide both sides by a negative number — flip the inequality sign.
The same applies in two variables: ax + by \,\square\, c.

Geometric picture:







Inequality
Picture in \mathbb{R}^2




ax + by \le c
half-plane below/left of the line ax+by = c, solid boundary


ax + by < c
same half-plane, dashed boundary


ax + by \ge c
other half-plane, solid boundary


ax + by > c
other half-plane, dashed boundary



A system of linear inequalities has the intersection of the half-planes as its solution. This region is always convex and is the seed of linear programming (Class 12).

A short verse:

Add and subtract — the sign holds true; Multiply by minus — the sign turns askew. Draw a line, pick a side, shade and seal — Strict gets a dash, weak gets the deal.

title: Ch 5 — Linear Inequalities
text:
Apply

Worked example

Solve and graph: \dfrac{2x - 3}{4} + 9 \ge 3 + \dfrac{4x}{3}.

How a competent solver thinks:

Multiply both sides by 12 (LCM of denominators) to clear fractions: 3(2x - 3) + 108 \ge 36 + 16x.
Expand: 6x - 9 + 108 \ge 36 + 16x.
Simplify: 6x + 99 \ge 36 + 16x, i.e. 63 \ge 10x, i.e. x \le 6.3.

Solution set: (-\infty, 6.3]. On the number line: closed circle at 6.3, arrow to the left.


Practice — Warm
W1. Solve 5x + 3 > 18.


Full solution

5x > 15 \Rightarrow x > 3.

W2. Solve -4x + 1 \le 9.


Full solution

-4x \le 8 \Rightarrow x \ge -2 (sign flipped).

W3. Solve \dfrac{x}{2} - 1 < 3.


Full solution

\dfrac{x}{2} < 4 \Rightarrow x < 8.



Practice — Working
P1. Solve \dfrac{3(x - 1)}{2} \le \dfrac{2(x + 3)}{3}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Multiply both sides by 6 to clear fractions.



Full solution

9(x - 1) \le 4(x + 3) \Rightarrow 9x - 9 \le 4x + 12 \Rightarrow
5x \le 21 \Rightarrow x \le 21/5.

P2. Solve the system: 2x + y \le 6,\ x + 2y \le 6,\ x \ge 0,\ y \ge 0. Sketch.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Find intersection of the two slanted lines: 2x + y = 6 and x + 2y = 6.



Full solution

Intersection: subtract — (2x + y) - (x + 2y) = 0 gives x = y. Sub into either: 3x = 6, so x = y = 2.
Vertices of the region: (0, 0), (3, 0), (2, 2), (0, 3) — a quadrilateral.

P3. A man wants to cut three lengths from a 91-cm board. The second is to be 3 cm longer than the first; the third is to be twice as long as the first. What are the possible lengths of the first piece?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let first = x. Second = x + 3. Third = 2x. Total \le 91. Also x > 0 and the third must be at least 5 cm longer than the second.



Full solution

x + (x+3) + 2x \le 91 \Rightarrow 4x + 3 \le 91 \Rightarrow x \le 22.
Also 2x \ge (x+3) + 5 \Rightarrow x \ge 8.
Answer: 8 \le x \le 22 (cm).



Practice — Stretch
S1. Solve |x - 2| < 3 and |2x + 5| \ge 1 simultaneously.


Hint 1 — a nudge

|y| < c \Leftrightarrow -c < y < c. |y| \ge c \Leftrightarrow y \le -c or y \ge c.



Full solution

First: -3 < x - 2 < 3 \Rightarrow -1 < x < 5.
Second: 2x + 5 \le -1 or 2x + 5 \ge 1, i.e. x \le -3 or x \ge -2.
Intersection of (-1, 5) with \{x \le -3\} \cup \{x \ge -2\}: (-1, 5) \cap [-2, \infty) = (-1, 5). (The x \le -3 branch contributes nothing.)
Answer: -1 < x < 5.

S2. Find the region in the plane satisfying: x + 2y \le 8,\ 2x + y \le 8,\ x, y \ge 0. Identify the vertices.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Intersect each pair of lines.



Full solution

Intersection of slanted lines: x + 2y = 8, 2x + y = 8. Subtract: x - y = 0, so x = y = 8/3.
Vertices: (0, 0), (4, 0), (8/3, 8/3), (0, 4).



Board-Pattern Practice
1-mark (objective). The solution of -x + 2 \le 0 is (a) x \le 2 (b) x \ge 2 (c) x < 2 (d) x > 2.


Full solution

-x \le -2 \Rightarrow x \ge 2. Answer: (b).

2-mark (short answer). Solve and represent on a number line: 5x - 3 \ge 3x - 5.


Full solution

2x \ge -2 \Rightarrow x \ge -1. Closed circle at -1, arrow right.

3-mark (short answer II). Solve the system: 3x + 4y \le 60,\
x + 3y \le 30,\ x, y \ge 0. List the corner points.


Hint 1 — a nudge

The corners are intersections of pairs of boundary lines.



Full solution

Slanted lines meet: 3x + 4y = 60, x + 3y = 30. Multiply second by 3: 3x + 9y = 90. Subtract: 5y = 30, so y = 6, x = 30 - 18 = 12.
Vertices: (0, 0), (20, 0), (12, 6), (0, 10).

5-mark (long answer). A workshop produces tables and chairs. A table needs 2 hours of carpentry and 1 hour of finishing. A chair needs 1 hour of carpentry and 3 hours of finishing. The workshop has 40 hours of carpentry and 50 hours of finishing per week. Write the inequalities and sketch the feasible region. Identify all corner points.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let x = tables, y = chairs. Two resource constraints, plus non-negativity.



Full solution

Constraints: 2x + y \le 40, x + 3y \le 50, x \ge 0, y \ge 0.
Boundary lines:
2x + y = 40: passes through (0, 40) and (20, 0).
x + 3y = 50: passes through (0, 50/3) and (50, 0).
Their intersection: from 2x + y = 40, y = 40 - 2x. Sub: x + 3(40 - 2x) = 50 \Rightarrow x + 120 - 6x = 50 \Rightarrow -5x = -70
\Rightarrow x = 14, y = 12.
Corner points: (0, 0), (20, 0), (14, 12), (0, 50/3).
The feasible region is a quadrilateral.







NCERT Exemplar pointer: Exemplar Ch 5 LA “Solve graphically the system 2x + y \le 24, etc.” matches the 5-mark item. Class 12 / JEE pointer: this chapter is the foundation of linear programming in Class 12.





Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

Inequalities behave like equations — except for one rule. Multiplying or dividing both sides by a negative number flips < to > (and \le to \ge). Forget this once and the rest of the answer is wrong.
A two-variable inequality is a half-plane. Draw the boundary line; pick a test point (the origin if it isn’t on the line); shade the side that satisfies the inequality. Strict inequalities get a dashed boundary, weak ones get a solid boundary.
A system of inequalities is the intersection of half-planes. This feasible region is always convex, and its corner points are where Class 12 linear programming will look for the optimum.



Common confusions

“Multiplying both sides by anything keeps the inequality.” Wrong. Multiplying by a negative flips the sign. Multiplying by a variable of unknown sign is forbidden without case analysis.
“\le and < give the same picture.” They give the same shaded region but a different boundary: \le is solid, < is dashed. Boards mark this distinction.
“|x| < c means x < c.” No. |x| < c means -c < x < c (for c > 0). The absolute value translates into a bounded interval, not a half-line.



Self-Assessment
Be honest with yourself.

Could you solve a one-variable inequality including a negative coefficient without notes?
Did you solve at least 5 of the 6 Discover problems and 5 of the 7 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?
Can you sketch a system of two or three inequalities and identify the corner points?


3 yes: move on to Permutations and Combinations.
2 yes: redo Stretch S2 and the 5-mark item — Class 12 LP depends on this.
0–1 yes: sketch ten more two-inequality systems by hand.

title: Ch 5 — Linear Inequalities
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

Class 10 — Pair of Linear Equations — the boundary lines are exactly two-variable linear equations.
Linear Inequalities (T2) — one-variable warm-up.



What this unlocks

Class 12 — Linear Programming — optimise a linear function over a feasible region built exactly as this chapter teaches.



NCERT chapter alignment

Class 11, Chapter 5 — Linear Inequalities (NCERT 2023 rationalised). Sections 5.1–5.4: algebraic and graphical solutions in one and two variables; systems.
Sister synthesis lesson: t2.linear-inequalities.



Where this matters

AI / ML. Support Vector Machines define a separating half-plane between two classes — exactly the picture of a linear inequality. The “margin” is the distance from data points to the boundary.


Research / Operations Research. Every diet, transportation, and production-planning problem is a system of linear inequalities plus an objective. The simplex method (Dantzig, 1947) walks the corner points to find the optimum.








NoteAuthor’s notes





Touchstones: NRICH (the budget word problem is a low-floor entry to the two-variable case), Russian school (Discover problems force the sign-flip rule before it is named), Bhāskara (verse).

title: Ch 14 — Probability (Axiomatic Approach)
text:
Be honest before you begin.

Can you state the Class 10 definition P(E) = \dfrac{\text{favourable}}{\text{total}}?
Are you fluent with set operations \cup, \cap, ' from Sets?
Can you list the sample space when two coins are tossed?
Are you comfortable with the word “event” meaning “a subset of the sample space”?

Three confident yeses — read on.

title: Ch 14 — Probability (Axiomatic Approach)
Ch 14 — Probability (Axiomatic Approach)
text:
Wonder
Sit with these.

Toss two coins. Why is “exactly one head” not probability 1/3, even though there are three “kinds” of outcome (0H, 1H, 2H)?
The probability of an event A is between 0 and 1. What event has probability 0? Probability 1?
If P(A) = 0.4 and P(B) = 0.5 and P(A \cap B) = 0.1, what is P(A \cup B)? Is it 0.9? Or something else?
The event “either A or B” — is its probability the sum P(A) + P(B)? When?







TipWhat you might notice






is the eternal trap: outcomes must be equally likely. The four ordered pairs are equally likely; the three “kinds” are not. (4) is the addition rule: works as a sum only when A and B are disjoint.

title: Ch 14 — Probability (Axiomatic Approach)
text:
Discover

Problem 1 — Sample space
A die is rolled twice. Write the sample space.


Hint 1 — a nudge

36 ordered pairs.



Full solution

S = \{(i, j) : 1 \le i, j \le 6\}, |S| = 36.



Problem 2 — Probability of an event
Find the probability that the sum is 7 when a die is rolled twice.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Pairs summing to 7: (1,6), (2,5), (3,4), (4,3), (5,2), (6,1).



Full solution

6 favourable out of 36. P = 6/36 = 1/6.



Problem 3 — Complement
If P(A) = 3/8, find P(A').


Full solution

P(A') = 1 - P(A) = 5/8.



Problem 4 — Addition rule
If P(A) = 0.4, P(B) = 0.5, P(A \cap B) = 0.1, find P(A \cup B).


Hint 1 — a nudge

P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B).



Full solution

P(A \cup B) = 0.4 + 0.5 - 0.1 = 0.8.



Problem 5 — Mutually exclusive
A bag has 5 red, 3 blue, 4 green balls. One ball is drawn. Find the probability it is red or blue.


Hint 1 — a nudge

“Red” and “blue” are mutually exclusive: P(R \cup B) = P(R) + P(B).



Full solution

P(R) = 5/12, P(B) = 3/12. P(R \cup B) = 8/12 = 2/3.



Problem 6 — Card problem
A card is drawn from a standard 52-card deck. Find the probability the card is a heart or a king.


Hint 1 — a nudge

13 hearts, 4 kings, 1 king of hearts (in the overlap).



Full solution

P(H) + P(K) - P(H \cap K) = 13/52 + 4/52 - 1/52 = 16/52 = 4/13.



Problem 7 — Three coins
Three coins are tossed. Find the probability of getting at least two heads.


Hint 1 — a nudge

|S| = 8. Outcomes with \ge 2 heads: pick which 2 are H, plus all 3 H.



Full solution

At least two heads: \binom{3}{2} + \binom{3}{3} = 3 + 1 = 4 outcomes out of 8. P = 4/8 = 1/2.

You have just used sample spaces, events as subsets, the probability function, the complement rule, the addition rule, mutually exclusive events, and counting via combinations. Now we name them.

Kolmogorov axiomatises probability (1933). Pascal and Fermat exchanged the founding letters in 1654; Bernoulli, Laplace, and Bayes built the calculation rules over the next two centuries. But until 1933 nobody had answered “what is a probability, formally?” with anything better than counting equally-likely outcomes — a definition that breaks the moment outcomes are not finite or not equally likely. In a slim booklet titled Grundbegriffe der Wahrscheinlichkeitsrechnung, the Soviet mathematician Andrey Kolmogorov gave probability the same treatment Euclid gave geometry: three axioms (non-negativity, normalisation, countable additivity) and let everything else fall out as theorems. The three axioms in Name are his — distilled to fit a Class 11 page.


Probability is “fraction of outcomes” — but only when outcomes are equally likely. Get the sample space right and the rest is arithmetic. The addition rule P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B) is the universal version; disjoint events let you drop the overlap. Three reflexes carry the chapter: list the sample space, ask whether outcomes are equally likely, and use the complement when “at least one” appears.

title: Ch 14 — Probability (Axiomatic Approach)
text:
Name

Sample space S: the set of all outcomes of the experiment.
Event E: a subset E \subseteq S.
Equally-likely outcomes: if all |S| outcomes are equally likely, then for any event E,  P(E) = \dfrac{|E|}{|S|}. 
Kolmogorov’s axioms (for any sample space):

P(E) \ge 0 for every event E.
P(S) = 1.
If E_1, E_2 are mutually exclusive (E_1 \cap E_2 = \varnothing): P(E_1 \cup E_2) = P(E_1) + P(E_2).

Consequences:

P(\varnothing) = 0.
P(E') = 1 - P(E).
0 \le P(E) \le 1.
Addition rule (general): P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B).

Three-event addition: P(A \cup B \cup C) = P(A) + P(B) + P(C) - P(A \cap B) - P(A \cap C) - P(B \cap C) + P(A \cap B \cap C).

A short verse:

Outcomes equally likely — count and divide; Add when disjoint, subtract overlap aside. Complement adds to one; And every probability lies between zero and one.

title: Ch 14 — Probability (Axiomatic Approach)
text:
Apply

Worked example

A bag contains 4 red and 6 black balls. Two are drawn at random without replacement. Find the probability that both are red.

How a competent solver thinks:

Total ways to draw 2 balls: \binom{10}{2} = 45.
Ways to draw 2 red: \binom{4}{2} = 6.
P = 6/45 = 2/15.



Practice — Warm
W1. A die is rolled. Probability of an even number?


Full solution

3/6 = 1/2.

W2. Two coins tossed. Probability of two tails?


Full solution

1/4.

W3. A card drawn. Probability of a face card (J, Q, K)?


Full solution

12/52 = 3/13.



Practice — Working
P1. A bag contains 5 red and 3 blue balls. Two are drawn at random. Find the probability that they are of different colours.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Total ways: \binom{8}{2} = 28. Different colours: pick 1 red and 1 blue.



Full solution

Different colours: 5 \times 3 = 15. P = 15/28.

P2. Three letters are randomly placed in three envelopes (each envelope has its own correct letter). What is the probability that none of the letters is in its correct envelope?


Hint 1 — a nudge

The number of permutations with no fixed point is the derangement number. For 3 letters, this equals 2.



Full solution

Total permutations: 3! = 6. Derangements: 2 (the cycles 1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 1 and 1 \to 3 \to 2 \to 1).
P = 2/6 = 1/3.

P3. From a group of 7 men and 5 women, a committee of 4 is chosen. Find the probability that the committee has exactly 2 women.


Full solution

Total ways: \binom{12}{4} = 495. Favourable: \binom{5}{2}\binom{7}{2} = 10 \times 21 = 210.
P = 210/495 = 14/33.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Two dice are rolled. Find the probability that the sum is a prime number.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Possible sums: 2 to 12. Primes among them: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11. Count outcomes for each.



Full solution

Sum 2: 1 way. Sum 3: 2. Sum 5: 4. Sum 7: 6. Sum 11: 2.
Total: 1 + 2 + 4 + 6 + 2 = 15.
P = 15/36 = 5/12.

S2. A class has 25 students. Find the probability that at least two have the same birthday (assume 365 equally likely days, no leap years). State the answer to two decimals.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Use the complement: P(\text{all different}) = \dfrac{365}{365} \cdot \dfrac{364}{365} \cdots \dfrac{341}{365}.



Full solution

P(\text{all different}) = \prod_{k=0}^{24} \dfrac{365 - k}{365} \approx 0.4313.
P(\text{some match}) \approx 1 - 0.4313 = 0.5687 \approx 0.57.
This is the famous birthday paradox — over 50% chance with just 23 people.



Board-Pattern Practice
1-mark (objective). If P(A) = 0.4 and P(B) = 0.3 and A, B are mutually exclusive, then P(A \cup B) equals (a) 0.12 (b) 0.58 (c) 0.7 (d) 1.0.


Full solution

Mutually exclusive \Rightarrow A \cap B = \varnothing \Rightarrow
P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) = 0.4 + 0.3 = 0.7. Answer: (c).

2-mark (short answer). Two unbiased dice are rolled. Find the probability of getting a doublet (same number on both).


Full solution

Doublets: (1,1), (2,2), \ldots, (6,6) — 6 outcomes. P = 6/36 = 1/6.

3-mark (short answer II). From a deck of 52 cards, a card is drawn at random. Find the probability that it is (i) an ace, (ii) a face card, (iii) a red card or a king.


Full solution

(i) 4/52 = 1/13.
(ii) 12/52 = 3/13.
(iii) Red cards: 26. Kings: 4. Red kings (in overlap): 2. P = (26 + 4 - 2)/52 = 28/52 = 7/13.

5-mark (long answer). A box contains 4 defective and 6 non-defective items. Three items are drawn at random without replacement.

Find the probability that all three are non-defective.
Find the probability that exactly two are defective.
Find the probability that at least one is defective.



Hint 1 — a nudge

Total: \binom{10}{3} = 120.



Full solution

(i) \binom{6}{3}/\binom{10}{3} = 20/120 = 1/6.
(ii) \binom{4}{2}\binom{6}{1}/\binom{10}{3} = 6 \times 6/120 = 36/120 = 3/10.
(iii) Complement of “all non-defective”: 1 - 1/6 = 5/6.







NCERT Exemplar pointer: Exemplar Ch 14 LA “card-drawing combined events” matches the 5-mark item. JEE pointer: the birthday paradox (Stretch S2) is JEE Advanced adjacent — practice the complement-then-product technique.





Common confusions

“All outcomes are equally likely.” Sample-space outcomes must be defined so they are equally likely. “Three kinds of outcome” (0 heads, 1 head, 2 heads) is not equally likely; the four ordered pairs HH, HT, TH, TT are.
“P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) always.” Only when A \cap B =
\varnothing. In general, subtract the overlap.
“Probabilities can be greater than 1.” They cannot. If your formula gives > 1, either the events aren’t disjoint (and you need to subtract) or the question is malformed.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

Sample space first, then count. Make sure the outcomes you list are equally likely — that single check rules out most beginner errors.
Addition rule is “add, then remove the double-count.” P(A \cup
B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B). Disjoint events make the overlap zero.
“At least one” begs for the complement. P(\text{at least one}) =
1 - P(\text{none}) usually beats summing many disjoint cases.



Self-Assessment
Be honest with yourself.

Could you list the sample space for “draw 2 cards from a deck” and count the outcomes for “both red”?
Did you solve at least 5 of the 7 Discover problems and 5 of the 7 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?
Can you decide when to use the addition rule vs the complement vs the multiplication of independent events?


3 yes: you have completed Class 11 — congratulations!
2 yes: redo Stretch S2 and the 5-mark item.
0–1 yes: drill the addition rule on five fresh two-event problems.

title: Ch 14 — Probability (Axiomatic Approach)
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

Class 10 — Probability — the favourable/total ratio for equally likely outcomes.
Sets — events are subsets; operations \cup, \cap, ' are the heart of probability algebra.



What this unlocks

Class 12 — Probability — conditional probability, Bayes’ theorem, random variables, binomial and normal distributions.



NCERT chapter alignment

Class 11, Chapter 14 — Probability (NCERT 2023 rationalised). Sections 14.1–14.4: random experiments, events, axiomatic probability, addition rule.
Sister synthesis lessons: t3.basic-probability and t3.conditional-probability.



Where this matters

AI / ML. Every machine learning model encodes probabilities — class-conditional likelihoods, posterior beliefs, attention weights. Classifiers output P(\text{class} \mid \text{features}).


Encryption. A cipher is “secure” if the probability of guessing the plaintext from the ciphertext is essentially 1/|\text{key space}|. Cryptanalysis is probability theory weaponised.


Research. Statistical hypothesis testing rejects null hypotheses when the observed event has P < 0.05. The whole machinery of p-values starts from the axioms you’ve just learned.








NoteAuthor’s notes





Touchstones: NRICH (the birthday paradox), Russian school (Discover problems force the addition rule before it is named), Bhāskara (verse on disjoint vs overlapping events), Better Explained (“counting very carefully” framing).

title: Ch 8b — Series and Special Sums
text:
Be honest before you begin.

Can you write down the AP sum formula S_n = \dfrac{n}{2}(2a + (n-1)d)?
Are you fluent with the GP sum S_n = \dfrac{a(r^n - 1)}{r - 1}?
Are you comfortable with summation \sum notation?
Can you compute 1 + 2 + 3 + \cdots + 100 in your head with a trick?

Three confident yeses — read on.

title: Ch 8b — Series and Special Sums
Ch 8b — Series and Special Sums
text:
Wonder
Sit with these.

The sum \sum k has a n^2-flavoured answer. The sum \sum k^2 has a n^3-flavoured answer. What pattern do you predict for \sum k^4?
The sequence 1 \cdot 2, 2 \cdot 3, 3 \cdot 4, 4 \cdot 5, \ldots — what is its k-th term, and how would you sum it?
The series 1 + 2x + 3x^2 + 4x^3 + \cdots — neither AP nor GP, but a blend. Does it sum to something clean for |x| < 1?
Why does \sum k^3 = (\sum k)^2? Is that a coincidence?







TipWhat you might notice






is the seed of summing \sum k(k+1) = \sum (k^2 + k) by linearity. (3) is the arithmetic-geometric series — the headline of this lesson.

title: Ch 8b — Series and Special Sums
text:
Discover

Problem 1 — Sum of first n natural numbers
Compute 1 + 2 + 3 + \cdots + 100.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Pair: (1 + 100), (2 + 99), \ldots — Gauss’s trick.



Full solution

50 pairs, each summing to 101: 50 \times 101 = 5050. Equivalently, \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} = \dfrac{100 \cdot 101}{2} = 5050.



Problem 2 — Sum of squares
Compute 1^2 + 2^2 + 3^2 + \cdots + 10^2.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\dfrac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{6}.



Full solution

\dfrac{10 \cdot 11 \cdot 21}{6} = \dfrac{2310}{6} = 385.



Problem 3 — Sum of cubes
Verify 1^3 + 2^3 + 3^3 + 4^3 = (1 + 2 + 3 + 4)^2.


Full solution

LHS: 1 + 8 + 27 + 64 = 100. RHS: 10^2 = 100. ✓
In general \sum_{k=1}^{n} k^3 = \left(\dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}\right)^2.



Problem 4 — Linearity
Compute \sum_{k=1}^{20} (3k^2 + 2k - 5).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Split: 3 \sum k^2 + 2 \sum k - 5 \sum 1.



Full solution

3 \cdot \dfrac{20 \cdot 21 \cdot 41}{6} + 2 \cdot \dfrac{20 \cdot 21}{2} - 5 \cdot 20 = 3 \cdot 2870 + 420 - 100 = 8610 + 420 - 100 = 8930.



Problem 5 — Sum of k(k+1)
Compute \sum_{k=1}^{n} k(k+1) in closed form.


Hint 1 — a nudge

k(k+1) = k^2 + k.



Full solution

\sum k^2 + \sum k = \dfrac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{6} + \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}
= \dfrac{n(n+1)}{6}\left[(2n+1) + 3\right] = \dfrac{n(n+1)(2n+4)}{6} =
\dfrac{n(n+1)(n+2)}{3}.



Problem 6 — Arithmetic-geometric series
Find S = 1 + 2x + 3x^2 + 4x^3 + \cdots for |x| < 1.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Multiply by x, subtract.



Full solution

S = 1 + 2x + 3x^2 + 4x^3 + \cdots
xS = \quad x + 2x^2 + 3x^3 + \cdots
Subtract: S - xS = 1 + x + x^2 + x^3 + \cdots = \dfrac{1}{1 - x}.
So S(1 - x) = \dfrac{1}{1 - x}, giving S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - x)^2}.

You have just used the three special sums (\sum k, \sum k^2,
\sum k^3), linearity of summation, and the multiply-and-subtract trick for arithmetic-geometric (AGP) series. Now we name them.

The Indian astronomer-mathematician Aryabhata wrote the formulas for \sum k, \sum k^2, and \sum k^3 in his Aryabhatiya (499 CE) — verses II.19 and II.22 — more than a thousand years before Faulhaber’s general 1631 formula and Bernoulli’s Ars Conjectandi (1713) gave the rest of the family. Aryabhata stated the cube-sum identity \sum k^3 = (\sum k)^2 as a single Sanskrit verse, the length of a tweet. The three formulas you are about to memorise have been used continuously by working mathematicians for over fifteen hundred years.


Three closed-form sums — for \sum k, \sum k^2, \sum k^3 — plus linearity (\sum (au_k + bv_k) = a\sum
u_k + b\sum v_k) handles any polynomial \sum p(k). For sequences that are AP-times-GP the trick is multiply by r and subtract; for \sum 1/[k(k+1)] the trick is partial-fraction telescoping. Three formulas plus three tricks: that is the whole arsenal.

title: Ch 8b — Series and Special Sums
text:
Name

The three special sums:  \sum_{k=1}^{n} k = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}   \sum_{k=1}^{n} k^2 = \dfrac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{6}   \sum_{k=1}^{n} k^3 = \left(\dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}\right)^2 
Linearity: \sum (a u_k + b v_k) = a \sum u_k + b \sum v_k.
Arithmetic-Geometric Progression (AGP). Term: T_k = (a + (k-1)d) r^{k-1}. Sum to n terms (multiply-and-subtract method):  S_n = \dfrac{a - (a + (n-1)d) r^n}{1 - r} + \dfrac{d r (1 - r^{n-1})}{(1 - r)^2}. 
For |r| < 1, sum to infinity:  S_\infty = \dfrac{a}{1 - r} + \dfrac{d r}{(1 - r)^2}. 
(In practice: derive the formula on the spot by multiplying by r and subtracting. That’s the Class-11 standard method.)

A short verse:

Sum the first n — the n^2 pattern shows; Sum the squares — a cubic surprise grows. Sum the cubes — the linear sum, squared; And linearity splits any combined.

title: Ch 8b — Series and Special Sums
text:
Apply

Worked example

Find S = \sum_{k=1}^{n} (2k - 1)^2 in closed form.

How a competent solver thinks:

Expand: (2k - 1)^2 = 4k^2 - 4k + 1.
Sum: 4 \sum k^2 - 4 \sum k + \sum 1 = 4 \cdot \dfrac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{6} - 4 \cdot \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} + n.
Common factor n: = n\left[\dfrac{2(n+1)(2n+1)}{3} - 2(n+1) + 1\right] = \dfrac{n}{3}\left[2(n+1)(2n+1) - 6(n+1) + 3\right] = \dfrac{n}{3}\left[(n+1)(4n - 4) + 3\right] = \dfrac{n}{3}\left[4(n+1)(n-1) + 3\right] = \dfrac{n(4n^2 - 1)}{3} = \dfrac{n(2n-1)(2n+1)}{3}.

Answer: \sum_{k=1}^{n}(2k-1)^2 = \dfrac{n(2n-1)(2n+1)}{3}.


Practice — Warm
W1. Find \sum_{k=1}^{15} k.


Full solution

\dfrac{15 \cdot 16}{2} = 120.

W2. Find \sum_{k=1}^{8} k^2.


Full solution

\dfrac{8 \cdot 9 \cdot 17}{6} = 204.

W3. Find \sum_{k=1}^{6} k^3.


Full solution

\left(\dfrac{6 \cdot 7}{2}\right)^2 = 21^2 = 441.



Practice — Working
P1. Find the sum of the first n terms of the series whose k-th term is T_k = k(k+1)(k+2).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Expand: T_k = k^3 + 3k^2 + 2k.



Full solution

\sum k^3 + 3 \sum k^2 + 2 \sum k = \left(\dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}\right)^2 + 3 \cdot \dfrac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{6} +
2 \cdot \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} = \dfrac{n^2(n+1)^2}{4} + \dfrac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{2} + n(n+1) = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{4}\left[n(n+1) + 2(2n+1) + 4\right] = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{4}\left[n^2 + 5n + 6\right] = \dfrac{n(n+1)(n+2)(n+3)}{4}.

P2. Find the sum to n terms of 1 + 3x + 5x^2 + 7x^3 + \cdots.


Hint 1 — a nudge

AGP: a = 1, d = 2, r = x. Multiply by x and subtract.



Full solution

S = 1 + 3x + 5x^2 + \cdots + (2n - 1) x^{n-1}.
xS = \quad x + 3x^2 + \cdots + (2n - 3) x^{n-1} + (2n - 1) x^n.
Subtract: S(1 - x) = 1 + 2x + 2x^2 + \cdots + 2x^{n-1} - (2n-1)x^n = 1 + 2 \cdot \dfrac{x(1 - x^{n-1})}{1 - x} - (2n-1)x^n (GP from the middle).
So S = \dfrac{1}{1-x} + \dfrac{2x(1 - x^{n-1})}{(1-x)^2} - \dfrac{(2n-1)x^n}{1-x} (for x \ne 1).

P3. Find \sum_{k=1}^{20} (3k - 2)^2.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Expand (3k - 2)^2 = 9k^2 - 12k + 4.



Full solution

9 \cdot \dfrac{20 \cdot 21 \cdot 41}{6} - 12 \cdot \dfrac{20 \cdot 21}{2} + 4 \cdot 20 = 9 \cdot 2870 - 12 \cdot 210 + 80 = 25830 - 2520 + 80 = 23390.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Find \sum_{k=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{k(k+1)} in closed form.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Partial fractions: \dfrac{1}{k(k+1)} = \dfrac{1}{k} - \dfrac{1}{k+1}. Telescoping sum.



Full solution

\sum_{k=1}^{n} \left(\dfrac{1}{k} - \dfrac{1}{k+1}\right) =
1 - \dfrac{1}{n+1} = \dfrac{n}{n+1}.
In particular, \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{k(k+1)} = 1.

S2. Find S = 1 + \dfrac{2}{3} + \dfrac{3}{9} + \dfrac{4}{27} + \cdots to infinity.


Hint 1 — a nudge

AGP with a = 1, d = 1, r = 1/3.



Full solution

S = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{k}{3^{k-1}}. Using \sum k x^{k-1} =
1/(1-x)^2 for |x|<1:
S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 1/3)^2} = \dfrac{1}{(2/3)^2} = \dfrac{9}{4}.



Board-Pattern Practice
1-mark (objective). \sum_{k=1}^{10} k equals (a) 45 (b) 50 (c) 55 (d) 60.


Full solution

\dfrac{10 \cdot 11}{2} = 55. Answer: (c).

2-mark (short answer). Find \sum_{k=1}^{n} (2k + 3).


Full solution

2 \sum k + 3n = 2 \cdot \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} + 3n = n^2 + n + 3n = n^2 + 4n
= n(n + 4).

3-mark (short answer II). Find the sum to n terms of the series whose k-th term is T_k = k(2k + 1).


Hint 1 — a nudge

T_k = 2k^2 + k.



Full solution

2 \sum k^2 + \sum k = 2 \cdot \dfrac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{6} + \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} = \dfrac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{3} + \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{6}\left[2(2n+1) + 3\right] = \dfrac{n(n+1)(4n + 5)}{6}.

5-mark (long answer). (i) Sum the AGP 1 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} +
3 \cdot \dfrac{1}{4} + 4 \cdot \dfrac{1}{8} + \cdots to infinity. (ii) Sum the same series to n terms.


Hint 1 — a nudge

T_k = k \cdot (1/2)^{k-1}. Use \sum k x^{k-1} = 1/(1-x)^2.



Full solution

(i) S_\infty = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 1/2)^2} = \dfrac{1}{1/4} = 4.
(ii) S_n = 1 + 2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} + 3 \cdot \dfrac{1}{4} + \cdots
+ n \cdot \dfrac{1}{2^{n-1}}.
\dfrac{1}{2} S_n = \dfrac{1}{2} + 2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{4} + \cdots + (n-1) \cdot \dfrac{1}{2^{n-1}} + n \cdot \dfrac{1}{2^n}.
Subtract: \dfrac{1}{2} S_n = 1 + \dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{1}{4} + \cdots + \dfrac{1}{2^{n-1}} - n \cdot \dfrac{1}{2^n} = \dfrac{1 - (1/2)^n}{1 - 1/2} - \dfrac{n}{2^n} = 2 - \dfrac{2}{2^n} - \dfrac{n}{2^n} = 2 - \dfrac{n + 2}{2^n}.
So S_n = 4 - \dfrac{n + 2}{2^{n-1}}.
Check: as n \to \infty, S_n \to 4. ✓







NCERT Exemplar pointer: Exemplar Ch 8 LA “AGP sum to n terms” matches the 5-mark item. JEE pointer: AGP and telescoping sums (Stretch S1) appear in JEE Main every cycle.





Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

Three closed forms — \sum k = n(n+1)/2, \sum k^2 = n(n+1)(2n+1)/6, \sum k^3 = [n(n+1)/2]^2 — and almost every polynomial \sum is a one-line linear combination of them. Memorise the three; let linearity do the rest.
For an arithmetic-geometric series (AP \times GP), multiply by the common ratio r and subtract. What was an AGP becomes a plain GP plus a single boundary term. There is no “AGP formula” worth memorising — there is only the trick.
For \sum 1/[k(k+1)] and its cousins, partial-fractions makes the sum telescope — almost every term cancels and only the boundary survives. This is the cheapest trick in series and gives the cleanest infinite sums (here, exactly 1).



Common confusions

“\sum k^2 = (\sum k)^2.” No — that’s only true for cubes. Squares give a different polynomial in n.
“\sum k^4 equals \dfrac{n(n+1)(2n+1)(3n^2 + 3n - 1)}{30}? Memorise it.” You don’t need this for boards. But know it exists, and that the pattern continues to higher powers.
“AGP is just AP × GP.” It is the term-by-term product. There’s no “AGP sum formula” you can plug into; always derive by multiply-and-subtract.



Self-Assessment
Be honest with yourself.

Could you write the three special-sum formulas from memory?
Did you solve at least 5 of the 6 Discover problems and 5 of the 7 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?
Can you handle a \sum involving a polynomial in k by linearity?


3 yes: move on to Straight Lines.
2 yes: redo Stretch S2 and the 5-mark item.
0–1 yes: drill polynomial sums until linearity is automatic.

title: Ch 8b — Series and Special Sums
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

Sequences (AP and GP) — AP and GP sum formulas.



What this unlocks

Limits — \lim_{n \to \infty}
\dfrac{\text{sum}}{\text{closed form}} uses these formulas.
Class 12 — Definite Integrals — Riemann sums use \sum k and \sum k^2 to derive integrals from first principles.



NCERT chapter alignment

Class 11, Chapter 8 — Sequences and Series (NCERT 2023 rationalised). Section 8.5 (special sums) and 8.6 (sum-to-n-terms of AGP and similar mixed series).
Sister synthesis lesson: t3.sequences-series.



Where this matters

Research / Discrete Math. \sum k^p for general p is the seed of Faulhaber’s formula and the Bernoulli numbers, central to zeta-function values and number theory.


AI / ML. The sum-of-squares loss \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 is the most common training objective — the algebra of \sum k^2 informs how it scales with sample size.








NoteAuthor’s notes





Touchstones: 3Blue1Brown (visual proof of \sum k^3 = (\sum k)^2), Russian school (multiply-and-subtract derived from scratch in Discover), Bhāskara (verse on the three sums).

title: Ch 10a — Conic Sections (Circle and Parabola)
text:
Be honest before you begin.

Can you state the distance formula in the plane?
Are you fluent with completing the square on a quadratic?
Can you sketch y = x^2 from memory?
Have you heard the words focus and directrix, even informally?

Three confident yeses — read on.

title: Ch 10a — Conic Sections (Circle and Parabola)
Ch 10a — Conic Sections (Circle and Parabola)
text:
Wonder
Sit with these.

A circle is “all points at distance r from a centre”. Can you turn that sentence into an equation?
A parabola is “all points equidistant from a focus and a directrix”. Why does that lead to a curve, not a single point or a line?
Light arriving parallel to the axis of a parabola, after reflecting, passes through one point. Which?
The equation x^2 + y^2 = 25 — is the centre at the origin? What if you had (x - 3)^2 + (y + 4)^2 = 25 instead?







TipWhat you might notice






is why parabolic dishes (satellite, telescope, headlight) work.
is the standard form vs general form issue — completing the square shifts between them.

title: Ch 10a — Conic Sections (Circle and Parabola)
text:
Discover

Problem 1 — Equation of a circle
Find the equation of the circle with centre (2, -3) and radius 5.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Distance from (x, y) to (2, -3) equals 5.



Full solution

(x - 2)^2 + (y + 3)^2 = 25.
Expanded: x^2 - 4x + 4 + y^2 + 6y + 9 = 25 \Rightarrow x^2 + y^2 - 4x + 6y - 12 = 0.



Problem 2 — General to standard
Find the centre and radius of x^2 + y^2 - 6x + 8y - 11 = 0.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Complete the square in x and y separately.



Full solution

(x^2 - 6x) + (y^2 + 8y) = 11 \Rightarrow (x - 3)^2 - 9 + (y + 4)^2 - 16 = 11 \Rightarrow (x - 3)^2 + (y + 4)^2 = 36.
Centre (3, -4), radius 6.



Problem 3 — Circle through three points
Find the equation of the circle through (0, 0), (4, 0), (0, 6).


Hint 1 — a nudge

General circle: x^2 + y^2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0. Substitute each point.



Full solution

At (0,0): c = 0.
At (4, 0): 16 + 8g = 0 \Rightarrow g = -2.
At (0, 6): 36 + 12f = 0 \Rightarrow f = -3.
Equation: x^2 + y^2 - 4x - 6y = 0. Centre (2, 3), radius \sqrt{4 + 9} = \sqrt{13}.



Problem 4 — Standard parabola
Sketch y^2 = 8x. Find the focus and directrix.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Compare with y^2 = 4px. Then p identifies the focus and directrix.



Full solution

4p = 8 \Rightarrow p = 2. Focus (2, 0), directrix x = -2. Opens to the right.



Problem 5 — Parabola from focus
Find the equation of the parabola with focus (0, 3) and directrix y = -3.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Vertex at the midpoint of “focus to directrix” = (0, 0). Axis along the y-axis.



Full solution

x^2 = 4 \cdot 3 \cdot y = 12y.



Problem 6 — Tangent to a circle
Find the equation of the tangent to x^2 + y^2 = 25 at the point (3, 4).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Tangent to x^2 + y^2 = r^2 at (x_1, y_1) is x x_1 + y y_1 = r^2.



Full solution

3x + 4y = 25.

You have just used the standard circle equation, completing the square, the focus-directrix definition of the parabola, and the tangent formula. Now we name them.

Apollonius names the curves (~225 BCE). In his eight-book treatise Conics, Apollonius of Perga showed that one family of plane sections of a double cone produces all four curves we now call circle, ellipse, parabola, and hyperbola — and he coined those last three names from the Greek words for deficient, applied alongside, and thrown beyond. For nearly two millennia his work was the deepest geometry humans had, and the equations you’ll meet in Name are simply Cartesian translations of his synthetic theorems.


Two definitions, one family. A circle is fixed by one point and a distance; a parabola by one point and one line. Both of today’s equations follow by writing “distance equals distance” and squaring. Once you see the equation is a sentence about distances, every conic problem reduces to two moves: pick the right “distance equals distance” sentence, then complete the square to read off the standard form.

title: Ch 10a — Conic Sections (Circle and Parabola)
text:
Name

Circle. Locus of points at distance r from a fixed centre (h, k).
Standard form:  (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2. 
General form:  x^2 + y^2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0,  with centre (-g, -f) and radius \sqrt{g^2 + f^2 - c} (provided g^2 + f^2 - c > 0).
Tangent at (x_1, y_1) on x^2 + y^2 = r^2: x x_1 + y y_1 = r^2.
Parabola. Locus of points equidistant from a fixed point (focus) and a fixed line (directrix).



Equation
Vertex
Focus
Directrix
Opens




y^2 = 4px
(0, 0)
(p, 0)
x = -p
right (p > 0)


y^2 = -4px
(0, 0)
(-p, 0)
x = p
left


x^2 = 4py
(0, 0)
(0, p)
y = -p
up


x^2 = -4py
(0, 0)
(0, -p)
y = p
down



Latus rectum of y^2 = 4px: chord through focus perpendicular to axis, length |4p|.

A short verse:

Circle: square of distance equals r squared. Parabola: distance to point matches distance to line. Complete the square — the centre slides; Match 4p — and the focus aligns.

title: Ch 10a — Conic Sections (Circle and Parabola)
text:
Apply

Worked example

Find the equation of the circle that passes through (2, 3) and has centre on the line y = x + 1, with radius 5.

How a competent solver thinks:

Centre (h, h + 1) for some h.
Distance from (h, h+1) to (2, 3) equals 5: (h - 2)^2 + (h + 1 - 3)^2 = 25.
(h - 2)^2 + (h - 2)^2 = 25 \Rightarrow 2(h - 2)^2 = 25 \Rightarrow (h-2)^2 = 25/2 \Rightarrow h = 2 \pm 5/\sqrt{2}.
Two circles, with centres (2 + 5/\sqrt{2}, 3 + 5/\sqrt{2}) and (2 - 5/\sqrt{2}, 3 - 5/\sqrt{2}), both of radius 5.

Answer: two valid circles, with the centres above.


Practice — Warm
W1. Equation of the circle centred at origin, radius 3.


Full solution

x^2 + y^2 = 9.

W2. Find the centre and radius of x^2 + y^2 + 4x - 6y + 4 = 0.


Full solution

g = 2, f = -3, c = 4. Centre (-2, 3), radius \sqrt{4 + 9 - 4} = 3.

W3. Focus and directrix of y^2 = 12x.


Full solution

4p = 12 \Rightarrow p = 3. Focus (3, 0), directrix x = -3.



Practice — Working
P1. Find the equation of the circle that has (1, 1) and (5, 7) as the endpoints of a diameter.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Centre = midpoint. Radius = half the diameter length.



Full solution

Centre (3, 4). Radius = \dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{16 + 36} = \sqrt{13}.
Equation: (x - 3)^2 + (y - 4)^2 = 13.

P2. Find the equation of the parabola with vertex at (0, 0), focus on the y-axis, passing through (2, 4).


Hint 1 — a nudge

x^2 = 4py. Plug in (2, 4) to find p.



Full solution

4 = 4p \cdot 4 \Rightarrow 4 = 16p \Rightarrow p = 1/4.
Equation: x^2 = y.

P3. Find the length of the chord of the circle x^2 + y^2 = 25 cut off by the line 3x + 4y - 5 = 0.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Use the perpendicular-from-centre approach: chord length = 2\sqrt{r^2 - d^2}.



Full solution

Distance from origin to line: d = |{-5}|/\sqrt{9 + 16} = 1.
Chord length = 2\sqrt{25 - 1} = 2\sqrt{24} = 4\sqrt{6}.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Find the equation of the circle that passes through the origin and cuts intercepts of a on the x-axis and b on the y-axis.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Three points on the circle: (0,0), (a, 0), (0, b).



Full solution

General: x^2 + y^2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0.
At (0,0): c = 0.
At (a, 0): a^2 + 2ga = 0 \Rightarrow g = -a/2.
At (0, b): b^2 + 2fb = 0 \Rightarrow f = -b/2.
Equation: x^2 + y^2 - ax - by = 0.

S2. A parabola has its vertex at the origin and passes through the point (2, 3). The axis of the parabola is along the x-axis. Find its equation and the coordinates of the focus.


Full solution

y^2 = 4px. At (2, 3): 9 = 8p \Rightarrow p = 9/8.
Equation: y^2 = \dfrac{9}{2} x.
Focus: (p, 0) = (9/8, 0).



Board-Pattern Practice
1-mark (objective). The radius of the circle x^2 + y^2 - 4x + 6y + 4 = 0 is (a) 1 (b) 2 (c) 3 (d) 4.


Full solution

g = -2, f = 3, c = 4. Radius = \sqrt{4 + 9 - 4} = 3. Answer: (c).

2-mark (short answer). Find the focus and directrix of the parabola x^2 = 16y.


Full solution

4p = 16 \Rightarrow p = 4. Focus (0, 4), directrix y = -4.

3-mark (short answer II). Find the equation of the circle through (2, 3) and concentric with x^2 + y^2 - 4x + 6y - 5 = 0.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Concentric: same centre. Find centre, then compute the new radius.



Full solution

Centre of given circle: (2, -3). Distance from (2, -3) to (2, 3): 6.
So new radius = 6. Equation: (x - 2)^2 + (y + 3)^2 = 36, or x^2 + y^2 - 4x + 6y - 23 = 0.

5-mark (long answer). A parabolic dish for a satellite has its cross-section described by y^2 = 8x. (i) Where is the focus? (ii) The dish is 4 m wide at the rim. How deep is the dish? (iii) Where should the receiver be placed?


Hint 1 — a nudge

The receiver goes at the focus. “Width at the rim” gives the chord perpendicular to the axis at the depth.



Full solution

(i) 4p = 8 \Rightarrow p = 2. Focus at (2, 0).
(ii) Width at depth x: y ranges from -\sqrt{8x} to +\sqrt{8x}, so width = 2\sqrt{8x}. Set 2\sqrt{8x} = 4 \Rightarrow
\sqrt{8x} = 2 \Rightarrow 8x = 4 \Rightarrow x = 1/2. Depth = 0.5 m.
(iii) At the focus, (2, 0) — at horizontal distance 2 m from the back of the dish along the axis of symmetry.







NCERT Exemplar pointer: Exemplar Ch 10 LA “parabolic reflector” matches the 5-mark item. JEE pointer: the chord-length-from-distance-to-centre identity (P3) is JEE Main standard.





Common confusions

“x^2 + y^2 = -4 is a circle of radius 2i.” No — it has no real points. The radius squared must be non-negative.
“y^2 = 4x is the same as y = 2\sqrt{x}.” Half-true — the square root keeps only the upper branch. The full parabola has both y > 0 and y < 0 branches.
“The directrix is inside the parabola.” It is outside — on the opposite side from the focus, beyond the vertex.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A circle’s equation is “distance from centre equals r, squared.” Standard form (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2 is just the Pythagorean distance formula rewritten.
Complete the square to find the centre and radius. General form x^2 + y^2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0 collapses to standard form once you square-complete in x and y separately.
A parabola’s equation is “distance to focus equals distance to directrix.” Match y^2 = 4px (or its three rotations) and read off the focus and directrix from p.



Self-Assessment
Be honest with yourself.

Could you derive the standard parabola equation from the focus-directrix definition?
Did you solve at least 5 of the 6 Discover problems and 5 of the 7 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?
Can you complete the square on a general circle equation in under a minute?


3 yes: move on to Conic Sections (Ellipse and Hyperbola).
2 yes: redo Stretch S2 and the 5-mark item.
0–1 yes: drill the four parabola orientations and the centre-radius extraction by completing the square.

title: Ch 10a — Conic Sections (Circle and Parabola)
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

Straight Lines — directrix is a line; chords are line segments.
Class 10 — Quadratic Equations — conic equations are second-degree.



What this unlocks

Conic Sections (Ellipse and Hyperbola) — the other two slices of the cone, with two foci instead of one.



NCERT chapter alignment

Class 11, Chapter 10 — Conic Sections (NCERT 2023 rationalised). Sections 10.1–10.3: circle, parabola — definitions, standard equations, properties.
Sister synthesis lesson: t3.conic-sections.



Where this matters

Game development. Parabolic projectile motion: a thrown object’s path under gravity is a parabola. Characters’ jump trajectories, artillery shells, and bouncing physics use the equation y = -\dfrac{g}{2v^2}x^2 + \dfrac{v_y}{v_x}x.


Research / Engineering. Parabolic mirrors focus parallel rays to a single point — used in solar concentrators, car headlights, satellite dishes. The reflective property is a consequence of the focus-directrix definition.








NoteAuthor’s notes





Touchstones: 3Blue1Brown (the cone-slice intuition for conics), Russian school (Discover problems force the focus-directrix definition before the equation), Bhāskara (verse on completing-the-square shifting the centre).

title: 11 · Formal Methods — Model Checking and Temporal Logic
text:
Have you done T5 L01 — Sets, Logic, Proofs and T6 L05 — Graph Theory?
Recall: propositional logic, predicate logic, finite-state machines.
Have you debugged a concurrent program? (For the motivation.)
crumbs: 11 · Formal Methods — Model Checking and Temporal Logic

title: 11 · Formal Methods — Model Checking and Temporal Logic
crumbs: 11 · Formal Methods — Model Checking and Temporal Logic

title: 11 · Formal Methods — Model Checking and Temporal Logic
text:
Wonder

Linear Temporal Logic (LTL): properties over infinite traces of states.
Computation Tree Logic (CTL): properties branching across all possible executions.
Model checking automates verification by exploring the state space.
crumbs: 11 · Formal Methods — Model Checking and Temporal Logic

title: 11 · Formal Methods — Model Checking and Temporal Logic
text:
Discover

Problem 1
A Kripke structure (S, S_0, R, L):

States S.
Initial states S_0 \subseteq S.
Transition relation R \subseteq S \times S.
Labelling L : S \to 2^{AP} (which atomic propositions hold).

Model a 2-state traffic-light controller (green / red). Add states for 2 directions (NS, EW). Define the safety property “never both red.”


Full solution

States: \{(NS \in \{G, R\}, EW \in \{G, R\})\} — 4 states. Initial: (G, R). Transitions: alternate.
Safety: “never both red” = for every reachable s, \neg
(s.NS = R \wedge s.EW = R).
If we add a “yellow” buffer, can we still preserve this? Yes — design ensures alternation.
Model checking traverses all 4 (or 6) reachable states and verifies.



Problem 2
Linear Temporal Logic (LTL). Formulas over future-time operators:

\mathbf{X} \phi — next state satisfies \phi.
\mathbf{F} \phi — eventually \phi.
\mathbf{G} \phi — globally / always \phi.
\phi \mathbf{U} \psi — \phi until \psi.

Translate: “the request is eventually served, and never lost.”


Full solution

\mathbf{G}(\text{request} \to \mathbf{F}\,\text{served}) \wedge \mathbf{G} \neg \text{lost}.
Or: every request leads-to (◇) a served, AND no lost.
This liveness + safety decomposition (Lamport) is core to specs.



Problem 3
Safety vs liveness.

Safety: “bad never happens.” E.g., \mathbf{G} \neg \text{deadlock}.
Liveness: “good eventually happens.” E.g., \mathbf{G} \mathbf{F} \text{progress}.

Why is the distinction useful?


Full solution

Lamport’s theorem: every property is the conjunction of a safety property and a liveness property.
Safety violations are detected by finite counterexamples (a bad trace prefix). Liveness needs infinite counterexamples (loops).
Model checkers handle these differently: safety via reachability, liveness via fair cycles in the state-transition graph.



Problem 4
Model checking algorithm (LTL).

Convert LTL formula \phi to a Büchi automaton A_{\neg \phi}.
Compute product M \times A_{\neg \phi}.
Check for a fair infinite run accepted by A_{\neg \phi}.

If yes: counterexample trace. If no: M \models \phi.
What’s the complexity?


Full solution

LTL model checking: PSPACE-complete in formula size, polynomial in state space.
For finite-state systems with |S| states and LTL formula of size |\phi|: O(|S| \cdot 2^{|\phi|}).
Tools: SPIN (Promela), NuSMV. Used in protocol verification, hardware design.



Problem 5
State space explosion. A system with n Boolean variables has 2^n states. For n = 100: 2^{100} \approx 10^{30}.
How do model checkers handle this?


Full solution

Symbolic model checking: represent state sets as Binary Decision Diagrams (BDDs). Reachability set is a BDD; image computation is operations on BDDs.
OBDD canonical form often compact for “structured” states. McMillan (1990s) verified hardware with 10^{20} states.
SAT-based: Bounded model checking unrolls to depth k, checks SAT. Fast for finding shallow bugs.
Abstraction: CEGAR (counterexample-guided abstraction refinement) — predicate abstraction tools (BLAST, SLAM). Microsoft’s SLAM verified Windows drivers.


Hilbert wanted a machine that could decide all of mathematics. Two mathematicians killed the dream. In 1900 at the International Congress of Mathematicians in Paris, David Hilbert posed his second problem: prove that the axioms of arithmetic are consistent. By the 1920s this had become Hilbert’s Programme — a plan to formalise all of mathematics into a finite, complete, consistent system whose theorems could be mechanically derived. In 1931, a 25-year-old logician named Kurt Gödel published On Formally Undecidable Propositions of Principia Mathematica and Related Systems, which contained two incompleteness theorems showing the programme was impossible: any sufficiently expressive formal system must contain true statements it cannot prove, and cannot prove its own consistency. Five years later, in 1936, Alan Turing’s On Computable Numbers delivered the computational sequel — the halting problem is undecidable, so no algorithm can verify all programs. Formal methods today live in the gap Gödel and Turing left open: finite-state systems are decidable (model checking), and for infinite-state systems we accept human-guided proof (Coq, Lean, Isabelle). Hilbert’s dream was mathematically dead by 1936; the engineering descendant is alive and verifying microkernels.


The deep idea behind model checking is that a temporal logic formula and a finite automaton are the same object viewed from two sides. You write a spec like \mathbf{G}(\text{req} \to
\mathbf{F}\,\text{served}); the tool compiles it into a Büchi automaton accepting exactly the bad infinite traces; then takes the product with your system and asks “is there a reachable cycle?” A yes is a bug, complete with a counterexample trace you can replay. The shift from testing to verification is the shift from “I tried 10,000 inputs” to “I have proved no input behaves badly” — and that shift is what makes seL4 trustworthy and TLA+ pay for itself at AWS.
crumbs: 11 · Formal Methods — Model Checking and Temporal Logic

title: 11 · Formal Methods — Model Checking and Temporal Logic
text:
Name

Kripke structure M = (S, S_0, R, L).
LTL (Linear Temporal Logic): - \mathbf{X}, \mathbf{F}, \mathbf{G}, \mathbf{U}. - Properties over linear traces.
CTL (Computation Tree Logic): branching-time. Path quantifiers \mathbf{A}, \mathbf{E} + temporal operators.
CTL* = LTL ∪ CTL. More expressive.
Safety = bad finite trace. Liveness = good never-reached. Lamport: every property = safety ∧ liveness.
Model checking: M \models \phi? Algorithmic for finite M + LTL/ CTL.
Büchi automata: finite automata over infinite words; recognise LTL.
Symbolic model checking: BDDs for state set; image computation.
Bounded model checking: SAT-based; finite unrolling.
Tools:

SPIN (LTL on Promela models — protocols).
NuSMV / nuXmv (BDD-based, hardware).
TLA+ (Lamport’s spec language; AWS, Microsoft Azure).
Coq / Lean / Isabelle (interactive theorem provers).
Z3 / CVC5 (SMT solvers — automated).

Theorem proving: machine-checked proofs. CompCert (verified C compiler), seL4 (verified microkernel).


Spec: LTL formula over states; Implementation: finite-state model M; Model check M ⊨ φ — counterexample if not.
crumbs: 11 · Formal Methods — Model Checking and Temporal Logic

title: 11 · Formal Methods — Model Checking and Temporal Logic
text:
Apply

Worked example

Mutual exclusion (Peterson’s algorithm).

Two processes P_0, P_1, each with critical section. Variables: \text{flag}_0, \text{flag}_1, \text{turn}.
Properties:

Mutual exclusion (safety): \mathbf{G} \neg(\text{cs}_0 \wedge \text{cs}_1).
Progress (liveness): if P_0 wants in, eventually gets in: \mathbf{G}(\text{want}_0 \to \mathbf{F}\,\text{cs}_0).

Modelled in Promela / TLA+: SPIN verifies both in milliseconds.
This is the canonical exercise — every formal-methods student does Peterson’s.


Practice — Warm
W1. What does \mathbf{F} \mathbf{G} \phi mean?


Full solution

“Eventually, \phi holds forever.” Stabilisation.

W2. Is \mathbf{G} \mathbf{F} \phi a safety or liveness property?


Full solution

Liveness — “infinitely often \phi.”

W3. What’s the difference between LTL and CTL?


Full solution

LTL: properties of each trace separately. CTL: properties of the tree of executions, with explicit path quantifiers \mathbf{A}, \mathbf{E}.
Example: \mathbf{AG}(\mathbf{EF}\,p) — “always possible to reach p.” Inexpressible in LTL.



Practice — Working
P1. What’s a Büchi automaton and why are they used for LTL?


Full solution

A Büchi automaton accepts an infinite word if it visits an accept state infinitely often.
LTL formula \phi → Büchi automaton A_\phi. Trace satisfies \phi iff accepted by A_\phi.
Composition with the system gives a model-checking algorithm.

P2. Why is TLA+ popular at AWS / Microsoft Azure?


Full solution

TLA+ specs distributed protocols at high level. AWS used it to verify DynamoDB, S3 consistency, EBS replication. Found bugs that testing did not.
Lamport: design, then verify before implementation. Catches subtle race conditions, weakenings, edge cases.
Modern industrial use: TLA+ has saved real money by catching bugs pre-deployment.

P3. Why is CompCert a milestone?


Full solution

Verified C compiler. Coq proof: source program semantics = compiled program semantics, modulo undefined behaviour.
Yang et al. (2011) compared CompCert vs gcc/clang via random testing. gcc/clang had compiler bugs; CompCert had zero.
Used in: critical avionics, automotive software (where compiler bugs can kill).



Practice — Stretch
S1. seL4 — verified operating-system microkernel.


Full solution

seL4 (Klein et al., 2009): formally verified that the C implementation refines an abstract spec. ~10,000 lines of C, hundreds of thousands of lines of Isabelle proofs.
Guarantees: no buffer overflows, no null pointer dereferences, process isolation.
Used in: military, automotive, secure devices.
This is the gold standard for formally verified systems software.

S2. Smart contracts and formal verification.


Full solution

Smart contracts (Solidity, Move): bugs lose millions of dollars (DAO hack, Parity wallet, etc.).
Tools:

Certora Prover: SMT-based, applies to Solidity.
K Framework: semantic specs of EVM.
Move Prover: for Diem (Aptos / Sui).

Specs: invariants, access control, no reentrancy. Industry adoption growing rapidly.



Common confusions

“Tested = correct.” Testing finds bugs but cannot prove their absence. (Dijkstra, 1969.)
“Verified = bug-free.” Verified against the spec. If the spec is wrong, system is wrong.
“Formal methods are too expensive.” True 30 years ago. Today: model checkers run in seconds, theorem provers help write proofs, SMT solvers solve millions of constraints.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A spec in temporal logic plus a finite-state model is enough to prove correctness — model checking decides M \models \phi algorithmically, not by sampling inputs but by exhausting reachable states.
Every property decomposes as safety ∧ liveness (Lamport): safety violations are finite traces (“bad happened”), liveness violations are infinite (“good never happened”), and tools check each via a different graph algorithm.
Formal verification has paid off industrially — TLA+ caught real DynamoDB and S3 bugs at AWS, CompCert eliminated compiler bugs that gcc and clang still have, and seL4 ships with a machine-checked proof of correctness for every line of its kernel.



Self-Assessment

State an LTL formula expressing “every request is eventually served.”
Why is model checking PSPACE-complete in formula size?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems?

(Answer: \mathbf{G}(\text{req} \to \mathbf{F}\,\text{served}).)

3 yes: ready for 12 · Algebraic Number Theory.
2 yes: redo P2 (TLA+).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P5.

title: 04 · Statistical Learning Theory — PAC, VC Dimension
text:
Have you done T5 L13 — Statistical Inference and T6 L08 — Convex Optimization?
Recall: i.i.d. sampling, Hoeffding’s inequality (or any concentration bound).
Comfortable with hypothesis classes (set of functions a model can express)?
crumbs: 04 · Statistical Learning Theory — PAC, VC Dimension

title: 04 · Statistical Learning Theory — PAC, VC Dimension
crumbs: 04 · Statistical Learning Theory — PAC, VC Dimension

title: 04 · Statistical Learning Theory — PAC, VC Dimension
text:
Wonder

What’s the right notion of “learnable”? Not “always perfect” — even a perfect Bayes classifier has irreducible error.
PAC (Probably Approximately Correct): with high probability, error is small.
VC dimension measures “capacity” of a hypothesis class — the largest set of points it can shatter.
crumbs: 04 · Statistical Learning Theory — PAC, VC Dimension

title: 04 · Statistical Learning Theory — PAC, VC Dimension
text:
Discover

Problem 1
A binary classifier h: \mathcal{X} \to \{0, 1\}. Risk R(h) =
P_{(X, Y) \sim D}[h(X) \neq Y]. Empirical risk \hat R_S(h) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i 1[h(X_i) \neq Y_i].
Why does \hat R_S(h) \to R(h) as n \to \infty, for fixed h?


Full solution

Hoeffding’s inequality. For h fixed and n i.i.d. samples, 
P[|\hat R_S(h) - R(h)| \geq \epsilon] \leq 2 e^{-2 n \epsilon^2}.
 So \hat R_S(h) \to R(h) in probability.
But this is for one h. For ML we select h based on data — much harder.



Problem 2
Uniform convergence. Want \sup_{h \in \mathcal{H}} |\hat R_S(h) -
R(h)| \leq \epsilon — not just for one h but all h.
For finite \mathcal{H} with |\mathcal{H}| = M, how large does n need to be?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Union bound over the M hypotheses.



Full solution

By union bound + Hoeffding: 
P\left[\sup_h |\hat R - R| \geq \epsilon\right] \leq 2 M e^{-2 n \epsilon^2}.
 Set \leq \delta: n \geq \frac{1}{2\epsilon^2}\log\frac{2M}{\delta}.
So \log M measures complexity for finite classes.



Problem 3
VC dimension. A set \{x_1, \ldots, x_d\} is shattered by \mathcal{H} if every binary labelling of it is realised by some h
\in \mathcal{H}.
VC(\mathcal{H}) = largest d that can be shattered.
What’s the VC dim of: (a) thresholds on \mathbb{R}? (b) intervals? (c) half-spaces in \mathbb{R}^d?


Full solution


VC = 1. Two points: can’t realise the labels (1, 0) (i.e., left point positive but right not).
VC = 2. Any 2 points can be shattered by an interval. 3 points: can’t realise label “(1, 0, 1)” with one interval.
VC = d + 1 for half-spaces in \mathbb{R}^d. (Affine hyperplane has d + 1 degrees of freedom.)




Problem 4
The fundamental theorem of statistical learning.
For a hypothesis class \mathcal{H} with VC dim d, and n i.i.d. samples, with probability \geq 1 - \delta: 
\sup_{h \in \mathcal{H}} |\hat R_S(h) - R(h)| \leq O\left(\sqrt{\frac{d \log(n/d) + \log(1/\delta)}{n}}\right).

Why does this matter for deep learning?


Full solution

Generalisation depends on capacity, not just parameter count.
Classical: VC dim of a feedforward NN with W weights is O(W \log
W), suggesting need n \gg W. Yet modern NNs with W \gg n generalise — the “deep learning paradox.”
Resolution: implicit regularisation by SGD effectively reduces the hypothesis class. Modern theory uses Rademacher complexity, margin bounds, NTK-based bounds — generalisations of VC.



Problem 5
Bias-variance and uniform convergence. For ERM (empirical risk minimization) — pick \hat h = \arg\min_h \hat R_S(h). Decomposition: 
R(\hat h) = \underbrace{\inf_{h^* \in \mathcal{H}} R(h^*)}_{\text{approximation error}} + \underbrace{R(\hat h) - \inf R(h^*)}_{\text{estimation error}}.

What’s the trade-off?


Full solution

Larger \mathcal{H}: smaller approximation error (richer hypotheses available) but larger estimation error (more to estimate from finite data).
Smaller \mathcal{H}: opposite.
Choosing \mathcal{H} = model selection — heart of practical ML. Cross-validation, regularisation, early stopping all act on this trade- off.


The two papers that founded learning theory came from opposite sides of the iron curtain, thirteen years apart. In 1971, in Moscow, Vladimir Vapnik and Alexey Chervonenkis published “On the uniform convergence of relative frequencies of events to their probabilities” — a slim probability paper introducing the combinatorial quantity that came to bear their initials, VC dimension, and proving the first uniform-convergence law for arbitrary hypothesis classes. The paper was largely ignored in the West for years; Vapnik did not visit the United States until 1990, and the support vector machine (his practical translation of the same theory) only landed in 1992. Then in 1984, at Harvard, Leslie Valiant published “A Theory of the Learnable” in Communications of the ACM — written from a complexity- theorist’s angle, framing learning as polynomial-time computation under a probability-and-confidence guarantee. Valiant called the guarantee Probably Approximately Correct (PAC), won the 2010 Turing Award for it, and gave learning theory the half of its name that wasn’t already Russian. The Blumer-Ehrenfeucht-Haussler-Warmuth paper of 1989 finally bolted the two halves together: a class is PAC learnable iff its VC dimension is finite — the Fundamental Theorem you used in Discover P4.


Generalisation is not about how many parameters you have — it is about how many fundamentally different functions your class can express on finite samples. VC dimension counts that capacity directly: it is the largest set of points your class can label every possible way. Once you fix that count, the rest is plumbing — Hoeffding plus a union bound — and the punchline is that the gap between training and test error shrinks like \sqrt{\text{capacity}/n}, regardless of what the data, the loss, or the algorithm look like.
crumbs: 04 · Statistical Learning Theory — PAC, VC Dimension

title: 04 · Statistical Learning Theory — PAC, VC Dimension
text:
Name

PAC learning. \mathcal{H} is PAC learnable if there exists an algorithm A and polynomial \text{poly}(1/\epsilon, 1/\delta) such that for any distribution D and target h^* \in \mathcal{H}, with probability \geq 1 - \delta, R(A(S)) \leq \epsilon given |S| \geq \text{poly}.
VC dimension d = \text{VC}(\mathcal{H}): largest d such that some d points are shattered.
Sauer-Shelah lemma: if VC dim is d, the number of distinct labellings on n points is \leq O(n^d).
Fundamental theorem: \mathcal{H} is PAC learnable iff \text{VC}(\mathcal{H}) < \infty.
Generalisation bound: 
R(\hat h) \leq \hat R_S(\hat h) + O\left(\sqrt{\frac{d + \log(1/\delta)}{n}}\right).

Rademacher complexity \mathcal{R}_n(\mathcal{H}): a distribution-dependent refinement; tighter modern bounds.


Train error close to true error if n \gg capacity; Capacity = VC dim or Rademacher complexity; Bigger class = more flexibility, more risk.
crumbs: 04 · Statistical Learning Theory — PAC, VC Dimension

title: 04 · Statistical Learning Theory — PAC, VC Dimension
text:
Apply

Worked example

Linear classifiers in \mathbb{R}^d: how much data to ensure ≤ 5% generalisation gap with 95% confidence?

VC dim = d + 1.
Bound: \epsilon \approx \sqrt{(d \log n + \log(1/\delta))/n}. Set \delta = 0.05, solve for n:
For d = 100: need n \approx 10^4 samples for \epsilon = 0.05.
For d = 10^6 (a small NN): would need n astronomical — but in practice, NNs do better. Hence active research on improved bounds.


Practice — Warm
W1. What’s the VC dim of axis-aligned rectangles in \mathbb{R}^2?


Full solution

4 — can shatter 4 points in general position; 5 cannot be shattered.

W2. Can VC dim be infinite?


Full solution

Yes — e.g., all subsets of \mathbb{R} has VC dim \infty. Such classes aren’t PAC learnable.

W3. What does \mathcal{R}_n(\mathcal{H}) \to 0 mean?


Full solution

The class can’t perfectly fit random labels — generalisation possible.



Practice — Working
P1. Compute VC dim of the class of all polynomials of degree \leq k on \mathbb{R} (used as classifiers via sign).


Full solution

k + 1 — by FTA, a degree-k polynomial has \leq k zeros, so sign changes at most k times. Can shatter k + 1 points by sign- flipping.

P2. Why does a neural network with bounded weights have finite Rademacher complexity?


Full solution

The norm bound limits how wildly the function can fit random labels. Specifically: weights bounded by B ⇒ Rademacher complexity bounded by O(B \cdot \prod \text{layer-norms} / \sqrt{n}).
This is the basis of modern margin-based bounds.

P3. Why is “deep learning generalises despite huge capacity” a paradox under VC theory?


Full solution

VC bound: gap \geq \sqrt{d \log n / n} where d \approx # weights. For modern NNs d \gg n, so VC bound is vacuous (≥ 1).
Yet empirically NNs generalise. Active research: implicit bias of SGD, flat minima, neural tangent kernel — refinements of “effective” VC dim.



Practice — Stretch
S1. No-free-lunch theorem. Without prior knowledge, no learning algorithm beats random guessing on average over all distributions.


Full solution

Formally: for any algorithm A, there exist distributions where A performs at chance. Bias (= choice of \mathcal{H}) is necessary.
Implication: every successful ML system encodes inductive bias — convolutions, attention, smoothness priors. There is no “universal” learner.

S2. Online learning and regret.


Full solution

Online learner sees (x_t, y_t) sequentially; predicts then learns. Regret = total loss minus best fixed hypothesis in hindsight.
For finite \mathcal{H}: Hedge / Exponential Weights achieves O(\sqrt{T \log |\mathcal{H}|}) regret. For convex losses on convex sets: gradient descent gets O(\sqrt{T}).
Connects to game theory (no-regret dynamics → Nash) and bandits (exploration vs exploitation).



Common confusions

“VC dim = number of parameters.” Not in general. ReLU NNs: O(W \log W). Some classes have VC ≪ params.
“Low training error ⇒ low generalisation error.” Only with enough data relative to capacity. Overfitting is the gap.
“PAC bounds are tight in practice.” Worst-case; often loose by orders of magnitude. Rademacher and PAC-Bayes bounds are tighter.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

VC dimension measures how many essentially different label-patterns your hypothesis class can produce — it is the capacity number that determines whether training error tracks test error.
A class is PAC learnable iff its VC dimension is finite — that one biconditional is the Fundamental Theorem of Statistical Learning, and it gives you the honest probability guarantee “with confidence 1 - \delta, the gap is at most O(\sqrt{d/n}).”
Big model plus small data has no provable guarantee — so every successful learner (CNNs, transformers, kernels) wins by encoding prior structure that quietly shrinks effective capacity below the parameter count.



Self-Assessment

Define VC dimension and PAC learnability.
State the fundamental theorem of statistical learning.
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems?

(Answer: \mathcal{H} is PAC learnable iff VC(\mathcal{H}) < \infty.)

3 yes: ready for 05 · Manifold Methods.
2 yes: redo P3 (deep-learning paradox).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P4.
crumbs: 04 · Statistical Learning Theory — PAC, VC Dimension

title: 04 · Statistical Learning Theory — PAC, VC Dimension
text:
Connect

What this unlocks

Margin bounds, NTK, double-descent theory.



Where this matters

Mathematical research. Connection to combinatorics (Sauer-Shelah), empirical processes, convex optimisation.
