title: 14 · Lattice-Based Post-Quantum Cryptography
text:
Have you done T5 L03 — Vector Spaces and T5 L04 — Linear Systems and Gaussian Elimination?
Recall: bases of \mathbb{R}^n, modular arithmetic.
Have you encountered the closest vector problem?

title: 14 · Lattice-Based Post-Quantum Cryptography
14 · Lattice-Based Post-Quantum Cryptography
text:
Wonder

Lattices generalise integer points; security comes from “good basis vs bad basis.”
Learning With Errors (LWE): decoding noisy linear equations modulo a prime. Believed quantum-hard.
Ring-LWE: structured variant for efficiency; basis of Kyber, NTRU, FALCON.

title: 14 · Lattice-Based Post-Quantum Cryptography
text:
Discover

Problem 1
A lattice L = \{\sum a_i b_i : a_i \in \mathbb{Z}\} where b_1,
\ldots, b_n form a basis.
Two bases of the same lattice can look very different. Show that \mathbb{Z}^2 has bases \{(1, 0), (0, 1)\} and \{(1, 0), (5, 1)\}.


Full solution

(5, 1) = 5 (1, 0) + 1 (0, 1) — integer combination of standard basis. And (1, 0), (0, 1) recoverable from the other: (0, 1) =
(5, 1) - 5 (1, 0).
Both bases ⇒ the same lattice. But “good” basis (orthogonal) makes CVP easy; “bad” basis (skewed) makes CVP hard.
This basis-quality asymmetry is the heart of lattice crypto.



Problem 2
Closest Vector Problem (CVP). Given basis B and target t, find the closest lattice point.
For orthogonal basis: trivial (round each coordinate).
For skewed basis: NP-hard (in general). But “approx-CVP” with factor > n: efficient (LLL algorithm).
Why does this matter for crypto?


Full solution


Public: bad (skewed) basis B' — anyone can describe the lattice but CVP is hard.
Private: good (near-orthogonal) basis B — CVP becomes easy.

Encryption: encode message as a lattice point + small noise. Recipient uses good basis to round to closest lattice point.
LLL reduction (Lenstra-Lenstra-Lovász, 1982) approximates CVP — a lower bound on attack quality.



Problem 3
Learning With Errors (LWE) (Regev, 2005). Public:

Random A \in \mathbb{Z}_q^{m \times n}.
b = As + e \mod q where s \in \mathbb{Z}_q^n secret, e \in
\mathbb{Z}_q^m small noise.

Goal: recover s.
Without noise: solvable (linear algebra). With small noise: believed hard.
What’s the connection to lattices?


Full solution

As + e \approx b — finding s ≈ solving CVP on a specific lattice.
Regev’s theorem: breaking LWE on average ⇔ solving lattice problems (GapSVP, SIVP) in worst case.
Worst-case → average-case reduction is unique to lattices among crypto candidates. Strong security guarantee.
Believed: LWE is quantum-hard. Best known quantum algorithms still exponential.



Problem 4
Ring-LWE. Replace \mathbb{Z}_q^n with the polynomial ring \mathbb{Z}_q[x]/(x^n + 1).
Why is this faster than vanilla LWE?


Full solution

LWE: matrix A has m \cdot n entries; ciphertext is O(n).
Ring-LWE: A a single polynomial; multiplication via NTT (Number Theoretic Transform — analogue of FFT). O(n \log n) vs O(n^2).
Trade-off: structured assumption (Ring-LWE) is stronger than LWE (more attack surface) — but most attacks haven’t found significantly better algorithms.
Module-LWE (Kyber): a balance of efficiency and security.



Problem 5
Why post-quantum? Shor’s algorithm (1994) factors integers and solves discrete log on a quantum computer in polynomial time.
⇒ RSA broken, ECC broken, Diffie-Hellman broken (when large quantum computers exist).
Lattice problems: no known polynomial-time quantum algorithm.
What’s the NIST PQC standardization outcome?


Full solution

NIST PQC competition (2016–2024):

CRYSTALS-Kyber (Module-LWE) — primary KEM. FIPS 203, 2024.
CRYSTALS-Dilithium (Module-SIS / Module-LWE) — primary signature. FIPS 204, 2024.
FALCON (NTRU lattice) — alternate signature. Faster but more complex.
SPHINCS+ (hash-based) — backup signature, not lattice-based.

Web is migrating (Cloudflare, Google, Apple are deploying hybrid classical+PQC TLS).


The two papers that bracket post-quantum crypto. In 1994, Peter Shor — then at Bell Labs — published Algorithms for Quantum Computation: Discrete Logarithms and Factoring, a paper that showed a sufficiently large quantum computer could factor integers and break discrete-log in polynomial time, instantly threatening RSA, Diffie-Hellman, and ECC the moment such a machine existed. Two years later, in 1996, a young Hungarian mathematician named Miklós Ajtai quietly published Generating Hard Instances of Lattice Problems — which proved a previously unimaginable result: breaking lattice problems on average is at least as hard as solving them in the worst case. No other crypto candidate has anything like this guarantee. Oded Regev’s 2005 paper on Learning With Errors (LWE) then made the average-case problem look like noisy linear algebra, suitable for actual schemes. NIST’s Post-Quantum Cryptography competition (2016-2024) finished by standardising CRYSTALS-Kyber (FIPS 203) and CRYSTALS-Dilithium (FIPS 204) in 2024 — both lattice-based, both descendants of Ajtai’s worst-case-to-average-case reduction from 1996. The “harvest now, decrypt later” threat means every TLS connection today should already be migrating: adversaries are storing encrypted traffic in the bet that quantum will arrive sometime in the next decade.


Lattice crypto runs on a single asymmetry: a “good” basis makes finding the closest lattice point easy, a “bad” basis makes it hopeless — even though both bases describe the same lattice. The public key is a deliberately ugly basis; the private key is an orthogonal-ish one only you know. Add small noise (LWE) to a linear system and recovery becomes a CVP-style problem, and Regev’s worst-case-to-average-case reduction promises that every random LWE instance is at least as hard as the worst lattice problem in that dimension. This is the only crypto family with that kind of guarantee — and it’s why the post-quantum standards bet humanity’s encrypted future on geometry.

title: 14 · Lattice-Based Post-Quantum Cryptography
text:
Name

Lattice L \subset \mathbb{R}^n: discrete subgroup of full rank. L = B \mathbb{Z}^n for basis B.
Hard problems:

SVP (Shortest Vector): find shortest non-zero vector. NP-hard (Ajtai 1998). \gamma-approx-SVP for poly \gamma: also believed hard.
CVP (Closest Vector): given target, find closest lattice point.
LWE (Learning With Errors): decode b = As + e \mod q, e small.
Ring-LWE / Module-LWE: structured.

Regev’s worst-case to average-case reduction: breaking LWE → solving worst-case lattice problems.
LLL algorithm: poly-time approx-SVP factor 2^{n/2}. Foundational.
Schemes:

NTRU (1996, Hoffstein-Pipher-Silverman). Trapdoor lattice.
GGH (1997). Broken (1999).
Regev’s LWE encryption (2005).
CRYSTALS-Kyber — NIST FIPS 203.
CRYSTALS-Dilithium — NIST FIPS 204.
FALCON, NTRU.
Fully Homomorphic Encryption (Gentry 2009): based on lattice assumptions.

Post-quantum: secure against quantum computers (no known polytime quantum algorithm for lattice problems).


Lattice = integer combinations of basis vectors; Good basis solves CVP, bad basis doesn’t; LWE / Ring-LWE: post-quantum hard, even quantum.

title: 14 · Lattice-Based Post-Quantum Cryptography
text:
Apply

Worked example

Kyber-512 KEM. Module-LWE with n = 256, k = 2, modulus q = 3329.

Key gen:

Sample A \in R_q^{k \times k} where R_q = \mathbb{Z}_q[x]/(x^{256} + 1).
Sample small s, e \in R_q^k (small coefficients).
Public key: (A, t = As + e).

Encapsulate:

Sample small r, e_1, e_2.
u = A^T r + e_1.
v = t^T r + e_2 + \lfloor q/2 \rfloor m (encode message m).

Decapsulate: v - s^T u ≈ \lfloor q/2 \rfloor m + \text{noise} → round to recover m.
Security: ~128 quantum bits at small public-key size (~800 bytes).


Practice — Warm
W1. Is \mathbb{Z}^n \subset \mathbb{R}^n a lattice?


Full solution

Yes — the standard integer lattice. Basis: standard basis vectors.

W2. What does LLL produce?


Full solution

A “reduced” basis — short, near-orthogonal. Good enough for many applications, including some crypto attacks.

W3. What’s the role of noise in LWE?


Full solution

Noise prevents linear-algebra recovery; turns a linear system into a hard decoding problem.



Practice — Working
P1. Why is NTRU older than LWE but still relevant?


Full solution

NTRU (1996) predates LWE (2005). Cryptosystem in \mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^n - 1).
Doesn’t have Regev’s worst-case reduction — security relies on specific hardness assumption. But: practical, fast, broadly studied.
NTRU and Falcon (lattice signatures) use NTRU lattices. Kyber is Module-LWE — distinct lineage.

P2. What’s Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE)?


Full solution

Operations on ciphertexts → ciphertexts of computed result.
\text{Enc}(a) + \text{Enc}(b) = \text{Enc}(a + b) (additive homomorphic).
FHE: arbitrary circuits. Enables computing on encrypted data without seeing it.
Gentry (2009): first construction. Modern: BGV, BFV, CKKS, TFHE — all based on (Ring)-LWE.
Applications: privacy-preserving ML inference, secure cloud computing. Slow today (1000s× overhead), but improving.

P3. Why is constant-time implementation essential for lattice crypto?


Full solution

Branching on secret data leaks info via timing side-channels.
Many proposed schemes break in practice due to timing leaks (e.g., the GGH scheme).
Constant-time NTT, modular reduction, sampling — all required. Implemented in Kyber and Dilithium reference code.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Why is LLL one of the most consequential algorithms?


Full solution

Lenstra-Lenstra-Lovász (1982): polynomial-time approx-SVP, factor 2^{n/2}.
Applications: factoring polynomials over \mathbb{Q}, knapsack cryptanalysis, Coppersmith’s method (RSA attacks), integer relation finding (PSLQ).
LLL turns “give up — too many vectors” into “search efficiently in the reduced lattice.” Central to algorithmic number theory.

S2. The post-quantum migration timeline.


Full solution


2024: NIST FIPS 203/204/205 finalised. Major TLS libraries (Google, Cloudflare, Apple) deploy hybrid classical+PQC.
2030 (target): US federal CNSA 2.0 deadline for PQC.
“Harvest now, decrypt later”: adversaries collecting encrypted traffic now to decrypt when quantum computers arrive. Hence: migrate before quantum is real.
Quantum estimate: breaking RSA-2048 needs ~20M physical qubits (Gidney-Ekerå 2021). Current systems: ~1000 qubits.
Window: maybe a decade. Migrate now.




Common confusions

“Lattice crypto = ECC for quantum era.” Different math. ECC uses elliptic-curve groups; lattice crypto uses geometric / algebraic number-theoretic structures.
“Bigger keys = better security.” True up to a point. PQC keys are larger than ECC (~1KB vs ~32 bytes); engineering trade-off.
“Quantum already breaks crypto.” Not yet — Shor needs many logical qubits. But cryptographic agility now is essential.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A lattice is integer combinations of basis vectors, and a single lattice has many bases — some easy (orthogonal, CVP trivial), some hard (skewed, CVP NP-hard).
LWE reduces “find s from b = As + e \pmod q” to a lattice problem, and Regev’s worst-case-to-average-case reduction means the average instance you receive is as hard as the worst lattice in that dimension — uniquely strong among crypto candidates.
Post-quantum security comes from problems Shor’s algorithm doesn’t crack: lattice problems have no known polynomial-time quantum algorithm, which is why CRYSTALS-Kyber and CRYSTALS-Dilithium are now NIST’s post-quantum cryptography standards.



Self-Assessment

State the LWE problem.
Why are lattice problems believed quantum-hard?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems?

(Answer: given b = As + e \mod q with small e, recover s.)

3 yes: ready for 15 · The Langlands Program.
2 yes: redo P2 (FHE).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P5.

Connect

Prerequisites used

T5 L04 — Linear Systems and Gaussian Elimination
T7 L12 — Algebraic Number Theory (for ring-LWE on \mathcal{O}_K).



What this unlocks

Modern PQC standards; FHE; identity-based crypto.



Where this matters

Encryption. Standard for post-quantum security.


Privacy-preserving computing. FHE enables secure outsourced computation.


National security. Migration to PQC is a major government and industry priority.

title: 01 · Measure Theory and Lebesgue Integration
text:
Have you done T5 L15 — Real Analysis Foundation and T5 L08 — Continuous Distributions?
Comfortable with \epsilon-\delta proofs?
Recall Riemann integration limits (Lesson T4 L08).

title: 01 · Measure Theory and Lebesgue Integration
01 · Measure Theory and Lebesgue Integration
text:
Wonder

Why isn’t the Riemann integral “good enough”?
The set of rationals in [0, 1] has Lebesgue measure 0 — yet is dense. Why?
Lebesgue’s monotone / dominated convergence theorems make limits and integrals interchangeable.

title: 01 · Measure Theory and Lebesgue Integration
text:
Discover

Problem 1
A σ-algebra \mathcal{F} on X is a collection of subsets closed under countable unions, intersections, and complements, containing \emptyset and X.
A measurable space is (X, \mathcal{F}).
A measure \mu: \mathcal{F} \to [0, \infty] satisfies: \mu(\emptyset)
= 0 and σ-additivity: \mu(\bigcup_n A_n) = \sum_n \mu(A_n) for disjoint A_n.
Why σ-additivity (not just finite additivity)?


Full solution

To handle limits properly. Many natural sets (e.g., the rationals, decreasing intersections of intervals) only become well- defined under countable operations.
Example: \mathbb{Q} \cap [0, 1] = \bigcup_n \{q_n\} (countable union). \mu(\mathbb{Q}) = \sum \mu(\{q_n\}) = 0 (each point has Lebesgue measure 0). Without σ-additivity, this wouldn’t follow.



Problem 2
The Lebesgue measure \lambda on \mathbb{R} is the unique translation-invariant measure with \lambda([a, b]) = b - a on intervals.
Compute \lambda(\mathbb{Q}) and \lambda([0, 1] \setminus \mathbb{Q}).


Full solution

\lambda(\mathbb{Q}) = 0 (countable union of singletons, each length 0).
\lambda([0, 1] \setminus \mathbb{Q}) = \lambda([0, 1]) -
\lambda(\mathbb{Q} \cap [0, 1]) = 1 - 0 = 1.
The irrationals in [0, 1] have full measure. “Almost every” real is irrational.
This couldn’t be expressed in Riemann’s framework.



Problem 3
The Lebesgue integral of a non-negative simple function \varphi
= \sum c_i \mathbf{1}_{A_i}: \int \varphi \, d\mu = \sum c_i \mu(A_i).
For general f \ge 0: \int f \, d\mu = \sup\{\int \varphi : \varphi
\le f, \varphi \text{ simple}\}.
Compute \int_{[0, 1]} \mathbf{1}_{\mathbb{Q}}(x) \, d\lambda.


Full solution

\int \mathbf{1}_\mathbb{Q} d\lambda = \lambda(\mathbb{Q} \cap [0, 1])
= 0.
The Riemann integral of \mathbf{1}_\mathbb{Q} does not exist (upper Riemann sums = 1, lower = 0). But the Lebesgue integral does — and equals 0.
Lebesgue handles many more functions than Riemann.



Problem 4
Dominated convergence theorem (DCT). If f_n \to f pointwise and |f_n| \le g for some integrable g, then \int f_n \to \int f.
Why is this useful?


Full solution

Lets you swap limit and integral. Riemann doesn’t always allow this. DCT is the workhorse of advanced analysis.
Sister theorems: Monotone convergence (MCT) for increasing sequences; Fatou’s lemma for general non-negative.
These three theorems — MCT, DCT, Fatou — are what make Lebesgue integration so much more flexible than Riemann.



Problem 5
Probability via measure theory (Kolmogorov, 1933). Replace “sample space” with measurable space (\Omega, \mathcal{F}). Probability P is a measure with P(\Omega) = 1.
Random variables are measurable functions X: \Omega \to \mathbb{R}. Expected value: E[X] = \int_\Omega X \, dP.
Why is this rigour necessary?


Full solution

To handle continuous random variables, infinite sequences, and stochastic processes consistently. Pre-Kolmogorov probability had inconsistent foundations (Bertrand’s paradox, etc.).
After Kolmogorov: probability is just measure theory with the constraint P(\Omega) = 1. All tools of measure theory apply seamlessly.
Stochastic calculus (Tier 7+++ for finance), conditional expectation, martingales — all rest on this foundation.


Lebesgue’s 1902 doctoral thesis Intégrale, longueur, aire (Paris) was, by his own admission, the result of trying to give a rigorous answer to “what is the area under a curve?” — and finding Riemann’s reply inadequate. Within three years, his student Giuseppe Vitali (1905) used the freshly minted axiom of choice to construct a subset of [0, 1] that has no Lebesgue measure at all — pick one representative from each coset of \mathbb{Q} in \mathbb{R} and you get a set whose measure cannot be 0 (the countable translates would still measure 0, contradicting \mu([0, 1]) = 1) and cannot be positive (the translates would have infinite total measure). It was the first warning that on \mathbb{R} “every set” is too much to ask for — Lebesgue’s \sigma-algebra was not a convenience, it was a necessity. Two decades later Banach and Tarski (1924) sharpened the warning into legend: in \mathbb{R}^3, a solid ball can be cut into five non-measurable pieces and reassembled into two solid balls of the original size. The pieces are not measurable, so no volume is preserved — the paradox is a theorem about the axiom of choice, not about geometry.


A measure is the rule “the size of a whole equals the sum of the sizes of disjoint pieces” — promoted from finite to countably infinite. Once you commit to that one rule for all the sets you’ll ever use, integration stops being a fragile limit of rectangles and becomes a robust sum-against-a-measure: every limit you wanted to swap with an integral, you now can — and probability is just the special case where the total is 1.

title: 01 · Measure Theory and Lebesgue Integration
text:
Name

σ-algebra \mathcal{F} on X: closed under countable unions, complements, contains \emptyset, X.
Measure \mu: \mathcal{F} \to [0, \infty]: \mu(\emptyset) = 0, σ-additive on disjoint countable unions.
Lebesgue measure \lambda on \mathbb{R}: translation-invariant, \lambda([a, b]) = b - a.
Measurable function f: X \to \mathbb{R}: preimages of Borel sets are in \mathcal{F}.
Lebesgue integral for non-negative measurable f: \int f \, d\mu = \sup\{\int \varphi \, d\mu : \varphi \le f, \varphi \text{ simple}\}.
For general f: \int f = \int f^+ - \int f^-.
Convergence theorems (when can we swap \lim and \int?):

Monotone convergence: 0 \le f_n \uparrow f \Rightarrow \int f_n
\to \int f.
Dominated convergence: f_n \to f pointwise, |f_n| \le g \in L^1
\Rightarrow \int f_n \to \int f.
Fatou’s lemma: \int \liminf f_n \le \liminf \int f_n for f_n
\ge 0.

L^p spaces: measurable f with \int |f|^p < \infty. Banach spaces. L^2 is a Hilbert space.
Probability axioms (Kolmogorov): (\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P) with P(\Omega) = 1. Random variables = measurable functions.


Measure: assigns sizes to sets in a σ-additive way; Lebesgue integral handles many more functions than Riemann; DCT, MCT, Fatou: limit-integral swaps; Kolmogorov: probability = measure theory with P(Ω) = 1.

title: 01 · Measure Theory and Lebesgue Integration
text:
Apply

Worked example

Compute \int_0^\infty x e^{-x} dx using Lebesgue (or Riemann — both agree here).

Integration by parts: \int x e^{-x} dx = -x e^{-x} - e^{-x} + C. Evaluated 0 to \infty: 0 - (0 - 1) = 1.
(For “nice” functions, Riemann and Lebesgue agree. Lebesgue’s advantage is for pathological cases and for the convergence theorems.)


Practice — Warm
W1. Lebesgue measure of \{1, 2, 3\}?


Full solution

0 (finite, hence countable, set).

W2. Is the Riemann integral of \mathbf{1}_\mathbb{Q} defined?


Full solution

No. Lebesgue integral is.

W3. Probability that X \in \mathbb{Q} for X \sim N(0, 1)?


Full solution

0 (since \lambda(\mathbb{Q}) = 0 and the normal is absolutely continuous w.r.t. Lebesgue).



Practice — Working
P1. Show \int_0^1 \mathbf{1}_{\mathbb{Q}}(x) dx = 0 (Lebesgue).


Full solution

By Discover P3.

P2. Apply DCT to compute \lim_{n \to \infty} \int_0^1 (1 -
x/n)^n dx.


Hint 1 — a nudge

(1 - x/n)^n \to e^{-x} pointwise. Bounded by 1 on [0, 1].



Full solution

\int_0^1 e^{-x} dx = 1 - e^{-1} \approx 0.632.
DCT lets us swap: \lim_n \int = \int \lim_n.

P3. What’s an L^2 function?


Full solution

Measurable f with \int |f|^2 < \infty. Forms a Hilbert space — foundation of Fourier analysis, quantum mechanics.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Why is the Lebesgue integral the “right” generalisation? What does it gain over Riemann?


Full solution

Properties Riemann lacks:

Limit-integral swaps (DCT, MCT, Fatou). Riemann needs uniform convergence, often too strong.
Completeness: L^p spaces are Banach. Riemann-integrable functions form incomplete spaces under L^p norms.
Wide function class: characteristic functions of arbitrary measurable sets, almost-everywhere defined functions.
Probability foundation: Kolmogorov axiomatisation only works with measures.

Modern probability, functional analysis, harmonic analysis — all require Lebesgue.

S2. Connection to ML. Many ML quantities are integrals over high-dimensional spaces. Why is measure theory the right framework?


Full solution

Universal language for “probabilities” and “expectations”. Whether your random variable is discrete, continuous, mixed, or defined on weird spaces (functions, neural network parameter spaces) — measure theory handles all of them uniformly.
Modern ML uses: - Probability measures on weight spaces (Bayesian deep learning). - Wasserstein distance (Tier 7+) for comparing distributions (GANs, optimal transport). - Pushforward measures (how distributions transform under maps).
Without measure theory, deep theoretical work in ML wouldn’t be possible.



Common confusions

“Lebesgue replaces Riemann.” No — Lebesgue generalises. For nice functions (continuous, piecewise smooth), they agree.
“Almost every” is colloquial. It means “except on a set of measure zero” — a precise mathematical concept.
“All sets are measurable.” Not in \mathbb{R} under Lebesgue + axiom of choice (Vitali set). Tier 7+++ explores non-measurable sets.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A measure is just “size adds when you chop into countably many disjoint pieces” — that one σ-additivity axiom rebuilds length, area, volume, and probability on the same footing.
The Lebesgue integral integrates the function by slicing the output axis, not the input — which is why it handles wild functions the Riemann integral chokes on, and why limit-and-integral can finally be swapped (DCT, MCT, Fatou).
Kolmogorov’s axiomatisation says probability is measure theory with P(\Omega) = 1 — random variables are measurable functions, expectations are integrals, and every modern result in stochastic processes lives downstream of that one identification.



Self-Assessment

Could you state σ-additivity?
Without scrolling back, what does DCT say?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: limit and integral can be swapped under domination.)

3 yes: ready for 02 · Topology.
2 yes: redo S1 (Lebesgue’s advantages).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P5.

title: 01 · Measure Theory and Lebesgue Integration
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

T5 L15 — Real Analysis Foundation
T5 L08 — Continuous Distributions



What this unlocks

Functional analysis on L^p spaces.
Stochastic processes, martingales.
Geometric measure theory.



Where this matters

Mathematical research. The language of modern analysis, probability, and ergodic theory.


AI / Machine Learning. Generative models (S2), Bayesian inference, optimal transport (Wasserstein GANs), measure-valued neural networks.


Quantitative finance. Stochastic calculus, martingale theory, risk-neutral pricing — all built on measure-theoretic probability.

title: 08 · Quaternions and 3D Rotations
text:
Have you done T6 L02 — Groups and Symmetry and T5 L02 — Linear Algebra Foundation?
Recall: complex numbers a + bi, matrix multiplication, dot/cross products.
Have you encountered Euler angles or rotation matrices?

title: 08 · Quaternions and 3D Rotations
08 · Quaternions and 3D Rotations
text:
Wonder

Complex numbers a + bi encode 2D rotations. Could there be a 4D number system encoding 3D rotations?
Quaternions \mathbb{H}: a + bi + cj + dk with i^2 = j^2 =
k^2 = ijk = -1.
Multiplication is non-commutative — exactly what’s needed for 3D rotation (which is also non-commutative).

title: 08 · Quaternions and 3D Rotations
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Define \mathbb{H} = \{a + bi + cj + dk : a, b, c, d \in \mathbb{R}\} with i^2 = j^2 = k^2 = ijk = -1.
Derive ij and ji.


Full solution

From ijk = -1 multiply right by k^{-1} = -k: ij = -1 \cdot (-k) =
k. Similarly jk = i, ki = j.
For ji: from ij = k, multiply left by j^{-1} = -j: i = -jk, so ji = -k.
So ij = -ji = k, jk = -kj = i, ki = -ik = j. Anti-commutative on imaginaries — like cross product.
This non-commutativity is what makes \mathbb{H} encode 3D rotations.



Problem 2
For q = a + bi + cj + dk, define conjugate \bar q = a - bi - cj
- dk and norm |q| = \sqrt{q \bar q}.
Show |pq| = |p||q|.


Full solution

|pq|^2 = (pq)(\overline{pq}) = pq \bar q \bar p = p (q \bar q) \bar
p = p |q|^2 \bar p = |q|^2 p \bar p = |q|^2 |p|^2.
(We used \overline{pq} = \bar q \bar p — order reverses!)
So |pq| = |p| |q|. Multiplicativity. (Hence non-zero quaternions form a group under multiplication.)



Problem 3
A unit quaternion has |q| = 1. Set q = \cos(\theta/2) + \sin(\theta/2)(u_1 i + u_2 j + u_3 k) where \hat{u} = (u_1, u_2, u_3) is a unit vector.
Define rotation: for a 3D vector \vec v = v_1 i + v_2 j + v_3 k, 
R_q(\vec v) = q \vec v \bar q.
 Verify this is a rotation by angle \theta around axis \hat u.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Try \hat u = (1, 0, 0), so q = \cos(\theta/2) + \sin(\theta/2) i. Compute q (v_2 j + v_3 k) \bar q — should rotate the jk-plane.



Full solution

Yes — a careful computation shows R_q rotates \vec v by angle \theta around axis \hat u.
Verify: R_q(\hat u) = \hat u (axis is fixed); R_q preserves length; the rotation in the plane orthogonal to \hat u has the right angle.
Note: q and -q give the same rotation. So unit quaternions are a double cover of SO(3): S^3 \to SO(3), kernel \{\pm 1\}.



Problem 4
Composition of rotations. R_{q_2} \circ R_{q_1} = ?


Full solution

R_{q_2}(R_{q_1}(\vec v)) = q_2 (q_1 \vec v \bar q_1) \bar q_2 = (q_2
q_1) \vec v \overline{(q_2 q_1)} = R_{q_2 q_1}(\vec v).
So composition = quaternion product. To combine rotations, just multiply the quaternions.
Compare to rotation matrices: also composition by multiplication, but 9 entries vs 4 — quaternions are more compact and avoid singularities.



Problem 5
SLERP (spherical linear interpolation). For unit quaternions q_0,
q_1, define 
\text{slerp}(q_0, q_1, t) = \frac{\sin((1-t)\Omega)}{\sin\Omega} q_0 + \frac{\sin(t \Omega)}{\sin\Omega} q_1
 where \cos\Omega = q_0 \cdot q_1.
Why is slerp the right interpolation for rotations?


Full solution

Slerp traces a geodesic on S^3 (the unit-quaternion sphere) at constant angular velocity.
Naive linear interpolation ((1-t)q_0 + t q_1) gives non-unit quaternions and uneven angular speed.
Slerp gives:

Smooth motion.
Constant angular velocity.
Shortest path through orientation space.

Used in: every modern animation system, character rigging, IK solvers.


Hamilton spent fifteen years stuck on the same problem before the answer hit him on a walk. Since 1828 William Rowan Hamilton — Astronomer Royal of Ireland, fluent in thirteen languages by his teens, the man who recast classical mechanics as the Hamiltonian formalism — had been trying to extend the multiplication of complex numbers from the plane to space. Every attempt foundered: the triplets a + bi + cj refused to multiply consistently, because there was no way to define ij without breaking either norm or distributivity. On the morning of 16 October 1843, walking with his wife along the Royal Canal in Dublin towards a meeting of the Royal Irish Academy, the missing trick suddenly arrived: give up commutativity, add a fourth dimension. He stopped at Brougham Bridge (now Broom Bridge) and carved i^2 = j^2 = k^2 = ijk = -1 into the stonework with his penknife — the carving has long since worn away, but a plaque now marks the spot. Hamilton spent the rest of his life arguing — wrongly, it turned out — that quaternions would replace vectors in physics. Vectors won the textbook battle in the 1880s, and quaternions languished for nearly a century. Then in April 1970 the Apollo 13 service module suffered an attitude control crisis in which the spacecraft’s gimbal-mounted IMU came within a degree of gimbal lock — a singularity inherent to the three-Euler-angle representation, in which two rotation axes align and one degree of freedom vanishes. Modern flight software, every modern game engine (Unity, Unreal, Godot), every CGI animation pipeline, and the attitude control of every modern satellite stores rotations as quaternions for exactly this reason: S^3 has no singularities, so gimbal lock cannot happen. Hamilton’s dead-end notation became the working representation of three-dimensional rotation in the digital age.


A quaternion is a rotation written so that composition becomes multiplication and there are no singularities — at the price of non-commutativity (which is the right price, because rotations in 3D do not commute either) and a curious double cover: q and -q are the same rotation, so a 360° turn in space corresponds to travelling halfway around the quaternion sphere S^3, and only at 720° do you return to where you started. The half-angle in q = \cos(\theta/2) + \sin(\theta/2)\hat u is not a notational quirk — it is a record of that doubling, the same doubling that physics calls spin and animation calls the reason slerp works.

title: 08 · Quaternions and 3D Rotations
text:
Name

Quaternions \mathbb{H} = \{a + bi + cj + dk\} with i^2 = j^2 =
k^2 = ijk = -1.
Multiplication: non-commutative. ij = k, jk = i, ki = j.
Conjugate \bar q = a - bi - cj - dk. Norm |q|^2 = q\bar q. \overline{pq} = \bar q \bar p.
Inverse of unit q: q^{-1} = \bar q.
Unit quaternions \cong S^3 form a group, double-covering SO(3).
Rotation by \theta around unit axis \hat u: 
q = \cos(\theta/2) + \sin(\theta/2) (u_1 i + u_2 j + u_3 k); \qquad R_q(\vec v) = q \vec v \bar q.

Composition: R_{q_2} \circ R_{q_1} = R_{q_2 q_1}.
SLERP for smooth rotation interpolation. Geodesic on S^3.
Advantages over Euler angles: no gimbal lock. Over rotation matrices: more compact (4 vs 9 numbers); cheaper composition.


Hamilton’s i, j, k satisfy i^2 = j^2 = k^2 = ijk = -1; Unit quaternions \cong S^3 double-cover SO(3); Rotate by q\vec v\bar q, compose by multiplication, interpolate by slerp.

title: 08 · Quaternions and 3D Rotations
text:
Apply

Worked example

Rotate (1, 0, 0) by 90° around the z-axis.

\hat u = (0, 0, 1), \theta = 90°. So 
q = \cos 45° + \sin 45° \cdot k = \frac{\sqrt 2}{2}(1 + k).

\vec v = i, \bar q = \frac{\sqrt 2}{2}(1 - k).
q \vec v = \frac{\sqrt 2}{2}(1 + k) i = \frac{\sqrt 2}{2}(i + ki) =
\frac{\sqrt 2}{2}(i + j).
(q \vec v) \bar q = \frac{\sqrt 2}{2}(i + j) \cdot \frac{\sqrt 2}{2}(1
- k) = \frac{1}{2}(i - ik + j - jk) = \frac{1}{2}(i + j + j - i) = j.
So i \mapsto j — exactly a 90° rotation around z. ✓


Practice — Warm
W1. Compute (1 + i)(1 + j).


Full solution

1 + j + i + ij = 1 + i + j + k.

W2. What’s |2 + i + j + k|?


Full solution

\sqrt{4 + 1 + 1 + 1} = \sqrt{7}.

W3. What rotation does q = i encode?


Full solution

q = i = \cos 90° + \sin 90° \cdot i, so 180° rotation around x-axis.



Practice — Working
P1. Why are quaternions a division ring but not a field?


Full solution

Every non-zero quaternion has an inverse: q^{-1} = \bar q / |q|^2.
But multiplication is non-commutative (ij \neq ji), so \mathbb{H} is not a field. It’s a division ring (or “skew field”).
Frobenius: the only finite-dimensional associative division algebras over \mathbb{R} are \mathbb{R}, \mathbb{C}, \mathbb{H}.

P2. Why is the relation between quaternions and SO(3) a double cover?


Full solution

Both q and -q produce the same rotation (since (-q)\vec v(-\bar q)
= q\vec v \bar q).
Topologically: S^3 (unit quaternions) is simply connected; SO(3) is not. The map q \mapsto R_q is 2-to-1.
Consequence: a 360° rotation in SO(3) corresponds to going from 1 to -1 in S^3 — not back to identity. 720° gets back. (“Belt trick” / “Dirac string”.)

P3. Why does the NLERP (normalised linear interpolation) often substitute for slerp in games?


Full solution

NLERP: ((1-t)q_0 + t q_1)/|...| — linear then renormalise.
Faster than slerp (no trig). Doesn’t have constant angular velocity, but the visual difference is tiny for small angles.
Trade-off: speed vs angular-velocity smoothness. Most games use NLERP when frame-rate matters more than physical correctness.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Octonions \mathbb{O}: 8-dim, non-associative.


Full solution

After \mathbb{R}, \mathbb{C}, \mathbb{H}, the octonions \mathbb{O} are 8-dim with multiplicative norm — but non-associative: (ab)c \neq
a(bc) in general.
Hurwitz: only normed division algebras are \mathbb{R}, \mathbb{C},
\mathbb{H}, \mathbb{O} (dimensions 1, 2, 4, 8).
Used in: exceptional Lie groups, string theory, certain finite simple groups.

S2. Dual quaternions for rigid-body motion (rotation + translation).


Full solution

Dual quaternion: \hat q = q + \epsilon p where \epsilon^2 = 0.
Encodes both rotation (q) and translation (p) compactly. Composition is just dual-quaternion multiplication. Interpolation: “DLB” (dual quaternion linear blending) for character skinning.
Used in robotics, character animation. Avoids “candy wrapper” artefacts of separate rotation + translation interpolation.



Common confusions

“Quaternions are 4D rotations.” No — they encode 3D rotations (using 4 numbers). Genuine 4D rotations need a different setup.
“q\vec v \bar q vs \bar q \vec v q.” Both rotate, but in opposite directions (one is the inverse of the other). Pick a convention and stick to it.
“Half-angle is a hack.” It’s deep — ties to the double cover S^3 \to SO(3). The half is necessary.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A quaternion is four real numbers a + bi + cj + dk obeying i^2 = j^2 = k^2 = ijk = -1 — and the consequent non-commutativity (ij = -ji = k) is exactly what is needed to encode the non-commutativity of 3D rotation.
Unit quaternions form S^3 and double-cover the rotation group SO(3) — both q and -q act the same way via \vec v \mapsto q\vec v\bar q, so a full 360° rotation lands on -1, and only 720° gets you home (the belt trick).
Composition becomes multiplication and interpolation becomes slerp — quaternions store a rotation in 4 numbers instead of the 9 entries of a matrix, avoid the gimbal-lock singularities of Euler angles, and slide along geodesics of S^3 when you blend two orientations smoothly.



Self-Assessment

Define quaternion multiplication (ij = ?, ji = ?).
Why are unit quaternions a double cover of SO(3)?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems?

(Answer: ij = k, ji = -k. \pm q map to same rotation.)

3 yes: ready for 09 · Relational Algebra.
2 yes: redo P2 (double cover).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P5.

title: 00 · Introduction
text:
This is the summit. Tier 7 takes the foundations built across six tiers and applies them to mathematical research, modern AI, graphics, databases, systems, and cryptography — finishing with a glimpse of the deepest unification programme in mathematics.
Each lesson is a doorway: not exhaustive, but a faithful introduction to a graduate-level subject. The goal is to equip you to keep learning in any of these directions.
By the end of Tier 7 you can:

Read measure theory and topology at a graduate level.
Understand the structure of Galois theory.
Reason about statistical learning (PAC, VC), manifolds in ML, and reinforcement learning theory.
Work with differential geometry, quaternions, and modern 3D graphics structures.
Reason about relational algebra and database query optimisation.
Analyse system performance via queueing theory and verify correctness via formal methods.
Understand modern cryptography: algebraic number theory, elliptic curves, and lattice-based post-quantum schemes.
Glimpse the Langlands Program — one of the great unifications of mathematics.

title: 13 · Elliptic Curves and ECC
text:
Have you done T6 L03 — Rings and Fields?
Recall: groups, finite fields \mathbb{F}_p, modular arithmetic.
Have you encountered Diffie-Hellman or RSA?

title: 13 · Elliptic Curves and ECC
13 · Elliptic Curves and ECC
text:
Wonder

Over \mathbb{Q} or \mathbb{R}: continuous group of points.
Over \mathbb{F}_p (finite field): finite group; size \approx p.
Discrete log on this group is hard — basis of ECC.

title: 13 · Elliptic Curves and ECC
text:
Discover

Problem 1
An elliptic curve over a field K: E: y^2 = x^3 + ax + b with discriminant \Delta = -16(4 a^3 + 27 b^2) \neq 0 (smooth).
Add a point at infinity \mathcal{O}. Define addition P + Q:

Draw the chord from P to Q (or tangent if P = Q).
It meets E in a third point R.
P + Q := -R (reflection over x-axis).

Show \mathcal{O} is the identity.


Full solution

The vertical line through P “meets infinity” at \mathcal{O}, then the third meeting point of the line P\mathcal{O} extended is the reflection of P. So P + \mathcal{O} = P.
This is the chord-tangent group law.



Problem 2
For E: y^2 = x^3 + 7 over \mathbb{F}_{11}, compute all points.


Full solution

For each x \in \{0, 1, \ldots, 10\}, compute x^3 + 7 \mod 11. Determine if it’s a quadratic residue (a square mod 11):



x
x^3 + 7
square?
y




0
7
no
—


1
8
no
—


2
4
yes
2, 9


3
1
yes
1, 10


4
5
yes
4, 7


…






Plus point at infinity \mathcal{O}. Group order ≈ 12 (small example).
For real ECC: p \approx 2^{256}.



Problem 3
Hasse’s theorem. For E over \mathbb{F}_q: 
|\#E(\mathbb{F}_q) - (q + 1)| \leq 2\sqrt{q}.
 So |E(\mathbb{F}_q)| \approx q.
Why does this matter for cryptography?


Full solution

Crypto wants a group with \sim q elements. Hasse guarantees the size is in a narrow window around q + 1.
For p \approx 2^{256}: |E(\mathbb{F}_p)| \approx 2^{256} — large enough that brute-force discrete log is infeasible.
Pick a curve where the order is prime (so subgroups are trivial) → secure.



Problem 4
Discrete log on E(\mathbb{F}_p). Given P, Q \in E with Q = nP, find n.
Best known classical: Pollard’s rho — O(\sqrt{p}).
For p \approx 2^{256}: 2^{128} operations — infeasible.
Why is this better than \mathbb{Z}_p^* discrete log?


Full solution

For \mathbb{Z}_p^*: index calculus gives sub-exponential algorithms (NFS-like).
For elliptic curves: no known sub-exponential algorithm. ECDLP appears genuinely O(\sqrt{p}).
Hence: 256-bit ECC ≈ 3072-bit RSA in security. Smaller keys, less bandwidth, less power. Used in TLS 1.3, signal protocol, mobile crypto.



Problem 5
Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman (ECDH). Public: curve E, point G of large prime order.

Alice: secret a, public A = aG.
Bob: secret b, public B = bG.
Shared key: aB = abG = bA.

Why is the eavesdropper stuck?


Full solution

Eavesdropper sees G, A = aG, B = bG. To compute abG, needs to find a (or b) — that’s discrete log.
If ECDLP is hard (O(\sqrt{p})), eavesdropper is stuck.
This is CDH (computational Diffie-Hellman) on elliptic curves.
ECDSA, ECDH, ed25519: all variants. Ubiquitous in TLS, SSH, crypto wallets.


The 350-year-old problem that fell to elliptic curves. In 1922 Louis Mordell proved that the rational points on an elliptic curve over \mathbb{Q} form a finitely generated abelian group — the Mordell-Weil theorem, later extended by André Weil to number fields. For seventy years this lived as beautiful pure mathematics. Then in 1985 Gerhard Frey noticed that any hypothetical counterexample to Fermat’s Last Theorem, a^p + b^p = c^p, would yield an elliptic curve y^2 = x(x - a^p)(x + b^p) with such weird arithmetic properties that it could not possibly be modular. Ken Ribet made this rigorous in 1990, and on 23 June 1993, at a Cambridge conference, Andrew Wiles announced he had proved the Modularity Theorem for semistable elliptic curves — therefore Fermat. A gap was found in the proof that summer; Wiles spent another year patching it with Richard Taylor, and the corrected proof was published in 1995. The same elliptic-curve group law that the chord-and-tangent widget shows you also factors integers (Hendrik Lenstra, 1987) and signs every Bitcoin transaction (secp256k1, 2009) — three completely different applications of one y^2 = x^3 + ax + b.


The chord-and-tangent picture is doing something genuinely magical: a geometric construction (draw a line, find the third intersection, reflect) is silently defining an abelian group — associativity and all — on a one-dimensional curve. Crypto exploits this twice over: the group has \sim p elements (Hasse), and discrete log on this group is harder than discrete log on \mathbb{Z}_p^* because no index-calculus attack is known. That hardness gap — no sub-exponential algorithm — is the entire reason a 256-bit ECC key gives you the same security as a 3072-bit RSA key.

title: 13 · Elliptic Curves and ECC
text:
Name

Elliptic curve E: y^2 = x^3 + ax + b, \Delta \neq 0.
Group law: chord-and-tangent. Identity \mathcal{O} at infinity.
Hasse’s theorem: |\#E(\mathbb{F}_q) - (q+1)| \leq 2\sqrt{q}.
E(\mathbb{Q}) — Mordell’s theorem: finitely generated abelian group. E(\mathbb{Q}) \cong T \oplus \mathbb{Z}^r where T is torsion and r is rank.
BSD conjecture (Birch-Swinnerton-Dyer, \$1M Clay prize): rank of E over \mathbb{Q} equals order of vanishing of L-function L(E, s) at s = 1.
ECDLP: given P, Q with Q = nP, find n. Believed O(\sqrt{p}).
ECC schemes:

ECDH: key exchange.
ECDSA: signatures.
EdDSA / ed25519: modern signatures (Curve25519).
secp256k1: Bitcoin and Ethereum.

Pairing-based crypto: Weil / Tate pairings on elliptic curves enable identity-based encryption, BLS signatures.
Modularity theorem: every elliptic curve over \mathbb{Q} is modular (Wiles, Breuil-Conrad-Diamond-Taylor) — bridge to FLT and Langlands (T7 L15).


Elliptic curve = smooth y² = x³ + ax + b; Chord-and-tangent makes points a group; ECDLP is hard ⇒ ECC keys are small.

title: 13 · Elliptic Curves and ECC
text:
Apply

Worked example

Bitcoin’s secp256k1 signing.

Curve: y^2 = x^3 + 7 over \mathbb{F}_p for p = 2^{256} - 2^{32} - 977.
Generator G, order n \approx 2^{256}.
Private key: d \in \{1, \ldots, n-1\}. Public key: Q = dG.
To sign message m: pick random k, compute kG = (x_1, y_1), r =
x_1 \mod n, s = k^{-1}(H(m) + dr) \mod n. Signature: (r, s).
Verification: compute u_1 = H(m) s^{-1}, u_2 = r s^{-1}, u_1 G + u_2
Q = (x_1', \cdot), check x_1' = r.
This is ECDSA, used by every Bitcoin transaction.


Practice — Warm
W1. Why does \Delta = 0 disqualify a curve?


Full solution

The curve has a singular point — chord-tangent breaks down.

W2. Is the chord-tangent law commutative?


Full solution

Yes — the chord through P, Q doesn’t depend on order.

W3. Why does ECDH need the base point G to have prime order?


Full solution

Composite order → small subgroups → Pohlig-Hellman attack reduces ECDLP to smaller groups via CRT.
Prime order: no proper subgroups, no shortcut.



Practice — Working
P1. What’s the Lenstra factorization algorithm?


Full solution

Use elliptic curves over \mathbb{Z}/N to factor N.
Pick random curve mod N. Try to compute kP for large k. If group arithmetic fails (some inversion impossible mod N), gcd of the denominator with N gives a factor.
Sub-exponential. Used in factoring with small factors. (For balanced RSA: NFS is faster.)

P2. Why are pairings revolutionary?


Full solution

A pairing e: E \times E \to \mathbb{F}_q^* that is bilinear: e(aP,
bQ) = e(P, Q)^{ab}.
Enables: identity-based encryption (Boneh-Franklin 2001), short signatures (BLS), zk-SNARKs.
Pairing-friendly curves are specially constructed.

P3. Why is Curve25519 preferred for modern systems?


Full solution


Constant-time arithmetic by design (no timing side-channels).
128-bit security at 256-bit keys.
No magic constants (unlike NIST P-256).
Fast scalar multiplication.

Used in: TLS 1.3, Signal, WireGuard, OpenSSH, Tor.
Designer: Daniel J. Bernstein. Adopted broadly because of clear security properties + simple implementation.



Practice — Stretch
S1. The Modularity theorem and FLT.


Full solution

Every elliptic curve E over \mathbb{Q} corresponds to a modular form f_E.
Frey-Ribet (1980s): if FLT had a counterexample a^p + b^p = c^p, the curve y^2 = x(x - a^p)(x + b^p) would be non-modular.
So Modularity → FLT.
Wiles (+Taylor, 1995): proved Modularity for the relevant class. FLT falls.
This is the most famous theorem of the 20th century in pure math.

S2. BSD conjecture — Clay $1M problem.


Full solution

For E over \mathbb{Q}, define L-function L(E, s) from local factors. Conjecture:

\text{rank}(E(\mathbb{Q})) = \text{ord}_{s=1} L(E, s).

I.e., # independent rational points = order of vanishing of L at s = 1.
Proved: rank-0, rank-1 cases (Kolyvagin, Gross-Zagier). Rank ≥ 2: open.
This is the example of the deep arithmetic-analytic connection that Langlands generalises (T7 L15).



Common confusions

“Elliptic = ellipse-shaped.” No — name from elliptic integrals. Visually: a curve with a hole or a ring.
“All elliptic curves over \mathbb{F}_p are secure.” Some have small group order, some have efficient pairing reductions — choose carefully (NIST or Curve25519, not random).
“ECC is post-quantum.” No — Shor’s algorithm breaks ECDLP too. ECC is broken by quantum computers (T7 L14: lattice crypto for PQ).



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

An elliptic curve y^2 = x^3 + ax + b (with \Delta \ne 0) carries a group law built from chords and tangents — and Mordell’s theorem says the rational points form a finitely generated abelian group.
ECDLP is harder than \mathbb{Z}_p^* DLP because no sub-exponential algorithm (no index calculus) is known on elliptic-curve groups, which is why 256-bit ECC matches 3072-bit RSA for security.
The same curves that secure TLS, Bitcoin, and Signal also anchor Wiles’s proof of Fermat’s Last Theorem — modularity says every elliptic curve over \mathbb{Q} corresponds to a modular form, the first major case of the Langlands program.



Self-Assessment

State the chord-tangent group law informally.
Why is ECDLP harder than \mathbb{Z}_p^* DLP?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems?

(Answer: chord meets curve in third point; sum is its reflection.)

3 yes: ready for 14 · Lattice Crypto.
2 yes: redo P3 (Curve25519).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P5.

title: 10 · Queueing Theory and Performance Modelling

Have you done T5 L12 — Continuous Distributions?

title: 10 · Queueing Theory and Performance Modelling
10 · Queueing Theory and Performance Modelling
text:
Wonder

Little’s Law L = \lambda W: items in system = arrival rate × average time spent.
Pollaczek-Khinchine + variants: explicit formulas for various queueing disciplines.
Why utilization > 80% is a danger zone.

title: 10 · Queueing Theory and Performance Modelling
text:
Discover

Problem 1
A Poisson arrival process with rate \lambda: inter-arrival times \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda), i.i.d.
If arrivals are Poisson(\lambda), what’s P(N(t) = n)?


Full solution

P(N(t) = n) = \frac{(\lambda t)^n}{n!} e^{-\lambda t}.
Memoryless property of exponential: P(\text{wait} > s + t \mid \text{wait} > s) = P(\text{wait} > t).
Crucial: this enables Markovian analysis of queues.



Problem 2
M/M/1 queue: Markovian arrivals (rate \lambda), Markovian service (rate \mu), 1 server, infinite buffer.
Define \rho = \lambda/\mu (utilization). At steady state: 
P(\text{n in system}) = (1 - \rho) \rho^n.

Derive expected number in system L.


Full solution

L = \sum_{n=0}^\infty n (1-\rho) \rho^n = (1-\rho) \rho \sum n \rho^{n-1} = (1-\rho) \rho \cdot \frac{1}{(1-\rho)^2} = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho}.
For \rho = 0.5: L = 1. For \rho = 0.9: L = 9. For \rho = 0.99: L = 99.
Hyperbolic explosion as \rho \to 1.



Problem 3
Little’s Law. For any stationary queue: 
L = \lambda W,
 where L = avg # in system, \lambda = arrival rate, W = avg time in system.
Apply to M/M/1: derive W.


Full solution

W = L / \lambda = \frac{\rho/(1-\rho)}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}.
For \mu = 10, \lambda = 9: W = 1. For \lambda = 9.9: W = 10. For \lambda = 9.99: W = 100.
Little’s Law is the most useful identity in performance modelling. Holds for any stationary system — independent of arrival/service distributions.



Problem 4
Pollaczek-Khinchine formula (M/G/1 — Markovian arrivals, general service distribution): 
W_q = \frac{\lambda \mathbb{E}[S^2]}{2(1 - \rho)}.

How does service-time variance affect wait?


Full solution

Note \mathbb{E}[S^2] = \text{Var}(S) + (\mathbb{E}[S])^2. So 
W_q = \frac{\rho^2 (1 + C_v^2)}{2(1 - \rho)} \cdot \mathbb{E}[S]
 where C_v = \sigma_S / \mathbb{E}[S] is the coefficient of variation.
Variance hurts. Doubling variance doubles wait time at fixed utilization.
Intuition: a single long request blocks subsequent ones (especially under FIFO).



Problem 5
Why is high utilization dangerous? Plot W_q(\rho) for M/M/1.


Full solution

W_q = \rho/((1-\rho)\mu) \cdot \mathbb{E}[S] = \rho/(1-\rho) \cdot \mathbb{E}[S].
\rho = 0.5: wait = 1× service. \rho = 0.8: wait = 4×. \rho = 0.9: wait = 9×. \rho = 0.99: wait = 99×.
Knee of the curve ≈ 80%. Above, latency variance also explodes.
Practical implication: capacity-plan for \rho \leq 0.7-0.8. Saturated systems see super-linear latency growth.


Queueing theory was invented to keep telephones working. In 1909 Agner Krarup Erlang, a Danish mathematician working for the Copenhagen Telephone Company, published The Theory of Probabilities and Telephone Conversations — the first paper to model call arrivals as a Poisson process and to derive the formula now known as Erlang B for sizing trunk lines. The unit of telephone traffic is named the erlang in his honour. Half a century later, in 1961, John Little at MIT proved the deceptively simple identity L = \lambda W — Little’s Law — and the proof was so general it felt suspicious; the result has since been re-proven independently at least four times, in increasing levels of generality (Stidham 1974, Brumelle 1971, Heyman-Stidham 1980), each author convinced earlier proofs must have hidden assumptions. They didn’t. The law really does hold for any stationary system — no Markov, no Poisson, no distributional assumption at all.


The wait time on a queue isn’t linear in load — it’s hyperbolic. At 50% utilisation a request waits about as long as it takes to serve. At 90%, it waits nine times longer. At 99%, ninety-nine times. There is no smooth degradation; there is only the cliff at \rho \to 1. That single curve, \rho/(1-\rho), is why every production system in the world is provisioned at 70-80% load instead of 99% — not because engineers are wasteful, but because variance plus high utilisation equals catastrophe, and queueing theory is the math that tells you how close to the edge you really are.

title: 10 · Queueing Theory and Performance Modelling
text:
Name

Kendall notation A/S/c: arrival/service distribution + servers.

M = Markovian (Poisson arrivals or exponential service).
D = deterministic.
G = general.

Utilization \rho = \lambda / (c \mu).
Little’s Law: L = \lambda W. Always holds in steady state.
M/M/1: L = \rho/(1-\rho), W = 1/(\mu - \lambda), W_q = \rho W.
M/M/c (multi-server): Erlang C formula for P(\text{wait} > 0).
M/G/1 (Pollaczek-Khinchine): W_q = \frac{\lambda \mathbb{E}[S^2]}{2(1-\rho)} = \frac{\rho^2(1 + C_v^2)}{2(1-\rho)} \mathbb{E}[S].
Burke’s theorem: for stable M/M/c, departure process is also Poisson(\lambda). (Composability!)
Jackson networks: if each station is M/M/c and routing is Markovian, the joint stationary distribution factorises.
Operational laws (independent of distributions): Little’s, forced flow, response-time law.
Heavy traffic: \rho \to 1 → diffusion approximation, reflected Brownian motion.


Little’s law L = λW always holds; M/M/1 wait = ρ/(1−ρ) — explodes near 1; Variance hurts: P-K formula scales with C_v² + 1.

title: 10 · Queueing Theory and Performance Modelling
text:
Apply

Worked example

Web server. 100 req/sec arrival rate (Poisson), service time mean 8 ms, std-dev 4 ms. Single thread.

\rho = \lambda \cdot \mathbb{E}[S] = 100 \times 0.008 = 0.8. C_v^2 = (4/8)^2 = 0.25.
P-K: W_q = \frac{0.8^2 (1 + 0.25)}{2 (1 - 0.8)} \cdot 0.008 = \frac{0.8 \cdot 1.25}{0.4} \cdot 0.008 = 2.5 \cdot 0.008 = 0.02 s = 20 ms wait.
Total response: 8 ms service + 20 ms wait = 28 ms. Median user OK; tail latency much worse.
To halve wait: reduce ρ (add capacity), or reduce variance (cap expensive operations).


Practice — Warm
W1. What’s \mathbb{E}[\text{Exp}(\mu)]?


Full solution

1/\mu.

W2. If \rho = 0.5 in M/M/1, what’s L_q?


Full solution

L_q = \rho^2/(1-\rho) = 0.25/0.5 = 0.5.

W3. Does Little’s Law need exponential assumptions?


Full solution

No. Holds for any stationary system regardless of distributions.



Practice — Working
P1. Why does load balancing with c servers beat c separate M/M/1 queues?


Full solution

c separate queues at \rho each: each has L = \rho/(1-\rho); total wait \sim \rho/(1-\rho).
One M/M/c with same total \rho: shorter wait — server idleness can absorb a single long request.
Hence: single shared queue + multiple servers (load balancer) beats sticky routing.

P2. What’s the tail-latency problem in distributed systems?


Full solution

For one server, P99 is bad but rare. For n replicas in series, P99 of each contributes — total P99 ≈ 1 - (1 - 0.01)^n \approx 0.01n.
For n = 100: 63% of requests hit some P99. Fan-out + per-stage variance = compounded tail.
Mitigations: hedged requests, request cancellation, jitter.
Dean & Barroso, “The Tail at Scale” (Google, 2013).

P3. Throughput vs latency trade-off. Why can you not maximise both?


Full solution

Throughput maxed at high \rho; latency minimised at low \rho. P-K wait grows with \rho.
Cannot have both:

High \rho → high utilization → throughput-good but latency-bad.
Low \rho → idle capacity → latency-good but throughput-bad.

Capacity planning: pick a target P99 latency, work backward to max \rho.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Network of queues — Jackson’s theorem.


Full solution

For a network of M/M/c stations with Poisson external arrivals and Markovian routing, the joint stationary distribution is the product of per-station M/M/c distributions.
Each station looks “independent” to the user. Practical: simulate / analyse multi-tier services as a Jackson network.
Caveat: Jackson assumes Markovian routing; non-trivial state-dependent routing breaks it.

S2. Diffusion / heavy-traffic approximation.


Full solution

Near \rho = 1, scaled queue length converges to reflected Brownian motion (Iglehart-Whitt). Lets you analyse intractable queues by approximating with a tractable diffusion.
Tools: Skorohod’s reflection mapping, Brownian limits.
Heavy traffic + large-deviations theory → modern performance modelling (Sigman, Whitt, Harrison).



Common confusions

“Higher utilization = better.” Latency-wise: no. Above 80%, P99 explodes.
“Adding servers always helps.” Yes for \rho < 1; if traffic is bursty, may not be enough.
“Little’s law needs Markov.” No — pure conservation law.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

Little’s Law L = \lambda W is a conservation identity — it needs no probability distribution assumption, only that the system reaches steady state.
M/M/1 wait time is \rho/(1-\rho) — a hyperbola that explodes as utilisation approaches 100%, which is why “knee of the curve” capacity planning targets \rho \le 0.8.
Variance hurts: the Pollaczek-Khinchine formula scales wait with 1 + C_v^2, so a single fat-tailed random variable in service times can blow up your tail latency even at modest load.



Self-Assessment

State Little’s Law.
Why does M/M/1 latency blow up near 100% utilization?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems?

(Answer: L = \lambda W — items in system = arrival rate × time in system.)

3 yes: ready for 11 · Formal Methods.
2 yes: redo P2 (tail latency).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P4.

title: Path to Systems

If you’re early-career and want the foundation: walk Tier 5 in order, then arrive at Tier 7.

Move comfortably between the analytic, algebraic, and topological views of the same object.

T5 L01 · Matrices → T5 L03 · Vector Spaces → T5 L04 · Eigenvalues — model-view-projection, change of basis.
T5 L05 · Partial Derivatives → T5 L06 · Multiple Integrals — radiance integrals, Monte-Carlo rendering.
T6 L06 · Second-Order ODEs → T6 L07 · PDEs — cloth, fluids, deformation.
T6 L08 · Numerical Methods — explicit/implicit Euler, RK4, stability.



Tier 7 — Masters Game Dev capstone

T7 L07 · Differential Geometry — manifolds, geodesics, Theorema Egregium.
T7 L08 · Quaternions and 3D Rotations — slerp, gimbal lock, the standard rotation representation.

title: Path to Game Development
text:
Where to start right now
If you have no preparation: walk Tier 1 from 01.
If you can solve a quadratic and remember \sin/\cos: jump to T4 L13 · Vectors.
If you’ve shipped a 2D game and want to handle 3D well: go straight to T7 L07 and T7 L08.

title: Path to Encryption
text:
Understand why every modern cryptographic system rests on the difficulty of certain number-theoretic problems.
Implement RSA and ECC from scratch and explain why each is secure (and what breaks them).
Read modern crypto papers on elliptic curves, pairings, and lattice-based post-quantum schemes without the maths blocking you.
