text:
Wonder

The chess square e4 and the pair (5, 4) both name the same place, but in different ways. Could you invent a third way?
The point (3, 2) sits to the upper right of the centre. Where does (-3, 2) sit? (-3, -2)? (3, -2)?
Are (2, 3) and (3, 2) the same point?
The two number lines cross at one place. What is that place’s name in the new system?







TipA whisper, no answer yet





Q3 is the trap. Q4 has a single, very simple answer that the Name beat will state. Q2 is what we mean by “quadrants”.

title: Ch 3 — Coordinate Geometry
text:
Discover

Problem 1
A child stands at her front door. She walks 3 paces east, then 4 paces north. Mark her path on graph paper. Where is she relative to the door?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Take east as the positive x direction, north as the positive y direction. Door is at the origin.



Full solution

She is at (3, 4). East-of-door is the x-coordinate; north-of-door is the y-coordinate. The order matters: (3,4) is not the same place as (4,3).



Problem 2
Plot the four points A(2, 3), B(-2, 3), C(-2, -3), D(2, -3) on the same diagram. What kind of figure do they form?


Hint 1 — a nudge

A is upper-right; B is upper-left; C is lower-left; D is lower-right. Connect the dots in order.



Full solution

A rectangle. Sides AB and CD are horizontal of length 4; sides BC and AD are vertical of length 6. The four points sit symmetrically about both axes.



Problem 3
In which quadrant (upper-right, upper-left, lower-left, lower-right) does each of these points lie?
P(4, -1), \quad Q(-3, -2), \quad R(0, 5), \quad S(-1, 4).


Hint 1 — a nudge

The sign of x tells you left/right; the sign of y tells you up/down.



Full solution

P: x > 0, y < 0 — lower-right (Q-IV). Q: both negative — lower-left (Q-III). R: x = 0, so on the y-axis (no quadrant). S: x < 0, y > 0 — upper-left (Q-II).



Problem 4
A point lies on the x-axis. What can you say about its y-coordinate? A point lies on the y-axis. What can you say about its x-coordinate?


Full solution

On x-axis: y = 0, so the point is (x, 0). On y-axis: x = 0, so the point is (0, y). The origin (0, 0) is on both axes.



Problem 5
Plot (2, 0) and (2, 5). The segment between them is vertical. How long is it? Now plot (-1, 3) and (4, 3) — how long is that segment?


Hint 1 — a nudge

For points sharing an x-coordinate, the distance is just the difference of y-values. For points sharing a y-coordinate, the distance is the difference of x-values.



Full solution

Vertical segment: |5 - 0| = 5 units. Horizontal segment: |4 - (-1)| =
5 units. Both have length 5.

You have just discovered: every point on the plane has a unique label (x, y); signs of the two coordinates split the plane into four quadrants; points on the axes have one coordinate zero; horizontal and vertical distances reduce to subtraction.

René Descartes invented coordinate geometry in La Géométrie (1637), the appendix to his Discourse on Method. The legend — possibly true — is that he conceived the idea while lying ill in bed, watching a fly crawl across the ceiling, and realising he could pin down the fly’s position at any instant by its perpendicular distances from two adjacent walls. From that one image, the entire bridge between algebra and geometry was built. Newton and Leibniz could not have invented calculus without it.


A point is just an ordered pair. Pinning a position needs two pieces of information — across, and up — and the order is sacred: (2, 3) and (3, 2) are different addresses on the same street. Geometry now has a phone number for every point.

title: Ch 3 — Coordinate Geometry
text:
Name

The Cartesian plane is two perpendicular number lines (the x-axis, horizontal; the y-axis, vertical) meeting at the origin (0, 0).
Every point P in the plane is named by its coordinates (x, y): - x is the perpendicular distance from P to the y-axis (positive right, negative left). - y is the perpendicular distance from P to the x-axis (positive up, negative down).
The four quadrants, numbered counter-clockwise from the upper right:



Quadrant
Sign of x
Sign of y




I (upper-right)
+
+


II (upper-left)
−
+


III (lower-left)
−
−


IV (lower-right)
+
−



Points on the x-axis have y = 0; points on the y-axis have x = 0.

A short verse:

Two lines that meet at right angles — Four quadrants, an origin, a name. Read x first, then y; Order matters, lest two points share the same.

title: Ch 3 — Coordinate Geometry
text:
Apply

Worked example

Plot A(3, 4), B(3, -2), C(-1, -2). Name the figure ABC might belong to and find the lengths of AB and BC.

A competent solver thinks:

A and B share x = 3, so AB is vertical with length |4 - (-2)| = 6.
B and C share y = -2, so BC is horizontal with length |3 - (-1)| = 4.
The angle \angle ABC is therefore 90^\circ.
ABC is a right triangle, right-angled at B.

Answer: Right triangle. AB = 6, BC = 4.


Practice — Warm
W1. Write the coordinates of the origin and of any point on the positive x-axis at distance 5 from origin.


Full solution

Origin (0, 0); the point is (5, 0).

W2. In which quadrant does (-7, 2) lie?


Full solution

x < 0, y > 0 — Quadrant II.

W3. Plot A(0, 3), B(-4, 0), C(0, -3), D(4, 0) and join them in order. What shape is ABCD?


Full solution

A rhombus (in fact a square’s cousin — a kite that is symmetric, so a rhombus). Diagonals lie on the axes; lengths 6 and 8 on the two axes. Each side has length \sqrt{16 + 9} = 5 — all equal, so it is a rhombus.



Practice — Working
P1. Three vertices of a rectangle are A(1, 1), B(5, 1), C(5, 4). Find the fourth vertex D.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Sketch. D is across from B.



Full solution

For a rectangle, D shares its x with A and its y with C: D = (1, 4).

P2. A point P lies on the y-axis at a distance of 4 units below the origin. What are its coordinates?


Full solution

On the y-axis means x = 0. Below origin means y < 0. So P = (0, -4).

P3. Plot (2, 3), (3, 2), (-2, 3), (-3, -2). Group them by quadrant.


Full solution

Q-I: (2,3) and (3,2). Q-II: (-2, 3). Q-III: (-3, -2). Note (2,3) \ne (3,2) — both are in Q-I but they are different points.



Practice — Stretch
S1. The vertices of a triangle are A(0, 0), B(6, 0), C(0, 8). Without using a distance formula, find the perimeter of the triangle.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Two sides lie on the axes — those are easy. For the third, you need the hypotenuse of a right triangle.



Hint 2 — the technique

AB has length 6, AC has length 8. Use Pythagoras for BC.



Full solution

AB = 6 (along x-axis), AC = 8 (along y-axis). The angle at A is 90^\circ. By Pythagoras BC = \sqrt{36 + 64} = 10. Perimeter = 6 + 8 + 10 = 24.

S2. Plot the points (1, 1), (1, 4), (4, 4), (4, 1). Now plot (2, 2), (2, 3), (3, 3), (3, 2). What relationship do you see between the two figures, and how is it visible from the coordinates alone?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Compare side lengths. Compare centres.



Full solution

Both are squares centred at (2.5, 2.5). The first has side 3; the second has side 1. The second is the first scaled by 1/3 about the common centre. The coordinates make this scaling visible: each coordinate of the inner square is the centre 2.5 plus \pm 0.5, while the outer is \pm 1.5.



Board-Pattern Practice
1-mark (objective). The point (0, -7) lies on: (a) Quadrant III (b) Quadrant IV (c) the x-axis (d) the y-axis.


Full solution

x = 0 — on the y-axis. Answer: (d).

2-mark (short answer). Without plotting, in which quadrant or on which axis do these points lie? A(2, -3), B(0, 5), C(-4, -1), D(7, 0).


Full solution

A: Q-IV. B: y-axis. C: Q-III. D: x-axis.

3-mark (short answer II). Plot the points A(-2, 4), B(-2, -2),
C(4, -2) and find the area of triangle ABC.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Are A, B, C aligned with the axes?



Hint 2 — the technique

AB is vertical. BC is horizontal. So \triangle ABC is right-angled at B.



Full solution

AB = |4 - (-2)| = 6. BC = |4 - (-2)| = 6. The right angle is at B. Area = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot AB \cdot BC = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 6 \cdot 6 = 18 square units.

4-mark (long answer). Plot the points P(1, 0), Q(4, 0), R(4, 3),
S(1, 3). Identify the shape and find its area and perimeter.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Sides parallel to the axes; opposite sides should have equal lengths.



Full solution

PQ: horizontal, length 3. QR: vertical, length 3. RS: horizontal, length 3. SP: vertical, length 3. All four sides = 3; all angles 90^\circ. Square. Area = 9 sq.units. Perimeter = 12 units.







NCERT Exemplar pointer: Class 9 Exemplar Ch. 3, Problem 3.2 (axis classification, similar to 1-mark). Previous-year pointer: CBSE Class 9 sample paper “plot four points and identify shape” (similar to 4-mark).





Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

Two perpendicular number lines name every point on the plane. That single act builds the bridge from algebra to geometry.
Order matters. (x, y) is across first, then up — always. Swap them and you get a different point.
Signs slice the plane into four quadrants, plus two axes (one coordinate zero) and the origin (both zero).



Common confusions

“(2, 3) and (3, 2) are the same point.” No — order matters. The first coordinate is always x, the second is always y.
“The y-axis is the horizontal line.” No — x is horizontal, y is vertical. (The mnemonic: “x is across, y is high.”)
“(0, 0) is in some quadrant.” No — the origin is on both axes and lies in no quadrant.



Self-Assessment
Be honest with yourself.

Could you sketch a labelled coordinate plane from memory and place a given point in the correct quadrant?
Did you solve W1–P3 with at most one hint each?
Can you read off horizontal and vertical distances between two points without thinking about a formula?


3 yes: move on to Linear Equations in Two Variables.
2 yes: redo S1 and S2.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover and Apply.

title: Ch 3 — Coordinate Geometry
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

cbse8.introduction-to-graphs — bar graphs, pictographs, simple line graphs.
t1.negative-numbers — sign rules, the number line going both ways.



What this unlocks

cbse9.linear-equations-two-vars — every solution (x, y) becomes a point.
cbse10.coordinate-geometry — distance formula, section formula, area of a triangle.



NCERT chapter alignment

Class 9, Chapter 3 — Coordinate Geometry (NCERT 2023 rationalised syllabus). Covers Sections 3.1–3.2: the Cartesian plane, plotting points.
Sister synthesis lesson (different angle): t2.coordinate-plane.
Read alongside the NCERT textbook.



Where this matters

Game development. Every 2D game treats the screen as a coordinate plane (with y usually flipped, increasing downward). When a sprite moves, the engine adds a velocity vector to its (x, y). The chess board in Touch is the simplest possible case — a discrete plane.


AI / ML. A dataset of two-feature points (e.g. height vs weight) lives in \mathbb{R}^2. A linear classifier draws a line. Generalised to \mathbb{R}^{784} (an MNIST digit), the same idea — points in a plane, separated by a hyperplane — drives modern image classifiers.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones honoured: Pestalozzi (chessboard Touch), Boole (the Wonder questions surface the asymmetry (2,3) \ne (3,2) before the text states it), Better Explained (the “Aha” is one point, two numbers, in this order).

title: Ch 7 — Triangles (Congruence and Properties)
text:
Be honest before you begin.

Can you state the angle-sum theorem of a triangle? (If no, revisit cbse9.lines-and-angles.)
Can you write a two-column proof?
Can you recognise an isosceles triangle (two sides equal)?
Do you know what perpendicular means? (If no, revisit t1.shapes-angles.)

Three or four yes? Read on.

title: Ch 7 — Triangles (Congruence and Properties)
Ch 7 — Triangles (Congruence and Properties)
text:
Wonder

If two triangles share just one equal side, are they identical? What if they share two equal sides?
To uniquely build a triangle, how many measurements do you need — three? more? Which three?
Could you have two different triangles with the same three angles? With the same three sides?
Why does the carpenter’s rule “diagonals brace a square” work — why does adding a diagonal to a four-sided frame stop it from wobbling?







TipA whisper, no answer yet





Q3 hides the difference between congruence (identical) and similarity (same shape, different size). This year is congruence; similarity comes next year.

title: Ch 7 — Triangles (Congruence and Properties)
text:
Discover

Problem 1 (SSS)
You and a friend each draw a triangle with sides 5, 7, 8. Without seeing each other’s drawings, would you trust that the two triangles are identical (after possibly flipping)?


Hint 1 — a nudge

There is only one way to fit three given sides together (up to a flip). Try with strips of paper.



Full solution

Yes. Three sides — and nothing else — uniquely determine a triangle up to flipping. This is the SSS congruence rule.



Problem 2 (SAS)
A bridge truss is built with two diagonal members of 3 m and 4 m, meeting at a 60^\circ angle. A second truss is built the same way. Are both trusses interchangeable parts?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Two sides and the angle between them — does that pin down the third side?



Full solution

Yes, by SAS (side–angle–side). Two sides and the included angle fix the triangle uniquely.



Problem 3 (ASA)
A surveyor measures an angle from one bank of a river (60^\circ), walks 50 m along the bank, and measures a second angle from there (45^\circ). Can he reconstruct the river-crossing triangle without ever crossing the river?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Two angles plus the side between them.



Full solution

Yes, by ASA (angle–side–angle). Two angles and the included side fix the triangle uniquely. (And once two angles are known, the third is forced by the angle-sum theorem.)



Problem 4 (Isosceles theorem)
In \triangle ABC, AB = AC. Drop the bisector AD from A onto BC (so \angle BAD = \angle CAD). Prove \triangle ABD \cong \triangle ACD, and deduce \angle B = \angle C.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Compare \triangle ABD and \triangle ACD. Which sides and angles match?



Full solution








Statement
Reason




1. AB = AC.
Given.


2. \angle BAD = \angle CAD.
AD is the bisector.


3. AD = AD.
Common side.


4. \triangle ABD \cong \triangle ACD.
SAS, from 1, 2, 3.


5. \angle B = \angle C.
Corresponding parts of congruent triangles (CPCT). \blacksquare



The angles opposite equal sides of a triangle are equal.



Problem 5 (Converse)
Now suppose only that \angle B = \angle C in \triangle ABC. Use ASA with the bisector to prove AB = AC.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Drop the bisector AD as before. Try ASA on \triangle ABD and \triangle ACD.



Full solution




Statement
Reason




1. \angle B = \angle C.
Given.


2. \angle BAD = \angle CAD.
AD is the angle bisector.


3. AD = AD.
Common.


4. \triangle ABD \cong \triangle ACD.
ASA.


5. AB = AC.
CPCT. \blacksquare



The sides opposite equal angles are equal (converse).



Problem 6 (RHS)
Two right triangles have hypotenuse 13 cm and one leg 5 cm. Are they congruent?


Hint 1 — a nudge

You can’t use SSA in general — it’s the one rule that fails (the “AAS” imitator). But for right triangles, the right angle helps.



Full solution

Yes, by RHS (right angle, hypotenuse, side). The third side is forced by Pythagoras: \sqrt{13^2 - 5^2} = 12. So both triangles have sides 5, 12, 13 and hence are congruent by SSS.

You discovered: SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, RHS — five rules, each pinning down a unique triangle from a small set of measurements. CPCT lets you read off any unknown part once you have a congruence.

The Indian śulbasūtras (~800 BCE — “rules of the cord”) contain the oldest known recipe for laying out a perfect right angle on the ground: stretch a rope into a closed loop knotted into 12 equal segments, and pull it taut as a 3, 4, 5 triangle. The right angle sits at the corner between the sides of length 3 and 4. Vedic priests used this to align fire-altars centuries before Pythagoras stated the algebraic identity.


A small handful of measurements (three, in the right combination) is enough to force a triangle into existence — and once forced, every other side and angle is determined. Congruence is the language for saying “two triangles are not just similar, they are the same”.

title: Ch 7 — Triangles (Congruence and Properties)
text:
Name

Two triangles are congruent (notation \triangle ABC \cong
\triangle DEF) if all corresponding sides and corresponding angles are equal. The five tests for congruence:

SSS — three sides equal.
SAS — two sides and the included angle equal.
ASA — two angles and the included side equal.
AAS — two angles and any non-included side equal (follows from ASA, because the third angle is forced).
RHS — for right triangles only: hypotenuse and one other side equal.

Once two triangles are congruent, CPCT (corresponding parts of congruent triangles) is the rule that lets you conclude any other side or angle is equal.
Isosceles triangle theorem (and converse). In a triangle, two sides are equal if and only if their opposite angles are equal.
Triangle inequality. The sum of any two sides of a triangle is greater than the third side.

A short verse:

Three sides, or two-and-included, or angle-side-angle — Each is a recipe; the triangle obeys it once. Right-angled? Then hypotenuse-and-side suffice. Equal sides? Equal angles. Each implies the other.

title: Ch 7 — Triangles (Congruence and Properties)
text:
Apply

Worked example

In \triangle PQR, PQ = PR. The bisector of \angle P meets QR at M. Prove PM \perp QR and that M is the midpoint of QR.

A competent solver:

Compare \triangle PQM and \triangle PRM.
PQ = PR (given), \angle QPM = \angle RPM (bisector definition), PM = PM (common). So SAS ⟹ \triangle PQM \cong \triangle PRM.
CPCT gives QM = RM (so M is midpoint) and \angle PMQ =
\angle PMR.
The two angles at M are also a linear pair (they sit on QR), so \angle PMQ + \angle PMR = 180^\circ. With both equal, \angle PMQ = 90^\circ, hence PM \perp QR.

Conclusion: PM is both the perpendicular and the median from P. \blacksquare


Practice — Warm
W1. State which congruence rule (SSS / SAS / ASA / AAS / RHS) is suggested by each of these descriptions.

“Two sides and the angle between them are equal.”
“Two angles and a corresponding side are equal.”
“Two right triangles share hypotenuse and a leg.”



Full solution


SAS. (b) AAS or ASA depending on which side. (c) RHS.


W2. In \triangle ABC, AB = AC and \angle A = 40^\circ. Find \angle B and \angle C.


Full solution

\angle B = \angle C. 40 + 2x = 180 \Rightarrow x = 70^\circ. So \angle B = \angle C = 70^\circ.

W3. Sides of a triangle are 4, 5, 9. Possible?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Triangle inequality: each pair sums to more than the third side.



Full solution

4 + 5 = 9 — equal, not greater. So the “triangle” collapses to a line. Not a valid triangle.



Practice — Working
P1. AB and CD are two segments bisecting each other at O. Prove \triangle AOC \cong \triangle BOD.


Hint 1 — a nudge

AO = OB, CO = OD (bisecting). Where is the angle?



Full solution




Statement
Reason




1. AO = OB.
AB bisected at O.


2. CO = OD.
CD bisected at O.


3. \angle AOC = \angle BOD.
Vertically opposite angles.


4. \triangle AOC \cong \triangle BOD.
SAS. \blacksquare




P2. In \triangle ABC, AB = AC. Two points D on AB and E on AC are chosen so AD = AE. Prove BE = CD.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Look at \triangle ABE and \triangle ACD.



Full solution




Statement
Reason




1. AB = AC.
Given.


2. AE = AD.
Given.


3. \angle A = \angle A.
Common.


4. \triangle ABE \cong \triangle ACD.
SAS.


5. BE = CD.
CPCT. \blacksquare




P3. AD is a median of \triangle ABC (so D is the midpoint of BC). AD is extended to a point E such that DE = AD. Prove \triangle ADB \cong \triangle EDC, and conclude that AB = EC and AB \parallel EC.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Two segments meet at D; what kind of angles form there?



Hint 2 — the technique

You have one pair of equal sides (DB = DC, AD = DE). You need an angle between those sides — vertically opposite angles at D. SAS.



Full solution

In \triangle ADB and \triangle EDC:







Statement
Reason




1. AD = ED.
Given (E is on the extension of AD with DE = AD).


2. BD = DC.
D is the midpoint of BC (AD is a median).


3. \angle ADB = \angle EDC.
Vertically opposite angles at D.


4. \triangle ADB \cong \triangle EDC.
SAS (steps 1, 3, 2).


5. AB = EC.
CPCT.


6. \angle DAB = \angle DEC.
CPCT.


7. AB \parallel EC.
Step 6: alternate interior angles equal \Rightarrow lines parallel (transversal AE). \blacksquare



This is a classic NCERT result — it tells you that doubling a median produces a parallelogram ABEC. We come back to it in Lesson 8: Quadrilaterals.



Practice — Stretch
S1. In \triangle ABC, the bisector of \angle A meets BC at D. If AB = AC, prove D is the midpoint of BC and AD \perp BC.


Hint 1 — a nudge

This is the worked example, generalised. Use SAS on \triangle ABD and \triangle ACD.



Full solution

Mirror the worked example exactly. SAS gives the congruence; CPCT gives BD = DC (midpoint) and \angle ADB = \angle ADC, which together with the linear pair forces both to be 90^\circ. \blacksquare

S2. Show that the perpendicular from any vertex of an equilateral triangle to the opposite side bisects that side.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Equilateral is “extra-isosceles”. Use RHS.



Full solution

Drop AD \perp BC. In right triangles \triangle ABD and \triangle ACD: AB = AC (equilateral), AD = AD (common), \angle ADB = \angle ADC = 90^\circ. So \triangle ABD \cong
\triangle ACD by RHS. CPCT gives BD = DC. \blacksquare



Board-Pattern Practice
1-mark (objective). In \triangle ABC, if \angle A = \angle B, which sides are equal? (a) AB = BC (b) BC = CA (c) CA = AB (d) all three.


Full solution

(b) BC = CA — the sides opposite equal angles are equal. Side BC is opposite A; side CA is opposite B.

2-mark (short answer). Two sides of a triangle are 7 cm and 9 cm. Between which two integer values must the third side lie?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Triangle inequality: third side < sum, > difference.



Full solution

|7 - 9| < x < 7 + 9, so 2 < x < 16. Integer values: 3, 4, \ldots,
15.

3-mark (short answer II). ABCD is a quadrilateral in which AB = AD and CB = CD. Prove that AC bisects both \angle A and \angle C.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Compare \triangle ABC and \triangle ADC.



Full solution




Statement
Reason




1. AB = AD.
Given.


2. CB = CD.
Given.


3. AC = AC.
Common.


4. \triangle ABC \cong \triangle ADC.
SSS.


5. \angle BAC = \angle DAC and \angle BCA = \angle DCA.
CPCT.


6. So AC bisects \angle A and \angle C.
\blacksquare




5-mark (long answer). Prove the isosceles triangle theorem: in \triangle ABC, if AB = AC, then \angle B = \angle C. Use a clear figure and a two-column proof.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Drop the bisector from A. Prove the two halves congruent by SAS.



Full solution

Reproduce Discover Problem 4. Construction: angle bisector from A meets BC at D. Compare \triangle ABD and \triangle ACD: AB = AC (given), \angle BAD = \angle CAD (bisector), AD = AD (common). By SAS, \triangle ABD \cong \triangle ACD. By CPCT, \angle B = \angle C. \blacksquare







NCERT Exemplar pointer: Class 9 Exemplar Ch. 7, Problem 7.2 (SAS recognition, similar to W1). Previous-year pointer: CBSE Class 9 sample paper “prove AC bisects in a kite” (similar to 3-mark).





Common confusions

“SSA is also a congruence rule.” No — two sides and a non-included angle can describe two different triangles (the “ambiguous case”). Only when the angle is right (RHS) does it work.
“AAA proves congruence.” No — three equal angles only force similarity (same shape), not congruence (same size). A small equilateral triangle and a large one share AAA but are different sizes.
“CPCT can be used before proving congruence.” It cannot. CPCT is a consequence of congruence; you must establish the congruence first, then quote CPCT to extract a side or angle equality.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

Three matching measurements (in the right pattern) force two triangles to be identical. SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, RHS — five recipes, no others.
CPCT is a payoff, not a tool. First establish congruence, then read off any equal side or angle for free.
Equal sides and equal angles always come in pairs. Opposite-side to opposite-angle, in both directions — that is the isosceles theorem and its converse.



Self-Assessment
Be honest with yourself.

Could you list the five congruence rules and explain why SSA is not among them?
Did you write each two-column proof yourself, with reasons in the right column, before peeking?
Could you tackle a fresh “two sides + bisector” or “median + midpoint” problem and find the right congruence rule on the first try?


3 yes: move on to Quadrilaterals.
2 yes: redo S1 and S2 fresh.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover 1–6. The five rules are a small vocabulary that takes a week to internalise.

title: Ch 7 — Triangles (Congruence and Properties)
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

cbse9.lines-and-angles — vertically opposite angles, parallel-line pairs, triangle angle-sum.
t1.shapes-angles — naming triangles, types (isosceles, equilateral, right).



What this unlocks

cbse9.quadrilaterals — almost every parallelogram proof reduces to triangle congruence.
cbse9.herons-formula — area of a triangle when only sides are known.
cbse10.triangles-similarity — same shape, possibly different size.



NCERT chapter alignment

Class 9, Chapter 7 — Triangles (NCERT 2023 rationalised syllabus). Covers Sections 7.1–7.4: congruence criteria, properties of triangles, inequalities.
Sister synthesis lesson (different angle): t2.pythagorean-theorem treats the right-triangle special case from a coordinate angle. The congruence story is the new contribution here.
Read alongside the NCERT textbook.



Where this matters

Game development. Mesh deformation in 3D engines preserves triangle topology (which vertices are connected) but changes geometry. When a 3D character moves, the rig sends each triangle to a congruent copy in a new position — that’s why characters don’t suddenly grow extra limbs.


Research. The congruence axioms became one of Hilbert’s groups in his 1899 axiomatisation. They survive almost unchanged into modern Tarski-style geometry, the version most amenable to automated theorem provers.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones honoured: Kiselev (proof discipline), AoPS (graduated stretch), Russian problem-first (six Discover problems unfold the five rules + the isosceles theorem before naming them).

title: Ch 6b — Maxima and Minima
text:
Can you find f'(x) and solve f'(x) = 0?
Can you compute f''(x)?
Have you done Ch 6a?

title: Ch 6b — Maxima and Minima
text:
Touch
You have 20 m of fencing and want to enclose a rectangular garden of maximum area. Let one side be x. The opposite side is x, and the other two sides total 20 - 2x, so each is 10 - x.

Area A(x) = x(10 - x) = 10x - x^2, with 0 < x < 10.



x
1
3
5
7
9




A
9
21
25
21
9




The table peaks at x = 5. Calculus confirms it: A'(x) = 10 - 2x = 0
\Rightarrow x = 5. A''(x) = -2 < 0, so the critical point is a maximum.
The garden of maximum area is a square of side 5 (area 25 m²).
That’s the whole lesson: find f'(x) = 0 to locate the critical points, then check the sign of f'' (or sign change of f') to classify as max or min.

title: Ch 6b — Maxima and Minima
text:
Wonder

Could a function have a max but no value where f' = 0? (Hint: endpoints, corners.)
If f''(c) = 0 at a critical point, is it max, min, or neither?
Why does the maximum-area rectangle from a fixed perimeter turn out to be a square?

title: Ch 6b — Maxima and Minima
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Find the critical points of f(x) = x^3 - 3x^2 - 9x + 5.


Full solution

f'(x) = 3x^2 - 6x - 9 = 3(x-3)(x+1) = 0 \Rightarrow x = 3, -1.



Problem 2
Use the first-derivative test to classify the critical points in Problem 1.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Test sign of f' on intervals (-\infty, -1), (-1, 3), (3, \infty).



Full solution

f' is +, -, + on the three intervals. x = -1: sign changes +
\to -, so local max. x = 3: sign changes - \to +, local min.



Problem 3
Apply the second-derivative test to the same function.


Full solution

f''(x) = 6x - 6. f''(-1) = -12 < 0 ⇒ max. f''(3) = 12 > 0 ⇒ min. ✓



Problem 4
Find the absolute max and min of f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 1 on [0,
5].


Full solution

f'(x) = 3x^2 - 12x + 9 = 3(x-1)(x-3) = 0 \Rightarrow x = 1, 3. Evaluate: f(0) = 1, f(1) = 5, f(3) = 1, f(5) = 21. Absolute max: 21 at x = 5. Absolute min: 1 at x = 0 and x =
3.



Problem 5
Two positive numbers add to 16. Find the maximum value of their product.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let one be x, the other 16 - x. Maximise P = x(16 - x).



Full solution

P' = 16 - 2x = 0 \Rightarrow x = 8. P'' = -2 < 0 ⇒ max. Maximum product is 8 \cdot 8 = 64. (AM-GM gives the same answer.)


Fermat’s adequality — finding maxima before calculus existed. Around 1636, Pierre de Fermat circulated a short manuscript called Methodus ad disquirendam maximam et minimam — “Method to find maxima and minima.” His trick: to maximise f(x), replace x with x + e, adequate (Fermat’s word, vague between “set equal” and “set approximately equal”) f(x) with f(x + e), divide both sides by e, then drop every remaining e. The recipe is exactly setting f'(x) = 0 — written down half a century before Newton or Leibniz had a name for the derivative. Descartes hated the method (he couldn’t justify the “drop e” step) and the two argued bitterly. But Fermat’s recipe was correct: every fencing-the-garden, box-from- sheet, cone-in-sphere problem you solve below is a 21st-century descendant of Methodus.


Optimisation = find candidates, then classify. Differentiable maxima and minima on an open interval can only happen at a critical point — where f'(c) = 0 or fails to exist. The second derivative test sorts those candidates: f''(c) > 0 means cup (min), f''(c) < 0 means cap (max), f''(c) = 0 means “first-derivative test, please.” On a closed interval [a, b], also check the endpoints. That’s the entire optimisation algorithm.

title: Ch 6b — Maxima and Minima
text:
Name

A point x = c is critical for f iff f'(c) = 0 or f'(c) DNE. Maxima and minima of differentiable f on an open interval occur only at critical points.
First-derivative test. At a critical point c:

f' changes + \to - at c: local max.
f' changes - \to + at c: local min.
No sign change: neither.

Second-derivative test (when f'' exists):

f''(c) > 0: local min.
f''(c) < 0: local max.
f''(c) = 0: inconclusive — fall back to first-derivative test.

Absolute extrema on [a, b]. Compare f at every critical point inside (a, b) and at endpoints a, b.

A verse:

Set f' to zero — the candidates appear; Sign change of f' tells max from min — that’s clear. Or check f'': positive cup — minimum; Negative cap — maximum; zero — your test’s undone.

title: Ch 6b — Maxima and Minima
text:
Apply

Worked example

An open box is to be made from a 24 cm × 9 cm sheet by cutting equal squares of side x from each corner and folding up the flaps. Find x that maximises volume.

A competent solver:

After folding: length 24 - 2x, width 9 - 2x, height x.
V(x) = x(24 - 2x)(9 - 2x) = x(216 - 66x + 4x^2) = 4x^3 - 66x^2 +
216x, for 0 < x < 9/2.
V'(x) = 12x^2 - 132x + 216 = 12(x^2 - 11x + 18) = 12(x - 2)(x - 9).
Critical points: x = 2, 9. Only x = 2 is in domain.
V''(x) = 24x - 132. V''(2) = -84 < 0 ⇒ max.
V_{\max} = V(2) = 2 \cdot 20 \cdot 5 = 200 cm³.



Practice — Warm
W1. Find local extrema of f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 7.


Full solution

f' = 2x - 4 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 2. f'' = 2 > 0, so local min. Value: f(2) = 3.

W2. Absolute max/min of f(x) = \sin x + \cos x on [0, \pi/2].


Full solution

f'(x) = \cos x - \sin x = 0 \Rightarrow x = \pi/4. f(0) = 1, f(\pi/4) = \sqrt{2}, f(\pi/2) = 1. Max \sqrt{2} at \pi/4, min 1 at endpoints.

W3. Show f(x) = x^3 has no local extremum.


Full solution

f'(x) = 3x^2 = 0 at x = 0. f''(0) = 0 — inconclusive. Use first-derivative test: f' is \ge 0 everywhere, no sign change. No extremum.



Practice — Working
P1. Find two positive numbers whose sum is 24 and whose product is maximum.


Full solution

Let them be x, 24 - x. P(x) = x(24 - x). P' = 24 - 2x = 0
\Rightarrow x = 12. P_{\max} = 144. Numbers are 12, 12.

P2. A rectangle is inscribed in a semicircle of radius r with one side on the diameter. Find dimensions of the rectangle of maximum area.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Place semicircle as x^2 + y^2 = r^2, y \ge 0. Rectangle has corners (\pm x, 0), (\pm x, y).



Full solution

Area A = 2xy = 2x\sqrt{r^2 - x^2}. Square: A^2 = 4x^2(r^2 - x^2). Maximise A^2: \frac{d}{dx}[4x^2(r^2 - x^2)] = 4(2x \cdot r^2 -
4x^3) = 0 \Rightarrow 2r^2 = 4x^2 \Rightarrow x = r/\sqrt{2}. Then y
= r/\sqrt{2}. Width r\sqrt{2}, height r/\sqrt{2}, area r^2.

P3. Find the point on y = x^2 closest to (3, 0).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Distance squared: D^2 = (x - 3)^2 + x^4. Differentiate.



Full solution

\frac{d(D^2)}{dx} = 2(x - 3) + 4x^3 = 0 \Rightarrow 2x^3 + x - 3 = 0. Try x = 1: 2 + 1 - 3 = 0 ✓. So x = 1, point (1, 1).



Practice — Stretch
S1. Show that of all rectangles with given perimeter P, the square has the largest area; and of all rectangles with given area A, the square has the smallest perimeter.


Full solution

Sides x, P/2 - x. A(x) = x(P/2 - x). A' = P/2 - 2x = 0 \Rightarrow
x = P/4. Square. ✓ For fixed area: sides x, A/x. Perimeter 2(x + A/x). Differentiate: 2(1 - A/x^2) = 0 \Rightarrow x = \sqrt{A}. Square again. ✓



Board-Pattern Practice
1-mark MCQ #1. Local max of f(x) = -x^2 + 4x is at x = (a) 0 (b) 2 (c) 4 (d) -2.


Full solution

f'(x) = -2x + 4 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 2. (b).

1-mark MCQ #2. f(x) = x \log x on (0, \infty) has (a) only max (b) only min (c) both (d) neither.


Full solution

f'(x) = \log x + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 1/e. f''(x) = 1/x > 0 ⇒ min. (b).

2-mark. Find the maximum value of f(x) = 12x - x^3 on [-1, 3].


Full solution

f'(x) = 12 - 3x^2 = 0 \Rightarrow x = \pm 2. x = 2 \in [-1, 3]. f(-1) = -11, f(2) = 16, f(3) = 9. Max 16 at x = 2.

2-mark. A wire of length 28 cm is bent into a square + circle. Show that the side and radius giving minimum total area are in ratio 2:1 when the square wire = 4r.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Length of square’s wire = 4s, of circle’s = 2\pi r. 4s + 2\pi r =
28. Minimise A = s^2 + \pi r^2.



Full solution

From constraint s = 7 - \pi r/2. A = (7 - \pi r/2)^2 + \pi r^2. Differentiate: 2(7 - \pi r/2)(-\pi/2) + 2\pi r = 0 \Rightarrow solve for r. -\pi(7 - \pi r/2) + 2\pi r = 0 \Rightarrow -7 + \pi r/2 + 2r = 0
\Rightarrow r(\pi/2 + 2) = 7 \Rightarrow r = 14/(\pi + 4). Then s = 28/(\pi + 4). Ratio s/r = 2. ✓

3-mark. A cylindrical tank of radius r and height h holds V litres. Find the dimensions that minimise surface area for given V.


Full solution

V = \pi r^2 h, S = 2\pi r^2 + 2\pi r h. Eliminate h = V/(\pi r^2): S(r) = 2\pi r^2 + 2V/r. S'(r) = 4\pi r - 2V/r^2 = 0 \Rightarrow
r^3 = V/(2\pi). Then h = V/(\pi r^2) = 2r. Height equals diameter.

3-mark. Find local extrema of f(x) = x^4 - 8x^3 + 22x^2 - 24x + 1.


Hint 1 — a nudge

f'(x) = 4x^3 - 24x^2 + 44x - 24 = 4(x - 1)(x - 2)(x - 3).



Full solution

Critical: x = 1, 2, 3. f''(x) = 12x^2 - 48x + 44. f''(1) = 8 > 0 min. f''(2) = -4 < 0 max. f''(3) = 8 > 0 min. f(1) = -8, f(2) = -7, f(3) = -8. Local min at x = 1, 3 (value -8); local max at x = 2 (value -7).

5-mark long answer. A right-circular cone of maximum volume is inscribed in a sphere of radius R. Show that the height of the cone is 4R/3 and find the maximum volume.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let cone height h, base radius r. Cone-base center is at distance h - R below the sphere center. So r^2 = R^2 - (h - R)^2 = h(2R -
h).



Full solution

V = \frac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h = \frac{\pi}{3} h \cdot h(2R - h) =
\frac{\pi}{3}(2Rh^2 - h^3). V'(h) = \frac{\pi}{3}(4Rh - 3h^2) = \frac{\pi h}{3}(4R - 3h) = 0
\Rightarrow h = 4R/3 (other root h = 0 trivial). V''(h) = \frac{\pi}{3}(4R - 6h). At h = 4R/3: V'' = \frac{\pi}{3}(
4R - 8R) < 0 ⇒ max. V_{\max} = \frac{\pi}{3} \cdot \frac{4R}{3} \cdot \frac{4R}{3} \cdot
\frac{2R}{3} = \frac{32\pi R^3}{81}.

6-mark Case Study. Maximising profit on a stall.
A street vendor sells x samosas at price p(x) = 30 - x/10 rupees each. Cost of producing x samosas is C(x) = 100 + 5x rupees.

Express revenue R(x) and profit \Pi(x) as functions of x. (2 marks)
Find the value of x that maximises profit. (3 marks)
What is the maximum profit, and is it a true max (use \Pi'')? (1 mark)



Full solution

(i) R(x) = x(30 - x/10) = 30x - x^2/10. \Pi(x) = R - C = 30x -
x^2/10 - 100 - 5x = 25x - x^2/10 - 100. (ii) \Pi'(x) = 25 - x/5 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 125 samosas. (iii) \Pi(125) = 25 \cdot 125 - 125^2/10 - 100 = 3125 - 1562.5 -
100 = ₹1462.5. \Pi''(x) = -1/5 < 0 ⇒ max. ✓







NCERT Exemplar pointer: Ex 6.5, 6.6. Previous-year pointer: CBSE 2023 Set 1 Q33 (5-mark cone in sphere); CBSE 2022 Q35.





Common confusions

“Critical point = maximum or minimum.” Not always — could be a saddle/inflection (e.g. f(x) = x^3 at 0).
“Max on [a, b] must be at a critical point.” Could also be at an endpoint. Always check f(a), f(b).
“f''(c) = 0 means c is not an extremum.” Wrong — f''(c) =
0 is inconclusive; you must use the first-derivative test.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

Set f'(x) = 0 to find candidates. No critical point, no interior extremum (for a differentiable function on an open interval).
Classify by f'' when you can; by sign-change of f' when f'' goes silent. Cup-positive is min, cap-negative is max, zero is “go back to the first-derivative test.”
On a closed interval, never forget the endpoints. The absolute extremum may live at a or b even when the calculus says “no critical point in (a, b).”



Self-Assessment

Could you set up and solve a fencing or box-volume optimisation in under 10 minutes?
Did you handle 5 of 7 board items unaided?
Can you choose between first- and second-derivative tests appropriately?


3 yes: move on.
2 yes: redo the cone-in-sphere problem.
0–1 yes: practice 5 box-volume problems with fresh dimensions.

title: Ch 6b — Maxima and Minima
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

cbse12.tangents-normals-rates — the derivative as slope, increasing/decreasing.



What this unlocks

cbse12.indefinite-integrals — calculus pivots from \frac{d}{dx} to \int.



NCERT chapter alignment

Class 12, Chapter 6 (NCERT 2023). Sections 6.4–6.5.
Sister synthesis lessons: t4.optimization.
Read alongside the NCERT textbook.



Where this matters

AI/ML. Training a neural network is minimising a loss function. Gradient descent finds critical points; second-order methods use the Hessian (the matrix analogue of f'').


Game development. AI agents pick actions to maximise a reward function. The Bellman equation embeds an \arg\max over choices.


Research. Lagrangian mechanics: physical paths are critical points of an action functional. The whole calculus of variations is multi-variable maxima/minima.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones: Gelfand (problem-first), Bhāskara (verse), Khan Academy (mastery sequence on optimisation).

title: Ch 9 — Differential Equations
text:
Comfortable with substitution and by-parts?
Can you compute \int e^x \, dx and \int 1/x \, dx?
Have you done Ch 7b?

title: Ch 9 — Differential Equations
text:
Touch
A culture of bacteria doubles every hour. If N(t) is the count at time t, the rate of change of N is proportional to N itself: \frac{dN}{dt} = kN.

This is a differential equation — an equation involving an unknown function and its derivative.
To solve: use separation of variables. \frac{dN}{N} = k\, dt \implies \int \frac{dN}{N} = \int k \, dt
\implies \log N = kt + C \implies N(t) = N_0 e^{kt}.
For doubling per hour: N(1) = 2N_0 \Rightarrow e^k = 2 \Rightarrow k =
\log 2.

A differential equation is an integration problem in disguise. The chapter teaches three forms:

Variable-separable — push y to one side, x to the other.
Homogeneous — substitute y = vx.
Linear (first-order) — multiply by an integrating factor.

title: Ch 9 — Differential Equations
text:
Wonder

Why does an indefinite integration give one constant — but the general solution of a first-order ODE has one arbitrary constant while a second-order ODE has two?
Could a function fail to satisfy any differential equation?
The IF for y' + Py = Q is e^{\int P dx}. Why?

title: Ch 9 — Differential Equations
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Solve \dfrac{dy}{dx} = x^2.


Full solution

y = x^3/3 + C.



Problem 2
Solve \dfrac{dy}{dx} = xy.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Separate.



Full solution

\frac{dy}{y} = x \, dx \Rightarrow \log|y| = x^2/2 + C \Rightarrow y =
A e^{x^2/2}.



Problem 3
Solve \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{y}{x}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Homogeneous of degree 0. Try y = vx, so \frac{dy}{dx} = v + x
\frac{dv}{dx}.



Full solution

v + x \frac{dv}{dx} = v \Rightarrow x \frac{dv}{dx} = 0 \Rightarrow v
= C \Rightarrow y = Cx.



Problem 4
Solve \dfrac{dy}{dx} + y = e^x.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Linear with P = 1, Q = e^x. IF = e^{\int 1 dx} = e^x.



Full solution

Multiply by e^x: e^x y' + e^x y = e^{2x}, i.e. \frac{d}{dx}(e^x y)
= e^{2x}. Integrate: e^x y = e^{2x}/2 + C \Rightarrow y = e^x/2 + C
e^{-x}.



Problem 5
Find the order and degree of \left(\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}\right)^3 + 5
\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right) = 0.


Full solution

Order: highest derivative = 2. Degree: power of highest derivative when polynomial = 3.


Malthus’s exponential alarm (1798). In An Essay on the Principle of Population, Thomas Malthus argued that human populations grow geometrically (dN/dt = kN, the same ODE as your bacteria) while food supply grows only arithmetically. His conclusion — recurring famine — turned out wrong, because he hadn’t accounted for the nutrient ceiling that makes the rate k shrink as N grows. In 1838 Pierre Verhulst patched the model with one extra term: dN/dt = kN(1 -
N/K), the logistic equation. That one ODE later went on to model neuron firing, viral spread, and the adoption curve of new technology. Newton’s law of cooling (your case study) and Malthus’s growth law are the same separable equation with opposite signs on k.


Three patterns swallow most of Class 12’s ODEs. Look at dy/dx =
\ldots: if you can split it into “stuff with y” times “stuff with x”, it’s separable. If both sides depend only on the ratio y/x, substitute y = vx — homogeneous. If it has the shape y' + P(x)y =
Q(x), multiply by e^{\int P} — linear. The hard part of CBSE is classifying the equation; once classified, the recipe is mechanical.

title: Ch 9 — Differential Equations
text:
Name

A differential equation (DE) is an equation in x, y, y', y'',
\ldots Its order is the highest derivative; its degree is that derivative’s polynomial power (after clearing radicals/fractions).
General solution. Contains as many arbitrary constants as the order. Particular solution. Constants fixed by initial / boundary conditions.
Three solvable types (Class 12 syllabus):

Variable-separable. \dfrac{dy}{dx} = f(x) g(y) → \int dy/g(y)
= \int f(x) dx.
Homogeneous. \dfrac{dy}{dx} = F(y/x). Let y = vx; reduces to separable in v, x.
Linear of first order. \dfrac{dy}{dx} + P(x) y = Q(x). Integrating factor \text{IF} = e^{\int P \, dx}. Solution: y \cdot \text{IF} = \int Q \cdot \text{IF} \, dx + C.


A verse:

Order: highest derivative. Degree: its polynomial coat. Separable — split sides; integrate; arbitrary constant on the float. Homogeneous — let y = vx, reduce to separable; Linear — IF is e^{\int P}, the trick that makes it tameable.

title: Ch 9 — Differential Equations
text:
Apply

Worked example

Solve x \dfrac{dy}{dx} + 2y = x^2 \log x.

A competent solver:

Standard form: y' + (2/x)y = x \log x. Linear with P = 2/x.
IF = e^{\int 2/x dx} = e^{2 \log x} = x^2.
Multiply: x^2 y' + 2x y = x^3 \log x \Rightarrow \frac{d}{dx}(x^2 y)
= x^3 \log x.
Integrate by parts: \int x^3 \log x \, dx. u = \log x, dv = x^3
dx. = (x^4/4)\log x - x^4/16.
So x^2 y = (x^4/4)\log x - x^4/16 + C \Rightarrow y = (x^2/4) \log
x - x^2/16 + C/x^2.



Practice — Warm
W1. Solve \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \cos x.


Full solution

y = \sin x + C.

W2. Order and degree of \sqrt{1 + (y')^2} = y''.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Square first.



Full solution

1 + (y')^2 = (y'')^2. Order 2, degree 2.

W3. Solve \dfrac{dy}{dx} = -y.


Full solution

y = Ce^{-x}.



Practice — Working
P1. Solve \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{1 + y^2}{1 + x^2}.


Full solution

Separable: \frac{dy}{1 + y^2} = \frac{dx}{1 + x^2} \Rightarrow
\tan^{-1}y = \tan^{-1}x + C.

P2. Solve \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{x + y}{x}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\frac{dy}{dx} = 1 + y/x — homogeneous. Or rearrange to linear.



Full solution

Linear: y' - y/x = 1. IF = e^{-\int dx/x} = 1/x. \frac{d}{dx}(y/x) = 1/x \Rightarrow y/x = \log|x| + C \Rightarrow y =
x \log|x| + Cx.

P3. Solve (1 + x^2) \dfrac{dy}{dx} + 2xy = 4x^2 given y(0) = 0.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Linear. P = 2x/(1+x^2), Q = 4x^2/(1+x^2).



Full solution

IF = e^{\int 2x/(1+x^2) dx} = e^{\log(1+x^2)} = 1 + x^2. \frac{d}{dx}((1+x^2)y) = 4x^2 \Rightarrow (1+x^2)y = 4x^3/3 + C. y(0) = 0 \Rightarrow C = 0. So y = \frac{4x^3}{3(1+x^2)}.



Practice — Stretch
S1. A radioactive substance decays at a rate proportional to its mass. If half decays in 1620 years, find the time required to decay to 1/4 of its original mass.


Full solution

dm/dt = -km, m = m_0 e^{-kt}. Half-life: 1/2 = e^{-1620 k}
\Rightarrow k = \log 2 / 1620. To reach 1/4: 1/4 = e^{-kt}
\Rightarrow t = 2 \cdot 1620 = 3240 years.



Board-Pattern Practice
1-mark MCQ #1. Order and degree of y'' + (y')^3 = 0 are (a) 1, 1 (b) 2, 1 (c) 2, 3 (d) 1, 3.


Full solution

Order 2 (highest derivative), degree 1 (power of y''). (b).

1-mark MCQ #2. IF for y' + 3y = e^x is (a) e^x (b) e^{3x} (c) 3 e^x (d) \log 3 + x.


Full solution

P = 3, IF = e^{3x}. (b).

2-mark. Solve (x + 1) \frac{dy}{dx} = 2 y.


Full solution

\frac{dy}{y} = \frac{2 dx}{x+1} \Rightarrow \log y = 2 \log(x+1) + C
\Rightarrow y = A(x+1)^2.

2-mark. Solve \frac{dy}{dx} = e^{x + y}.


Full solution

e^{-y} dy = e^x dx \Rightarrow -e^{-y} = e^x + C \Rightarrow e^x +
e^{-y} = -C (combine constants).

3-mark. Solve \frac{dy}{dx} + y \tan x = \sin x.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Linear. P = \tan x, IF = e^{\int \tan x \, dx} = \sec x.



Full solution

Multiply by \sec x: \frac{d}{dx}(y \sec x) = \sin x \sec x = \tan x. y \sec x = \log|\sec x| + C \Rightarrow y = \cos x \log|\sec x| + C
\cos x.

3-mark. Solve x \frac{dy}{dx} - y = x^2.


Full solution

y' - y/x = x. Linear. IF = 1/x. \frac{d}{dx}(y/x) = 1 \Rightarrow
y/x = x + C \Rightarrow y = x^2 + Cx.

5-mark long answer. Solve (x^2 + y^2) \, dx - 2xy \, dy = 0 (homogeneous).


Hint 1 — a nudge

\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{x^2 + y^2}{2xy}. Substitute y = vx.



Full solution

\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{1 + v^2}{2v}. With y = vx: v + x v' = (1 +
v^2)/(2v), so x v' = (1 + v^2)/(2v) - v = (1 - v^2)/(2v). Separate: \frac{2v\, dv}{1 - v^2} = \frac{dx}{x}. Integrate: -\log|1
- v^2| = \log|x| + C \Rightarrow \log|1 - v^2| = -\log|x| + C'
\Rightarrow 1 - v^2 = K/x where K = e^{C'}. Substitute v = y/x: 1 - y^2/x^2 = K/x \Rightarrow x^2 - y^2 = Kx.

6-mark Case Study. Newton’s law of cooling.
A coffee cup at 80°C cools in a room at 25°C. After 5 minutes the coffee is at 60°C. Newton’s law: \dfrac{dT}{dt} = -k(T - 25).

Solve the ODE for T(t). (3 marks)
Use T(5) = 60 to find k. (1 mark)
Find T(15). (2 marks)



Full solution

(i) \frac{dT}{T - 25} = -k \, dt \Rightarrow \log(T - 25) = -kt +
C \Rightarrow T = 25 + A e^{-kt}. With T(0) = 80: A = 55. So T =
25 + 55 e^{-kt}. (ii) 60 = 25 + 55 e^{-5k} \Rightarrow e^{-5k} = 35/55 = 7/11
\Rightarrow k = \frac{1}{5}\log(11/7) \approx 0.090. (iii) T(15) = 25 + 55 e^{-15k} = 25 + 55 (7/11)^3 = 25 + 55 \cdot
343/1331 \approx 25 + 14.18 \approx 39.2°C.







NCERT Exemplar pointer: Ex 9.1, 9.2, 9.3. Previous-year pointer: CBSE 2023 Set 1 Q35 (5-mark linear ODE); CBSE 2022 Q34.





Common confusions

“Forgetting the constant of integration.” Always present.
“Linear equations are also separable.” Sometimes — but the IF method works even when separation fails.
“Order = degree.” Different concepts. Order is the highest derivative; degree is its polynomial power.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

An ODE is an integration problem in disguise. Each method (separation, y = vx, integrating factor) reshapes the equation until both sides are integrable.
Classify before you compute. Variable-separable, homogeneous, linear — 30 seconds of inspection saves five minutes of wrong path.
The general solution carries one constant per order. Pin it down with an initial condition to get the particular solution that matches the real-world setup.



Self-Assessment

Could you classify a fresh ODE as separable / homogeneous / linear in 30 seconds?
Did you handle 5 of 7 board items unaided?
Can you derive the IF for any first-order linear ODE?


3 yes: move on.
2 yes: redo the 5-mark homogeneous problem.
0–1 yes: rewrite the three solution forms from memory.

title: Ch 9 — Differential Equations
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

cbse12.integration-techniques — every step is an integration.



What this unlocks

cbse12.vector-algebra.



NCERT chapter alignment

Class 12, Chapter 9 (NCERT 2023). Sections 9.1–9.5.
Sister synthesis lessons: t4.differential-equations, t6.second-order-odes.
Read alongside the NCERT textbook.



Where this matters

Research / engineering. Population dynamics, radioactive decay, RC and RL circuits, drug elimination — all are first-order linear or separable ODEs.


AI/ML. Neural ODEs treat hidden states as continuous-time trajectories — the network learns f in dh/dt = f(h, t).


Game development. Particle physics, spring-mass systems, projectile motion — all simulated by integrating ODEs each frame.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones: Better Explained (“ODE = future from now”), Bhāskara (verse), Gelfand (problem-first).

title: Ch 5b — Differentiability and Differentiation Rules
text:
Can you compute \dfrac{d}{dx}(x^n), \dfrac{d}{dx}\sin x, etc.?
Comfortable with the product rule and quotient rule?
Have you done Ch 5a — Continuity?

title: Ch 5b — Differentiability and Differentiation Rules
text:
Touch
A car’s position s(t) = t^2 at time t. Its velocity at t = 3 is s'(3) = 6. You learnt this last year.

Now imagine the car’s position depends on a control u, which itself depends on time: s = u^2 and u = 5t. Velocity is \frac{ds}{dt}.
Compute it directly: s = (5t)^2 = 25t^2, so s'(t) = 50t.
Compute it via the chain: \frac{ds}{dt} = \frac{ds}{du} \cdot
\frac{du}{dt} = 2u \cdot 5 = 2(5t) \cdot 5 = 50t. Same.

The shortcut — the chain rule — saves you from re-expanding every composition by hand.
This lesson covers the four heavy techniques you need on the paper:

Chain rule for compositions.
Implicit differentiation for relations like x^2 + y^2 = 25.
Logarithmic differentiation for messy products and powers.
Parametric differentiation when x = f(t), y = g(t).

title: Ch 5b — Differentiability and Differentiation Rules
text:
Wonder

Why must the chain rule give the same answer as expanding by hand?
Could implicit differentiation work for y defined only locally — e.g. the upper half of a circle?
For y = x^x, neither the power rule nor the exponential rule applies directly. Why?

title: Ch 5b — Differentiability and Differentiation Rules
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Let y = (3x + 1)^5. Find \dfrac{dy}{dx}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Outer function: u^5. Inner: u = 3x + 1.



Full solution

\frac{dy}{dx} = 5(3x+1)^4 \cdot 3 = 15(3x+1)^4.



Problem 2
Differentiate y = \sin(x^2) with respect to x.


Full solution

\frac{dy}{dx} = \cos(x^2) \cdot 2x = 2x \cos(x^2).



Problem 3
Find \dfrac{dy}{dx} if x^2 + y^2 = 25.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Differentiate both sides w.r.t. x. Treat y as a function of x.



Full solution

2x + 2y \frac{dy}{dx} = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{dy}{dx} = -x/y.



Problem 4
For y = x^x, find \dfrac{dy}{dx}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Take \log of both sides first.



Full solution

\log y = x \log x. Differentiate: \frac{1}{y}\frac{dy}{dx} = \log x
+ 1 \Rightarrow \frac{dy}{dx} = x^x(\log x + 1).



Problem 5
Find \dfrac{dy}{dx} if x = a\cos t,\ y = a\sin t.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\frac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{dy/dt}{dx/dt}.



Full solution

\frac{dx}{dt} = -a\sin t,\ \frac{dy}{dt} = a\cos t. Ratio: -\cot t.


Weierstrass’s monster (1872). Karl Weierstrass shocked the mathematical world by exhibiting a function W(x) = \sum_{n=0}^\infty
a^n \cos(b^n \pi x) (with 0 < a < 1 and ab > 1 + 3\pi/2) that is continuous everywhere but differentiable nowhere. Before 1872, the working belief was that any “reasonable” continuous function had to have a derivative at most points — a corner here or there at worst. Weierstrass’s construction is jagged at every scale: zoom in anywhere and you see the same wild oscillation. The example forced mathematicians to take Bolzano’s epsilon-delta definitions seriously and stopped them from confusing continuous functions with differentiable ones. The next time you write “continuous \not\Rightarrow differentiable,” you are repeating the lesson Weierstrass taught the world.


Differentiation is one rule applied four ways. First-principles gives you the chain rule; the chain rule run implicitly lets you differentiate F(x, y) = 0 without solving for y; run on \log y it tames products and powers; run through a parameter t it handles parametric curves. Four “techniques” in the textbook, but only one underlying move: differentiate both sides, treat y as a function of x, and let the chain rule sort out the rest.

title: Ch 5b — Differentiability and Differentiation Rules
text:
Name

A function f is differentiable at a iff \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(a + h) - f(a)}{h} exists. Differentiability \Rightarrow continuity (not the reverse).
Standard derivatives (memorise):
\frac{d}{dx}\sin^{-1}x = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}, \frac{d}{dx}\cos^{-1}x = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}, \frac{d}{dx}\tan^{-1}x = \frac{1}{1+x^2}, \frac{d}{dx}e^x = e^x, \frac{d}{dx}\log x = \frac{1}{x}.
Chain rule. If y = f(u),\ u = g(x), then \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{dy}{du}\cdot\dfrac{du}{dx}.
Implicit differentiation. Differentiate both sides of an equation in x, y w.r.t. x, treating y as a function of x.
Logarithmic differentiation. For y = f(x)^{g(x)}, take \log first.
Parametric differentiation. If x = f(t),\ y = g(t), then \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{dy/dt}{dx/dt}.

A verse:

Chain — outer derivative, then the inner; Implicit — differentiate both sides, y' left over. Logarithm tames products and powers in disguise; Parameter t as the puppeteer — divide the two streams.

title: Ch 5b — Differentiability and Differentiation Rules
text:
Apply

Worked example

Find \dfrac{dy}{dx} at x = 1 if y = \sqrt{1 + \log x}.

A competent solver:

Outer: u^{1/2}. Inner: u = 1 + \log x.
\frac{dy}{du} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{u}}. \frac{du}{dx} = 1/x.
\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{1 + \log x}} \cdot \frac{1}{x}.
At x = 1: \log 1 = 0, so = \frac{1}{2 \cdot 1 \cdot 1} = 1/2.



Practice — Warm
W1. y = \cos(\sqrt{x}). Find y'.


Full solution

y' = -\sin(\sqrt{x}) \cdot \frac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}.

W2. y = \tan^{-1}(2x). Find y'.


Full solution

y' = \frac{1}{1 + 4x^2} \cdot 2 = \frac{2}{1 + 4x^2}.

W3. Find \frac{dy}{dx} if x^2 - y^2 = 1.


Full solution

2x - 2y\frac{dy}{dx} = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{dy}{dx} = x/y.



Practice — Working
P1. y = (\sin x)^x. Find y'.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Logarithmic differentiation.



Full solution

\log y = x \log(\sin x). \frac{y'}{y} = \log(\sin x) + x \cdot
\frac{\cos x}{\sin x}. So y' = (\sin x)^x [\log(\sin x) + x \cot x].

P2. x = a(t - \sin t),\ y = a(1 - \cos t) (cycloid). Find \frac{dy}{dx}.


Full solution

\frac{dx}{dt} = a(1 - \cos t). \frac{dy}{dt} = a\sin t. \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{\sin t}{1 - \cos t} = \cot(t/2) (using half-angle).

P3. If y = e^{x \cos x}, find y'.


Full solution

y' = e^{x \cos x}(\cos x - x \sin x) (chain rule, with product rule inside).



Practice — Stretch
S1. Show that the function f(x) = |x| is continuous at 0 but not differentiable there.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Compute \lim_{h\to0^+}\frac{|h|}{h} and \lim_{h\to0^-}\frac{|h|}{h}.



Full solution

Continuity: shown last lesson. Differentiability at 0: RHS difference quotient is 1, LHS is -1. They disagree, so derivative DNE. Continuous, not differentiable. This is the canonical example of the implication going one way only.



Board-Pattern Practice
1-mark MCQ #1. \frac{d}{dx}(\sin^{-1}(2x\sqrt{1-x^2})) = (a) \frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} (b) \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} (c) \frac{2}{1+x^2} (d) -\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\sin^{-1}(2x\sqrt{1-x^2}) = 2\sin^{-1} x for |x| \le 1/\sqrt{2}.



Full solution

Differentiate 2\sin^{-1}x: (a) \frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}.

1-mark MCQ #2. If y = a^x, then \frac{dy}{dx} = (a) a^x (b) x a^{x-1} (c) a^x \log a (d) a^x / \log a.


Full solution

(c) a^x \log a.

2-mark. y = \log(\log x). Find \frac{dy}{dx}.


Full solution

Chain: \frac{1}{\log x} \cdot \frac{1}{x} = \frac{1}{x \log x}.

2-mark. x^y \cdot y^x = 1. Find \frac{dy}{dx}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Take log: y \log x + x \log y = 0.



Full solution

Differentiate: y' \log x + y/x + \log y + x \cdot y'/y = 0. y'(\log x + x/y) = -(y/x + \log y). y' = -\frac{y/x + \log y}{\log x + x/y} = -\frac{y(y/x + \log y)}{y
\log x + x}.

3-mark. Differentiate \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{2x}{1 + x^2}\right) with respect to x.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Substitute x = \tan\theta. Then the argument becomes \sin 2\theta.



Full solution

Let x = \tan\theta. \frac{2x}{1+x^2} = \sin 2\theta. So \sin^{-1}(\cdot) = 2\theta = 2\tan^{-1} x (for |x| \le 1). Derivative: \frac{2}{1+x^2}.

3-mark. x = a\cos^3 t,\ y = a\sin^3 t. Find \frac{dy}{dx}.


Full solution

\frac{dx}{dt} = -3a\cos^2 t \sin t. \frac{dy}{dt} = 3a\sin^2 t \cos
t. Ratio: -\tan t.

5-mark long answer. If y = (\tan^{-1}x)^2, prove (1 + x^2)^2 y'' +
2x(1 + x^2)y' = 2.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Compute y' first; multiply both sides by something to clear 1/(1+x^2).



Full solution

y' = 2\tan^{-1}x \cdot \frac{1}{1+x^2}, so (1+x^2)y' = 2\tan^{-1}x. Differentiate: 2x \cdot y' + (1+x^2) y'' = \frac{2}{1+x^2}. Multiply through by (1+x^2): 2x(1+x^2)y' + (1+x^2)^2 y'' = 2. ✓

6-mark Case Study. A bouncing ball.
A ball is dropped, and its height (m) above the ground is h(t) =
\begin{cases} 5 - 5t^2 & 0 \le t \le 1 \\ 5(t-1)^2 & 1 < t \le 2
\end{cases} for t in seconds.

Is h continuous at t = 1? (1 mark)
Is h differentiable at t = 1? Justify. (2 marks)
Compute h'(t) on each branch and interpret what changes physically at t = 1. (3 marks)



Full solution

(i) LHS: 5 - 5 = 0. RHS: 0. ✓ continuous. (ii) LHS derivative: -10t |_{t=1} = -10 (downward velocity). RHS derivative: 10(t-1)|_{t=1} = 0. They disagree — not differentiable at t = 1. (iii) Branch 1: h'(t) = -10t, increasing in magnitude — falling faster. Branch 2: h'(t) = 10(t-1), starts at 0 — the bounce. The kink is the bounce; impact reverses velocity instantaneously, hence the corner.







NCERT Exemplar pointer: Ex 5.2–5.7. Previous-year pointer: CBSE 2023 Set 1 Q24 (3-mark inverse trig); CBSE 2022 Q34 (5-mark proof).





Common confusions

“Continuous = differentiable.” No. |x| at 0 is the counterexample.
“Chain rule = product rule.” Different. Chain rule for compositions, product for products.
“Implicit differentiation only works for circles.” Works for any relation F(x,y) = 0 where y is locally a function of x.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

Differentiable \Rightarrow continuous, never the reverse. |x| is the canonical witness: smooth-looking but with a corner the derivative refuses to cross.
The chain rule is the workhorse. Outer derivative times inner derivative — and it powers implicit, logarithmic, and parametric differentiation as special cases.
Pick the technique by shape. f(x)^{g(x)} → take \log first; F(x, y) = 0 → differentiate both sides; x = f(t),\ y = g(t) → ratio of t-derivatives. The shape of the problem tells you which tool to grab.



Self-Assessment

Could you list the four techniques (chain, implicit, log, parametric) and one example of each?
Did you solve at least 5 of 7 board items unaided?
Can you spot which technique a problem demands within 30 seconds?


3 yes: move on.
2 yes: redo the 5-mark problem.
0–1 yes: practice basic chain rule on 10 fresh examples.

title: Ch 5b — Differentiability and Differentiation Rules
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

cbse12.continuity — differentiable \Rightarrow continuous.
cbse11.derivatives-intro — basic rules.



What this unlocks

cbse12.tangents-normals-rates
cbse12.indefinite-integrals



NCERT chapter alignment

Class 12, Chapter 5 (NCERT 2023). Sections 5.2–5.8.
Sister synthesis lessons: t4.differentiation-rules, t4.derivatives-trig-exp-log.
Read alongside the NCERT textbook.



Where this matters

AI/ML. Backpropagation is the chain rule applied repeatedly through a deep network. Implicit differentiation appears in implicit deep-learning layers (Deep Equilibrium Models).


Game development. Camera follow-cams use parametric paths \vec
r(t) and need \vec r'(t) for tangent direction.


Research. Implicit differentiation handles thermodynamic relations where state variables are interlocked. Logarithmic differentiation appears in elasticity in economics.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones: 3Blue1Brown (“chain rule = function-of-a-function”), Bhāskara (verse), Gelfand (problem-first), AoPS (graded practice).

title: Ch 8 — Application of Integrals (Areas)
text:
Can you compute a definite integral of a polynomial?
Comfortable sketching y = x^2, y = \sqrt{x}, y = \sin x?
Have you done Ch 7c?

title: Ch 8 — Application of Integrals (Areas)
text:
Touch
The region under y = x from x = 0 to x = 4 is a triangle. Its area by elementary geometry is \frac{1}{2}\cdot 4\cdot 4 = 8.

Calculus gives the same: \int_0^4 x \, dx = [x^2/2]_0^4 = 8.
Now consider the region between y = x and y = x^2 from x = 0 to x = 1. Sketch it: y = x^2 is below y = x on [0, 1]. The “strip” at column x has height x - x^2.
Area = \int_0^1 (x - x^2) \, dx = [x^2/2 - x^3/3]_0^1 = 1/2 - 1/3 =
1/6.

The recipe is: 1. Sketch. 2. Identify which curve is upper, which is lower. 3. Find the limits where they meet. 4. Integrate (\text{upper} - \text{lower}).

title: Ch 8 — Application of Integrals (Areas)
text:
Wonder

What if the curves cross more than once on the interval?
What if the upper curve is on the left and the lower on the right — could you integrate w.r.t. y instead?
Does \int_a^b f(x) dx ever give a negative “area”?

title: Ch 8 — Application of Integrals (Areas)
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Find the area under y = x^2 from x = 0 to x = 3.


Full solution

\int_0^3 x^2 dx = 9.



Problem 2
Find the area under y = \sin x from x = 0 to x = \pi.


Full solution

\int_0^\pi \sin x dx = [-\cos x]_0^\pi = 2.



Problem 3
Find the area between y = x and y = x^3 from x = 0 to x = 1.


Hint 1 — a nudge

On [0, 1], which is bigger: x or x^3?



Full solution

x \ge x^3 on [0,1]. Area = \int_0^1 (x - x^3) dx = 1/2 - 1/4 =
1/4.



Problem 4
Find the area enclosed by y^2 = 4x and x = 4 (parabola and vertical line).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Easier to integrate w.r.t. y. x = y^2/4. Range of y at x = 4: y^2 = 16 \Rightarrow y = \pm 4.



Full solution

Area = \int_{-4}^4 (4 - y^2/4) dy = 2\int_0^4 (4 - y^2/4) dy = 2[4y -
y^3/12]_0^4 = 2(16 - 64/12) = 2(16 - 16/3) = 64/3.



Problem 5
Find the area of the region bounded by y = x^2 and y = x.


Full solution

Intersect: x^2 = x \Rightarrow x = 0, 1. On [0,1], x \ge x^2. Area = \int_0^1 (x - x^2) dx = 1/6.


Archimedes squares the parabola (~240 BCE). Two thousand years before Newton, Archimedes proved in Quadrature of the Parabola that the area enclosed between a parabola and a chord is exactly \tfrac{4}{3} of the inscribed triangle’s area. He used the method of exhaustion: fit a triangle, then fit smaller triangles into the leftover slivers, then smaller still — the total area is T + T/4 + T/16 + \cdots, a geometric series summing to \tfrac{4}{3} T. Run the same parabola through your \int_a^b recipe today and the answer drops out in two lines. Archimedes had to prove his answer was right; you only have to apply the Fundamental Theorem.


A region is a tower of strips. Every “area between curves” problem reduces to one rule: at each x, the region’s height is (\text{upper}) - (\text{lower}); integrate the height. The whole chapter is bookkeeping — which curve is upper on which sub-interval, and where they cross. Sketch first, integrate second; never the other way around.

title: Ch 8 — Application of Integrals (Areas)
text:
Name

Area under y = f(x), f \ge 0, on [a, b]: A = \int_a^b f(x) \, dx.
Area between y = f(x) and y = g(x), with f \ge g on [a, b]: A = \int_a^b [f(x) - g(x)] \, dx.
If the curves cross at c inside [a, b], split and use absolute values: A = \int_a^c |f - g| \, dx + \int_c^b |f - g| \, dx.
Integration w.r.t. y: when curves are functions of y, write x
= F(y) and integrate over [c, d].

A verse:

Sketch first — the picture decides the answer; Upper minus lower across the interval — that’s the dancer. Cross-over? Split and take absolute values, lest the signs deceive; Integrate y when the curves prefer to be solved that way — believe.

title: Ch 8 — Application of Integrals (Areas)
text:
Apply

Worked example

Find the area of the region bounded by y^2 = x and y = x.

A competent solver:

Intersect: y = x and y^2 = x. Substitute: y^2 = y \Rightarrow
y = 0, 1. Points: (0, 0), (1, 1).
On [0, 1] the parabola y = \sqrt x lies above the line y = x (check x = 1/4: \sqrt{1/4} = 1/2 > 1/4 ✓).
Area = \int_0^1 (\sqrt x - x) dx = [2x^{3/2}/3 - x^2/2]_0^1 = 2/3 -
1/2 = 1/6.



Practice — Warm
W1. Area under y = 4 - x^2 from x = -2 to x = 2.


Full solution

\int_{-2}^2 (4 - x^2) dx = 2\int_0^2 (4 - x^2) dx = 2[4x - x^3/3]_0^2
= 2(8 - 8/3) = 32/3.

W2. Area enclosed by y = x^2 and y = 4.


Full solution

Intersect: x^2 = 4 \Rightarrow x = \pm 2. Area = \int_{-2}^2 (4 -
x^2) dx = 32/3 (from W1).

W3. Area under y = e^x from x = 0 to x = 1.


Full solution

[e^x]_0^1 = e - 1.



Practice — Working
P1. Area enclosed by y^2 = 8x and y = 2x.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Solve for intersections.



Full solution

Substitute y = 2x into y^2 = 8x: 4x^2 = 8x \Rightarrow x = 0, 2. Points (0, 0), (2, 4). On [0, 2], parabola above line: \sqrt{8x} \ge 2x for 0 \le x \le
2. Area = \int_0^2 (\sqrt{8x} - 2x) dx = \int_0^2 (2\sqrt 2 \sqrt x -
2x) dx = 2\sqrt 2 \cdot \frac{2}{3} x^{3/2} \Big|_0^2 - x^2 \Big|_0^2 =
2\sqrt 2 \cdot \frac{2}{3} \cdot 2\sqrt 2 - 4 = \frac{16}{3} - 4 =
\frac{4}{3}.

P2. Area of the region bounded by y = |x| and y = 1.


Hint 1 — a nudge

y = |x| is a V; y = 1 a horizontal line.



Full solution

The region is a triangle with vertices (-1, 1), (1, 1), (0, 0). Area = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2 \cdot 1 = 1. By calculus: \int_{-1}^1 (1 -
|x|) dx = 2 \int_0^1 (1 - x) dx = 1. ✓

P3. Area enclosed by x = 1, x = 4, y = 0, y = \sqrt x.


Full solution

\int_1^4 \sqrt x \, dx = [2x^{3/2}/3]_1^4 = (16 - 2)/3 = 14/3.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Find the area enclosed by the ellipse x^2/9 + y^2/4 = 1.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Solve for y: y = \pm \frac{2}{3}\sqrt{9 - x^2}. Use the quarter-area formula with x = 3\sin\theta.



Full solution

Area = 4 \int_0^3 \frac{2}{3}\sqrt{9 - x^2}\, dx = \frac{8}{3} \cdot
\frac{1}{4}\pi(3)^2 = \frac{8}{3} \cdot \frac{9\pi}{4} = 6\pi. Total area = \pi a b = \pi \cdot 3 \cdot 2 = 6\pi. ✓



Board-Pattern Practice
1-mark MCQ #1. Area under y = \cos x from 0 to \pi/2 is (a) 0 (b) 1 (c) \pi/2 (d) 2.


Full solution

[\sin x]_0^{\pi/2} = 1. (b).

1-mark MCQ #2. Area enclosed by y^2 = x and x = 1 is (a) 2/3 (b) 4/3 (c) 1 (d) 1/2.


Full solution

2 \int_0^1 \sqrt x \, dx = 2 \cdot 2/3 = 4/3. (b).

2-mark. Area under y = x^2 + 1 from -1 to 1.


Full solution

\int_{-1}^1 (x^2 + 1) dx = 2 \int_0^1 (x^2 + 1) dx = 2(1/3 + 1) = 8/3.

2-mark. Area of the circle x^2 + y^2 = 16.


Full solution

\pi r^2 = 16\pi. (Or compute 4 \int_0^4 \sqrt{16 - x^2} dx = 4 \cdot
4\pi = 16\pi.)

3-mark. Area enclosed by y = x^2 and y = 2x.


Full solution

Intersect: x^2 = 2x \Rightarrow x = 0, 2. On [0, 2], 2x \ge x^2. Area = \int_0^2 (2x - x^2) dx = [x^2 - x^3/3]_0^2 = 4 - 8/3 = 4/3.

3-mark. Area enclosed by y = \sin x, y = \cos x, between x = 0 and x = \pi/2.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Curves meet at x = \pi/4. Split.



Full solution

On [0, \pi/4]: \cos x \ge \sin x. On [\pi/4, \pi/2]: \sin x \ge
\cos x. A = \int_0^{\pi/4} (\cos - \sin) dx + \int_{\pi/4}^{\pi/2} (\sin -
\cos) dx. = [\sin + \cos]_0^{\pi/4} + [-\cos - \sin]_{\pi/4}^{\pi/2} = (\sqrt 2 - 1) + (-1 - (-\sqrt 2)) = \sqrt 2 - 1 + \sqrt 2 - 1 = 2\sqrt 2 - 2.

5-mark long answer. Find the area enclosed by y = x^2 + 2, y =
x, x = 0, x = 3.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Check sign: for x \in [0, 3], x^2 + 2 \ge x.



Full solution

x^2 + 2 - x \ge 0 for all real x (discriminant negative). So A = \int_0^3 (x^2 + 2 - x) dx = [x^3/3 + 2x - x^2/2]_0^3 = 9 + 6 - 4.5
= 10.5 sq units.

6-mark Case Study. A garden of two grass varieties.
A landlord has a region bounded by y = \sqrt x (variety A) and y = x
/2 (variety B), for x \in [0, 4].

Sketch the two curves and find their intersection. (2 marks)
Find the area where A grows above B. (3 marks)
If A costs ₹50 / sq.m and B costs ₹30 / sq.m, what is the cost saved if A’s area is replaced with B? (1 mark)



Full solution

(i) \sqrt x = x/2 \Rightarrow x = x^2/4 \Rightarrow x(x - 4) = 0
\Rightarrow x = 0, 4. (ii) On [0, 4]: \sqrt x \ge x/2 (check x = 1: 1 \ge 0.5 ✓). A = \int_0^4 (\sqrt x - x/2) dx = [2x^{3/2}/3 - x^2/4]_0^4 = 16/3 - 4
= 4/3 sq m. (iii) Saving = (50 - 30) \cdot 4/3 = 80/3 \approx ₹26.67.







NCERT Exemplar pointer: Ex 8.1, 8.2. Previous-year pointer: CBSE 2023 Set 1 Q32 (5-mark area between curves); CBSE 2022 Q33.





Common confusions

“\int_a^b f \, dx always gives positive area.” Only when f
\ge 0 on [a, b]. Otherwise it’s signed area.
“Forgetting to find intersection points.” The integration limits are the intersections — skip them and you integrate over the wrong interval.
“Sketching is optional.” It is not. Without a sketch, you cannot know which curve is upper.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

Area = integral of (upper − lower). With a sketch in hand, every problem becomes a single definite integral.
Find the intersections first; they are your limits. Solve f(x) = g(x) before you integrate — those roots set a and b.
Switch axes when it’s easier. If a curve is naturally x = F(y) (sideways parabolas, lying ellipses), integrate w.r.t. y instead; you’ll save a square root.



Self-Assessment

Could you set up an area problem with a sketch in 5 minutes?
Did you handle 5 of 7 board items unaided?
Can you choose between integrating w.r.t. x vs. y smoothly?


3 yes: move on.
2 yes: redo the 5-mark problem with a fresh sketch.
0–1 yes: sketch each Working problem from scratch.

title: Ch 8 — Application of Integrals (Areas)
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

cbse12.definite-integrals-properties — FTC and properties.



What this unlocks

cbse12.differential-equations



NCERT chapter alignment

Class 12, Chapter 8 (NCERT 2023). Sections 8.1–8.2.
Sister synthesis lessons: t4.integration-applications.
Read alongside the NCERT textbook.



Where this matters

Game development. Pixel-shading and texture mapping integrate contributions over a region; collision-detection algorithms compute the area of intersection of two shapes.


Research / engineering. Cumulative distribution functions are areas; consumer surplus in economics is the area between demand and price curves.


AI/ML. ROC-AUC is the area under the ROC curve — a definite integral over [0, 1].








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones: Better Explained (“integral as accumulated area”), Bhāskara (verse), AoPS (sketch-then-integrate).

title: Ch 10a — Vector Algebra
text:
Comfortable with (x, y, z) coordinates in 3D?
Can you compute the distance between two points in 3D?
Have you done Class 11 · Ch 11 — 3D geometry intro?

title: Ch 10a — Vector Algebra
text:
Touch
A force pushes a box. The push has magnitude (how hard) and direction (which way). A pure number can’t express both. So we need vectors.

A vector \vec a = a_1 \hat\imath + a_2 \hat\jmath + a_3 \hat k — three numbers, in 3D — encodes a directed quantity. We picture it as an arrow from one point to another.







Concept
Notation




Vector
\vec a, \vec b


Magnitude
|\vec a| = \sqrt{a_1^2 + a_2^2 + a_3^2}


Unit vector
\hat a = \vec a / |\vec a|


Position vector of P = (x, y, z)
\vec{OP} = x\hat\imath + y
\hat\jmath + z\hat k




Two vectors add by attaching tail-to-tip — equivalently, by adding components: (a_1, a_2, a_3) + (b_1, b_2, b_3) = (a_1+b_1, a_2+b_2,
a_3+b_3).
This lesson covers vector arithmetic — addition, scalar multiplication, position vectors, direction cosines, and unit vectors. Dot and cross products come next.

title: Ch 10a — Vector Algebra
text:
Wonder

If two vectors point in the same direction, what does \vec a +
\vec b look like?
The vector from A to B is \vec{OB} - \vec{OA}. Why?
A unit vector has magnitude 1. Why divide by |\vec a| to get one?
Direction cosines (l, m, n) satisfy l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1. Why exactly that constraint?

title: Ch 10a — Vector Algebra
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Find the magnitude of \vec a = 2\hat\imath - 3\hat\jmath + 6\hat k.


Full solution

|\vec a| = \sqrt{4 + 9 + 36} = \sqrt{49} = 7.



Problem 2
Find the unit vector in the direction of \vec a from Problem 1.


Full solution

\hat a = \vec a / 7 = (2\hat\imath - 3\hat\jmath + 6\hat k)/7.



Problem 3
If \vec a = \hat\imath + 2\hat\jmath and \vec b = -\hat\imath +
3\hat\jmath, find 2\vec a - \vec b.


Full solution

2\vec a = 2\hat\imath + 4\hat\jmath. 2\vec a - \vec b = (2 - (-1))
\hat\imath + (4 - 3)\hat\jmath = 3\hat\imath + \hat\jmath.



Problem 4
P = (1, 2, 3),\ Q = (4, 6, 8). Find \vec{PQ}.


Full solution

\vec{PQ} = \vec{OQ} - \vec{OP} = 3\hat\imath + 4\hat\jmath + 5\hat k. |\vec{PQ}| = \sqrt{9 + 16 + 25} = \sqrt{50} = 5\sqrt 2.



Problem 5
Find direction cosines of \vec a = \hat\imath + \hat\jmath + \hat k.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Direction cosines (l, m, n) = (a_1, a_2, a_3)/|\vec a|.



Full solution

|\vec a| = \sqrt 3. (l, m, n) = (1/\sqrt 3, 1/\sqrt 3, 1/\sqrt 3). l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1. ✓


Gibbs and Heaviside split the quaternion (1880s). William Rowan Hamilton spent two decades after his 1843 quaternion epiphany insisting that \mathbf{i}, \mathbf{j}, \mathbf{k} obey \mathbf{i}^2 =
\mathbf{j}^2 = \mathbf{k}^2 = \mathbf{ijk} = -1 and that physicists should learn the whole 4-component algebra. Two engineers — Josiah Willard Gibbs at Yale and Oliver Heaviside in England — refused. Working independently in the 1880s, each pulled out only the vector part of a quaternion product, kept the symbols \hat\imath, \hat\jmath,
\hat k, and split Hamilton’s single product into two: a scalar (dot) and a vector (cross). It was a bitter feud while it lasted; Gibbs and Heaviside won. The notation you use in this lesson is theirs, not Hamilton’s.


A vector is two things at once. Geometrically, an arrow with length and direction. Algebraically, a triple (a_1, a_2, a_3) you can add component-wise. The whole power of vector algebra comes from sliding between the two views: pick whichever makes the problem easy. Magnitude and direction cosines are how we measure the arrow; components are how we compute with it.

title: Ch 10a — Vector Algebra
text:
Name

A vector in 3D is \vec a = a_1 \hat\imath + a_2 \hat\jmath + a_3
\hat k, where \hat\imath, \hat\jmath, \hat k are unit vectors along the axes.

Magnitude: |\vec a| = \sqrt{a_1^2 + a_2^2 + a_3^2}.
Unit vector: \hat a = \vec a / |\vec a|.
Position vector of point P = (x, y, z): \vec{OP} = x\hat\imath
+ y\hat\jmath + z\hat k.
Vector from A to B: \vec{AB} = \vec{OB} - \vec{OA}.
Direction cosines (l, m, n): cosines of angles with the three axes; (l, m, n) = (a_1, a_2, a_3)/|\vec a|. They satisfy l^2 + m^2
+ n^2 = 1.
Direction ratios (a_1, a_2, a_3): any scalar multiple of (l,
m, n).

Section formula. If \vec r divides the segment from \vec a to \vec b in ratio m : n (internal), then \vec r = \frac{m\vec b + n\vec a}{m + n}.

A verse:

Three components, one arrow — that’s a vector; Magnitude under root, direction by division — these are inspectors. Position \vec{OP} from origin to point; \vec{AB} = \vec{OB} - \vec{OA} — never disjoint.

title: Ch 10a — Vector Algebra
text:
Apply

Worked example

Find the position vector of the point dividing A(1, 2, 3) and B(4,
5, 6) in ratio 2:3 internally.

A competent solver:

\vec a = \hat\imath + 2\hat\jmath + 3\hat k, \vec b = 4\hat\imath
+ 5\hat\jmath + 6\hat k.
\vec r = \frac{2\vec b + 3\vec a}{2 + 3} = \frac{2\vec b + 3\vec
a}{5}.
Components: ((2\cdot4 + 3\cdot1)/5, (2\cdot5 + 3\cdot2)/5,
(2\cdot6 + 3\cdot3)/5) = (11/5, 16/5, 21/5).



Practice — Warm
W1. Magnitude of 3\hat\imath - 4\hat\jmath.


Full solution

\sqrt{9 + 16} = 5.

W2. Unit vector parallel to \hat\imath + \hat\jmath.


Full solution

(\hat\imath + \hat\jmath)/\sqrt 2.

W3. \vec a = 2\hat\imath + \hat\jmath, \vec b = \hat\imath -
\hat\jmath. Find \vec a + 3\vec b.


Full solution

5\hat\imath - 2\hat\jmath.



Practice — Working
P1. Find the unit vector along \vec{AB} where A = (1, 0, 1), B =
(3, 4, 5).


Full solution

\vec{AB} = 2\hat\imath + 4\hat\jmath + 4\hat k. |\vec{AB}| =
\sqrt{4 + 16 + 16} = 6. \hat{AB} = (\hat\imath + 2\hat\jmath + 2\hat
k)/3.

P2. Direction cosines of the line joining (1, -1, 2) and (3, 2,
1).


Full solution

DR = (2, 3, -1). Magnitude \sqrt{4 + 9 + 1} = \sqrt{14}. DC = (2/\sqrt{14}, 3/\sqrt{14}, -1/\sqrt{14}).

P3. Show that the points (1, 2, 3), (-2, 3, 5), (7, 0, -1) are collinear.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Compare direction ratios of \vec{AB} and \vec{AC}.



Full solution

\vec{AB} = (-3, 1, 2). \vec{AC} = (6, -2, -4) = -2 \vec{AB}. Parallel and share point A, so collinear. ✓



Practice — Stretch
S1. Find \lambda such that \vec a = 2\hat\imath + \lambda
\hat\jmath + \hat k has magnitude \sqrt{14}.


Full solution

4 + \lambda^2 + 1 = 14 \Rightarrow \lambda^2 = 9 \Rightarrow \lambda =
\pm 3.



Board-Pattern Practice
1-mark MCQ #1. Magnitude of \hat\imath - \hat\jmath + \hat k is (a) 1 (b) \sqrt 2 (c) \sqrt 3 (d) 3.


Full solution

\sqrt{1+1+1} = \sqrt 3. (c).

1-mark MCQ #2. If \hat a is a unit vector along \vec a = 3
\hat\imath + 4\hat\jmath, then \hat a = (a) (3, 4, 0) (b) (3/5,
4/5, 0) (c) (3/5, 4/5) (d) (1, 1).


Full solution

|\vec a| = 5. (b).

2-mark. If A = (1, 2, 3), B = (4, 5, 7), find |\vec{AB}|.


Full solution

\vec{AB} = (3, 3, 4). |\vec{AB}| = \sqrt{9 + 9 + 16} = \sqrt{34}.

2-mark. Find \vec a if it has DR proportional to 1, 2, 3 and magnitude \sqrt{14}.


Full solution

\vec a = (1, 2, 3). |\vec a| = \sqrt{14}. ✓

3-mark. If \vec a = 2\hat\imath + \hat\jmath + 2\hat k, \vec b =
4\hat\imath - \hat\jmath + 3\hat k, find a unit vector parallel to \vec a + \vec b.


Full solution

\vec a + \vec b = 6\hat\imath + 0\hat\jmath + 5\hat k. |.| = \sqrt{61}. \hat{u} = (6, 0, 5)/\sqrt{61}.

3-mark. Find the position vector of the midpoint of segment joining \vec a = 2\hat\imath - \hat\jmath and \vec b = 4\hat\imath + 3
\hat\jmath - 2\hat k.


Full solution

(\vec a + \vec b)/2 = 3\hat\imath + \hat\jmath - \hat k.

5-mark long answer. ABCD is a parallelogram with A = (1, 2, 3), B
= (3, 5, 7), C = (5, 7, 9). Find position vector of D, and verify \vec{AB} = \vec{DC}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

In a parallelogram, diagonals bisect each other; or \vec{AD} =
\vec{BC}.



Full solution

\vec{BC} = (2, 2, 2). D = A + \vec{BC} = (1, 2, 3) + (2, 2, 2) = (3,
4, 5). \vec{AB} = (2, 3, 4). \vec{DC} = C - D = (2, 3, 4). ✓

6-mark Case Study. Drone navigating a building.
A drone is at position \vec p = 5\hat\imath + 2\hat\jmath + 8\hat k (metres) and needs to fly to \vec q = 9\hat\imath - \hat\jmath + 4\hat
k.
