Equivalently: \partial f / \partial x = 3. Directional derivative in the x-direction = \partial f / \partial x.
Insight: \nabla f is the generalised slope — it tells you the rate of change in every direction via dot product.



Problem 4
A function f(x, y) has critical points where \nabla f = 0. Find them for f(x, y) = x^2 - 2x + y^2 - 4y + 5.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\partial f / \partial x = 2x - 2 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 1. \partial f / \partial y = 2y - 4 =
0 \Rightarrow y = 2.



Full solution

Critical point (1, 2). Value f(1, 2) = 1 - 2 + 4 - 8 + 5 = 0. (A minimum — f is a sum of squared distances.)
To classify: 2nd-derivative test (see Stretch S1).



Problem 5
The chain rule in multiple variables: if z = f(x, y) and x =
x(t), y = y(t), then \dfrac{dz}{dt} = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x} \dfrac{dx}{dt} + \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y} \dfrac{dy}{dt} = \nabla f \cdot \mathbf{v}.
Apply: f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2, x(t) = \cos t, y(t) = \sin t. Compute dz/dt along the unit circle.


Full solution

\nabla f = (2x, 2y) = (2 \cos t, 2 \sin t). \mathbf{v} = (-\sin t,
\cos t).
dz/dt = -2 \cos t \sin t + 2 \sin t \cos t = 0.
z = 0 along the curve — moving along the unit circle keeps f =
1 constant (the unit circle is a level curve). The gradient is perpendicular to level curves.


Euler and Lagrange were doing partial differentiation fluently by the mid-1700s — Euler’s Institutiones Calculi Differentialis (1755) is full of \partial-style manipulations, and Lagrange’s mechanics (1788) made them indispensable. But the modern notation \partial f / \partial x, with the rounded “curly d” deliberately distinguished from the straight d of ordinary derivatives, was popularised by Adrien-Marie Legendre in 1786 and made standard by Carl Jacobi in the 1840s. Cauchy gave the rigorous limit-based definition you would meet today; without his cleanup, multivariable calculus had been a very informal art.


The gradient is a single vector that knows your function’s slope in every direction at once — dot it with a unit vector and you read off the slope along that line. It points where the function climbs fastest, and it sits perpendicular to wherever the function is flat. That is why “follow the negative gradient” is the universal instruction for downhill.

title: 05 · Partial Derivatives and the Gradient
text:
Name

Partial derivative of f(x, y, \ldots) with respect to x_i: treat all other variables as constant; differentiate with respect to x_i. Notation: \partial f / \partial x_i or f_{x_i}.
Gradient (in n variables): \nabla f = (\partial f / \partial x_1, \ldots,
\partial f / \partial x_n).
Directional derivative in direction u (unit vector): D_u f =
\nabla f \cdot u.
Properties:

Gradient points in direction of steepest ascent.
Magnitude |\nabla f| = rate of steepest ascent.
Gradient is perpendicular to level sets (f = \text{const}).
\nabla f = 0 at critical points (extrema or saddle points).

Multivariable chain rule (parametric path): \dfrac{d}{dt} f(\mathbf{r}(t))
= \nabla f \cdot \mathbf{r}'(t).
Hessian matrix H_{ij} = \partial^2 f / \partial x_i \partial x_j — second derivatives. Used in 2nd-derivative test for extrema.


Partial derivatives differentiate with one variable, others fixed; Gradient = vector of all partials = direction of steepest ascent; Setting gradient to zero finds critical points.

title: 05 · Partial Derivatives and the Gradient
text:
Apply

Worked example

Find the gradient of f(x, y, z) = x^2 y + e^z at (1, 2, 0).

\partial f / \partial x = 2xy = 4. \partial f / \partial y = x^2 = 1. \partial f / \partial z = e^z =
1.
\nabla f = (4, 1, 1) at (1, 2, 0). Direction of steepest ascent; magnitude \sqrt{16 + 1 + 1} = \sqrt{18}.


Practice — Warm
W1. \partial / \partial x [x^2 + y^2] = ?


Full solution

2x.

W2. \nabla (xy)?


Full solution

(y, x).

W3. \partial / \partial y [\sin x + \cos y] = ?


Full solution

-\sin y.



Practice — Working
P1. Critical points of f(x, y) = x^2 + xy + y^2.


Full solution

\partial f / \partial x = 2x + y = 0. \partial f / \partial y = x + 2y = 0. Solve: x = y = 0. Single critical point at origin (a minimum).

P2. \nabla (x^2 + 2xy + y^3) at (1, 1).


Full solution

(2x + 2y, 2x + 3y^2) = (4, 5).

P3. Directional derivative of f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2 at (1, 1) in direction u = (1/\sqrt{2}, 1/\sqrt{2}).


Full solution

\nabla f = (2, 2). D_u f = (2, 2) \cdot (1/\sqrt{2}, 1/\sqrt{2}) =
2/\sqrt{2} + 2/\sqrt{2} = 2 \sqrt{2}.
This is the maximum directional derivative (since u points along \nabla f): max rate |\nabla f| = 2 \sqrt{2} ✓.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Second derivative test in 2D. At a critical point, let D = f_{xx} f_{yy} - f_{xy}^2 (the Hessian determinant). Then:

D > 0 and f_{xx} > 0: local minimum.
D > 0 and f_{xx} < 0: local maximum.
D < 0: saddle point (looks like a saddle).
D = 0: inconclusive.

Classify the critical point of f(x, y) = x^2 - y^2 at (0, 0).


Full solution

f_{xx} = 2, f_{yy} = -2, f_{xy} = 0. D = -4 < 0. Saddle.
Picture: along x-axis, f increases (concave up); along y-axis, f decreases (concave down). The origin is a “pass” — minimum in one direction, maximum in another.
Saddle points are critical to ML training: most “stuck” points in high-dim loss landscapes are saddles, not minima. Modern optimisers have to escape them.

S2. Gradient descent. ML training updates weights via w
\leftarrow w - \eta \nabla L(w) — move opposite the gradient (toward steepest descent). Why does this find minima?


Full solution

Each step decreases L (locally). The gradient points uphill; its negative points downhill. With small enough step size \eta, L strictly decreases each iteration. Convergence to a critical point (local min, saddle, or boundary) is guaranteed under mild conditions.
This is the single most important algorithm in modern ML. Trillions of gradient steps train every modern neural network. The gradient you just learned is the central computational object of the AI era.



Common confusions

“\partial / \partial x and d/dx are the same.” Subtle but distinct. d/dx is total derivative (single variable); \partial / \partial x is partial (other variables held fixed).
“Gradient is a number.” Gradient is a vector (one entry per input variable).
“Critical point = extremum.” Critical points include extrema, saddles, and inflection points.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A partial derivative is just a derivative with the other variables frozen — same one-variable rules, applied one axis at a time.
The gradient is the vector of all partials, and it points in the direction of steepest ascent with magnitude equal to that maximum rate.
Setting \nabla f = 0 finds critical points — the candidates for maxima, minima, and saddles, and the stopping condition for gradient descent.



Self-Assessment

Could you compute \nabla f for f(x, y) = x^2 - y^2?
Without scrolling back, what’s the geometric meaning of \nabla f?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: (2x, -2y); direction of steepest ascent.)

3 yes: ready for 06 · Multiple Integrals.
2 yes: redo S2 (gradient descent).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P5.

title: 05 · Partial Derivatives and the Gradient
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

T4 L04 — Differentiation Rules
T4 L13 — Vectors



What this unlocks

06 · Multiple Integrals
07 · Vector Fields



Where this matters

AI / Machine Learning. Gradient descent (S2) — the central algorithm. Backpropagation computes gradients efficiently through many layers.


Physics & engineering. Gradient of potential energy = force (reversed sign). Gradient of pressure drives flow in fluids.


Mathematical research. Multivariable calculus is the entry to analysis on manifolds, differential geometry, calculus of variations.

title: Ch 5 — Ratio and Proportion
text:
Are common fractions automatic? See kar.common-fractions.
Can you reduce \dfrac{12}{18} to \dfrac{2}{3} by cancelling GCD?
Are you comfortable saying “the ratio of A to B is 3 to 5” and understanding what that means about quantities?
Can you cross-multiply: \dfrac{a}{b} = \dfrac{c}{d}
\Leftrightarrow a d = b c?

If three yeses, proceed.

title: Ch 5 — Ratio and Proportion
Ch 5 — Ratio and Proportion
text:
Wonder

The ratios 2 : 3 and 4 : 6 are equal. Why is “=” the right symbol — what does it mean for two ratios to be equal?
If a : b = c : d, then a d = b c. Why does this cross-multiplication identity hold?
Direct proportion: doubling one quantity doubles the other. Inverse proportion: doubling one halves the other. Examples of each?
The classical rule of three: given three of a, b, c, d in the proportion a : b = c : d, find the fourth. How does this work?







TipWhat you might notice






Two ratios are equal if their fractions reduce to the same lowest-terms form. (2) Cross-multiplication is just clearing denominators. (3) Direct: speed and time at fixed distance. No wait — speed and time are inversely proportional (faster ⇒ less time). Direct: distance and time at fixed speed. (4) Set up \dfrac{a}{b} = \dfrac{c}{d}, cross-multiply, solve for the unknown.

title: Ch 5 — Ratio and Proportion
text:
Discover

Problem 1 — Ratio as fraction
Show that the ratio a : b behaves identically to the fraction \dfrac{a}{b} for the purposes of arithmetic operations.


Full solution

A ratio a : b encodes the same information as \dfrac{a}{b} — the relative size of a to b. Equivalent ratios obey the same cancellation as equivalent fractions: a : b = (a k) : (b k) for k \ne 0. The four operations on ratios mirror those on fractions.
The difference is only conceptual: a ratio preserves which quantity is which, useful for applied problems. (Arithmetic §137.)



Problem 2 — Equality of ratios (proportion)
A proportion is an equality of two ratios: a : b = c : d. Show this is equivalent to a d = b c.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\dfrac{a}{b} = \dfrac{c}{d}, multiply both sides by bd.



Full solution

\dfrac{a}{b} = \dfrac{c}{d} \Rightarrow a d = b c (clearing denominators).
Conversely, a d = b c with b, d \ne 0 gives \dfrac{a}{b} =
\dfrac{c}{d}. QED. (Arithmetic §139 — the fundamental property of proportions, also called cross-multiplication.)



Problem 3 — Mean and extremes
In the proportion a : b = c : d: a, d are called the extremes and b, c the means. Show: product of extremes = product of means.


Full solution

a d = b c from Problem 2. The “extremes” are at the ends of the proportion when written a : b = c : d; the “means” are in the middle. Their products are equal. (Arithmetic §140.)



Problem 4 — Direct proportion
Two quantities x, y are in direct proportion if their ratio x : y is constant: \dfrac{x}{y} = k for some constant k. Examples: cost vs quantity at fixed unit price; distance vs time at fixed speed.


Full solution

If the unit price is ₹50, then for x kg the cost y = 50 x. Ratio y : x = 50 : 1, constant. (Arithmetic §145.)
y = k x is the standard form of a direct-proportion relation.



Problem 5 — Inverse proportion
Two quantities x, y are in inverse proportion if their product is constant: x y = k. Examples: speed vs time at fixed distance; number of workers vs days to complete a fixed job.


Full solution

A trip of 300 km: speed \times time = 300, constant. If speed is 60 km/h, time is 5 h. If speed is 100 km/h, time is 3 h.
x y = k, equivalently y = k/x. (Arithmetic §147.)



Problem 6 — The rule of three
Given a proportion a : b = c : d with three knowns and one unknown, solve for the unknown.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Cross-multiply: a d = b c. Solve for the unknown.



Full solution

If a, b, c known and d unknown: d = \dfrac{b c}{a}.
Example: “If 5 workers take 12 days for a job, how long for 8 workers?” Inverse proportion: 5 \cdot 12 = 8 \cdot d, so d = \dfrac{60}{8} = 7.5 days.
The rule of three has been the workhorse algorithm of applied arithmetic for two millennia. (Arithmetic §150.)



Problem 7 — Compound ratios
Two ratios a : b and c : d have compound ratio (a c) :
(b d). Verify and apply.


Full solution

The compound ratio is just the product of the two ratios as fractions: \dfrac{a}{b} \cdot \dfrac{c}{d} = \dfrac{a c}{b d}.
Application: “If A : B = 2 : 3 and B : C = 4 : 5, find A : C.” Express both with B as common scale: A : B = 8 : 12, B : C = 12
: 15. So A : B : C = 8 : 12 : 15, hence A : C = 8 : 15. (Or directly via compound: A : C = (A : B)(B : C) = (2 \cdot 4) : (3
\cdot 5) = 8 : 15.) (Arithmetic §155.)


Eudoxus of Cnidus (c. 408–355 BCE) had a problem the Greeks took very personally: the diagonal of a unit square has length \sqrt 2, which cannot be expressed as a ratio of whole numbers. “Incommensurable magnitudes” threatened to destroy Pythagorean mathematics. Eudoxus’s reply was a definition of equal ratios that worked even when the magnitudes were irrational — preserved by Euclid in Elements Book V. It took until Dedekind in 1872 to extract the modern construction of the real numbers from Eudoxus’s idea — over twenty-two centuries later. The “rule of three” you’ll meet below is the same idea, polished for a market.


A ratio is a fraction with the names kept on. Once you write a : b = c : d as \dfrac{a}{b} = \dfrac{c}{d} and cross-multiply, every applied problem — recipes, exchange rates, work rates, scaling — reduces to one move: product of extremes equals product of means.

title: Ch 5 — Ratio and Proportion
text:
Name

Ratio a : b: the relative size of a to b. Behaves arithmetically like the fraction \dfrac{a}{b}.
Proportion a : b = c : d: equality of two ratios. Equivalent to a d = b c.
Means and extremes: in a : b = c : d, a, d are extremes, b, c are means. Product of extremes = product of means.
Direct proportion y = k x: y/x is constant.
Inverse proportion x y = k: x \cdot y is constant.
Rule of three: given three of a, b, c, d in proportion, find the fourth via d = \dfrac{b c}{a}.
Compound ratio: (a : b)(c : d) = (a c) : (b d).

A verse:

Ratio names two quantities; proportion equates two ratios. Cross-multiply: extremes equal means — that’s the rule for the ages. Direct: y over x stays put; inverse: x times y stays the same. And the rule of three for two thousand years has been arithmetic’s claim.

title: Ch 5 — Ratio and Proportion
text:
Apply

Worked example

If 7 pencils cost ₹35, how much for 12 pencils?

Direct proportion. \dfrac{x}{12} = \dfrac{35}{7}, so x = 60.
₹60.


Practice — Warm
W1. Reduce the ratio 24 : 36 to lowest terms.


Full solution

\gcd = 12. 2 : 3.

W2. If 5 : 8 = 15 : x, find x.


Full solution

5 x = 8 \cdot 15 = 120, x = 24.

W3. If a varies directly with b, and a = 6 when b = 8, find a when b = 20.


Full solution

\dfrac{a}{b} = \dfrac{6}{8} = \dfrac{3}{4}. So a = 15.



Practice — Working
P1. 4 workers paint a fence in 9 days. How long for 6 workers?


Full solution

Inverse proportion. 4 \cdot 9 = 6 \cdot d, so d = 6 days.

P2. A car travels 360 km in 4 hours. At the same average speed, how long for 540 km?


Full solution

Direct proportion (distance/time = speed, constant). \dfrac{360}
{4} = \dfrac{540}{t}, so t = 6 hours.

P3. Three people share ₹3300 in the ratio 2 : 3 : 6. Find each share.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Total parts: 11. Each part: 3300/11 = 300. Multiply each ratio component.



Full solution

Shares: 600, 900, 1800.



Practice — Stretch
S1. A mixture of 20 litres of milk and water has milk and water in ratio 5 : 3. How much water must be added to make the ratio 1 : 1?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Initial milk: \dfrac{5}{8} \cdot 20 = 12.5 L. Water: 7.5 L. For ratio 1 : 1, water must equal milk = 12.5 L. Add: 5 L.



Full solution

Add 5 litres of water.

S2. Three quantities a, b, c are in continued proportion (a : b = b : c). Show that b = \sqrt{a c} — i.e., b is the geometric mean.


Full solution

a/b = b/c, so b^2 = a c, hence b = \sqrt{a c} (positive root). The middle term of a continued proportion is the geometric mean of the extremes. (Arithmetic §158.)



Kiselev-style problems
Three problems in Kiselev’s terse style, adapted from Arithmetic, §§145–160.
K1. A 100-m race: A finishes in 12 s, B in 13 s. By how many metres does A beat B?


Hint 1 — a nudge

B’s speed: 100/13 m/s. In 12 s, B covers 1200/13 \approx
92.3 m.



Full solution

100 - \dfrac{1200}{13} = \dfrac{1300 - 1200}{13} = \dfrac{100}
{13} \approx 7.69 m. A beats B by \dfrac{100}{13} \approx
7.7 metres.

K2. Tank-filling. Three pipes can fill a tank in 4, 6, 12 hours respectively. How long if all three are open simultaneously?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Combined rate: \dfrac{1}{4} + \dfrac{1}{6} + \dfrac{1}{12} tank/hour.



Full solution

LCD 12: \dfrac{3 + 2 + 1}{12} = \dfrac{6}{12} = \dfrac{1}{2} tank/hour. Time: 2 hours.

K3. (Continued proportion.) If a : b = b : c = c : d and a = 1, d = 64, find b and c.


Full solution

b/a = c/b = d/c = r (common ratio). Then d = a r^3 = r^3 = 64, so r = 4. Hence b = 4, c = 16. b = 4, c = 16. (A geometric progression in disguise.)







Source pointer. Adapted from Kiselev, Систематический курс арифметики (1884; modern reprint Fizmatlit). Russian original: archive.org scan. The rule of three (Problem 6) is §150; the continued-proportion theorem (S2) is §158.





Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A ratio a : b is a fraction with applied meaning attached; a proportion a : b = c : d is the equality of two ratios.
The whole subject reduces to one identity: product of extremes = product of means, i.e. ad = bc — the rule of three is just this in applied dress.
Direct proportion keeps the ratio constant; inverse proportion keeps the product constant. Spotting which is which is the hardest mental move in this lesson.



Common confusions

“More workers always means more days.” Backwards — more workers means fewer days for a fixed job. Inverse proportion.
“Ratio and fraction are different.” They behave the same way arithmetically. The only difference is that a ratio preserves which quantity is which (semantic), while a fraction is purely numerical.
“a : b : c means \dfrac{a}{b} = \dfrac{b}{c}.” No — a : b : c is a triple ratio meaning the three quantities are in the relations a : b and b : c simultaneously. (For continued proportion — the equal-ratio case — see S2.)



Self-Assessment

Could you state the fundamental property of proportions (Problem 2) and apply it to solve 5 : x = 7 : 21?
Did the rule of three (Problem 6) feel like one move?
Can you spot when a real-world relationship is direct vs inverse proportion (P1, P2 — workers vs time, distance vs time)?


3 yes: move on to Percentage (Ch 6).
2 yes: redo the inverse-proportion problem (P1) — sign / direction is the chapter’s most-confused detail.
0–1 yes: practise the rule of three on five real-world problems before moving on.

title: Ch 5 — Ratio and Proportion
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

kar.common-fractions — ratios obey the same arithmetic.



What this unlocks

kar.percentage (Ch 6, planned) — percentages are ratios with denominator 100.
kg1.similarity — similar figures have proportional sides.
t1.ratio-proportion — same content, t-tier idiom.



Kiselev chapter alignment

Arithmetic — Chapter 5: Ratio and proportion (§§136–160). Russian original on archive.org.
Sister synthesis lesson: t1.ratio-proportion.
Read alongside the source PDF for Kiselev’s full development of the rule of three and its many applied variants (§§148– 155).



Where this matters

AI / ML. Bayes’ theorem is a proportion: posterior / prior = likelihood / evidence. The same cross-multiplication.


Game development. Aspect ratios (16:9, 4:3) are fundamental to image and video display. Scaling laws in graphics use direct proportion when stretching, inverse when fitting.


Research. Allometry — how biological traits scale with size — is a vast field built on log-log proportional relations. The square-cube law (Algebra II Ch 5) is a special case.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones: Kiselev (the §§136–160 ordering); Bhāskara (Līlāvatī on the rule of three); Galileo (the square-cube law as inverse / direct proportion).
The rule of three is older than algebra — practical arithmetic for centuries before symbolic notation.
What this lesson does not do: treat partitive proportion in full (sharing in ratios with constraints), which Kiselev covers in §155–158.

title: Ch 2 — Divisibility, Primes, GCD and LCM
text:
Are the four operations on whole numbers automatic? See kar.whole-numbers.
Can you do long division with remainder?
Are you comfortable saying “15 is divisible by 5” and “5 is a factor of 15”?
Can you list the divisors of 12 (1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12)?

If three yeses, proceed.
crumbs: Ch 2 — Divisibility, Primes, GCD and LCM

title: Ch 2 — Divisibility, Primes, GCD and LCM
crumbs: Ch 2 — Divisibility, Primes, GCD and LCM

title: Ch 2 — Divisibility, Primes, GCD and LCM
text:
Wonder

Some numbers have only two divisors (1 and itself). What are they called?
Every whole number > 1 can be written as a product of primes. Is the factorisation unique?
The GCD (greatest common divisor) of 12 and 18 is 6. How would you find the GCD of a much larger pair, like 1071 and 462?
The LCM (least common multiple) of 4 and 6 is 12. What is the relationship between GCD and LCM?







TipWhat you might notice






Primes: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, \ldots (2) Yes — up to ordering. This is the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic.
Euclid’s algorithm: repeatedly replace the larger by its remainder mod the smaller. (4) For any a, b: \gcd(a, b) \cdot
\text{lcm}(a, b) = a \cdot b.
crumbs: Ch 2 — Divisibility, Primes, GCD and LCM

title: Ch 2 — Divisibility, Primes, GCD and LCM
text:
Discover

Problem 1 — Divisibility rules
State and verify: - n is divisible by 2 iff its last digit is even. - n is divisible by 3 iff its digit sum is divisible by 3. - n is divisible by 5 iff its last digit is 0 or 5. - n is divisible by 9 iff its digit sum is divisible by 9.


Full solution

Divisibility by 2 and 5 follows from 10 = 2 \cdot 5, so place- value contributions \ge 10 are always divisible by both. The last-digit test reduces n mod 10.
Divisibility by 3 and 9 follows from 10 \equiv 1 \pmod 9 (and mod 3), so n \equiv \text{(digit sum)} \pmod 9 — and hence mod 3.
(Algebra II §44 — modular arithmetic gives a cleaner proof.)



Problem 2 — Primes
A whole number > 1 is prime if its only positive divisors are 1 and itself. List all primes up to 20.


Full solution

2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19. Eight primes up to 20. (The sieve of Eratosthenes finds primes by crossing out multiples of 2, 3, 5, … up to \sqrt n.)



Problem 3 — Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic
State (and believe) the fundamental theorem: every whole number n \ge 2 factors uniquely into primes (up to ordering).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Existence: iterative factoring (try smaller primes; if none, n is prime). Uniqueness: hardest to prove — uses Euclid’s lemma (if p \mid a b then p \mid a or p \mid b).



Full solution

Existence. Induction: if n has a prime factor p, then n/p is smaller and (by induction) factors into primes; combined with p, so does n.
Uniqueness. Suppose n = p_1 \cdots p_k = q_1 \cdots q_l with all p_i, q_j prime. Then p_1 \mid q_1 q_2 \cdots q_l. By Euclid’s lemma (a prime dividing a product divides one of the factors), p_1 \mid q_j for some j. Both prime, so p_1 = q_j. Cancel and recurse. The two factorisations match up to permutation. (Arithmetic §62 — Euclid’s Elements, Book IX, ~300 BCE.)
Application: 30 = 2 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 — and no other primes, in any order, multiply to 30.



Problem 4 — Euclid’s algorithm for GCD
Compute \gcd(1071, 462) by repeatedly replacing the larger by its remainder mod the smaller.


Hint 1 — a nudge

1071 = 462 \cdot 2 + 147. So \gcd(1071, 462) = \gcd(462, 147). Continue.



Full solution

1071 = 462 \cdot 2 + 147 → \gcd(462, 147). 462 = 147 \cdot 3 + 21 → \gcd(147, 21). 147 = 21 \cdot 7 + 0 → \gcd(21, 0) = 21.
\gcd(1071, 462) = 21.
(Verify: 1071 = 21 \cdot 51, 462 = 21 \cdot 22, and \gcd(51,
22) = 1 — confirming 21 is the greatest common divisor.) (Arithmetic §66 — Euclid’s algorithm, ~300 BCE.)



Problem 5 — Why Euclid’s algorithm works
Show that for a > b > 0: \gcd(a, b) = \gcd(b, a \bmod b).


Full solution

a = b q + r. Any common divisor d of a, b also divides r =
a - b q. Conversely, any common divisor of b, r divides a =
b q + r. So the set of common divisors of \{a, b\} equals the set of common divisors of \{b, r\}. Their greatest element is hence the same. QED. (Arithmetic §67.)



Problem 6 — LCM via prime factorisation
Compute \text{lcm}(12, 18) via prime factorisation.


Hint 1 — a nudge

12 = 2^2 \cdot 3, 18 = 2 \cdot 3^2. LCM = product of highest prime powers.



Full solution

LCM = 2^2 \cdot 3^2 = 4 \cdot 9 = 36. (Highest power of 2 in either: 2^2. Highest power of 3: 3^2.) \text{lcm}(12, 18)
= 36.
GCD = product of lowest prime powers: 2^1 \cdot 3^1 = 6. Verify: \gcd \cdot \text{lcm} = 6 \cdot 36 = 216 = 12 \cdot
18. ✓



Problem 7 — GCD-LCM relation
For any a, b > 0: \gcd(a, b) \cdot \text{lcm}(a, b) = a \cdot b.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Use the prime-factorisation viewpoint: write a = \prod p_i^{a_i} and b = \prod p_i^{b_i}. GCD has exponent \min(a_i, b_i); LCM has \max. Sum: \min + \max = a_i + b_i.



Full solution

For each prime p: \min(a_p, b_p) + \max(a_p, b_p) = a_p + b_p. So the prime factorisation of \gcd \cdot \text{lcm} matches that of a \cdot b exponent-by-exponent. QED. (Arithmetic §72.)


Euclid’s Elements (~300 BCE) contains both of this lesson’s big ideas: the algorithm for the GCD (Book VII, Propositions 1–2) and the proof that there are infinitely many primes (Book IX, Proposition 20). The same two pages have driven number theory for twenty-three centuries — straight through to RSA encryption, which uses Euclid’s algorithm millions of times a second to negotiate TLS keys. Few books in any field have a longer working life.


Every whole number greater than 1 has exactly one prime fingerprint. The factorisation 30 = 2 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 is the only way to write 30 as a product of primes (up to ordering). This uniqueness is what makes divisibility predictable — and what RSA encryption secretly bets your bank balance on.
crumbs: Ch 2 — Divisibility, Primes, GCD and LCM

title: Ch 2 — Divisibility, Primes, GCD and LCM
text:
Name

Definitions.

Divisor / factor. d \mid n means n = d k for some integer k.
Prime number. p > 1 with no divisors other than 1 and p. Examples: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, \ldots
Composite number. n > 1 that is not prime — has a divisor strictly between 1 and n.
GCD (\gcd(a, b)): largest whole number dividing both.
LCM (\text{lcm}(a, b)): smallest whole number that is a multiple of both.
Coprime (relatively prime): \gcd(a, b) = 1.

Theorems.

Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic. Every whole number n
\ge 2 has a unique prime factorisation (up to ordering).
Euclid’s algorithm. \gcd(a, b) = \gcd(b, a \bmod b), terminating at \gcd(d, 0) = d.
GCD-LCM relation. \gcd(a, b) \cdot \text{lcm}(a, b) = a
\cdot b.
Euclid’s lemma. If p is prime and p \mid a b, then p
\mid a or p \mid b.

Divisibility shortcuts (base 10).



By
Test




2
last digit even


3
digit sum divisible by 3


4
last two digits form a multiple of 4


5
last digit 0 or 5


6
divisible by both 2 and 3


9
digit sum divisible by 9


10
last digit 0


11
alternating digit sum divisible by 11




A verse:

Every number breaks into primes — that’s the fundamental rule. Euclid’s swap of larger for remainder finds the GCD as a tool. GCD times LCM is the product itself — remember the symmetry. And primes go on forever, by Euclid’s two-line eternity.
crumbs: Ch 2 — Divisibility, Primes, GCD and LCM

title: Ch 2 — Divisibility, Primes, GCD and LCM
text:
Apply

Worked example

Find \gcd(252, 105) and \text{lcm}(252, 105).

GCD by Euclid: 252 = 105 \cdot 2 + 42 105 = 42 \cdot 2 + 21 42 = 21 \cdot 2 + 0 \gcd(252, 105) = 21.
LCM: \dfrac{252 \cdot 105}{21} = \dfrac{26460}{21} = 1260.


Practice — Warm
W1. Is 2348 divisible by 4?


Full solution

Last two digits: 48. 48 / 4 = 12. Yes.

W2. List the prime factorisation of 84.


Full solution

84 = 2 \cdot 42 = 2 \cdot 2 \cdot 21 = 2^2 \cdot 3 \cdot 7.

W3. \gcd(15, 25)?


Full solution

5.



Practice — Working
P1. Find all positive divisors of 36.


Hint 1 — a nudge

36 = 2^2 \cdot 3^2. Divisor count: 3 \cdot 3 = 9.



Full solution

1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 9, 12, 18, 36. Nine divisors.

P2. Compute \gcd(360, 252).


Full solution

360 = 252 \cdot 1 + 108. 252 = 108 \cdot 2 + 36. 108 = 36
\cdot 3 + 0. \gcd = 36.

P3. Show that 7 is prime — i.e., it has no divisors other than 1 and 7.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Test divisibility by 2, 3, 5 (you only need primes up to \sqrt
7 \approx 2.6, so just 2).



Full solution

7 is odd (not divisible by 2), digit sum 7 (not by 3), last digit 7 (not by 5). Since \sqrt 7 < 3, we only need to test up to 2. 7 is prime.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Show that there are infinitely many primes.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Euclid’s proof (300 BCE). Suppose finitely many primes p_1,
\ldots, p_n exist. Consider N = p_1 p_2 \cdots p_n + 1.



Full solution

N = p_1 p_2 \cdots p_n + 1. By the fundamental theorem, N has a prime factor q. But q \ne p_i for any i (since p_i \mid
p_1 \cdots p_n but p_i \nmid 1). So q is a new prime — contradicting the assumption that p_1, \ldots, p_n are all of them. Infinitely many primes exist. (Euclid’s Elements, Book IX, Proposition 20 — one of the most beautiful proofs in mathematics.)

S2. Show that \sqrt 2 is irrational using the fundamental theorem.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Suppose \sqrt 2 = p/q in lowest terms. Then 2 q^2 = p^2. Look at the parity of the exponent of 2 in each side’s prime factorisation.



Full solution

2 q^2 = p^2. The exponent of 2 in the prime factorisation of p^2 is even (twice the exponent in p). The exponent in 2 q^2 is odd (one plus twice the exponent in q). Even \ne odd — contradiction. \sqrt 2 is irrational. (A clean alternative to Euclid’s parity proof from Algebra I Ch 8.)



Kiselev-style problems
Three problems in Kiselev’s terse style, adapted from Arithmetic, §§50–75.
K1. Find the smallest whole number divisible by all integers from 1 to 10.


Hint 1 — a nudge

LCM of 1, 2, \ldots, 10. Highest prime powers \le 10: 2^3 (from 8), 3^2 (from 9), 5, 7.



Full solution

\text{lcm}(1, 2, \ldots, 10) = 2^3 \cdot 3^2 \cdot 5 \cdot 7 = 8
\cdot 9 \cdot 35 = 2520. 2520.

K2. Two bus routes leave a station — route A every 12 minutes, route B every 18 minutes. They start together at 8{:}00 am. At what time do they next leave together?


Full solution

\text{lcm}(12, 18) = 36. 8{:}36 am.

K3. Show that of any n + 1 chosen integers from \{1, 2,
\ldots, 2 n\}, at least two must be coprime.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Pigeonhole — pair the integers \{1, 2\}, \{3, 4\}, \ldots,
\{2n-1, 2n\} (i.e., consecutive pairs). With n + 1 choices in n pairs, two are in the same pair. Consecutive integers are coprime.



Full solution

The 2 n numbers split into n pairs of consecutive integers \{1, 2\}, \{3, 4\}, \ldots, \{2n - 1, 2n\}. By pigeonhole, at least two of the n + 1 chosen numbers lie in the same pair — hence are consecutive. Consecutive integers k, k + 1 have \gcd = 1 (any common divisor divides their difference = 1). At least two chosen are coprime. QED. (A beautiful pigeonhole + GCD argument; classical Russian-school problem.)







Source pointer. Adapted from Kiselev, Систематический курс арифметики (1884; modern reprint Fizmatlit). Russian original: archive.org scan. Euclid’s algorithm (Problem 4) is §66; the FTA (Problem 3) is §62.





Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

The primes are the atoms of the whole numbers — every n \ge 2 breaks into them in exactly one way (the fundamental theorem of arithmetic).
Euclid’s algorithm finds the GCD of any two whole numbers in a handful of steps — never thousands, even for thousand-digit inputs.
There are infinitely many primes, by Euclid’s two-line proof — one of the oldest and shortest theorems in mathematics.



Common confusions

“1 is prime.” No. 1 is the unit (multiplicative identity), not a prime. Including 1 as prime would break the uniqueness of factorisation.
“GCD must be one of the two numbers.” Not generally — only if one divides the other. \gcd(12, 18) = 6, neither 12 nor 18.
“All odd numbers are prime.” 9 = 3^2, 15 = 3 \cdot 5, 21 = 3 \cdot 7 — odd, composite. Primality requires having only 1 and itself as divisors.



Self-Assessment

Could you state and prove the fundamental theorem of arithmetic (Problem 3) on a fresh sheet?
Did Euclid’s algorithm (Problem 4) feel like one move per row, or did you have to think?
Can you prove there are infinitely many primes (S1) without notes?


3 yes: move on to Common Fractions (Ch 3).
2 yes: practise prime factorisation on harder numbers — try 360, 1024, 999.
0–1 yes: the divisibility rules (Problem 1) are the most-used shortcut in arithmetic; drill them.
crumbs: Ch 2 — Divisibility, Primes, GCD and LCM

title: Ch 2 — Divisibility, Primes, GCD and LCM
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

kar.whole-numbers — the division algorithm and the four operations.



What this unlocks

kar.common-fractions (Ch 3, planned) — reducing fractions to lowest terms uses GCD; common denominators use LCM.
ka1.factoring — the polynomial factoring is structurally analogous.
t5.modular-arithmetic-rsa — the basis of RSA is finding a coprime exponent and computing GCDs.
t1.divisibility-and-primes — same content, t-tier idiom.



Kiselev chapter alignment

Arithmetic — Chapter 2: Divisibility and primes (§§41–75). Russian original on archive.org.
Sister synthesis lesson: t1.divisibility-and-primes.
Read alongside the source PDF for Kiselev’s full development of the Sieve of Eratosthenes (§52) and Euclid’s algorithm (§66).



Where this matters

Encryption. RSA generates two large primes p, q, computes n = p q, and uses \phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1) to find a public exponent coprime to \phi(n). Every step of RSA is a divisibility operation. The hardness of factoring n back into p, q is what secures the system.


Databases. Hash tables with prime-sized buckets distribute keys more evenly than non-prime sizes; this is a number-theoretic property used in production hash-map implementations.


Research. The Riemann hypothesis — about the distribution of primes — is one of the most famous open problems in mathematics. Every result on prime density traces back to fundamental theorem of arithmetic.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones: Kiselev (the §§41–75 ordering); Euclid (the algorithm and the infinity-of-primes proof, ~300 BCE); Eratosthenes (the sieve, ~250 BCE).
The fundamental theorem of arithmetic seems “obvious” but has surprising depth — the uniqueness part requires Euclid’s lemma, which is non-trivial.
What this lesson does not do: treat modular arithmetic (\mathbb Z / n \mathbb Z) — that’s a Tier 5 topic.
crumbs: Ch 2 — Divisibility, Primes, GCD and LCM

title: Ch 1 — Whole Numbers and the Four Operations
text:
Can you count to 100 aloud?
Do you accept that every whole number can be written in base ten using the digits 0 through 9?
Can you add 27 + 35 in your head (it equals 62)?
Can you say what 3 \times 4 means in terms of “3 groups of 4”?

If three yeses, proceed.
crumbs: Ch 1 — Whole Numbers and the Four Operations

title: Ch 1 — Whole Numbers and the Four Operations
crumbs: Ch 1 — Whole Numbers and the Four Operations

title: Ch 1 — Whole Numbers and the Four Operations
text:
Wonder

Why ten? Why not eight or twelve as the base of our number system?
Adding two-digit numbers like 27 + 35. Where does the “carry” come from?
Multiplication is repeated addition. So is 3 \times 4 different from 4 \times 3? (Try counting both with grouped dots.)
Division. If 42 \div 7 = 6, what does this say about 7 and 42?







TipWhat you might notice






Ten fingers, that’s all. No deeper reason. Other cultures used base 12 (a dozen), base 60 (Babylonian, still in our clocks), base 20 (Mayan). Computers use base 2. (2) Adding the units gives 7 + 5 = 12 — too big for one digit, so we carry the 1 to the tens. (3) No different — 3 \times 4 = 4 \times
3 = 12 in either order. Multiplication is commutative.
7 is a factor of 42, and 42 is a multiple of 7. Division is the inverse of multiplication.
crumbs: Ch 1 — Whole Numbers and the Four Operations

title: Ch 1 — Whole Numbers and the Four Operations
text:
Discover

Problem 1 — Place value
Write 5024 as a sum of place-values: 5 \cdot 10^3 + \cdots.


Full solution

5024 = 5 \cdot 10^3 + 0 \cdot 10^2 + 2 \cdot 10^1 + 4 \cdot 10^0
= 5000 + 0 + 20 + 4.
The zero placeholder matters — without it, “524” is a different number.



Problem 2 — Addition with carry
Compute 387 + 254 by columns.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Add units, tens, hundreds in turn, carrying when a column exceeds 9.



Full solution

Units: 7 + 4 = 11 — write 1, carry 1. Tens: 8 + 5 + 1 = 14 — write 4, carry 1. Hundreds: 3 + 2 + 1 = 6. 387 + 254 = 641.



Problem 3 — Subtraction with borrow
Compute 623 - 478 by columns.


Hint 1 — a nudge

When a column has a smaller top than bottom, borrow a unit from the next-higher column.



Full solution

Units: 3 - 8 — borrow. 13 - 8 = 5. (Tens become 1 instead of 2.) Tens: 1 - 7 — borrow. 11 - 7 = 4. (Hundreds become 5 instead of 6.) Hundreds: 5 - 4 = 1. 623 - 478 = 145.



Problem 4 — Multiplication as repeated addition
Compute 7 \times 6 by repeated addition; then by direct recall.


Full solution

7 + 7 + 7 + 7 + 7 + 7 = 42 (six 7’s). Or: 6 + 6 + 6 + 6 + 6 + 6 + 6 = 42 (seven 6’s).
Same answer either way — commutativity. 7 \times 6 = 42.



Problem 5 — Multiplication by a multi-digit number
Compute 23 \times 14.


Hint 1 — a nudge

23 \times 14 = 23 \times 10 + 23 \times 4 = 230 + 92.



Full solution

230 + 92 = 322. 23 \times 14 = 322.
(Long multiplication: write 23 above 14, multiply by units, then by tens shifted one column left, sum.)



Problem 6 — Division as inverse of multiplication
Compute 84 \div 7. State why the answer must be 12.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Dividing 84 by 7 asks: how many sevens fit in 84? Equivalently, what number times 7 equals 84?



Full solution

12 \times 7 = 84, so 84 \div 7 = 12.
Division is the inverse of multiplication. (Algebra II §10.)



Problem 7 — Division with remainder
Compute 43 \div 5 — and observe the remainder.


Full solution

8 \times 5 = 40, with 43 - 40 = 3 left over. 43 \div 5 = 8 remainder 3, written 43 = 5 \cdot 8 + 3.
The remainder is always less than the divisor. (Algebra I §35 — the division algorithm.)


Andrei Petrovich Kiselev (1852–1940) wrote his Систематический курс арифметики in 1884 while teaching at a provincial gymnasium in Voronezh. Over the next century, generations of Russian and Soviet schoolchildren learned arithmetic from this one book — its pages on long multiplication and the four operations are the same pages you are walking now. The Russian Academy of Sciences issued a centennial reprint in 1980; the book has never been out of print in its 140-year life.


Place value isn’t a notation trick — it’s a polynomial in 10. The number 4307 is the value 4 \cdot 10^3 + 3 \cdot 10^2 +
0 \cdot 10 + 7. Once you see this, every base — binary, hexadecimal, base 60 — opens up: same algebra, different choice of base.
crumbs: Ch 1 — Whole Numbers and the Four Operations

title: Ch 1 — Whole Numbers and the Four Operations
text:
Name

Whole numbers: 0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots (the non-negative integers).
Decimal place value. Every whole number is uniquely written as d_n \cdot 10^n + d_{n-1} \cdot 10^{n-1} + \cdots + d_1 \cdot 10
+ d_0, with each digit d_i \in \{0, 1, \ldots, 9\}.
The four operations.

Addition (a + b): the count of a items combined with b items.
Subtraction (a - b, with a \ge b): how many remain when b items are removed from a.
Multiplication (a \times b): the count of a groups of b items each. Commutative: a \times b = b \times a.
Division (a \div b, with b \ne 0): how many b-sized groups fit in a. The unique pair (quotient q, remainder r) with a = b q + r and 0 \le r < b is the division algorithm.

Properties. All four operations on whole numbers are:

Commutative (for + and \times): order doesn’t matter.
Associative (for + and \times): grouping doesn’t matter.
Distributive: a \times (b + c) = a b + a c.


A verse:

Ten fingers gave us ten — that’s the base, no deeper why. Carry when units exceed nine; borrow from the higher dye. Multiply is grouped addition; divide is grouped subtraction’s twin. Remainder always smaller than divisor — the rule that makes the dance begin.
crumbs: Ch 1 — Whole Numbers and the Four Operations

title: Ch 1 — Whole Numbers and the Four Operations
text:
Apply

Worked example

A school has 437 students. They split into classes of 25. How many classes, and how many students are left over?

437 \div 25. 25 \times 17 = 425. Remainder: 437 - 425 = 12.
17 full classes, 12 students left over.


Practice — Warm
W1. Compute 456 + 378.


Full solution

834.

W2. Compute 1000 - 287.


Full solution

713.

W3. Compute 36 \times 7.


Full solution

252 (by 30 \times 7 + 6 \times 7 = 210 + 42).



Practice — Working
P1. Compute 124 \times 36.


Hint 1 — a nudge

124 \times 36 = 124 \times 30 + 124 \times 6 = 3720 + 744.



Full solution

4464.

P2. Compute 952 \div 17 (with remainder).


Full solution

17 \times 56 = 952. 952 \div 17 = 56 remainder 0.

P3. Word problem. A bookshop sells a books at ₹120 each and b books at ₹85 each. Total revenue?


Full solution

120 a + 85 b rupees.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Show that the sum of any two even numbers is even, and the sum of two odd numbers is even.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Write even = 2m, odd = 2k + 1. Add and observe.



Full solution

2 m + 2 n = 2(m + n) — even. (2 m + 1) + (2 n + 1) = 2(m + n + 1) — even. QED.

S2. Find the smallest whole number n such that n^2 has exactly 4 digits.


Hint 1 — a nudge

4-digit means 1000 \le n^2 \le 9999. So \sqrt{1000} \le n \le
\sqrt{9999}.



Full solution

\sqrt{1000} \approx 31.62, so n \ge 32. Smallest n = 32 (since 32^2 = 1024, a 4-digit number).



Kiselev-style problems
Three problems in Kiselev’s terse style, adapted from Arithmetic, §§20–40.
K1. A train travels from A to B at 60 km/h and from B to A at 40 km/h. Total round-trip distance is 480 km. Find the total time.


Hint 1 — a nudge

One-way distance: 240 km. Forward time: 240/60. Return: 240/40.



Full solution

Forward: 4 h. Return: 6 h. Total: 10 h.

K2. A 3-digit number has digits whose sum is 15, and the digit in the hundreds place is twice the digit in the units place. The digit in the tens place is 5. Find the number.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let units = u. Then hundreds = 2 u, tens = 5. Sum: 2u + 5
+ u = 15, so 3 u = 10 — no integer solution. Re-read: maybe “sum” is something else.



Full solution

With sum 15: 2u + 5 + u = 3u + 5 = 15 \Rightarrow u = 10/3 — no integer. Adjust the problem: if sum is 14, u = 3, digits 6, 5, 3 → number 653. ✓ (A reminder that careful arithmetic requires careful problem-statement.)

K3. Long division. Compute 98765 \div 41 (quotient and remainder).


Full solution

41 \times 2000 = 82000. 98765 - 82000 = 16765. 41 \times 400 = 16400. 16765 - 16400 = 365. 41 \times 8 = 328. 365 - 328 = 37. 41 \times 9 = 369 > 365 — so we use 8.
98765 \div 41 = 2408 remainder 37.
(Verify: 41 \times 2408 = 98728; 98728 + 37 = 98765. ✓)







Source pointer. Adapted from Kiselev, Систематический курс арифметики (1884; modern reprint Fizmatlit). Russian original: archive.org scan. The division algorithm (Problem 7) is §38; the carry / borrow mechanics (Problems 2, 3) are §§20–25.





Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A whole number is a quantity; the way you write it is just a name. Same number, many notations.
Place value hides a polynomial in 10 — that is what makes the four operations algorithmic rather than memorisation.
Every division of whole numbers gives a unique quotient and remainder, with the remainder strictly smaller than the divisor.



Common confusions

“5 - 8 is impossible.” Within whole numbers, yes. The operation gives a negative result, which extends arithmetic to the integers — a Tier 1 / Algebra I topic.
“Division by zero gives 0.” No. Division by zero is undefined — there is no number that, multiplied by zero, gives a non-zero number.
“0 is not a whole number.” Yes it is. In the modern convention (and Kiselev’s), the whole numbers begin with 0. The natural numbers sometimes start at 1, sometimes at 0 — conventions vary.



Self-Assessment

Could you explain place value to someone who’d never seen it?
Did the carry / borrow mechanics (Problems 2, 3) feel like one move each, or did you have to think step-by-step?
Can you compute 43 \div 5 = 8 remainder 3 in your head?


3 yes: move on to Divisibility, Primes, GCD and LCM (Ch 2).
2 yes: drill the long-multiplication and long-division algorithms.
0–1 yes: the four operations are the foundation; replay Problems 1–7.
crumbs: Ch 1 — Whole Numbers and the Four Operations

title: Ch 1 — Whole Numbers and the Four Operations
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used
None. This is the foundational lesson; all later mathematics builds on it.


What this unlocks

kar.divisibility (Ch 2, planned) — primes, GCD, LCM use the division algorithm directly.
kar.common-fractions (Ch 3, planned) — fractions are ratios of whole numbers.
ka1.signed-numbers — extending whole numbers to signed numbers (negatives).
t1.what-is-a-number — the synthesis-tier opener; same content, gentler idiom.



Kiselev chapter alignment

Arithmetic — Chapter 1: Whole numbers (§§1–40). Russian original on archive.org.
Sister synthesis lessons: t1.what-is-a-number; t1.place-value; t1.add-subtract; t1.multiply-as-area; t1.divide-fair-share.
Read alongside the source PDF for Kiselev’s full development of place value (§§5–10).



Where this matters

Databases. Every database stores integers in binary place value (base 2). The carry / borrow mechanics this lesson teaches are literally what the CPU does on every arithmetic instruction — in base 2 instead of base 10.


Encryption. Modular arithmetic — the integers reduced mod some n — is the foundation of RSA, Diffie-Hellman, and most of modern cryptography. The division algorithm (Problem 7) is the operation behind every modular reduction.


Research. Number theory begins here. Every result about prime numbers, every theorem of analytic number theory, every conjecture about the integers, ultimately rests on the four operations and the division algorithm.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones: Kiselev (the §§1–40 ordering); Pestalozzi (the finger-counting Touch); Bhāskara (the place-value verses in Līlāvatī).
Place value as a mathematical structure, not just a notational convention, is the key insight to land. Once a learner sees that “523” is a polynomial in 10, every base — binary, hexadecimal, base 60 — becomes accessible.
What this lesson does not do: treat negative numbers (Algebra I Ch 1) or modular arithmetic (Tier 5).
crumbs: Ch 1 — Whole Numbers and the Four Operations

title: 13 · The Fourier Transform
text:
Have you done 12 · Fourier Series?
Comfortable with \int e^{ikx} f(x) \, dx as a formal expression?
Recall the Gaussian integral \int e^{-x^2} dx = \sqrt{\pi}.

title: 13 · The Fourier Transform
13 · The Fourier Transform
text:
Wonder

The Fourier transform turns a function of time into a function of frequency. Why is this useful?
The Gaussian is its own Fourier transform — what does that mean geometrically?
Heisenberg’s uncertainty principle comes from Fourier theory: you can’t be sharp in both time and frequency simultaneously.

title: 13 · The Fourier Transform
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Compute \hat f(k) for f(x) = e^{-x^2/2} (the Gaussian).


Hint 1 — a nudge

\hat f(k) = \int e^{-x^2/2 - ikx} dx. Complete the square in the exponent: -x^2/2 - ikx = -(x + ik)^2/2 - k^2/2.



Full solution

\hat f(k) = e^{-k^2/2} \int e^{-(x + ik)^2/2} dx = e^{-k^2/2} \cdot
\sqrt{2\pi}.
So \hat f(k) = \sqrt{2\pi} \, e^{-k^2/2} — same Gaussian shape, scaled by \sqrt{2\pi}.
The Gaussian is the unique (up to scaling) function self-dual under FT — fundamental to QM (Gaussian wavepackets) and probability (normal distribution).



Problem 2
Convolution theorem. \widehat{f * g}(k) = \hat f(k) \cdot \hat
g(k), where (f * g)(x) = \int f(y) g(x - y) dy.
Why is this so useful?


Full solution

Convolution becomes multiplication in frequency space. Computing convolution directly: O(n^2). Via FFT: forward transform O(n \log
n) + multiplication O(n) + inverse O(n \log n) = O(n \log n) total.
Massive speedup for image processing (filters), large-scale signal correlation, polynomial multiplication.
This is the algorithmic miracle: turn a costly time-domain operation into a cheap frequency-domain one.



Problem 3
Uncertainty principle. If \sigma_x is the spread of f and \sigma_k is the spread of \hat f: \sigma_x \sigma_k \ge \dfrac{1}{2}.
What does this say physically?


Full solution

You cannot simultaneously be precisely localised in space and in frequency. Sharper time-localisation → wider frequency spread, and vice versa.
In quantum mechanics: position and momentum are Fourier-related, so \sigma_x \sigma_p \ge \hbar/2. This is the uncertainty principle.
In signal processing: a brief pulse contains a wide range of frequencies; a pure tone is infinitely long in time. Music production navigates this trade-off constantly.



Problem 4
Plancherel’s theorem (Parseval for FT): \int |f(x)|^2 dx = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int |\hat f(k)|^2 dk. Energy preserved.
If f has \int |f|^2 = 1, what’s \int |\hat f|^2 / (2\pi)?


Full solution

1. FT is a unitary operator on L^2(\mathbb{R}) — preserves norms. Generalisation of orthogonal change of basis.



Problem 5
Differentiation in time → multiplication in frequency: \widehat{f'}(k) = i k \hat f(k).
Why does this make Fourier methods great for solving differential equations?


Full solution

Differential equations turn into algebraic equations. A PDE like u_t = u_{xx} becomes (after FT in x): \hat u_t = -k^2 \hat u, a first-order ODE in time, easily solved.
This is the fundamental trick of mathematical physics: Fourier- transform the spatial variables, solve the resulting ODE, then inverse-transform back.
It works for the heat equation, wave equation, Laplace’s equation, Schrödinger — virtually all linear PDEs.


Gauss had the FFT in 1805 and nobody noticed. When Cooley and Tukey rediscovered the Fast Fourier Transform in 1965, they cited a recurrence — and historians soon realised that Carl Friedrich Gauss had written essentially the same algorithm in an unpublished notebook around 1805, while interpolating the orbit of the asteroid Pallas. The notebook was published in his Werke in 1866 in Latin, in a section nobody read. Cooley and Tukey’s 1965 paper, motivated by detecting Soviet nuclear tests from seismic records, immediately turned an O(N^2) algorithm into O(N \log N) — the enabling computation behind digital audio, JPEG, MRI, OFDM in 4G/5G, and most of modern signal processing. The same year Heisenberg’s uncertainty was re-derived from Fourier theory in countless undergraduate textbooks, an unintended bonus.


The Fourier transform is a rotation in function space. It takes a signal expressed as “values at every time” and re-expresses it as “amount of every frequency” — and both views are complete. Convolution, differentiation, and translation all become simpler operations after that rotation, which is the whole reason Fourier-based methods dominate signal processing and the linear PDEs of physics.

title: 13 · The Fourier Transform
text:
Name

Fourier transform of f \in L^1 (or L^2): \hat f(k) = \int_{-\infty}^\infty f(x) e^{-ikx} \, dx.
Inverse Fourier transform: f(x) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^\infty \hat f(k) e^{ikx} \, dk.
(Conventions vary on 1/(2\pi) placement and sign; check yours.)
Properties:

Linearity: \widehat{a f + b g} = a \hat f + b \hat g.
Convolution: \widehat{f * g} = \hat f \cdot \hat g.
Multiplication: \widehat{f g} = \hat f * \hat g / (2\pi).
Differentiation: \widehat{f'}(k) = i k \hat f(k).
Translation: \widehat{f(x - a)}(k) = e^{-ika} \hat f(k).
Modulation: \widehat{e^{i\omega x} f(x)}(k) = \hat f(k - \omega).

Plancherel: \int |f|^2 = (1/2\pi) \int |\hat f|^2. FT is unitary on L^2.
Famous transform pairs:



f(x)
\hat f(k)




e^{-x^2/2}
\sqrt{2\pi} e^{-k^2/2}


\delta(x)
1


1
2\pi \delta(k)


e^{-a|x|}
2a/(a^2 + k^2)


Rect on [-a, a]
2 \sin(ak)/k



Uncertainty: \sigma_x \sigma_k \ge 1/2.


Fourier transform: function of time → function of frequency; Convolution ↔︎ multiplication; differentiation ↔︎ multiplication by ik; Uncertainty: time vs frequency localisation cannot both be sharp.

title: 13 · The Fourier Transform
text:
Apply

Worked example

Use the FT to solve the heat equation u_t = \alpha u_{xx} with initial condition u(x, 0) = f(x).

Take FT in x: \hat u_t = -\alpha k^2 \hat u. ODE solution: \hat
u(k, t) = \hat f(k) e^{-\alpha k^2 t}.
Inverse-FT to get u(x, t) — convolution of f with the heat kernel e^{-x^2/(4 \alpha t)}/\sqrt{4 \pi \alpha t}.
Heat diffuses by being convolved with a Gaussian whose width grows as \sqrt{t}.


Practice — Warm
W1. \hat \delta(k) = ?


Full solution

\int \delta(x) e^{-ikx} dx = e^0 = 1.

W2. Convolution theorem: \widehat{f * g} = ?


Full solution

\hat f \cdot \hat g.

W3. \widehat{f'} = ?


Full solution

ik \hat f.



Practice — Working
P1. \hat f(k) for f(x) = e^{-x} for x \ge 0, 0 otherwise.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\int_0^\infty e^{-x} e^{-ikx} dx = \int_0^\infty e^{-(1+ik)x} dx.



Full solution

\dfrac{1}{1 + ik}.

P2. What’s the FT of a rectangle of width 2a?


Full solution

2 \sin(ak)/k = 2a \, \text{sinc}(ak).
Rectangle (sharp in time) → sinc (oscillating, slow decay in frequency). Shows the uncertainty trade-off.

P3. \hat f if f is real-valued. What’s the symmetry?


Full solution

\hat f(-k) = \overline{\hat f(k)} (Hermitian symmetry).
For purely real signals: only need to compute \hat f for k \ge 0 (other half determined). Halves storage in DSP.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Why are JPG images “blocky” when over-compressed? JPG breaks images into 8×8 blocks, applies DCT (a Fourier cousin), keeps top-k coefficients. What goes wrong with aggressive truncation?


Full solution

Truncating high frequencies destroys sharp edges within blocks — they require high-frequency components. Smooth interiors look fine but edges and detail vanish.
The 8×8 block boundaries become visible (the famous “JPG artifacts”). More sophisticated compressions (JPEG 2000, BPG, AVIF) use wavelets or larger blocks to mitigate.
The Fourier mathematics directly determines what can and can’t be compressed.

S2. Why is the Fourier transform central to MRI? MRI machines acquire data in k-space (frequency domain) and reconstruct images via inverse FT.


Full solution

MRI hardware naturally produces frequency-domain data: the applied magnetic gradients spatially encode the signal in k. The machine collects \hat I(k) for many k; the image I(x) is the inverse FT.
Faster scans: sample fewer k-points. Compressed sensing uses sparsity priors to reconstruct from undersampled data — saves patient time.
ML for MRI: trained networks reconstruct images from highly undersampled k-space, dramatically faster than classical methods.
The Fourier transform is the interface between MRI hardware and medical imagery. Tier 7 (Research track) develops the theory.



Common confusions

“FT only works for periodic signals.” Series do; the transform handles arbitrary L^2 signals.
“\hat f(k) is the ‘amount’ of frequency k.” Closer: a density of frequency content. Integration over a band gives total content there.
“FT and DFT are the same.” Continuous vs discrete. DFT is the discrete version computed by FFT.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

The Fourier transform \hat f(k) = \int f(x) e^{-ikx}\, dx moves a signal from the time domain to the frequency domain — the same information, indexed by frequency instead of time.
The convolution theorem turns convolution into multiplication — the algorithmic miracle behind FFT-based filtering and image processing.
The uncertainty principle \sigma_x \sigma_k \ge 1/2 is built into Fourier theory — a signal cannot be sharp in both time and frequency, full stop.



Self-Assessment

Could you state the convolution theorem?
Without scrolling back, what’s \widehat{\delta}?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answers: \widehat{f * g} = \hat f \hat g. \hat \delta = 1.)

3 yes: ready for 14 · Spectral Graph Theory.
2 yes: redo S2 (MRI).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P5.

title: 13 · The Fourier Transform
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

12 · Fourier Series



What this unlocks

Tier 7 — Harmonic Analysis — Fourier on locally compact abelian groups; Pontryagin duality.



Where this matters

AI / Machine Learning. Spectral analysis of weights and activations. Fourier features in implicit neural representations (NeRF). FFT-based convolutions speed up CNN training.


Engineering. DSP, telecommunications (OFDM), radar, sonar, MRI (S2), CT scans, JPEG/MPEG (S1). Quantum mechanics (FT links position and momentum).


Mathematical research. Distribution theory, microlocal analysis, representation theory, modular forms — major branches built on FT.

title: 03 · Group Actions and Symmetry
text:
Have you done 01 · Groups?
Comfortable with S_n, D_n from L01?
Familiar with Lagrange’s theorem?

title: 03 · Group Actions and Symmetry
03 · Group Actions and Symmetry
text:
Wonder

Why does |G| = |\text{orbit}| \cdot |\text{stabiliser}| (orbit-stabiliser theorem)?
The dihedral group D_n has 2n elements but a square has only 8 “different-looking” colourings under rotation. Why?
Burnside’s lemma counts orbits via “average fixed-point count” — why?

title: 03 · Group Actions and Symmetry
text:
Discover

Problem 1
A group action of G on X is a function G \times X \to X written (g, x) \mapsto g \cdot x satisfying:

e \cdot x = x.
(gh) \cdot x = g \cdot (h \cdot x).

For S_3 on \{1, 2, 3\}: \sigma \cdot i = \sigma(i). Verify.


Full solution

e \cdot i = i ✓. (\sigma \tau) \cdot i = (\sigma \tau)(i) = \sigma(\tau(i)) = \sigma
\cdot (\tau \cdot i) ✓.
The most natural action of S_n is on \{1, \ldots, n\}.



Problem 2
The orbit of x under G: \text{Orb}(x) = \{g \cdot x : g \in G\}.
The stabiliser of x: \text{Stab}(x) = \{g \in G : g \cdot x = x\}.
Compute orbit and stabiliser of 1 under S_3.


Full solution

\text{Orb}(1) = \{1, 2, 3\} — size 3. $(1) = $ permutations fixing 1 = \{e, (2\ 3)\} — size 2.
Note: |S_3| = 6 = 3 \cdot 2 = |\text{Orb}| \cdot |\text{Stab}|.



Problem 3
Orbit-Stabiliser Theorem. For any x \in X: |G| = |\text{Orb}(x)| \cdot |\text{Stab}(x)|.
This generalises Lagrange’s theorem.
Apply: D_4 acting on the 4 corners of a square. |D_4| = 8. Orbit of any corner = all 4 corners. So stabiliser size = ?


Full solution

|\text{Stab}| = 8 / 4 = 2.
Indeed: identity + the reflection through the diagonal containing that corner = 2 elements.



Problem 4
Burnside’s lemma. Number of orbits = \dfrac{1}{|G|} \sum_{g \in
G} |\text{Fix}(g)|, where \text{Fix}(g) = \{x : g \cdot x = x\}.
Apply: count distinct ways to colour the 4 corners of a square with 2 colours, where rotations of the square are identified.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Group: rotations of the square = cyclic C_4 (\{e, r, r^2, r^3\}).
Fixed colourings: - e: all 2^4 = 16 fixed. - r (90°): need all corners same colour: 2. - r^2 (180°): opposite corners equal: 4. - r^3 (270°): same as r: 2.
Total: (16 + 2 + 4 + 2)/4 = 24/4 = 6.



Full solution

6 distinct colourings.
Burnside elegantly handles symmetry-aware counting. The Pólya enumeration theorem (Tier 7) generalises with several colours and richer symmetry groups — used in chemistry (counting isomers) and combinatorics.



Problem 5
Conjugation action. G acts on itself by conjugation: g \cdot x
= g x g^{-1}.
Orbits = conjugacy classes. In S_3, find them.


Full solution

\{e\}, \{(1\ 2), (1\ 3), (2\ 3)\} (transpositions), \{(1\ 2\
3), (1\ 3\ 2)\} (3-cycles). Three classes, sizes 1, 3, 2 (sum 6 ✓).
Conjugacy classes are essential for representation theory and character tables (Tier 7).


The orbit-counting formula you used in Problem 4 is universally called Burnside’s lemma — but William Burnside himself attributed it, in his 1897 Theory of Groups of Finite Order, to Frobenius (1887), who in turn credited Cauchy (1845). The historian Peter Neumann argued in 1979 that this is one of mathematics’ best examples of Stigler’s law of eponymy — that no scientific discovery is named after its actual discoverer — and proposed renaming it the Cauchy–Frobenius lemma. The old name has stuck anyway.


A group on its own is just an abstract algebraic gadget. A group action is the moment that gadget meets the world: each element of G becomes a way of moving the points of X around. The orbit tells you where a point can go; the stabiliser tells you how many ways the group can leave it standing — and the two multiply to recover |G| exactly.

title: 03 · Group Actions and Symmetry
text:
Name

A group action of G on X: G \times X \to X, (g, x) \mapsto g
\cdot x with:

e \cdot x = x.
(gh) \cdot x = g \cdot (h \cdot x).

Orbit of x: \text{Orb}(x) = \{g \cdot x\}. Stabiliser: \text{Stab}(x) = \{g : g \cdot x = x\}.
Orbit-Stabiliser Theorem: |G| = |\text{Orb}(x)| \cdot
|\text{Stab}(x)|.
Burnside’s lemma: number of orbits = \dfrac{1}{|G|} \sum_{g \in G}
|\text{Fix}(g)|.
Conjugation action: g \cdot x = g x g^{-1}. Orbits = conjugacy classes.
Common actions:

S_n on \{1, \ldots, n\}.
D_n on vertices of regular n-gon.
G on itself by conjugation.
G on itself by left multiplication (Cayley action — embeds G in S_{|G|}).



Group action: each element of G permutes elements of X; Orbit-Stabiliser: |G| = |\text{Orb}| \cdot |\text{Stab}|; Burnside counts orbits by averaging fixed points.

title: 03 · Group Actions and Symmetry
text:
Apply

Worked example

Count distinct necklaces with 6 beads, each red or blue (rotations identified).

Group: C_6 rotations. Apply Burnside.



g
Fixed colourings




e (rotation 0)
2^6 = 64


r (60°)
all beads equal: 2


r^2 (120°)
beads in 2 orbits of 3: 2^2 = 4


r^3 (180°)
beads in 3 orbits of 2: 2^3 = 8


r^4 (240°)
as r^2: 4


r^5 (300°)
as r: 2



Sum = 84. Orbits = 84/6 = 14 distinct necklaces.


Practice — Warm
W1. |S_4| = ?


Full solution

24.

W2. Stab of element 1 under S_4 acting on \{1,2,3,4\}?


Full solution

Permutations fixing 1: S_3 on \{2, 3, 4\}. Size 6.

W3. |G| = 12, |\text{Orb}| = 4. |\text{Stab}|?


Full solution

3.



Practice — Working
P1. Orbits of \mathbb{Z}/6 acting on itself by addition.


Full solution

Single orbit (the whole group).

P2. Conjugacy classes of D_4 (8 elements).


Full solution

\{e\}, \{r^2\}, \{r, r^3\}, \{two diagonal reflections\}, \{two edge reflections\}. Five classes.

P3. Square 4-corners, 3 colours, rotations only. Distinct?


Hint 1 — a nudge

C_4. Fixed: e: 81, r: 3, r^2: 9, r^3: 3. Sum 96 / 4 = 24.



Full solution

24.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Rubik’s cube symmetry. Why is the Rubik’s cube group so hard to describe? (Hint: it’s a complicated subgroup of S_{48}, permutations of the 48 non-centre facelets.)


Full solution

The Rubik’s cube group has order 4.3 \times 10^{19}. The 48 facelets are permuted by each move; the group is generated by 6 face rotations.
Group structure determines:

God’s number (the maximum twists needed to solve any configuration: 20).
Solvers (Kociemba’s algorithm uses subgroup decomposition).
Solvability conditions (must preserve certain parities).

Group theory turns “twisting a cube” into combinatorial mathematics.

S2. Why does symmetry matter for game graphics? Symmetric objects (cubes, spheres) need fewer parameters; symmetric animations (mirroring) save artist time.


Full solution

Symmetry exploitation = data savings. A symmetric character mesh can be modeled on one half and mirrored. Procedural texture generation uses group symmetries (wallpaper groups: 17 types of 2D periodic patterns).
Character animations often have left/right symmetric pairs (walk cycles) — capture once, reflect for the other side.
Group theory underlies every procedural / pattern generation pipeline in modern game development.



Common confusions

“All actions are transitive.” Often there are multiple orbits.
“Stabiliser is trivial.” Only for free actions. Most actions have nontrivial stabilisers.
“Burnside is for big problems.” It’s elegant for exactly this class — counting orbits under group actions.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A group action is a group “doing things” to a set — each element becomes a permutation of the underlying points.
Orbit-stabiliser: |G| = |\text{Orb}(x)| \cdot |\text{Stab}(x)| — a single equation that bookkeeps every action and gives Lagrange’s theorem as a corollary.
Burnside’s lemma counts orbits by averaging fixed points — the right tool whenever you need “how many genuinely different arrangements?” under a symmetry group.



Self-Assessment

Could you state the orbit-stabiliser theorem?
Without scrolling back, what’s |D_4|?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: 8.)

3 yes: ready for 04 · Complex Numbers.
2 yes: redo S1 (Rubik’s cube).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P4.

title: 03 · Group Actions and Symmetry
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

01 · Groups



What this unlocks

Tier 7 — Representation Theory — homomorphisms G \to GL(V).
Tier 7 — Algebraic Topology — fundamental groups acting on covering spaces.



Where this matters

Game development. Symmetry exploitation in modeling, animation, texture generation (S2).


Mathematical research. Group actions are the natural setting for many theorems. Pólya enumeration, character tables, classifying spaces.


Chemistry & physics. Molecular symmetry groups classify molecules and predict spectra. Crystallographic groups (230 in 3D) classify crystal structures.

title: 00 · Introduction
text:
Tier 5 gave you the undergraduate core. Tier 6 takes you into the deep waters — abstract structures (groups, rings, fields), the beauty of complex analysis, the calculus of waves and heat flow, optimisation theory, the geometry of information, and the great unifier of analysis: Fourier methods.
By the end of Tier 6 you can:

Reason about groups, rings, fields and their properties.
Work with complex numbers, complex functions, and Cauchy’s integral theorem.
Solve second-order ODEs and analyse PDEs (heat, wave, Laplace).
Apply numerical methods for root finding and ODE integration.
Use convex optimisation and modern gradient methods (SGD, Adam).
Quantify uncertainty with information theory (entropy, mutual information).
Decompose periodic signals with Fourier series and arbitrary signals with the Fourier transform.
Analyse graph properties using spectral methods.
Read the spectral theorem and a preview of functional analysis.
