title: 06 · Second-Order Linear ODEs
text:
Have you done T4 L12 — Differential Equations (Intro)?
Recall y' = ky has solution y = A e^{kx}?
Comfortable with complex numbers (L04)?

title: 06 · Second-Order Linear ODEs
06 · Second-Order Linear ODEs
text:
Wonder

Why does y'' + \omega^2 y = 0 give sinusoidal solutions?
The characteristic equation a r^2 + b r + c = 0 — same algebra as quadratic. Why does the complex roots case give oscillation?
The harmonic oscillator (mass on a spring) m \ddot{x} + k x = 0 is one specific case. What real systems share this equation?

title: 06 · Second-Order Linear ODEs
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Try y = e^{rt} in y'' - 5 y' + 6 y = 0. What equation does r satisfy?


Full solution

y' = r e^{rt}, y'' = r^2 e^{rt}. Substituting: (r^2 - 5r + 6) e^{rt}
= 0. Since e^{rt} \ne 0: r^2 - 5r + 6 = 0.
This is the characteristic equation. Its roots are r = 2, 3.
General solution: y = A e^{2t} + B e^{3t} (linear combination since the ODE is linear).



Problem 2
For y'' + \omega^2 y = 0: characteristic equation is r^2 + \omega^2
= 0 \Rightarrow r = \pm i \omega.
The solutions y = e^{\pm i\omega t} combine (using Euler’s formula) into real solutions:


Full solution

y = A \cos \omega t + B \sin \omega t.
The simple harmonic oscillator! Frequency \omega/(2\pi) Hz.
Springs, pendulums (small angles), LC circuits, atomic vibrations — all governed by this equation.



Problem 3
For y'' + 2y' + 5y = 0: characteristic equation r^2 + 2r + 5 = 0
\Rightarrow r = -1 \pm 2i.
Find the real general solution.


Full solution

Combining e^{(-1 + 2i)t} and e^{(-1 - 2i)t} into reals: y = e^{-t} (A \cos 2t + B \sin 2t).
Damped oscillation: the e^{-t} factor decays, the cosine/sine oscillates. A real pendulum with friction.



Problem 4
For y'' + 4y' + 4y = 0: characteristic equation has double root r = -2.
The general solution is not A e^{-2t} + B e^{-2t} (only one parameter). Need a second solution: y_2 = t e^{-2t}. So y = (A + B t) e^{-2t}.
Verify y_2 satisfies the ODE.


Full solution

y_2 = t e^{-2t}. y_2' = e^{-2t} - 2t e^{-2t} = (1 - 2t) e^{-2t}. y_2'' = (-2)(1 - 2t) e^{-2t} + (-2) e^{-2t} = (-4 + 4t) e^{-2t}.
Plug in: y_2'' + 4 y_2' + 4 y_2 = ((-4 + 4t) + 4(1 - 2t) + 4t) e^{-2t}
= 0 ✓.
Critical damping: the boundary between under-damped (oscillates) and over-damped (no oscillation). Used in shock absorbers (cars return to neutral as fast as possible without oscillation).



Problem 5
Forced oscillation: y'' + \omega^2 y = F \cos(\omega_0 t) (driving force at frequency \omega_0). Resonance occurs when \omega_0 = \omega.
What happens to the amplitude in resonance?


Full solution

Amplitude grows linearly with time (without damping). With damping, amplitude reaches a peak proportional to 1/\text{damping} — resonance amplification.
Famous failures from resonance: Tacoma Narrows Bridge (1940, collapsed), opera singer breaking glass, swing pumping.
Resonance design is essential in mechanical engineering, civil engineering, and acoustics.


Joseph-Louis Lagrange wrote out the wave equation \partial_{tt} u = c^2 \partial_{xx} u in 1759 while still in his early twenties, in a Turin Academy paper on the vibrating string. Jean le Rond d’Alembert had published the left-and-right-travelling-waves solution u(x,t) = f(x-ct) +
g(x+ct) just two years earlier (1747). The trick of substituting y = e^{rt} into a constant-coefficient linear ODE — the move that turns a calculus problem into the quadratic equation for r — is Euler’s, from his 1743 paper De integratione aequationum differentialium altiorum graduum. Three of the eighteenth century’s giants, in twenty years, gave you the entire toolkit you used in this lesson.


A second-order linear ODE with constant coefficients is, after one substitution, just a quadratic equation. The discriminant of that quadratic decides everything: real distinct roots give pure exponentials, a repeated root gives an exponential dressed with a t, and complex conjugate roots give the oscillation that shows up in every spring, pendulum, and circuit you’ll ever model.

title: 06 · Second-Order Linear ODEs
text:
Name

A second-order linear ODE with constant coefficients: a y'' + b y' + c y = 0.
Characteristic equation: a r^2 + b r + c = 0.
Three cases (discriminant \Delta = b^2 - 4ac):








\Delta
Roots
General solution




> 0
Two distinct real r_1, r_2
A e^{r_1 t} + B e^{r_2 t}


= 0
Repeated root r
(A + B t) e^{r t}


< 0
Complex pair \alpha \pm i \beta
e^{\alpha t}(A \cos \beta t + B \sin \beta t)



Initial conditions y(0), y'(0) determine A, B.
Forced (non-homogeneous) ODE: a y'' + b y' + c y = f(t). General solution: homogeneous + particular.
Resonance: when forcing matches natural frequency, amplitude grows unboundedly (no damping) or to a large peak (with damping).


Linear, constant coefficients: substitute y = e^(rt), get char eq; Three cases: real distinct, repeated, complex (oscillation); Resonance: forcing at the natural frequency builds large amplitudes.

title: 06 · Second-Order Linear ODEs
text:
Apply

Worked example

Solve y'' - 4y' + 13y = 0 with y(0) = 1, y'(0) = 0.

Char eq: r^2 - 4r + 13 = 0 \Rightarrow r = 2 \pm 3i.
General: y = e^{2t} (A \cos 3t + B \sin 3t).
y(0) = A = 1. y'(0) = 2A + 3B = 0 \Rightarrow B = -2/3.
y = e^{2t} (\cos 3t - (2/3) \sin 3t). (Growing oscillation — exponential envelope e^{2t} amplifies the oscillation.)


Practice — Warm
W1. Char eq for y'' + 4y = 0?


Full solution

r^2 + 4 = 0, roots \pm 2i.

W2. General solution of y'' = 0?


Full solution

y = A + Bt (repeated root r = 0).

W3. y'' - y = 0. Solution?


Full solution

r^2 - 1 = 0, r = \pm 1. y = A e^t + B e^{-t}.



Practice — Working
P1. Solve y'' + 6y' + 9y = 0.


Full solution

r^2 + 6r + 9 = (r+3)^2 = 0. Repeated root -3. y = (A + Bt) e^{-3t}.

P2. Solve y'' + y' - 6y = 0 with y(0) = 5, y'(0) = 0.


Full solution

r = -3, 2. y = A e^{-3t} + B e^{2t}. y(0): A + B = 5. y'(0): -3A
+ 2B = 0 \Rightarrow B = 3A/2. So A + 3A/2 = 5 \Rightarrow A = 2, B
= 3.
y = 2 e^{-3t} + 3 e^{2t}.

P3. Solve y'' + 4y' + 5y = 0.


Full solution

r = -2 \pm i. y = e^{-2t} (A \cos t + B \sin t).



Practice — Stretch
S1. The pendulum equation. Small-angle approximation: \ddot
\theta + (g/L) \theta = 0. What’s the period?


Full solution

\omega^2 = g/L \Rightarrow \omega = \sqrt{g/L}. Period T = 2\pi/\omega
= 2\pi \sqrt{L/g}.
(Galileo’s pendulum law. Period depends only on length, not on amplitude or mass — a remarkable physical fact, true only for small oscillations. Powered the first accurate clocks.)

S2. In game physics, springs are everywhere — character joints, suspension, cloth, hair, water. Why is the simple harmonic oscillator at the heart of game physics?


Full solution

Springs model elasticity / restoration force. Hooke’s law: F = -k
x. Combined with damping -c v, Newton’s F = ma gives m \ddot
x + c \dot x + k x = 0 — the damped oscillator.
Game physics engines integrate this equation millions of times per second:

Cloth: spring network simulating fabric.
Hair / fur: chains of springs.
Vehicle suspension: spring + damper between wheel and chassis.
Soft-body objects: lattice of springs.

Tuning k (stiffness) and c (damping) gives the desired feel. The critical damping case (Discover P4) makes things settle without overshoot.



Common confusions

“All ODEs have closed-form solutions.” Only special classes (linear, constant coefficients, separable). Most ODEs need numerical methods (Lesson 08).
“Forced ODE has only homogeneous solution.” No — general solution = homogeneous + particular.
“Real solutions require real characteristic roots.” No: complex roots give real sinusoidal solutions (via Euler).



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

Substitute y = e^{rt} and the differential equation collapses to a quadratic — the characteristic equation in r.
Three cases by discriminant: real distinct roots give two exponentials, a repeated root needs a te^{rt} second solution, and a complex conjugate pair gives damped or pure oscillation via Euler’s formula.
Resonance happens when forcing frequency matches the natural frequency — the engineering reason bridges, glass, and swings amplify particular driving signals to spectacular effect.



Self-Assessment

Could you solve y'' + 9y = 0?
Without scrolling back, what’s resonance?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answers: y = A \cos 3t + B \sin 3t. Forcing at the natural frequency.)

3 yes: ready for 07 · PDEs.
2 yes: redo S2 (springs in games).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P4.

title: 06 · Second-Order Linear ODEs
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

T4 L12 — Differential Equations — first-order foundation.



What this unlocks

07 · PDEs — partial differential equations.
Tier 7 — Dynamical Systems — qualitative theory of ODEs.



Where this matters

Game development. Spring physics (S2), oscillating animations, character motion, vehicle suspensions.


Engineering. Mechanical vibrations, electrical circuits (RLC), control systems — all governed by 2nd-order ODEs.


Physics. Quantum harmonic oscillator (foundational quantum mechanics), classical pendulums and springs, electromagnetic oscillations.

title: 08 · Numerical Methods
text:
Have you done T4 L07 — Related Rates / Linear Approximation?
Recall Newton’s method (T4 L06 S2)?
Comfortable with linear systems (T5 L02)?

title: 08 · Numerical Methods
08 · Numerical Methods
text:
Wonder

Newton’s method is fast but needs f'. Bisection is slow but robust. What’s the trade-off?
ODE solvers integrate forward in time. How accurate is a single step?
Why does halving the step often quadruple the accuracy in good methods?

title: 08 · Numerical Methods
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Newton’s method. To find a root of f: iterate x_{n+1} = x_n -
f(x_n)/f'(x_n).
Find a root of f(x) = x^3 - 2 starting at x_0 = 1.


Hint 1 — a nudge

f'(x) = 3 x^2. x_{n+1} = x_n - (x_n^3 - 2)/(3 x_n^2) = (2 x_n^3 +
2)/(3 x_n^2) = (2 x_n + 2/x_n^2)/3.



Full solution


x_0 = 1. f(1) = -1. x_1 = 1 - (-1)/3 = 4/3 \approx 1.333.
x_1 = 1.333. x_1^3 = 2.370. f = 0.370. x_2 = 1.333 -
0.370/(3 \cdot 1.778) = 1.264.
x_3 \approx 1.260, x_4 \approx 1.2599 — converging to \sqrt[3]{2}
\approx 1.2599.

Quadratic convergence when close: number of correct digits roughly doubles each step.



Problem 2
Bisection method. Find a root of f on [a, b] with f(a) \cdot
f(b) < 0 (sign change). Iteratively halve.
Apply to f(x) = x^2 - 2 on [1, 2] for 4 iterations.


Full solution


f(1) = -1, f(2) = 2. Midpoint 1.5, f(1.5) = 0.25 > 0. Recurse on [1, 1.5].
Midpoint 1.25, f = -0.4375. Recurse on [1.25, 1.5].
Midpoint 1.375, f = -0.109. Recurse on [1.375, 1.5].
Midpoint 1.4375, f = 0.066. Recurse on [1.375, 1.4375].

After 4 iterations: root in [1.375, 1.4375]. Linear convergence: one bit per iteration. Slow but always works.



Problem 3
Euler’s method. For y' = f(t, y) with y(t_0) = y_0: iterate y_{n+1} = y_n + h f(t_n, y_n).
Apply to y' = y, y(0) = 1, h = 0.1, for 3 steps. Compare with exact y = e^t.


Full solution


y_0 = 1. y_1 = 1 + 0.1 \cdot 1 = 1.1. (Exact: e^{0.1} \approx
1.1052.)
y_2 = 1.1 + 0.1 \cdot 1.1 = 1.21. (Exact: e^{0.2} \approx 1.2214.)
y_3 = 1.331. (Exact: e^{0.3} \approx 1.3499.)

Error grows \sim h per step → cumulative O(h) over fixed time. First-order method.



Problem 4
Runge-Kutta 4 (RK4). Higher-order ODE solver: k_1 = f(t_n, y_n), \quad k_2 = f(t_n + h/2, y_n + h k_1/2), k_3 = f(t_n + h/2, y_n + h k_2/2), \quad k_4 = f(t_n + h, y_n + h k_3), y_{n+1} = y_n + (h/6)(k_1 + 2 k_2 + 2 k_3 + k_4).
Why is this so much better than Euler?


Full solution

RK4 has O(h^4) accuracy per step, vs Euler’s O(h^2). Halving h → 16× more accurate (vs Euler’s 4×).
For most problems, RK4 is the default: cheap, accurate, easy to implement. Built into MATLAB, SciPy, virtually every numerical library.



Problem 5
Trapezoidal rule. \int_a^b f \approx h (f(x_0)/2 + f(x_1) +
\cdots + f(x_{n-1}) + f(x_n)/2) with h = (b-a)/n.
Apply to \int_0^1 x^2 \, dx with n = 4.


Full solution

h = 0.25. f values at 0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1: 0, 0.0625, 0.25,
0.5625, 1.
Sum: 0/2 + 0.0625 + 0.25 + 0.5625 + 1/2 = 1.375. Trapezoid: 0.25 \cdot 1.375/4… let me redo.
Trap formula: h \cdot (sum). 0.25 \cdot 1.375 = 0.34375.
Exact: 1/3 \approx 0.333. Slightly over (trap rule overestimates for convex functions). Error O(h^2) scales predictably.
Simpson’s rule (uses parabolic interpolation): error O(h^4) — much faster convergence.


Newton described the root-finding iteration you used in Problem 1 in 1669, in his De analysi per aequationes numero terminorum infinitas, but only as a method for polynomials and without the derivative-based form we now teach. Joseph Raphson gave the modern recursive update in 1690 — which is why the method is also called Newton–Raphson. Three centuries later, the IEEE 754 standard (1985, revised 2008 and 2019) finally pinned down what “floating-point arithmetic” even means across hardware vendors, and James H. Wilkinson’s 1965 Rounding Errors in Algebraic Processes introduced backward error analysis — the idea that a numerical answer is correct if it’s the exact answer to a slightly perturbed problem. Modern numerical analysis stands on these three legs.


Numerical methods don’t replace mathematics — they honour it by admitting that almost no real problem has a closed form. The unifying knob across the whole subject is the convergence order p: error per step shrinks like h^p, so halving the step multiplies accuracy by 2^p. Newton’s quadratic (p = 2 in error of error), RK4’s p = 4, Simpson’s p = 4 — choosing p is choosing how fast you trade compute for digits.

title: 08 · Numerical Methods
text:
Name

Numerical methods = algorithms that approximate solutions when closed forms are unavailable.
Root finding:

Newton’s method: x_{n+1} = x_n - f(x_n)/f'(x_n). Quadratic convergence; needs derivative.
Bisection: halve a sign-change bracket. Linear; always works.
Secant method: Newton-like without derivative; superlinear.

ODE integration (y' = f(t, y)):

Euler: y_{n+1} = y_n + h f(t_n, y_n). O(h) accuracy.
RK4: averages 4 evaluations. O(h^4).
Stiff equations need implicit methods (backward Euler, Rosenbrock).

Numerical integration:

Trapezoidal rule: O(h^2).
Simpson’s rule: O(h^4) for smooth integrands.
Gauss quadrature: ultra-high order via clever node placement.

Linear systems:

Gaussian elimination: O(n^3) direct.
Iterative: Jacobi, Gauss-Seidel, conjugate gradient, GMRES — faster for sparse / large systems.

Convergence order p: error per step \sim h^p. Higher p = better accuracy for given h.


Numerical methods solve what algebra can’t; Order p: halving step → 2^p improvement; Newton (fast), bisection (robust), RK4 (default ODE), Simpson (default integral).

title: 08 · Numerical Methods
text:
Apply

Worked example

Use Newton’s method to find \sqrt{5} to 4 decimals.

f(x) = x^2 - 5. Newton: x_{n+1} = (x_n + 5/x_n)/2.

x_0 = 2. x_1 = (2 + 2.5)/2 = 2.25.
x_2 = (2.25 + 5/2.25)/2 = (2.25 + 2.222)/2 = 2.236.
x_3 = (2.236 + 2.236)/2 \approx 2.2361.

\sqrt{5} \approx 2.2361 in 3 iterations.


Practice — Warm
W1. Newton’s update for f(x) = x^2 - a?


Full solution

x_{n+1} = (x_n + a/x_n)/2 — Babylonian square root.

W2. Bisection on [0, 1] for 5 iterations: bracket width?


Full solution

1/32 \approx 0.031.

W3. Order of trapezoidal rule?


Full solution

2.



Practice — Working
P1. Apply Newton’s method to find a root of f(x) = \cos x - x starting at x_0 = 0.5, 3 iterations.


Full solution

f'(x) = -\sin x - 1. x_1 = 0.5 - (\cos 0.5 - 0.5)/(-\sin 0.5 - 1) =
0.5 - 0.378/(-1.479) \approx 0.7552.
x_2 \approx 0.7391. x_3 \approx 0.7391. Dottie number.

P2. One Euler step for y' = y^2, y(0) = 1, h = 0.1.


Full solution

y_1 = 1 + 0.1 \cdot 1 = 1.1. (Exact y = 1/(1 - t) = 1.111, blows up at t = 1.)

P3. \int_0^\pi \sin x \, dx by trapezoidal rule with n = 2.


Full solution

h = \pi/2. f(0) = 0, f(\pi/2) = 1, f(\pi) = 0. Sum: 0/2 + 1 +
0/2 = 1. Trap: \pi/2 \cdot 1 = \pi/2 \approx 1.571.
(Exact: 2. Coarse n gives 21% error.)



Practice — Stretch
S1. Why is the condition number important? A linear system Ax = b might have a “well-determined” solution mathematically but be wildly sensitive to small errors in A or b — ill-conditioned.


Full solution

Condition number \kappa(A) = \|A\| \cdot \|A^{-1}\|. If \kappa is huge, small input errors get amplified. Newton’s method, matrix inversion, and many algorithms degrade.
In ML: ill-conditioned Hessians cause slow convergence of gradient descent. Modern optimisers (Adam) effectively rescale the gradient to handle this.
In physics simulation: \kappa governs stability and accuracy budgets. Sometimes the model is intrinsically ill-conditioned and needs a different formulation.
Numerical analysis is the discipline of designing algorithms to control these errors.

S2. Why are GPUs so fast at numerical linear algebra?


Full solution

Matrix operations are embarrassingly parallel — each output entry can be computed independently. GPUs have thousands of small cores; each handles a subset of the matrix.
The theoretical peak of a top GPU is ~50 TFLOPS (50 trillion floating-point ops/sec). Modern AI training relies entirely on this parallelism.
Tensor cores (NVIDIA, since Volta 2017) accelerate mixed-precision matrix multiply specifically for ML, achieving even higher throughput.
The numerical methods you just learned scale up to industrial training runs because of this hardware-algorithm co-evolution.



Common confusions

“Newton’s method always converges.” Only with a good initial guess in a region where f' doesn’t vanish.
“Higher order = better.” Not for non-smooth integrands. Choose method based on problem.
“Numerical = approximate (so inferior).” Most real-world problems have no closed form. Numerical methods are the only answer.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

Newton’s method finds roots by repeatedly linearising the function and stepping to the linear root — quadratic convergence when it works, but it needs the derivative and a decent guess.
Higher-order methods buy accuracy with sub-steps, not finer grids — RK4’s four function evaluations per step give O(h^4) error, vs Euler’s O(h), for the same number of grid points.
The condition number \kappa(A) tells you how much input error a linear system amplifies — and is the right first diagnostic when a numerical method silently produces nonsense.



Self-Assessment

Could you state Newton’s method?
Without scrolling back, what’s the order of RK4?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: 4.)

3 yes: ready for 09 · Convex Optimisation.
2 yes: redo S1 (condition number).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P4.

title: 08 · Numerical Methods
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

T4 L07 — Related Rates / Linear Approximation — Newton’s method.
T5 L02 — Linear Systems



What this unlocks

09 · Convex Optimisation
10 · Gradient Methods



Where this matters

AI / Machine Learning. Every training step is numerical linear algebra at scale (S2). Optimisers, ODE solvers (in neural ODEs), Bayesian methods — all numerical.


Engineering. Finite element, finite volume methods are discretised PDEs. Computational fluid dynamics, structural analysis.


Game development. Physics integrators (Verlet, RK4, semi-implicit Euler). Real-time collision detection. Procedural generation.

title: 04 · Complex Numbers and Operations
text:
Have you done T2 L14 — Quadratic Equations and T3 L08 — Unit Circle?
Recall that x^2 + 1 = 0 has no real solution?
Comfortable with \sin, \cos and the unit circle?

title: 04 · Complex Numbers and Operations
04 · Complex Numbers and Operations
text:
Wonder

\sqrt{-1} has no real solution. So why “invent” a number i for it?
Multiplying complex numbers rotates them. Why?
Euler’s identity e^{i\pi} + 1 = 0 — why is this profound?

title: 04 · Complex Numbers and Operations
text:
Discover

Problem 1
A complex number z = a + bi, a, b \in \mathbb{R}. The conjugate is \bar{z} = a - bi. Modulus: |z| = \sqrt{a^2 +
b^2}.
Compute |3 + 4i|, \overline{2 - 5i}, (1 + i)(1 - i).


Full solution

|3 + 4i| = \sqrt{9 + 16} = 5. \overline{2 - 5i} = 2 + 5i. (1 + i)(1 - i) = 1 - i^2 = 1 + 1 = 2.
Note: z \bar{z} = |z|^2 for any z.



Problem 2
Division: to compute 1/(a + bi), multiply by conjugate: \dfrac{1}{a + bi} \cdot \dfrac{a - bi}{a - bi} = \dfrac{a - bi}{a^2 +
b^2}.
Compute 1/(3 + 4i).


Full solution

\dfrac{3 - 4i}{25} = 0.12 - 0.16 i.



Problem 3
Polar form. Every z \neq 0 writes as z = r (\cos \theta + i
\sin \theta) = r e^{i \theta} where r = |z|, \theta = \arg(z).
Convert 1 + i to polar form.


Full solution

|1 + i| = \sqrt{2}. \arg(1 + i) = \pi/4 (first quadrant, equal real/imag).
1 + i = \sqrt{2} \, e^{i \pi/4}.



Problem 4
Euler’s formula: e^{i \theta} = \cos \theta + i \sin \theta.
Special case: \theta = \pi: e^{i \pi} = \cos \pi + i \sin \pi = -1 + 0 = -1. Rearranging: e^{i \pi} + 1 = 0 — Euler’s identity.
Why is this so famous?


Full solution

It connects the five most important constants: e (calculus base), i (imaginary unit), \pi (geometry), 1 (multiplicative identity), 0 (additive identity), via the most basic operations (+, \cdot, =, exponentiation).
Often called “the most beautiful equation in mathematics.” A unification of analysis (e), algebra (i), and geometry (\pi).
The general e^{i\theta} = \cos \theta + i \sin \theta is even more useful: it converts trig identities into exponential identities, making derivations much shorter.



Problem 5
Multiplication as rotation. z \cdot w = (r e^{i \theta}) (s e^{i
\phi}) = (rs) e^{i (\theta + \phi)}.
Use this to compute (1 + i)(1 - i) in polar.


Hint 1 — a nudge

1 + i = \sqrt{2} e^{i\pi/4}, 1 - i = \sqrt{2} e^{-i\pi/4}.



Full solution

(1 + i)(1 - i) = (\sqrt{2})^2 e^{i (\pi/4 - \pi/4)} = 2 \cdot 1 = 2 ✓ (matches direct).
Multiplying complex numbers = rotating + scaling. This makes complex numbers ideal for representing 2D rotations.


The Italian engineer Rafael Bombelli stumbled onto \sqrt{-1} in 1572 while staring at Cardano’s cubic formula, which kept producing square roots of negative numbers en route to perfectly real answers. He swallowed his discomfort, declared the rules +\sqrt{-1} \cdot +\sqrt{-1} = -1 in his Algebra, and the calculations worked. The geometric picture took two more centuries: Caspar Wessel (1797), Jean-Robert Argand (1806), and finally Carl Friedrich Gauss (1831) showed that complex numbers live naturally on a plane. On 16 October 1843 William Rowan Hamilton carved i^2 = j^2 = k^2 = ijk = -1 into the stonework of Brougham Bridge in Dublin — extending the idea to 4D quaternions, the rotations now used by every game engine in the world.


The single rule i^2 = -1 does something the real numbers never could: it turns multiplication into rotation. Once you write z = r e^{i\theta}, multiplying by z scales by r and spins by \theta. Geometry and algebra become two views of the same object — and that fusion is why complex numbers refuse to stay inside pure mathematics.

title: 04 · Complex Numbers and Operations
text:
Name

Complex number z = a + bi with a, b \in \mathbb{R} and i^2 =
-1.
Operations:

(a + bi) + (c + di) = (a + c) + (b + d) i.
(a + bi)(c + di) = (ac - bd) + (ad + bc) i.
Conjugate \bar{z} = a - bi. z \bar{z} = a^2 + b^2 = |z|^2.
1/z = \bar{z} / |z|^2.

Polar form: z = r e^{i \theta} with r = |z|, \theta = \arg(z).
Multiplication in polar: magnitudes multiply, arguments add. |zw| = |z| |w|, \arg(zw) = \arg(z) + \arg(w).
Euler’s formula: e^{i \theta} = \cos \theta + i \sin \theta.
Euler’s identity: e^{i \pi} + 1 = 0.
De Moivre’s theorem: (\cos \theta + i \sin \theta)^n = \cos n
\theta + i \sin n \theta.
The complex numbers \mathbb{C} form a field (Lesson 02), are algebraically closed (every polynomial of degree n has n complex roots — fundamental theorem of algebra).


i² = −1 — the defining axiom; Polar form: r e^(iθ) reveals multiplication as rotation+scaling; Euler’s identity: e^(iπ) + 1 = 0 unites e, i, π, 1, 0.

title: 04 · Complex Numbers and Operations
text:
Apply

Worked example

Compute (1 + i)^8.

In polar: 1 + i = \sqrt{2} e^{i \pi/4}. So (1 + i)^8 =
(\sqrt{2})^8 e^{i \cdot 8 \pi/4} = 16 e^{i 2\pi} = 16 \cdot 1 = 16.
(Direct expansion would be much more tedious.)


Practice — Warm
W1. (2 + 3i) + (1 - i) = ?


Full solution

3 + 2i.

W2. |5 + 12i| = ?


Full solution

13.

W3. i^4 = ?


Full solution

1 (since i^2 = -1, so i^4 = (-1)^2 = 1).



Practice — Working
P1. Solve z^2 = -9.


Full solution

z = \pm 3i.

P2. Convert -1 + i \sqrt{3} to polar.


Full solution

|z| = 2. \arg = 2\pi/3 (second quadrant). So z = 2 e^{i 2\pi/3}.

P3. (2 e^{i \pi/3})(3 e^{i \pi/6}) = ?


Full solution

6 e^{i \pi/2} = 6 i.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Solve z^3 = 1. Find all 3 cube roots of unity.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Polar: 1 = e^{i 0} = e^{i 2\pi} = e^{i 4\pi} = \ldots. Take cube roots: z = e^{i 0/3}, e^{i 2\pi/3}, e^{i 4\pi/3}.



Full solution

z = 1, e^{i 2\pi/3}, e^{i 4\pi/3}. Equivalently: z = 1, -1/2 + i \sqrt{3}/2, -1/2 - i \sqrt{3}/2.
In general, z^n = 1 has n solutions: the n-th roots of unity e^{i 2\pi k/n}, equally spaced on the unit circle.
These are the eigenvalues of the cyclic permutation matrix and play key roles in Fourier analysis (Lessons 12, 13) and the Discrete Fourier Transform.

S2. Quaternions generalise complex numbers to 4D: q = a + bi
+ cj + dk with i^2 = j^2 = k^2 = ijk = -1. Used for 3D rotations in graphics and robotics. Why are quaternions preferred over rotation matrices?


Full solution

Quaternions:

4 numbers vs 9 for a 3×3 rotation matrix (compact).
No “gimbal lock” (a degeneracy in Euler-angle representations).
Smooth interpolation (SLERP — spherical linear interpolation) between orientations.
Natural composition by multiplication.

Used in: every modern game engine (Unity, Unreal), spacecraft attitude control, robotic arm orientation, motion capture, virtual reality.
The complex numbers’ rotation-via-multiplication property generalises to quaternions. Tier 7 (Game Dev track) develops this.



Common confusions

“i is imaginary, so it’s not real.” Yes — but it’s a valid number. “Imaginary” is a historical term; complex numbers are as “real” as the reals.
“Conjugate just changes sign.” It changes sign of the imaginary part only. Useful for division and computing modulus.
“Polar form is just notation.” It encodes deep geometric meaning: multiplication by e^{i\theta} is rotation by \theta.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A complex number z = a + bi is a point in the plane with the rule i^2 = -1 glued on — and that one rule turns the real line into a 2D arena where every polynomial finally factors.
In polar form z = r e^{i\theta}, multiplication = rotate by \theta and scale by r — the geometry of \mathbb{C} in one move, and the reason 2D rotations feel so natural here.
Euler’s identity e^{i\pi} +
1 = 0 stitches together e, i, \pi, 1, and 0 in a single equation — the most-cited candidate for the most beautiful formula in mathematics.



Self-Assessment

Could you state Euler’s identity?
Without scrolling back, what’s (1 + i)(1 - i)?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answers: e^{i\pi} + 1 = 0; 2.)

3 yes: ready for 05 · Complex Functions.
2 yes: redo S2 (quaternions).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P4.

Where this matters

Engineering & physics. AC circuit analysis (impedance is complex). Quantum mechanics (states are complex vectors). Signal processing (Fourier transforms operate over \mathbb{C}).


Mathematical research. Complex analysis (Lesson 05) reveals deep symmetries; the Riemann hypothesis is a statement about complex zeros of the zeta function.

title: 11 · Information Theory — Entropy and Channel Capacity
text:
Have you done T3 L13 — Basic Probability and T3 L05 — Logarithms?
Comfortable with \log_2 as bits?
Recall expected value E[X] = \sum p_i x_i?
crumbs: 11 · Information Theory — Entropy and Channel Capacity

title: 11 · Information Theory — Entropy and Channel Capacity
crumbs: 11 · Information Theory — Entropy and Channel Capacity

title: 11 · Information Theory — Entropy and Channel Capacity
text:
Wonder

Why is entropy maximised by the uniform distribution?
What does it mean to “communicate H bits per symbol on average”?
Shannon’s source coding theorem: entropy H is the minimum bits per symbol any lossless code can achieve.
crumbs: 11 · Information Theory — Entropy and Channel Capacity

title: 11 · Information Theory — Entropy and Channel Capacity
text:
Discover

Problem 1
For a fair coin: H = -(\tfrac{1}{2} \log_2 \tfrac{1}{2} +
\tfrac{1}{2} \log_2 \tfrac{1}{2}) = -(\tfrac{1}{2} \cdot (-1) +
\tfrac{1}{2} \cdot (-1)) = 1 bit.
Compute H for a fair 4-sided die.


Full solution

H = -4 \cdot \tfrac{1}{4} \log_2 \tfrac{1}{4} = \log_2 4 = 2 bits.
For uniform on n outcomes: H = \log_2 n.



Problem 2
Compute H for a biased coin with p = 0.9 (heads) and 0.1 (tails).


Full solution

H = -(0.9 \log_2 0.9 + 0.1 \log_2 0.1) = -(0.9 \cdot -0.152 + 0.1
\cdot -3.322) = 0.137 + 0.332 = 0.469 bits.
Less than 1 bit per toss — because outcomes are predictable. You can compress a sequence of biased coin flips below 1 bit per toss.
(The fair coin maximises entropy at 1; bias reduces it.)



Problem 3
Why log_2? H is interpreted as bits — average yes/no questions needed to identify the outcome. With n outcomes, \lceil \log_2 n
\rceil questions suffice (binary search).
What if we use \log_e = \ln instead? What unit?


Full solution

Nats (natural units of information). 1 nat = \log_2 e \approx
1.44 bits.
For machine learning loss functions, \ln is more convenient (differentiation gives clean formulas).



Problem 4
Mutual information I(X; Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X, Y) measures how much knowing Y reduces uncertainty about X. Equivalently, I(X; Y) = H(X) - H(X | Y) where H(X | Y) is conditional entropy.
If X and Y are independent, what is I?


Full solution

I(X; Y) = 0. Independence means H(X | Y) = H(X) — knowing Y gives no information about X.
Mutual information is always non-negative and equals 0 iff independent. Used in:

Feature selection (which features inform the label most?).
Network analysis (information flow).
Statistical physics (entropy + mutual information of subsystems).




Problem 5
Shannon’s channel coding theorem. A noisy channel with capacity C bits/symbol can transmit at any rate R < C with arbitrarily low error (for sufficiently long codes). At R > C: errors unavoidable.
For a binary symmetric channel (flips bit with probability p): capacity C = 1 - H(p).
Compute C for p = 0.1.


Full solution

H(0.1) = -(0.1 \log 0.1 + 0.9 \log 0.9) \approx 0.469. C = 1 -
0.469 = 0.531 bits per channel use.
So a 10%-noisy channel can transmit ~0.53 useful bits per channel use. Modern error-correcting codes (LDPC, polar codes) approach this limit — fundamental to wireless communications, deep-space transmission (Voyager, Mars rovers).


Information theory was founded in a single 1948 paper. A Mathematical Theory of Communication, by Claude Shannon at Bell Labs, defined entropy, mutual information, channel capacity, the source coding theorem, and the channel coding theorem all at once. Before that paper, “information” was a vague engineering notion; after it, it was a measurable quantity in bits. Shannon worked partly from his wartime experience on cryptography for the SIGSALY voice scrambler used by Roosevelt and Churchill — the same machinery that classifies a ciphertext also tells you the least number of bits a faithful copy of a message must occupy. Shannon later built a maze-running mechanical mouse named Theseus, one of the first machine-learning demos, in his spare time.


Entropy answers a single question: on average, how surprised will you be? A fair coin: maximally surprised — 1 full bit per flip. A loaded coin that almost always lands heads: barely surprised — far less than 1 bit. Compression, channel capacity, and the cross-entropy loss your neural net minimises are all the same “average surprise” measured in three different costumes.
crumbs: 11 · Information Theory — Entropy and Channel Capacity

title: 11 · Information Theory — Entropy and Channel Capacity
text:
Name

Entropy of discrete random variable X with PMF p: H(X) = -\sum_i p_i \log_2 p_i.
Units: bits (using \log_2), nats (using \ln).
Properties:

H(X) \ge 0. H = 0 iff deterministic.
H \le \log_2 n where n is the number of outcomes; equal iff uniform.
H(X, Y) \le H(X) + H(Y), equal iff independent.

Conditional entropy: H(X | Y) = E_Y[H(X | Y = y)].
Mutual information: I(X; Y) = H(X) - H(X | Y) = H(X) + H(Y) -
H(X, Y) \ge 0.
Cross entropy (between distributions p, q): H(p, q) = -\sum p_i \log q_i.
KL divergence: D_{KL}(p \| q) = \sum p_i \log(p_i / q_i) =
H(p, q) - H(p) \ge 0.
Shannon’s source coding theorem: H = minimum average bits per symbol for lossless coding.
Shannon’s channel coding theorem: capacity C = max rate for arbitrarily-low-error transmission.


Entropy: average bits to identify an outcome; Uniform = max entropy; deterministic = zero entropy; Mutual information: knowledge of Y about X; Cross entropy = ML loss; channel capacity = comms limit.
crumbs: 11 · Information Theory — Entropy and Channel Capacity

title: 11 · Information Theory — Entropy and Channel Capacity
text:
Apply

Worked example

An English letter (uniform over 26): H = \log_2 26 \approx 4.7 bits. Real English (after accounting for letter frequencies and sequential dependencies): \approx 1 bit per character.

This is why English text compresses to about 25% of its raw size (gzip, etc.) — Shannon’s bound. Letter sequences carry less than \log_2 26 bits per character due to redundancy.


Practice — Warm
W1. H of a uniform 8-outcome distribution?


Full solution

\log_2 8 = 3 bits.

W2. H of a deterministic outcome?


Full solution

0 bits.

W3. H for p = (0.5, 0.25, 0.25)?


Full solution

0.5 \cdot 1 + 0.25 \cdot 2 + 0.25 \cdot 2 = 1.5 bits.



Practice — Working
P1. H for p = (0.5, 0.5) vs (0.99, 0.01). Which is higher?


Full solution

First: 1 bit. Second: \approx 0.081 bits. Fair coin is higher (maximum entropy on 2 outcomes).

P2. Mutual information of two independent fair coins?


Full solution

I = 0 (independent).

P3. Capacity of a binary symmetric channel with p = 0.5 (random flipping)?


Full solution

H(0.5) = 1. C = 1 - 1 = 0 bits/use. No information transmits through a maximally noisy channel.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Cross entropy in ML. A classifier outputs probabilities \hat p; truth is p. Cross entropy loss: L = -\sum p_i \log \hat p_i.
If true label is i (so p_i = 1, others 0): L = -\log \hat p_i. Why is this the right loss?


Full solution

Penalty for being wrong with confidence. L \to \infty as \hat
p_i \to 0 (predicting the right class with zero probability is catastrophic). L = 0 when \hat p_i = 1 (perfect).
KL divergence connection: minimising cross entropy = minimising D_{KL}(p \| \hat p) + constant. So you’re minimising the “distance” from your prediction to truth.
Cross entropy is the standard classification loss in deep learning. Information theory directly motivates it.

S2. Compression and AI. Modern language models like GPT can be viewed as probabilistic models of text. The model assigns probability to each next token. Lower cross-entropy on test text means better compression.
What’s the connection between language modeling and intelligence?


Full solution

Compression = understanding. Hutter’s prize for lossless text compression of Wikipedia argues that compressing it well requires understanding it (knowing what’s redundant requires knowing what the text means).
LLMs that predict next tokens with low entropy implicitly model syntax, grammar, common knowledge, reasoning. Their training objective (cross-entropy) directly optimises for compression of human text.
Some researchers argue this is why LLMs work: the path to general intelligence might be the path to optimal text compression. Others disagree. The debate is active and consequential for AI safety.



Common confusions

“Higher entropy = more order.” Reverse — higher entropy = more disorder / unpredictability.
“Bits are exact information per observation.” Bits are expected (average); a single observation might be more or less informative than H.
“Channel capacity is theoretical.” Modern codes (LDPC, polar) approach Shannon’s bound within fractions of a percent. Far from purely theoretical.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

Entropy H(X) = -\sum p_i \log p_i is the average number of bits needed to encode one outcome — maximised by uniform distributions, zero for certainties.
Mutual information I(X;Y) is “how much Y tells you about X” — zero when independent, the basic measure of statistical dependence.
Cross entropy is the standard classification loss in deep learning — minimising it pulls the predicted distribution toward the true one in KL distance.



Self-Assessment

Could you compute H for (0.5, 0.25, 0.125, 0.125)?
Without scrolling back, what’s mutual information of independent variables?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answers: 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.375 + 0.375 = 1.75 bits; 0.)

3 yes: ready for 12 · Fourier Series.
2 yes: redo S1 (cross entropy).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P5.
crumbs: 11 · Information Theory — Entropy and Channel Capacity

title: 11 · Information Theory — Entropy and Channel Capacity
text:
Connect

Where this matters

AI / Machine Learning. Cross-entropy loss (S1). LLM perplexity = 2^entropy. Information bottleneck theory of deep learning.


Encryption. Perfect secrecy (Shannon, 1949): one-time pad has I(M; C) = 0 — the ciphertext reveals zero information about the message. Defining what secure means.


Systems & networks. Data compression (gzip, JPEG, MP3, video codecs). Wireless capacity (5G, Wi-Fi). Storage and transmission limits.

title: 15 · The Spectral Theorem and Functional Analysis Preview
text:
Have you done T5 L04 — Eigenvalues and T5 L15 — Real Analysis Foundation?
Comfortable with eigenvalues, eigenvectors, diagonalisation?
Recall ε-δ continuity?
crumbs: 15 · The Spectral Theorem and Functional Analysis Preview

title: 15 · The Spectral Theorem and Functional Analysis Preview
crumbs: 15 · The Spectral Theorem and Functional Analysis Preview

title: 15 · The Spectral Theorem and Functional Analysis Preview
text:
Wonder

Why are real symmetric matrices so special?
PCA, SVD, Fourier expansions are all spectral decompositions in disguise. Why?
The infinite-dimensional generalisation: Hilbert spaces and self-adjoint operators (preview of Tier 7).
crumbs: 15 · The Spectral Theorem and Functional Analysis Preview

title: 15 · The Spectral Theorem and Functional Analysis Preview
text:
Discover

Problem 1
For symmetric A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}, find eigenvalues and eigenvectors.


Full solution

\det(A - \lambda I) = (2-\lambda)^2 - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = 1,
3.
For \lambda = 1: \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} v = 0
\Rightarrow v_1 = -v_2. Eigenvector (1, -1)^T.
For \lambda = 3: v_1 = v_2. Eigenvector (1, 1)^T.
Note: the two eigenvectors are orthogonal: (1, -1) \cdot (1, 1)
= 0. No coincidence — symmetric matrices always have orthogonal eigenvectors for distinct eigenvalues.



Problem 2
The spectral theorem (real): every symmetric matrix A admits A = Q D Q^T where Q is orthogonal (Q^T Q = I) and D is diagonal with real eigenvalues.
For A from Problem 1: write the decomposition.


Full solution

Normalised eigenvectors: (1/\sqrt{2}, -1/\sqrt{2})^T and (1/\sqrt{2}, 1/\sqrt{2})^T.
Q = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}, D = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix}.
Check: Q D Q^T = A. ✓
Geometric meaning: A scales by 1 along (1, -1)/\sqrt{2} and by 3 along (1, 1)/\sqrt{2}. Pure stretching, no rotation, in the orthogonal eigenbasis.



Problem 3
SVD (Singular Value Decomposition): for any matrix A (not just symmetric), A = U \Sigma V^T where U, V orthogonal, \Sigma diagonal with non-negative entries (singular values).
How does SVD generalise the spectral theorem?


Full solution

SVD applies to any matrix, including non-symmetric and rectangular. Eigenvalues of A^T A are squared singular values.
For a symmetric A: SVD reduces (essentially) to spectral decomposition.
SVD is the workhorse of:

PCA: SVD of the data matrix.
Latent semantic analysis (search, NLP).
Image compression: keep only top-k singular values.
Matrix completion (Netflix prize): assume low-rank structure.

Eckart-Young theorem: truncated SVD gives the optimal low-rank approximation in Frobenius norm.



Problem 4
PCA via SVD. Centre data matrix X (n \times p, n samples, p features). Compute SVD: X = U \Sigma V^T. Columns of V are principal components.
Why?


Full solution

Covariance matrix C = X^T X / n has eigenvectors = principal components, eigenvalues = variances. Spectral decomposition of C = columns of V from SVD of X.
Top principal components = directions of greatest variance = columns of V for largest singular values.
Used pervasively: data visualisation (project to top 2-3 PCs), dimensionality reduction before clustering, denoising, feature extraction.



Problem 5
Functional analysis preview. Generalise: a Hilbert space is an infinite-dim inner product space, complete (Cauchy sequences converge).
Examples:

\ell^2: square-summable sequences.
L^2[a, b]: square-integrable functions on [a, b].

A self-adjoint operator T on a Hilbert space generalises a symmetric matrix. The spectral theorem generalises:
T = \int_\sigma \lambda \, dE(\lambda)
(integral against a spectral measure).
Quantum mechanical observables are self-adjoint operators — eigenvalues are measured values.


Full solution

Eigenvalues of position operator: continuous spectrum (the real line). Eigenvalues of energy of harmonic oscillator: discrete (E_n = \hbar \omega (n + 1/2)).
The spectral theorem unifies:

Linear algebra (finite dim).
Fourier analysis (operators on L^2 — Fourier basis is the spectral decomposition of differentiation).
Quantum mechanics (observables → spectra → measurement outcomes).

A genuine unification across mathematics.


The spectral theorem closed a programme that took David Hilbert the better part of a decade. Between 1904 and 1910 Hilbert published a series of six memoirs on integral equations and “infinite-dimensional matrices” — the work that introduced Hilbert space, Eigenwert (eigenvalue), and the spectral decomposition of self-adjoint operators in his characteristic slow-burning style. He proved the theorem for bounded operators; John von Neumann, working out the new quantum mechanics in 1929–1932, extended it to unbounded operators (position and momentum are unbounded), and put the whole apparatus on rigorous ground in his Mathematische Grundlagen der Quantenmechanik. The word spectrum, originally coined for the rainbow of frequencies emitted by hot atoms, turned out to literally be the set of eigenvalues of the atom’s Hamiltonian — Hilbert’s terminology matched physics by happy accident, not by design.


The spectral theorem is the statement that symmetric matrices are secretly diagonal — in the right orthogonal frame, a symmetric matrix is just stretching along perpendicular axes. SVD says every matrix is secretly two such stretchings glued together. In infinite dimensions, the same picture survives, just with “sum over eigenvalues” replaced by “integral over a spectrum” — and that one sentence is the entire mathematical foundation of quantum mechanics.
crumbs: 15 · The Spectral Theorem and Functional Analysis Preview

title: 15 · The Spectral Theorem and Functional Analysis Preview
text:
Name

Spectral theorem (real, finite-dim): every real symmetric matrix A admits A = Q D Q^T with Q orthogonal and D diagonal with real eigenvalues. Equivalently: \mathbb{R}^n has an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors of A.
Hermitian (complex) version: A^* = A \Rightarrow A = U D U^*.
Positive (semi-)definite: symmetric with all eigenvalues > 0 (\ge 0).
Singular value decomposition (SVD): any m \times n matrix A = U \Sigma V^T with U \in O(m), V \in O(n), \Sigma non-negative diagonal.
Functional analysis (Tier 7 preview):

Hilbert space = complete inner-product space.
Self-adjoint operator: \langle T x, y \rangle = \langle x, T y
\rangle.
Spectral theorem: every self-adjoint operator has a spectral decomposition.

Examples:

Quantum observables: position X, momentum P, Hamiltonian H — all self-adjoint on L^2.
Multiplication and differentiation operators.



Spectral theorem: symmetric/self-adjoint = orthogonal eigenbasis with real eigenvalues; SVD: every matrix has a decomposition (PCA, compression, denoising); Generalises to infinite dimensions: Hilbert spaces, quantum mechanics.
crumbs: 15 · The Spectral Theorem and Functional Analysis Preview

title: 15 · The Spectral Theorem and Functional Analysis Preview
text:
Apply

Worked example

Find the SVD of A = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 \end{pmatrix}.

Already diagonal with non-negative entries. U = I, \Sigma = A, V =
I. Trivial SVD.
For non-square or non-trivial: more work, but doable in closed form for small matrices.


Practice — Warm
W1. Symmetric: \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix}?


Full solution

Yes (entries above and below diagonal match).

W2. Diagonal of a positive-definite matrix?


Full solution

All entries > 0 (necessary, not sufficient).

W3. Singular values of A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 5
\end{pmatrix}?


Full solution

5, 2 (always sorted descending).



Practice — Working
P1. Eigenvalues of A = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 1 & 4
\end{pmatrix}? Verify orthogonal eigenvectors.


Full solution

(4 - \lambda)^2 - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = 3, 5. Eigenvectors (1, -1), (1, 1) — orthogonal ✓.

P2. Is A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 0 \end{pmatrix} positive semi-definite?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Eigenvalues: \lambda^2 - \lambda - 4 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = (1
\pm \sqrt{17})/2 \approx -1.56, 2.56.



Full solution

No — one negative eigenvalue.

P3. What does SVD compute that eigendecomposition can’t?


Full solution

Decomposition for non-square and non-symmetric matrices. Eigendecomposition only works for square matrices, and only nicely (diagonalisable) for some. SVD is the universal generalisation.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Why is the SVD the foundation of modern data analysis?


Full solution

SVD reveals low-rank structure. Most real-world data matrices are approximately low-rank: top few singular values capture most of the variance.
Applications:

PCA / dimensionality reduction.
Latent semantic analysis (NLP — find topics in documents).
Recommendation systems (Netflix Prize, collaborative filtering).
Image compression.
Computational physics (model reduction).
Pseudoinverse (handle non-invertible / over-determined systems).

The SVD’s combination of universality (works on any matrix), optimality (Eckart-Young), and computational efficiency makes it the tool for matrix analysis.

S2. Quantum mechanics in one sentence. A quantum state is a unit vector in a Hilbert space; observables are self-adjoint operators; measurement of observable O yields one of O’s eigenvalues, with the post-measurement state being the corresponding eigenvector.
The full machinery of QM rests on the spectral theorem in Hilbert space.


Full solution


Particle position: continuous spectrum on \mathbb{R}.
Energy of bound state: discrete (quantised) spectrum.
Spin: finite spectrum (e.g., \pm 1/2 for electrons).

Interference and uncertainty principles emerge from the non- commutativity of observables: XP - PX = i\hbar.
The 20th-century triumph of mathematical physics: realising that quantum phenomena require infinite-dimensional spectral theory. Tier 7 (research track) develops this.



Common confusions

“Spectral theorem only applies to symmetric matrices.” It applies to normal matrices (A A^* = A^* A), which include symmetric, Hermitian, unitary, orthogonal — many cases.
“PCA is exotic.” It’s just the spectral decomposition of the covariance matrix.
“Infinite dim is too abstract.” Functional analysis is the language of quantum mechanics, signal processing, modern PDE theory.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

The spectral theorem says every real symmetric matrix factors as A = QDQ^T — orthogonal eigenbasis, real eigenvalues, no funny business.
The SVD generalises this to any matrix — square or not, symmetric or not — and is the workhorse of PCA, recommender systems, and low-rank approximation.
In infinite dimensions, the same theorem powers quantum mechanics — observables are self-adjoint operators on a Hilbert space, and their spectra are the values you can actually measure.



Self-Assessment

Could you state the spectral theorem for real symmetric matrices?
Without scrolling back, what’s SVD for any matrix?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: A = U \Sigma V^T with orthogonal U, V and diagonal non-negative \Sigma.)

3 yes: You have completed Tier 6! Tier 7 (Masters specialisation tracks: AI/ML, Game Dev, Encryption, Databases, Systems, Research) awaits.
2 yes: redo S1 (SVD applications).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P2 and P5.
crumbs: 15 · The Spectral Theorem and Functional Analysis Preview

title: 15 · The Spectral Theorem and Functional Analysis Preview
text:
Connect

What this unlocks

Tier 7 — Functional Analysis — full spectral theory in Hilbert space; bounded vs unbounded operators.
Tier 7 — Operator Algebras — von Neumann algebras, C*-algebras.



Where this matters

AI / Machine Learning. SVD-based decompositions everywhere (S1). Spectral methods for graphs, kernels, embeddings.


Mathematical research. Spectral theory unifies linear algebra (finite), Fourier analysis (operators on L^2), and quantum mechanics. Riemann hypothesis, Langlands program — all rely on spectral ideas.


Physics. Quantum mechanics (S2). Vibration analysis (eigenmodes of structures). Quantum field theory uses operators on infinite-dim spaces.

Across 15 lessons you have:

Congratulations.

title: 12 · Shapes and Angles
text:
This lesson stands alone — it has no maths prerequisites in this curriculum. You only need:

To know what a straight line looks like (a taut string, the edge of a ruler).
The willingness to think about shape and direction rather than numbers for a while.
(Optional) Pencil and paper to sketch.

title: 12 · Shapes and Angles
12 · Shapes and Angles
text:
Wonder

Open and close the angle. Is the length of the lines part of the angle? Or only the spread between them?
What angles do you see in the corners of a book, a window, a phone screen?
A triangle has three corners. If you measured the angles at each corner and added them up, do you think the answer would always be the same?

title: 12 · Shapes and Angles
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Sketch (or imagine) these shapes and name them:

Three straight sides.
Four straight sides, all the same length, all corners right-angled.
Four straight sides, opposite sides equal, all corners right-angled.
A flat curve where every point is the same distance from one fixed centre.



Full solution


Triangle. (b) Square. (c) Rectangle. (d) Circle.

Notice the structure: shapes are described by number of sides, side lengths, and angles. These three pieces of data determine almost every elementary shape.



Problem 2
Sketch a triangle. Cut it out (or just imagine cutting it). Tear off the three corners. Place them next to each other along a straight line.
What do you find?


Hint 1 — a nudge

A straight line corresponds to an angle of 180°.



Full solution

The three corners exactly fill a straight line — i.e. they sum to 180°.
This is the most famous fact in elementary geometry:

The three angles of any triangle sum to 180°.

True for any triangle — pointy, flat, equilateral, scalene. The classroom experiment with torn corners is a physical proof. Tier 3 will give a careful proof using parallel lines.



Problem 3
A triangle has angles 40° and 75°. What is the third angle?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Use the rule from P2: total = 180°.



Full solution

180 - 40 - 75 = 65°. 65°. Algebra (Lesson 11) makes the calculation trivial: if the third angle is x, then 40 + 75 + x = 180, so x = 65.



Problem 4
Imagine walking around the boundary of a square. Each corner, you turn by 90° (a right angle). After four corners, you’ve turned through how much in total — and back to your starting direction?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Four right angles, each 90°. Sum?



Full solution

4 \times 90° = 360° — a full turn. After walking around any convex shape, you turn through 360° in total.
This generalises beautifully: a triangle’s exterior angles (the turn at each corner if you walked along it) also sum to 360°. So do a pentagon’s, a hexagon’s, and any convex shape. The interior angles add up differently for each shape, but the exterior angles always sum to 360°. Geometry is full of such conserved totals.



Problem 5
Two lines meet, forming an angle of 53° on one side. What is the angle on the other side of the same line?


Hint 1 — a nudge

The two angles together fill a straight line.



Full solution

180 - 53 = 127°. 127°. Two angles forming a straight line are called supplementary, and they sum to 180° by definition. Two angles forming a right angle are called complementary and sum to 90°.
The two angles on opposite sides of a single straight line are called vertical angles when two lines cross — and an important Tier 3 fact is that they are always equal.


Euclid’s Elements (c. 300 BCE) opens with twenty-three definitions — point, line, angle, circle, triangle — and five postulates, from which every theorem of plane geometry follows. The triangle angle-sum (Discover P2) appears as Book I, Proposition 32. The Elements was the second-most printed book in Western history (after the Bible) and was the standard geometry textbook in European schools for over 2,000 years. Why 360° for a full turn? That’s older still — inherited from the Babylonians (c. 2000 BCE) and their base-60 number system, which also gave us 60 minutes in an hour.


Length is one kind of measurement; turn is another. An angle is what you measure when length doesn’t matter — only how much one direction swings away from another.

title: 12 · Shapes and Angles
text:
Name

A point has position but no size. A line is the path traced by moving in one fixed direction. A line segment is a finite piece of a line, with two endpoints.
An angle measures the turn between two lines (or segments) that meet at a common point — the vertex. We measure angles in degrees: a full turn is 360°, a half-turn 180°, a quarter-turn 90° (a right angle).
Special angle names:

Acute: less than 90°.
Right: exactly 90°.
Obtuse: between 90° and 180°.
Straight: exactly 180°.

A polygon is a closed shape with straight sides. Common ones:

Triangle (3 sides), quadrilateral (4), pentagon (5), hexagon (6), …
A square has 4 equal sides and 4 right angles.
A rectangle has opposite sides equal and 4 right angles.
A regular polygon has all sides equal and all angles equal.

A circle is the set of points at a fixed radius from a centre.
The triangle angle-sum: the three interior angles of any triangle sum to 180°.


Two lines meeting — the angle between them; Three lines closing — a triangle, whose corners always sum to 180°; A walk around a closed shape returns you 360° to the start.

title: 12 · Shapes and Angles
text:
Apply

Worked example

A triangle has two equal sides and a vertex angle (between the equal sides) of 50°. Find the other two angles.

A competent solver uses a small fact about isoceles triangles:

In an isoceles triangle, the angles opposite the two equal sides are equal.

So the other two angles are equal — call each x. Sum to 180°: 50 + x + x = 180 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 2x = 130 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x = 65°.
Answer: the other two angles are 65° each. Verify: 50 + 65 + 65
= 180° ✓.
The “equal sides imply equal opposite angles” fact will be proved properly in Tier 2 / Tier 3 geometry.


Practice — Warm
W1. Classify each: 30°, 90°, 130°, 180°.


Full solution

acute, right, obtuse, straight.

W2. A triangle has two angles of 50° and 70°. Find the third.


Full solution

180 - 50 - 70 = 60°.

W3. A square’s four angles are all equal and sum to… ? Hence each is… ?


Full solution

Sum = 360° (the four corners). Each = 90° — confirming squares are right-angled.



Practice — Working
P1. A triangle has all three angles equal. What is each angle?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Three equal angles summing to 180°. So each is…



Full solution

180 \div 3 = 60°. This is the equilateral triangle: all three sides equal, all three angles equal to 60°. The most symmetric triangle.

P2. A regular pentagon has 5 equal interior angles. The interior angles of any pentagon sum to (5 - 2) \times 180° = 540°. What is each interior angle of a regular pentagon?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Total ÷ 5.



Full solution

540 \div 5 = 108°. (Each angle of a regular pentagon is 108°.)
The general formula for the interior-angle sum of a polygon with n sides is (n - 2) \times 180°. You can derive it by noticing that any n-sided polygon can be sliced into (n - 2) triangles from a single vertex.

P3. Two angles of a triangle are equal, and the third is twice each of them. Find all three angles.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let each equal angle be x. Then the third is 2x. Use Lesson 11.



Full solution

x + x + 2x = 180, so 4x = 180 and x = 45°. The angles are 45°, 45°, 90°. A right-angled isoceles triangle. This shape is the diagonal of a square — a recurring shape in geometry.



Practice — Stretch
S1. What angle do the hands of a clock make at exactly 3:00?


Hint 1 — a nudge

The clock face is a circle (360°) divided into 12 equal segments.



Full solution

At 3:00, the minute hand is at 12 and the hour hand is at 3. They are 3 hours apart, which is \tfrac{3}{12} = \tfrac{1}{4} of the full circle. \tfrac{1}{4} \times 360 = 90°.
90° — a right angle. That’s why a clock at 3:00 (or 9:00) makes a visually familiar L-shape.

S2. A 3D triangle in a video game is described by three points (vertices). The game engine needs to compute the angles between pairs of edges to do lighting (which way is the surface facing?). The angle between two vectors \vec{u} and \vec{v} is given by \cos\theta = \frac{\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}}{|\vec{u}|\,|\vec{v}|}. You don’t need to compute this now — but recognise: even 3D shading is a question about angles. What does this say about the importance of angles?


Full solution

Every modern game renders 3D scenes by decomposing surfaces into millions of tiny triangles, and computing the angle between each triangle’s surface direction and the direction of the light. The result controls the brightness of each pixel.
The simple “three corners of a triangle add to 180°” you met today generalises into the trigonometry of Tier 3 and the vector algebra of Tier 5. From a child’s protractor to real-time 3D graphics, the same idea — angle between two directions — is the through line.
Angles are not a small topic. They are one of the most reused ideas in all of applied mathematics.



Common confusions

“Bigger lines mean a bigger angle.” No — angle measures only the turn between the lines, not their length. Two short pencils at a wide angle, and two long swords at a narrow angle, can have very different angles regardless of length.
“All four-sided shapes are squares or rectangles.” No — there are also rhombuses (equal sides, no right angles), parallelograms, trapeziums, and irregular quadrilaterals.
“360° is special — it’s the only full turn.” It’s the convention for full turn (chosen by the Babylonians for their base-60 number system). Mathematicians often use radians instead (a full turn is 2\pi), which we’ll meet in Tier 3.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

An angle measures turn between two directions, not length — short pencils and long swords can meet at the same angle.
The three angles of any triangle sum to 180°, and walking the boundary of any closed convex shape turns you through 360° in total.
Shapes get their names from sides and angles — and these two pieces of data carry every elementary geometric fact, from a square to a 3D game model.



Self-Assessment

Could you tear the corners off a paper triangle and convince a friend that they sum to 180°?
Without scrolling back, can you find the third angle of a triangle given two of them are 35° and 95°?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: 180 - 35 - 95 = 50°.)

3 yes: ready for Lesson 13 — Area, Perimeter, Volume.
2 yes: redo P2 — the polygon angle-sum formula is one of geometry’s gems.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P2 and P4.

title: 12 · Shapes and Angles
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used
None within this curriculum. Geometry has its own logical foundation — this lesson sits at the start of the geometric strand.


What this unlocks

Area, Perimeter, Volume — with shapes and angles named, we can measure them.
Tier 3 — Trigonometry — the ratios of triangle sides as functions of the angles. Every angle calculation in physics or graphics ultimately uses trigonometry.



Where this matters

Game development & graphics. Every 3D model is a mesh of triangles. Every lighting calculation is an angle calculation. The angle between the camera and a surface determines what you see. Stretch S2 gives the high-level view.


Mathematical research. Geometry is one of mathematics’ two oldest branches (with arithmetic). Euclid’s Elements (c. 300 BCE) systematised plane geometry from a few axioms — its method (axiom → theorem → proof) is the model for all subsequent rigorous mathematics. Kiselev’s Geometry (Russia, 19th century) is the modern reincarnation.


Everywhere else. Architecture, navigation (compass bearings), astronomy (positions of stars), surveying, engineering, art and design, sports (camera angles in cricket replays), and traffic engineering (intersection angles) all depend on the angle vocabulary.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones honoured: Pestalozzi (the angle widget makes turning literal), Boole (the do the angles depend on the lengths? question surfaces a key intuition), Russian school (the corner-tearing experiment is a physical proof; Kiselev’s geometry texts use this exact demonstration), Bhāskara (single-box Name + verse), Euclid (the whole geometric vocabulary traces to Elements).
Self-study guarantees all present.

title: 14 · Data and the Average
text:
Have you done Lesson 6 — Division as Fair Share?
Can you compute 84 \div 7 = 12?
Have you ever heard a sports commentator say “his batting average is 45” or seen “average rainfall: 800 mm”? That number is the topic of this lesson.

title: 14 · Data and the Average
14 · Data and the Average
text:
Wonder

Set all five values to the same number — say 40. What is the mean? The median?
Set four values to 10 and one to 100. What is the mean? The median? Which is more “honest” about the typical value?
If you knew only the mean of a dataset, could you reconstruct the original numbers?

title: 14 · Data and the Average
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Three friends took a test. Their scores were 80, 75, 85 out of 100. Find the mean (average) score.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Mean = sum of values ÷ count.



Full solution

Sum: 80 + 75 + 85 = 240. Count: 3. Mean: 240 \div 3 = 80. 80.
The mean is “if everyone had the same score, what would it be so that the total stays the same?” — it spreads the total evenly.



Problem 2
Ten children’s heights (in cm): 120, 125, 130, 132, 132, 135, 138, 140, 142, 150. Find the median (middle value).


Hint 1 — a nudge

For an even number of values, the median is the average of the two middle ones.



Full solution

Already sorted. Ten values → middle two are the 5th and 6th: 132 and 135. Median: (132 + 135) \div 2 = 133.5 cm.
For an odd count, the median is just the single middle value (no averaging needed). The median is robust against extreme values — it doesn’t get pulled by outliers the way the mean does.



Problem 3
Five workers earn (in thousand rupees per month): 30, 32, 35, 38, 175 (the last is the boss). Compute the mean and the median. Which is a more honest summary of “what a typical worker earns”?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Mean: sum ÷ count. Median: middle of the sorted list.



Full solution

Sum: 30 + 32 + 35 + 38 + 175 = 310. Mean: 310 \div 5 = 62. Median (middle of sorted [30,32,35,38,175]): 35.
The mean is 62 thousand, but four of the five workers earn well below that. The single outlier (the boss at 175) drags the mean up. The median (35 thousand) reflects the typical worker more honestly.
This is one of the most important practical points about averages: when data is skewed (one extreme value), the median tells the truth better than the mean. News stories about “average income” almost always mean the median income for this exact reason.



Problem 4
The mean of five numbers is 20. What is their sum?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Mean = sum ÷ count, so sum = mean × count.



Full solution

Sum = 20 \times 5 = 100. Multiplication and division are inverses (Lesson 6); algebra (Lesson 11) gives the same answer in symbols: \bar{x} = S/n implies S = \bar{x} \cdot n.
Knowing the mean and the count is equivalent to knowing the sum, but not equivalent to knowing the individual values. The mean is a compression of the data — it loses information.



Problem 5
A class of 5 students has test scores 40, 60, 70, 80, 90. What is the range? What does it tell you that the mean and median don’t?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Range = largest − smallest.



Full solution

Range = 90 - 40 = 50.
The range tells you about the spread of the data — how far apart the values are. Two classes can have the same mean (both 68) but very different ranges: one might be tightly clustered (60, 65, 68, 71, 76) and one widely spread (40, 50, 68, 86, 96). The mean alone can’t distinguish them.
We need at least two numbers to summarise a dataset honestly: a centre (mean or median) and a spread (range, or more refined measures we’ll meet in Tier 5: variance and standard deviation).


Florence Nightingale (1820–1910), best known as the founder of modern nursing, was also a pioneering statistician — the first woman elected to the Royal Statistical Society. During the Crimean War (1853– 56) she invented the coxcomb (rose) diagram to show that British soldiers were dying far more from poor sanitation than from battle wounds. Her data-driven argument persuaded Parliament to overhaul military hospitals. The takeaway is older than her work: data without a summary nobody understands changes nothing; the right summary changes the world.


A single number can never tell the whole story of a dataset. Honest summarising needs at least two numbers — a centre (mean or median) and a spread — and the choice between mean and median is itself a moral decision about which voices the summary represents.

title: 14 · Data and the Average
text:
Name

Given a list of numbers x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n (the data):

Sum: S = x_1 + x_2 + \cdots + x_n.
Count: n.
Mean (average): \bar{x} = \dfrac{S}{n}. The “balance point” of the data.
Median: the middle value when the data is sorted (for even n, the average of the two middle values). The “halfway” value.
Range: largest minus smallest. A measure of spread.
Mode: the most frequently occurring value. Useful for categorical data.

A single summary number throws away information. Honest data reporting usually requires both a centre and a spread.


Sum the values, divide by count — that is the mean; Sort the values, take the middle — that is the median; Largest minus smallest — that is the spread; No single number can tell the whole story.

title: 14 · Data and the Average
text:
Apply

Worked example

A cricket player’s last 6 innings scored: 42, 0, 78, 113, 5, 22. Compute the mean, median, range. Which best describes “his form”?

A competent solver works in steps:

Sum: 42 + 0 + 78 + 113 + 5 + 22 = 260.
Mean: 260 \div 6 \approx 43.3.
Sorted: 0, 5, 22, 42, 78, 113. Middle two: 22 and 42. Median: (22 + 42)/2 = 32.
Range: 113 - 0 = 113.

The range tells the real story. The player is wildly inconsistent — one century, two single-digit failures, a duck. Saying “his average is 43” is technically true but misleading. Both the median (32) and the huge range (113) reveal the inconsistency. A coach would care about all three numbers.


Practice — Warm
W1. Find the mean of: 5, 8, 10, 7, 10.


Full solution

Sum 40, count 5, mean 8.

W2. Find the median of: 12, 7, 15, 9, 20.


Full solution

Sorted: 7, 9, 12, 15, 20. Middle: 12.

W3. Find the range of: 3, 11, 7, 22, 8, 15.


Full solution

Max 22, min 3, range 19.



Practice — Working
P1. Six students’ marks: 65, 72, 80, 80, 91, 88. Compute mean, median, mode.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Mode is the most frequent. Median (even count) is average of middle two.



Full solution

Sum: 65 + 72 + 80 + 80 + 91 + 88 = 476. Mean: 476 \div 6 \approx 79.3. Sorted: 65, 72, 80, 80, 88, 91. Middle two: 80 and 80. Median: 80. Mode: 80 (appears twice).
All three centre measures are close (\approx 80) — a good sign the data is well-clustered.

P2. A test had a mean score of 62. There were 25 students. The sum of all scores is…?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Sum = mean × count.



Full solution

62 \times 25 = 1{,}550. The sum is 1550 marks.

P3. A box of 10 chocolates has masses (grams): 18, 20, 21, 19, 20, 22, 21, 19, 20, 20. Compute the mean. Then say which chocolate is above and which is below the mean.


Full solution

Sum: 200. Mean: 20 g. Above: the two 21 g and the 22 g. Below: the 18 g and the two 19 g. The four 20 g are exactly at the mean.
A useful test: the deviations from the mean (each value minus the mean) sum to zero — by construction. Try it: (-2) + 0 + 1 + (-1) + 0
+ 2 + 1 + (-1) + 0 + 0 = 0 ✓. This will be central in Tier 5 when we define variance.



Practice — Stretch
S1. A neural network is trained on batches of data. For each batch, it computes the average loss and updates its weights based on the gradient of that average. Why average instead of summing?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Imagine batch size 100 vs batch size 10. If we used the sum, would the loss values be comparable?



Full solution

Averaging makes losses comparable across batch sizes. A sum of 100 losses is naturally 10× larger than a sum of 10 losses, even if the per-example loss is identical. By averaging, the loss number depends only on the typical per-example error, not on how many examples happened to be in the batch.
This matters because the learning rate (Lesson 9) is calibrated for the per-example gradient. Using a sum would force the learning rate to be adjusted whenever batch size changes — a fragile setup. Averaging decouples the two.
Almost every loss function in modern ML is an average for this reason. The humble Tier 1 idea of “mean” is doing real work in cutting-edge research.

S2. The median income in a country is reported as Rs. 25,000 per month, but the mean income is Rs. 80,000 per month. What does this tell you about the income distribution? Which number would a politician use, and why?


Full solution

The mean is much higher than the median, which means a small number of very high earners are pulling the mean up while half the population earns ≤ 25,000. The income distribution is right-skewed — a long tail of high earners.
A politician arguing the country is doing well might quote the mean (Rs. 80,000 — sounds prosperous). A politician arguing the country needs intervention might quote the median (Rs. 25,000 — reveals the typical struggle). Both numbers are true; both are honest answers to slightly different questions.
This is why statistically literate citizens demand to know which average is being quoted. “The average X” is meaningless without the qualifier.



Common confusions

“Average always means the mean.” No. “Average” can mean mean, median, or mode in ordinary speech. In serious data work, say which.
“The mean is the typical value.” Often, but not always. When data is skewed (S2), the median is more typical. The mean is the balance point, not necessarily the typical case.
“You can summarise data with one number.” No — you need at least a centre and a spread. A mean of 50 with range 1 vs. range 99 are wildly different datasets.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

The mean is the balance point of the data; the median is the middle entry — and they only agree when the data is symmetric.
When a few extreme values are pulling the mean around, the median tells the more honest story — that’s why “average income” almost always means median.
No single number summarises a dataset; you also need a measure of spread like the range to know how clustered or scattered the values are.



Self-Assessment

Could you explain to a friend why the median is more honest than the mean for income data?
Without scrolling back, can you compute the mean and median of: 4, 7, 9, 12, 18?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: mean = 50/5 = 10; median = 9.)

3 yes: ready for Lesson 15 — Divisibility and Primes.
2 yes: redo S2 — the income/distribution intuition is crucial data literacy.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P5.

title: 14 · Data and the Average
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

Lesson 6 — Division as Fair Share — the mean is total ÷ count, exactly the fair-share calculation.



What this unlocks

Tier 3 — Probability and basic statistics — frequency tables, histograms, expected value.
Tier 5 — Probability theory and statistics — variance, standard deviation, sampling, hypothesis testing, regression.



Where this matters

AI / Machine Learning. Almost every loss function is an average (Stretch S1). Many activation functions and metrics involve means (softmax probabilities, BLEU scores, F1 scores). Data normalisation subtracts the mean of each feature. Statistical thinking is half of ML practice.


Databases. SQL’s AVG(), MEDIAN() (in some dialects), percentile functions, COUNT(), SUM() are the daily tools of analytical query writing. Every dashboard, every business report, runs on aggregations.


Mathematical research. The journey from “mean of a finite list” to “expected value of a random variable” to “integral with respect to a probability measure” is one of the great unifications in mathematics — the subject of measure theory in Tier 7.


Everywhere else. Sports statistics, nutrition (per 100 g), economic indicators (CPI, GDP per capita), grades and rankings, weather (average temperature), polling. Almost every numerical claim in public discourse is a kind of average.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones honoured: Pestalozzi (the data-points + mean-line widget makes “balance point” visual), Boole (the which is more honest? question is the heart of statistical literacy), Russian school (the salary problem in P3 surfaces skew without naming it), Bhāskara (single-box Name + verse).
Self-study guarantees all present.

title: 00 · Introduction
text:
Tier 1 is the bedrock. Whether you arrived here as an eleven-year-old, an adult who never quite trusted their grasp of fractions, or a software engineer who wants the encryption door to open later — this tier gives you the understanding of why every operation works, not just how to push the symbols.
By the end of Tier 1 you can:

Place any number — whole, fraction, or decimal — on a number line and say what it means.
Reason about ratios and proportions in real situations.
Compute area, perimeter, and volume of basic shapes from first principles.
Read and write simple algebraic expressions.
Tell whether a number is prime, and explain why that question matters (it is the door to modern encryption).

This page reflects the snapshot of _curriculum.yml at the time of build. Lesson statuses there are the source of truth.

title: 06 · Division as Fair Share
text:
Can you compute 4 \times 6 = 24 in your head?
Have you done Lesson 5 — Multiplication as Area?
Have you ever split a pizza, a packet of biscuits, or pocket money between friends? That mental act is division — even if you have never written it as a sum.

title: 06 · Division as Fair Share
06 · Division as Fair Share
text:
Wonder

Set total = 12, friends = 4. Now flip: total = 12, friends = 3. What changes?
Try total = 7, friends = 2. The two friends can’t get the same whole number of biscuits. What are your three options for what to do with the leftover one?
What does division by 1 look like? What about division by the total itself (e.g. 12 ÷ 12)?

title: 06 · Division as Fair Share
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Twelve children are split into 3 equal teams. How many on each team? Twelve children are split into 4 equal teams. How many on each team?


Full solution

4 children per team (3 teams) and 3 children per team (4 teams). You computed 12 \div 3 = 4 and 12 \div 4 = 3. Notice the symmetry — this is the same fact, told two ways: 3 \times 4 = 12 \iff 12 \div 3 = 4 \iff 12 \div 4 = 3. Division is the inverse of multiplication.



Problem 2
A bag has 23 marbles to share among 5 friends equally. How many does each get? How many are left over?


Hint 1 — a nudge

What is the largest multiple of 5 that is ≤ 23?



Full solution

5 \times 4 = 20 ≤ 23, but 5 \times 5 = 25 > 23. So each friend gets 4 and 3 marbles are left over: 23 = 5 \times 4 + 3. Every division-with-remainder fits this template: \text{total} =
\text{divisor} \times \text{quotient} + \text{remainder}.



Problem 3
A rectangle has area 24 and one side of length 6. What is the other side?


Hint 1 — a nudge

This is a multiplication you already know — but in reverse.



Full solution

24 \div 6 = 4. The other side is 4.
Picture: a rectangle of area 24 with one side fixed at 6. The other side must be 4, because 4 \times 6 = 24. Division by 6 is “given the area and one side, find the other side”. This is the geometric meaning of division.



Problem 4
What is 0 \div 5? What is 5 \div 0?


Hint 1 — a nudge

For the first: 0 things shared between 5 friends. Each friend gets… ?
For the second: try the inverse. “5 \div 0 = q” would mean “q \times
0 = 5”. Is there such a q?



Full solution

0 \div 5 = 0. Zero things shared evenly: each friend gets nothing.
5 \div 0 is undefined. No number q exists such that q \times 0 = 5, because q \times 0 is always 0 (Lesson 5). So “divide by zero” is not a sensible question — it has no answer in the ordinary number system. Computers and calculators usually return an error or “infinity” symbol when you try.



Problem 5
You have 17 sweets to share among 5 children. After you give each child their fair whole share, how many sweets are left over? What options do you have for the leftovers?


Hint 1 — a nudge

5 \times 3 = 15, so each child gets at least 3. How many remain?



Full solution

Each child gets 3 sweets, with 2 left over.
Three options for the leftovers:

Set them aside (the leftover stays as a remainder).
Cut them up into smaller equal pieces and share — each child gets \frac{2}{5} of an extra sweet. This forces us to invent a new kind of number — fractions — which is the next lesson.
Don’t share them (one child gets a bonus, or you keep them).

Mathematics took option (b) seriously, and that gave us the fractions of Lesson 7.
