The Egyptian Rhind Mathematical Papyrus (around 1650 BCE, copied from an even older source) is full of division problems — but Egyptian scribes expressed every leftover as a sum of unit fractions like \tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{1}{4} + \tfrac{1}{7}. The problem of sharing “9 loaves of bread among 10 men” produced a whole technology of unit-fraction tables. The Greeks inherited the system; only with Brahmagupta and the Indian tradition (7th century CE) did the modern fraction \tfrac{p}{q} — quotient as a single number — take over.


Division is multiplication run backwards — given the area and one side, find the other side. The remainder is the part that wouldn’t fit into a clean rectangle. And the fact that you can’t divide by zero isn’t a rule imposed from outside — it’s the rectangle refusing to exist.

title: 06 · Division as Fair Share
text:
Name

Division is the inverse of multiplication.
For two whole numbers a (the dividend) and b (the divisor, b \ne 0), the equation a \div b = q \ \text{remainder} \ r means: a = b \times q + r, where q is the quotient (the share) and r is the remainder (0 \le r < b).
When r = 0, we say “b divides a exactly” and write a \div b = q.
Division by zero is undefined.


Take the total, share it equally — that share is the quotient. Whatever cannot be shared is the remainder. Total equals divisor times quotient, plus remainder — And no number can be divided by nothing.

title: 06 · Division as Fair Share
text:
Apply

Worked example

Compute 84 \div 7.

A competent solver thinks “what number, multiplied by 7, gives 84?”:

Try multiples: 7 \times 10 = 70, 7 \times 12 = 84. ✓
So 84 \div 7 = 12, remainder 0.

Or, by long division: 7 goes into 8 once (with 1 left); pull down the 4 to make 14; 7 goes into 14 twice (with 0 left). Quotient: 12.
Answer: 84 ÷ 7 = 12.


Practice — Warm
W1. Compute (each is exact): 36 \div 6, \quad 56 \div 8, \quad 100 \div 4.


Full solution

36 \div 6 = 6, \ 56 \div 8 = 7, \ 100 \div 4 = 25.

W2. Compute, finding the quotient and remainder: 25 \div 4, \quad 38 \div 7.


Full solution

25 = 4 \times 6 + 1, so 25 \div 4 = 6 remainder 1. 38 = 7 \times 5 + 3, so 38 \div 7 = 5 remainder 3.

W3. What is 0 \div 100? What is 100 \div 0?


Full solution

0 and undefined. (See Discover P4.)



Practice — Working
P1. A library has 156 books to fit on shelves of 12 books each. How many shelves are needed?


Full solution

156 \div 12 = 13 exactly. 13 shelves. Notice this is a “given the area and one side, find the other side” problem — 12 wide rectangle, total of 156 squares.

P2. A baker has 47 cupcakes. He sells them in boxes of 6. How many full boxes does he sell, and how many cupcakes are left to sell loose?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Largest multiple of 6 that is ≤ 47?



Full solution

47 = 6 \times 7 + 5, so 7 full boxes with 5 left over.
Real-world division almost always has remainders. The “extras” need to be handled — sold separately, discounted, donated, or thrown out. Whether to round the quotient up or down depends on the context. (For “shelves needed”, you round up to make sure all books fit; for “boxes that fit”, you round down.)

P3. Without computing, decide whether 385 is divisible by 5 exactly. Then decide for 385 \div 4.


Hint 1 — a nudge

A number ending in 0 or 5 is always divisible by 5. (Why? Hint: place value.)



Full solution

385 ends in 5, so it is divisible by 5 exactly. 385 \div 5 = 77.
385 \div 4 — last two digits are 85; 4 \times 21 = 84, so 85 = 4
\times 21 + 1, and the rest of the digits (3{,}800) are divisible by 4 since 3800 = 4 \times 950. Total: 385 = 4 \times 96 + 1, so quotient 96, remainder 1.
These shortcuts (last-digit tests for divisibility by 2, 5, 10) and related ones (sum-of-digits for 3 and 9) are powerful. We will explore them properly in Lesson 15: Divisibility and Primes.



Practice — Stretch
S1. A clock face has 12 hours. If it is currently 9 o’clock, what hour will it be in 100 hours?


Hint 1 — a nudge

After 12 hours the clock returns to 9. So you really care about 100
\div 12 — the remainder is what matters.



Full solution

100 \div 12 = 8 remainder 4. So 100 hours is “8 full clock cycles plus 4 hours”. Starting at 9, advance 4 hours: 1 o’clock.
This is modular arithmetic — division remainders considered as their own kind of arithmetic. It governs clocks, days of the week, and — most importantly — every modern cryptographic system. RSA, the algorithm protecting most secure web traffic, is built almost entirely on remainders modulo a very large number. We met this idea informally here; we meet it formally in Lesson 15.

S2. A hash table — a fundamental data structure in databases and programming — places an item with key k into bucket number k \bmod n (the remainder when k is divided by n, where n is the table size).
If n = 7 buckets, into which bucket does key k = 23 go?


Full solution

23 \div 7 = 3 remainder 2. So key 23 goes into bucket 2.
Hash tables are how databases (and most programming languages’ built-in maps and dictionaries) achieve fast lookup. The division-with-remainder operation you just performed runs trillions of times per second across the world’s computers. The reason: it spreads keys roughly evenly across buckets, and computing a remainder is cheap.



Common confusions

“Dividing always makes a number smaller.” True only when dividing by a number greater than 1. Once we meet fractions and decimals, dividing by \tfrac{1}{2} doubles the number. (Why? Sneak preview: “how many halves fit in 5?” The answer is 10.)
“Remainders are mistakes.” No — they are honest reports that the total didn’t divide evenly. The remainder is part of the answer.
“You can divide by anything.” No — division by zero is undefined. Calculators that return “infinity” or an error are reporting that the question itself is malformed.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

Division is the inverse of multiplication: given a total and one side, find the other.
When the split isn’t exact, you get a quotient (each fair share) and a remainder (what wouldn’t fit) — \text{total} = \text{divisor} \times \text{quotient} + \text{remainder}.
Dividing by zero is undefined — and the remainder turns out to be its own arithmetic (modular) that runs clocks, hash tables, and modern cryptography.



Self-Assessment

Could you explain why 5 \div 0 is undefined, using the inverse relationship to multiplication?
Without computing, can you say whether 385 is divisible by 5? By 7? (Trick question for the second — you have to actually compute.)
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?


3 yes: ready for Lesson 7 — Fractions as Parts.
2 yes: redo S1 (the clock-modular problem) — that proves you see how remainders open up a whole new arithmetic.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P5 and the worked example.

title: 06 · Division as Fair Share
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

Lesson 5 — Multiplication as Area — division is multiplication run backwards: “given the area and one side, find the other”.



What this unlocks

Fractions as Parts — when a total doesn’t divide exactly, fractions extend the number system to cover the leftover.
Data and the Average — the average is the total divided by the count.
Divisibility and Primes — the encryption seed: the structure of which numbers divide which is the entry point to number theory.



Where this matters

Encryption. Almost all of modern cryptography is built on modular arithmetic — the arithmetic of remainders, which is what Discover P5 and Stretch S1 introduced. RSA’s security depends on the hardness of “given a \times b \bmod n, recover a and b” when n is a product of two large primes.


Databases. Hash tables (Stretch S2) — the core data structure behind nearly every key-value store, every dictionary, every cache — use the remainder operation to map keys to buckets. The choice of n (table size) and the speed of remainder computation are first-order performance concerns.


AI / Machine Learning. “Average loss across a batch” is a division; “learning rate” is a division-like scaling; data normalisation divides each feature by its range. ML pipelines are dense with division and the reciprocal idea.


Everywhere else. Splitting a bill at a restaurant, dividing inheritance, computing miles per litre, finding a unit price — division is how we turn totals into per-thing rates.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones honoured: Pestalozzi (the friend-share widget makes fair share visible), Boole (the what to do with leftovers? question in Wonder leads naturally into fractions), Russian school (the remainder structure a = bq + r is discovered in Problem 2, not declared), Bhāskara (single-box Name + verse).
Self-study guarantees all present.

title: 07 · Fractions
text:
Have you done Lesson 6 — Division as Fair Share?
Can you compute 12 \div 4 = 3 and 13 \div 4 = 3 remainder 1?
Have you ever cut a cake, a pizza, or a chapati into equal pieces?

title: 07 · Fractions
07 · Fractions
text:
Wonder

Set the cut to 4, take 4. What did you take? Set the cut to 4, take

What does that even mean?

Set the cut to 8, take 2. Then set the cut to 4, take 1. The shaded region looks the same. Is there a relationship between \tfrac{2}{8} and \tfrac{1}{4}?
The number \tfrac{1}{2} — where does it sit on the number line?

title: 07 · Fractions
text:
Discover

Problem 1
You have 2 pizzas. You want to share them between 3 friends. What does each friend get?
You can’t give each friend a whole pizza (only 2 pizzas, 3 friends). You can’t give them whole halves either (you’d have 4 halves but 3 friends). What’s the right way to cut?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Cut each pizza into 3 equal pieces. Now you have 6 pieces total. Share them.



Full solution

Cut each pizza into 3 equal slices: that’s 6 slices. Each friend gets 2 slices. Each slice is one third of a pizza, so each friend gets two thirds, written \frac{2}{3}.
This is the answer to “2 \div 3” — a number that did not exist in our system before fractions. Division now always has an answer; we just needed to invent the number to hold it.



Problem 2
Take a pie chart cut into 8 equal pieces and shade 2 of them. Now take a pie chart cut into 4 equal pieces and shade 1. Is the shaded area the same?


Full solution

Yes — exactly the same. Two-eighths and one-quarter cover the same fraction of the whole. They are different names for the same number: \frac{2}{8} = \frac{1}{4}.
Multiplying or dividing both numerator and denominator by the same non-zero number gives an equivalent fraction. This is the same “different names for the same number” idea we met for whole numbers in Lesson 1.



Problem 3
What is \tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{1}{4} as a single fraction?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Imagine cutting a circle into 4 equal pieces. Half of it is 2 of those pieces. A quarter is 1. So you have…



Full solution

Rewrite \tfrac{1}{2} as \tfrac{2}{4} (using the equivalence from P2). Now we are adding fractions with the same denominator: \frac{2}{4} + \frac{1}{4} = \frac{3}{4}.
Rule discovered: to add fractions, first make the denominators the same; then add only the numerators.



Problem 4
What is \tfrac{3}{4} of 20 sweets?


Hint 1 — a nudge

“Of” usually means multiplication. \tfrac{3}{4} of 20 means: split 20 into 4 equal parts, take 3 of them.



Full solution

Split 20 into 4 equal parts: 20 \div 4 = 5 sweets in each part. Take 3 parts: 3 \times 5 = 15 sweets. So \tfrac{3}{4} of 20 = 15.
This is also \tfrac{3}{4} \times 20 = \tfrac{3 \times 20}{4} =
\tfrac{60}{4} = 15. A fraction times a whole number = (numerator × whole) ÷ denominator.
This is also where Multiplication-as-Area gets generalised: 0.75 of a strip — three-quarters of it — is \tfrac{3}{4} \times \text{length}. The rectangle picture survives even when the numbers are no longer whole.



Problem 5
Set the widget to “cut into 3, take 5”. What does the picture look like? What does the fraction \tfrac{5}{3} mean?


Hint 1 — a nudge

You’ve taken more pieces than fit in one whole circle. So the picture maxes out at “all shaded” — but the fraction \tfrac{5}{3} still has a meaning.



Full solution

\tfrac{5}{3} means 5 thirds, which is more than one whole (since \tfrac{3}{3} is already one whole). Specifically, 5 thirds = 3 thirds + 2 thirds = 1 + \tfrac{2}{3} = 1\tfrac{2}{3}.
A fraction with numerator ≥ denominator is called improper and represents a number ≥ 1. We can write it either as \tfrac{5}{3} or as the mixed number 1\tfrac{2}{3} — they are the same number.
The widget had to cap at “fully shaded” because one circle holds at most 3 thirds. To show \tfrac{5}{3} honestly, we’d need a second circle. The fraction itself is undisturbed; the picture has its own limits.


The Indian astronomer-mathematician Bhāskara II, writing his Līlāvatī in 1150 CE, gave the modern bar-form fraction \tfrac{p}{q} its full algebraic life — addition, subtraction, multiplication, division by reciprocal, all already there. Līlāvatī (named for his daughter) phrases problems as little stories — bees on a lotus, peacocks on a pillar — and was used as a textbook in India for over six hundred years. The pizza-and-pie pictures you just used are direct descendants of his worked examples.


A fraction isn’t a “broken number” — it’s the answer division gave us when the leftovers refused to disappear. Once we accept \tfrac{p}{q} as a real number, division always has an answer (except by zero), and the gaps in the number line start filling in.

title: 07 · Fractions
text:
Name

A fraction is written \dfrac{p}{q} where p is the numerator (“how many parts”) and q is the denominator (“the size of each part: 1 of q equal pieces of a whole”), with q \neq 0.
The fraction \dfrac{p}{q} is the answer to p \div q.
Two fractions are equivalent if multiplying (or dividing) numerator and denominator by the same non-zero number gives one from the other: \frac{p}{q} = \frac{p \times k}{q \times k} \quad \text{for any } k \neq 0.
To add or subtract fractions, first rewrite them with a common denominator, then add or subtract the numerators.
To multiply \dfrac{a}{b} \times \dfrac{c}{d}, multiply numerators and denominators: \dfrac{a \times c}{b \times d}.


Numerator on top — how many parts you have; Denominator below — the size of each piece. Common denominators must come first to add; Multiply across to multiply.

title: 07 · Fractions
text:
Apply

Worked example

Compute \dfrac{2}{3} + \dfrac{1}{6}.

A competent solver finds a common denominator first.

The denominators are 3 and 6. The smallest common multiple is 6.
Rewrite \tfrac{2}{3} with denominator 6: multiply top and bottom by 2 to get \tfrac{4}{6}.
Now both fractions share the denominator 6: \frac{4}{6} + \frac{1}{6} = \frac{5}{6}.

Answer: \dfrac{5}{6}. Check by picture: cut a circle into 6 pieces, shade 4 (which is two-thirds) and then 1 more — five out of six.


Practice — Warm
W1. Write three fractions equivalent to \dfrac{1}{2}.


Full solution

\tfrac{2}{4}, \tfrac{3}{6}, \tfrac{50}{100} — multiply top and bottom by any non-zero number.

W2. Compute \dfrac{3}{8} + \dfrac{2}{8} and \dfrac{7}{10} -
\dfrac{3}{10}.


Full solution

\tfrac{5}{8} and \tfrac{4}{10} = \tfrac{2}{5}. With common denominators, add or subtract only the numerators.

W3. Find \dfrac{2}{5} of 35.


Full solution

35 \div 5 = 7, then 2 \times 7 = 14.



Practice — Working
P1. Compute \dfrac{1}{3} + \dfrac{1}{4}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Common denominator: smallest multiple of 3 and 4 is 12.



Full solution

\tfrac{1}{3} = \tfrac{4}{12}. \tfrac{1}{4} = \tfrac{3}{12}. Sum: \tfrac{7}{12}.

P2. Compute \dfrac{2}{3} \times \dfrac{4}{5}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

For multiplication: multiply across. No common denominator needed.



Full solution

\tfrac{2 \times 4}{3 \times 5} = \tfrac{8}{15}.
Picture (the rectangle returns): take a 1-by-1 square. Shade horizontally to \tfrac{2}{3} of its height, vertically to \tfrac{4}{5} of its width. The doubly-shaded inner rectangle is \tfrac{2}{3} \times
\tfrac{4}{5} = \tfrac{8}{15} of the whole.

P3. Convert the improper fraction \dfrac{17}{5} to a mixed number.


Hint 1 — a nudge

This is just division-with-remainder from Lesson 6.



Full solution

17 \div 5 = 3 remainder 2. So \dfrac{17}{5} = 3\tfrac{2}{5}. The quotient is the whole part; the remainder over the divisor is the fractional part.



Practice — Stretch
S1. What is \dfrac{1}{2} \div \dfrac{1}{4}? (How many quarters fit in one half?)


Hint 1 — a nudge

Picture a half-circle. How many quarter-circles do you need to cover the half-circle?



Full solution

\tfrac{1}{2} \div \tfrac{1}{4} = 2 — two quarters fit in a half.
The general rule: dividing by a fraction is the same as multiplying by its reciprocal (flip top and bottom): \frac{a}{b} \div \frac{c}{d} = \frac{a}{b} \times \frac{d}{c}.
Here: \tfrac{1}{2} \times \tfrac{4}{1} = \tfrac{4}{2} = 2. ✓
This is one reason fraction division feels strange: dividing by a fraction less than 1 makes the result bigger than the original — contradicting the intuition from whole-number division.

S2. A database stores user ages and computes “the average age”. The ages of three users are 22, 31, and 25. What is the average? Is the answer always a whole number?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Average = total ÷ count. The count is 3.



Full solution

Total: 22 + 31 + 25 = 78. Count: 3. Average: 78 \div 3 = 26. 26.
But not always whole: if the ages had been 22, 31, 26, the total would be 79, and 79 \div 3 = 26\tfrac{1}{3}. Statistical computations almost always produce non-whole numbers — the average is a summary of the data, not necessarily a value that any individual user takes. We will unpack the average properly in Lesson 14: Data and the Average.



Common confusions

“To add fractions, add numerators and add denominators.” This is wrong: \tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{1}{2} would give \tfrac{2}{4} =
\tfrac{1}{2} — but the answer is one whole, not a half. Common denominators first; then add only the top.
“A bigger denominator means a bigger fraction.” No — bigger denominator means more pieces in the whole, hence each piece is smaller. \tfrac{1}{8} is smaller than \tfrac{1}{2}.
“Dividing by a fraction always gives a smaller number.” As S1 showed, the opposite happens when the divisor is less than 1.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A fraction \tfrac{p}{q} is the answer to p \div q — the leftover that wouldn’t fit, written as a number in its own right.
The same fraction has many names (equivalent fractions) — multiplying top and bottom by the same nonzero number keeps the value unchanged.
To add or subtract, line the denominators up first; to multiply, multiply across; to divide, flip and multiply (reciprocal).



Self-Assessment

Could you draw a picture showing why \tfrac{2}{8} and \tfrac{1}{4} are the same number?
Without scrolling back, can you compute \tfrac{1}{3} + \tfrac{1}{4}?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?


3 yes: ready for Lesson 8 — Decimals.
2 yes: redo S1 — fraction division is the most-mis-taught topic in primary maths.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P2 and P3.

title: 07 · Fractions
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

Lesson 6 — Division as Fair Share — fractions are exactly the answer to division when the total doesn’t split evenly. We invented them to fill the gap left by remainders.



What this unlocks

Decimals — decimals are fractions whose denominators are powers of 10, written in place-value form.
Ratio and Proportion — \tfrac{p}{q} also expresses “the ratio p to q”.



Where this matters

Mathematical research. Fractions extend the natural numbers (Lesson 1) into the rational numbers \mathbb{Q} — the second great number system after \mathbb{N}. Tier 5 will revisit this construction formally and meet the irrationals (numbers like \sqrt{2} or \pi that can’t be written as fractions).


AI / Machine Learning. Probabilities are fractions: “70% chance” is \tfrac{7}{10}. Loss functions are averages (fractions). Gradients are ratios of small changes. Most of ML’s numerical machinery lives in the fraction world (and its decimal cousin).


Databases. Aggregate queries (averages, percentages, ratios) all produce fractional answers. SQL’s AVG() is a fraction. Data normalisation often divides each value by the maximum, producing values in [0, 1] — pure fractions.


Everywhere else. Recipe scaling, currency conversion, slope of a ramp, batting averages, taxi fares (per kilometre), interest rates — fractions are how the world parameterises rate and proportion.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones honoured: Pestalozzi (the pie-chart widget — a centuries-old fraction manipulative made interactive), Boole (the Wonder questions surface equivalence and improper fractions before naming), Russian school (P2 forces equivalence, P5 forces improper fractions), Bhāskara (single-box Name + verse). Bhāskara’s Līlāvatī itself contains some of the most beautifully phrased fraction problems in mathematical history.
Self-study guarantees all present.

title: 11 · Variables
text:
Have you done Lesson 4 — Add & Subtract and Lesson 5 — Multiplication as Area?
In Lesson 4 (P2), you found the missing number in \ ? + 36 = 100. Did that feel like a new kind of computation — solving for the unknown — rather than just doing arithmetic?
Have you used a spreadsheet, where one cell’s value depends on others? That dependence is exactly the variable idea.

title: 11 · Variables
11 · Variables
text:
Wonder

If x = 0 what is y? If x = -3? If x = 100?
The recipe 2x + 5 is one expression. Could you describe the same recipe with different letters? Would it still be the same?
If you knew the output y = 17, could you work backwards to the input x?

title: 11 · Variables
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Compute y = 2x + 5 for x = 0, 1, 2, 3, -2.


Full solution

y(0) = 5, \ y(1) = 7, \ y(2) = 9, \ y(3) = 11, \ y(-2) = 1.
Notice the pattern: as x goes up by 1, y goes up by 2. The “2” in 2x + 5 controls the rate of change; the “5” sets the value when x = 0.
You have just met your first linear function. Tier 2 will explore these in depth — they are the spine of much of applied mathematics.



Problem 2
A taxi charges Rs. 30 as a base fare, plus Rs. 15 per kilometre. Write an expression for the total cost C if you travel d kilometres.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Base fare is fixed. Per-km charge depends on distance.



Full solution

C = 30 + 15 \times d, \quad \text{or simply} \quad C = 30 + 15d.
(Recall from Lesson 5 Stretch S2 that we drop the × sign in algebraic expressions because × looks like the letter x.)
You have written your first algebraic formula — an expression involving a variable (here, d) that captures a real-world rule. For d = 8 km: C = 30 + 15 \times 8 = 30 + 120 = 150 rupees.



Problem 3
Solve for the unknown: x + 7 = 12.
What is x?


Hint 1 — a nudge

What plus 7 gives 12? Use the inverse operation (Lesson 4 P2).



Full solution

x = 5. Method: subtract 7 from both sides of the equation. x + 7 - 7 = 12 - 7 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x = 5.
The “do the same thing to both sides” idea is the fundamental move of algebra. The equation is a balance scale; whatever you do to the left you must do to the right, or the balance breaks.



Problem 4
Solve for x: 3x = 21.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Inverse of multiplication is division. Divide both sides by 3.



Full solution

x = 21 \div 3 = 7. Check: 3 \times 7 = 21 ✓.
To “isolate” a variable, undo the operations one at a time, using inverses, while keeping both sides equal.



Problem 5
Verify the distributive law by substituting numbers. The law says: for any a, b, c, a \times (b + c) = a \times b + a \times c.
Try a = 3, b = 4, c = 5. Compute both sides.


Full solution

Left: 3 \times (4 + 5) = 3 \times 9 = 27. Right: 3 \times 4 + 3 \times 5 = 12 + 15 = 27. ✓ Both equal 27.
Try with any other numbers — they will always agree. That is the meaning of “law”: a statement true for all values of the variables.
Picture (recall Lesson 5 Discover P4): a 3 \times 9 rectangle is the same as a 3 \times 4 rectangle and a 3 \times 5 rectangle placed side by side. The distributive law is the rectangle-splitting picture put into symbols.
This is the power of variables: one symbolic statement captures infinitely many numerical facts.


The French mathematician François Viète (1540–1603) was the first to use letters for both knowns and unknowns — vowels for unknowns, consonants for parameters — in his In artem analyticem isagoge (1591). Before Viète, “algebra” was done in words and ad-hoc abbreviations. The modern x, y, z for unknowns was a tweak introduced by Descartes in La Géométrie (1637); the letters stuck. The word algebra itself comes from al-Khwārizmī’s 9th-century al-jabr — “reunion of broken parts” — the move you make when you add the same thing to both sides.


A variable is a promise about a number, not the number itself. Once you treat it like a real number — adding, multiplying, doing the same thing to both sides — the unknown reveals itself.

title: 11 · Variables
text:
Name

A variable is a letter (usually x, y, n, t, \ldots) that stands in for a number whose value is either unknown, or allowed to vary across some range.
An algebraic expression is a recipe built from numbers, variables, and operations: 2x + 5, 3x - 7, x^2, \frac{a + b}{2}.
An equation is a statement that two expressions are equal: x + 7 =
12. To solve an equation is to find the value(s) of the variable that make it true.
The fundamental move: whatever you do to one side of an equation, do to the other. (Add the same number, subtract the same number, multiply by the same non-zero number, etc.) This preserves equality.


A letter for a number we do not know; A recipe written once, used many times; Both sides equal — keep the balance, and the balance reveals the unknown.

title: 11 · Variables
text:
Apply

Worked example

Solve for x: 2x + 5 = 17.

A competent solver works one inverse at a time:

Subtract 5 from both sides to peel off the “+5”: 2x + 5 - 5 = 17 - 5 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 2x = 12.
Divide both sides by 2 to peel off the “× 2”: \frac{2x}{2} = \frac{12}{2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x = 6.
Check: 2 \times 6 + 5 = 12 + 5 = 17 ✓.

Answer: x = 6. Always check by substituting your answer back into the original equation. It catches arithmetic slips immediately.


Practice — Warm
W1. Evaluate 3x - 4 for x = 0, 2, 5, -1.


Full solution

-4, \ 2, \ 11, \ -7.

W2. Solve: \ x + 9 = 15. Then: \ y - 4 = 10.


Full solution

x = 6 (subtract 9 from both sides). y = 14 (add 4 to both sides).

W3. Solve: \ 4x = 32. Then: \ \dfrac{y}{5} = 6.


Full solution

x = 8 (divide both sides by 4). y = 30 (multiply both sides by 5).



Practice — Working
P1. Solve: \ 3x + 7 = 25.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Subtract 7 first; then divide by 3.



Full solution

3x = 18, then x = 6. Check: 3 \times 6 + 7 = 25 ✓.

P2. A rectangular garden has length L metres and width 6 metres. Its area is 42 square metres. Find L.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Area = length × width. Set up an equation.



Full solution

6L = 42, so L = 7 metres. The variable lets us write one equation that captures all possible problems of this shape — change the numbers and the same algebra still works.

P3. A taxi from Discover P2 charges C = 30 + 15d. The fare for a ride was Rs. 195. How far did the trip go?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Set C = 195 and solve for d.



Full solution

30 + 15d = 195. Subtract 30: 15d = 165. Divide by 15: d = 11 km.
This is the power of equations: one formula solves both forward (given distance, find cost) and backward (given cost, find distance) problems.



Practice — Stretch
S1. The distributive law (Discover P5) lets us expand products of expressions. Compute (x + 3)(x + 5) in expanded form.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Picture: a rectangle with one side (x + 3) and the other (x + 5). Split into 4 sub-rectangles.



Full solution

The rectangle splits into four pieces: x \cdot x, x \cdot 5, 3 \cdot
x, 3 \cdot 5. Areas: x^2, 5x, 3x, 15.
(x + 3)(x + 5) = x^2 + 5x + 3x + 15 = x^2 + 8x + 15.
You just multiplied your first polynomial. Algebra II in Tier 3 will revisit this thoroughly. The rectangle picture from Lesson 5 is once again the engine behind everything.

S2. A neural network’s prediction for one input is computed as \hat{y} = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b, where x_1, x_2 are the input values, w_1, w_2 are the weights, and b is the bias. Why are the variables grouped this way? What real problem does the algebra solve?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Compare the structure to the taxi formula C = 30 + 15d.



Full solution

The neural network formula is exactly the taxi formula — generalised. b plays the role of the base fare; each weight w_i plays the role of “rate per unit of x_i”. The whole machinery of deep learning is built on stacking many of these linear formulas, with non-linear “squashing” functions in between.
When the network trains, it solves equations — but in reverse: given many input/output examples, it adjusts the w’s and b’s to make \hat{y} match the true y as closely as possible. The “fundamental move” of algebra (do the same thing to both sides) becomes the “fundamental move” of training (adjust the weights using the gradient).
You have just met the deepest connection between algebra and AI: a neural network is a (very) large algebraic expression, and learning is solving for its variables.



Common confusions

“x is a fixed number.” Sometimes (when solving an equation, the solution x is fixed). But variables are also used to mean any number (when stating laws like the distributive law). Context picks.
“You can do anything to one side of an equation.” No — only if you do it to the other side too. Otherwise, the balance breaks and the equation no longer holds.
“Algebra is just arithmetic with letters.” Almost true — but the letters let you express general facts and infinitely many computations at once. That generalisation is what makes algebra powerful.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A variable is a placeholder for a number — sometimes one specific unknown, sometimes any number in a range.
An equation is a balance: do the same thing to both sides and the balance holds — that one move solves almost every Tier 1 equation.
One algebraic expression like 2x + 5 stands for infinitely many arithmetic facts at once — that compression is why algebra runs the modern world.



Self-Assessment

Could you explain the “fundamental move” (do the same thing to both sides) and why it’s allowed?
Without scrolling back, can you solve 4x + 3 = 19?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?


3 yes: ready for Lesson 12 — Shapes and Angles.
2 yes: redo S1 — the rectangle picture for (x+3)(x+5) is fundamental.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P5.

title: 11 · Variables
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

Lesson 4 — Addition & Subtraction — the inverse-operation idea (subtract to undo addition) is the engine.
Lesson 5 — Multiplication as Area — the rectangle picture justifies the distributive law and polynomial multiplication.



What this unlocks

Tier 2 — Linear Equations and Systems — many variables, many equations, all solved by the same fundamental move.
Tier 2 — Functions as Machines — the input-output picture from Touch generalised.
Tier 5 — Linear Algebra — all of ML is built on this.



Where this matters

AI / Machine Learning. A neural network is, mathematically, a giant algebraic expression in (sometimes billions of) variables. Training is the process of finding the values of those variables that minimise a loss function. Stretch S2 makes the connection explicit.


Encryption. Modern cryptography is full of equations of the form “given g^x \bmod p, find x” (the discrete logarithm problem) — its hardness is what protects your messages. Variables and equations are the language in which these problems are stated.


Mathematical research. Variables and the equational style are the language of mathematics. From abstract algebra to differential equations to category theory, every advanced subject extends what you have just begun.


Everywhere else. Spreadsheets, programming (every variable in code), recipe scaling, financial planning, scientific modelling, engineering design — the moment you label something with a name and reason about it abstractly, you are doing algebra.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones honoured: Pestalozzi (the input-output machine widget makes a function visible as a literal device), Boole (the can you work backwards question opens equation-solving), Russian school (P5 derives the distributive law from the rectangle picture, not as an axiom), Bhāskara (single-box Name + verse — al-Khwārizmī’s Book of Restoration and Balancing, c. 820 CE, gave us the word “algebra” and codified the do the same thing to both sides method).
Self-study guarantees all present.

title: 09 · Ratio and Proportion
text:
Have you done Lesson 7 — Fractions?
Can you compute \tfrac{6}{8} = \tfrac{3}{4} — i.e. simplify a fraction by dividing numerator and denominator by their common factor?
Can you scale a recipe — for example, double the ingredients?

title: 09 · Ratio and Proportion
09 · Ratio and Proportion
text:
Wonder

Set 4 : 1, then 8 : 2, then 12 : 3. The mixture colour stays the same. Why?
What if you set 4 : 4? What does the mixture look like compared to 1 : 1?
A recipe says “milk and flour in the ratio 2:5”. You have only 200ml of milk. How much flour?

title: 09 · Ratio and Proportion
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Set the ratio to 4 : 1. Then 8 : 2. Then 12 : 3. The strength of the lemonade did not change. What’s going on?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Each ratio reduces to the same simpler form.



Full solution

4:1 and 8:2 and 12:3 are all the same ratio — they all reduce to 4:1 (divide both sides by their common factor).
A ratio is just a fraction in different clothes: \frac{\text{water}}{\text{juice}} = \frac{4}{1} = \frac{8}{2} = \frac{12}{3}.
Equivalent fractions (Lesson 7) and equivalent ratios are the same idea. The “strength” depends only on the ratio, not the absolute amounts.



Problem 2
A class has boys and girls in the ratio 3 : 2. The class has 30 students in total. How many boys, how many girls?


Hint 1 — a nudge

The ratio means: out of every 5 students, 3 are boys and 2 are girls. 30 ÷ 5 = ?



Full solution

Total ratio parts: 3 + 2 = 5. So 30 \div 5 = 6 students per “part”. Boys: 3 \times 6 = 18. Girls: 2 \times 6 = 12. Check: 18 + 12 = 30. ✓
This is the standard “split a quantity in a given ratio” recipe: divide the total by the sum of the ratio parts, then multiply by each.



Problem 3
A recipe says flour : sugar = 5 : 2. You have 300 g of flour. How much sugar?


Hint 1 — a nudge

“For every 5 g of flour, you need 2 g of sugar.” How many “5 g of flour” go into 300 g?



Full solution

300 \div 5 = 60 “units” of flour. Each unit needs 2 g of sugar. Sugar: 60 \times 2 = 120 g.
Equivalently: set up the proportion \frac{5}{2} = \frac{300}{x} \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = \frac{2 \times 300}{5} = 120. Cross-multiplying gives the same answer. Both methods are correct; pick whichever feels natural.



Problem 4
A map has scale 1 cm = 25 km. Two cities are 8 cm apart on the map. How far apart in real life?


Hint 1 — a nudge

The map is just a scaled-down picture. Each cm is 25 km.



Full solution

8 \text{ cm} \times 25 \text{ km/cm} = 200 km. Same idea as the recipe — ratio scaling. Maps, blueprints, model trains, and game-engine “world-to-screen” coordinates are all scale ratios.



Problem 5
A snail moves 3 metres in 5 minutes. At the same pace, how far does it travel in 20 minutes? In 1 hour?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Find “metres per minute” first, then scale.



Full solution

Speed: 3 \div 5 = 0.6 m/minute.
In 20 minutes: 0.6 \times 20 = 12 m. In 60 minutes: 0.6 \times 60 = 36 m.
A speed (or rate) is a ratio with a unit attached: m/minute, km/litre, $/kg, beats/second. Every “per” expression in the world is a ratio. We use the same arithmetic for “per litre” and “per minute” as for “boys per girl” — that is the unifying power of the idea.


The first careful theory of ratio is in Book V of Euclid’s Elements (c. 300 BCE), credited to Eudoxus of Cnidus (c. 408–355 BCE). Eudoxus needed it to handle ratios of incommensurable lengths — like the diagonal of a square to its side — that no fraction can capture. His definition of “equal ratios” was so subtle that it foreshadowed the modern definition of a real number, given by Dedekind more than 2,000 years later.


A ratio is the shape of a comparison, not its size. Multiply both quantities by the same number and the shape — the strength, the colour, the flavour — stays the same.

title: 09 · Ratio and Proportion
text:
Name

A ratio a : b compares two quantities. It is the same number as the fraction \dfrac{a}{b}. Two ratios are equivalent if they reduce to the same fraction: a : b \ = \ k a : k b \quad \text{for any } k \neq 0.
A proportion is a statement that two ratios are equal: \frac{a}{b} = \frac{c}{d} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad a \times d = b \times c. This is cross-multiplication — the workhorse for solving “given three of a, b, c, d, find the fourth”.
A rate is a ratio of two quantities with different units (km/litre, $/kg, m/minute). Every “per” expression is a rate.


Two quantities in a ratio — multiply both by the same number, And the ratio holds. Set two ratios equal — cross-multiply to find the missing piece. Per-this-per-that is a rate, and a rate is a ratio in disguise.

title: 09 · Ratio and Proportion
text:
Apply

Worked example

A car travels 240 km on 16 litres of petrol. At the same fuel efficiency, how far can it travel on 25 litres?

A competent solver sets up a proportion: \frac{240 \text{ km}}{16 \text{ L}} = \frac{x \text{ km}}{25 \text{ L}}.
Cross-multiply: 240 \times 25 = 16 \times x. So x = \dfrac{240 \times
25}{16} = \dfrac{6000}{16} = 375 km.
Answer: 375 km. Or, equivalently: efficiency = 240 \div 16 = 15 km/L, then 15 \times 25 = 375 km. Same answer; same idea.


Practice — Warm
W1. Reduce these ratios to simplest form: 6 : 9, \ 20 : 50, \ 100 : 25.


Full solution

2 : 3, \ 2 : 5, \ 4 : 1. (Divide both sides by the largest common factor.)

W2. Split 42 in the ratio 3 : 4.


Full solution

Total parts: 3 + 4 = 7. Each part: 42 \div 7 = 6. Result: 18 and 24. Check: 18 + 24 = 42, and 18 : 24 = 3 : 4 ✓.

W3. A bicycle wheel makes 120 turns per minute. How many turns in 5 minutes?


Full solution

120 \times 5 = 600 turns.



Practice — Working
P1. A photograph is 6 inches wide and 4 inches tall. To enlarge it without distortion, you keep the width : height ratio. If the enlarged width is 15 inches, what is the new height?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Original ratio: 6 : 4. Set up the proportion with the new width.



Full solution

\dfrac{6}{4} = \dfrac{15}{h}. Cross-multiply: 6h = 60, so h = 10 inches. The “aspect ratio” idea is the heart of every image, video, and screen-design decision.

P2. A recipe for 4 people uses 300 g of pasta and 2 eggs. You’re cooking for 10 people. How much pasta and how many eggs?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Find the per-person quantities first; then scale.



Full solution

Per person: 300 \div 4 = 75 g pasta and 2 \div 4 = 0.5 eggs. For 10: 75 \times 10 = 750 g pasta and 0.5 \times 10 = 5 eggs.
Recipe scaling is one of the oldest applications of proportion in human history.

P3. Percentages are just ratios with denominator 100. Convert: (a) \tfrac{3}{4} to a percentage; (b) 0.85 to a percentage; (c) “30% off” on an Rs. 1200 item — what is the discount?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Percent literally means “per hundred”. x\% = \tfrac{x}{100}.



Full solution


\tfrac{3}{4} = \tfrac{75}{100} = 75\%.
0.85 = \tfrac{85}{100} = 85\%.
30\% \text{ of } 1200 = \tfrac{30}{100} \times 1200 = 360. Rs. 360 off, leaving Rs. 840 to pay.




Practice — Stretch
S1. Two recipes for orange squash:

Recipe A: 3 parts juice : 5 parts water
Recipe B: 2 parts juice : 4 parts water

Which is stronger (more concentrated)?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Compute the fraction of juice in each total mix.



Full solution

Recipe A: juice fraction = \dfrac{3}{3+5} = \dfrac{3}{8} = 37.5\%. Recipe B: juice fraction = \dfrac{2}{2+4} = \dfrac{2}{6} = \dfrac{1}{3}
\approx 33.3\%.
Recipe A is slightly stronger. Many learners get this wrong by comparing 3 : 5 to 2 : 4 part-by-part. The right comparison is the fraction of juice in the total mix.

S2. A neural network learns by adjusting weights based on a learning rate — typically a small ratio like 0.001 or 0.0001. What is this expressing? Why does the choice matter?


Hint 1 — a nudge

A learning rate is “how much to change the weight per unit of error”. A rate is a ratio with units.



Full solution

The learning rate is the ratio “weight change per unit of error”. If it’s too high, weights jump around wildly and never settle. If it’s too low, training takes forever. Choosing it well — the field calls this “hyperparameter tuning” — is one of the practical arts of machine learning.
Behind every modern AI system is a careful set of ratios — gradient scaling, regularisation strength, dropout rates, batch fractions — each a Tier 1 idea applied at scale.



Common confusions

“Ratio is the same as ‘difference’.” No. A ratio of 4 : 1 does not mean “4 more”; it means “4 times as much”. The two ideas (additive vs multiplicative comparison) get confused all the time.
“Compare ratios part by part.” As Stretch S1 showed, 3:5 vs 2:4 doesn’t yield to part-by-part. Always compute the fraction of the total (or equivalent decimals) and compare those.
“Percentage is a different thing from a ratio.” Percent is just “per hundred” — a ratio with a fixed denominator. 25\% is the same as \tfrac{1}{4} is the same as 0.25 is the same as 1 : 3 in “part : rest” form (careful: 1 : 3 means 1 of every 4, not 1 of every 3 — see common error #1).



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A ratio is a comparison that survives scaling — double both sides and the comparison is unchanged.
A proportion is two ratios set equal, and cross-multiplication finds the missing piece.
Every “per” in the world — km per litre, rupees per kg, learning rate per error — is a ratio in disguise.



Self-Assessment

Could you explain to a friend why 4 : 1, 8 : 2, and 12 : 3 are the same ratio?
Without scrolling back, can you split 60 in the ratio 2 : 3 : 5?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(For W2-style three-term split: total parts =2+3+5=10, each part =60 \div 10 = 6, so split is 12, 18, 30.)

3 yes: ready for Lesson 10 — Negative Numbers.
2 yes: redo S1 — comparing two ratios correctly is genuinely hard.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P2 and P5.

title: 09 · Ratio and Proportion
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

Lesson 7 — Fractions — a ratio is a fraction with two named parts.



What this unlocks

Tier 2 — Linear functions — every linear relationship y = mx is a constant ratio between x and y.
Tier 3 — Trigonometry — the basic trig ratios (sin, cos, tan) are just ratios of triangle sides.
Tier 5 — Probability — every probability is a ratio: favourable outcomes over total outcomes.



Where this matters

Game development. Every image, sprite, and screen has an aspect ratio. Coordinate systems are linear maps — pure ratios. Physics constants (gravity per second-squared, drag per velocity) are rates.


AI / Machine Learning. Learning rate (Stretch S2), batch size ratio, train/validation/test splits, dropout rate, label-class ratios in imbalanced datasets — modern ML is a thicket of carefully chosen ratios.


Databases. SQL aggregates yield ratios constantly: percentages, counts-per-group, averages. The very query “the percentage of users who clicked the link” is a ratio computation.


Everywhere else. Currency exchange rates, mortgage interest rates, nutritional labels (per 100 g), election results (vote shares), sports statistics, fuel efficiency, exam scores out of 100 — the world is governed by ratios.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones honoured: Pestalozzi (the lemonade widget makes ratio a tasteable property), Boole (the why does the colour stay the same? question precedes the equivalence rule), Russian school (S1 forces the part-by-part comparison error to be caught), Bhāskara (single-box Name + verse — the Indian tradition is the source of most of the cross-multiplication intuition for proportions, going back at least to Brahmagupta).
Self-study guarantees all present.

title: 15 · Divisibility and Primes
text:
Have you done Lesson 6 — Division as Fair Share?
Can you compute 35 \div 5 = 7 and recognise that 35 \div 4 has a remainder?
(Optional, but rewarding) Have you done Lesson 11 — Variables? You’ll appreciate the general statements more if so.

title: 15 · Divisibility and Primes
15 · Divisibility and Primes
text:
Wonder

Is 1 a prime? Try the widget. What does it say? (1 has only one divisor — itself.)
Set n to: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13. Notice anything? Now try 4, 6, 8, 9, 10, 12.
Could there be infinitely many primes? Or do they eventually run out?

title: 15 · Divisibility and Primes
text:
Discover

Problem 1
List all the primes less than 20.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Use the widget. Step through 2, 3, 4, … and write down the ones the widget marks “PRIME”.



Full solution

2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19. Eight primes below 20.
Note: 2 is the only even prime — every other even number is divisible by 2. After 2, primes appear less and less often, and they never settle into a simple pattern. The “irregularity” of the primes is one of the deepest mysteries in mathematics.



Problem 2
Factor 60 into primes — write 60 as a product of prime numbers.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Start by dividing by the smallest primes. 60 \div 2 = 30. 30 \div 2
= 15. 15 \div 3 = 5. 5 is prime — stop.



Full solution

60 = 2 \times 2 \times 3 \times 5 = 2^2 \times 3 \times 5.
Try a different starting point: 60 = 4 \times 15 = (2 \times 2) \times
(3 \times 5) = 2 \times 2 \times 3 \times 5. The same primes — just in a different order.

The Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic. Every whole number greater than 1 can be written as a product of primes in exactly one way (up to the order). Primes are the building blocks of all whole numbers.

This is one of mathematics’ great theorems — and it is the reason primes matter at all.



Problem 3
Are there infinitely many primes, or do they run out at some big number?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Suppose, for contradiction, that there were only finitely many primes — say p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_k. What is special about the number N = p_1 \times p_2 \times \cdots \times p_k + 1?



Full solution

There are infinitely many primes. Here is Euclid’s proof (c. 300 BCE) — one of the most beautiful proofs in mathematics, accessible to a careful Tier 1 reader.
Suppose for contradiction that there were only finitely many primes, p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_k. Consider the number N = p_1 \times p_2 \times \cdots \times p_k + 1.
When we divide N by any of the p_i, we get remainder 1 (because N is exactly 1 more than a multiple of p_i). So no p_i divides N exactly. By the Fundamental Theorem (Problem 2), N must factor into some primes — but those primes can’t be in our list. So there must be primes outside the list, contradicting our assumption.
Conclusion: the list of primes can never be complete. There are infinitely many primes.
This proof, more than 2300 years old, still works. It is a milestone moment for many learners — the first time they see a proof of infinity. Take a minute. Re-read it.



Problem 4
Two friends each pick a prime number. Anu picks 17. Bina picks 23. They tell each other the product: 17 \times 23 = 391.
Now imagine you only know the product (391). Can you recover Anu’s and Bina’s numbers?


Hint 1 — a nudge

You’d need to factor 391. Try dividing by small primes: 391 \div 2? By 3? By 5? …



Full solution

391 \div 17 = 23 ✓. So 391 = 17 \times 23.
For these small primes, factoring takes seconds. But what if Anu and Bina had each picked a 300-digit prime? Their product would be a 600-digit number. No known algorithm can factor a random 600-digit number in any reasonable amount of time — even with all the world’s computers running for the lifetime of the universe.
Multiplying two large primes is easy. Factoring the product back is hard. This asymmetry is the mathematical heart of RSA, the encryption used by HTTPS, banking, messaging apps — almost every secure system on the internet.
When you visit a secure website, your browser and the server are silently performing this trick at scale. Your security depends on the mathematical fact you just discovered.



Problem 5
Find the greatest common divisor (GCD) of 24 and 36 — the largest number that divides both.


Hint 1 — a nudge

List the divisors of each. Find the largest in both lists.



Full solution

Divisors of 24: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24. Divisors of 36: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 9, 12, 18, 36. Common divisors: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12. Largest: 12.
So GCD(24, 36) = 12. Equivalently, factor each into primes: 24 = 2^3 \times 3, 36 = 2^2 \times 3^2. Take the minimum power of each shared prime: 2^{\min(3,2)} \times 3^{\min(1,2)} = 2^2 \times 3 = 12.
GCD is used everywhere: simplifying fractions (\tfrac{24}{36} =
\tfrac{2}{3} via dividing both by 12), scheduling repeating events, and — in cryptography — the extended Euclidean algorithm, which underpins how RSA generates its keys.


Eratosthenes of Cyrene (c. 276–194 BCE) — the same scholar who measured the Earth’s circumference using the angle of a shadow — gave us the first algorithm for finding primes: the Sieve of Eratosthenes. Write the numbers 2 to n in a row; circle 2 and cross out every multiple of 2; circle the next surviving number (3) and cross out every multiple of 3; repeat. The numbers still uncircled when you reach \sqrt{n} are all the primes up to n. Twenty-three centuries later, the sieve is still essentially how computers list primes — the asymptotically fastest variant, by Atkin and Bernstein, dates to 2003.


Multiplying two primes is easy; recovering them from the product is hard. That single asymmetry — trivial in one direction, intractable in the other — is what hides every secret on the internet.

title: 15 · Divisibility and Primes
text:
Name

A whole number a is divisible by another whole number b if a \div b has remainder 0 — equivalently, if a = b \times k for some whole number k. We say “b divides a” and write b \mid a.
A prime is a whole number \ge 2 with exactly two divisors: 1 and itself. The first few primes: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19, 23, \ldots
A composite number is a whole number \ge 2 that is not prime — equivalently, has at least three divisors.
The Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic: every integer \ge 2 has a unique prime factorisation (up to order).
The GCD (greatest common divisor) of two numbers is the largest number that divides both.
There are infinitely many primes (Euclid, c. 300 BCE).


Two divisors only — that is a prime; Every number is a unique product of primes; Multiplying primes is easy; factoring back is hard; And on that hardness, the secrets of the modern world rest.

title: 15 · Divisibility and Primes
text:
Apply

Worked example

Find the prime factorisation of 84 and the GCD of 84 and 60.

A competent solver factors each, then takes the minimum power of each shared prime:

Factor 84: 84 \div 2 = 42. 42 \div 2 = 21. 21 \div 3 = 7. 7 is prime. So 84 = 2^2 \times 3 \times 7.
Factor 60: 60 = 2^2 \times 3 \times 5 (from Discover P2, essentially).
Shared primes: 2 and 3. Minimum powers: 2^{\min(2,2)} = 2^2, 3^{\min(1,1)} = 3.
GCD: 2^2 \times 3 = 12.

Answer: 84 = 2^2 \times 3 \times 7, GCD(84, 60) = 12.


Practice — Warm
W1. Is each of these prime or composite? 15, 17, 21, 23, 29, 33.


Full solution

Composite: 15 (=3×5), 21 (=3×7), 33 (=3×11). Prime: 17, 23, 29.

W2. Factor into primes: 18, 50, 100.


Full solution

18 = 2 \times 3^2. 50 = 2 \times 5^2. 100 = 2^2 \times 5^2.

W3. Find GCD: (a) 12 and 18 (b) 20 and 35.


Full solution


12 = 2^2 \times 3, 18 = 2 \times 3^2. GCD = 2 \times 3 = 6.
20 = 2^2 \times 5, 35 = 5 \times 7. GCD = 5.




Practice — Working
P1. Use the prime factorisation method to simplify \dfrac{42}{56} to lowest terms.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Find GCD(42, 56), then divide top and bottom by it.



Full solution

42 = 2 \times 3 \times 7. 56 = 2^3 \times 7. GCD = 2 \times 7 = 14. \dfrac{42}{56} = \dfrac{42 \div 14}{56 \div 14} = \dfrac{3}{4}.
Simplifying fractions (Lesson 7) and finding GCDs are the same task.

P2. A box of 48 chocolates can be evenly distributed in how many different ways (number of friends per box, each getting a whole number)?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Each “way” corresponds to a divisor of 48.



Full solution

Divisors of 48: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 16, 24, 48 — that’s 10 divisors. (Each divisor is a valid “number of friends”.)
Quick way: 48 = 2^4 \times 3. The number of divisors is (4+1)(1+1) = 10. (The general formula: if n = p_1^{a_1} \cdots
p_k^{a_k}, then n has (a_1+1) \cdots (a_k+1) divisors.)

P3. Use the divisibility shortcuts from Lesson 6 P3 — last digit for 2 and 5; sum of digits for 3 and 9 — to test whether 3,825 is divisible by 2, 3, 5, 9.


Full solution


By 2: last digit is 5 (odd) → not divisible.
By 5: last digit is 5 → divisible. (3825 \div 5 = 765.)
By 3: 3+8+2+5 = 18, divisible by 3 → yes. (3825 \div 3 = 1275.)
By 9: same digit-sum 18, divisible by 9 → yes. (3825 \div 9 =
425.)

These shortcuts are old (the digit-sum rule for 3 is sometimes called the “rule of nines” and dates to ancient India). They turn many divisibility questions into one quick calculation.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Bob picks two primes: p = 7, q = 11. He computes N = p \times q = 77 and broadcasts N publicly. Alice wants to send him a private message; she uses N to encrypt it. Anyone listening sees only the encrypted text and the value 77.
For an attacker to decrypt the message, they need p and q individually. Can you recover them from N = 77?


Full solution

77 = 7 \times 11. Factoring 77 takes seconds, so the attacker wins — this is a toy example.
In real RSA, p and q are typically 300-digit primes, so N is a 600-digit number. No known classical algorithm can factor such an N in less than billions of years. RSA’s security is precisely the hardness of factoring.
A note on the future: a sufficiently large quantum computer could factor N quickly using Shor’s algorithm (1994). This is why “post-quantum cryptography” — using problems other than factoring — is an active research area. The lessons in Tier 7 (Encryption track) will cover this in detail.
The factoring problem you just considered for N = 77 is, mathematically, the same problem the world’s most secure systems rest on. Tier 1, the seed of modern cryptography.

S2. What’s the largest prime you can find with no calculator? Try just one number: is 97 prime? (Hint: you only need to check divisibility by primes p \le \sqrt{97} \approx 9.8.)


Hint 1 — a nudge

You only need to test primes ≤ \sqrt{97} — that’s just 2, 3, 5, 7. (Why? If 97 = a \times b with a, b > 1, the smaller of a, b is at most \sqrt{97}.)



Full solution

Check by 2: no (97 is odd). By 3: digit sum 9 + 7 = 16, not divisible by 3. By 5: doesn’t end in 0 or 5. By 7: 7 \times 13 = 91, 7 \times
14 = 98 — no. So 97 is prime.
The “p \le \sqrt{n}” trick is a huge speedup. Without it, you’d test all the way up to 97. With it, you only test up to 9. This idea — bounded search using square roots — is everywhere in computer algorithms.
The largest known prime as of writing has more than 24 million digits (it is a Mersenne prime, 2^{82{,}589{,}933} - 1, found in 2018). The hunt for primes is a major collaborative computing project (GIMPS).



Common confusions

“1 is a prime.” No — by convention, primes are at least 2 and have exactly two divisors. 1 has only one. This convention is chosen so the Fundamental Theorem (unique factorisation) works cleanly.
“Prime numbers are rare.” They become less dense as numbers grow, but there are infinitely many (Euclid, Discover P3). About \tfrac{n}{\ln n} primes lie below n — a beautiful estimate from the Prime Number Theorem (Tier 5+).
“You can find prime factors quickly.” For small numbers, yes. For 600-digit numbers, no — and the security of the internet depends on this fact.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A prime is a whole number ≥ 2 with exactly two divisors — and primes are the building blocks of every other whole number, in exactly one way.
There are infinitely many primes (Euclid’s proof, c. 300 BCE), and to test whether n is prime you only need to try divisors up to \sqrt{n}.
Multiplying primes is easy; factoring the product back is hard — and that gap is the foundation of RSA and most modern encryption.



Self-Assessment

Could you state Euclid’s proof that there are infinitely many primes, in your own words?
Without scrolling back, can you factor 84 into primes?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: 84 = 2^2 \times 3 \times 7.)

3 yes: You have completed Tier 1 — Foundations! The next step is the Tier 1 index for a recap, or pick a goal from one of the applied bridges.
2 yes: redo S1 — the encryption-asymmetry idea is the prize of this lesson. Then proceed.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P2 and P3. The Fundamental Theorem and Euclid’s proof are the heart of the lesson.

title: 15 · Divisibility and Primes
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

Lesson 6 — Division as Fair Share — divisibility is the question of zero remainder.



What this unlocks
This is the last lesson of Tier 1. Going forward:

Tier 5 — Number Theory — modular arithmetic, Fermat’s little theorem, the Euclidean algorithm in full generality, the Prime Number Theorem.
Tier 7 — Encryption track — RSA, Diffie–Hellman, elliptic curves, lattice-based post-quantum cryptography. All built on the foundation laid here.
Tier 7 — Research / number theory — analytic number theory, the Riemann hypothesis (the most famous unsolved problem in mathematics is about the distribution of primes).



Where this matters

Encryption. Every secure connection on the internet (HTTPS, SSH, TLS, signed software, blockchain transactions, encrypted messaging) is built on the hardness of factoring large products of primes (RSA), or on closely related problems (discrete log, elliptic curves). The problem you just considered for N = 77 is the same problem the world’s secrets rest on.


Mathematical research. Number theory is one of the oldest and deepest branches of mathematics. The distribution of primes touches analysis (Riemann zeta function), algebra (algebraic number theory), and even physics (random matrix theory). The most famous unsolved problem in mathematics — the Riemann hypothesis, with a $1 million prize — is about primes.


Computer science. Hash functions, error-correcting codes, random number generators, distributed systems consensus protocols (e.g. Schoenmakers’s threshold signatures), and many more all use number theory. Knuth devotes most of The Art of Computer Programming Volume 2 to this.


Everywhere else. ISBN check digits, credit card validation (Luhn algorithm), CRC error detection in network protocols, even some musical scales — divisibility ideas appear in surprisingly many practical engineering systems.

title: 15 · Divisibility and Primes
text:
You have finished Tier 1
This lesson closes Tier 1 — the Foundations of the curriculum. Across 15 lessons you have re-met:

What a number is.
Where numbers live (the line).
How we name them (place value).
The four operations, re-grounded as walks and rectangles.
Fractions, decimals, ratios, and negative numbers — the extensions that make arithmetic complete.
Variables and equations — the language of mathematics.
Geometry — shapes, angles, and measurements.
Statistics — averages, the language of data.
Number theory — the seed of modern encryption.

You are now ready for Tier 2 — Pre-Algebra & Algebra I (Grade 8–9), or for any of the applied tracks. The dependency graph is in _curriculum.yml; the visual map is on the home page.
Congratulations.







NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones honoured: Pestalozzi (the divisor-display widget makes prime/composite visible at a glance), Boole (the what makes primes special? question precedes the definition), Russian school (Euclid’s proof in Discover P3 is presented as a real proof, not hand-waved), Bhāskara (single-box Name + verse — and India is the origin of much of early number theory, from Brahmagupta to Aryabhata to the Kerala school), Better Explained (the “Aha!” framing is multiply-easy / factor-hard, and that’s why the internet is secure).
Self-study guarantees all present.

title: 06 · Applications of Derivatives — Optimisation
text:
Have you done 04 · Differentiation Rules?
Comfortable that the vertex of a parabola is its max or min (T3 L02)?
Comfortable computing f'(x) and finding where f'(x) = 0?

title: 06 · Applications of Derivatives — Optimisation
06 · Applications of Derivatives — Optimisation
text:
Wonder

Set “x² − 4x + 5.” One critical point at x = 2. Is it a max or min?
The cubic has two critical points. Which is the max, which the min?
The quartic has three. How do you tell each apart?

title: 06 · Applications of Derivatives — Optimisation
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Find the minimum of f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 5.


Hint 1 — a nudge

f'(x) = 2x - 4 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 2. Compute f(2).



Full solution

f(2) = 4 - 8 + 5 = 1. Minimum value 1 at x = 2.
(Same answer T3 L02 would give via vertex form: f(x) = (x - 2)^2 +
1. Calculus gives a method that works for any function, not just quadratics.)



Problem 2
Find all critical points of f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x.


Hint 1 — a nudge

f'(x) = 3x^2 - 12x + 9 = 3(x^2 - 4x + 3) = 3(x - 1)(x - 3).



Full solution

f'(x) = 0 \Rightarrow x = 1 or x = 3.
f(1) = 1 - 6 + 9 = 4. f(3) = 27 - 54 + 27 = 0.
To classify: use the second derivative test or the first derivative sign test.
f''(x) = 6x - 12. f''(1) = -6 < 0 → local max at x = 1. f''(3) = 6 > 0 → local min at x = 3.
Maxima: f''(c) < 0 (“concave down” at c). Minima: f''(c) > 0 (“concave up”).



Problem 3
A box has square base side x and height h. Volume must be 1000 cm³, so x^2 h = 1000 and h = 1000/x^2. Surface area is S = x^2 + 4 x h = x^2 + 4000/x. Find the dimensions minimising S.


Hint 1 — a nudge

S'(x) = 2x - 4000/x^2 = 0 \Rightarrow x^3 = 2000 \Rightarrow x =
\sqrt[3]{2000}.



Full solution

x = \sqrt[3]{2000} \approx 12.6 cm. Then h = 1000/x^2 \approx 6.3 cm.
Open-top boxes minimising surface area for a given volume have height \approx half the base side. This is the classic optimisation word problem — translate constraints, use derivative, solve.
(Check this is min: S''(x) = 2 + 8000/x^3 > 0 always — confirms min.)



Problem 4
A piece of wire 100 cm long is cut into two pieces. One piece is bent into a square, the other into a circle. Where to cut to minimise total area?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let x be the wire used for the square. Square side: x/4, area x^2
/16. Circle circumference: 100 - x, radius (100-x)/(2\pi), area (100 - x)^2/(4\pi).



Full solution

A(x) = x^2/16 + (100 - x)^2/(4\pi). A'(x) = x/8 - (100 - x)/(2\pi) = 0 \Rightarrow x/8 = (100 - x)/(2\pi)
\Rightarrow \pi x = 4(100 - x) \Rightarrow x = 400/(\pi + 4) \approx
56.0 cm.
So cut about 56 cm for the square, 44 cm for the circle.
(Check max vs min by A'': positive everywhere — this is indeed a minimum. Boundaries: x = 0 all-circle, x = 100 all-square — both give larger total areas.)



Problem 5
The maximum of a function on a closed interval [a, b] is either at a critical point in (a, b) or at an endpoint. Why must both be checked?


Full solution

Endpoints are not critical points (the derivative isn’t zero there in general — but the function might still hit its highest value at the boundary).
Algorithm for closed-interval optimisation: (1) find critical points where f' = 0 in (a, b). (2) Evaluate f at all critical points and at a, b. (3) Largest value = max; smallest = min.
EVT (Lesson 02) guarantees max and min exist for continuous f on [a, b].


Pierre de Fermat found maxima and minima around 1636 by a trick he called adequality — comparing f(x) with f(x + e), dividing by e, then “letting e vanish.” It was essentially the derivative-equals-zero test, three decades before Newton or Leibniz formalised calculus. Fermat never published a proof, and his method sat in private letters until Newton studied them. The modern slogan “set the derivative to zero” is, in a real sense, Fermat’s trick rebranded.


At every peak and every valley, the curve is — for one fleeting instant — perfectly flat. Optimisation is just hunting for those flat spots, then sorting which ones are summits and which are sinks.

title: 06 · Applications of Derivatives — Optimisation
text:
Name

A critical point of f is an x where f'(x) = 0 or f'(x) doesn’t exist.
Local extremum at x = c:

Local max: f(c) \ge f(x) for x near c.
Local min: f(c) \le f(x) for x near c.

Tests for classification at a critical point c (where f'(c) = 0):

First derivative test: sign change of f' at c.

f' > 0 before c, f' < 0 after → local max.
f' < 0 before, f' > 0 after → local min.
No sign change → neither (saddle/inflection point).

Second derivative test:

f''(c) < 0 → local max.
f''(c) > 0 → local min.
f''(c) = 0 → inconclusive; use first derivative test.


Optimisation strategy:

Identify the function to optimise.
Identify constraints; reduce to one variable.
Differentiate, set to zero, solve.
Verify max vs min (second derivative or endpoint check).
State the answer in original-problem terms.



Critical points: where f' = 0; Concave down (f'' < 0) → max; concave up (f'' > 0) → min; Always check endpoints on a closed interval.

title: 06 · Applications of Derivatives — Optimisation
text:
Apply

Worked example

A farmer fences a rectangular plot against a wall (so only 3 sides are fenced). Total fence: 60 m. Maximum area?

(T3 L02 P3 was a similar problem; here we redo it with calculus.)

Variables: width w, length \ell.
Constraint: \ell + 2w = 60 \Rightarrow \ell = 60 - 2w.
Objective: A = w \ell = w(60 - 2w) = 60 w - 2 w^2.
Derivative: A'(w) = 60 - 4w = 0 \Rightarrow w = 15.
Then \ell = 60 - 30 = 30. Area = 450 m².
Verify max: A''(w) = -4 < 0 — concave down → max.

15 m × 30 m, area 450 m².


Practice — Warm
W1. Critical points of f(x) = x^2 - 6x + 1?


Full solution

f'(x) = 2x - 6 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 3. (Min, since f'' = 2 > 0.)

W2. Local extrema of f(x) = x^3?


Full solution

f'(x) = 3 x^2 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 0. But f' \ge 0 everywhere — no sign change — so x = 0 is neither max nor min (an inflection point, the classic example).

W3. Max/min of f(x) = x^2 - 4 on [-3, 5]?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Critical: x = 0. Evaluate at -3, 0, 5.



Full solution

f(-3) = 5, f(0) = -4, f(5) = 21. Min: -4 at x = 0. Max: 21 at x = 5.



Practice — Working
P1. Find local extrema of f(x) = x^4 - 4 x^2.


Hint 1 — a nudge

f'(x) = 4 x^3 - 8 x = 4 x (x^2 - 2).



Full solution

Critical: x = 0, \pm \sqrt{2}. f''(x) = 12 x^2 - 8. f''(0) = -8 < 0 → local max f(0) = 0. f''(\pm \sqrt{2}) = 24 - 8 = 16 > 0 → local min f(\pm \sqrt{2})
= 4 - 8 = -4.
The “double-well” shape — a classic ML loss-landscape mental model.

P2. A 2-litre cylindrical can has volume \pi r^2 h = 2000 cm³. Minimise the surface area A = 2\pi r^2 + 2\pi r h over r.


Full solution

h = 2000/(\pi r^2). A = 2\pi r^2 + 2\pi r \cdot 2000/(\pi r^2) =
2\pi r^2 + 4000/r. A'(r) = 4\pi r - 4000/r^2 = 0 \Rightarrow r^3 = 1000/\pi \Rightarrow
r = (1000/\pi)^{1/3} \approx 6.83 cm.
Then h = 2000/(\pi r^2) \approx 13.65 cm = 2r. Optimal cans have h = 2r (height equals diameter). Most actual cans don’t — because of brand and ergonomic constraints.

P3. A revenue function: R(p) = -2p^2 + 100 p (revenue depends on price p). What price maximises revenue?


Full solution

R'(p) = -4p + 100 = 0 \Rightarrow p = 25. Max revenue: R(25) =
-1250 + 2500 = 1250.
Revenue maximisation by setting marginal revenue to zero is the microeconomic foundation of pricing. (Profit max would set marginal revenue = marginal cost.)



Practice — Stretch
S1. Gradient descent. ML training is “gradient descent on the loss landscape.” For a quadratic loss L(w) = (w - 5)^2, find the minimum by both calculus and by gradient descent (start at w_0 = 0, step size \eta = 0.1, update w_{n+1} = w_n - \eta L'(w_n)).


Full solution

Calculus: L'(w) = 2(w - 5) = 0 \Rightarrow w^* = 5. Gradient descent: L'(w) = 2(w - 5) = 2w - 10. - w_0 = 0, L' = -10, w_1 = 0 - 0.1 \cdot (-10) = 1. - w_1 = 1, L' = -8, w_2 = 1.8. - w_2 = 1.8, L' = -6.4, w_3 = 2.44. - w_3 = 2.44, L' = -5.12, w_4 = 2.952. - … converging to w^* = 5.
For non-quadratic losses (most ML problems), no closed form exists, so gradient descent (or its many variants) is the only path. The calculus you just did is the mathematical foundation of every ML training run.

S2. Newton’s method for finding roots of f(x) = 0: iteratively x_{n+1} = x_n - f(x_n)/f'(x_n). To find \sqrt{2}, take f(x) = x^2 - 2 and iterate from x_0 = 1.


Hint 1 — a nudge

f'(x) = 2x. x_{n+1} = x_n - (x_n^2 - 2)/(2 x_n) = (x_n + 2/x_n)/2.



Full solution


x_0 = 1. x_1 = (1 + 2)/2 = 1.5.
x_2 = (1.5 + 2/1.5)/2 = (1.5 + 1.333)/2 \approx 1.4167.
x_3 \approx (1.4167 + 1.4118)/2 \approx 1.4142.
x_4 \approx 1.41421356\ldots

After 4 iterations, accurate to 8 decimal places. Newton’s method converges quadratically — the number of correct digits roughly doubles per step.
This is one of the fastest root-finders known and the workhorse of numerical computing.



Common confusions

“Critical point = max or min.” Could also be neither (a saddle or inflection point — see W2).
“f'(c) = 0 guarantees a local extremum.” No — see x^3. The second derivative or sign-change test is needed.
“Endpoints don’t matter.” They do! On a closed interval, the global extremum can be at an endpoint even if no critical points are there.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A critical point — where f'(x) = 0 — is the only place a smooth function can hide a local max or min in the interior.
The second derivative test classifies it instantly: f'' < 0 peak, f'' > 0 valley, f''
= 0 inconclusive.
On a closed interval, always check the endpoints too — the global extremum can sit at the boundary even when no critical point is nearby.



Self-Assessment

Could you state the second-derivative test in your own words?
Without scrolling back, can you find the critical points of f(x)
= x^2 - 8x + 1?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: x = 4, a min.)

3 yes: ready for 07 · Related Rates.
2 yes: redo S1 (gradient descent) — the central computational ML idea.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P5.

title: 06 · Applications of Derivatives — Optimisation
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

04 · Differentiation Rules
T3 L02 — Parabolas — vertex of a parabola foreshadowed this.



What this unlocks

Tier 5 — Multivariable Calculus — partial derivatives optimise many-variable functions; gradients generalise the derivative.
Tier 6 — Optimisation Theory — convex optimisation, linear programming, Lagrange multipliers.



Where this matters

AI / Machine Learning. Gradient descent (S1) is the workhorse of training. Adam, momentum, RMSprop — all variations of the same core idea: descend the loss surface using the derivative as a direction.


Engineering & operations research. Minimum cost, minimum material, maximum strength, optimal scheduling — calculus optimisation underlies design across industries.


Economics. Profit maximisation, utility maximisation, cost minimisation — all formalised as derivative-zero conditions.

title: 07 · Related Rates and Linear Approximation
text:
Have you done 04 · Differentiation Rules?
Comfortable with the chain rule \dfrac{d}{dx}[f(g(x))] = f'(g(x)) g'(x)?
Comfortable with implicit-equation reasoning?

title: 07 · Related Rates and Linear Approximation
07 · Related Rates and Linear Approximation
text:
Wonder

As a balloon inflates, the radius grows at 1 cm/s. Is the area growing at the same constant rate?
The chain rule connects dA/dt to dr/dt via dA/dr. How?
Linear approximation: near x = 9, \sqrt{x} \approx 3 + (x - 9)/6. Why?

title: 07 · Related Rates and Linear Approximation
text:
Discover

Problem 1
A spherical balloon’s radius grows at 0.5 cm/s. How fast is the volume growing when r = 10?


Hint 1 — a nudge

V = \tfrac{4}{3} \pi r^3. Differentiate both sides with respect to t (treating r as r(t)).



Full solution

\dfrac{dV}{dt} = 4 \pi r^2 \cdot \dfrac{dr}{dt}.
At r = 10 and dr/dt = 0.5: \dfrac{dV}{dt} = 4 \pi \cdot 100 \cdot
0.5 = 200 \pi \approx 628.3 cm³/s.
The chain rule turns “rate of V with respect to r” times “rate of r with respect to t” into “rate of V with respect to t.”



Problem 2
A 5-m ladder leans against a wall. The base slides away at 0.3 m/s. When the base is 3 m from the wall, how fast is the top sliding down?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let x = distance from wall, y = height up wall. Pythagoras: x^2
+ y^2 = 25. Differentiate w.r.t. t.



Full solution

2x \dfrac{dx}{dt} + 2y \dfrac{dy}{dt} = 0 \Rightarrow \dfrac{dy}{dt}
= -\dfrac{x}{y} \dfrac{dx}{dt}.
At x = 3: y = \sqrt{25 - 9} = 4. So \dfrac{dy}{dt} = -\dfrac{3}{4}
\cdot 0.3 = -0.225 m/s.
Top slides down at 0.225 m/s. Negative because y decreases.
(Notice the ladder’s base speed is constant, but the top speed changes with position — and accelerates as the base nears the wall.)



Problem 3
Linear approximation. Near x = a, f(x) \approx f(a) + f'(a)(x -
a) — the tangent line at a.
Use this to approximate \sqrt{4.1} near a = 4.


Hint 1 — a nudge

f(x) = \sqrt{x}, f'(x) = 1/(2\sqrt{x}). At a = 4: f(4) = 2,
f'(4) = 1/4.



Full solution

\sqrt{4.1} \approx 2 + (1/4)(4.1 - 4) = 2 + 0.025 = 2.025.
(True value: 2.02485\ldots. Approximation error \approx 0.0001.)
Linear approximation = “use the tangent line as a stand-in for f near a.” Excellent for small departures from a known value.



Problem 4
Differentials. df = f'(x) dx — a “small change in f” given a “small change in x.”
If f(x) = x^2 and x changes from 5 to 5.01, estimate df.


Full solution

df = 2x \cdot dx = 2 \cdot 5 \cdot 0.01 = 0.1.
So f(5.01) \approx f(5) + 0.1 = 25.1. (True: 25.1001.)
Differentials are linear approximations dressed in infinitesimal notation — the language Leibniz preferred. They make sense rigorously as limits.



Problem 5
A cone-shaped tank has water depth h and radius (at water level) r
= h/3 (so r/h = 1/3). Water flows in at 2 m³/min. How fast is the water level rising when h = 6 m?


Hint 1 — a nudge

V = \tfrac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h = \tfrac{1}{3} \pi (h/3)^2 h = \pi h^3/27. Differentiate.



Full solution

\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{\pi h^2}{9} \dfrac{dh}{dt}.
At h = 6: \dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 36}{9} \dfrac{dh}{dt}
= 4\pi \dfrac{dh}{dt}.
Set equal to 2: 4 \pi \dfrac{dh}{dt} = 2 \Rightarrow \dfrac{dh}{dt} =
\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \approx 0.159 m/min.
Water rises at \approx 0.16 m/min when 6 m deep. As the cone gets wider higher up, the level rises more slowly for the same inflow rate.


Linear approximation is the oldest trick in numerical mathematics — Babylonian scribes were using something equivalent to \sqrt{a^2 + b} \approx a + b/(2a) on clay tablets by 1800 BCE to refine square roots. The same identity, dressed in modern notation as f(x) \approx f(a) + f'(a)(x - a), is the very first term of Brook Taylor’s series (1715). Three and a half millennia of mathematics rests on the same idea: when you can’t solve, replace the curve with its tangent and try again.


A function close to a known point looks just like its tangent line. That single sentence underwrites two enormous edifices — related rates (differentiate the constraint, then read off how fast everything moves) and linear approximation (use the tangent in place of the function). Both are the chain rule earning its keep.

title: 07 · Related Rates and Linear Approximation
text:
Name

Related rates strategy:

Sketch the situation; label variables.
Identify the constraint equation linking the variables.
Differentiate both sides w.r.t. time (chain rule everywhere).
Substitute known values and rates.
Solve for the unknown rate.

Linear approximation of f near a: L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a) \approx f(x) \quad \text{(for $x$ near $a$)}.
Differential notation: df = f'(x) dx. Used heavily in physics and engineering — the idea is “infinitesimal change.”
Linear approximation = first-order Taylor approximation. Higher- order Taylor series (Tier 4 / Tier 5+) extend this to better approximations.


Related rates: differentiate the constraint equation w.r.t. time; Linear approximation: tangent line as a stand-in near a known point; Differentials: df = f'(x) dx — infinitesimal language.

title: 07 · Related Rates and Linear Approximation
text:
Apply

Worked example

A 6-foot person walks at 4 ft/s away from a 20-foot lamppost. How fast is the tip of their shadow moving?

Variables: x = person’s distance from post, s = shadow length. Similar triangles: 20/6 = (x + s)/s \Rightarrow 20 s = 6(x + s)
\Rightarrow 14 s = 6 x \Rightarrow s = 3x/7.
Tip of shadow’s distance from post: x + s = x + 3x/7 = 10x/7.
\dfrac{d(x + s)}{dt} = \dfrac{10}{7} \dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{10}{7}
\cdot 4 = \dfrac{40}{7} \approx 5.71 ft/s.
Tip moves at about 5.71 ft/s. Notably faster than the person.


Practice — Warm
W1. A cube’s side grows at 1 cm/s. Rate of volume growth when side is 5 cm?


Full solution

V = s^3, dV/dt = 3 s^2 \cdot ds/dt = 75 cm³/s.

W2. Linearly approximate \sqrt{16.2}.


Full solution

\sqrt{16.2} \approx 4 + 0.2/(2 \cdot 4) = 4.025. (True: 4.0249\ldots.)

W3. f(x) = x^3. df when x = 2, dx = 0.1?


Full solution

df = 3 x^2 dx = 12 \cdot 0.1 = 1.2.



Practice — Working
P1. A circle’s radius grows at 2 mm/s. Rate of area growth when r = 10 mm?


Full solution

dA/dt = 2 \pi r \cdot dr/dt = 40 \pi \approx 125.66 mm²/s.

P2. Linearly approximate \sin(0.1) near a = 0.


Hint 1 — a nudge

f(0) = 0, f'(0) = \cos 0 = 1. So \sin x \approx x near 0.



Full solution

\sin(0.1) \approx 0.1. (True: 0.0998\ldots.) The “small angle approximation” \sin x \approx x is widely used in physics.

P3. A 13-foot ladder leans against a wall, base 5 ft out (top is \sqrt{169 - 25} = 12 ft up). The base slides at 2 ft/s away. Top’s speed?


Full solution

x^2 + y^2 = 169 \Rightarrow 2x \cdot dx/dt + 2y \cdot dy/dt = 0
\Rightarrow dy/dt = -(x/y) \cdot dx/dt = -(5/12)(2) = -5/6 ft/s (\approx -0.83).
The top slides down at \approx 0.83 ft/s.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Newton’s method as a linearisation argument. To find a root of f, replace f near current guess x_n by its linear approximation L(x) = f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(x - x_n). Find where L(x) =
0. Show this gives Newton’s update x_{n+1} = x_n - f(x_n)/f'(x_n).


Full solution

L(x) = 0 \Rightarrow f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(x - x_n) = 0 \Rightarrow x =
x_n - f(x_n)/f'(x_n).
So Newton’s method finds where the tangent line hits zero — and uses that as the next guess. Iterating shrinks the error quadratically (when convergent).
This insight — use the linear approximation in place of the function — pervades numerical analysis. Many algorithms are “locally linearise, solve, iterate.”

S2. Derivative-as-sensitivity. A neural network output \hat y depends on weight w via \hat y = f(w). The derivative \partial
\hat y / \partial w tells you the sensitivity of the output to a small weight change. Why is this critical for ML interpretation?


Full solution

Sensitivity → importance. Weights with large |\partial \hat y /
