Problem 3 — Trapezoid area
A trapezoid has parallel bases of lengths a and b, with perpendicular distance h between them. Show its area is \dfrac{a + b}{2} \cdot h.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Cut along a diagonal — two triangles, both with height h.



Full solution

Cut along diagonal BD. The two triangles \triangle ABD and \triangle BCD have: - \triangle ABD base a (along AB), altitude h, area \tfrac{1}{2} a h. - \triangle BCD base b (along CD), altitude h, area \tfrac{1}{2} b h. Sum: \tfrac{1}{2}(a + b) h = \dfrac{a + b}{2} \cdot h. QED. (Planimetry §223 — the trapezoid is the average of its bases times the height.)



Problem 4 — Heron’s formula
Show that the area of a triangle with sides a, b, c is 
A = \sqrt{s(s - a)(s - b)(s - c)},
\quad s = \frac{a + b + c}{2}.



Hint 1 — a nudge

Express h (altitude on side c) using Pythagoras after splitting c into two pieces by the foot of the altitude. Then use A =
\tfrac{1}{2} c h and simplify.



Full solution

Drop altitude h from C to AB (length c); let the foot H split AB into |AH| = m and |HB| = c - m. Pythagoras: h^2 =
b^2 - m^2 = a^2 - (c - m)^2. Solve for m: m = (b^2 - a^2 + c^2)
/ (2c). Then h^2 = b^2 - m^2. Algebraic simplification (the difference of two squares twice over): h^2 = \dfrac{(2bc)^2 - (b^2 + c^2 - a^2)^2}{(2c)^2}. The numerator factors: (2bc - (b^2 + c^2 - a^2))(2bc + (b^2 + c^2 - a^2))
= (a^2 - (b - c)^2)((b + c)^2 - a^2)
= (a - b + c)(a + b - c)(b + c - a)(a + b + c)
= 16 s (s - a)(s - b)(s - c) (after using s = (a+b+c)/2). So h = \dfrac{2\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}}{c}. Then A = \tfrac{1}{2} c h = \sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}. QED. (Planimetry §234 — Kiselev follows Heron’s original derivation.)



Problem 5 — Area of a regular n-gon
Show that a regular n-gon with circumradius R has area A_n = \tfrac{1}{2} n R^2 \sin\left(\tfrac{2\pi}{n}\right).


Full solution

The polygon decomposes into n congruent isoceles triangles, each with two sides R and included angle 2\pi / n at the centre. Each triangle area: \tfrac{1}{2} R^2 \sin(2\pi/n). Total: \tfrac{1}{2} n R^2 \sin(2\pi/n). QED. (Planimetry §238; also stated as the result of S1 in Ch 8.)



Problem 6 — Area of a circle
Show that the area of a circle of radius R is \pi R^2.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Take the limit of the regular n-gon area as n \to \infty. \sin(2\pi/n) / (2\pi/n) \to 1.



Full solution

By Problem 5, A_n = \tfrac{1}{2} n R^2 \sin(2\pi/n) = \pi R^2 \cdot
\dfrac{\sin(2\pi/n)}{2\pi/n} \to \pi R^2 \cdot 1 = \pi R^2 as n \to \infty. The inscribed regular n-gon area is sandwiched between the inscribed and circumscribed polygon areas (sandwich argument from Ch 8 Pr 7), and both bracket \pi R^2. Circle area = \pi R^2. (Planimetry §241 — Eudoxus’ method of exhaustion in the school-friendly Kiselev form.)


Heron of Alexandria (1st century CE) gave the formula in Problem 4 in his Metrica — a pragmatic surveyor’s manual that also describes a steam-powered toy and a coin-operated holy-water dispenser. The formula’s striking feature: it computes area from the sides alone, without ever measuring an angle or an altitude. Greek surveyors using nothing but knotted rope could find a triangle’s area to high precision. Heron’s derivation is the one Kiselev follows in Problem 4 — difference of two squares, twice over.



Problem 7 — Areas of similar figures
Two similar figures (any shape) with linear ratio k have areas in ratio k^2.


Full solution

We proved this for triangles in Ch 7 Problem 5. For any polygon, triangulate; each triangle has area scaling by k^2, so the sum does. For any region (curved boundary), approximate by a sequence of polygons; the ratio is k^2 for each, hence in the limit too. QED. (Planimetry §242 — generalisation of the triangle case to all plane figures.)


Every area formula in the plane reduces to rectangle = base × height. Slide it to a parallelogram (shears preserve area). Cut it in half for a triangle. Average the bases for a trapezoid. Triangulate any polygon. Take the regular-n-gon limit for the circle. One axiom, one cascade — Kiselev’s whole §§216–245 in a single sentence.

title: Ch 9 — Areas
text:
Name

Area formulas.







Figure
Formula




Rectangle, base b, height h
b h (working axiom)


Parallelogram, base b, height h
b h


Triangle, base b, altitude h
\tfrac{1}{2} b h


Trapezoid, parallel bases a, b, height h
\tfrac{a + b}{2} h


Triangle, sides a, b, c (Heron)
\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}, s = \tfrac{a+b+c}{2}


Triangle, two sides a, b, included angle C
\tfrac{1}{2} a b \sin C


Triangle, inradius r, semi-perimeter s
r s


Regular n-gon, circumradius R
\tfrac{1}{2} n R^2 \sin(2\pi/n)


Regular n-gon, side s_n, apothem \rho
\tfrac{1}{2} (n s_n) \rho = \tfrac{1}{2} perim · apothem


Circle, radius R
\pi R^2


Sector of angle \theta (radians) in circle R
\tfrac{1}{2} R^2 \theta



Theorem (similarity scaling). Similar plane figures with linear ratio k have areas in ratio k^2.

A verse:

Rectangle base times height — that is the seed. Slide to a parallelogram, area unchanged — that’s all you need. Half a parallelogram is a triangle — half base times height. Average the bases times height — the trapezoid is light. And in the limit of n-gons, the circle’s area becomes π r squared.

title: Ch 9 — Areas
text:
Apply

Worked example

A trapezoid has parallel bases 5 and 11, and the perpendicular distance between them is 4. Find its area.

A = \dfrac{5 + 11}{2} \cdot 4 = 8 \cdot 4 = 32. Area = 32.


Practice — Warm
W1. A triangle has base 8 and altitude 5. Find the area.


Full solution

\tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 8 \cdot 5 = 20.

W2. A circle has radius 7. Find the area.


Full solution

\pi \cdot 49 = 49\pi \approx 153.94.

W3. A rhombus has diagonals 6 and 8. Find the area.


Hint 1 — a nudge

The diagonals of a rhombus are perpendicular and bisect each other. The rhombus is four right triangles each with legs 3 and 4.



Full solution

Half-diagonals 3 and 4. Each of the four triangles has area \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 3 \cdot 4 = 6. Total = 24. Or: rhombus area = \tfrac{1}{2} d_1 d_2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 6 \cdot 8 = 24.



Practice — Working
P1. A triangle has sides 13, 14, 15. Find its area.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Heron’s formula. s = 21.



Full solution

s = 21. A = \sqrt{21 \cdot 8 \cdot 7 \cdot 6} = \sqrt{7056} =
84. (A famous integer-area triangle.)

P2. A circle is inscribed in a square of side 10. A second circle is circumscribed about the same square. Find the difference between the two circles’ areas.


Full solution

Inscribed circle: radius 5, area 25\pi. Circumscribed: radius 5\sqrt{2}, area 50\pi. Difference: 25\pi.

P3. A regular hexagon has side length 6. Find its area.


Hint 1 — a nudge

A regular hexagon decomposes into 6 equilateral triangles of side s.



Full solution

Each equilateral triangle of side 6 has area \dfrac{\sqrt 3}{4}
\cdot 36 = 9\sqrt 3. Total: 6 \cdot 9\sqrt 3 = 54\sqrt 3 \approx
93.53.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Show that for any triangle, A = rs where r is the inradius and s the semi-perimeter — and derive Heron’s formula by combining A = rs with A = \tfrac{1}{2} a b \sin C.


Full solution

A = rs proved as Ch 7 K3. Combining with Heron requires the trigonometric identity \sin C = 2 \sin(C/2) \cos(C/2) and half-angle formulas in the triangle (using the law of cosines). The two identities yield the same expression; the unification is A^2 = s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c). (See Planimetry §245 for Kiselev’s derivation.)

S2. A circle of radius r is inscribed in a sector of angle \theta in a larger circle of radius R. Show that r = \dfrac{R \sin(\theta/2)}{1 + \sin(\theta/2)}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

The inscribed circle is tangent to both radii of the sector and to the arc. Set up an equation using the distance from the centre of the inscribed circle to the centre of the sector (along the angle bisector).



Full solution

Let O be the centre of the big circle, C the centre of the inscribed circle. C lies on the angle bisector at distance |OC| = R - r from O (so the inscribed circle is tangent to the arc). The perpendicular distance from C to either radius of the sector is r — so |OC| \sin(\theta/2) = r. Combining: (R - r) \sin(\theta/2) = r, hence r = \dfrac{R \sin(\theta/2)}
{1 + \sin(\theta/2)}. QED.



Kiselev-style problems
Three problems in Kiselev’s classical style, adapted from Givental, Planimetry, §§220–245.
K1. In \triangle ABC, points D, E, F are taken on sides BC, CA, AB respectively, with |BD|/|BC| = |CE|/|CA| = |AF|/|AB|
= 1/3. Show that the area of \triangle DEF is \tfrac{1}{3} of \triangle ABC.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Each of the three “corner” triangles \triangle ADF, \triangle
BEF, \triangle CDE has area \tfrac{2}{9} of the whole. Sum their areas and subtract from the whole.



Full solution

\triangle ADF: |AF|/|AB| = 1/3 and the foot D divides BC at 2/3 from B, so the cevian AD has length related to the opposite-side configuration. Each “corner” triangle has area \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot \tfrac{2}{3} = \tfrac{2}{9} of the whole (using the side-product formula for triangle area inside a triangle). Three corners: \tfrac{6}{9} = \tfrac{2}{3}. Inner triangle: 1 - \tfrac{2}{3} = \tfrac{1}{3}. QED. (Planimetry §236 — “the inner triangle of one-third division”. Kiselev’s elegant result; the general case is Routh’s theorem.)

K2. A circle of radius r is inscribed in a right triangle with legs a, b and hypotenuse c. Show that r = \dfrac{a + b - c}{2}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Use A = rs. Right-triangle area: A = \tfrac{1}{2} a b. Semi-perimeter: s = (a + b + c)/2.



Full solution

A = \tfrac{1}{2} ab and A = rs = r \cdot (a + b + c)/2. So ab = r(a + b + c), i.e. r = \dfrac{ab}{a + b + c}. Multiply top and bottom by (a + b - c): r = \dfrac{ab(a + b - c)}
{(a + b + c)(a + b - c)} = \dfrac{ab(a + b - c)}{(a + b)^2 - c^2}. By Pythagoras, a^2 + b^2 = c^2, so (a + b)^2 - c^2 = 2ab. Hence r = \dfrac{a + b - c}{2}. QED. (Planimetry §244 — the right-triangle inradius formula.)

K3. A circle is inscribed in a regular hexagon of side a, and another circle is circumscribed about the hexagon. Find the ratio of the two circles’ areas.


Hint 1 — a nudge

For a regular hexagon: circumradius = a, apothem = a \sqrt 3 /
2. The circles have these radii.



Full solution

Inscribed circle (touching the sides) has radius \rho = a \sqrt 3
/ 2. Circumscribed circle (through vertices) has radius R = a. Areas: \pi \rho^2 = \tfrac{3}{4} \pi a^2 and \pi R^2 = \pi a^2. Ratio: \dfrac{\pi \rho^2}{\pi R^2} = \tfrac{3}{4}. The inscribed circle is three-fourths the area of the circumscribed. (Planimetry §241 exercise.)







Source pointer. Adapted from Givental, Kiselev’s Geometry, Book I — Planimetry, §§216–245 (Sumizdat 2006). Free PDF on cimat.mx. K1 generalises to Routh’s theorem; Kiselev’s §236 gives only the 1/3 case but explicitly notes the generalisation.





Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this chapter, remember these:

The parallelogram area b h comes from the rectangle by a cut-and-slide — a shear preserves area.
The triangle is half a parallelogram on the same base and height; the trapezoid is the average of its bases times its height — every polygon area is built from these.
The circle’s area \pi r^2 is the limit of regular-n-gon areas — Eudoxus’ method of exhaustion, the ancestor of integral calculus.



Common confusions

“Area scales linearly with side length.” It does not. Area scales by the square of the linear ratio. A triangle with doubled sides has quadrupled area. This is the source of the square-cube law in biology and engineering.
“Heron’s formula needs a right angle.” It does not — Heron’s formula works for any triangle whose three side lengths form a valid triangle (triangle inequality). The Pythagorean special case is just one application.
“\pi r^2 is the formula for circumference.” No — circumference is 2 \pi r. Area is \pi r^2. The two are related but distinct.



Self-Assessment

Could you derive the parallelogram-area formula (Problem 1) by the cut-and-slide argument on a fresh sheet?
Did Heron’s formula (Problem 4) feel like reachable algebra, or like memorisation? Re-do the difference-of-squares step.
Can you compute the area of a regular hexagon of side s in one mental step, and the area of an equilateral triangle of side s likewise?


3 yes: You have completed Kiselev Planimetry in Russian-classical style. Continue to Stereometry (kg2) or return to a synthesis tier for breadth.
2 yes: redo the Heron derivation — it is the chapter’s algebraic centrepiece.
0–1 yes: Problems 1, 2, 3 form the area chain; re-walk them.

title: Ch 9 — Areas
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

kg1.similarity — area-scales-by-k^2 used everywhere; also the triangle-area-by-altitude.
kg1.regular-polygons — the limit definition of \pi via n-gon perimeters/areas.



What this unlocks

kg2.cylinder-cone — surface and volume formulas in 3D inherit the inscribed-polygon- limit logic from this chapter.
t4.definite-integral — the Riemann integral generalises the polygon-limit-equals-region argument used in Problem 6.
t6.fourier-series — Parseval’s identity is the area-under-a-function version of the Pythagorean theorem in function space.



Kiselev chapter alignment

Geometry, Book I — Chapter 9: Areas (§§216–245). Givental translation, Sumizdat 2006. Free PDF on cimat.mx.
Sister synthesis lessons: t1.area-perimeter-volume (the Touch of area as counting unit squares); cbse9.herons-formula (board-exam Heron problems); cbse10.areas-related-to-circles (sectors and segments).
Read alongside the source PDF for Kiselev’s full Heron derivation (§234) and the area-of-a-circle limit proof (§241).



Where this matters

Game development. Polygon area is computed every frame for collision response, light maps, and texture atlasing. The shoelace formula (a Cartesian-coordinate version of Heron) is the runtime workhorse. Kiselev’s synthetic argument (cut-and-slide) is the geometric why; the shoelace is the algorithmic how.


AI / ML. Image classification loss functions are typically computed as integrals over the prediction landscape — areas in high-dimensional space. The intuition that area is “what fits inside” lives on; the formulas generalise to measure theory (t7.measure-theory).


Research. The isoperimetric inequality — among all plane figures of a given perimeter, the circle has the largest area — is the gateway to a vast theory in calculus of variations and mathematical physics. Kiselev’s chapter sets up the area language in which it can be stated.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones: Kiselev (the §§216–245 ordering); Heron of Alexandria (the formula); Eudoxus (the limit argument for the circle area).
The cut-and-slide proof of parallelogram area (Problem 1) is the cleanest in the literature — and it generalises to equal areas under shears, the seed of integral calculus’s change-of-variables.
What this lesson does not do: prove the isoperimetric inequality. That belongs to a separate undergraduate-level treatment.
Kiselev Planimetry — track complete. Nine chapters, all aligned to Givental’s English translation. The natural next step is Stereometry (kg2) for the three-dimensional version, or Algebra (ka1, ka2) for the parallel non-geometry track.

title: Ch 2 — The Triangle (Congruence)
text:
Can you state and use the vertical-angles-equal theorem? See kg1.lines-and-angles.
Can you describe what a triangle is — three vertices, three sides, three angles?
Are you comfortable measuring an angle and a length?
Do you accept that two figures are the same shape and size — that is, congruent — if you can lay one on top of the other?

If three yeses, proceed.

title: Ch 2 — The Triangle (Congruence)
Ch 2 — The Triangle (Congruence)
text:
Wonder

Three lengths fix a triangle. Two lengths and the angle between them — does that fix a triangle too?
Two angles and the side between them — does that fix a triangle?
Two triangles match perfectly when laid one over the other. What are the minimum pieces of information you’d need to verify they match without lifting the paper?
A triangle has two equal sides. Must it have two equal angles? Why?







TipWhat you might notice






Yes — Side-Angle-Side (SAS). (2) Yes — Angle-Side-Angle (ASA). (3) Three pieces — and they must be of the right kinds. SSS, SAS, ASA work; SSA does not (the ambiguous case). (4) Yes, and the proof is one of the cleanest in Kiselev — the isosceles triangle theorem.

title: Ch 2 — The Triangle (Congruence)
text:
Discover

Problem 1 — The triangle inequality
For any triangle with sides a, b, c, show that a + b > c.


Hint 1 — a nudge

The straight path from one vertex to another is the shortest one (an axiom). Compare it to the path that goes via a third vertex.



Full solution

Let the triangle be ABC with |BC| = a, |CA| = b, |AB| = c. The straight segment from A to B has length c. The path A \to C
\to B has length b + a. By the straight-line shortest axiom, c < a + b. By symmetry, the same applies to the other two sides. (Planimetry §39.)



Problem 2 — Side-Side-Side congruence (SSS)
Two triangles ABC and A'B'C' have |AB| = |A'B'|, |BC| =
|B'C'|, |CA| = |C'A'|. Show that the triangles are congruent — i.e., a rigid motion takes one onto the other.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Lay \triangle A'B'C' on \triangle ABC so that A' matches A and B' matches B. Argue that C' must land on C.



Full solution

Place A' \to A and B' \to B (possible because |A'B'| = |AB|). Now C' must lie at distance |A'C'| = |AC| from A and at distance |B'C'| = |BC| from B. There are at most two such points (the two intersections of two circles), one on each side of line AB. If C' lands on the same side as C, then C' = C — done. If on the opposite side, flip \triangle A'B'C' across AB and now C' lands on C. Either way, a rigid motion (possibly including a flip) takes one triangle onto the other. Triangles are congruent. QED. (Planimetry §44.)



Problem 3 — Side-Angle-Side congruence (SAS)
Two triangles have two pairs of equal sides and the included angle equal. Show they are congruent.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Lay them so the equal angle and one of its sides match. Where must the third vertex go?



Full solution

Suppose |AB| = |A'B'|, \angle B = \angle B', |BC| = |B'C'|. Lay B' \to B, A' \to A, and aligned-side onto aligned-side. The angle at B matches the angle at B', so the direction from B to C matches the direction from B' to C'. Since the lengths |BC| and |B'C'| are equal, C' \to C. The third vertices coincide. Triangles congruent. QED. (Planimetry §45.)



Problem 4 — Angle-Side-Angle congruence (ASA)
Two triangles have two pairs of equal angles and the included side equal. Show they are congruent.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Lay the equal sides on top. The two rays from each end go off at the matching angles — where must the third vertex be?



Full solution

Suppose |BC| = |B'C'|, \angle B = \angle B', \angle C = \angle C'. Lay B \to B', C \to C' aligned. The ray from B at angle \angle B matches the ray from B' at angle \angle B' — they are the same ray. Similarly for the rays from C and C'. The two rays meet at one point — the apex A, which equals A'. Congruent. (Planimetry §47.)



Problem 5 — Isosceles triangle theorem
Let \triangle ABC have |AB| = |AC|. Show that \angle B =
\angle C.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Compare \triangle ABC with itself, but with vertices renamed A \to
A, B \to C, C \to B — i.e., flipped. Use SSS or SAS.



Full solution

Compare \triangle ABC with \triangle ACB (same triangle, vertices renamed). Sides: |AB| = |AC| (given), |AC| = |AB| (given), |BC|
= |CB| (same segment). By SSS the two triangles are congruent. Hence the corresponding angles are equal — in particular, \angle B = \angle C. QED. (Planimetry §51 — Pappus’ proof, recovered by Kiselev.)
Three lines, no auxiliary construction. The isosceles triangle is its own mirror.



Problem 6 — Converse: equal angles ⇒ equal sides
Let \triangle ABC have \angle B = \angle C. Show that |AB| =
|AC|.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Same trick — compare \triangle ABC with \triangle ACB, but use ASA this time.



Full solution

Compare \triangle ABC with \triangle ACB. Angles: \angle B =
\angle C (given), \angle C = \angle B (given), and |BC| = |CB| (same segment). By ASA, the two triangles are congruent. Hence |AB| = |AC| (corresponding sides). QED.



Problem 7 — Exterior-angle theorem (weak form)
In \triangle ABC, extend side BC past C to a ray. The angle formed outside the triangle at C is the exterior angle at C. Show that this exterior angle is strictly greater than either remote interior angle (\angle A or \angle B).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let M be the midpoint of AC. Extend BM past M to a point D with |BM| = |MD|. Compare \triangle ABM and \triangle CDM using SAS.



Full solution

Let M be the midpoint of AC. Extend BM to D with |MD| =
|BM|. Then \triangle ABM \cong \triangle CDM by SAS (|AM| = |MC| given by midpoint; \angle AMB = \angle CMD vertical angles; |MB| = |MD| by construction). Hence \angle BAM = \angle DCM, i.e. \angle A = \angle DCM. But \angle DCM is strictly inside the exterior angle at C (since D is inside the angle), so the exterior angle at C is strictly larger than \angle A. The same argument with the midpoint of AB (rather than AC) handles \angle B. QED. (Planimetry §50.)


Euclid Book I, propositions 4, 8, 26. The three congruence criteria you just proved have been the spine of Western geometry for 2,300 years. Euclid stated SAS as Proposition 4 of Book I of the Elements (~300 BCE), SSS as Proposition 8, and ASA as Proposition 26 — the three pillars on which every later theorem in his thirteen books rests. Euclid’s proof of SAS used a method of superposition (lay one triangle on top of the other) that mathematicians have argued about ever since: is “lay one figure on another” really an axiom, a theorem, or hand-waving? Hilbert in 1899 simply elevated SAS to an axiom, dispensing with the debate. The flip step in our proof of SSS (Problem 2) is borrowed straight from Euclid.


Three pieces are enough to fix a triangle. Three sides (SSS), two sides + included angle (SAS), or two angles + included side (ASA) — any of these three trios determines the triangle completely. The whole notion of congruence in plane geometry boils down to: do you have one of the three good trios?

title: Ch 2 — The Triangle (Congruence)
text:
Name

Triangle. A figure formed by three line segments connecting three non-collinear points (the vertices). Three sides, three angles; three angles sum to 180° (proved next chapter — Parallels — but already a familiar fact).
Congruence. Two triangles are congruent if a rigid motion of the plane (translation + rotation, possibly with a flip) takes one onto the other. Equivalently: corresponding sides and corresponding angles are equal.
The three congruence theorems.

SSS: three pairs of equal sides ⇒ congruent.
SAS: two pairs of equal sides + included angle ⇒ congruent.
ASA: two pairs of equal angles + included side ⇒ congruent.

Isosceles triangle theorem (Planimetry §51). A triangle with two equal sides has the angles opposite them equal. Conversely: a triangle with two equal angles has the sides opposite them equal.
Exterior-angle theorem, weak form (§50). An exterior angle of a triangle is strictly greater than either remote interior angle.

A verse:

Three sides, three angles — but only three pieces are needed. Side-side-side, side-angle-side, angle-side-angle have all succeeded. Equal sides force equal angles, equal angles force equal sides — And no exterior angle ever shrinks below either remote that hides.

title: Ch 2 — The Triangle (Congruence)
text:
Apply

Worked example

In \triangle ABC, |AB| = |AC| and \angle A = 40°. Find \angle B and \angle C.

By isosceles (|AB| = |AC|), \angle B = \angle C. Triangle angle sum gives \angle B + \angle C = 180° - 40° = 140°. Each is 70°.
\angle B = \angle C = 70°.


Practice — Warm
W1. Two triangles have all three sides 5, 6, 8. Are they congruent?


Full solution

Yes — by SSS.

W2. \triangle ABC has |AB| = |AC|. If \angle B = 65°, find \angle A and \angle C.


Full solution

\angle C = \angle B = 65° (isosceles). \angle A = 180° - 130° =
50°.

W3. Two triangles have two equal sides 5 and 7 and the included angle of 50° in each. Are they congruent?


Full solution

Yes — by SAS.



Practice — Working
P1. \triangle ABC is equilateral — all three sides equal. Find each angle.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Apply isosceles twice.



Full solution

|AB| = |AC| \Rightarrow \angle B = \angle C. |BA| = |BC|
\Rightarrow \angle A = \angle C. So \angle A = \angle B =
\angle C. Sum = 180° \Rightarrow each is 60°.

P2. Two triangles have |AB| = 5, |BC| = 7, \angle A = 40° in both. Are they necessarily congruent?


Hint 1 — a nudge

This is “Side-Side-Angle” — the ambiguous case. Try drawing it.



Full solution

No. SSA does not guarantee congruence; sometimes two distinct triangles fit (the famous ambiguous case in trigonometry). Three of the four canonical “congruence shortcuts” (SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS) work; SSA does not. (Kiselev addresses this explicitly in §49.)

P3. \triangle ABC has |AB| = |AC|. Let D be the foot of the perpendicular from A to BC. Show that |BD| = |DC|.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Compare \triangle ABD and \triangle ACD. AD is shared, |AB| =
|AC| given, \angle ADB = \angle ADC = 90°.



Full solution

\triangle ABD and \triangle ACD share AD. |AB| = |AC| (given). \angle ADB = \angle ADC = 90°. This is Side-Angle-Side iff the angle is included between the two equal sides — but here the angle is at D, not between |AD| and the equal sides. Use right-triangle Pythagoras: |BD|^2 = |AB|^2 - |AD|^2 =
|AC|^2 - |AD|^2 = |DC|^2, so |BD| = |DC|. (Or: by the isosceles theorem, \angle B = \angle C; then ASA gives \triangle ABD \cong
\triangle ACD, so |BD| = |DC|.) The altitude from the apex of an isosceles triangle bisects the base.



Practice — Stretch
S1. In \triangle ABC, the bisector of \angle A meets BC at D. Show that if |AB| = |AC|, then D is the midpoint of BC.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Compare \triangle ABD and \triangle ACD using SAS at vertex A.



Full solution

\triangle ABD and \triangle ACD: |AB| = |AC| given, \angle BAD = \angle CAD (bisector), AD shared. By SAS, congruent. So |BD| = |DC|. The angle bisector from the apex of an isosceles triangle bisects the base. (Companion to P3.)

S2. Show that if two altitudes of a triangle are equal in length, then the triangle is isosceles.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Suppose the altitude from B and the altitude from C are equal. Use right triangles formed by these altitudes and a third side.



Full solution

Drop altitudes BE \perp AC and CF \perp AB with |BE| = |CF|. The right triangles \triangle BEC and \triangle CFB share side BC, and have equal legs |BE| = |CF|. By the right-triangle hypotenuse-leg criterion (a corollary of SSS), they are congruent. So \angle BCE = \angle CBF, i.e. \angle C = \angle B. By the isosceles converse, |AB| = |AC|. Triangle isosceles. (A nice Kiselev exercise from §53.)



Kiselev-style problems
Three problems in Kiselev’s classical style, adapted from Givental, Planimetry, §§44–55.
K1. In a triangle, the median from a vertex is also the perpendicular from that vertex. Show the triangle is isosceles.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let AD be the median (so |BD| = |DC|) and also the perpendicular (AD \perp BC). Compare \triangle ABD and \triangle ACD.



Full solution

\triangle ABD and \triangle ACD: |BD| = |DC| (median), AD shared, \angle ADB = \angle ADC = 90°. By SAS (the angle is included between the two equal sides), congruent. So |AB| = |AC| — triangle isosceles. (Planimetry §53.)

K2. Two segments AB and CD bisect each other at a point O (so |AO| = |OB| and |CO| = |OD|). Show that |AC| = |BD| and AC \parallel BD.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\triangle AOC and \triangle BOD — find the SAS pair.



Full solution

\triangle AOC and \triangle BOD: |AO| = |OB|, |CO| = |OD|, \angle AOC = \angle BOD (vertical angles). By SAS, congruent. Hence |AC| = |BD|, and corresponding angles \angle OAC = \angle
OBD, which are alternate-interior angles for the transversal AB cutting lines AC and BD. By the converse alternate-interior- angles theorem (we’ll prove it formally in Ch 4), AC \parallel BD. QED. (Planimetry §47, exercise.)

K3. A triangle ABC has a point D on side BC with |AD| =
|AB|. Compare \angle ADC to \angle ABC.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\triangle ABD is isosceles with |AB| = |AD| — what does that say about its angles?



Full solution

\triangle ABD isosceles, so \angle ABD = \angle ADB. Now \angle ADC is the exterior angle of \triangle ABD at D, so \angle ADC = 180° - \angle ADB = 180° - \angle ABD. Hence \angle ADC is the supplement of \angle ABC. (Planimetry §52, typical Kiselev exterior-angle exercise.)







Source pointer. Adapted from Givental, Kiselev’s Geometry, Book I — Planimetry, §§44–55 (Sumizdat 2006). Free PDF on cimat.mx. K1 and K2 follow Kiselev’s own §47 and §53 exercise patterns closely; statements have been re-worded.





Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

SSS, SAS, ASA — three trios that fix a triangle. Memorise the three included pieces and you can decide congruence at sight; SSA does not make the list.
Equal sides ⇔ equal opposite angles. The isosceles theorem and its converse are one idea seen from two sides — and Pappus’s three-line proof is the cleanest in school geometry.
Congruence is rigid motion. Two triangles are congruent exactly when one can be slid, rotated, and (if needed) flipped onto the other. The three criteria are checks that this motion exists.



Common confusions

“SSA is a congruence theorem.” It is not. The ambiguous case — two non-included sides and a non-included angle — admits two non-congruent triangles in general. (See P2.) Only SSS, SAS, ASA, and the right-triangle hypotenuse-leg case are reliable.
“Isosceles requires an axis of symmetry.” The theorem says equal sides ⇒ equal angles, but does not require you to find the axis. Pappus’ proof (Problem 5) doesn’t construct an axis at all.
“Congruence is the same as similarity.” It is not. Congruent = same shape and size. Similar = same shape, possibly different size — which we’ll cover in Ch 7. Congruence is similarity with scale factor 1.



Self-Assessment

Could you state and prove the SSS theorem from scratch, including the flip step?
Did the isosceles-theorem proof (Problem 5) feel like one move, or did you need scaffolding?
Can you take a problem with an unmarked figure and decide whether you’d reach for SSS, SAS, or ASA — and identify the three pieces?


3 yes: move on to Perpendiculars (Ch 3).
2 yes: redo Problem 5 — the Pappus argument is the cleanest proof in the book and worth memorising.
0–1 yes: the Discover sequence is the chapter; replay it with paper and pencil.

title: Ch 2 — The Triangle (Congruence)
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

kg1.lines-and-angles — segments, angles, and the vertical-angles theorem (used in K2).



What this unlocks

kg1.perpendiculars (Ch 3, planned) — congruence applied to right triangles; the perpendicular as the unique shortest segment from a point to a line.
kg1.parallels — uses the exterior-angle theorem (Problem 7) in the converse alternate- interior proof.
kg1.quadrilaterals (Ch 5, planned) — diagonal-and-triangle decompositions reduce quadrilateral theorems to triangle ones.



Kiselev chapter alignment

Geometry, Book I — Chapter 2: The triangle (§§35–55). Givental translation, Sumizdat 2006. Free PDF on cimat.mx.
Sister synthesis lessons: cbse9.triangles — same congruence theorems with board-exam practice; and cbse10.triangles-similarity — pushes into similarity, the next step.
Read alongside the source PDF for Kiselev’s full exterior- angle proof in §50, which we sketched here.



Where this matters

Game development. Triangle congruence is the foundation of bone rigging in 3D animation — when a limb deforms, the polygons on its surface must remain congruent up to rigid motion to avoid visible distortion. The SSS / SAS / ASA invariants are the correctness conditions for any deformation model.


Research. The isosceles-triangle theorem (and Pappus’ proof) generalises to any symmetric configuration: if a structure is invariant under a transformation, the orbits of points under that transformation force equality between corresponding parts. This is the seed of Klein’s Erlangen Programme — geometry as the study of group actions.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones: Pappus (the isosceles proof — three lines, no auxiliary construction); Kiselev (the §44–55 ordering and the weak-exterior-angle proof of §50); Hadamard (the SSS argument via flip).
The exterior-angle theorem (Problem 7) is famously delicate; Kiselev’s midpoint-extend-double construction is the cleanest in the literature.
What this lesson does not do: prove the strong exterior- angle theorem (= sum of two remote interior angles), which is the triangle-angle-sum + arithmetic. That belongs in Ch 4 once the parallel postulate is in play.

title: Ch 5 — Quadrilaterals
text:
Can you state and use the alternate-interior-angles theorem from kg1.parallels?
Are SSS, SAS, ASA congruence reflexes for you? See kg1.triangle-congruence.
Do you accept that the angles of any triangle sum to 180°?
Can you split a four-sided figure into two triangles by drawing one diagonal?

If three yeses, proceed.

title: Ch 5 — Quadrilaterals
Ch 5 — Quadrilaterals
text:
Wonder

A parallelogram has both pairs of opposite sides parallel. Must the opposite sides be equal in length? Why?
A rectangle has four right angles. Could you have three right angles and the fourth different? Try to draw one.
A rhombus has four equal sides. Are its diagonals related to the sides — perpendicular, equal, bisecting?
A trapezoid has exactly one pair of parallel sides. The two non-parallel sides — when do they have equal length, making the trapezoid isosceles?







TipWhat you might notice






Yes — opposite sides of a parallelogram are equal (proved below). (2) Three right angles forces 360° - 270° = 90° for the fourth, so all four are right — every “3-right-angled quadrilateral” is a rectangle. (3) The diagonals of a rhombus bisect each other and are perpendicular (and they bisect the angles at the vertices too). (4) An isosceles trapezoid has equal non-parallel sides and equal base-angles.

title: Ch 5 — Quadrilaterals
text:
Discover

Problem 1 — Opposite sides of a parallelogram are equal
Let ABCD be a parallelogram with AB \parallel CD and AD
\parallel BC. Show |AB| = |CD| and |AD| = |BC|.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Draw diagonal AC. Compare \triangle ABC and \triangle CDA.



Full solution

Diagonal AC. In \triangle ABC and \triangle CDA: |AC| = |CA| shared; \angle BAC = \angle DCA (alternate-interior angles for AB \parallel CD with transversal AC); \angle BCA =
\angle DAC (alternate-interior for AD \parallel BC with same transversal). By ASA, \triangle ABC \cong \triangle CDA. Hence |AB| = |CD| and |BC| = |AD|. QED. (Planimetry §99.)



Problem 2 — Diagonals of a parallelogram bisect each other
In parallelogram ABCD, the diagonals AC and BD meet at O. Show |AO| = |OC| and |BO| = |OD|.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\triangle AOB and \triangle COD — use ASA with the alternate-interior angles.



Full solution

\triangle AOB and \triangle COD: |AB| = |CD| (Problem 1); \angle OAB = \angle OCD (alternate-interior for AB \parallel
CD); \angle OBA = \angle ODC (same reason). By ASA, congruent. So |AO| = |OC| and |BO| = |OD|. QED. (Planimetry §100.)



Problem 3 — Converse: bisecting diagonals ⇒ parallelogram
Suppose ABCD is a quadrilateral whose diagonals bisect each other at O. Show ABCD is a parallelogram.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\triangle AOB and \triangle COD are congruent by SAS using the vertical angle at O.



Full solution

|AO| = |OC|, |BO| = |OD| (given), \angle AOB = \angle COD (vertical). By SAS, \triangle AOB \cong \triangle COD. Hence |AB| = |CD| and \angle OAB = \angle OCD — these are alternate- interior angles for transversal AC cutting lines AB and CD, which are therefore parallel. Similarly AD \parallel BC. A parallelogram. (Planimetry §101.)



Problem 4 — Rectangle ⇔ parallelogram with one right angle
Show that a parallelogram with one right angle is a rectangle (all four angles right).


Full solution

Opposite angles in a parallelogram are equal (corollary of Problem 1; or directly from alternate-interior angles). Adjacent angles sum to 180° (co-interior). So one 90° angle forces the others: the opposite is 90°, and each adjacent is 180° - 90° = 90°. All four are 90°. (Planimetry §103.)



Problem 5 — Rhombus diagonals are perpendicular bisectors
In a rhombus ABCD (parallelogram with all sides equal), the diagonals AC and BD meet at O. Show AC \perp BD and that each diagonal bisects the angles at its endpoints.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\triangle AOB \cong \triangle COB via SSS — what does that say about the angles at O and at B?



Full solution

By Problem 2, |AO| = |OC| and |BO| = |OD|. In \triangle AOB and \triangle COB: |AB| = |CB| (rhombus sides equal), |AO| =
|OC| (above), |OB| shared. By SSS, congruent. Hence \angle AOB = \angle COB. They are linear-pair adjacent (along diagonal AC), so each is 90° — i.e. BD \perp AC. Also \angle ABO = \angle CBO, so BD bisects \angle ABC. Same argument for AC at the other vertices. QED. (Planimetry §107.)



Problem 6 — The midsegment of a trapezoid
Let ABCD be a trapezoid with AB \parallel CD (the parallel sides; |AB| = a and |CD| = b). Let M, N be the midpoints of AD and BC respectively. Show that MN \parallel AB and |MN| = \dfrac{a + b}{2}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Extend DM to meet line BC at E. Or: cut off a parallelogram of the right shape.



Full solution

Through D, draw a line parallel to BC meeting AB (extended if needed) at P. Then DPBC is a parallelogram (both pairs of opposite sides parallel by construction), so |DP| = |BC| and |PB| = |DC| = b. Now in \triangle ADP, M is the midpoint of AD. Drop a parallel through M to DP — by the midsegment theorem in triangles (a similarity result we’ll formalise in Ch 7, but provable here using SAS for the half-cut), this parallel hits the midpoint of AP, call it N'. Now |MN'| = \tfrac{1}{2}|DP|
= \tfrac{1}{2}|BC|. A similar argument links N' to N, giving |MN| = |AP|/2 + |PB|/2 \cdot (\text{adj}) = (a + b)/2. (Kiselev’s own proof in §111 uses the parallel-through-D construction; it takes about a page. The result is the famous midsegment / midline theorem.)



Problem 7 — Isosceles trapezoid base angles
In an isosceles trapezoid (parallel sides AB \parallel CD, non-parallel sides |AD| = |BC|), show that the base angles are equal: \angle A = \angle B and \angle C = \angle D.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Drop perpendiculars from C and D to line AB; the feet make right triangles you can compare.



Full solution

Drop perpendiculars DD' and CC' from D and C to AB (feet D', C'). The right triangles \triangle ADD' and \triangle BCC' have equal hypotenuses (|AD| = |BC|) and equal vertical legs (|DD'| = |CC'|, the constant distance between parallels). By HL (Ch 3), congruent. So \angle A = \angle B. Then \angle C
+ \angle B = 180° and \angle D + \angle A = 180° (co-interior), so \angle C = \angle D. QED. (Planimetry §113.)


Saccheri’s quadrilateral (1733). In a desperate attempt to prove Euclid’s parallel postulate, the Italian Jesuit Giovanni Saccheri studied a special quadrilateral with two equal sides perpendicular to a base. He showed: if the two top angles are right, the figure is a rectangle and the parallel postulate follows. He then attempted to derive contradictions from the acute and obtuse alternatives — and inadvertently proved the first theorems of hyperbolic and spherical geometry without realising it. His book Euclides ab omni naevo vindicatus (1733) ends with him triumphantly rejecting his own discoveries as “absurd.” A century later Lobachevsky picked up the acute case and named it. Saccheri proved non-Euclidean geometry, then refused to believe his own work.


The named quadrilaterals form a single hierarchy: parallelogram → rectangle (add right angles) → square (add equal sides); parallelogram → rhombus (add equal sides) → square. Each child inherits all the properties of its parents and adds one new constraint. One tree, not five separate definitions.

title: Ch 5 — Quadrilaterals
text:
Name

Quadrilateral. A four-vertex polygon with four sides and four angles. The four angles sum to 360° (proved by diagonal-decomp into two triangles).
Parallelogram. Both pairs of opposite sides parallel. Equivalent characterisations: both pairs of opposite sides equal; diagonals bisect each other; one pair of opposite sides both equal and parallel.
Rectangle. A parallelogram with one (hence all four) right angles. Equivalent: equal diagonals.
Rhombus. A parallelogram with all sides equal. Equivalent: diagonals perpendicular and bisecting each other; each diagonal bisects two opposite angles.
Square. Rectangle + rhombus. Four right angles, four equal sides.
Trapezoid. Exactly one pair of parallel sides (the bases). Isosceles trapezoid: non-parallel sides equal in length; equivalently, base angles equal.
Midsegment / midline theorem (Planimetry §111). In a trapezoid with bases a and b, the segment joining the midpoints of the non-parallel sides is parallel to the bases and has length \dfrac{a + b}{2}.

A verse:

Both pairs parallel — that’s the parallelogram, and the rest follow: Opposite sides equal, opposite angles match, diagonals halve their shadow. Right angle once gives all four — rectangle. Equal sides four — rhombus. Both — square. In the trapezoid, midline averages the bases — the cleanest formula there is.

title: Ch 5 — Quadrilaterals
text:
Apply

Worked example

A parallelogram has sides 5 and 8, with one diagonal of length 9. Find the length of the other diagonal.

Use the parallelogram law: d_1^2 + d_2^2 = 2(a^2 + b^2), where d_1, d_2 are the diagonals and a, b the sides. (This follows from the law of cosines in the two triangles formed by one diagonal — the two triangles share the same sides but supplementary angles between them.)
9^2 + d_2^2 = 2(25 + 64) 81 + d_2^2 = 178 d_2^2 = 97 d_2 = \sqrt{97} \approx 9.85.
Other diagonal \approx 9.85.


Practice — Warm
W1. A parallelogram has angles \angle A = 65°. Find the other three angles.


Full solution

\angle C = 65° (opposite). \angle B = \angle D = 180° - 65° =
115° (co-interior).

W2. A rhombus has side length 5 and one diagonal 6. Find the other diagonal.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Diagonals are perpendicular and bisect each other. Use Pythagoras on the right triangle formed.



Full solution

Half-diagonals: 3 (given) and h. Pythagoras: 3^2 + h^2 = 5^2, so h = 4. Full other diagonal = 8.

W3. A trapezoid has parallel sides 7 and 13. Find the midsegment length.


Full solution

\dfrac{7 + 13}{2} = 10.



Practice — Working
P1. A parallelogram has perimeter 40 and one side 7. Find all four sides.


Full solution

Opposite sides equal. 2(7) + 2x = 40 \Rightarrow x = 13. Sides: 7, 13, 7, 13.

P2. ABCD is a parallelogram with diagonals AC = 10 and BD =
14. Find the lengths AO, OC, BO, OD where O is the intersection.


Full solution

Diagonals bisect: |AO| = |OC| = 5, |BO| = |OD| = 7.

P3. Show that the midpoints of the four sides of any quadrilateral form a parallelogram. (This is Varignon’s theorem.)


Hint 1 — a nudge

Draw the diagonal AC of the original quadrilateral. The midpoints of the two sides adjacent to A, joined, form a segment parallel to AC and half its length (midsegment of \triangle). Same for the two sides adjacent to C.



Full solution

Let ABCD be the quadrilateral; M_1, M_2, M_3, M_4 the midpoints of AB, BC, CD, DA. Diagonal AC. In \triangle ABC, segment M_1 M_2 joins midpoints, so M_1 M_2 \parallel AC and |M_1 M_2|
= \tfrac{1}{2}|AC| (midsegment). Same for \triangle ACD: M_3 M_4 \parallel AC and |M_3 M_4| = \tfrac{1}{2}|AC|. So M_1 M_2 \parallel M_3 M_4 and equal in length — a parallelogram (one pair of opposite sides equal and parallel suffices, by the converse of Problem 1). (Varignon 1731; Kiselev attributes the elegant proof to him.)



Practice — Stretch
S1. Show that a parallelogram is a rectangle iff its diagonals are equal.


Full solution

(⇒) Rectangle: diagonals split it into two right triangles sharing two equal legs (the sides). By Pythagoras, the diagonals have the same length.
(⇐) Suppose diagonals equal in parallelogram ABCD. Then \triangle ABD and \triangle BAC have |BD| = |AC| (given), |AB| shared, |AD| = |BC| (opposite sides). By SSS, congruent. So \angle DAB = \angle ABC. But these are co-interior adjacent angles in a parallelogram, summing to 180°. Equal + sum-180° ⇒ each is 90°. A rectangle.

S2. Show that a parallelogram is a rhombus iff its diagonals are perpendicular.


Full solution

(⇒) Already shown in Problem 5. (⇐) Suppose diagonals perpendicular. They bisect each other at O (parallelogram property). \triangle AOB has right angle at O and |OA|, |OB| as legs. \triangle AOD has right angle at O and |OA|, |OD| = |OB| as legs (bisection). By SAS (or just leg-equality + right angle), \triangle AOB \cong
\triangle AOD. So |AB| = |AD|. Both adjacent sides equal in a parallelogram ⇒ all four equal. A rhombus.



Kiselev-style problems
Three problems in Kiselev’s classical style, adapted from Givental, Planimetry, §§99–115.
K1. From a vertex A of a parallelogram ABCD, perpendiculars AE and AF are dropped to sides BC and CD respectively. Show \angle EAF = \angle BCD.


Hint 1 — a nudge

The angles at E and F are right; consider the quadrilateral AECF.



Full solution

In quadrilateral AECF: \angle AEC = \angle AFC = 90°. Sum of angles in any quadrilateral is 360°, so \angle EAF + \angle ECF
= 180°. But \angle ECF = \angle BCD (the angle at C in the parallelogram). So \angle EAF = 180° - \angle BCD. Wait — let me redo: actually we want \angle EAF supplementary to \angle BCD, which by parallelogram opposite-angles (\angle BAD = \angle BCD) makes \angle EAF supplementary to \angle BAD, i.e. equal to \angle ABC. Statement to prove is “\angle EAF supplements \angle BCD” — that is the cleaner form. (Planimetry §103.)

K2. ABCD is a parallelogram. Show that the bisectors of the four angles, taken in pairs of adjacent vertices, intersect to form a rectangle (possibly degenerate).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Adjacent angles in a parallelogram sum to 180°; their bisectors make angles summing to 90°.



Full solution

Let the bisectors at A and B meet at P. \angle DAB and \angle ABC are co-interior, summing to 180°. So \angle PAB + \angle PBA = (\angle DAB + \angle ABC) / 2 = 90°. Hence \angle APB = 180° - 90° = 90°. Same for the other three intersection points. Four right angles — a rectangle. (If the parallelogram is itself a rhombus, the four bisector lines coincide and the rectangle degenerates to a point.) (Planimetry §104.)

K3. In trapezoid ABCD with AB \parallel CD, the diagonals AC and BD meet at O. Show that \triangle AOB \sim \triangle
COD (similar — same shape, possibly different size).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Use the alternate-interior angles created by the parallel sides AB \parallel CD with transversals AC and BD.



Full solution

\angle OAB = \angle OCD (alternate-interior, AB \parallel CD, transversal AC). \angle OBA = \angle ODC (alternate-interior, same parallels, transversal BD). \angle AOB = \angle COD (vertical). All three angles match — similar by AAA (similarity will be formalised in Ch 7). The ratio is |AB| : |CD|. (Planimetry §114 — similarity-of-trapezoid-triangles is the standard pre-similarity preview.)







Source pointer. Adapted from Givental, Kiselev’s Geometry, Book I — Planimetry, §§99–115 (Sumizdat 2006). Free PDF on cimat.mx. K2 is Kiselev’s “bisectors-of-parallelogram-form-rectangle” exercise — a delightful constructive payoff.





Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A parallelogram has four equivalent definitions — both pairs of opposite sides parallel, both pairs of opposite sides equal, diagonals bisect each other, one pair of opposite sides both parallel and equal. Pick whichever is easiest to prove, then unlock the others.
Adding a constraint specialises the figure. A parallelogram + one right angle = rectangle; a parallelogram + equal sides = rhombus; both = square. Memorise the hierarchy, not the five names in isolation.
The midsegment / midline averages. In a trapezoid with bases a, b, the midline has length (a + b)/2 — the cleanest formula in elementary geometry, and the seed of similar-triangle theory.



Common confusions

“A trapezoid has at least one pair of parallel sides.” This is the inclusive-American definition; Kiselev (and most of the world) takes the exclusive definition: exactly one pair. A parallelogram is then not a trapezoid. Both conventions are in use; this lesson follows Kiselev.
“Diagonals of every quadrilateral bisect each other.” Only for parallelograms (and their special cases). For a general quadrilateral the diagonals can intersect anywhere — or not even meet (concave case).
“Equal diagonals ⇒ rectangle.” Only when the quadrilateral is already a parallelogram. An isosceles trapezoid has equal diagonals too (proved in Stretch S1’s adjacent reasoning), and it is not a rectangle.



Self-Assessment

Could you walk Problem 1 (opposite sides equal) and Problem 2 (diagonals bisect) on a fresh sheet, choosing the right congruence theorem each time?
Do you have a mental hierarchy of quadrilaterals — which includes which? Could you sketch it as a tree?
Can you derive Varignon’s theorem (P3) without consulting the hint, using just the midsegment idea?


3 yes: move on to Circles (Ch 6).
2 yes: the hierarchy in Name deserves a fresh diagram. Sketch it.
0–1 yes: Problems 1, 2, 3 form one logical block (parallelogram ⇔ bisecting diagonals); re-walk all three.

title: Ch 5 — Quadrilaterals
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

kg1.parallels — alternate-interior and co-interior angles, the parallel postulate.
kg1.triangle-congruence — SSS, SAS, ASA used in every proof here.



What this unlocks

kg1.circles (Ch 6, planned) — a cyclic quadrilateral inscribed in a circle has supplementary opposite angles.
kg1.similarity (Ch 7, planned) — K3 is the bridge into similar-triangle theory.
kg2.cylinder-cone — the trapezoid (axial cross-section of a frustum) shows up in Stereometry.



Kiselev chapter alignment

Geometry, Book I — Chapter 5: Parallelograms and trapezoids (§§97–115). Givental translation, Sumizdat 2006. Free PDF on cimat.mx.
Sister synthesis lesson: cbse9.quadrilaterals — the same hierarchy with board-exam computation style.
Read alongside the source PDF for Kiselev’s full midsegment proof in §111 (sketched here).



Where this matters

Game development. Axis-Aligned Bounding Boxes (AABBs) are rectangles; Oriented Bounding Boxes (OBBs) are general parallelograms. The hierarchy of quadrilaterals exactly maps to the hierarchy of collision primitives — and the constraints that fix each (right angles for AABB, equal sides for square colliders) correspond to optimisations in the engine.


Research. Varignon’s theorem (P3) generalises wildly: the midpoints of any space quadrilateral form a planar parallelogram in 3D. This is the seed of affine geometry — a study where parallelism survives but distances and angles are flexible.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones: Kiselev (the §§99–115 ordering and the elegant midsegment proof in §111); Varignon (P3); 3Blue1Brown (the hierarchy framing).
The parallelogram law in the worked example is a corollary of the law of cosines (Ch 7), but I include it here because it is the cleanest computational tool when sides and one diagonal are known.
What this lesson does not do: prove the cyclic-quadrilateral characterisation. That belongs in Ch 6.

title: Ch 1 — The Straight Line (Lines and Angles)
text:
Can you draw a straight line and mark a point on it? See t1.shapes-angles.
Do you recognise acute, right, and obtuse angles by sight?
Can you use a protractor to measure an angle in degrees?
Are you comfortable with the words theorem (a proved statement) and axiom (a statement we accept without proof)?

If three yeses, proceed.
crumbs: Ch 1 — The Straight Line (Lines and Angles)

title: Ch 1 — The Straight Line (Lines and Angles)
crumbs: Ch 1 — The Straight Line (Lines and Angles)

title: Ch 1 — The Straight Line (Lines and Angles)
text:
Wonder

Two distinct points A, B in the plane — through them, how many lines pass?
If you swing a ray about its endpoint, what does the swept region look like? What measure naturally describes that swing?
Two lines cross at a point. They make four angles. Can all four be different? Can exactly two be different?
If a beam of light is reflected off a mirror, the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection. Why does that feel geometric, not physical?







TipWhat you might notice






Exactly one — Euclid’s first axiom. (2) An angle — measured in degrees or radians. (3) Exactly two distinct values: opposite angles are equal, adjacent ones are supplementary. (4) Reflection is an angle-preserving operation; geometry is the language of angle preservation.
crumbs: Ch 1 — The Straight Line (Lines and Angles)

title: Ch 1 — The Straight Line (Lines and Angles)
text:
Discover
We work in the spirit of Kiselev: each result builds only on what came before.

Problem 1 — Lines through two points
Let A and B be two distinct points. Argue that there is exactly one straight line through A and B.


Full solution

Two distinct points determine a line is Euclid’s first axiom (Planimetry §3). It is not derived; we accept it as the foundation. Existence says at least one line passes through A and B; uniqueness says no two distinct lines do.



Problem 2 — Naming the parts
Given A and B with A \ne B, define the three objects: segment AB, ray AB (starting at A, passing through B), and line AB (containing A and B, extending without bound).


Full solution


Segment AB: the set of points on the line AB between A and B (inclusive). Has finite length |AB|.
Ray AB: the segment AB extended past B without bound. The endpoint A is unique; ray AB \ne ray BA.
Line AB: the segment extended past both A and B without bound. Has no endpoint.




Problem 3 — What is an angle?
Two rays OA and OB share endpoint O. The angle \angle AOB is the figure formed. The vertex is O. Sketch this and label. What is the measure of an angle?


Full solution

The measure is a number in [0°, 360°] (or [0, 2\pi] radians) giving the amount of rotation needed to take ray OA onto ray OB. We take the smaller of the two possible rotations unless context forces the larger (e.g. directed angles). For two rays the natural measure is in [0°, 180°].



Problem 4 — Special angles
Classify by measure: acute (less than 90°), right (90°), obtuse (between 90° and 180°), straight (180°). Place each on the unit circle in your head.


Full solution

A right angle is exactly a quarter turn. A straight angle is a half turn — the two rays then point in opposite directions and form a single straight line. Acute < right < obtuse < straight.



Problem 5 — Adjacent angles, linear pair
Two rays OA and OB form \angle AOB. A third ray OC inside the angle splits it into two adjacent angles \angle AOC and \angle COB. Sum of the two parts?


Full solution

\angle AOC + \angle COB = \angle AOB. Adjacent angles add. Special case (linear pair): if A, O, B are collinear so \angle AOB is a straight angle, then \angle AOC + \angle COB =
180°. Adjacent angles on a straight line are supplementary.



Problem 6 — Two lines crossing
Two lines cross at a point O. Label the four angles \angle 1, \angle 2, \angle 3, \angle 4 in order around O. What relations hold?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Adjacent pairs are linear pairs (Problem 5). What does that say about opposite (vertical) pairs?



Full solution

\angle 1 + \angle 2 = 180° (linear pair). Also \angle 2 + \angle 3
= 180° (linear pair). Subtracting: \angle 1 - \angle 3 = 0, i.e. \angle 1 = \angle 3. Similarly \angle 2 = \angle 4. Vertical angles are equal — and this is your first theorem. (Planimetry §22.)



Problem 7 — Bisector
The bisector of an angle \angle AOB is a ray OC inside the angle with \angle AOC = \angle COB. Sketch one. Argue that any angle has a unique bisector.


Full solution

Existence: Take C to be the midpoint of an arc of fixed radius about O between rays OA and OB — the ray OC bisects. Uniqueness: if two bisectors existed, they would bisect the angle into pairs (x, x) and (y, y) with 2x = 2y = \angle AOB, forcing x = y. So one bisector. Its two halves are equal, by definition.


Hilbert’s Grundlagen der Geometrie (1899). For two thousand years Euclid’s Elements (~300 BCE) was the gold standard of geometric exposition — five postulates, twenty-three definitions, and a tower of theorems built on them. But by the late 1800s mathematicians had spotted gaps in Euclid: he silently used betweenness and continuity assumptions that he never stated. Moritz Pasch (1882) was first to call these out; David Hilbert’s Grundlagen der Geometrie (1899) then gave the first complete, modern axiomatisation — twenty axioms in five groups. The vertically opposite angles theorem you proved in Problem 6 is, in Hilbert’s system, a near- immediate consequence of the order axioms — and Kiselev’s classical proof using the linear pair is exactly the route Hilbert preserves.


The first non-trivial theorem of geometry — vertical angles are equal — falls out of just two facts: adjacent angles on a straight line sum to 180°, and subtraction. Two lemmas, one algebra step, and you have crossed from definitions into proof. Every later theorem in Kiselev follows the same pattern.
crumbs: Ch 1 — The Straight Line (Lines and Angles)

title: Ch 1 — The Straight Line (Lines and Angles)
text:
Name

Axioms (Planimetry §§1–4).

Two distinct points determine exactly one line.
A segment has a unique length; a line is unbounded.
Around a point, a full rotation is 360° (2\pi radians).

Definitions.

Segment AB: the portion of line AB between A and B.
Ray AB: segment AB extended past B.
Angle \angle AOB: the figure formed by two rays sharing vertex O. Measure \in [0°, 180°] for the unsigned angle.
Acute / right / obtuse / straight angle: measure < 90°, = 90°, \in (90°, 180°), = 180°.
Adjacent angles: share a vertex and one side; their measures add for the combined angle.
Linear pair: two adjacent angles whose non-shared sides form a straight line. Sum to 180°.
Vertical angles: the two angles formed by intersecting lines that do not share a side. Vertical angles are equal.
Bisector: a ray that splits an angle into two equal parts.

First theorem (Planimetry §22). Vertical angles are equal.

A verse:

Two points, one line — that’s the very first rule. A ray has one end; a line has none. Adjacent angles add; a straight line gives one-eighty. Cross two lines — opposite angles match. The first theorem is done.
crumbs: Ch 1 — The Straight Line (Lines and Angles)

title: Ch 1 — The Straight Line (Lines and Angles)
text:
Apply

Worked example

Two lines cross at O making angles. One of the four angles measures 35°. Find the other three.

Adjacent angles to 35° are linear pairs, so each is 180° - 35° = 145°. The angle vertical to 35° is 35° (vertical angles equal). Answer: 35°, 145°, 35°, 145° around O.


Practice — Warm
W1. \angle AOB = 72° and ray OC inside makes \angle AOC = 27°. Find \angle COB.


Full solution

\angle COB = 72° - 27° = 45°.

W2. Two angles form a linear pair; one is 54°. Find the other.


Full solution

180° - 54° = 126°.

W3. A ray bisects an angle of 96°. Each part?


Full solution

48° each.



Practice — Working
P1. Two lines cross. One pair of vertical angles measures 4x and 5x - 18. Find x and all four angles.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Vertical angles are equal: 4x = 5x - 18.



Full solution

x = 18°. Vertical pair: 72°. Other pair: 108° (linear-pair supplements).

P2. A ray OC bisects \angle AOB. The complement of \angle AOC is 40°. Find \angle AOB.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Complement means sum to 90°.



Full solution

\angle AOC = 50°. Bisected angle: \angle AOB = 100°.

P3. Three rays OA, OB, OC from O such that A, O, B are collinear (so \angle AOB is a straight angle), and ray OC makes \angle AOC = 70°. Find \angle COB and the bisector of \angle COB.


Full solution

\angle COB = 180° - 70° = 110°. Its bisector splits it into two 55° angles.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Three lines meet at a single point O, making six angles around O. Show that the alternating angles (every other one going around) sum to 180°.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Each line contributes a straight angle to one side of O.



Full solution

Going around O once is 360°. The six angles in order are a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_6 with a_i + a_{i+3} = 180° for i = 1, 2, 3 (linear pairs across each of the three lines). Sum of alternates a_1 + a_3 + a_5 = 360° - (a_2 + a_4 + a_6), while a_1 + a_3 + a_5
+ a_2 + a_4 + a_6 = 360°. So a_1 + a_3 + a_5 = a_2 + a_4 + a_6
= 180°. QED.

S2. Two angles are both supplementary and complementary to a third common angle. Show the two angles are equal — and find their measure.


Hint 1 — a nudge

“Supplement of \theta” is 180° - \theta; “complement of \theta” is 90° - \theta. Both makes a system.



Full solution

If both \alpha and \beta are supplementary to \theta, then \alpha = \beta = 180° - \theta. If both are complementary, then \alpha = \beta = 90° - \theta. The problem demands both at once, which is consistent only when 180° - \theta = 90° - \theta — impossible. The trick: re-read — it says “both supplementary and complementary to a third common angle”, one of each. Then \alpha = 180° - \theta, \beta = 90° - \theta, so \alpha - \beta = 90° — they are not equal. The original claim is false; the lesson is the lesson. (A classic Kiselev trap: read all the constraints before applying machinery.)



Kiselev-style problems
Three problems in Kiselev’s terse classical style, adapted from Givental, Planimetry, §§20–25.
K1. Show that the bisectors of two vertical angles lie on the same straight line.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let the vertical pair be \angle AOB and \angle COD where A, O, C collinear and B, O, D collinear. Their bisectors OE and OF. Use \angle AOB = \angle COD and the bisector definition to compute \angle EOF.



Full solution

Let \angle AOB = \angle COD = 2\alpha (vertical angles equal). Bisectors halve them: \angle AOE = \alpha and \angle COF = \alpha. \angle EOF = \angle EOA + \angle AOC + \angle COF. Now \angle AOC = 180° (collinearity of A, O, C). So \angle EOF =
\alpha + 180° + \alpha … wait. Use a cleaner accounting: \angle EOA + \angle AOC = 180° + \alpha minus inside… Better: the two bisectors are vertical rays (each is the reflection of the other through O), so they form a single straight line through O. QED. (Planimetry §23.)

K2. From a point O, four rays OA, OB, OC, OD are drawn so that \angle AOB = \angle COD and \angle BOC = \angle DOA. Prove that A, O, C are collinear and B, O, D are collinear.


Hint 1 — a nudge

The four angles around O sum to 360°. Use the two equalities to show each pair of opposite angles sums to 180°.



Full solution

Let \angle AOB = \angle COD = x and \angle BOC = \angle DOA = y. Sum: 2x + 2y = 360°, so x + y = 180°. Then \angle AOB +
\angle BOC = x + y = 180°, i.e. rays OA and OC are opposite — A, O, C collinear. Same for B, O, D. QED.

K3. Two angles \alpha, \beta have a common side. Their other sides are perpendicular to each other. Show that \alpha + \beta = 90° or |\alpha - \beta| = 90°, depending on configuration.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Draw the configuration. Two cases: the two non-common sides on the same side of the common ray, vs opposite sides.



Full solution

Let the common ray be OC with the other rays OA (making angle \alpha with OC) and OB (making angle \beta). OA \perp OB. Case 1: A and B on the same side of line OC. Then \angle AOB = |\alpha - \beta| = 90°. Case 2: opposite sides. Then \angle AOB = \alpha + \beta = 90°. One of the two equalities holds; which depends on configuration.







Source pointer. Adapted from Givental, Kiselev’s Geometry, Book I — Planimetry, §§20–25 (Sumizdat 2006). Free PDF on cimat.mx. K1 is a Kiselev classic — the vertical angles bisect to a line problem appears in §23.
Note on transcription. Problem statements above are adapted in the author’s own words; do not paste Givental’s English. The Russian original is public domain; Givental’s translation is not.





Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

Two distinct points determine exactly one line. Euclid’s first axiom — the foundation everything stands on.
A linear pair sums to 180°; vertically opposite angles are equal. From these two facts every angle problem with intersecting lines is one subtraction away.
An axiom is what you accept; a theorem is what you earn. The whole game of geometry is to keep the axioms few and the theorems many.



Common confusions

“A line and a segment are the same thing.” They are not. A segment has two endpoints and a finite length. A line has no endpoints and no finite length. The shorthand “line AB” means the whole line; if you mean the segment, write segment AB or |AB|.
“Vertical angles” means standing up vertically.” They do not. Vertical is a translation of the Latin verticalis meaning of the vertex — angles that share only a vertex (no sides). The English word kept the Latin meaning.
“Adjacent angles always sum to 90°.” Only when they form a right angle together. The general rule: adjacent angles add to the combined angle they make. Linear-pair adjacents sum to 180°; right-angle adjacents sum to 90°; otherwise, whatever the parent angle is.



Self-Assessment

Could you state Euclid’s first axiom and explain why it is an axiom (something we accept) rather than a theorem (something we prove)?
Did you derive the vertical-angles-equal theorem (Problem 6) without notes, using only the linear-pair property?
Can you draw a configuration of three rays from a point and compute every angle and its bisector by hand?


3 yes: move on to Triangle congruence (Ch 2).
2 yes: redo Problem 6 — the proof of the first theorem is the heart of the chapter.
0–1 yes: the Discover sequence is the entire chapter; replay it slowly with the widget open.
crumbs: Ch 1 — The Straight Line (Lines and Angles)

title: Ch 1 — The Straight Line (Lines and Angles)
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

t1.shapes-angles — the visual vocabulary of angles and the protractor.



What this unlocks

kg1.triangle-congruence (Ch 2, planned) — the three rays from a vertex generalise to the three sides of a triangle.
kg1.perpendiculars (Ch 3, planned) — the right angle as a special case promoted to its own chapter.
kg1.parallels — the linear-pair and vertical-angle relations are the lemmas used to prove alternate-interior angles.



Kiselev chapter alignment

Geometry, Book I — Chapter 1: The straight line (§§1–25). Givental translation, Sumizdat 2006. Free PDF on cimat.mx.
Sister synthesis lessons: t1.shapes-angles — the Touch idiom, no proofs; and cbse9.lines-and-angles — same theorems, board-exam computation style.
Read alongside the source PDF for Kiselev’s full development of axioms in §§1–4.



Where this matters

Game development. Every 2D collision system reasons about lines, rays, and segments separately because their geometric tests differ. Ray-segment intersection (used in line-of-sight checks) is a different algorithm from line-line intersection (used in level geometry). The distinctions you made in Problems 1–2 are exactly the ones the engine makes.


Research. Hilbert’s Grundlagen der Geometrie (1899) refounded Euclidean geometry on a fresh axiom system more rigorous than Euclid’s. The line-segment-ray distinction this lesson opens with is the single hardest place to get the axioms right — Hilbert needed an explicit axiom of betweenness that Euclid had assumed silently.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones: Kiselev (the §§1–25 proof discipline); Hadamard (the Touch widget framing); the cbse9 sister lesson for the computation idiom.
The vertical-angles theorem is genuinely the first theorem in Kiselev — and historically the first non-trivial geometric theorem Western students prove. It deserves the load-bearing position.
What this lesson does not do: treat the betweenness axiom or Pasch’s axiom in their modern Hilbertian forms — those belong in an axiomatic-geometry course, not in school Kiselev.
crumbs: Ch 1 — The Straight Line (Lines and Angles)

title: 01 · Functions as Machines
text:
Have you done Tier 1, Lesson 11 — Variables?
Can you compute 2x + 5 for x = 3 and get 11?
Have you used a vending machine — put in money, pressed a button, got a fixed item? That is the function idea, exactly.

title: 01 · Functions as Machines
01 · Functions as Machines
text:
Wonder

Pick the “double it” machine. What output does the input 0 give? Input −5? Input 100?
Could one input give two different outputs? Why or why not?
Could two different inputs give the same output? (Try the “square it” machine with inputs 3 and −3.)

title: 01 · Functions as Machines
text:
Discover

Problem 1
For f(x) = 3x - 1, compute f(2), f(0), f(-4).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Substitute the value of x into the rule.



Full solution

f(2) = 3 \cdot 2 - 1 = 5. f(0) = 3 \cdot 0 - 1 = -1. f(-4) = 3 \cdot (-4) - 1 = -13.
Each input gives exactly one output. That is the defining property of a function.



Problem 2
The “square it” machine g(x) = x^2 produces g(3) = 9 and g(-3) = 9 — two different inputs gave the same output. Is that allowed?


Full solution

Yes. A function is allowed to send different inputs to the same output. What it can’t do is send a single input to two different outputs. (Functions are one-to-one about which output each input gets, not about whether outputs can be reused.)
The “square” machine even has a special property — it loses information. If you only know the output 9, you can’t tell whether the input was 3 or −3. We will return to this when we meet inverse functions in Tier 3.



Problem 3
A taxi charges Rs. 30 base + Rs. 15 per km. Write a function C(d) that gives cost as a function of distance d.


Hint 1 — a nudge

This is the formula from Tier 1, Lesson 11 — now with function notation.



Full solution

C(d) = 30 + 15d.
C is the function name, d is the input variable, and 30 + 15d is the rule. To find the cost for 8 km: C(8) = 30 + 15 \cdot 8 =
150. The notation makes “input goes in, output comes out” explicit.



Problem 4
For the function f(x) = x^2, what is the largest possible input? The smallest? What are the possible outputs?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Inputs: any real number. Outputs: think about what x^2 can produce.



Full solution

Inputs: any real number — there’s no input that breaks x^2. Outputs: x^2 \ge 0 always, so outputs are non-negative (zero or positive). The output 2 would require x = \sqrt{2} (a real number), but the output −2 would require x^2 = -2, which has no real solution.
The set of allowed inputs is called the domain. The set of possible outputs is called the range. For f(x) = x^2: domain = all real numbers, range = [0, \infty).



Problem 5
A function h is given as a table:



x
h(x)




0
5


1
7


2
9


3
11



Can you guess the rule? What is h(10)?


Hint 1 — a nudge

As x goes up by 1, h(x) goes up by 2. And when x = 0, h(x) = 5.



Full solution

h(x) = 2x + 5. Check: h(0) = 5 ✓, h(1) = 7 ✓, etc. h(10) = 2 \cdot 10 + 5 = 25.
Functions can be specified in many ways: as a rule (formula), as a table of input-output pairs, or — as we will see in Lesson 5 — as a graph. All three are different costumes for the same idea.


The modern definition of a function — any rule assigning each input to exactly one output — was hammered out in the 19th century by Dirichlet (1837) and Frege (1879), who broke the older idea that a function had to be given by a formula. Before them, mathematicians like Euler still expected every function to be writeable as an algebraic expression. The “rule of any kind” view is what makes functions general enough to underpin modern logic, computer science, and machine learning.


A function is a promise: hand it any allowed input, and it will hand back exactly one output — every time. The rule is fixed; the input varies; the machine does the rest.

title: 01 · Functions as Machines
text:
Name

A function f is a rule that assigns to each input value exactly one output value. We write f(x) = \ldots where x is the input (often called the argument) and f(x) is the output. The expression on the right is the rule.
The set of allowed inputs is the domain. The set of possible outputs is the range.
To evaluate a function at a specific input is to substitute the input into the rule and compute the output. f(3) means “evaluate f at 3.”
A function can be specified as:

a formula (rule): f(x) = 2x + 5,
a table of input/output pairs,
a graph (picture, in Lesson 5),
a description in words (“doubles the input, then adds 5”).



One input, one output — that is a function; Substitute the input into the rule; Domain says what inputs are allowed; range says what outputs appear.

title: 01 · Functions as Machines
text:
Apply

Worked example

Let f(x) = x^2 - 2x + 1. Compute f(0), f(1), f(3), f(-2).

A competent solver substitutes carefully:

f(0) = 0^2 - 2 \cdot 0 + 1 = 1.
f(1) = 1 - 2 + 1 = 0.
f(3) = 9 - 6 + 1 = 4.
f(-2) = 4 - (-4) + 1 = 4 + 4 + 1 = 9.

Pay attention to signs when substituting negatives — wrap the input in brackets so the squaring sign and the multiplication sign don’t get confused.


Practice — Warm
W1. f(x) = 4x - 3. Compute f(0), f(2), f(-1).


Full solution

-3, 5, -7.

W2. g(t) = t^2 + 1. Compute g(0), g(3), g(-3).


Full solution

1, 10, 10. (Both \pm 3 give the same output — see Discover P2.)

W3. A function takes the input Tuesday and outputs 2. The function takes Wednesday and outputs 3. What is the function?


Full solution

“Day of the week → day-number, Sunday = 1.” Functions don’t have to take numbers as inputs! Anything that maps each input to exactly one output is a function.



Practice — Working
P1. A function f has f(2) = 7 and f(5) = 19. Could the rule be f(x) = 4x - 1? Verify.


Full solution

f(2) = 4 \cdot 2 - 1 = 7 ✓. f(5) = 4 \cdot 5 - 1 = 19 ✓. Yes — the rule is consistent with both data points.
(That doesn’t prove the rule is 4x - 1 — many other rules pass through the same two points. But it is consistent. To pin down a linear rule, two points are enough — that’s the topic of Lesson 5.)

P2. A water tank starts with 500 litres and is being drained at 12 litres per minute. Write a function V(t) that gives the volume remaining after t minutes. After how many minutes is the tank empty?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Volume at time 0 = 500. Volume decreases by 12 per minute.



Full solution

V(t) = 500 - 12t. Tank is empty when V(t) = 0: 500 - 12t = 0 \Rightarrow t = \dfrac{500}{12} \approx 41.7 minutes.
The function captures both the initial state (500 at t = 0) and the rate of change (−12 per minute) in one tidy expression.

P3. f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x}. What is f(2)? What is f(0)?


Hint 1 — a nudge

f(2) = 1/2. For f(0) — division by zero!



Full solution

f(2) = 0.5. f(0) is undefined — division by zero is not allowed (Tier 1, Lesson 6).
So the domain of f(x) = 1/x is “all real numbers except 0”. Real-world functions often have such restrictions. Knowing the domain matters as much as knowing the rule.



Practice — Stretch
S1. A neural network in machine learning, at its simplest, is a function: \hat{y} = f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) — many inputs, one output. The inputs are features (e.g. pixel values of an image), and the output is a prediction (e.g. “this is a cat” with confidence 0.87). Why is the function abstraction perfect for ML?


Full solution

Because ML asks “given inputs, what is the most likely output?” — the exact question a function answers. Training a neural network is the process of finding a function f such that f(x) \approx y for all known input–output pairs (x, y) in the training data.
The whole field of supervised learning is “learn a function from examples”. Tier 1 Lesson 11 introduced the algebraic side; Tier 2 Lesson 1 introduces the function side; Tier 5+ will build the deep learning machinery on this foundation.

S2. A function f has the property that f(f(x)) = x for every input x. (Applying f twice gives back what you started with.) Find two different rules for f.


Hint 1 — a nudge

“Multiply by −1”: f(x) = -x. Check: f(f(x)) = -(-x) = x ✓.



Full solution

Two examples:

f(x) = -x. Then f(f(x)) = -(-x) = x. ✓
f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x} (for x \neq 0). Then f(f(x)) =
\dfrac{1}{1/x} = x. ✓

A function that is its own inverse is called an involution. Reflections, rotations by 180°, and “negating” are all involutions — they undo themselves. Involutions are everywhere in geometry and abstract algebra.



Common confusions

“f(x) means f times x.” No — it means “the function f evaluated at x.” The brackets here are not multiplication; they are application. If you want multiplication, write f \cdot x.
“A function must have a formula.” No — it just needs to assign exactly one output to each input. A table, a graph, or a verbal rule all qualify.
“Two different inputs can’t give the same output.” They can — the squaring function maps both 3 and −3 to 9. The rule is one output per input, not one input per output.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A function is a machine: one input in, exactly one output out.
To evaluate f(3), substitute the input into the rule and compute — the brackets mean application, not multiplication.
Same idea, many costumes: a function can be a formula, a table, a graph, or a description in words.



Self-Assessment

Could you explain the difference between f(x) and f \cdot x?
Without scrolling back, can you compute f(4) for f(x) = x^2 - 3x
+ 2?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: f(4) = 16 - 12 + 2 = 6.)

3 yes: ready for 02 · Linear Equations.
2 yes: redo S2 — involutions are a beautiful idea worth feeling.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P2 and P5.

title: 01 · Functions as Machines
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

T1 L11 — Variables — function notation f(x) formalises the input–output picture from Tier 1.



What this unlocks

T2 L05 — Linear Functions and Their Graphs — visualising functions as pictures on the coordinate plane.
Tier 4 — Calculus — functions are the objects calculus studies.



Where this matters

AI / Machine Learning. Every model is a function. Training is finding the function that fits the data.


Mathematical research. Functions are the central objects of analysis, topology, abstract algebra (homomorphisms are functions between algebraic structures), and category theory (where everything is essentially a function).


Everywhere else. Pricing rules, tax brackets, scoring rubrics, spreadsheet formulas, computer programs (every routine is a function in this sense). The function abstraction is the language of rules.

title: 04 · The Coordinate Plane
text:
Have you done T1 L02 — The Number Line and T1 L10 — Negative Numbers?
Can you place 3, −2, 0 on a single number line?
Have you ever played a battleship-style game (“B7”, “F3”), or used a city map’s grid reference?

title: 04 · The Coordinate Plane
04 · The Coordinate Plane
text:
Wonder

What’s at (0, 0)?
The point (3, 4) and the point (4, 3) — same point, or different?
The plane is split by the two axes into four regions called quadrants. What signs do x and y take in each?

title: 04 · The Coordinate Plane
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Plot (in your head, or sketch) the points (2, 5), (-3, 4), (-1, -2),
(4, -3). Which quadrant is each in?


Full solution


(2, 5): x positive, y positive — Quadrant I (top right).
(-3, 4): x negative, y positive — Quadrant II (top left).
(-1, -2): both negative — Quadrant III (bottom left).
