(4, -3): x positive, y negative — Quadrant IV (bottom right).

The four quadrants are numbered counter-clockwise starting from the top right.



Problem 2
What does the set of all points (x, 0) look like? What about (0, y)?


Full solution

(x, 0): every point with y-coordinate 0 — that’s the x-axis (the horizontal line). (0, y): the y-axis (vertical line).
Points on an axis have one coordinate equal to zero.



Problem 3
Find the distance between the points A = (2, 0) and B = (5, 0).


Full solution

Both lie on the x-axis. Distance = 5 - 2 = 3.
For two points on the same horizontal line, distance is just the difference in x-coordinates. Same idea on a vertical line.
For points not on the same line, we need the Pythagorean theorem — Lesson 10.



Problem 4
Three friends meet at a park entrance and walk to: Anya goes to (3, 4), Bina goes to (-2, 5), Cara goes to (0, -3). Sketch their positions.
Whose position is furthest from the entrance (the origin)?


Hint 1 — a nudge

For a point (x, y), distance from origin is the length of the line from (0, 0) to (x, y). The Pythagorean theorem (Lesson 10) says this is \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}, but you can compare without the square root: just compare x^2 + y^2.



Full solution


Anya: x^2 + y^2 = 9 + 16 = 25.
Bina: 4 + 25 = 29.
Cara: 0 + 9 = 9.

Largest is Bina at 29 → distance \sqrt{29} \approx 5.39. Bina is furthest from the origin.
The square-of-distance comparison avoids square roots; this trick is heavily used in computer graphics for performance.



Problem 5
A point starts at (2, 3). It moves 3 right and 2 down. Where does it end up?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Right adds to x; down subtracts from y.



Full solution

(2 + 3, 3 - 2) = (5, 1).
A movement in the plane is an addition to coordinates: this idea becomes vectors in Tier 4.


The coordinate plane is named the Cartesian plane after the French philosopher René Descartes, whose La Géométrie (1637) showed how to attach an address — a pair of numbers — to every point of the plane. This single move fused algebra and geometry: a curve became an equation, an equation became a curve. Calculus, linear algebra, and modern physics were all built on Descartes’ bridge. Pierre de Fermat discovered the same idea independently around the same time.


Two number lines crossed at zero turn every point in the plane into a pair of numbers — and turn every geometric question (where, how far, which direction) into an arithmetic one.

title: 04 · The Coordinate Plane
text:
Name

The coordinate plane (or Cartesian plane, after Descartes) is two number lines — the x-axis (horizontal) and y-axis (vertical) — crossed at right angles at a point called the origin.
A point in the plane is an ordered pair (x, y) — first the x-coordinate, then the y-coordinate. Order matters: (3, 4) \neq (4, 3).
The two axes divide the plane into four quadrants:

I (top right): x > 0, y > 0
II (top left): x < 0, y > 0
III (bottom left): x < 0, y < 0
IV (bottom right): x > 0, y < 0

Distance between two points on the same horizontal line: difference in x-coordinates. On the same vertical line: difference in y-coordinates. (For general distance, use Pythagoras — Lesson 10.)


Two number lines, crossed at zero — the plane; Each point gets an ordered pair (x, y); Order matters — (3, 4) is not (4, 3).

title: 04 · The Coordinate Plane
text:
Apply

Worked example

A robot at (1, 2) is told: “go 4 east, then 3 north.” What are its new coordinates? Then: “from there, go 5 west, 6 south.” Now where?

A competent solver tracks coordinates step by step:

Start: (1, 2).
After “4 east, 3 north”: (1 + 4, 2 + 3) = (5, 5).
After “5 west, 6 south”: (5 - 5, 5 - 6) = (0, -1).

Final position: (0, -1). On the y-axis, just below the origin.
This kind of coordinate tracking is exactly how a game character or a robot moves through its world.


Practice — Warm
W1. Which quadrant contains (7, -4)? (-2, -8)? (0, 5)?


Full solution

IV; III; on the y-axis (between II and I, not in any quadrant).

W2. Plot mentally: (0, 0), (1, 1), (2, 4), (3, 9). Pattern?


Full solution

y = x^2. Each point lies on the curve we’ll meet in Lesson 14 (quadratic graph).

W3. Two points: A = (1, 5), B = (1, -3). Distance?


Full solution

Same x-coordinate → on a vertical line. Distance = |5 - (-3)| = 8.



Practice — Working
P1. Sketch the four points (0, 0), (4, 0), (4, 3), (0, 3). What shape do they form?


Full solution

A rectangle — width 4, height 3, area 12. The coordinate plane makes geometric reasoning algebraic: areas, lengths, slopes all become calculations.

P2. A point’s coordinates are (2k, k - 1) where k is unknown. For which value of k does the point lie on the x-axis?


Hint 1 — a nudge

On the x-axis means y = 0.



Full solution

y = k - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow k = 1. Then the point is (2, 0).
You combined coordinate geometry with equation-solving (Lesson 2). The two skills meet again everywhere from now on.

P3. Three points are the corners of a triangle: A = (0, 0), B =
(4, 0), C = (4, 3). Compute the lengths of the sides AB, BC, CA that lie along axes.


Hint 1 — a nudge

AB and BC lie on lines parallel to axes. CA does not — note that you can find it but you’ll need a new tool (Pythagoras).



Full solution

AB: from (0,0) to (4,0), on the x-axis. Length = 4. BC: from (4,0) to (4,3), vertical. Length = 3. CA: from (4,3) back to (0,0), diagonal. Length needs Pythagoras — but with sides 3 and 4 it’s the famous 5. (We will derive that properly in Lesson 10.)



Practice — Stretch
S1. A computer screen places its origin at the top-left corner, with x growing rightward and y growing downward. (Yes — the y-axis is flipped compared to maths convention.) What’s the screen coordinate of a pixel that is “50 from the left, 80 from the top” of a 1920×1080 screen?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Just (50, 80) — but the convention difference matters for game and graphics programming.



Full solution

(50, 80) in screen coordinates. To convert to standard maths coordinates with the origin at the bottom-left, you’d flip y: (50, 1080 - 80) = (50, 1000).
The flipped y-axis is a famous source of confusion when learning graphics programming. It is a historical accident — early screens drew top-to-bottom, like text — and is now standardised across operating systems and graphics libraries.

S2. A point in 3D space needs three coordinates: (x, y, z). Game engines, physics simulations, and CAD software all live in 3D. What changes when you move from 2D to 3D? What stays the same?


Full solution

Stays the same: axes meet at right angles; ordered tuples name points; movements are coordinate-by-coordinate. Changes: there are now eight “octants” (instead of 4 quadrants); distance formula needs a third term (\sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}); rotations become much more interesting (introducing quaternions in Tier 7 — game-dev track); the third axis (z) has no canonical direction (computer graphics often uses right-handed coordinates, but some packages use left-handed).
The 2D plane you have just met is the foundation. 3D is the same idea plus one more dimension. The leap from 1D (number line) to 2D (plane) is the conceptually big step — going to 3D is just one more.



Common confusions

“x comes after y alphabetically, so (y, x).” No: ordered pairs are always (x, y) — horizontal first, vertical second. This convention is universal in mathematics.
“The plane has 4 quadrants, including the axes.” Points on an axis are on the boundary, not in any quadrant.
“Computer screens use the same coordinates as maths.” They flip the y-axis (Stretch S1). One of those facts that bites every programmer at least once.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

The coordinate plane gives every point a unique address (x, y) — horizontal first, vertical second.
Order matters: (3, 4) and (4, 3) are different points, in different parts of the plane.
Geometry becomes arithmetic — distances, shapes, and movements all reduce to operations on the coordinates.



Self-Assessment

Could you sketch the point (-3, 5) on a coordinate plane and say which quadrant it’s in?
Without scrolling back, what are the coordinates of the point that’s 3 right and 4 down from (2, 5)?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: (5, 1).)

3 yes: ready for 05 · Linear Functions and Their Graphs.
2 yes: redo P3 — the diagonal-distance hint sets up Pythagoras nicely.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P4 and the worked example.

title: 04 · The Coordinate Plane
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

T1 L02 — Number Line — the plane is two number lines.
T1 L10 — Negative Numbers — needed for the four quadrants.



What this unlocks

05 · Linear Functions and Their Graphs — visualising functions as pictures.
Tier 4 — Vectors in 2D and 3D — coordinates extended to model direction and magnitude.



Where this matters

Game development & graphics. Every pixel, every sprite, every mesh vertex is a coordinate tuple. Game-state is largely just lots of points and the rules for moving them.


AI / Machine Learning. A feature vector in ML is a point in high-dimensional space. Embeddings, decision boundaries, latent spaces — all are points in coordinate spaces (just with hundreds or thousands of axes instead of two).


Mathematical research. Descartes’ invention of coordinate geometry (1637) unified the previously separate worlds of algebra and geometry. Calculus and modern analysis were built on this unification.

title: 14 · Quadratic Equations (Intro)
text:
Have you done 02 · Linear Equations and 12 · Factoring Polynomials?
Can you factor x^2 + 5x + 6 into (x+2)(x+3)?
Comfortable with the idea that an equation can have two solutions (e.g., x^2 = 25 has x = \pm 5, from Lesson 9 P2)?

title: 14 · Quadratic Equations (Intro)
14 · Quadratic Equations (Intro)
text:
Wonder

For x^2 - 5x + 6 = 0: the parabola crosses at x = 2 and x = 3. These are the roots. Why those numbers?
What if c = 0? Like x^2 - 5x = 0. What are the roots?
What if the parabola never touches the x-axis? Does the equation have any solutions?

title: 14 · Quadratic Equations (Intro)
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Solve x^2 - 5x + 6 = 0 by factoring.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Factor (Lesson 12). Then if a product is zero, one of the factors must be zero.



Full solution

x^2 - 5x + 6 = (x - 2)(x - 3) = 0.
Zero-product property: if A \cdot B = 0, then A = 0 or B = 0 (or both). So x - 2 = 0 ⇒ x = 2, or x - 3 = 0 ⇒ x = 3.
Solutions: x = 2 or x = 3. A quadratic typically has two solutions.



Problem 2
Solve x^2 - 5x = 0.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Factor out x.



Full solution

x(x - 5) = 0, so x = 0 or x - 5 = 0 ⇒ x = 5.
Solutions: x = 0 or x = 5. When c = 0, x = 0 is always a root (and the other is -b).



Problem 3
Solve x^2 - 9 = 0.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Difference of squares: x^2 - 9 = (x - 3)(x + 3).



Full solution

(x - 3)(x + 3) = 0 \Rightarrow x = 3 or x = -3.
Equivalently, x^2 = 9 \Rightarrow x = \pm 3 (Lesson 9 P2).



Problem 4
Solve x^2 - 6x + 9 = 0.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Recognise the perfect square: x^2 - 6x + 9 = (x - 3)^2.



Full solution

(x - 3)^2 = 0 \Rightarrow x - 3 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 3.
One repeated root. A perfect-square quadratic has its parabola tangent to the x-axis at the single root. The two solutions “coincide” — algebraically you can think of it as x = 3 “twice”.



Problem 5
Try to solve x^2 + 1 = 0.


Hint 1 — a nudge

x^2 = -1. Is there a real number whose square is -1?



Full solution

No real solutions. x^2 \ge 0 for all real x, so x^2 + 1 \ge 1
> 0. The parabola never touches the x-axis.
Over the complex numbers (Tier 6), the solutions are x = \pm i where i^2 = -1. But over the reals, this equation has no solution.
The trichotomy: every quadratic has 2, 1, or 0 real roots. The discriminant b^2 - 4ac tells which: - > 0: two distinct real roots - = 0: one repeated root (perfect square) - < 0: no real roots


The 9th-century Persian scholar Muhammad ibn Mūsā al-Khwārizmī solved quadratic equations geometrically, by literally completing the square — drawing an x \times x square, attaching b/2 rectangles to two sides, and noting what extra little square was needed to make the whole figure a perfect square. His Baghdad treatise Kitāb al-jabr wa-l-muqābala (c. 820 CE) gave us both the procedure and the name: al-jabr (“restoration”) became the English word algebra. His own name became algorithm.


A product is zero only when one of its pieces is zero. That trivial-sounding fact is the entire engine of solving by factoring: (x-r)(x-s)=0 collapses immediately into “either x=r or x=s.” A quadratic stops being scary the moment it has been split in two.

title: 14 · Quadratic Equations (Intro)
text:
Name

A quadratic equation in x has the form a x^2 + b x + c = 0 \qquad (a \neq 0).
To solve by factoring: factor the polynomial, then use the zero-product property: if (x - r)(x - s) = 0, then x = r or x = s.
Three possibilities for real roots:



Discriminant b^2 - 4ac
Number of real roots




> 0
2 distinct


= 0
1 repeated


< 0
0 (the parabola misses the x-axis)



The full quadratic formula (for cases that don’t factor nicely over the integers) is x = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}. We will use this from Tier 3 onward; for Tier 2 we focus on factoring.


Factor and use the zero-product property — the easy path; Two roots, one root, or none — count by the discriminant; The quadratic formula handles the rest.

title: 14 · Quadratic Equations (Intro)
text:
Apply

Worked example

Solve x^2 + 7x + 12 = 0.

A competent solver factors first.

Find r, s: r + s = 7, rs = 12 → r = 3, s = 4.
So (x + 3)(x + 4) = 0.
Zero-product: x + 3 = 0 \Rightarrow x = -3, or x + 4 = 0
\Rightarrow x = -4.
Check: (-3)^2 + 7(-3) + 12 = 9 - 21 + 12 = 0 ✓.

Solutions: x = -3 or x = -4. Notice: roots are opposites of the factor constants. (x + 3) has root -3.


Practice — Warm
W1. Solve x^2 - 4 = 0.


Full solution

(x-2)(x+2) = 0 \Rightarrow x = \pm 2.

W2. Solve x^2 + 6x = 0.


Full solution

x(x+6) = 0 \Rightarrow x = 0 or x = -6.

W3. Solve x^2 - 5x + 6 = 0.


Full solution

(x-2)(x-3) = 0 \Rightarrow x = 2 or x = 3. (Confirms Discover P1.)



Practice — Working
P1. Solve x^2 + 2x - 8 = 0.


Full solution

(x + 4)(x - 2) = 0 \Rightarrow x = -4 or x = 2.

P2. Solve x^2 - 10x + 25 = 0.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Perfect square.



Full solution

(x - 5)^2 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 5 (repeated root).

P3. A rectangular garden’s length is 3 m more than its width. Its area is 40 m². Find the dimensions.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let width = w. Length = w + 3. Area: w(w + 3) = 40, i.e., w^2
+ 3w - 40 = 0.



Full solution

w^2 + 3w - 40 = 0 \Rightarrow (w + 8)(w - 5) = 0 \Rightarrow w = -8 or w = 5.
Width must be positive, so width = 5 m, length = 8 m. Check: 5
\times 8 = 40 ✓.
The negative root w = -8 is algebraically valid but physically meaningless (no negative widths). Real-world problems often produce extra algebraic solutions that must be discarded based on context.



Practice — Stretch
S1. A ball is thrown straight up with an initial speed of 20 m/s from a height of 1.8 m. Its height after t seconds is h(t) = 1.8 + 20t - 5t^2 \quad (\text{metres}) (approximating gravity). When does the ball hit the ground?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Hit the ground means h(t) = 0. Solve 1.8 + 20t - 5t^2 = 0 — or equivalently 5t^2 - 20t - 1.8 = 0.



Full solution

5t^2 - 20t - 1.8 = 0. This doesn’t factor cleanly over the integers, but the quadratic formula gives: t = \dfrac{20 \pm \sqrt{400 + 36}}{10} = \dfrac{20 \pm \sqrt{436}}{10}
\approx \dfrac{20 \pm 20.88}{10}.
Two roots: t \approx 4.09 s (positive — when the ball returns to ground) and t \approx -0.09 s (negative — physically meaningless, “when the ball was thrown if reversed in time”). Drop the negative.
Ball lands at about 4.09 seconds. Quadratics describe trajectories of projectiles under uniform gravity — the foundational physics of falling and throwing.

S2. In machine learning, the mean squared error loss is L(w) = \sum_i (y_i - wx_i)^2 — a quadratic in the weight w. Training finds the w that minimises L. Why does a quadratic loss make optimisation easier than other choices?


Hint 1 — a nudge

A quadratic in w is a parabola — it has a unique minimum or maximum. And the slope (derivative) is linear in w.



Full solution

Two reasons. First, a parabola has a unique minimum point — no local minima to get stuck in (unlike higher-degree or non-convex losses).
Second, the gradient (the rate of change with respect to w) is itself a linear function of w. So setting gradient = 0 gives a linear equation that we can solve directly — no iteration needed.
This is why linear regression has a famous closed-form solution (“least squares” — Tier 5). Modern ML uses non-quadratic losses for many problems, but quadratic loss is the first choice and often the right one.
The quadratic equation you just learned is the algebra behind the single most-used ML algorithm of the last century.



Common confusions

“Always exactly two solutions.” No — perfect squares give 1 (repeated); some quadratics have 0 real solutions. The discriminant tells you which.
“Solve x^2 = 25 as x = 5.” Forgets the negative root. Always \pm when taking square roots in equations.
“Discard solutions that don’t seem right.” Discard solutions that violate physical or contextual constraints (negative widths, negative time before throwing) — but never discard a mathematically valid root without justification.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A quadratic equation usually has two solutions — the two places where its parabola crosses the x-axis.
The fastest route to those solutions is to factor and use the zero-product property. A product is zero exactly when one of its factors is.
The discriminant b^2 - 4ac tells you, before you factor, whether you’ll find two roots, one, or none.



Self-Assessment

Could you explain the zero-product property to a friend, in your own words?
Without scrolling back, can you solve x^2 - 7x + 10 = 0?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: (x - 2)(x - 5) = 0 \Rightarrow x = 2 or x = 5.)

3 yes: ready for 15 · Sequences (Intro).
2 yes: redo S1 (projectile) — quadratic-trajectory physics is one of the great applications.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P1 and P5.

title: 14 · Quadratic Equations (Intro)
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

12 · Factoring Polynomials
02 · Linear Equations — zero-product property reduces to two linear equations.



What this unlocks

Tier 3 — The Quadratic Formula — the general solution method, including completing the square.
Tier 3 — Conics — parabolas, circles, ellipses, hyperbolas as graphs of quadratics in 2 variables.



Where this matters

Game development & physics. Projectile motion (S1), collision detection (when does this trajectory intersect this surface?), ballistic calculations — all involve solving quadratics in real time.


AI / Machine Learning. Mean-squared-error loss (S2) is the oldest and still most common choice. Many regularisation terms (\ell_2, ridge regression) are also quadratic.


Mathematical research. Quadratic forms — generalisations of ax^2 + bx + c — are central in number theory, physics (energy = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2), and pure algebra.


Everywhere else. Maximum profit problems (parabolic revenue curves), trajectory optimisation, structural engineering (parabolic arches and cables), antenna design (parabolic dishes — focusing property of parabolas).

title: 02 · Linear Equations in One Variable
text:
Have you done T1 L11 — Variables?
In Lesson 11 you solved 2x + 5 = 17 and got x = 6. Comfortable with that?
Have you done T1 L10 — Negative Numbers? We’ll need to handle negative coefficients here.

title: 02 · Linear Equations in One Variable
02 · Linear Equations in One Variable
text:
Wonder

For 3x + 4 = 13, can you guess x? Now check: substitute back.
What is the solution to 0 \cdot x + 5 = 5? To 0 \cdot x + 5 = 7?
If you multiply both sides of 3x = 12 by 2, do you get a different equation? Does it have the same solution?

title: 02 · Linear Equations in One Variable
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Solve 5x = 30. Then 5x + 7 = 30. Then 5x + 7 = 32.


Full solution

5x = 30 \Rightarrow x = 6. 5x + 7 = 30 \Rightarrow 5x = 23 \Rightarrow x = 23/5 = 4.6. 5x + 7 = 32 \Rightarrow 5x = 25 \Rightarrow x = 5.
Same shape, three different solutions. The equation is a frame; the specific numbers fill it.



Problem 2
Solve 4x - 3 = 2x + 9 (variable on both sides).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Get all the x’s on one side first: subtract 2x from both sides.



Full solution

4x - 3 = 2x + 9. Subtract 2x from both sides: 2x - 3 = 9. Add 3: 2x = 12. Divide by 2: x = 6.
The “fundamental move” (do the same thing to both sides) handles variables-on-both-sides without trouble. Strategy: collect x’s on one side, constants on the other, then divide.



Problem 3
Solve 2(x + 3) = 14. Solve it two ways: (a) distribute first, (b) divide first.


Full solution

(a) Distribute first: 2x + 6 = 14 \Rightarrow 2x = 8 \Rightarrow x
= 4. (b) Divide first: x + 3 = 7 \Rightarrow x = 4.
Both work — and (b) is faster. Recognising that both sides are divisible by 2 saves a step.



Problem 4
Solve \dfrac{x}{3} + 2 = 7.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Subtract 2 first; then multiply both sides by 3 to undo the division.



Full solution

\tfrac{x}{3} = 5 \Rightarrow x = 15. Check: \tfrac{15}{3} + 2 = 5 +
2 = 7 ✓.
The inverses pair up: addition undoes subtraction; multiplication undoes division.



Problem 5
Solve 0x + 5 = 5. Then solve 0x + 5 = 7.


Hint 1 — a nudge

0x = 0 regardless of x. So both equations reduce to “is 5 = 5?” and “is 5 = 7?”.



Full solution

0x + 5 = 5: any x satisfies this — every value of x is a solution. Infinitely many solutions.
0x + 5 = 7: no x satisfies this — it reduces to “5 = 7”, which is false. No solution.
So a linear equation can have one solution (the typical case), no solution, or infinitely many solutions. This trichotomy reappears when we meet systems of equations in Lesson 6.


The word algebra comes from the Arabic al-jabr — “the reunion of broken parts” — coined by the 9th-century Persian scholar al-Khwārizmī in his book Kitāb al-jabr wa’l-muqābala (~825 CE). Al-jabr meant moving a subtracted term to the other side of the equation; al-muqābala meant cancelling like terms on both sides. Those are exactly the moves you just made. al-Khwārizmī’s name also gave us the word algorithm.


An equation is a balance scale. Whatever you do to one side, do to the other, and the balance holds — keep going until x is alone on one pan.

title: 02 · Linear Equations in One Variable
text:
Name

A linear equation in one variable has the form ax + b = c (possibly with several terms involving x, which can always be collected). Here a, b, c are known constants and x is the unknown.
To solve is to find the value(s) of x that make the equation true. The fundamental move: do the same operation to both sides to preserve the balance.
Strategy:

Distribute / clear brackets.
Collect like terms (variables on one side, constants on the other).
Divide to isolate x.
Always check by substituting the solution into the original equation.

Three possible outcomes:

Unique solution: the typical case (a \neq 0).
No solution: if the equation reduces to a false statement (0 = 5).
Infinitely many solutions: if it reduces to a true statement (0 = 0, meaning every x works).



Distribute, collect, divide — three moves to isolate the unknown; Always do the same to both sides; Always check by substitution.

title: 02 · Linear Equations in One Variable
text:
Apply

Worked example

Solve 3(2x - 5) + 4 = x + 14.

A competent solver works systematically:

Distribute: 6x - 15 + 4 = x + 14, i.e., 6x - 11 = x + 14.
Collect x: subtract x from both sides: 5x - 11 = 14.
Collect constants: add 11 to both sides: 5x = 25.
Divide: x = 5.
Check in the original: 3(2 \cdot 5 - 5) + 4 = 3 \cdot 5 + 4 =
19. RHS: 5 + 14 = 19. ✓

Answer: x = 5.


Practice — Warm
W1. Solve: 7x = 49.


Full solution

x = 7.

W2. Solve: 2x + 11 = 23.


Full solution

2x = 12 \Rightarrow x = 6.

W3. Solve: 5x - 8 = 17.


Full solution

5x = 25 \Rightarrow x = 5.



Practice — Working
P1. Solve: 4(x - 2) = 20.


Full solution

x - 2 = 5 \Rightarrow x = 7. (Or distribute: 4x - 8 = 20, etc.)

P2. Solve: 3x - 5 = 7x + 11.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Subtract 7x from both sides: -4x - 5 = 11. Add 5; divide.



Full solution

3x - 5 = 7x + 11 \Rightarrow -4x = 16 \Rightarrow x = -4.
When the variable’s coefficient becomes negative, divide normally — the answer is just negative. (T1 L10 trained this reflex.)

P3. A father is 3 times as old as his son. In 12 years, the father will be twice as old as his son. How old is the son now?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let the son’s current age be s. Father is 3s. In 12 years: son is s + 12, father is 3s + 12. Father is twice the son: 3s + 12 = 2(s
+ 12).



Full solution

3s + 12 = 2s + 24 \Rightarrow s = 12. Son is 12; father is 36. Check: in 12 years, son is 24, father is 48. 48 = 2 \cdot 24 ✓.
Word problems become equations once you give the unknown a name. That’s the entire skill of modelling — translating life into algebra.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Solve: \dfrac{2x - 3}{4} = \dfrac{x + 1}{3}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Cross-multiply (Tier 1 Lesson 9, proportions): 3(2x - 3) = 4(x + 1).



Full solution

3(2x - 3) = 4(x + 1) \Rightarrow 6x - 9 = 4x + 4 \Rightarrow 2x = 13
\Rightarrow x = 6.5.
Check: LHS = (2 \cdot 6.5 - 3)/4 = 10/4 = 2.5. RHS = (6.5 + 1)/3 =
7.5/3 = 2.5. ✓
Solutions need not be whole numbers. Algebra is honest about that.

S2. A neural network with one weight w and one bias b outputs y = wx + b for input x. To make it predict 7 when x = 2, we need 2w + b = 7. To make it predict 11 when x = 4, we need 4w + b = 11. Two equations in two unknowns. By inspection (or by clever manipulation) — what are w and b?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Subtract the first from the second: (4w + b) - (2w + b) = 11 - 7, so 2w = 4.



Full solution

2w = 4 \Rightarrow w = 2. Then 2 \cdot 2 + b = 7 \Rightarrow b = 3.
So y = 2x + 3. Check: y(2) = 7 ✓, y(4) = 11 ✓.
You just trained a one-weight, one-bias linear model — by hand, with two data points. Real neural networks have millions of weights and millions of equations, and they are solved approximately by gradient descent (Tier 6). But the underlying idea — find the parameters that make the equations hold — is exactly what you did here.
We meet systems of two equations in two unknowns properly in Lesson 06.



Common confusions

“You can’t subtract a variable.” You can — variables behave like numbers under all the standard operations. 4x - 2x = 2x exactly as 4 - 2 = 2.
“Always solve in the order written.” No — collect like terms first, then isolate. The order of operations and the strategy for solving are different things.
“If you can’t get a whole-number answer, you’ve made a mistake.” No — algebraic solutions can be fractions, decimals, irrational, or negative. Trust the algebra, then check.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A linear equation is a question: what value of x makes both sides agree?.
Do the same operation to both sides — distribute, collect, divide — until x stands alone, then check by substituting back.
One solution is the common case, but “no solution” and “infinitely many” both appear and both mean something real.



Self-Assessment

Could you explain the “do the same thing to both sides” rule and why it preserves equality?
Without scrolling back, can you solve 3x + 4 = 5x - 8?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: -2x + 4 = -8 \Rightarrow -2x = -12 \Rightarrow x = 6.)

3 yes: ready for 03 · Linear Inequalities.
2 yes: redo the age-problem P3 — translation skill matters.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P2 (variables on both sides) and the worked example.

title: 02 · Linear Equations in One Variable
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

T1 L11 — Variables
T1 L10 — Negative Numbers



What this unlocks

T2 L03 — Linear Inequalities — the same strategy with one twist around negation.
T2 L06 — Systems of Linear Equations — two equations, two unknowns.



Where this matters

AI / Machine Learning. Stretch S2 is the entire game in miniature: ML training is solving equations to find parameters that fit data.


Mathematical research. Equation-solving is one of the oldest mathematical activities. The systematic algorithmic style — al-jabr — that you used here is named after al-Khwārizmī’s 9th-century book.


Everywhere else. Pricing analysis, budget balancing, planning, chemistry stoichiometry, physics motion problems, every spreadsheet “goal seek” — anywhere you need to find a value that satisfies a constraint.

title: 03 · Linear Inequalities
text:
Have you done 02 · Linear Equations?
Comfortable with the symbols <, >, ≤, ≥? (less, greater, less-or-equal, greater-or-equal)
Comfortable that on the number line, “−7 < −2” because −7 is further left?

title: 03 · Linear Inequalities
03 · Linear Inequalities
text:
Wonder

The inequality x > 3 has solutions 4, 5, 6, … but also 3.5, 3.0001, \pi. Is 3 itself a solution?
x \ge 3 and x > 3 — what’s the difference?
Multiplying both sides of -2x > 6 by -1: does x < ? or x > ? result? (This is the rule that catches everyone first time.)

title: 03 · Linear Inequalities
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Solve x + 5 > 9.


Full solution

x > 4. Same fundamental move as equations: subtract 5 from both sides. The inequality sign stays the same direction when you add or subtract.



Problem 2
Solve 3x \le 12.


Full solution

x \le 4. Divide both sides by 3 (a positive number); inequality direction is unchanged.



Problem 3
Solve -2x > 6.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Try a value: does x = -4 satisfy -2x > 6? -2 \cdot -4 = 8 > 6 ✓. Does x = 0 satisfy it? 0 > 6 is false. So the solution must be more negative, not more positive.



Full solution

x < -3. When you divide (or multiply) both sides by a negative number, flip the inequality.
-2x > 6, divide by -2 (negative!): x < -3.
Why? Multiplying by -1 reflects everything across zero. If 5 > 3, then -5 < -3 — they swap order. The inequality sign records “left vs right on the number line”, and reflecting flips left and right.
This is the only rule of inequalities that differs from equations. Burn it in: negative multiply or divide → flip the sign.



Problem 4
Solve 4x - 7 \ge 2x + 5.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Same as solving an equation, but watch the sign-flip rule. (Here you won’t need it — the coefficient of x stays positive.)



Full solution

4x - 2x \ge 5 + 7 \Rightarrow 2x \ge 12 \Rightarrow x \ge 6.
The solution set: \{ x : x \ge 6 \}, the half-line from 6 onward, including 6 itself.



Problem 5
A delivery van weighs 800 kg empty and can carry packages totalling at most 1500 kg total weight (van + cargo). What is the maximum package weight w it can carry? Write as an inequality and solve.


Hint 1 — a nudge

“At most 1500” means \le 1500.



Full solution

800 + w \le 1500 \Rightarrow w \le 700 kg.
Real-world constraints — capacity limits, budget caps, time deadlines — almost always become inequalities, not equations. Inequalities are the maths of constraint.


The symbols < and > were introduced by the English mathematician Thomas Harriot in his posthumous Artis Analyticae Praxis (1631). Earlier writers had used clumsier phrases or ad-hoc notations. The “open mouth always faces the larger number” mnemonic — small point toward the small number — is exactly Harriot’s design intent. The non-strict forms ≤ and ≥ came later, when 19th-century analysts needed to talk about boundaries that were included.


An equation pins down a single point on the number line; an inequality shades a half-line. Solving means finding which side — and the only move that surprises is multiplying by a negative, which flips the direction.

title: 03 · Linear Inequalities
text:
Name

A linear inequality in one variable looks like ax + b \ < \ c, \quad ax + b \le c, \quad ax + b > c, \quad ax + b \ge c.
The solution set is every value of x that makes it true — a half-line on the number line.
Solving rules:

Add or subtract the same number on both sides — direction unchanged.
Multiply or divide both sides by a positive number — direction unchanged.
Multiply or divide both sides by a negative number — flip the inequality.

The strict inequalities <, > exclude the boundary point (open circle on the number line). The non-strict ≤, ≥ include it (filled circle).


Same rules as equations, with one twist: Multiply or divide by a negative — flip the sign. Strict inequalities exclude the boundary; non-strict include it.

title: 03 · Linear Inequalities
text:
Apply

Worked example

Solve -3(x + 1) > 6.

A competent solver:

Distribute: -3x - 3 > 6.
Add 3: -3x > 9.
Divide by -3 — flip! — : x < -3.

Answer: x < -3, the half-line strictly less than -3.
Check with one value: x = -5 < -3 ✓. -3(-5 + 1) = -3 \cdot -4 = 12 >
6 ✓.


Practice — Warm
W1. Solve: x - 4 < 2.


Full solution

x < 6.

W2. Solve: 5x \ge 35.


Full solution

x \ge 7.

W3. Solve: -x \le 3.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Multiply both sides by -1 — flip!



Full solution

x \ge -3.



Practice — Working
P1. Solve 2x + 7 < 19.


Full solution

2x < 12 \Rightarrow x < 6.

P2. Solve -4x + 1 \ge 9.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Subtract 1; then divide by -4 (flip!).



Full solution

-4x \ge 8 \Rightarrow x \le -2.

P3. A taxi charges Rs. 30 base + Rs. 15/km. You have Rs. 200. What is the maximum distance you can travel?


Hint 1 — a nudge

30 + 15d \le 200.



Full solution

30 + 15d \le 200 \Rightarrow 15d \le 170 \Rightarrow d \le \tfrac{170}{15}
\approx 11.33 km.
Round down (you can’t pay for fractional km in this scenario): max 11 km, leaving Rs. 5 unused.
The “round down” choice came from real-world context, not algebra. The algebra gave the bound; you picked the integer.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Solve the compound inequality -3 < 2x + 1 \le 7.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Solve “-3 < 2x + 1” and “2x + 1 \le 7” — both must hold. Equivalently, do operations on all three parts simultaneously.



Full solution

Subtract 1 from all three parts: -4 < 2x \le 6. Divide by 2: -2 < x
\le 3.
The solution is the interval (-2, 3] — strictly greater than -2, up to and including 3. Compound inequalities express bounded ranges — the natural way to write “between a and b”.

S2. A neural network’s prediction loss must be a non-negative number — and ideally as small as possible. We want L(w) < 0.01 where L depends on the model’s weights w. We don’t know L explicitly, but we know L(w) \ge 0 for all w. What does the inequality 0 \le
L(w) < 0.01 describe in plain words?


Full solution

“The loss is non-negative AND less than 0.01” — i.e., the loss is in the interval [0, 0.01). This is the acceptance condition for a trained model: training succeeds when the loss lies in this region.
Inequalities (and their generalisation, constraints) are the language of optimisation problems — and ML training is the largest optimisation problem the world has ever computed.



Common confusions

“Inequalities have one answer.” No — they have a region. Multiple values (often infinitely many) satisfy them.
“The inequality always points the same way after manipulation.” No — multiplying or dividing by a negative number flips it.
“Strict and non-strict are the same.” x > 3 excludes x = 3; x \ge 3 includes it. The boundary matters in constraints — especially in optimisation.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A linear inequality picks out a region on the number line, not a single value.
Solve it like an equation, with one new rule: multiplying or dividing by a negative number flips the inequality sign.
The world’s “at most” and “at least” wording almost always hides an inequality — they are the maths of constraint.



Self-Assessment

Could you explain why multiplying both sides by a negative flips the inequality, using the number-line picture?
Without scrolling back, can you solve -2x + 5 < 11?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: -2x < 6 \Rightarrow x > -3.)

3 yes: ready for 04 · The Coordinate Plane.
2 yes: redo S1 (compound inequalities).
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 — the sign-flip rule is the inequality-specific gotcha.

title: 03 · Linear Inequalities
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

02 · Linear Equations — same fundamental move; same strategy.
T1 L10 — Negative Numbers — the sign-flip rule comes from how negation reflects across zero.



What this unlocks

Tier 3 — Quadratic Inequalities — same idea, parabolic regions.
Tier 6 — Linear Programming & Optimisation — entire systems of inequalities defining feasible regions.



Where this matters

AI / Machine Learning. Constraint satisfaction is the heart of optimisation. Linear programming (an entire branch of operations research) handles thousands of inequalities at once. Modern ML uses constraint-based formulations everywhere.


Everywhere else. Budgets (“don’t spend more than X”), capacity limits, deadlines, regulatory thresholds, fitness goals (“at least X steps per day”) — the moment a problem says “at most” or “at least”, an inequality is hiding in the wording.

title: 08 · Scientific Notation
text:
Have you done 07 · Exponents and T1 L08 · Decimals?
Can you compute 10^3 = 1000 and 10^{-2} = 0.01?
Have you seen something like “1.5 × 10⁸” on a science textbook?

title: 08 · Scientific Notation
08 · Scientific Notation
text:
Wonder

Set mantissa to 1 and power to 100. The number is enormous. Could you even write it out longhand?
Set mantissa to 6.022 and power to 23. That’s Avogadro’s number — the count of atoms in 12 g of carbon-12. Why would you want notation for such a number?
Set the power to -9. What is “1 nanometre” as 10^{-9} metre?

title: 08 · Scientific Notation
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Write in normal form:

4 \times 10^3
5.7 \times 10^5
2 \times 10^{-3}



Full solution


4000. (b) 570{,}000. (c) 0.002.

Each step in the exponent moves the decimal point one place. Positive exponent → move right (bigger number). Negative exponent → move left (smaller number).



Problem 2
Convert to scientific notation:

73{,}000{,}000
0.000045
1{,}234



Hint 1 — a nudge

The mantissa must be in [1, 10). Move the decimal point to make this true; count how many places.



Full solution


7.3 \times 10^7. (b) 4.5 \times 10^{-5}. (c) 1.234 \times 10^3.

Count the places carefully — the most common error is being off by one.



Problem 3
Multiply: (2 \times 10^4) \times (3 \times 10^5).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Multiply mantissas; add exponents.



Full solution

(2 \times 3) \times 10^{4+5} = 6 \times 10^9.
Scientific notation makes multiplication of huge numbers trivial. 200{,}000 \times 30{,}000 = 6{,}000{,}000{,}000 — you’d have to count zeros without it.



Problem 4
Multiply: (5 \times 10^4) \times (4 \times 10^3).


Hint 1 — a nudge

5 \times 4 = 20, but the mantissa must be < 10. So renormalise.



Full solution

(5 \times 4) \times 10^{4+3} = 20 \times 10^7. Renormalise: 20 = 2
\times 10^1, so result = 2 \times 10^8.
Always finish by ensuring the mantissa is in [1, 10). The “carry” from mantissa to exponent is just place-value bookkeeping.



Problem 5
The mass of an electron is about 9.1 \times 10^{-31} kg. The mass of the Sun is about 2 \times 10^{30} kg. By what factor is the Sun heavier than the electron?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Divide. Mantissas divide; exponents subtract.



Full solution

\dfrac{2 \times 10^{30}}{9.1 \times 10^{-31}} = \dfrac{2}{9.1} \times
10^{30 - (-31)} = 0.22 \times 10^{61} \approx 2.2 \times 10^{60}.
The Sun is about 2.2 \times 10^{60} times as heavy as an electron. Numbers like this — spanning 60 orders of magnitude — are literally unwritable without scientific notation.


Archimedes (3rd century BCE), in his short work The Sand Reckoner (Psammites), set out to count the number of grains of sand needed to fill the universe. The Greek number system topped out at the “myriad-myriad” (10^8), so Archimedes invented his own system of orders of magnitude — essentially a powers-of-10^8 scheme — and estimated the answer at around 10^{63} grains. Two and a half thousand years before “scientific notation” got its name, he was already doing it.


Scientific notation splits a number into its shape (the mantissa, between 1 and 10) and its size (the power of 10). Once split, you can compare and compute across 60 orders of magnitude as easily as you compare 3 and 7.

title: 08 · Scientific Notation
text:
Name

Scientific notation writes a number as a \times 10^n with 1 \le a < 10 and n an integer.

a is the mantissa (or significand).
n is the exponent (or order of magnitude).

Operations:

Multiply: mantissas multiply, exponents add. Renormalise if mantissa ≥ 10.
Divide: mantissas divide, exponents subtract.
Add/subtract: convert to the same exponent first, then add the mantissas.

The notation lets you compare orders of magnitude just by looking at the exponent.


Mantissa between one and ten; exponent says how big or small; Multiply — add exponents; divide — subtract; Add only after exponents agree.

title: 08 · Scientific Notation
text:
Apply

Worked example

Compute (3 \times 10^7) + (4 \times 10^6).

A competent solver puts both terms in the same exponent first:

4 \times 10^6 = 0.4 \times 10^7.
Now add the mantissas: 3 \times 10^7 + 0.4 \times 10^7 = 3.4 \times
10^7.

Answer: 3.4 \times 10^7. Addition (and subtraction) requires matched exponents — analogous to needing a common denominator for fractions (Tier 1 Lesson 7).


Practice — Warm
W1. Write in normal form: 1.2 \times 10^4, 7 \times 10^{-2}.


Full solution

12{,}000, 0.07.

W2. Write in scientific notation: 345{,}000, 0.0006.


Full solution

3.45 \times 10^5, 6 \times 10^{-4}.

W3. Compute (3 \times 10^4) \times (2 \times 10^3).


Full solution

6 \times 10^7.



Practice — Working
P1. Compute (8 \times 10^5) \div (2 \times 10^2).


Full solution

\dfrac{8}{2} \times 10^{5-2} = 4 \times 10^3.

P2. Compute (7 \times 10^4) + (3 \times 10^5).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Match exponents.



Full solution

7 \times 10^4 = 0.7 \times 10^5. Add: 0.7 + 3 = 3.7. Answer: 3.7 \times 10^5 = 370{,}000.

P3. The distance from Earth to the Sun is about 1.5 \times 10^{11} m. Light travels 3 \times 10^8 m/s. How long does light from the Sun take to reach Earth?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Time = distance ÷ speed.



Full solution

\dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{11}}{3 \times 10^8} = 0.5 \times 10^{3} = 500 seconds, or about 8.3 minutes.
Sunlight you see right now left the Sun about 8 minutes ago. Scientific notation makes that calculation a one-liner.



Practice — Stretch
S1. A neural network has 1 trillion parameters (10^{12}). Each parameter is stored as a 32-bit floating-point number (4 bytes). What is the total storage required, in gigabytes (10^9 bytes)?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Total bytes = 10^{12} \times 4. Divide by 10^9.



Full solution

Bytes = 4 \times 10^{12}. Gigabytes = 4 \times 10^{12} / 10^9 = 4
\times 10^3 = 4000 GB = 4 terabytes.
Modern AI models routinely require terabytes of storage. The largest research models in 2025 have over 10^{13} parameters — tens of terabytes just for the weights.
This is why GPU memory and high-bandwidth networking matter so much: moving weights around is the bottleneck of large-scale training.

S2. Floating-point on a computer is itself essentially scientific notation — a mantissa and an exponent stored separately. A 32-bit float uses 23 bits for mantissa and 8 bits for exponent (plus 1 for sign). What is the largest representable number, roughly?


Hint 1 — a nudge

With 8 exponent bits, the exponent can range up to about ±127. The maximum value is mantissa × 2^{127}.



Full solution

\approx 2^{127} \approx 1.7 \times 10^{38}. The standard IEEE 754 single-precision range is from about \pm 10^{-38} to \pm 10^{38}.
This is why scientific computing uses double-precision (64-bit) for work that needs greater range or accuracy — exponent fields of 11 bits, allowing values up to \sim 10^{308}.
Floating-point representation is applied scientific notation in silicon. The same mathematics you just learned governs how every calculator, phone, and supercomputer stores fractional numbers.



Common confusions

“1.5 \times 10^3 is bigger than 1.5 \times 10^{-3} because the first has a positive exponent.” True here, but mantissas matter. 9 \times 10^3 vs 1 \times 10^4: the exponent of the second is bigger, so it wins (10000 > 9000).
“You can add scientific-notation numbers by adding both parts.” No — you must align exponents first, then add only mantissas.
“Scientific notation is just a fancy way to show big numbers.” It also works for tiny numbers (negative exponents) and for any number whose order of magnitude is unintuitive.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

Scientific notation writes any number as a \times 10^n with 1 \le a < 10 — a shape and a size, kept separate.
Multiply mantissas and add exponents; divide mantissas and subtract exponents; align exponents before adding or subtracting.
The exponent tells you the order of magnitude — that one number lets you compare things 60 zeros apart at a glance.



Self-Assessment

Could you compute (2 \times 10^5) \times (5 \times 10^{-3}) in your head?
Without scrolling back, can you convert 0.0042 to scientific notation?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answers: 10^3 = 1000; 4.2 \times 10^{-3}.)

3 yes: ready for 09 · Square Roots and Irrationals.
2 yes: redo S1 — billions and trillions of parameters in ML systems is now standard.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P5.

title: 08 · Scientific Notation
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

07 · Exponents and Powers
T1 L08 — Decimals



What this unlocks

Tier 3 — Logarithms — the inverse of exponentials; pH, decibels, Richter scale.



Where this matters

Systems. Floating-point numbers (Stretch S2) are scientific notation in binary. Numerical precision, range, overflow, and underflow are all consequences.


Mathematical research. Almost every constant in physics is quoted in scientific notation: Planck’s constant (6.6 \times 10^{-34}), gravitational constant (6.7 \times 10^{-11}), speed of light (3 \times 10^8), etc.


AI / Machine Learning. Model parameter counts (10^{12}), training compute (FLOPs \sim 10^{24}), dataset sizes (10^{14} tokens) — all use scientific notation, often via the abbreviations “trillion,” “exa-,” etc.


Everywhere else. Astronomy (light-years, parsecs), biology (cell counts, gene lengths), economics (national budgets, populations), pharmacology (microgram doses) — any field with extreme scales.

title: 07 · Exponents and Powers
text:
Have you done T1 L05 — Multiplication as Area?
Can you compute 3 \times 3 \times 3 = 27? 5 \times 5 = 25?
Comfortable with the notation 5^2 = 25 (“five squared”) from T1 L13?

title: 07 · Exponents and Powers
07 · Exponents and Powers
text:
Wonder

What is 2^0? 5^0? Should it be 0, or 1, or something else?
What is 3^1? 7^1?
What about 2^{-1}? Does that make any sense?

title: 07 · Exponents and Powers
text:
Discover

Problem 1
Compute (without a calculator): 2^3, 3^2, 5^3, 10^4.


Full solution

2^3 = 8, 3^2 = 9, 5^3 = 125, 10^4 = 10{,}000.
Notice 2^3 \neq 3^2 — the base and exponent are not interchangeable. Order matters.



Problem 2
Compute 2^3 \times 2^4 in two ways:

Compute each, then multiply.
Just count the 2’s.



Full solution


2^3 = 8, 2^4 = 16. Product: 128.
$2^3 ^4 = (2 ) (2 


= 2^7 = 128$. ✓

General rule: a^m \times a^n = a^{m+n}. Multiplying same-base powers means adding exponents. Why? Because you’re just stacking the two chains of multiplication into one longer chain.



Problem 3
Compute \dfrac{2^7}{2^4}.


Full solution

\dfrac{2^7}{2^4} = \dfrac{2 \cdot 2 \cdot 2 \cdot 2 \cdot 2 \cdot 2 \cdot 2}{2 \cdot 2 \cdot 2 \cdot 2}. Cancel four 2’s from top and bottom: = 2^3 = 8.
General rule: \dfrac{a^m}{a^n} = a^{m - n}. Dividing same-base powers means subtracting exponents.



Problem 4
What should a^0 be? Use the division rule from P3: \dfrac{a^n}{a^n} should be both a^{n-n} = a^0 AND should be 1 (anything divided by itself is 1).


Full solution

\dfrac{a^n}{a^n} = a^0, but also \dfrac{a^n}{a^n} = 1. So a^0 = 1 for any a \neq 0.
This is forced by the rules. a^0 = 1 isn’t an arbitrary convention — it’s the only choice that keeps the division rule consistent.



Problem 5
What should a^{-1} be? Use the rule a^m \times a^n = a^{m+n} with m = 1, n = -1: this gives a^1 \times a^{-1} = a^0 = 1. So a^{-1} is the number that, multiplied by a, gives 1.


Hint 1 — a nudge

a \times \text{(what)} = 1?



Full solution

a^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{a} — the reciprocal of a.
So 2^{-1} = 0.5, 3^{-1} = 1/3, 10^{-3} = 1/1000 = 0.001.
Negative exponents extend powers to fractions. Combined with a^0 =
1, you now have a meaning for a^n for any integer n — positive, zero, or negative.


The compact “raised number” notation for exponents — writing a^3 instead of aaa — was popularised by René Descartes in La Géométrie (1637), the same book that gave us the coordinate plane. Before Descartes, mathematicians wrote out the multiplications in full or used special words (“cube,” “quadrato-quadratum”) for each power. The little superscript looks trivial today, but it was a notational revolution: a whole rule like a^m \cdot a^n = a^{m+n} becomes obvious once the exponent is visible as a number.


An exponent is a counter for how many copies of the base get multiplied together. Once you see it that way, every law of exponents — including the strange-looking a^0 = 1 and a^{-n} = 1/a^n — is just bookkeeping for that count.

title: 07 · Exponents and Powers
text:
Name

For a base a and a positive integer n, the power a^n = \underbrace{a \times a \times \cdots \times a}_{n \text{ times}}.
Extended:

a^0 = 1 (for a \neq 0).
a^{-n} = \dfrac{1}{a^n}.

Laws of exponents:







Law
Statement




Product
a^m \cdot a^n = a^{m+n}


Quotient
\dfrac{a^m}{a^n} = a^{m-n}


Power of a power
(a^m)^n = a^{m \cdot n}


Power of a product
(ab)^n = a^n \cdot b^n


Power of a quotient
\left(\dfrac{a}{b}\right)^n = \dfrac{a^n}{b^n}



Each law is provable by counting copies of a in the multiplication.


Multiply same-base powers — add the exponents; Divide same-base powers — subtract the exponents; Anything to the zero is one; negative exponents flip to reciprocals.

title: 07 · Exponents and Powers
text:
Apply

Worked example

Simplify \dfrac{2^5 \cdot 2^3}{2^6}.

A competent solver applies the laws step by step:

Numerator: 2^5 \cdot 2^3 = 2^{5+3} = 2^8.
Quotient: \dfrac{2^8}{2^6} = 2^{8-6} = 2^2 = 4.

Answer: 4. Check: numerator = 32 \cdot 8 = 256; denominator = 64; quotient = 4 ✓.


Practice — Warm
W1. Compute: 4^2, 10^3, 2^6.


Full solution

16, 1000, 64.

W2. Simplify: a^3 \cdot a^4, \dfrac{x^7}{x^2}, (b^2)^5.


Full solution

a^7, x^5, b^{10}.

W3. Compute: 5^0, 3^{-1}, 2^{-3}.


Full solution

1, 1/3, 1/8.



Practice — Working
P1. Simplify: \dfrac{x^5 \cdot x^3}{x^4}.


Full solution

x^{5+3-4} = x^4.

P2. Simplify: (2x^3)^2.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Apply (ab)^n = a^n b^n.



Full solution

2^2 \cdot (x^3)^2 = 4 x^6.

P3. A bacterial colony doubles every hour. Starting with 100 bacteria, how many after 8 hours?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Each hour multiplies by 2. After n hours, there are 100 \times 2^n.



Full solution

100 \times 2^8 = 100 \times 256 = 25{,}600 bacteria.
Exponential growth — each step multiplies, not adds — produces huge numbers fast. Compound interest, viral spread (epidemiology and internet “going viral”), Moore’s Law for computing power: all are exponential.



Practice — Stretch
S1. A standard chess board has 64 squares. A legend says a king offered a wise man any reward; the wise man asked for 1 grain of rice on the first square, 2 on the second, 4 on the third, doubling each time. The king laughed and agreed. How much rice on the 64th square alone? How does that compare to the world rice harvest of ~500 million tonnes per year?


Hint 1 — a nudge

On square n, there are 2^{n-1} grains. Square 64: 2^{63}.



Full solution

2^{63} \approx 9.2 \times 10^{18} grains. At about 25 grains per gram, that’s \approx 3.7 \times 10^{17} g = 3.7 \times 10^{14} kg = 370 trillion kg = 370 billion tonnes — about 700 years of the world’s entire rice production, for one square.
The total over all 64 squares is 2^{64} - 1, roughly twice this number. The king lost.
This is the central lesson about exponential growth: it always wins against intuition. Whoever underestimates it gets blindsided. Modelling pandemics, computing the energy impact of a small percentage change in efficiency, predicting AI capabilities — all require taking exponentials seriously.

S2. In computer storage: 1 KB = 1024 bytes, not 1000 bytes (though hard-drive marketers use both definitions). Why 1024?


Hint 1 — a nudge

1024 = 2^?



Full solution

1024 = 2^{10}. Computer memory is binary, organised in powers of 2. The “round number” of 1024 is exactly 2^{10}, the closest power of 2 to 1000. Larger units: 1 MB = 2^{20}, 1 GB = 2^{30}.
There is now a separate notation: KiB, MiB, GiB (kibibyte, mebibyte, gibibyte) for the powers-of-2 versions, and KB, MB, GB for the powers-of-10 versions. The discrepancy is why “your 1 TB hard drive” shows as ~931 GiB in the operating system.
Powers of 2 are the native counting system of binary computers, just as powers of 10 are the native system of decimal humans.



Common confusions

“a^n means a \times n.” No — it means a multiplied by itself n times. 2^3 = 8, not 6.
“0^0 = 0 or = 1?” Convention varies. In most contexts, 0^0 = 1 (consistent with a^0 = 1 for a \neq 0 and useful in combinatorics and series). Some textbooks call it undefined. Tier 1 doesn’t need this; Tier 4+ analyses it carefully.
“(a + b)^2 = a^2 + b^2.” False. Common error. The correct expansion is (a + b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2 (Lesson 11). Confusing “exponent of a sum” with “sum of exponents” trips up countless students.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

An exponent counts copies of the base being multiplied — a^n is a multiplied by itself n times.
Multiplying same-base powers adds the exponents; dividing subtracts them — every law of exponents is just bookkeeping.
Exponential growth wins against intuition every time — the chess-board grain doubles itself into more rice than the world has ever produced.



Self-Assessment

Could you derive a^0 = 1 from the quotient rule, in your own words?
Without scrolling back, can you simplify \dfrac{x^4 \cdot x^{-1}}{x^2}?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: x^{4-1-2} = x^1 = x.)

3 yes: ready for 08 · Scientific Notation.
2 yes: redo S1 (chessboard rice). Internalise exponential growth.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P4 and P5.

title: 07 · Exponents and Powers
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

T1 L05 — Multiplication as Area — exponents are repeated multiplication.



What this unlocks

08 · Scientific Notation — expressing very large/small numbers as a \times 10^n.
09 · Square Roots and Irrationals — the inverse: which number, raised to the power 2, gives this?
11 · Polynomials — expressions with variable powers.



Where this matters

Systems. Powers of 2 are the natural language of binary computers (Stretch S2). RAM, addresses, file sizes, network packet limits — all in powers of 2.


Encryption. RSA computes a^e \bmod n for very large a, e, n. The size of the exponent is what makes the security tunable.


AI / Machine Learning. Network parameters scale with powers of 2; training compute requirements grow with model size as a power law. GPT-style models double in parameter count every ~12 months — pure exponential growth.


Everywhere else. Compound interest, population growth, radioactive decay (negative exponential), Richter scale (logarithmic, the inverse of exponential), pH scale, decibels — all use exponents.

title: 05 · Linear Functions and Their Graphs
text:
Have you done 01 · Functions as Machines and 04 · The Coordinate Plane?
Can you evaluate y = 2x + 5 for x = 0, 1, 2?
Can you plot a point like (3, 4) on graph paper?

title: 05 · Linear Functions and Their Graphs
05 · Linear Functions and Their Graphs
text:
Wonder

Set m = 0. What kind of line do you get? Why?
Set m = 1, b = 0. What is the line? What is its equation?
Set m = 2. Now compare the line to m = -2. What changes?

title: 05 · Linear Functions and Their Graphs
text:
Discover

Problem 1
For the function y = 2x + 1, fill in the table:



x
y




-2
?


-1
?


0
?


1
?


2
?



Plot the points. What do you notice?


Full solution

y = -3, -1, 1, 3, 5.
The five points all lie on a single straight line. Every linear function y = mx + b has a graph that is a straight line — that is why it’s called “linear”.



Problem 2
For y = 2x + 1, what is the value of y when x = 0? When x = 1? By how much does y increase when x increases by 1?


Full solution

y(0) = 1, y(1) = 3. Increase: 3 - 1 = 2.
The increase per unit x is the slope. For y = 2x + 1, the slope is 2 — and indeed y jumps by 2 every time x jumps by 1. This is the geometric meaning of the coefficient of x.



Problem 3
Two points are given: (1, 3) and (4, 9). Compute the slope of the line connecting them.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Slope = “rise over run” = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} =
\dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}.



Full solution

m = \dfrac{9 - 3}{4 - 1} = \dfrac{6}{3} = 2.
The slope formula is just change in y divided by change in x — “rise over run”. Two points are enough to specify a line; the slope between them is fixed by simple arithmetic.



Problem 4
A line has slope m = 3 and passes through the point (0, -2). Write its equation.


Full solution

The point (0, -2) is the y-intercept (it has x = 0), so b = -2. Equation: y = 3x - 2.
When you know the slope and a point on the y-axis, you have m and b directly.



Problem 5
Two lines: y = 2x + 3 and y = 2x - 1. What is true of these lines?
What about y = 2x + 3 and y = -\tfrac{1}{2}x + 3?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Try plotting (in your head). What’s the same? What’s different?



Full solution

y = 2x + 3 and y = 2x - 1: same slope (2), different intercepts — parallel lines. They never meet, like railway tracks.
y = 2x + 3 and y = -\tfrac{1}{2}x + 3: slopes are 2 and -\tfrac{1}{2}. Their product is -1. Such lines meet at right angles — perpendicular lines. (Both pass through (0, 3) here, so they cross at right angles there.)
Rule: parallel = same slope; perpendicular = slopes multiply to -1. The geometry of crossings is encoded entirely in slope arithmetic.


The “slope-intercept” picture you just used is a direct descendant of Descartes’ coordinate geometry (1637, see Lesson 4). But the words slope and intercept themselves come from English textbook tradition of the 18th and 19th centuries. The letter m for slope is an American convention with no widely-agreed origin story — most plausibly from the French monter (“to climb”). Different countries pick different letters: many European books write y = ax + b instead of y = mx + b.


Two numbers — slope and intercept — pin down a whole infinite line. The slope tells you the line’s tilt; the intercept tells you where it sits. Change one and the line tips; change the other and it slides.

title: 05 · Linear Functions and Their Graphs
text:
Name

A linear function has the form y = mx + b where m is the slope and b is the y-intercept. Its graph is a straight line.

Slope m: how steep the line is — the change in y per unit change in x. Computed from any two points: m =
\dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}.
y-intercept b: the value of y when x = 0 — where the line crosses the y-axis.

Special cases:

m = 0: horizontal line y = b.
m undefined (vertical line x = a): not a function (one x has many y).

Two lines:

Parallel \iff same slope.
Perpendicular \iff slopes multiply to -1.



Slope is the climb per step rightward; Intercept is where the line crosses the y-axis; Two points determine a line — the slope between them is its tilt.

title: 05 · Linear Functions and Their Graphs
text:
Apply

Worked example

Find the equation of the line passing through (2, 1) and (5, 7).

A competent solver finds the slope first, then uses one point to find the intercept.

Slope: m = \dfrac{7 - 1}{5 - 2} = \dfrac{6}{3} = 2.
Use point-slope form y - y_1 = m(x - x_1) with (2, 1): y - 1 = 2(x - 2) = 2x - 4. So y = 2x - 3.
Check: y(5) = 10 - 3 = 7 ✓.

Equation: y = 2x - 3.


Practice — Warm
W1. Slope of the line through (0, 0) and (3, 6)?


Full solution

m = 6/3 = 2.

W2. y-intercept of y = -3x + 7?


Full solution

b = 7.

W3. Plot 3 points on the line y = -x + 4.


Full solution

(0, 4), (1, 3), (4, 0) — three points, all collinear (on a line).



Practice — Working
P1. Find the equation of the line through (0, -2) with slope 3.


Full solution

b = -2, m = 3. Equation: y = 3x - 2.

P2. A taxi cost function: C(d) = 30 + 15d. What is the y-intercept, and what does it mean? What is the slope, and what does it mean?


Full solution

y-intercept = 30 = the cost when d = 0 km — the base fare. Slope = 15 = cost increase per additional km — the rate.
Real-world linear functions almost always have meaningful intercept and slope. Identifying them is half the modelling skill.

P3. Find the equation of the line through (1, 5) and (4, 11).


Full solution

m = (11-5)/(4-1) = 2. Using point (1, 5): y - 5 = 2(x - 1) \Rightarrow
y = 2x + 3.



Practice — Stretch
S1. A linear function f(x) = mx + b is fitted to the data:



x
1
2
3
4
5




y
5
7
9
11
13



Find m and b by eye.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Each step in x adds the same amount to y.



Full solution

y goes up by 2 for each step in x, so m = 2. Extrapolate to x =
0: y(0) = 5 - 2 = 3, so b = 3.
f(x) = 2x + 3.
This is the simplest case of linear regression — fitting a line to data — which underpins much of statistics and the simplest neural network.

S2. A neural network with one input has the form \hat{y} = wx + b — a linear function! What does training mean for it?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Compare with what you did in S1.



Full solution

Training a one-input neural network is finding the slope w and intercept b that best fit the training data. Exactly the same task as in S1, just done by an iterative algorithm (gradient descent) instead of by eye.
The “w” is called a weight in ML; the “b” is the bias. But they’re just the slope and intercept of a line. The Tier 2 picture is literally the inside of every neural unit (before the activation function gets involved in Tier 5+).



Common confusions

“Linear means a line.” Yes for one input. With two inputs, “linear” means a plane in 3D (z = ax + by + c). With many inputs, a hyperplane.
“b is always positive.” b can be any real number. Negative b means the line crosses below the origin.
“The slope of a vertical line is infinite.” Strictly: a vertical line is not a function in y = f(x) form (since one x has many y). Slope is undefined for vertical lines.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A linear function y = mx + b has a straight-line graph — that’s literally why it’s called linear.
The slope m is rise over run; the y-intercept b is where the line crosses the y-axis. Together they fix the line.
Same slope means parallel; slopes multiplying to -1 means perpendicular — geometry encoded as arithmetic.



Self-Assessment

Could you sketch y = -2x + 3 on graph paper, identifying its slope and y-intercept?
Without scrolling back, can you find the equation of the line through (0, 4) with slope -1?
Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 Discover problems and 4 of the 6 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?

(Answer: y = -x + 4.)

3 yes: ready for 06 · Systems of Linear Equations.
2 yes: redo S2 — the neural-network connection is the punchline.
0–1 yes: re-read Discover P3 and P5.

title: 05 · Linear Functions and Their Graphs
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

01 · Functions as Machines
04 · The Coordinate Plane



What this unlocks

06 · Systems of Linear Equations — two lines, when do they meet, where, how often.
Tier 4 — Calculus — the slope of a curve at a point is its derivative; calculus generalises slope to non-linear functions.
Tier 5 — Linear Algebra — the high-dimensional analogue: hyperplanes, matrices, eigenvectors.



Where this matters

AI / Machine Learning. Every neuron in a neural network is a linear function (followed by a non-linearity). Training is finding the right slopes and intercepts. The simplest ML model — linear regression — is exactly what Stretch S1 did.


Game development & graphics. Every transformation (translation, scaling) is built from linear functions. Every line, every edge of a mesh, every interpolation between two states uses the slope-intercept idea.


Mathematical research. Linearity is one of the most studied concepts in mathematics. Linear algebra (Tier 5) is the most reused toolkit in applied maths.

title: Ch 5 — Regular Polyhedra and Similar Solids
text:
Are prism, pyramid, sphere formulas in your active memory? See kg2.polyhedra and kg2.sphere.
Can you state the similarity-area-cube rule from Planimetry’s similarity chapter?
Are you comfortable with the names cube, tetrahedron, octahedron — even if you’ve never met dodecahedron or icosahedron?
Can you compute the interior angle of a regular pentagon (108°)?

If three yeses, proceed.
crumbs: Ch 5 — Regular Polyhedra and Similar Solids

title: Ch 5 — Regular Polyhedra and Similar Solids
crumbs: Ch 5 — Regular Polyhedra and Similar Solids

title: Ch 5 — Regular Polyhedra and Similar Solids
text:
Wonder

Why exactly five regular polyhedra? Could there be a sixth?
The five Platonic solids have V - E + F = 2. Is this special to them, or true for any convex polyhedron?
Two solids similar with linear ratio k. The volumes are in ratio k^3, which everyone knows. Why k^3 and not, say, k^2 or k^{2.5}?
The square-cube law: a giant ant cannot exist. What does that have to do with the geometry of similar solids?







TipWhat you might notice






The proof of the 5-Platonic-solids theorem — that no sixth exists — is one of the most elegant in geometry; it falls out of V - E + F = 2 and the angle constraint at each vertex. (2) Euler’s formula holds for every convex polyhedron — and indeed for every polyhedron that is topologically a sphere. (3) Volume is length cubed because volume is a triple integral — three length-dimensions, one each for x, y, z. (4) Doubling a creature’s size multiplies its mass (∝ volume) by 8 but its leg-cross-section (∝ area) by only 4. The legs cannot bear the doubled weight.
crumbs: Ch 5 — Regular Polyhedra and Similar Solids

title: Ch 5 — Regular Polyhedra and Similar Solids
text:
Discover

Problem 1 — Definition of regular polyhedron
A regular polyhedron is a convex polyhedron such that: (a) every face is the same regular polygon; (b) the same number of faces meet at every vertex. Show that this also forces all dihedral angles to be equal and all edges to be the same length.


Full solution

By (a), all face edges have the same length (regular faces of the same shape). Since adjacent faces share an edge, all edges of the polyhedron have the same length.
For dihedral angles: at any edge, two faces meet. By (a) and the constancy of vertex configuration, the local geometry near every edge is congruent to the local geometry near every other edge — hence the dihedral angle is the same. QED. (Stereometry §492.)



Problem 2 — Euler’s formula
Show that for any convex polyhedron with V vertices, E edges, F faces, V - E + F = 2.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Cauchy’s proof. Remove one face, flatten the remaining shell onto the plane (no creasing — pretend it’s elastic). The flattened result is a connected planar graph on V vertices, E edges, and F - 1 “regions” (the missing face becomes the outside). Apply Euler’s formula for planar graphs: V - E + (F - 1) = 1, i.e. V - E + F = 2.



Full solution

Cauchy’s proof, sketched (Stereometry §494):
Remove one face from the polyhedron, leaving a shell with a hole. Stretch the shell flat onto the plane — the hole becomes the infinite region. The result is a connected planar graph with V vertices, E edges, and F - 1 bounded regions plus one infinite region (= F total regions, but the infinite one is the “removed” face we had taken away).
For any connected planar graph, Euler’s formula for planar graphs asserts V - E + F_{\text{regions}} = 2. Since F_{\text{regions}} = F here (counting the infinite region in place of the removed face), we get V - E + F = 2.
The planar-graph version of Euler’s formula is proved by induction: on a single edge, 2 - 1 + 1 = 2. Adding an edge that creates a new region preserves V - E + F (loses 1 from E, gains 1 from F). Adding an edge that connects to a new vertex preserves it too (gains 1 from V, gains 1 from E, no new region). QED. (Stereometry §494 — the celebrated Euler-Cauchy formula.)



Problem 3 — Only five regular polyhedra
Suppose every face is a regular p-gon and exactly q faces meet at each vertex. Show that (p, q) must be one of the five pairs: (3, 3), (4, 3), (3, 4), (5, 3), (3, 5).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Vertex-angle constraint: the q face-angles meeting at a vertex have measures summing to less than 360° (since the polyhedron is convex). Each face-angle is the interior angle of a regular p-gon, (p - 2) \cdot 180° / p.



Full solution

At each vertex, q regular p-gon corners meet, total angle q \cdot \dfrac{(p - 2) \cdot 180°}{p} < 360° (convexity). Simplify: q (p - 2) < 2p, i.e. \dfrac{1}{p} + \dfrac{1}{q} >
\dfrac{1}{2}.
Both p \ge 3 and q \ge 3 (need at least 3 sides per face, at least 3 faces per vertex). Solutions:



(p, q)
\tfrac{1}{p} + \tfrac{1}{q}
Solid




(3, 3)
\tfrac{2}{3} > 1/2
Tetrahedron


(3, 4)
\tfrac{7}{12}
Octahedron


(3, 5)
\tfrac{8}{15}
Icosahedron


(4, 3)
\tfrac{7}{12}
Cube


(5, 3)
\tfrac{8}{15}
Dodecahedron


(3, 6)
\tfrac{1}{2} — fails
(degenerate flat tiling)


(4, 4)
\tfrac{1}{2} — fails
(square tiling)


(6, 3)
\tfrac{1}{2} — fails
(hex tiling)



Five — no more. QED. (Stereometry §493 — the Platonic classification. Plato’s Timaeus* names them; Euclid’s Elements proves the count in Book XIII.)*



Problem 4 — Volume of similar solids scales as k^3
Two similar solids have linear ratio k. Show that their volumes are in ratio k^3.


Hint 1 — a nudge

For cubes, scaling each side by k multiplies volume by k^3. For arbitrary solids, decompose into small cubes and take a limit (a 3D analogue of the polygon-into-triangles argument from Ch 9 of Planimetry).



Full solution

Cube case. Cube of side s: volume s^3. Cube scaled by k: volume (ks)^3 = k^3 s^3.
General case. Approximate the solid by a union of small cubes of side \epsilon. Volume of the union \approx N \epsilon^3 where N is the cube count. The scaled solid is approximated by the same union of cubes, each scaled to side k\epsilon — total volume \approx N (k\epsilon)^3 = k^3 N \epsilon^3. The ratio is k^3 for every \epsilon; in the limit \epsilon \to 0, the ratio remains k^3. QED. (Stereometry §502 — the cube-of- linear-ratio scaling.)



Problem 5 — Surface area scales as k^2
Show that the surface area of similar solids scales as k^2.


Full solution

The same argument as for area in 2D (Ch 7 of Planimetry): each face scales by k^2 in area, and surface is the sum of face areas. QED.



Problem 6 — Volumes of the Platonic solids
Compute the volumes of all five Platonic solids of edge length 1, in closed form.


Full solution




Solid
Volume (edge = 1)




Tetrahedron
\dfrac{\sqrt 2}{12} \approx 0.1178


Cube
1


Octahedron
\dfrac{\sqrt 2}{3} \approx 0.4714


Dodecahedron
\dfrac{15 + 7\sqrt 5}{4} \approx 7.6631


Icosahedron
\dfrac{5(3 + \sqrt 5)}{12} \approx 2.1817



The tetrahedron formula was derived in Ch 2 P1. The cube is trivial. Octahedron: think of it as two square pyramids glued at their square bases — each pyramid has square base of side 1 and height \sqrt{2}/2, giving volume \sqrt 2 / 6 each, total \sqrt 2 / 3. The dodecahedron and icosahedron involve the golden ratio \varphi = (1 + \sqrt 5)/2 and require careful coordinate computation. (Stereometry §498 — see Givental for the dodecahedron and icosahedron derivations.)



Problem 7 — Square-cube law
A creature is scaled up uniformly by factor k. Mass scales by k^3 (volume). Bone cross-section scales by k^2 (area). Stress on bone (force per area) scales by what?


Full solution

Force per area $= $ (mass \cdot gravity) / (cross-section area) \propto k^3 / k^2 = k. (Stress increases linearly with size.)
A doubled creature has doubled bone stress — and bone material strength is fixed. At some scaling factor, bones break under their own weight. (Galileo, Two New Sciences, 1638 — the square-cube law explains why elephants have proportionally thicker legs than mice.)


Plato’s Timaeus (~360 BCE) assigned the five regular solids to the four classical elements and the cosmos itself: tetrahedron = fire, cube = earth, octahedron = air, icosahedron = water, dodecahedron = the quintessence of the heavens. Euclid’s Elements ends, deliberately, with Book XIII — a complete classification proving that no sixth Platonic solid exists. Two and a half millennia later, the proof you just walked through (Problem 3, the angle inequality \tfrac{1}{p} + \tfrac{1}{q} > \tfrac{1}{2}) has not been improved on. Three lines, total certainty — Euclid’s gift to every mathematician.


The whole chapter is two scaling laws chained: at every vertex of a convex polyhedron, the angles must sum to less than 360° (else the figure flattens), and at every edge the two faces fold across a dihedral angle. Combine those two constraints with V - E + F = 2 and you get exactly five — no choice, no exceptions, no more.
