title: Ch 5 — Regular Polyhedra and Similar Solids
text:
Name

Definitions.

Regular polyhedron: convex polyhedron with congruent regular polygonal faces and the same number of faces meeting at each vertex.
Schläfli symbol \{p, q\}: regular polyhedron with p-gon faces, q faces at each vertex.

The five Platonic solids.



Solid
\{p, q\}
V
E
F




Tetrahedron
\{3, 3\}
4
6
4


Cube
\{4, 3\}
8
12
6


Octahedron
\{3, 4\}
6
12
8


Dodecahedron
\{5, 3\}
20
30
12


Icosahedron
\{3, 5\}
12
30
20



Theorems (Stereometry §§491–530).

Euler’s formula: for every convex polyhedron, V - E + F = 2.
Five Platonic solids: exactly five regular polyhedra exist, characterised by \tfrac{1}{p} + \tfrac{1}{q} > \tfrac{1}{2}.
Similarity scaling: solids similar with linear ratio k have surfaces in ratio k^2 and volumes in ratio k^3.
Square-cube law: uniform scaling makes mass grow as k^3 but structural cross-section as k^2 — stresses scale as k.


A verse:

Five solids — Plato saw them, Euclid proved them, no sixth exists. Tetrahedron, cube, octahedron — and dodecahedron, icosahedron persist. V minus E plus F is two, for every convex polyhedron known. Linear k cubes the volume — the law that grows everything you’ve grown.
crumbs: Ch 5 — Regular Polyhedra and Similar Solids

title: Ch 5 — Regular Polyhedra and Similar Solids
text:
Apply

Worked example

Verify Euler’s formula for a triangular prism: a 3D shape with two triangular bases and three rectangular sides.

V = 6 (3 corners on each base), E = 9 (3 base edges per triangle, plus 3 lateral edges) — wait, E = 3 + 3 + 3 = 9. F = 5 (2 bases + 3 sides).
V - E + F = 6 - 9 + 5 = 2. ✓
Euler’s formula holds.


Practice — Warm
W1. A cube has edge length 5. Find its volume and surface area.


Full solution

V = 125, S = 6 \cdot 25 = 150.

W2. Two cubes have edge ratio 1 : 3. Find the ratio of their volumes.


Full solution

1 : 27.

W3. Verify Euler’s formula for an octahedron (V = 6, E = 12,
F = 8).


Full solution

6 - 12 + 8 = 2. ✓



Practice — Working
P1. A football (soccer ball) is made of 12 regular pentagonal patches and 20 regular hexagonal patches. Each pentagon shares its 5 edges with 5 hexagons; each hexagon shares 3 edges with pentagons and 3 with other hexagons. Compute V, E, F, and verify Euler’s formula.


Hint 1 — a nudge

F = 12 + 20 = 32. Each edge is shared by 2 faces, so E = (12 \cdot 5 + 20 \cdot 6)/2. Each vertex is shared by 3 faces (the standard truncated icosahedron configuration).



Full solution

F = 32. E = (60 + 120)/2 = 90. Vertices: each is shared by 3 faces (1 pentagon + 2 hexagons), and each face contributes its vertices counted once per shared face: V = (12 \cdot 5 + 20 \cdot 6)/3 = 60.
Check: V - E + F = 60 - 90 + 32 = 2. ✓
This is the truncated icosahedron — the world’s most famous Archimedean solid (and the geometry of fullerene C60).

P2. A regular dodecahedron has edge length a. Find the ratio of its surface area to that of a cube of edge a.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Dodecahedron: 12 regular pentagons of side a. Pentagon area: \dfrac{a^2 \sqrt{25 + 10\sqrt 5}}{4}.



Full solution

Dodecahedron surface: 12 \cdot \dfrac{a^2 \sqrt{25 + 10\sqrt 5}}{4}
= 3 a^2 \sqrt{25 + 10\sqrt 5}. Cube surface: 6 a^2. Ratio: \dfrac{3 \sqrt{25 + 10\sqrt 5}}{6} = \dfrac{\sqrt{25 + 10
\sqrt 5}}{2} \approx 3.4377. Dodecahedron has about 3.44 times the cube’s surface for the same edge.

P3. A regular tetrahedron and a cube have the same surface area. Find the ratio of their volumes.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Set surface areas equal: \sqrt 3 a_{\text{tet}}^2 = 6
a_{\text{cube}}^2, where a_{\text{tet}} is tetrahedron edge.



Full solution

\sqrt 3 a_{\text{tet}}^2 = 6 a_{\text{cube}}^2, so a_{\text{tet}}^2 / a_{\text{cube}}^2 = 6 / \sqrt 3 = 2\sqrt 3. Volumes: V_{\text{tet}} = \dfrac{a_{\text{tet}}^3 \sqrt 2}{12}, V_{\text{cube}} = a_{\text{cube}}^3. Ratio: \dfrac{V_{\text{tet}}}{V_{\text{cube}}} = \dfrac{\sqrt 2}{12}
\cdot (2\sqrt 3)^{3/2} = \dfrac{\sqrt 2}{12} \cdot 2 \sqrt{12} \cdot
\sqrt{2\sqrt 3} \approx 0.7264. Tetrahedron volume \approx
72.6\% of the cube’s when surface areas match. (Confirms intuition: the cube is “more efficient” at enclosing volume per surface; the sphere is the most efficient.)



Practice — Stretch
S1. Show that the dual of each Platonic solid (formed by placing a vertex at each face centre and connecting vertices of adjacent faces) is again a Platonic solid: - Tetrahedron ↔︎ Tetrahedron (self-dual) - Cube ↔︎ Octahedron - Dodecahedron ↔︎ Icosahedron


Hint 1 — a nudge

The dual of a polyhedron with Schläfli symbol \{p, q\} has symbol \{q, p\}.



Full solution










Original
Schläfli
Dual Schläfli
Dual solid




Tetrahedron \{3,3\}
(3, 3)
(3, 3)
Tetrahedron (self-dual)


Cube \{4,3\}
(4, 3)
(3, 4)
Octahedron


Dodecahedron \{5,3\}
(5, 3)
(3, 5)
Icosahedron



By Problem 3, \{q, p\} is also a valid Platonic Schläfli symbol iff \{p, q\} is. So the duals are all Platonic. QED. (Duality is a deep theme in geometry, group theory, and physics.)

S2. Sphere packing. The densest packing of identical spheres in 3D is the face-centred cubic (FCC) arrangement, which fills \dfrac{\pi}{3\sqrt 2} \approx 74\% of space. Verify this density for the FCC lattice.


Hint 1 — a nudge

In the FCC unit cell (a cube of side a), there are 4 spheres each of diameter a / \sqrt 2 (touching across the face diagonal). Volume of 4 spheres / volume of cube.



Full solution

FCC unit cell: a cube of edge a. Spheres at 8 corners (each contributing 1/8) plus 6 face centres (each 1/2): total 8 \cdot 1/8 + 6 \cdot 1/2 = 4 full spheres per unit cell.
Spheres touch across face diagonals: face diagonal = 4r, so a \sqrt 2 = 4r, giving r = a \sqrt 2 / 4 = a / (2\sqrt 2).
Total sphere volume: 4 \cdot \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3 = \dfrac{16}{3}
\pi \cdot \dfrac{a^3}{16 \sqrt 2} = \dfrac{\pi a^3}{3 \sqrt 2}.
Density: \dfrac{\pi a^3 / (3\sqrt 2)}{a^3} = \dfrac{\pi}{3\sqrt 2}
\approx 0.7405. Verified. (Kepler’s conjecture, finally proved by Hales 1998–2017.)



Kiselev-style problems
Three problems in Kiselev’s classical style, adapted from Givental, Stereometry, §§493–530.
K1. Show that the centres of the six faces of a cube of side a are the vertices of an octahedron, and find that octahedron’s edge length.


Hint 1 — a nudge

The face-centre points of a cube are at (\pm a/2, 0, 0), (0, \pm a/2, 0), (0, 0, \pm a/2) — six points symmetric about the cube’s centre. Each pair of “adjacent” face centres is at distance…



Full solution

The six face centres are at (\pm a/2, 0, 0), (0, \pm a/2, 0), (0, 0, \pm a/2) relative to the cube’s centre. Adjacent face centres (e.g. (a/2, 0, 0) and (0, a/2, 0)) are at distance \sqrt{(a/2)^2 + (a/2)^2} = a / \sqrt 2. The six points form a regular octahedron of edge a / \sqrt 2 \approx 0.707 a. Octahedron edge = a / \sqrt 2. (Stereometry §495 — cube and octahedron are duals.)

K2. A regular tetrahedron of edge a is inscribed in a cube of edge a' = a / \sqrt 2. (I.e., the tetrahedron’s vertices are 4 of the cube’s 8 vertices, no two adjacent.) Verify this and find the volume ratio.


Hint 1 — a nudge

4 cube vertices: (0,0,0), (a',a',0), (a',0,a'), (0,a',a') — check they form a regular tetrahedron of edge a' \sqrt 2.



Full solution

Pairwise distances: \sqrt{(a')^2 + (a')^2} = a' \sqrt 2. So the inscribed tetrahedron has edge a' \sqrt 2. With a = a' \sqrt 2, a' = a / \sqrt 2 — confirmed.
Volume ratio: V_{\text{tet}} = \dfrac{a^3 \sqrt 2}{12}, V_{\text{cube}} = (a')^3 = \dfrac{a^3}{2\sqrt 2}. Ratio V_{\text{tet}} / V_{\text{cube}} = \dfrac{\sqrt 2 / 12}
{1 / (2\sqrt 2)} = \dfrac{\sqrt 2 \cdot 2\sqrt 2}{12} = \dfrac{4}{12}
= \dfrac{1}{3}. The inscribed tetrahedron is \tfrac{1}{3} of the cube’s volume. (Stereometry §496 exercise.)

K3. Show that the area of an equilateral triangle of side s is \dfrac{s^2 \sqrt 3}{4}, and use it to derive the volume of a regular tetrahedron of side s as \dfrac{s^3 \sqrt 2}{12}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Equilateral triangle area: drop the altitude, use Pythagoras. Tetrahedron volume: \tfrac{1}{3} base × height; height =
s \sqrt{2/3} (Ch 1 S2 in this tier).



Full solution

Equilateral triangle area: altitude h = s \sqrt 3 / 2. Area = \tfrac{1}{2} s \cdot s\sqrt 3 / 2 = \dfrac{s^2 \sqrt 3}{4}.
Tetrahedron volume: V = \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot \dfrac{s^2 \sqrt 3}{4}
\cdot s\sqrt{2/3} = \dfrac{s^3 \sqrt 3 \cdot \sqrt 2}{12 \sqrt 3}
= \dfrac{s^3 \sqrt 2}{12}. QED. (Stereometry §498.)







Source pointer. Adapted from Givental, Kiselev’s Geometry, Book II — Stereometry, §§491–530 (Sumizdat 2008). Free PDF on cimat.mx. The five-Platonic-solids classification (Problem 3) is Kiselev’s §493 — directly following Euclid Book XIII.





Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this chapter, remember these:

Exactly five Platonic solids exist — tetrahedron, cube, octahedron, dodecahedron, icosahedron — forced by the convexity inequality \tfrac{1}{p} + \tfrac{1}{q} > \tfrac{1}{2}.
Euler’s formula V - E + F = 2 holds for every convex polyhedron — the first theorem of topology, hiding inside classical geometry.
The square-cube law: linear scale k multiplies surface by k^2 and volume by k^3. Why elephants have thick legs and giant ants are impossible.



Common confusions

“Euler’s formula is V + E + F = 2.” It is minus in the middle: V - E + F = 2. The minus is essential — the alternating sum is what makes the topology come out right (it generalises to the Euler characteristic).
“There are six Platonic solids.” No — there are exactly five in 3D. (Six in 4D, three in dimensions \ge 5 — but that’s a different topic.)
“Volume scales by k^2 for similar solids.” No — by k^3. Surface area scales by k^2; volume by k^3. Mixing them up is the most common error in this area.



Self-Assessment

Could you prove the 5-Platonic-solids theorem (Problem 3) on a fresh sheet, deriving the angle inequality \tfrac{1}{p} + \tfrac{1}{q} > \tfrac{1}{2}?
Did Euler’s formula (Problem 2) feel like a planar-graph argument once stretched flat?
Can you state the three scaling laws — linear k, area k^2, volume k^3 — and explain the square-cube law (Problem 7)?


3 yes: You have completed Kiselev Stereometry in Russian-classical style. The four-tier Kiselev geometry track is now in place.
2 yes: redo Problem 3 — the Platonic classification is the chapter’s centrepiece.
0–1 yes: the trio Problems 2, 3, 4 form one big idea (Euler

classification + scaling); replay them.
crumbs: Ch 5 — Regular Polyhedra and Similar Solids

title: Ch 5 — Regular Polyhedra and Similar Solids
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

kg2.polyhedra — vertex / edge / face vocabulary; volume formulas for prisms and pyramids.
kg2.sphere — the sphere as the n \to \infty limit of a regular n-hedron, and the comparison point for shape-efficiency arguments.



What this unlocks

t6.groups — the symmetries of each Platonic solid form a finite group (the icosahedral group of order 120 is one of the great finite simple groups).
t7.langlands-program — the dihedral, tetrahedral, octahedral, icosahedral groups are the simplest Galois representations.
t6.spectral-graph-theory — Euler’s formula generalises to the Euler characteristic of any surface, the gateway to topology.



Kiselev chapter alignment

Geometry, Book II — Chapter 5: Regular polyhedra and similarity (§§491–530). Givental translation, Sumizdat 2008. Free PDF on cimat.mx.
Sister synthesis lessons: cbse9.surface-areas-and-volumes (computational mensuration); cbse10.surface-areas-and-volumes (combined solids).
Read alongside the source PDF for Kiselev’s full development of Cauchy’s rigidity theorem (every convex polyhedron is rigid) in the appendix.



Where this matters

Game development. Game engines render Platonic solids as primitives — the icosahedron is the standard low-poly sphere because it has the highest face-symmetry-to-face-count ratio. Dual relationships (cube↔︎octahedron) are exploited in normal-map encoding and cubemap projection.


AI / ML. Graph neural networks generalise the Euler-formula intuition: structure flows along edges, with vertex-edge-face incidences encoded as adjacency matrices. The Platonic solids are the canonical small vertex-transitive graphs — natural test cases for symmetry-aware architectures.


Research. Cauchy’s rigidity theorem (1813): every convex polyhedron is rigid — its shape is determined by its face shapes and combinatorial structure. This was the first major theorem in combinatorial geometry and is still the gateway to questions in mechanism theory and protein folding.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones: Plato (Timaeus — the cosmological role of the five solids); Euclid (Elements Book XIII — first complete classification proof); Cauchy (Mémoire sur les polygones et les polyèdres — the rigidity theorem and the polyhedron-flatten proof of Euler).
The argument \tfrac{1}{p} + \tfrac{1}{q} > \tfrac{1}{2} (Problem 3) is one of the cleanest classifications in mathematics — three lines, total certainty. Worth memorising.
What this lesson does not do: classify the Archimedean solids (semi-regular: same vertex configuration, but different face polygons). There are 13 of them; that’s a separate topic worth its own lesson.
Kiselev Stereometry — track complete. Five chapters, all aligned to Givental’s English translation. Together with Planimetry’s nine chapters, the Kiselev geometry track is in place from definitions to the Platonic classification.
crumbs: Ch 5 — Regular Polyhedra and Similar Solids

title: Ch 1 — Lines and Planes in Space
text:
Are the parallel-line theorems automatic for you in 2D? See kg1.parallels.
Can you state the perpendicular-bisector locus theorem from kg1.perpendiculars?
Can you visualise three points in space that are not on a single line — and the unique plane they determine?
Are you comfortable with the everyday objects: a wall (plane), the floor (plane), the line where they meet (their intersection)?

If three yeses, proceed.
crumbs: Ch 1 — Lines and Planes in Space

title: Ch 1 — Lines and Planes in Space
crumbs: Ch 1 — Lines and Planes in Space

title: Ch 1 — Lines and Planes in Space
text:
Wonder

In the plane, two distinct lines must meet or be parallel. In space, that is no longer true. Why does the third option (skew) exist?
A point and a line not through the point — how many planes contain both?
A line is parallel to a plane if it never meets the plane. What is the intersection of a non-parallel line with a plane?
A line is perpendicular to a plane if it makes a right angle with every line in the plane through the foot. Is checking one line enough? (Trick question.)







TipWhat you might notice






Two lines determine a plane only if they meet or are parallel. Skew lines force a 3D-only configuration. (2) Exactly one plane contains a given line and a point not on it.
Either a single point or the whole line (if the line lies in the plane). (4) Checking one line is not enough — but checking two distinct lines through the foot is. The proof is one of the prettiest in Stereometry.
crumbs: Ch 1 — Lines and Planes in Space

title: Ch 1 — Lines and Planes in Space
text:
Discover

Problem 1 — Three points determine a plane
Three points A, B, C in space, not collinear, determine exactly one plane.


Full solution

This is Stereometry’s first axiom (Planimetry §3 generalised): through any three non-collinear points there passes exactly one plane. Existence and uniqueness are taken as foundational. (A plane is essentially a 2D Euclidean copy embedded in 3D.) (Stereometry §374.)



Problem 2 — Three configurations of two lines
Show that any two distinct lines in space fall into exactly one of three classes: (a) intersecting (meet at a point), (b) parallel (same direction, no meeting, lie in a common plane), (c) skew (no meeting, no common plane).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Two lines lie in a common plane iff they are intersecting or parallel. The contrapositive is skew = no common plane = no meeting.




Full solution

Coplanar case. If the two lines are in a common plane, they are either parallel or intersecting (the planar dichotomy from Planimetry).
Non-coplanar case. If they share no plane, they cannot meet — since a single intersection point + the rest of either line + non-trivial extension forces both to lie in the plane through that point and any other two points (one from each line), contradiction. And they cannot be parallel — parallel lines lie in a common plane by definition.
So two distinct lines are exactly one of: intersecting, parallel, or skew. QED. (Stereometry §378.)



Problem 3 — Three positions of a line and a plane
Classify the possible relationships between a line \ell and a plane \pi.


Full solution

Three cases: - \ell lies in \pi (every point of \ell is in \pi). - \ell intersects \pi at exactly one point. - \ell is parallel to \pi (no intersection — \ell and \pi have no common point).
There are no other cases: a line either intersects the plane in the empty set (parallel), one point, or has at least two points in the plane (in which case the whole line is in the plane, since two points determine a line within a plane). (Stereometry §380.)



Problem 4 — Line perpendicular to a plane
Define: a line \ell is perpendicular to a plane \pi at a point F \in \ell \cap \pi if \ell is perpendicular to every line in \pi through F. Show that this is equivalent to: \ell is perpendicular to two distinct lines in \pi through F.


Hint 1 — a nudge

If \ell is perpendicular to two distinct lines m_1, m_2 in \pi through F, then any third line m in \pi through F can be expressed as a “linear combination” of m_1 and m_2 — and the perpendicularity to \ell is preserved by this combination.



Full solution

The harder direction: two perpendiculars suffice. Let \ell be perpendicular at F to two distinct lines m_1, m_2 \subset \pi through F. Take any third line m in \pi through F.
Choose points A_1 \in m_1, A_2 \in m_2 at unit distance from F. Any point X on m at unit distance from F has the form X = a A_1 + b A_2 with a^2 + b^2 = 1 (parameterising the unit circle in \pi in the basis A_1, A_2).
Take any point P on \ell, P \ne F. Use the three-point perpendicular formula (a coordinate / vector argument): |PX|^2 =
|PF|^2 + |FX|^2 iff \angle PFX = 90°. The hypothesis \angle PFA_1 = \angle PFA_2 = 90° gives |PA_i|^2 = |PF|^2 +
|FA_i|^2 for i = 1, 2. By a careful expansion (treating X as a linear combination), one finds |PX|^2 = |PF|^2 + |FX|^2, i.e. \ell \perp m at F. Since m was arbitrary, \ell is perpendicular to every line through F in \pi — and so \ell
\perp \pi. QED. (Stereometry §389 — the perpendicular-line- to-plane theorem.)



Problem 5 — Existence and uniqueness of perpendicular line through a point
Through a given point P, there exists exactly one line perpendicular to a given plane \pi.


Hint 1 — a nudge

If P \in \pi: erect two perpendiculars in \pi to two intersecting reference lines through P in \pi, then use Problem 4 in reverse.
If P \notin \pi: drop a perpendicular from P to \pi — existence by reflection symmetry; uniqueness by an angle-sum argument analogous to Planimetry §57.



Full solution

Existence (point on the plane). Let P \in \pi. Choose two distinct lines m_1, m_2 \subset \pi through P. Erect a line \ell_1 in the plane through P and m_1 (the “vertical plane” above m_1) perpendicular to m_1. Similarly \ell_2 in the plane through P and m_2 perpendicular to m_2. Now \ell_1 and \ell_2 are two distinct lines through P in the “vertical pencil” above P. By a standard argument (the perpendicular plane through P), there is a unique line \ell through P perpendicular to both m_1 and m_2 in \pi — and by Problem 4 this \ell is perpendicular to \pi.
Uniqueness. Suppose two distinct perpendiculars \ell_1, \ell_2 existed through P. Each is perpendicular to every line in \pi through P. The plane through \ell_1 and \ell_2 would intersect \pi in some line m through P, and both \ell_1 and \ell_2 would be perpendicular to m in this plane through \ell_1, \ell_2 — so \ell_1 = \ell_2 (uniqueness of the planar perpendicular). (Stereometry §391.)
The case P \notin \pi is similar; one drops a perpendicular foot. QED.



Problem 6 — The perpendicular is the shortest
The perpendicular from a point P to a plane \pi has the shortest length among all segments from P to points in \pi.


Hint 1 — a nudge

This is exactly the planar shortest-segment theorem (Ch 3 of Planimetry) lifted to 3D — the same argument works on the right triangle formed by P, the perpendicular foot F, and any other point Q \in \pi.



Full solution

Let F be the foot of the perpendicular from P to \pi, and Q \in \pi any other point. The triangle \triangle PFQ has \angle PFQ = 90° (since \ell \perp \pi implies \ell is perpendicular to every line in \pi through F, including line FQ). By the planar Pythagoras: |PQ|^2 = |PF|^2 + |FQ|^2 >
|PF|^2. So |PQ| > |PF|. The perpendicular minimises. (Stereometry §392.)



Problem 7 — Dihedral angles
Two half-planes sharing a common edge \ell form a dihedral angle. Its measure is the planar angle one sees by intersecting both half-planes with a plane perpendicular to \ell. Show that this measure does not depend on the choice of the perpendicular plane.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Two perpendicular cross-sections of the dihedral angle are parallel to each other (both perpendicular to \ell). The two planar angles are corresponding under that parallelism.



Full solution

Take two perpendicular planes \sigma_1 and \sigma_2, both perpendicular to \ell at points F_1, F_2 respectively. The two half-planes’ intersections with \sigma_1 are two rays out of F_1 at some angle \theta_1; similarly \theta_2 at F_2. By construction, \sigma_1 \parallel \sigma_2 (both perpendicular to \ell). Each pair of rays in \sigma_1 and \sigma_2 are translations of each other along \ell — so \theta_1 =
\theta_2. QED. (Stereometry §400 — well-defined dihedral measure.)


Eratosthenes of Cyrene (~240 BCE), librarian at Alexandria, estimated the Earth’s circumference using only the geometry of two lines in space. He noted that on the summer solstice the Sun was directly overhead at Syene (modern Aswan) — a vertical stick cast no shadow — while at Alexandria, ~800 km north, a stick cast a shadow corresponding to about 7.2° — one-fiftieth of a full turn. So the full circumference was about 50 \times 800 =
40\,000 km. The modern value is 40\,075 km. The whole calculation hinges on the parallel-lines-in-space and skew-line geometry of this chapter.


The plane has two relationships between two lines: meet, or parallel. Space adds a third — skew. And the right-angle condition between a line and a plane needs only two perpendiculars in the plane to be certified — Kiselev’s two-perpendicular theorem — the synthetic ancestor of the linear-algebra fact that orthogonality to a spanning set is orthogonality to the whole subspace.
crumbs: Ch 1 — Lines and Planes in Space

title: Ch 1 — Lines and Planes in Space
text:
Name

Definitions (Stereometry §§373–415).

Plane: a flat 2D surface in 3D space; uniquely determined by three non-collinear points.
Two lines in space: intersecting, parallel, or skew (none of the above).
Line and plane: the line lies in the plane, intersects it at one point, or is parallel to the plane.
Two planes: intersect in a line, or are parallel.
Line perpendicular to plane (\ell \perp \pi): \ell is perpendicular to every line in \pi through the foot. Equivalently, perpendicular to two distinct lines in \pi through the foot (the two-perpendiculars theorem).
Dihedral angle: the figure formed by two half-planes sharing a common edge. Its measure is the planar angle of any cross-section perpendicular to the edge.

Theorems.

Three non-collinear points uniquely determine a plane.
Two lines in space are intersecting, parallel, or skew — no other option.
A line perpendicular to two distinct lines in a plane (through their foot) is perpendicular to every line in the plane through the foot.
Through any point in space, there is a unique line perpendicular to a given plane.
The perpendicular from a point to a plane is the shortest segment from the point to the plane.
The dihedral-angle measure is well-defined: independent of the perpendicular cross-section chosen.


A verse:

In the plane, lines meet or run parallel — that’s all. In space, a third path appears — skew, two strangers passing. Perpendicular to two lines through one foot? Perpendicular to all. And the perpendicular is shortest, like its planar elder, never lapsing.
crumbs: Ch 1 — Lines and Planes in Space

title: Ch 1 — Lines and Planes in Space
text:
Apply

Worked example

A line \ell pierces a plane \pi at a point F. Two lines m_1, m_2 \subset \pi both pass through F and are perpendicular to \ell. Is \ell necessarily perpendicular to \pi?

If m_1 and m_2 are distinct, then yes — by the two-perpendicular theorem (Problem 4). If they are the same line, no — \ell is perpendicular only to that one line in \pi, and to many other choices of one-line-in-\pi it might not be perpendicular.
Conclusion: yes, provided m_1 \ne m_2.


Practice — Warm
W1. Two lines in space pass through the same point and are not parallel. Are they skew?


Full solution

No. They meet at that point, so they are intersecting, not skew. Skew lines never meet.

W2. Through a given point in space and a given plane, how many perpendiculars to the plane through that point exist?


Full solution

Exactly one (Problem 5). The point can be on the plane or off it; either way, one perpendicular.

W3. Two parallel planes are cut by a third plane. The intersections are two lines. What is their relationship?


Full solution

They are parallel. (Each lies in the cutting plane and is parallel to the same direction inherited from the parallel planes.)



Practice — Working
P1. Show that if a line is perpendicular to a plane, then any plane containing that line is perpendicular to the original plane.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Two planes are perpendicular iff their dihedral angle is 90°. Construct the perpendicular-cross-section.



Full solution

Let \ell \perp \pi at F, and let \sigma be any plane containing \ell. The dihedral angle between \sigma and \pi is measured by intersecting both with a plane perpendicular to their common edge (which is \sigma \cap \pi, a line through F). The cross-section through F perpendicular to this edge contains \ell (since \ell \perp \pi \perp edge means \ell is perpendicular to the edge — wait, let me check: \ell perpendicular to \pi means \ell is perpendicular to the edge, which lies in \pi. So \ell is perpendicular to the edge of the dihedral angle, and the cross-section perpendicular to that edge through F contains \ell). The cross-section also intersects \pi in a line perpendicular to the edge (in \pi). The angle between \ell and this line in \pi is — by the perpendicularity \ell \perp \pi — exactly 90°. So the dihedral angle is 90°, i.e. \sigma \perp \pi. QED. (Stereometry §405.)

P2. Show that two planes both perpendicular to a third plane need not be parallel.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Look at two walls of a room — they are both perpendicular to the floor, but they are perpendicular to each other, not parallel.



Full solution

Consider three mutually perpendicular planes (walls and floor of a room). Two of the walls are both perpendicular to the floor, but they are perpendicular to each other, not parallel. Hence two planes perpendicular to a third can be perpendicular, parallel, or even neither (intersecting at any angle other than 0° or 90°).

P3. Show that the distance from a point to a plane is the length of the perpendicular from the point to the plane (and is well-defined — independent of any other configuration).


Full solution

Direct application of Problem 6 (the perpendicular minimises) and Problem 5 (the perpendicular is unique). The minimum is achieved once, at the perpendicular foot.



Practice — Stretch
S1. (The three-perpendiculars theorem.) A point P off a plane \pi has perpendicular foot F in \pi. From F, drop a perpendicular within \pi to a line \ell \subset \pi, foot G. Show that PG \perp \ell — i.e. the line from P to the foot of the perpendicular within \pi is also perpendicular to \ell.


Hint 1 — a nudge

PF \perp \pi means PF \perp \ell. FG \perp \ell by construction. So \ell is perpendicular to two lines PF, FG through G in the plane PFG — and thus perpendicular to every line in that plane through G, in particular PG.



Full solution

PF \perp \pi ⇒ PF \perp \ell (since \ell \subset \pi). FG \perp \ell by construction. Hence \ell is perpendicular to both PF and FG, two lines in plane PFG meeting at G. By Problem 4 (two-perpendiculars), \ell \perp plane PFG, hence \ell \perp PG (any line through G in plane PFG). QED. (Stereometry §394 — the three-perpendiculars theorem, the geometry-engine of every planar-shadow problem.)

S2. A regular tetrahedron ABCD has all four faces equilateral triangles of side s. Find the distance from any vertex to the opposite face.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Drop the perpendicular from A to the centroid of \triangle BCD. Use the equilateral-triangle centroid (at distance s/\sqrt 3 from each vertex) and Pythagoras.



Full solution

The centroid of equilateral \triangle BCD is at distance \dfrac{s}{\sqrt 3} from each of B, C, D. The apex A has |AB| = s. Pythagoras: h^2 + \left(\dfrac{s}{\sqrt 3}\right)^2 =
s^2, so h^2 = s^2 - \dfrac{s^2}{3} = \dfrac{2 s^2}{3}, hence h = s \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{3}} = \dfrac{s \sqrt 6}{3}. (A canonical 3D-Pythagoras computation; Kiselev §403.)



Kiselev-style problems
Three problems in Kiselev’s classical style, adapted from Givental, Stereometry, §§385–410.
K1. Through a point P off a plane \pi, all the segments to points on a circle in \pi have the same length. Show that the perpendicular from P to \pi lands on the centre of that circle.


Hint 1 — a nudge

The set of points in \pi equidistant from P is the locus of the foot F’s perpendicular cone — which intersects \pi in a circle centred at F.



Full solution

Let F be the foot of the perpendicular from P to \pi. For any point Q \in \pi, |PQ|^2 = |PF|^2 + |FQ|^2. So |PQ| is a monotonic function of |FQ|. The set ${Q : |PQ| = $ constant\} is therefore ${Q : |FQ| = $ constant’\} — a circle in \pi centred at F. QED. (Stereometry §394.)

K2. Two skew lines \ell_1, \ell_2 in space have a unique common perpendicular — a single line meeting both at right angles. Sketch this and explain why it must exist and be unique.


Full solution

Existence. Through \ell_1 pass a plane \pi_1 parallel to \ell_2 (this exists: take any plane containing \ell_1 and a line through any point of \ell_1 parallel to \ell_2). \ell_2 projects onto a line \ell_2' in \pi_1 parallel to \ell_2. The lines \ell_1 and \ell_2' in \pi_1 either meet at a point X or are parallel; if parallel, \ell_1 and \ell_2 are parallel (contradiction). So they meet at X. The perpendicular to \pi_1 at X — call it m — is perpendicular to \ell_1 (since \ell_1 \subset \pi_1) and perpendicular to \ell_2', hence (by translation argument) to \ell_2.
Uniqueness. Two common perpendiculars would force \ell_1 and \ell_2 to lie in the plane of those two perpendiculars, contradicting “skew”. (Stereometry §397 — the common-perpendicular theorem.)

K3. A point P in space is equidistant from three given points A, B, C. Show that P lies on the perpendicular line through the circumcentre of \triangle ABC (perpendicular to the plane ABC).


Hint 1 — a nudge

|PA| = |PB| and |PA| = |PC| — interpret these as planar perpendicular-bisector loci, in a 3D-perpendicular-plane sense.



Full solution

|PA| = |PB|: the locus is the perpendicular plane through the midpoint of AB — i.e. the plane perpendicular to AB through its midpoint M_{AB}. Similarly |PA| = |PC| gives the perpendicular plane through M_{AC}. Both planes contain the perpendicular line through the circumcentre of \triangle ABC to the plane ABC. The intersection of the two planes is exactly that perpendicular line. QED. (Stereometry §410 — the 3D circumcentre theorem, basis for the circumsphere* of a tetrahedron.)*







Source pointer. Adapted from Givental, Kiselev’s Geometry, Book II — Stereometry, §§373–415 (Sumizdat 2008). Free PDF on cimat.mx. S1 is the three-perpendiculars theorem, §394 — Kiselev’s favourite theorem of Stereometry’s first chapter, used heavily later.





Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this chapter, remember these:

Two distinct lines in space are intersecting, parallel, or skew — three options, not two as in the plane.
A line is perpendicular to a plane iff it makes a right angle with two distinct lines in the plane through the foot — not just one.
The perpendicular from a point to a plane is the shortest segment from the point to the plane — the same minimisation principle as in 2D, lifted one dimension up.



Common confusions

“Two non-parallel lines must meet.” True in the plane; false in space, where skew lines exist.
“A line perpendicular to one line in a plane is perpendicular to the plane.” False. Need two distinct lines in the plane through the foot — the two-perpendicular theorem (Problem

is about two, not one.

“Perpendicular planes both contain the same perpendicular line.” No — perpendicular planes are two planes whose dihedral angle is 90°; they do not share a common perpendicular line in general.



Self-Assessment

Could you state and prove the two-perpendicular theorem (Problem 4) — the heart of perpendicularity in space — without notes?
Did the three-perpendiculars theorem (S1) feel like one move, or did you need scaffolding?
Can you sketch a configuration of two skew lines and identify their common perpendicular?


3 yes: move on to Polyhedra (Ch 2).
2 yes: redo Problem 4 — it is the chapter’s core machinery.
0–1 yes: the four core problems (3, 4, 5, 7) form one logical block; replay them.
crumbs: Ch 1 — Lines and Planes in Space

title: Ch 1 — Lines and Planes in Space
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

kg1.parallels — parallel-line theorems lifted to 3D (parallel planes, parallel lines in different planes).
kg1.perpendiculars — perpendicular-as-shortest-segment, lifted to 3D.



What this unlocks

kg2.polyhedra (Ch 2, planned) — prisms, pyramids, regular polyhedra all rely on perpendicular-line-to-plane (for heights) and dihedral angles.
kg2.cylinder-cone — the axis of a cylinder is perpendicular to its base; understanding perpendicular lines in space is the prerequisite.
t4.vectors-2d-3d — the synthetic geometry here translates directly to vector dot products and cross products.
cbse12.lines-in-3d — CBSE Class 12’s coordinate version of the same theorems.



Kiselev chapter alignment

Geometry, Book II — Chapter 1: Lines and planes (§§373–415). Givental translation, Sumizdat 2008. Free PDF on cimat.mx.
Sister synthesis lessons: cbse11.three-d-geometry-intro (CBSE 11 introduction); cbse12.lines-in-3d and cbse12.planes-in-3d (board-exam coordinate treatments).
Read alongside the source PDF for Kiselev’s full development of the three-perpendiculars theorem (§394).



Where this matters

Game development. Every 3D engine relies on plane equations, ray-plane intersections, and perpendicular foot calculations. Lighting (the normal vector is the perpendicular to a surface), collision (perpendicular distance from a point to a wall), and physics (resolution of forces in 3D) all use the synthetic theory you just built.


Research. The two-perpendiculars theorem (Problem 4) is the synthetic seed of linear algebra in \mathbb R^3: a vector is orthogonal to a 2D subspace iff it is orthogonal to two spanning vectors. The same statement, from synthetic to vector to inner-product space, is one continuous arc of mathematical abstraction.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones: Kiselev (the §§373–415 ordering and the proof of Problem 4); Hilbert’s Grundlagen (which laid out 3D axioms rigorously); the three-perpendiculars theorem from §394 — Kiselev’s favourite move.
Problem 4’s proof I gave is sketched; the full proof Kiselev presents uses an explicit isosceles-triangle in the perpendicular plane argument that takes about half a page. Worth reading in full.
What this lesson does not do: treat the parallel postulate’s 3D version (the existence of parallel planes through a point off a plane) as a separate axiom — it follows from the planar version applied to the right cross-section.
crumbs: Ch 1 — Lines and Planes in Space

title: Ch 2 — Polyhedra: Prisms and Pyramids
text:
Can you state the two-perpendicular theorem and the three-perpendiculars theorem from kg2.lines-and-planes?
Are 2D area formulas — triangle, parallelogram, regular n-gon — automatic for you? See kg1.areas.
Do you accept that the volume of a rectangular box is length × width × height?
Can you visualise cutting a 3D solid by a plane and getting a 2D cross-section?

If three yeses, proceed.

title: Ch 2 — Polyhedra: Prisms and Pyramids
Ch 2 — Polyhedra: Prisms and Pyramids
text:
Wonder

Why is the oblique prism (slid sideways) the same volume as the right prism? Visually — what stays constant?
The pyramid is exactly one third of the prism on the same base and height. Why one-third? (In 2D, the triangle is one-half of the parallelogram; this is the 3D version.)
A regular tetrahedron has all four faces congruent equilateral triangles. How many regular polyhedra exist in 3D? (Trick question — surprisingly few.)
Cut a prism diagonally: each piece is a prism with triangular base (a triangular prism). Could three congruent triangular pyramids tile a single triangular prism?







TipWhat you might notice






Cavalieri’s principle: if every cross-section parallel to the base has the same area, the total volume is the same. (2) Three pyramids of equal volume can tile one triangular prism — and they’re each 1/3 of the prism. The proof uses Cavalieri (or similar-pyramid scaling). (3) Exactly five regular polyhedra: tetrahedron, cube, octahedron, dodecahedron, icosahedron. (Plato.)
Yes — the answer to (4) is the proof of (2).

title: Ch 2 — Polyhedra: Prisms and Pyramids
text:
Discover

Problem 1 — Volume of a rectangular box
Show that the volume of a rectangular box with dimensions a \times b \times c is abc.


Full solution

Working axiom — Kiselev takes this as a foundational fact (the unit cube has unit volume; volume scales with each of the three dimensions). Just as area b \cdot h is foundational in Planimetry, volume abc is foundational here. (Stereometry §416.)



Problem 2 — Volume of a right prism
Show that the volume of a right prism (with congruent parallel polygonal bases of area A and lateral edges of length h perpendicular to the base) is V = A h.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Triangulate the base into triangles. Each triangular piece extrudes into a triangular prism. By the same argument used for area, each triangular prism’s volume scales linearly in base area and height.



Full solution

Triangulate the base polygon into triangles T_1, \ldots, T_m of areas A_1, \ldots, A_m summing to A. Each T_i extrudes into a triangular prism of volume A_i h (this is taken as known for triangular prisms — provable by sandwiching a triangular prism in a rectangular box and using Problem 1). Sum: V = h \sum A_i = A h. QED. (Stereometry §417.)



Problem 3 — Cavalieri’s principle
Two solids lie between the same two parallel planes. Every plane parallel to those two slices both solids in regions of equal area. Show that the two solids have equal volume.


Hint 1 — a nudge

This is Cavalieri’s principle. Approximation: slice both solids into thin parallel slabs; in the limit the slab volumes equal the cross-section areas times the slab thickness. Equal cross-sections ⇒ equal slab volumes ⇒ equal totals.



Full solution

Slice both solids by n equally-spaced parallel planes between the bounding planes, splitting each into n “slabs” of equal thickness h/n. By the equal-cross-section hypothesis, the i-th slab of solid 1 and solid 2 have approximately equal volume (each is approximately the cross-section area times h/n). Sum and let n \to \infty: total volumes equal. QED. (Stereometry §418 — Cavalieri’s principle, the key tool. Bonaventura Cavalieri 1635.)



Problem 4 — Volume of an oblique prism
Show that the volume of an oblique prism (lateral edges not perpendicular to the base) is also A h, where h is the perpendicular distance between the parallel bases.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Apply Cavalieri’s principle: every cross-section parallel to the base has area A (by the parallel-line theorems in space).



Full solution

For both right and oblique prisms with the same base area A and same perpendicular height h, every cross-section parallel to the base is congruent to the base — hence area A. By Cavalieri, the volumes are equal. The right-prism volume is Ah, so the oblique- prism volume is also Ah. QED. (Stereometry §419.)



Problem 5 — Volume of a triangular pyramid (one third of the prism)
Three congruent triangular pyramids tile a triangular prism. Sketch the construction and conclude that each pyramid has volume \tfrac{1}{3} A h.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Take a triangular prism A B C / A' B' C' (top primes). Three pyramids: (i) A B C A', (ii) B B' A' C' … err, let me state this carefully.



Full solution

Take a triangular prism with bottom \triangle ABC and top \triangle A'B'C'. Slice along plane BA'C' and along plane BCC'. This produces three tetrahedra: T_1 = ABCA', T_2 = BCC'A', T_3 = BC'B'A'. Two of them — T_1 and T_2 — share base \triangle ABC and apex A' vs. C' respectively — wait, they share the opposite face. Let me re-organise:
The three tetrahedra are pairwise equivolumetric: each pair shares a base triangle (and the apex projects to the same height to the opposite base). By Cavalieri, each pair has equal volume — and the three together fill the prism. So each is \tfrac{1}{3} of the prism. Pyramid volume = \tfrac{1}{3} A h. (Stereometry §425 — the prism-into-three-pyramids dissection. Euclid XII proved this in the 3rd century BCE.)



Problem 6 — Volume of any pyramid
Show that the volume of any pyramid (with polygonal base of area A and perpendicular height h) is \tfrac{1}{3} A h.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Triangulate the base; each triangular piece becomes a triangular pyramid sharing the same apex. Sum.



Full solution

Triangulate the polygonal base into triangles T_1, \ldots, T_m of areas A_i. Each triangulated piece together with the apex forms a triangular pyramid of volume \tfrac{1}{3} A_i h (Problem 5). Sum: V = \tfrac{1}{3} h \sum A_i = \tfrac{1}{3} A h. QED. (Stereometry §426.)


Bonaventura Cavalieri (1598–1647), an Italian Jesuit and student of Galileo, published Geometria indivisibilibus continuorum nova quadam ratione promota in 1635 — the book that named the principle in Problem 3. Cavalieri imagined every solid as a stack of infinitely thin “indivisibles” (cross-sections), and computed volumes by comparing those slices. The idea was controversial — paradoxes about infinity terrified mathematicians of the day — but Cavalieri’s principle resolved them through the clean if-cross-sections-equal-then-volumes-equal rule. Fifty years later Newton and Leibniz folded the same intuition into integral calculus.



Problem 7 — Lateral surface of a regular pyramid
A regular pyramid has a regular polygonal base and the apex directly above the centre of the base. The lateral surface consists of n congruent isosceles triangles (the slant faces). Find the lateral surface area in terms of base perimeter P and slant height \ell (the perpendicular from the apex to a base edge).


Full solution

Each slant face is an isosceles triangle with base = side of the n-gon and altitude \ell. Its area is \tfrac{1}{2} (\text{side}) \cdot \ell. Sum over n faces: \tfrac{1}{2} P \cdot \ell where P is the base perimeter. Lateral area = \tfrac{1}{2} P \ell. (Stereometry §428 — the regular-pyramid lateral surface formula.)


The whole chapter rests on two moves: (1) the prism’s volume is base × height, by Cavalieri-on- slabs; (2) three congruent pyramids tile a triangular prism — so each one is exactly one-third. Every other formula (oblique prism, arbitrary pyramid, frustum) is one of these two with extra bookkeeping.

title: Ch 2 — Polyhedra: Prisms and Pyramids
text:
Name

Definitions.

Prism: a polyhedron with two parallel congruent polygonal bases connected by parallelograms. Right prism: lateral edges perpendicular to the base. Regular prism: right prism with regular polygonal base.
Pyramid: a polyhedron with one polygonal base and an apex point. Regular pyramid: regular polygonal base + apex directly above the centre.
Cavalieri’s principle: if two solids between parallel planes have equal cross-section areas at every level, they have equal volumes.

Theorems (Stereometry §§416–429).








Solid
Volume
Surface




Rectangular box, a \times b \times c
abc
2(ab + bc + ca)


Prism (right or oblique), base area A, height h
Ah
A_{\text{lat}} + 2A


Pyramid, base area A, height h
\tfrac{1}{3} Ah
A_{\text{lat}} + A


Regular n-gon prism, perimeter P, height h
A h
Ph + 2A (lateral = Ph)


Regular pyramid, perim P, slant height \ell
\tfrac{1}{3} A h
\tfrac{1}{2} P \ell + A




A verse:

Box, prism, pyramid — three solids, three formulas, one rule. Volume is base times height — that’s the prism’s tool. One third of that for the pyramid — three of them tile one prism. Cavalieri stitches it all: equal slices, equal volumes, no schism.

title: Ch 2 — Polyhedra: Prisms and Pyramids
text:
Apply

Worked example

A right rectangular prism has base 4 \times 5 and height 9. Find the volume and total surface area.

V = 4 \cdot 5 \cdot 9 = 180 cubic units. S = 2(4 \cdot 5 + 5 \cdot 9 + 9 \cdot 4) = 2(20 + 45 + 36) =
2 \cdot 101 = 202 square units.
V = 180,\quad S = 202.


Practice — Warm
W1. A pyramid has square base of side 6 and height 8. Find the volume.


Full solution

V = \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot 36 \cdot 8 = 96.

W2. A regular hexagonal prism has base side 3 and height 10. Find the volume.


Full solution

Hexagon area: \tfrac{3\sqrt 3}{2} \cdot 9 = \tfrac{27\sqrt 3}{2}. V = \tfrac{27\sqrt 3}{2} \cdot 10 = 135 \sqrt 3 \approx 233.83.

W3. A right square pyramid has base 4 \times 4 and slant height 5. Find the lateral surface area.


Full solution

Base perimeter 16. Lateral = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 16 \cdot 5 = 40.



Practice — Working
P1. A regular tetrahedron of side s has all four faces equilateral triangles. Find its volume.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Use the height h = s\sqrt{2/3} from Ch 1 Stretch S2. Base area \dfrac{\sqrt 3}{4} s^2.



Full solution

V = \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt 3}{4} s^2 \cdot \dfrac{s
\sqrt 6}{3} = \dfrac{s^3 \sqrt{18}}{36} = \dfrac{s^3 \sqrt 2}{12}.

P2. A pyramid has square base of side 6 and lateral edges all equal to 5. Find the height.


Hint 1 — a nudge

The diagonal of the square base is 6\sqrt 2. The lateral edge, the half-diagonal, and the height form a right triangle.



Full solution

Half-diagonal of base: 3\sqrt 2. By Pythagoras: h^2 + (3\sqrt 2)^2 = 5^2 \Rightarrow h^2 = 25 - 18 = 7
\Rightarrow h = \sqrt 7.

P3. Two pyramids have congruent bases and the same height, but the apex of the second is shifted horizontally (so it’s not directly above the centre of the base). Show the volumes are equal.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Cavalieri.



Full solution

At every height z above the base, the cross-section is similar to the base (with linear factor (h - z)/h in both pyramids). The shape of the cross-section depends on apex position, but the area depends only on the linear factor — hence is the same in both pyramids. By Cavalieri, the volumes are equal. QED. (The shifted-apex pyramid has the same volume as the regular one.)



Practice — Stretch
S1. Show that the volume of a frustum (truncated pyramid with parallel bases of areas A_1, A_2 and perpendicular distance h between them) is V = \tfrac{h}{3}(A_1 + A_2 + \sqrt{A_1 A_2}).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Extend the frustum into the full pyramid; subtract the small pyramid that was cut off.



Full solution

Let the full pyramid have base A_1, height H. The cut-off pyramid has similar shape with base A_2 and height H - h. Linear scale: \sqrt{A_2 / A_1}. So H - h = H \sqrt{A_2 / A_1}, giving H = h / (1 - \sqrt{A_2/A_1}). Frustum volume: \tfrac{1}{3} A_1 H - \tfrac{1}{3} A_2 (H - h). Substituting and simplifying (multiplying through by appropriate forms of \sqrt{A_2/A_1}) yields the stated formula. (Standard frustum volume; Stereometry §427.)

S2. Show that the volumes of three pyramids on the same base with apexes lying on the same plane parallel to the base are equal — and conclude that the centroid of a tetrahedron divides the line from any vertex to the centroid of the opposite face in ratio 3:1.


Hint 1 — a nudge

First half: same base, apexes at the same height ⇒ equal volumes (Cavalieri). Second half: in a tetrahedron, the four medians (vertex to centroid of opposite face) concur at the centroid, dividing each in ratio 3:1.



Full solution

First half is direct Cavalieri.
Second half: in tetrahedron ABCD, the median from A goes to the centroid G_A of \triangle BCD. By a similarity-style argument using the centroid construction (see Planimetry Stretch S1), the four medians concur at the 3D centroid G, and |AG| : |GG_A| = 3 : 1. QED. (Stereometry §429 — the tetrahedron centroid theorem.)



Kiselev-style problems
Three problems in Kiselev’s classical style, adapted from Givental, Stereometry, §§417–429.
K1. Show that the volume of a regular tetrahedron of side s is \dfrac{s^3 \sqrt 2}{12}, and that of a cube of side s is s^3. Compute the ratio of their volumes when both are inscribed in the same sphere.


Hint 1 — a nudge

A cube of side s inscribes in a sphere of diameter s\sqrt 3 (space diagonal). A regular tetrahedron of side s inscribes in a sphere of radius \dfrac{s \sqrt 6}{4}.



Full solution

Tetrahedron volume: P1 above gives \dfrac{s^3\sqrt 2}{12}.
For the cube: side = s, volume s^3, circumsphere radius \dfrac{s\sqrt 3}{2}.
Inscribed in sphere of radius R: - Cube side: s_{\text{cube}} = \dfrac{2R}{\sqrt 3}, volume \left(\dfrac{2R}{\sqrt 3}\right)^3 = \dfrac{8 R^3}{3\sqrt 3}. - Tetrahedron side: s_{\text{tet}} = \dfrac{4R}{\sqrt 6}, volume \dfrac{1}{12} \sqrt 2 \cdot \left(\dfrac{4R}{\sqrt 6}\right)^3
  = \dfrac{8 R^3}{9\sqrt 3}.
Ratio: tetrahedron / cube = 1/3. The inscribed tetrahedron is \tfrac{1}{3} the volume of the inscribed cube.

K2. A pyramid is cut by a plane parallel to its base, halfway up. The resulting two pieces are a small pyramid (top) and a frustum (bottom). Show the small pyramid has \tfrac{1}{8} the volume of the original.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Linear scale halves; volume scales by the cube.



Full solution

The small pyramid is similar to the whole with linear ratio \tfrac{1}{2}. Volume ratio: (\tfrac{1}{2})^3 = \tfrac{1}{8}. QED. (The frustum has \tfrac{7}{8}.) (Stereometry §427 corollary.)

K3. A regular hexagonal prism has all edges equal to s (both base edges and lateral edges). Find the volume and the total surface area.


Full solution

Hexagon area: \dfrac{3\sqrt 3}{2} s^2. Volume: \dfrac{3\sqrt 3}
{2} s^3. Lateral surface: 6 rectangles each s \times s, area 6 s^2. Two hexagonal bases: 2 \cdot \dfrac{3\sqrt 3}{2} s^2 =
3\sqrt 3 s^2. Total surface: (6 + 3\sqrt 3) s^2 \approx
11.196 s^2. (Stereometry §417 example.)







Source pointer. Adapted from Givental, Kiselev’s Geometry, Book II — Stereometry, §§416–429 (Sumizdat 2008). Free PDF on cimat.mx. The prism-into-three-pyramids dissection (Problem 5) is Kiselev’s §425, following Euclid XII.





Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this chapter, remember these:

A prism has two congruent parallel bases — its volume is base area × height, straight or slanted (Cavalieri).
A pyramid has one base and one apex — its volume is one-third base area × height, the 3D analogue of the triangle being half a parallelogram.
Cavalieri’s principle — same cross-section areas at every level ⇒ same total volume — is the single tool that makes every 3D volume calculation in school manageable.



Common confusions

“Slant height equals lateral edge.” They are different. The slant height is the perpendicular from the apex to a base edge (used in lateral surface). The lateral edge is from the apex to a base vertex (used in pyramid height calculations).
“The pyramid volume formula needs a regular pyramid.” It does not. Any pyramid with base area A and perpendicular height h has volume \tfrac{1}{3} A h, regardless of where the apex is over the base (Problem 6, P3 above).
“Cavalieri requires the cross-sections to be congruent, not just equal in area.” Equal area is enough — that is precisely the strength of Cavalieri’s principle (P3 demonstrates this).



Self-Assessment

Could you prove the prism-volume formula (Problem 2) by base triangulation, on a fresh sheet?
Did the prism-into-three-pyramids dissection (Problem 5) feel like one move?
Can you explain Cavalieri’s principle to a friend in two sentences, with an everyday example (a leaning stack of coins has the same volume as a straight stack)?


3 yes: continue to Cylinder and Cone (Ch 3, the pilot).
2 yes: redo Problem 5 — the dissection is the chapter’s centrepiece.
0–1 yes: Problems 4–6 form the volume-of-pyramid chain; re-walk them.

title: Ch 2 — Polyhedra: Prisms and Pyramids
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

kg2.lines-and-planes — perpendicular line to a plane (defines pyramid height); plane positions (defines parallel cross-sections for Cavalieri).
kg1.areas — every prism / pyramid volume formula bottoms out in a 2D base- area formula.



What this unlocks

kg2.cylinder-cone — cylinders and cones are limits of regular prisms and pyramids as the number of base sides n \to \infty. The pilot uses this directly.
kg2.sphere (Ch 4, planned) — Cavalieri’s principle used to compute sphere volume against a cylinder + double-cone comparison.
kg2.regular-polyhedra (Ch 5, planned) — the five Platonic solids are special pyramidal / dipyramidal configurations.



Kiselev chapter alignment

Geometry, Book II — Chapter 2: Polyhedra (§§416–429). Givental translation, Sumizdat 2008. Free PDF on cimat.mx.
Sister synthesis lessons: cbse9.surface-areas-and-volumes (introductory mensuration) and cbse10.surface-areas-and-volumes (combined-solid mensuration).
Read alongside the source PDF for Kiselev’s full prism-into- three-pyramids dissection (§425).



Where this matters

Game development. Every 3D mesh is ultimately a polyhedron — made of triangular or polygonal faces. Volume queries (for buoyancy, mass, fluid simulation) use the same dissection-into- prisms idea, applied to triangulated meshes via the signed volume of a tetrahedron from origin to triangle.


Research. Cavalieri’s principle is the integration-by-slicing intuition that becomes Riemann’s \int A(z) dz formula in multivariable calculus. The pyramid-as-one-third-of-prism is the discrete dissection version; calculus is the continuous version.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones: Kiselev (the §416–429 ordering); Cavalieri (1635); Euclid (Books XI–XII for the original prism dissections).
The prism-into-three-pyramids dissection (Problem 5) is surprisingly subtle — Kiselev’s clean version requires checking that each pair of the three pyramids has equal volume by Cavalieri, which is non-trivial.
What this lesson does not do: prove Hilbert’s third problem — that not every two equal-volume polyhedra are scissors- congruent. That is a 20th-century result (Dehn 1900) belonging to a measure-theory or representation-theory course.

title: Ch 9 — Some Applications of Trigonometry (Heights & Distances)
text:
Can you state \sin\theta, \cos\theta, \tan\theta for a right triangle? See cbse10.trigonometry-intro.
Do you remember the special-angle values at 30°, 45°, 60°?
Can you draw a right triangle from a verbal description (“a tower of height h casts a shadow of length s”)?
Can you simplify \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} to \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{3}?

Three yeses, you are ready.
crumbs: Ch 9 — Some Applications of Trigonometry (Heights & Distances)

title: Ch 9 — Some Applications of Trigonometry (Heights & Distances)
crumbs: Ch 9 — Some Applications of Trigonometry (Heights & Distances)

title: Ch 9 — Some Applications of Trigonometry (Heights & Distances)
text:
Wonder

If the angle of elevation is 45°, what is the relationship between the height and the ground distance?
As the angle of elevation grows from 30° to 60°, does the ground distance get bigger or smaller (with the same height)? Why?
What is the angle of depression, and how is it related to angle of elevation?
If you measured a shadow but did not know the angle of the sun, could you still find the height of an object by comparing it to a stick of known height nearby?







TipWhat you might notice






Equal — \tan 45° = 1. (2) Smaller — \tan grows with the angle so D = H/\tan\theta shrinks. (3) Equal — they are alternate angles when sight is from the higher point. (4) Yes — that is the similar-triangle shortcut from Ch 6 (P2 there).
crumbs: Ch 9 — Some Applications of Trigonometry (Heights & Distances)

title: Ch 9 — Some Applications of Trigonometry (Heights & Distances)
text:
Discover

Problem 1 — A tower
The angle of elevation of the top of a tower from a point on the ground 30 m away from the foot is 30°. Find the height of the tower.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\tan 30° = H / 30.



Full solution

H = 30 \tan 30° = 30 \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = 10\sqrt{3} \approx 17.32 m.



Problem 2 — A taller tower
A tower stands vertically. From a point 20 m from its foot, the angle of elevation of the top is 60°. Find the height.


Full solution

H = 20 \tan 60° = 20 \sqrt{3} \approx 34.64 m.



Problem 3 — Shadows at two times
A vertical pole has shadow lengths L_1 at angle \theta_1 and L_2 at angle \theta_2. If \theta_1 = 60° and \theta_2 = 30°, what is the ratio L_2 : L_1?


Hint 1 — a nudge

L = H / \tan\theta = H \cot\theta.



Full solution

L_1 = H \cot 60° = H/\sqrt{3}. L_2 = H \cot 30° = H\sqrt{3}. Ratio L_2 : L_1 = \sqrt{3} : 1/\sqrt{3} = 3 : 1.



Problem 4 — Two angles, one tower
From two points on the ground, on the same side and in line with the foot of a tower, the angles of elevation of the top are 30° and 60°. The two points are 40 m apart. The closer point has elevation 60°. Find the height of the tower.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let height = H, distance from closer point to base = x. Then \tan 60°
= H/x and \tan 30° = H/(x + 40).



Hint 2 — the technique

x = H/\sqrt{3} from the first. Substitute into the second.



Full solution

\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{H}{H/\sqrt{3} + 40} \Rightarrow H/\sqrt{3} + 40 = H\sqrt{3} \Rightarrow 40 = H(\sqrt{3} - 1/\sqrt{3}) = H \cdot \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow H = 20\sqrt{3} \approx 34.64 m.



Problem 5 — Angle of depression
From the top of a 50 m lighthouse, the angle of depression of a boat in the sea is 30°. How far is the boat from the foot of the lighthouse?


Hint 1 — a nudge

“Angle of depression” from the top equals “angle of elevation” from the boat (alternate interior angles, with horizontal at top parallel to ground).



Full solution

\tan 30° = 50 / D \Rightarrow D = 50 / \tan 30° = 50\sqrt{3} \approx
86.60 m.



Problem 6 — Two ships
A man on a cliff of height 100 m sees two boats in the sea, on the same side. Their angles of depression are 30° and 45°. Find the distance between the two boats.


Hint 1 — a nudge

D_1 at 45°: \tan 45° = 100/D_1 \Rightarrow D_1 = 100. D_2 at 30°: \tan 30° = 100/D_2 \Rightarrow D_2 = 100\sqrt{3}.



Full solution

Distance = D_2 - D_1 = 100\sqrt{3} - 100 = 100(\sqrt{3} - 1) \approx
73.21 m.


Eratosthenes measures the Earth (~240 BCE). A Greek librarian at Alexandria heard that at noon on the summer solstice in Syene (modern Aswan), the sun was directly overhead — a vertical stick cast no shadow. On the same day in Alexandria, \sim 800 km north, a vertical stick cast a shadow at an angle of about 7.2° — a 50th of a full circle. The sun’s rays are effectively parallel, so 7.2° must be the arc on Earth’s surface between the two cities. Therefore Earth’s circumference is roughly 50 \times 800 \approx 40{,}000 km — within 2\% of the modern value. One stick, one angle, one planet measured.


The recipe is always the same: sketch first, identify the right triangle, write \tan\theta = \text{vertical} / \text{horizontal}, then substitute special-angle values. Two angles in the diagram means two equations; two unknowns become solvable. The mathematics is trivial; the mistake is almost always the sketch.
crumbs: Ch 9 — Some Applications of Trigonometry (Heights & Distances)

title: Ch 9 — Some Applications of Trigonometry (Heights & Distances)
text:
Name

Angle of elevation. The angle between the horizontal ground and your line of sight up to a higher object.
Angle of depression. The angle between the horizontal at your level and your line of sight down to a lower object.
Solving height-and-distance problems is a four-step recipe:

Sketch the situation as a right triangle (or two).
Identify which side is the height, which is the ground distance, and which angles are known.
Use the appropriate trig ratio: \tan\theta = \dfrac{\text{vertical}}
{\text{horizontal}} is the most common.
Substitute special-angle values from the table; simplify.

A key fact: angle of depression from a high point equals angle of elevation from the corresponding low point (alternate interior angles between the two horizontals).

A verse:

Look up — that’s elevation. Look down — depression’s the name. Tan of theta is height over ground; the rest is the same game. Sketch first, label twice, then trigonometry takes the lead; Substitute special angles — and the answer is your reward indeed.
crumbs: Ch 9 — Some Applications of Trigonometry (Heights & Distances)

title: Ch 9 — Some Applications of Trigonometry (Heights & Distances)
text:
Apply

Worked example

A boy is standing on the ground and flying a kite with a string of length 100 m at an angle of elevation of 60°. Another boy is standing on the roof of a 10 m high building, and is flying his kite at an elevation of 45°. Both boys are on the same side of their kites. If the kites coincide (are at the same point in the sky), find the length of the second boy’s string.

For the first boy: kite height above the ground = 100 \sin 60° = 50\sqrt{3} m. Horizontal distance from boy to point below kite = 100 \cos 60° = 50 m.
The kite is at the same point for both. The second boy is 10 m up.
Vertical from second boy to kite: 50\sqrt{3} - 10 m. At elevation 45°, vertical = horizontal, so the second boy’s string makes a 45°-45°-90° triangle with vertical leg 50\sqrt{3} - 10.
String length = (50\sqrt{3} - 10) \cdot \sqrt{2} = 10\sqrt{2}(5\sqrt{3} -
1) m.
Numerically: \approx 10 \cdot 1.414 \cdot (8.660 - 1) = 14.14 \cdot 7.66
\approx 108.3 m.


Practice — Warm
W1. A pole 6 m high casts a shadow 2\sqrt{3} m long. Find the angle of elevation of the sun.


Full solution

\tan\theta = 6/(2\sqrt{3}) = \sqrt{3}, so \theta = 60°.

W2. From the top of a building, the angle of depression of a car on the ground is 30°. The car is 30\sqrt{3} m from the foot. Find the building’s height.


Full solution

\tan 30° = H / (30\sqrt{3}) \Rightarrow H = 30\sqrt{3}/\sqrt{3} = 30 m.

W3. A ladder 10 m long leans against a wall, making an angle of 60° with the ground. How high up the wall does it reach?


Full solution

\sin 60° = H/10 \Rightarrow H = 5\sqrt{3} \approx 8.66 m.



Practice — Working
P1. From the top of a 50 m high tower, the angles of depression of two cars on the ground (on the same side and in line) are 45° and 60°. Find the distance between the two cars.


Hint 1 — a nudge

D_1 at depression 60°: closer car. D_2 at 45°: farther.



Full solution

D_1 = 50 / \tan 60° = 50/\sqrt{3}. D_2 = 50 / \tan 45° = 50. Distance between = D_2 - D_1 = 50 - 50/\sqrt{3} = 50(1 - 1/\sqrt{3}) =
50 \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{3} - 1}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{50(\sqrt{3} - 1)}{\sqrt{3}}
\cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{50\sqrt{3}(\sqrt{3} - 1)}{3} =
\dfrac{50(3 - \sqrt{3})}{3} \approx 21.13 m.

P2. A vertical tower stands on a horizontal plane and is surmounted by a vertical flagstaff of height h. At a point on the plane, the angles of elevation of the bottom and top of the flagstaff are \alpha and \beta. Show that the height of the tower is \dfrac{h \tan\alpha}{\tan\beta -
\tan\alpha}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let tower height = H, distance from observer = x. Then \tan\alpha =
H/x and \tan\beta = (H + h)/x.



Full solution

x = H/\tan\alpha. Substitute: \tan\beta = (H + h) \tan\alpha / H. H \tan\beta = H \tan\alpha + h \tan\alpha. H(\tan\beta - \tan\alpha) = h \tan\alpha. H = \dfrac{h \tan\alpha}{\tan\beta - \tan\alpha}. \square

P3. The angle of elevation of a cloud from a point 60 m above the surface of a lake is 30°, and the angle of depression of its reflection in the lake is 60°. Find the height of the cloud above the lake.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Reflection is at depth equal to the cloud’s height below the lake.



Full solution

Let cloud height above lake = h, horizontal distance = x. Observer is 60 m above lake. Vertical distance from observer to cloud = h - 60; to reflection = h + 60. \tan 30° = (h - 60)/x \Rightarrow x = (h - 60)\sqrt{3}. \tan 60° = (h + 60)/x \Rightarrow x = (h + 60)/\sqrt{3}. Equate: (h - 60)\sqrt{3} = (h + 60)/\sqrt{3} \Rightarrow 3(h - 60) =
h + 60 \Rightarrow 2h = 240 \Rightarrow h = 120 m.



Practice — Stretch
S1. The angle of elevation of the top of a tower from a point on the ground is 30°. On walking 40 m towards the tower, the angle becomes 60°. Find the height of the tower.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Two unknowns (height, original distance), two equations.



Full solution

Let H = height, x = distance after walking 40 m. \tan 60° = H/x \Rightarrow x = H/\sqrt{3}. \tan 30° = H/(x + 40) \Rightarrow x + 40 = H\sqrt{3}. H/\sqrt{3} + 40 = H\sqrt{3} \Rightarrow 40 = H\sqrt{3} - H/\sqrt{3} =
H(2/\sqrt{3}). H = 20\sqrt{3} \approx 34.64 m.

S2. A bird sits on the top of a tree 80 m high. From a point on the ground, the angle of elevation of the bird is 45°. The bird flies away horizontally and after 2 seconds, the angle becomes 30°. Find the speed of the bird in m/s.


Hint 1 — a nudge

At elevation 45°: ground distance = 80 m. At elevation 30°: ground distance = 80\sqrt{3} m.



Full solution

Distance flown = 80\sqrt{3} - 80 = 80(\sqrt{3} - 1) \approx 58.56 m in 2 seconds. Speed \approx 29.28 m/s. Exact: 40(\sqrt{3} - 1) m/s.



Board-Pattern Practice
Q1 (1 mark — MCQ). The angle of elevation of the top of a tower from a point on the ground at a distance 30 m is 60°. The height of the tower is: (a) 30 m (b) 30\sqrt{3} m (c) \dfrac{30}{\sqrt{3}} m (d) 60 m.


Full solution

H = 30 \tan 60° = 30\sqrt{3}. (b).

Q2 (1 mark — fill-in). If the shadow of a vertical pole is \sqrt{3} times its height, the angle of elevation of the sun is ____.


Full solution

\tan\theta = H / (H\sqrt{3}) = 1/\sqrt{3} \Rightarrow \theta = 30°.

Q3 (2 marks). From a point on the ground, the angles of elevation of the bottom and the top of a transmission tower fixed at the top of a 20 m high building are 45° and 60° respectively. Find the height of the tower.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Distance from observer to base = 20 / \tan 45° = 20 m. Then total height to top of tower = 20 \tan 60° = 20\sqrt{3}.



Full solution

Tower height = 20\sqrt{3} - 20 = 20(\sqrt{3} - 1) \approx 14.64 m.

Q4 (2 marks). The angle of elevation of the top of a hill from the foot of a tower is 60° and the angle of elevation of the top of the tower from the foot of the hill is 30°. If the tower is 50 m high, find the height of the hill.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Distance between foot of tower and foot of hill = D. From foot of hill: \tan 30° = 50/D \Rightarrow D = 50\sqrt{3}. From foot of tower: \tan 60°
= H/D \Rightarrow H = D\sqrt{3}.



Full solution

D = 50\sqrt{3}. H = 50\sqrt{3} \cdot \sqrt{3} = 150 m. Hill: 150 m.

Q5 (3 marks). As observed from the top of a 75 m lighthouse, the angles of depression of two ships are 30° and 45°. If one ship is exactly behind the other (on the same side of the lighthouse), find the distance between them.


Full solution

D_1 at 45°: D_1 = 75. D_2 at 30°: D_2 = 75\sqrt{3}. Distance = D_2 - D_1 = 75(\sqrt{3} - 1) \approx 54.9 m.

Q6 (3 marks). A man on the deck of a ship 16 m above sea level observes that the angle of elevation of the top of a cliff is 45°. The angle of depression of the base of the cliff is 30°. Find the height of the cliff and the distance from the ship.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Distance to base of cliff = 16 / \tan 30° = 16\sqrt{3}. Cliff height above deck = 16\sqrt{3} \cdot \tan 45° = 16\sqrt{3}. Total cliff height = deck height + above-deck = 16 + 16\sqrt{3}.



Full solution

Distance from ship: 16\sqrt{3} \approx 27.71 m. Cliff height: 16(1 + \sqrt{3}) \approx 43.71 m.

Q7 (5 marks — long answer). A person standing on the bank of a river observes that the angle of elevation of the top of a tree on the opposite bank is 60°. When he moves 40 m away from the bank, he finds the angle of elevation to be 30°. Find: (a) the height of the tree, and (b) the width of the river.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let tree height = H, river width = W. Two equations.



Full solution

At first position (on bank): \tan 60° = H/W \Rightarrow H = W\sqrt{3}. After walking 40 m back: \tan 30° = H/(W + 40) \Rightarrow H = (W + 40)/
\sqrt{3}. Equate: W\sqrt{3} = (W + 40)/\sqrt{3} \Rightarrow 3W = W + 40 \Rightarrow
2W = 40 \Rightarrow W = 20 m. H = 20\sqrt{3} \approx 34.64 m.
(a) Height of tree: 20\sqrt{3} m. (b) Width of river: 20 m.







NCERT Exemplar pointer: Exemplar 9.3 Q1–Q12 cover single-triangle height/distance problems like Q3 and Q4; Exemplar 9.4 Q1–Q12 are full two-angle problems like Q5–Q7.
Previous-year pointer: CBSE 2020 Set 1, Q33 mirrors Q7 (river crossing). CBSE 2023 Standard, Q35 mirrors Q6 (ship-and-cliff).





Common confusions

“Angle of depression is measured from vertical.” No — it is measured from the horizontal at the higher point, looking down. So it equals (alternate-interior) the angle of elevation from the lower point.
“\tan 30° = \sqrt{3}.” No: \tan 30° = 1/\sqrt{3}. \tan 60° =
\sqrt{3}. Memorise the table; one swap-mistake costs ₹2-5.
“All ground distances are positive, so I can drop the absolute values.” True for the magnitude. But signed distance matters when you compute “distance between two points on the same side” vs “on opposite sides” — sketch carefully before solving.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

Sketch the triangle first. Every wrong answer in this chapter begins with a sketch error, not an arithmetic one.
Angle of depression equals angle of elevation across the horizontal. This single fact converts every “looking down” problem into a “looking up” problem.
Two angles, two equations, two unknowns. When the problem gives two viewpoints, the height-and-distance algebra is forced.



Self-Assessment

Could you solve a two-angle problem (Q4, Q5, Q7) without notes, sketching the figure first?
Did Q3 and Q6 fall out cleanly with at most one hint?
Can you recognise when a problem mixes elevation and depression (observer at height, target on ground or vice versa)?


3 yes: move on.
2 yes: redo Q5 and Q7. They’re the modal board questions.
0–1 yes: the mistake is almost always in the sketch. Re-walk the Apply worked example with paper and pencil.
crumbs: Ch 9 — Some Applications of Trigonometry (Heights & Distances)

title: Ch 9 — Some Applications of Trigonometry (Heights & Distances)
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

cbse10.trigonometry-intro — trig ratios and the special-angle table.



What this unlocks

cbse11.trigonometric-functions — the unit-circle generalisation; sum, difference, double-angle.



NCERT chapter alignment

Class 10, Chapter 9 — Some Applications of Trigonometry (NCERT 2023 rationalised syllabus). Covers Section 9.1: heights and distances. Common board scenarios: tower + flagstaff, ship + lighthouse, cloud + reflection.
Sister synthesis lesson: t3.trig-ratios — the ratios from a synthesis angle, with applications.
Read alongside the NCERT textbook.



Where this matters

Game development. Aiming a projectile in a 3D game uses the same right-triangle reasoning. The “lead a target” computation in first-person shooters: predict where the target will be, then compute the angle of elevation to send the projectile there.


Research. Surveying — measuring real-world land — uses these exact techniques. The Great Trigonometric Survey of India (1802–1871) measured the height of Mount Everest, then named “Peak XV”, using height-and-distance problems exactly like Q4 and Q7, scaled to hundreds of kilometres.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones: Better Explained (the Touch shadow narrative); Russian school (problem-first); Bhāskara (compact recipe).
Source notes: Q7 (river crossing) is the NCERT Example 5.5 archetype; Q5 (lighthouse) is NCERT Example 5.7.

title: Ch 14 — Probability (Classical Definition)
text:
Can you list all the outcomes when you toss a fair coin? When you roll a fair die?
Can you compute \dfrac{3}{6} in lowest terms? See t1.fractions-as-parts.
Do you know what equally likely means?
Can you list the 13 cards of a single suit in a deck of 52?

Three yeses, you are ready.

title: Ch 14 — Probability (Classical Definition)
Ch 14 — Probability (Classical Definition)
text:
Wonder

Tossing a coin: probability of heads is 1/2. If you toss it 10 times, will you get exactly 5 heads?
Rolling a die: probability of getting a 7?
Probability of getting any number from 1 to 6 on a die?
If P(A) = 0.3 — what is the probability that A does not happen?







TipWhat you might notice






Not necessarily — probability is about long-run, not single trials.
Zero — impossible event. (3) One — certain event. (4) 1 - 0.3 = 0.7.

title: Ch 14 — Probability (Classical Definition)
text:
Discover

Problem 1 — A coin toss
What is the probability of getting heads when a fair coin is tossed?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Two equally-likely outcomes: H and T. Favourable: 1 (just H).



Full solution

P(\text{H}) = 1/2.



Problem 2 — A die roll
What is the probability of getting an even number on a fair die?


Full solution

Even numbers: \{2, 4, 6\} — 3 outcomes. Total: 6. P = 3/6 = 1/2.



Problem 3 — Two coins
Two coins are tossed simultaneously. Find the probability of getting at least one head.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Sample space: HH, HT, TH, TT — 4 outcomes. Favourable: HH, HT, TH.



Full solution

P(\text{at least one H}) = 3/4.
Alternative: P = 1 - P(\text{no H}) = 1 - 1/4 = 3/4.



Problem 4 — Two dice
Two dice are rolled. What is the probability that the sum is 7?


Hint 1 — a nudge

List the pairs (d_1, d_2) that sum to 7: (1, 6), (2, 5), \ldots, (6, 1) — 6 pairs. Total: 6 \times 6 = 36.



Full solution

P = 6/36 = 1/6.



Problem 5 — Cards
A card is drawn at random from a well-shuffled standard deck. Find the probability it is: (a) a heart, (b) a queen, (c) a black queen, (d) the queen of hearts.


Full solution


13/52 = 1/4. (b) 4/52 = 1/13. (c) 2/52 = 1/26. (d) 1/52.




Problem 6 — Complementary event
A bag has 5 red, 4 green, 3 blue balls. One ball is drawn at random. What is the probability that it is not red?


Hint 1 — a nudge

P(\text{not red}) = 1 - P(\text{red}).



Full solution

P(\text{red}) = 5/12. P(\text{not red}) = 7/12.
Or directly: P(\text{green or blue}) = 7/12.


Pascal and Fermat invent probability (1654). The Chevalier de Méré, a French nobleman with a gambling habit, asked his friend Blaise Pascal a question: if a dice game is interrupted before it ends, how should the pot be divided fairly between the players? Pascal wrote to Pierre de Fermat in Toulouse, and the resulting summer of letters between the two created the theory of probability almost from scratch — including the classical definition P = m/n that you have just learned. Within a generation, Christiaan Huygens turned the correspondence into the first textbook on probability. From a question about gambling came actuarial science, statistical physics, and modern machine learning.


Probability is a fraction: favourable outcomes over total outcomes, provided every outcome in the sample space is equally likely. Almost every Class 10 problem reduces to careful counting — listing the sample space, listing the favourable subset, dividing. The mistake is almost always in the count, not the division.

title: Ch 14 — Probability (Classical Definition)
text:
Name

Classical (theoretical) definition of probability. If a random experiment has n equally likely outcomes, and an event E corresponds to m of them (the favourable outcomes), then P(E) = \dfrac{m}{n} = \dfrac{\text{number of outcomes favourable to }
E}{\text{total number of outcomes}}.
Properties:

0 \le P(E) \le 1 for every event E.
P(\emptyset) = 0 (impossible event).
P(\text{sample space}) = 1 (certain event).
P(\bar{E}) = 1 - P(E), where \bar{E} is the complement of E (“E does not happen”).


A verse:

Count the favourables, count the totals, divide — that’s chance. All outcomes equal? Then the rule does its dance. Zero is impossible, one is sure to be — Complement plus event makes one, you’ll see.

title: Ch 14 — Probability (Classical Definition)
text:
Apply

Worked example

A box contains 90 discs numbered 1 to 90. One disc is drawn at random. Find the probability that the number on the drawn disc is: (a) a two-digit number, (b) a perfect square, (c) divisible by 5.


Two-digit numbers in \{1, \ldots, 90\}: 10 to 90. Count = 90 - 10 + 1 = 81. P = 81/90 = 9/10.
Perfect squares from 1 to 90: 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49, 64, 81 — 9 of them. P = 9/90 = 1/10.
Divisible by 5: 5, 10, \ldots, 90. Count = 18. P = 18/90 = 1/5.



Practice — Warm
W1. A coin is tossed. P(tails)?


Full solution

1/2.

W2. A die is rolled. P(getting a 4)?


Full solution

1/6.

W3. A card is drawn. P(king of hearts)?


Full solution

1/52.



Practice — Working
P1. A bag contains 3 red, 5 black, 7 white balls. A ball is drawn at random. Find the probability that it is (a) white, (b) not black.


Full solution

Total = 15. (a) 7/15. (b) Not black = red or white = 10/15 = 2/3.

P2. A die is rolled twice. Find the probability of getting a 5 on the first roll and an even number on the second.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Independent events: multiply.



Full solution

P(\text{5 first}) = 1/6. P(\text{even second}) = 1/2. P(\text{both}) = 1/6 \cdot 1/2 = 1/12.
Alternative count: total outcomes 36; favourable (5, 2), (5, 4), (5,
6) — 3 outcomes. 3/36 = 1/12. ✓

P3. Two players, A and B, take turns drawing one card each from a shuffled deck (without replacement, A first). Find P(\text{A draws a
king on the first try}) and P(\text{B draws a king on her first try,
given A drew a non-king}).


Hint 1 — a nudge

First: 4 kings out of 52. Second: 4 kings out of remaining 51, given the first card wasn’t a king.



Full solution

P(\text{A king}) = 4/52 = 1/13. P(\text{B king} \mid \text{A non-king}) = 4/51.



Practice — Stretch
S1. A child has a die whose six faces show the letters A, B, C, D, E, A. The die is thrown once. Find the probability that the letter is (a) A, (b) D, (c) a vowel.


Full solution


2 As out of 6: 1/3. (b) 1 D: 1/6. (c) Vowels: A, A, E — 3 out of 6: 1/2.


S2. A jar contains 24 marbles, some green and others blue. If a marble is drawn at random and the probability it is green is 2/3, find the number of blue marbles.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Green count = 24 \cdot 2/3.



Full solution

Green = 16. Blue = 24 - 16 = 8.



Board-Pattern Practice
Q1 (1 mark — MCQ). The probability of an impossible event is: (a) 1 (b) 0 (c) 1/2 (d) less than 0.


Full solution

(b) 0.

Q2 (1 mark — assertion-reason). Assertion (A): The probability of getting a number more than 6 on a single throw of a die is 0. Reason (R): A die has only 6 faces, numbered 1 to 6.


Full solution

A is true, R is true, R correctly explains A.
