Look at the “-1” or “+1” terms. They are nearly always asking you to substitute a Pythagorean identity in disguise.



Self-Assessment

Could you derive all three Pythagorean identities from a^2 + b^2 =
c^2, without notes?
Did Q5 and Q7 fall out cleanly with at most one hint?
Could you spot, in an unfamiliar identity, which side to start manipulating (the more complex one)?


3 yes: move on.
2 yes: redo P1, P3, Q5. The board paper picks one of these identity-proof shapes nearly every year.
0–1 yes: drill, drill, drill. The proofs are mechanical once you internalise the three identities and the “convert all to sin, cos” reflex.

title: Ch 8b — Trigonometric Identities
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

cbse10.trigonometry-intro — the six trig ratios and the special-angle table.



What this unlocks

cbse10.heights-and-distances — applications.
cbse11.trigonometric-functions — the unit-circle generalisation, and many more identities (sum, difference, double-angle).



NCERT chapter alignment

Class 10, Chapter 8 — Introduction to Trigonometry, Section 8.4 (NCERT 2023 rationalised syllabus). Covers the three identities and their applications.
Sister synthesis lesson: t3.unit-circle — the same identities derived from the unit-circle equation x^2 + y^2 = 1.
Read alongside the NCERT textbook.



Where this matters

Research. The Pythagorean identity is the simplest case of a conservation law: a quantity that does not change as a parameter varies. In physics and signal processing, \sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1 is the constancy of energy of a unit-amplitude oscillator.


Game development. Normalisation — making a 2D vector have length 1 — divides by \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}, the Pythagorean length. The result is exactly (\cos\theta, \sin\theta) for some \theta. The identity is the engine.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones: Bhāskara (compact Name); Russian school (problem- first, identities derived, not stated).
Source notes: Q5 is the NCERT-canonical “double identity” pattern; Q7 is the NCERT 8.4 Example 11 archetype.

title: Ch 4 — Quadratic Equations
text:
Can you factor x^2 - 5x + 6? See cbse10.polynomials.
Can you compute \sqrt{49} and \sqrt{50} (the second left in surd form)?
Do you know what a zero of a quadratic polynomial is?
Can you expand (x + 3)^2 in your head?

If yes to most, proceed.

title: Ch 4 — Quadratic Equations
Ch 4 — Quadratic Equations
text:
Wonder

The ball’s height is 0 at the start and at the end. So the equation h = 0 has two solutions. Could one of them have been negative? What would a negative time mean?
What if the equation were h = 25? Would there be a time the ball is at 25 m?
The expression 20t - 5t^2 factors easily. Try 5t(4 - t). Could every quadratic be factored over the integers?
If a parabola does not touch the x-axis at all (e.g. y = x^2 + 1), what does that say about the equation x^2 + 1 = 0 over the reals?







TipWhat you might notice






is the cleanest entry into the discriminant: peak height is 20 m, so h = 25 is impossible — there is no real solution. (4) is the same phenomenon: x^2 + 1 = 0 has no real solution.

title: Ch 4 — Quadratic Equations
text:
Discover

Problem 1 — Factoring
Solve x^2 - 7x + 12 = 0 by factoring.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Two numbers multiplying to 12 and summing to 7.



Full solution

(x - 3)(x - 4) = 0, so x = 3 or x = 4.



Problem 2 — Completing the square
Solve x^2 + 6x - 7 = 0 without factoring. Move the constant. Add and subtract 9 on the left to make a perfect square.


Hint 1 — a nudge

x^2 + 6x = 7. Add 9 to both sides.



Hint 2 — the technique

(x + 3)^2 = 16. Take square roots.



Full solution

(x + 3)^2 = 16 \Rightarrow x + 3 = \pm 4 \Rightarrow x = 1 \text{ or } -7.



Problem 3 — The general case
Apply the same trick to ax^2 + bx + c = 0 (with a > 0). What do you get?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Divide by a first. Then complete the square.



Hint 2 — the technique

x^2 + \dfrac{b}{a}x + \dfrac{c}{a} = 0. Complete the square: add and subtract \left(\dfrac{b}{2a}\right)^2.



Full solution

\left(x + \dfrac{b}{2a}\right)^2 = \dfrac{b^2 - 4ac}{4a^2}. Take square roots: x = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}. That is the quadratic formula. The thing under the root sign, D = b^2 - 4ac, decides everything.



Problem 4 — Discriminant cases
For each of the following, compute D = b^2 - 4ac and predict how many real roots the equation has: (a) x^2 - 4x + 4 = 0, (b) x^2 - 5x + 6 = 0, (c) x^2 + 2x + 3 = 0.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Plug into b^2 - 4ac.



Full solution


D = 16 - 16 = 0 — one real root (a “double” root).
D = 25 - 24 = 1 > 0 — two distinct real roots.
D = 4 - 12 = -8 < 0 — no real roots.

The pattern: D > 0 → two real roots; D = 0 → one (repeated); D < 0 → no real roots.



Problem 5 — A real situation
A field is rectangular. Its length is 3 m more than its breadth, and its area is 54 \text{ m}^2. Find the breadth.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let breadth = x. Then length = x + 3, and x(x + 3) = 54.



Full solution

x^2 + 3x - 54 = 0. Factor: (x + 9)(x - 6) = 0, so x = -9 or x = 6. A breadth cannot be negative, so x = 6 m. (Length = 9 m.)
Notice — both solutions came from the algebra, but only one fits the problem. Always check feasibility.



Problem 6 — Roots and coefficients
Quadratic with roots \alpha, \beta: \alpha + \beta = -b/a, \alpha\beta = c/a. (You proved this in cbse10.polynomials.) Use it: find a quadratic whose roots are 2 + \sqrt{3} and 2 - \sqrt{3}.


Full solution

Sum = 4. Product = 4 - 3 = 1. Polynomial: x^2 - 4x + 1 = 0.


The “completing the square” trick in P2 was originally a literal picture. al-Khwārizmī, in al-Kitāb al-mukhtaṣar fī ḥisāb al-jabr wa-l-muqābala (Baghdad, c. 820 CE — the book whose title gave us the word algebra), solved x^2 + 10x = 39 by drawing a square of side x, gluing on two 5 \times x rectangles, and literally completing the L-shape into a larger square of side x + 5. Three centuries later, Bhāskara II in his Bījagaṇita (1150 CE) gave essentially the modern formula along with the warning that a quadratic has two roots — including the negative one, which al-Khwārizmī’s geometric picture had quietly hidden.


Completing the square is the quadratic formula. Every step of the formula — halve b/a, square it, take a square root — is just bookkeeping for the geometric move of turning ax^2 + bx + c into a(x - h)^2 + k. Once the expression is a square, the discriminant tells you whether the right-hand side admits a real square root at all.

title: Ch 4 — Quadratic Equations
text:
Name

A quadratic equation in x is one of the form ax^2 + bx + c = 0, \qquad a \ne 0.
Quadratic formula. The roots are x = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}.
Discriminant D = b^2 - 4ac classifies the roots:



D > 0
D = 0
D < 0




Two distinct real roots
One real root (repeated)
No real roots



A quadratic has at most two real roots.

The verse:

Square the b, take four times a-c away, Plus or minus the root, then divide by two-a. Discriminant positive — twice the line is met; Zero, just kissed; negative, no real bet.

title: Ch 4 — Quadratic Equations
text:
Apply

Worked example

Solve 2x^2 - 5x + 3 = 0 by the quadratic formula.

a = 2, b = -5, c = 3. Discriminant: D = 25 - 24 = 1 > 0, so two real roots.
x = \dfrac{5 \pm \sqrt{1}}{4} = \dfrac{5 \pm 1}{4}.
So x = \dfrac{6}{4} = \dfrac{3}{2} or x = \dfrac{4}{4} = 1.
Roots: \dfrac{3}{2} and 1. (Verify by factoring: (2x - 3)(x - 1) =
2x^2 - 5x + 3. ✓)


Practice — Warm
W1. Solve x^2 - 9 = 0.


Full solution

x^2 = 9, x = \pm 3.

W2. Solve x^2 + 5x + 6 = 0.


Full solution

(x + 2)(x + 3) = 0 → x = -2, -3.

W3. Find the discriminant of 3x^2 - 2x - 1 = 0 and predict the nature of the roots.


Full solution

D = 4 + 12 = 16 > 0. Two distinct real roots. (Indeed (3x + 1)(x - 1) = 0 gives -1/3, 1.)



Practice — Working
P1. Solve by completing the square: x^2 - 4x - 1 = 0.


Hint 1 — a nudge

x^2 - 4x = 1. Add 4.



Full solution

(x - 2)^2 = 5 \Rightarrow x = 2 \pm \sqrt{5}.

P2. For what values of k does kx^2 + 2x + 1 = 0 have equal roots?


Hint 1 — a nudge

“Equal roots” means D = 0.



Full solution

D = 4 - 4k = 0 \Rightarrow k = 1. (Also need k \ne 0 for the equation to be quadratic at all.)

P3. Solve \sqrt{2} x^2 + 7x + 5\sqrt{2} = 0.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Look for two numbers multiplying to \sqrt{2} \cdot 5\sqrt{2} = 10 and summing to 7.



Full solution

5 and 2. Split: \sqrt{2}x^2 + 5x + 2x + 5\sqrt{2} = x(\sqrt{2}x + 5) + \sqrt{2}(\sqrt{2}x + 5) = (\sqrt{2}x + 5)(x + \sqrt{2}) = 0. x = -\dfrac{5}{\sqrt{2}} = -\dfrac{5\sqrt{2}}{2} or x = -\sqrt{2}.



Practice — Stretch
S1. A quadratic has integer coefficients and 2 - \sqrt{3} as one root. What is the other root, and write the smallest such polynomial.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Roots involving \sqrt{n} where n is not a perfect square come in conjugate pairs whenever the coefficients are rational.



Full solution

Other root: 2 + \sqrt{3}. Sum = 4, product = 4 - 3 = 1. Polynomial: \mathbf{x^2 - 4x + 1}.

S2. The product of two consecutive positive odd integers is 483. Find them.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let them be x and x + 2.



Full solution

x(x + 2) = 483 \Rightarrow x^2 + 2x - 483 = 0. D = 4 + 1932 = 1936, \sqrt{D} = 44. So x = \frac{-2 + 44}{2} = 21 (the positive root). The integers are 21 and 23.



Board-Pattern Practice
Q1 (1 mark — MCQ). The roots of the equation x^2 + x - 2 = 0 are: (a) -1, 2 (b) 1, -2 (c) -1, -2 (d) 1, 2.


Full solution

(x - 1)(x + 2) = 0. (b) 1, -2.

Q2 (1 mark — assertion-reason style). Assertion (A): The equation 2x^2 - 4x + 3 = 0 has no real roots. Reason (R): The discriminant of the equation is negative.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Compute D. Both A and R must be true.



Full solution

D = 16 - 24 = -8 < 0. So A is true, R is true, and R correctly explains A. Marked as such on board scripts.

Q3 (2 marks). Find the values of k for which 9x^2 + 3kx + 4 = 0 has equal roots.


Hint 1 — a nudge

D = 0.



Full solution

D = 9k^2 - 144 = 0 \Rightarrow k^2 = 16 \Rightarrow k = \pm 4.

Q4 (2 marks). Solve 4x^2 + 4\sqrt{3}\,x + 3 = 0 using the quadratic formula.


Hint 1 — a nudge

a = 4, b = 4\sqrt{3}, c = 3.



Full solution

D = 48 - 48 = 0. So x = \dfrac{-4\sqrt{3}}{8} = -\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} (repeated).

Q5 (3 marks). The sum of a number and its reciprocal is \dfrac{17}{4}. Find the number.


Hint 1 — a nudge

x + 1/x = 17/4. Multiply by 4x.



Hint 2 — the technique

4x^2 - 17x + 4 = 0.



Full solution

D = 289 - 64 = 225, \sqrt{D} = 15. x = \dfrac{17 \pm 15}{8} → x = 4 or x = \dfrac{1}{4}. The number is 4 (or \frac{1}{4}). (They are reciprocals of each other, consistent with the symmetry of the problem.)

Q6 (3 marks). A train travels 360 km at uniform speed. If the speed had been 5 km/h more, it would have taken 1 hour less. Find the speed.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let speed = u. Time = 360/u. Then \dfrac{360}{u + 5} = \dfrac{360}{u}
- 1.



Hint 2 — the technique

Multiply through by u(u + 5) and simplify to a quadratic.



Full solution

\dfrac{360}{u} - \dfrac{360}{u + 5} = 1 \Rightarrow \dfrac{360 \cdot 5}
{u(u + 5)} = 1 \Rightarrow u^2 + 5u - 1800 = 0. D = 25 + 7200 = 7225, \sqrt{D} = 85. u = \dfrac{-5 + 85}{2} = 40. Speed = 40 km/h.

Q7 (5 marks — long answer). Two pipes running together can fill a tank in \dfrac{40}{13} minutes. If one pipe takes 3 minutes more than the other to fill the tank separately, find the time in which each pipe would fill the tank alone.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let the faster pipe take t minutes alone; the slower, t + 3.



Hint 2 — the technique

“Working together” means rates add: in one minute, the faster fills \frac{1}{t} of the tank and the slower \frac{1}{t+3}. Their sum is the combined rate \dfrac{13}{40}.



Hint 3 — the first step

Set \dfrac{1}{t} + \dfrac{1}{t+3} = \dfrac{13}{40} and clear denominators by multiplying through by 40\,t(t+3).



Full solution

Set up. Let t = minutes the faster pipe alone needs. The slower needs t + 3. In one minute together they fill 
\dfrac{1}{t} + \dfrac{1}{t+3} \;=\; \dfrac{13}{40}.

Clear denominators. Multiply both sides by 40\,t(t+3): 
40(t+3) + 40t \;=\; 13\,t(t+3),
 
80t + 120 \;=\; 13t^2 + 39t,
 
13t^2 - 41t - 120 \;=\; 0.

Solve. D = 41^2 + 4\cdot 13 \cdot 120 = 1681 + 6240 = 7921, and \sqrt{7921} = 89. 
t \;=\; \dfrac{41 \pm 89}{26} \;=\; 5 \text{ or } -\dfrac{48}{26}.

Reject infeasible. A negative time has no physical meaning, so t = 5.
Answer. The faster pipe takes \boxed{5 \text{ minutes}} alone; the slower takes \boxed{8 \text{ minutes}} alone.
Check. \frac{1}{5} + \frac{1}{8} = \frac{8+5}{40} = \frac{13}{40} of the tank per minute, so together they need \frac{40}{13} minutes to fill it. ✓
Pedagogical takeaway. After setting up the quadratic, check that the discriminant is a perfect square — for clean board questions it always is. If \sqrt{D} comes out irrational, re-read the question for a mis-copied number before continuing.







NCERT Exemplar pointer: Exemplar 4.3 Q3–Q11 are “discriminant + nature-of-roots” questions like Q3; Exemplar 4.4 Q1–Q11 are full word-problems like Q5–Q7.
Previous-year pointer: CBSE 2023 Set 1, Q26 is essentially Q5. CBSE 2020 Set 1, Q33 is the same shape as Q6.





Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A quadratic equation is solved by factoring when you can see the factors — and by completing the square (i.e. the quadratic formula) when you cannot.
The discriminant D = b^2 - 4ac tells you the nature of the roots before you compute them: D > 0 two real, D = 0 one repeated, D < 0 none real.
Word problems demand a feasibility check. Both algebraic roots are valid algebraically; reject negative lengths, times, and counts using the zero-product property only after physical sanity.



Common confusions

“D < 0 means there’s an error.” No — it means no real roots. The parabola simply does not cross the x-axis. Real-world feasibility may force you to reject this case (no real time, no real length), but the algebra is still correct.
“Both roots of a quadratic are always valid answers.” Algebraically yes; physically no. A negative breadth, a negative time, or a non-integer number of children must be rejected. Always check.
“Equal roots means one root.” Convention: a double root is counted twice. The equation has one distinct value, but mathematicians say the equation has a repeated root of multiplicity 2.



Self-Assessment

Could you derive the quadratic formula by completing the square, without notes?
Did you correctly use the discriminant in Q3 and Q4 (read the case from D, not from a guess)?
Did you check feasibility in every word problem (reject negative lengths, times, etc.)?


3 yes: move on.
2 yes: redo Q5 and Q6 with feasibility commentary.
0–1 yes: completing-the-square is the load-bearing skill. Re-read Discover, Problem 3. Re-derive the formula.

title: Ch 4 — Quadratic Equations
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

cbse10.polynomials — zeros and Vieta relations for quadratics.
cbse9.number-systems — surds and rationals.



What this unlocks

cbse10.arithmetic-progressions — AP problems often reduce to quadratics in n.
cbse11.complex-numbers — D < 0 becomes meaningful when complex roots are admitted.
cbse11.sequences-series.



NCERT chapter alignment

Class 10, Chapter 4 — Quadratic Equations (NCERT 2023 rationalised syllabus). Covers Sections 4.1–4.4: factorisation, quadratic formula, nature of roots. (Section 4.3 completing the square method was retained as a derivation route to the formula.)
Sister synthesis lessons:

t2.quadratic-equations-intro — first encounter with quadratics, factoring focus.
t3.quadratic-formula — the formula derived again, plus complex roots.

Read alongside the NCERT textbook.



Where this matters

Game development. Projectile trajectories, collision predictions, and ball-bounce timings all reduce to quadratic equations like the football example in Touch. Every game-engine physics module solves thousands per second.


Research. The discriminant — and the qualitative branching it encodes — is the prototype for bifurcation in dynamical systems. The moment a parameter crosses D = 0, behaviour changes character.


AI/ML. The optimisation step in many ML algorithms (least squares, ridge regression) closes in one step on a quadratic objective — exactly the algebraic shape you’ve been solving by hand.








NoteAuthor’s notes






Touchstones: Better Explained (the projectile metaphor in Touch); Russian school (problem-first Discover); Bhāskara (compact Name).
Q7 caveat: the standard NCERT pipes-problem variant uses \frac{40}{13}; the printed \frac{40}{3} admits no clean factor pair. The full solution discusses this honestly to model board-exam diagnosis.

title: Ch 3 — Understanding Quadrilaterals
text:
Be honest before you begin.

Can you tell a triangle from a quadrilateral on sight?
Do you remember that the angles of a triangle add up to 180^\circ? If not, revisit Shapes and Angles.
Can you measure an angle with a protractor?
Do you know what parallel lines means — lines that never meet?

If three were a confident yes, read on.

title: Ch 3 — Understanding Quadrilaterals
Ch 3 — Understanding Quadrilaterals
text:
Wonder
Sit with these.

As you slid the corner, the four angles each changed. Did their sum change?
Two opposite sides of your loop look parallel. Does the loop have to have parallel sides for the corners to form?
If two adjacent sides are equal in length, what does the shape start to look like?
Could a four-sided closed shape have a reflex angle (more than 180^\circ) at one corner?







TipWhat you might notice (don’t peek until you’ve thought)





The angle sum stays at 360^\circ no matter how you push. The chapter is built around that fact, plus a tree of special quadrilaterals — each defined by which sides or angles are made equal or parallel.

title: Ch 3 — Understanding Quadrilaterals
text:
Discover

Problem 1 — Cut a square along a diagonal
Take a paper square and cut it along one diagonal. You now have two triangles. What can you say about each of them?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Are the triangles right-angled? Are they identical?



Full solution

The cut goes corner to corner, so each triangle has the right-angle corner of the square. The two legs are equal (sides of the square). The two triangles are congruent right isoceles triangles. Each has angles 90^\circ, 45^\circ, 45^\circ.
The angles of the square split as: at the cut corners, 90^\circ =
45^\circ + 45^\circ. At the uncut corners, the 90^\circ stays intact. Sum: 90 + 45 + 45 + 90 + 45 + 45 = 360^\circ.



Problem 2 — Cut any quadrilateral
Now do it again with a random (non-square) four-sided paper shape. Cut along one diagonal. You get two triangles. What is the sum of all the angles in those two triangles?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Each triangle’s angles add to 180^\circ. There are two of them.



Hint 2 — the technique

2 \times 180^\circ = 360^\circ. Now: those six triangle-angles together account for the four corners of the original quadrilateral — each quadrilateral corner is split into two pieces (or stays whole) by the diagonal.



Full solution

Two triangles together have 2 \times 180 = 360^\circ of angle. When you reassemble into the quadrilateral, those angles regroup into the four corner angles. So the angle sum of any quadrilateral is 360^\circ.



Problem 3 — A missing angle
A quadrilateral has angles 80^\circ, 90^\circ, 110^\circ, and a fourth that you don’t know. Find the fourth angle.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Use the fact you just proved.



Full solution

80 + 90 + 110 + x = 360 \Rightarrow x = 80^\circ.



Problem 4 — Spot the parallelogram property
Draw a parallelogram (a four-sided shape with both pairs of opposite sides parallel). Measure both pairs of opposite sides.


Hint 1 — a nudge

You don’t need to measure to predict.



Full solution

Opposite sides of a parallelogram are equal in length as well as parallel. (Reason: drawing a diagonal makes two congruent triangles by ASA — alternate interior angles plus the shared diagonal.)



Problem 5 — Diagonals of a rhombus
A rhombus has all four sides equal. Draw both diagonals. What do you notice about them where they cross?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Look at the angle they make. And the lengths from the crossing point to the corners.



Full solution

The diagonals of a rhombus cross at right angles and bisect each other. (Each diagonal divides the rhombus into two congruent triangles which are mirror images.)



Problem 6 — Exterior angles
You walk around a closed quadrilateral path, turning at each corner. You return to your start. Through what total angle have you turned?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Walking around any closed shape and ending up where you started, facing the same way, your total turn is one full circle.



Full solution

360^\circ. The sum of the exterior angles of any convex polygon — not just a quadrilateral — is 360^\circ.

You have just discovered the angle sum, the parallelogram side property, the rhombus diagonal property, and the exterior-angle fact — the four load-bearing facts of the chapter.

Euclid’s Elements (c. 300 BCE), the most-printed mathematics textbook in history, classified quadrilaterals in Book I, Definitions 22 — square, oblong (rectangle), rhombus, rhomboid (parallelogram), and trapezia — almost the same family tree you just met. Two thousand three hundred years later, the names live on; the only major change is that “trapezium” in modern Indian and British usage means “at least one pair of parallel sides”, whereas Euclid’s trapezia meant “no pairs parallel”. The geometry, though, is unchanged.


A quadrilateral is two triangles glued along a diagonal. That single move explains everything: angle sum is 2 \times 180^\circ = 360^\circ, parallelograms split into a pair of congruent triangles, rhombi into four right triangles, and rectangles into two equal right triangles. Whenever you are stuck on a quadrilateral problem, draw the diagonal — the triangles you already know will do the work.

title: Ch 3 — Understanding Quadrilaterals
text:
Name

A quadrilateral is a closed plane figure bounded by four line segments.
Angle-sum property: the four interior angles of any quadrilateral add to 360^\circ.
Family tree (each name adds one more constraint):

Trapezium — at least one pair of parallel sides.
Parallelogram — both pairs of opposite sides parallel; opposite sides equal; opposite angles equal; diagonals bisect each other.
Rectangle — parallelogram + all angles 90^\circ; diagonals equal.
Rhombus — parallelogram + all sides equal; diagonals perpendicular and bisect each other.
Square — rectangle + rhombus; all of the above at once.
Kite — two pairs of adjacent sides equal; one diagonal bisects the other at 90^\circ.


A short verse, in the spirit of Bhāskara’s Līlāvatī:

Four-sided closed — three-sixty in all; Both pairs parallel, sides match, angles match, diagonals halve. Square the prince of the family — every property at once; Rhombus the dancer — perpendicular cross at the heart.

title: Ch 3 — Understanding Quadrilaterals
text:
Apply

Worked example

In a parallelogram ABCD, \angle A = 70^\circ. Find the other three angles.

Competent solver’s path:

Opposite angles of a parallelogram are equal: \angle C = \angle A =
70^\circ.
Adjacent angles are supplementary (they sit on the same side of a transversal cutting two parallel lines): \angle B = 180 - 70 =
110^\circ.
By the same rule, \angle D = 110^\circ.

Check: 70 + 110 + 70 + 110 = 360^\circ. ✓


Practice — Warm
W1. Three angles of a quadrilateral are 60^\circ, 70^\circ, 130^\circ. Find the fourth.


Full solution

60 + 70 + 130 + x = 360 \Rightarrow x = 100^\circ.

W2. True or false: every square is a rectangle.


Full solution

True. A rectangle requires four right angles; a square has them plus equal sides. Every square satisfies the rectangle definition.

W3. True or false: every rhombus is a square.


Full solution

False. A rhombus needs only equal sides; its angles need not be 90^\circ. A square is a rhombus, but not every rhombus is a square.



Practice — Working
P1. In a parallelogram PQRS, \angle P = 3\angle Q. Find all four angles.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Adjacent angles of a parallelogram sum to 180^\circ.



Full solution

\angle P + \angle Q = 180 and \angle P = 3\angle Q. 3\angle Q + \angle Q = 180 \Rightarrow \angle Q = 45^\circ, \angle P = 135^\circ. Opposite angles equal: \angle R = 45^\circ, \angle S = 135^\circ.

P2. A rhombus has a diagonal of length 24 cm and the other of length 10 cm. Find the side length.


Hint 1 — a nudge

The diagonals bisect at right angles. Each side is the hypotenuse of a right triangle with half-diagonals as legs.



Full solution

Half-diagonals: 12 cm and 5 cm. Side = \sqrt{12^2 + 5^2} = \sqrt{144
+ 25} = \sqrt{169} = 13 cm.

P3. The exterior angle at one vertex of a regular hexagon is what?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Sum of exterior angles is 360^\circ, divided equally among 6 corners.



Full solution

360 / 6 = 60^\circ.



Practice — Stretch
S1. In quadrilateral ABCD, \angle A : \angle B : \angle C :
\angle D = 1 : 2 : 3 : 4. Find each angle and identify any pair of parallel sides if you can.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Total = 1+2+3+4 = 10 parts. Total angle = 360^\circ.



Full solution

Each part = 36^\circ. Angles: 36^\circ, 72^\circ, 108^\circ, 144^\circ.
Adjacent pairs: \angle A + \angle B = 108, \angle B + \angle C =
180^\circ, \angle C + \angle D = 252, \angle D + \angle A = 180^\circ.
Two adjacent sums equal 180^\circ: that means BC \parallel AD (co-interior angles supplementary). It is a trapezium.

S2. A kite has two pairs of consecutive equal sides AB = AD = 5 cm and CB = CD = 12 cm, with \angle B = \angle D = 90^\circ. Find the length of diagonal AC.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Triangle ABC is right-angled at B.



Full solution

AC = \sqrt{AB^2 + BC^2} = \sqrt{25 + 144} = \sqrt{169} = 13 cm.



Board-Pattern Practice
1-mark (objective). Sum of the interior angles of a polygon with 7 sides is (a) 720^\circ (b) 900^\circ (c) 1080^\circ (d) 540^\circ.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Formula: (n-2) \times 180^\circ.



Full solution

(7-2) \times 180 = 5 \times 180 = 900^\circ. Answer: (b).

2-mark (short answer). Two adjacent angles of a parallelogram are in the ratio 2:3. Find each angle of the parallelogram.


Full solution

Adjacent angles sum to 180^\circ. Parts: 2+3 = 5. Each part = 36^\circ. Angles: 72^\circ, 108^\circ, 72^\circ, 108^\circ.

3-mark (short answer II). ABCD is a parallelogram. The bisectors of \angle A and \angle B meet at P. Show that \angle APB =
90^\circ.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\angle A + \angle B = 180^\circ.



Hint 2 — the technique

In triangle APB: \angle PAB = \tfrac{1}{2}\angle A and \angle PBA =
\tfrac{1}{2}\angle B.



Full solution

\angle PAB + \angle PBA = \tfrac{1}{2}(\angle A + \angle B) =
\tfrac{1}{2}(180^\circ) = 90^\circ.
In triangle APB, angles sum to 180^\circ: \angle APB = 180^\circ - 90^\circ = 90^\circ. ✓

4-mark (long answer). ABCD is a quadrilateral with \angle A =
100^\circ, \angle B = 80^\circ, \angle C = 100^\circ. (i) Find \angle
D. (ii) Show that AB \parallel DC. (iii) What special quadrilateral is ABCD at minimum?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Use the angle sum, then look for supplementary co-interior pairs.



Full solution

(i) \angle D = 360 - (100 + 80 + 100) = 80^\circ.
(ii) Consider sides AB and DC with transversal BC. The co-interior angles are \angle B = 80^\circ and \angle C = 100^\circ, summing to 180^\circ. So AB \parallel DC.
(iii) Consider sides AD and BC with transversal AB. Co-interior angles \angle A + \angle B = 180^\circ as well. So AD
\parallel BC.
Both pairs of opposite sides are parallel — ABCD is at minimum a parallelogram.







NCERT Exemplar pointer: Exemplar Problem 3.4 (angle bisectors in a parallelogram) is a near-twin of the 3-mark item. Previous-year pointer: CBSE 2018 Set 1, Q11 (“if three angles of a quadrilateral are given…”) matches the 4-mark item closely.





Common confusions

“A square is not a rectangle.” It is. The hierarchy is inclusive — every square satisfies the rectangle definition (four right angles).
“All quadrilaterals with equal diagonals are rectangles.” No. An isoceles trapezium also has equal diagonals. The full criterion for a rectangle is parallelogram with one right angle (or parallelogram with equal diagonals).
“Diagonals of a kite bisect each other.” Only one of them gets bisected (by the axis of symmetry). The other does the bisecting.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A quadrilateral is a closed plane figure with four sides — and the four interior angles always sum to 360^\circ, no matter the shape.
The family is a hierarchy: trapezium \subset parallelogram \subset rectangle \cap rhombus = square. Each name adds one more constraint on sides or angles.
When stuck, draw a diagonal. It splits any quadrilateral into two triangles — and the triangle properties you already know will solve the problem.



Self-Assessment
Be honest with yourself.

Could you draw the family tree of quadrilaterals from memory and say what extra constraint each name adds?
Did you solve at least 5 of the 6 Discover problems and 5 of the 7 Practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?
Given a quadrilateral with three known angles or two known sides, can you immediately spot which property to invoke?


3 yes: move on to Data Handling.
2 yes: redo Stretch S1 and the 4-mark board item, then move on.
0–1 yes: rebuild from Discover. The angle-sum proof in Problem 2 is the foundation of everything else.

title: Ch 3 — Understanding Quadrilaterals
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

Shapes and Angles — angles, parallel lines, the 180^\circ sum in a triangle.



What this unlocks

Mensuration — areas of trapezia, parallelograms, rhombi.
Class 9 — Quadrilaterals — proves the side and diagonal properties rigorously.
Class 9 — Triangles — congruence rules underpin the parallelogram proofs.



NCERT chapter alignment

Class 8, Chapter 3 — Understanding Quadrilaterals (NCERT 2023 rationalised syllabus). Covers Sections 3.1–3.4: polygon angle sums, the family tree, properties of parallelograms.
Sister synthesis lesson (different pedagogical angle): no direct synthesis lesson at this level; the closest non-CBSE treatment of the same material lives in tier 3 geometry, but it assumes a fuller proof background. Stay with this chapter and the NCERT textbook.
Read alongside the NCERT textbook, which remains the canonical source for the board paper.



Where this matters

Game development. Every flat surface in a 2D game — platforms, tile maps, UI panels — is a rectangle or quadrilateral mesh. Collision detection between rotated rectangles uses the diagonal and side properties of this chapter.


Research. Tessellations of the plane by quadrilaterals are at the heart of finite-element methods, the numerical workhorse for solving partial differential equations. The mesh quality is judged by how close each cell is to a square — the prince of the family.

title: Ch 7 — Comparing Quantities
text:
Be honest before you begin.

Do you know that \tfrac{1}{2} means the same as 50\%? If not, revisit Fractions as Parts.
Are you comfortable with ratios like 3 : 4? If not, revisit Ratio and Proportion.
Can you compute 25\% of 200 in your head?
If something costs \text{₹}80 and you sell it for \text{₹}100, would you call that a profit or a loss?

If three were a confident yes, read on.

title: Ch 7 — Comparing Quantities
Ch 7 — Comparing Quantities
text:
Wonder
Sit with these.

A discount of 20\% on \text{₹}500 — is that the same number of rupees as 50\% on \text{₹}200?
If a price increases by 10\% and then decreases by 10\%, do you end up at the original price?
You earn \text{₹}100 interest in one year on \text{₹}1000. Next year, would you expect the same \text{₹}100 — or more, or less?
If a bag’s marked price is \text{₹}800 but you bought it for \text{₹}600, what fraction of the marked price was the discount?







TipWhat you might notice (don’t peek until you’ve thought)





Question 2 has a counter-intuitive answer: +10\% then -10\% does not return you to the start. (100 \to 110 \to 99.) Percentages compose in a multiplicative way, not an additive way. That is the seed of compound interest, the headline idea of this chapter.

title: Ch 7 — Comparing Quantities
text:
Discover

Problem 1 — Percent of a quantity
What is 35\% of 80?


Hint 1 — a nudge

“35\%” means “\tfrac{35}{100}”.



Full solution

\tfrac{35}{100} \times 80 = \tfrac{35 \times 80}{100} = \tfrac{2800}{100}
= 28.



Problem 2 — Express as a percent
A class has 25 students; 9 are absent. What percentage are absent?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Compute \tfrac{9}{25}, then multiply by 100.



Full solution

\tfrac{9}{25} \times 100 = \tfrac{900}{25} = 36\%.



Problem 3 — Profit or loss
A trader buys a chair for \text{₹}1{,}200 and sells it for \text{₹}1{,}500. What is the profit, and what is the profit percent?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Profit = \text{SP} - \text{CP}. Profit percent uses CP as the base.



Hint 2 — the technique

Profit% = \tfrac{\text{Profit}}{\text{CP}} \times 100.



Full solution

Profit = 1500 - 1200 = \text{₹}300. Profit% = \tfrac{300}{1200} \times 100 = 25\%.



Problem 4 — Discount
The marked price (MP) of a shirt is \text{₹}800. After a discount of 15\%, what is the selling price (SP)?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Compute the discount amount, then subtract.



Full solution

Discount = 15\% of \text{₹}800 = \text{₹}120. SP = 800 - 120 = \text{₹}680.
(Equivalently: SP = 85\% of MP = 0.85 \times 800 = 680.)



Problem 5 — Simple interest
Find the simple interest (SI) on \text{₹}5{,}000 for 3 years at 8\% per annum.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\text{SI} = \tfrac{P \times R \times T}{100}.



Full solution

\text{SI} = \tfrac{5000 \times 8 \times 3}{100} = \tfrac{120000}{100} =
\text{₹}1200.
Total amount after 3 years: 5000 + 1200 = \text{₹}6200.



Problem 6 — Compound interest, year by year
Find the compound interest (CI) on \text{₹}1{,}000 for 2 years at 10\% per annum, compounded annually.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Year 1: simple interest. Year 2: interest on the new principal.



Hint 2 — the technique

After year 1: amount = 1000 + 100 = 1100. Year 2 interest is on 1100, not on 1000.



Full solution


After year 1: 1000 \times 1.10 = 1100.
After year 2: 1100 \times 1.10 = 1210.

CI = 1210 - 1000 = \text{₹}210.
(Compare with SI for the same case: \tfrac{1000 \cdot 10 \cdot 2}{100}
= \text{₹}200. Compound interest beats simple interest by \text{₹}10 — the “interest on interest”.)

You have just used the same engine — percent of a quantity — to compute discount, profit, simple interest, and compound interest. Four shop-floor problems, one idea.

In 1202 CE, the Pisan merchant–mathematician Leonardo of Pisa (now better known as Fibonacci) published Liber Abaci — “The Book of Calculation” — and introduced European traders to Hindu-Arabic numerals, percentage calculations, and currency conversion. One full chapter taught the method of computing per cento — “per hundred” — for problems of profit, loss, and interest, almost exactly the formulas you just used. Without that book, the modern banking and accounting system would have arrived several centuries later than it did.


Every formula in this chapter — discount, profit %, loss %, simple interest, compound interest, depreciation — is the same one move: multiply by (1 + \tfrac{r}{100}) to grow, by (1 - \tfrac{r}{100}) to shrink, and repeat if the change happens more than once. The difference between simple and compound interest is just whether you do that multiplication once on the original sum (simple) or once each period on the running total (compound).

title: Ch 7 — Comparing Quantities
text:
Name

Percent. r\% means \tfrac{r}{100}. To find r\% of a quantity x: compute \tfrac{r}{100} \cdot x.
Profit / loss. \text{Profit\%} = \frac{\text{SP} - \text{CP}}{\text{CP}} \times 100,
\qquad \text{Loss\%} = \frac{\text{CP} - \text{SP}}{\text{CP}} \times 100.
Simple interest. \text{SI} = \dfrac{P \cdot R \cdot T}{100}.
Compound interest, annual compounding. Amount after n years: A = P \left(1 + \tfrac{R}{100}\right)^n, and \text{CI} = A - P.

A short verse, in the spirit of Bhāskara’s Līlāvatī:

Percent is per-hundred — divide and you read it right. Profit on cost; discount on price displayed. Simple grows by addition; compound grows by multiplication — Each year a new pile, each pile breeds the next.

title: Ch 7 — Comparing Quantities
text:
Apply

Worked example

An article was bought for \text{₹}4{,}000 and sold so as to gain 15\%. Find the selling price.

Competent solver’s path:

Profit = 15\% of \text{₹}4000 = \text{₹}600.
SP = \text{CP} + \text{Profit} = 4000 + 600 = \text{₹}4600.

(Or directly: SP = 1.15 \times \text{CP} = 1.15 \times 4000 = \text{₹}
4600.)
Answer: \text{₹}4600.


Practice — Warm
W1. Convert \tfrac{3}{8} to a percentage.


Full solution

\tfrac{3}{8} = \tfrac{3 \times 100}{8}\% = 37.5\%.

W2. A pen costs \text{₹}50. After a 20\% discount, what is the selling price?


Full solution

Discount = \text{₹}10. SP = \text{₹}40.

W3. SI on \text{₹}8000 at 12\% for 2.5 years?


Full solution

\tfrac{8000 \times 12 \times 2.5}{100} = \tfrac{240000}{100} = \text{₹}
2400.



Practice — Working
P1. A shopkeeper marks his goods at 40\% above cost price and gives a discount of 10\%. Find his profit percent.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Pick a convenient CP — say \text{₹}100.



Full solution

Take CP = \text{₹}100. MP = 100 + 40 = \text{₹}140. SP = 140 - 10\% of 140 = 140 - 14 = \text{₹}126. Profit = 126 - 100 = \text{₹}26. Profit% = 26\%.

P2. Find CI on \text{₹}8000 for 2 years at 5\% per annum, compounded annually.


Hint 1 — a nudge

A = P(1 + R/100)^n.



Full solution

A = 8000 \times (1.05)^2 = 8000 \times 1.1025 = \text{₹}8820. CI = 8820 - 8000 = \text{₹}820.

P3. A scooter is bought for \text{₹}60{,}000 and depreciates by 10\% each year. Find its value after 2 years.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Depreciation works just like compound interest, but with a minus sign.



Full solution

V = 60000 \times (0.9)^2 = 60000 \times 0.81 = \text{₹}48{,}600.



Practice — Stretch
S1. The population of a town is 1{,}21{,}000 and grows at 10\% per year. What was the population two years ago?


Hint 1 — a nudge

The current population is after two years of growth: P_0 \times
(1.1)^2 = 121000.



Full solution

P_0 = \tfrac{121000}{(1.1)^2} = \tfrac{121000}{1.21} = 1{,}00{,}000.
Two years ago: 1,00,000 people.

S2. Sameer borrowed \text{₹}25{,}000 at 12\% p.a. compounded half-yearly for 1 year. Find the amount he repays.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Half-yearly compounding: rate per period is \tfrac{12}{2} = 6\%, and there are 2 periods.



Full solution

A = 25000 \times (1.06)^2 = 25000 \times 1.1236 = \text{₹}28{,}090.



Board-Pattern Practice
1-mark (objective). 25\% of 80 is (a) 15 (b) 20 (c) 25 (d) 30.


Full solution

\tfrac{25}{100} \times 80 = 20. Answer: (b).

2-mark (short answer). A man bought a watch for \text{₹}1{,}500 and sold it at a loss of 8\%. Find the selling price.


Full solution

Loss = 8\% of 1500 = \text{₹}120. SP = 1500 - 120 = \text{₹}1380.

3-mark (short answer II). A sum of money becomes \text{₹}6655 in 3 years at 10\% per annum, compounded annually. Find the principal.


Hint 1 — a nudge

A = P(1 + R/100)^n. Solve for P.



Hint 2 — the technique

P = \dfrac{A}{(1.1)^3}. (1.1)^3 = 1.331.



Full solution

P = \tfrac{6655}{1.331} = \text{₹}5000.
Check: 5000 \to 5500 \to 6050 \to 6655. ✓

4-mark (long answer). A trader allows a discount of 10\% on his marked price and still gains 20\%. (i) If the cost price of an article is \text{₹}540, find the marked price. (ii) Find the discount amount and the selling price.


Hint 1 — a nudge

SP = 1.20 \times \text{CP} (since gain is 20\%). SP = 0.90 \times \text{MP} (since discount is 10\%).



Full solution

(i) SP = 1.20 \times 540 = \text{₹}648. SP = 0.90 \times \text{MP} \Rightarrow \text{MP} = \tfrac{648}{0.9} =
\text{₹}720.
(ii) Discount = 10\% of 720 = \text{₹}72. SP = 720 - 72 = \text{₹}648. ✓







NCERT Exemplar pointer: Exemplar Problem 7.5 (interlocking discount and gain percent) is a near-twin of the 4-mark item. Previous-year pointer: CBSE 2018 Set 2, Q21 (“compound interest half-yearly”) matches Stretch S2 exactly.





Common confusions

“Profit% is computed on the SP.” It is on the CP. Always. Discount% is on the MP. Mixing the bases is the most common error in this chapter.
“+10\% then -10\% returns to the start.” It does not. (100 \to 110 \to 99.) Percentage changes compose multiplicatively.
“Compound interest is just n times simple interest.” No. Compound is bigger than that, because each year’s interest itself earns interest the next year.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A percent is a ratio out of 100 — and percent of a quantity is just \tfrac{r}{100} \times x. Every other formula in the chapter is built on this single move.
Profit % uses the cost price as base; discount % uses the marked price. Pick the wrong base and the answer is wrong.
Simple interest grows by addition; compound interest grows by multiplication. After n years at rate r: A = P\bigl(1 + \tfrac{r}{100}\bigr)^n — the same formula governs population growth, depreciation (with a minus), and bank deposits.



Self-Assessment
Be honest with yourself.

Could you explain to a friend, without notes, the difference between simple and compound interest using only one example?
Did you solve at least 5 of the 6 Discover problems and 5 of the 7 Practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?
Given a word problem mixing CP, SP, MP, and discount, can you sort them out and pick the right base for each percent?


3 yes: move on to Algebraic Expressions and Identities.
2 yes: redo Stretch S2 and the 4-mark board item, then move on.
0–1 yes: redo Discover Problem 6 — until compound interest feels like interest on interest, the chapter is not yet yours.

title: Ch 7 — Comparing Quantities
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

Ratio and Proportion — percent is a ratio out of 100.
Fractions as Parts — percent is a fraction in disguise.



What this unlocks

Direct and Inverse Proportions — generalises proportional reasoning beyond money.
Class 9 — Statistics — pie charts, frequency percentages.



NCERT chapter alignment

Class 8, Chapter 7 — Comparing Quantities (NCERT 2023 rationalised syllabus). Covers Sections 7.1–7.4: percentages, profit and loss, simple and compound interest.
Sister synthesis lesson (different pedagogical angle): t1.ratio-proportion treats proportional reasoning at the foundation level; this chapter applies it to money.
Read alongside the NCERT textbook, which remains the canonical source for the board paper.



Where this matters

Databases. Every analytics dashboard reports percentages — year-over-year growth, click-through rate, conversion percentage. The formulas you learnt today are the ones that engineers actually type into SQL queries.


AI / ML. Model accuracy (\tfrac{\text{correct}}{\text{total}}
\times 100) is a percent. So is precision, recall, and F1. The whole language of evaluating a classifier is the language of this chapter.


Research. The compound-interest formula A = P (1 + r/n)^{nt} is the discrete cousin of A = P e^{rt}, the exponential growth equation that governs population biology, finance, and bacterial colonies.

title: Ch 2 — Linear Equations in One Variable
text:
Be honest before you begin.

Can you read 3x + 5 as “three times some unknown number, plus five”? If not, revisit Intro to Variables.
Are you comfortable adding \tfrac{2}{3} and \tfrac{1}{4}? If not, revisit Rational Numbers.
If both sides of 2 = 2 are multiplied by 5, do you trust that the equation still holds?

If three were a confident yes, read on.
crumbs: Ch 2 — Linear Equations in One Variable

title: Ch 2 — Linear Equations in One Variable
crumbs: Ch 2 — Linear Equations in One Variable

title: Ch 2 — Linear Equations in One Variable
text:
Wonder
Sit with these.

If both pans gain the same extra weight, do they remain balanced?
If both pans are halved (you remove half the weight from each), do they still hang level?
The puzzle on the slider had only one balance point. Could a similar puzzle have two answers — two different stone-weights that work?
Could a balanced equation have no answer at all?







TipWhat you might notice (don’t peek until you’ve thought)





The balance is preserved if you do the same thing to both sides. That is the only rule the chapter really uses. Whether the equation has one answer, no answer, or many answers depends on its shape — we’ll see all three cases.
crumbs: Ch 2 — Linear Equations in One Variable

title: Ch 2 — Linear Equations in One Variable
text:
Discover

Problem 1 — Undo addition
A box has x apples. You put in 5 more. Now there are 12. How many were there?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Write the situation as an equation, then undo the +5.



Hint 2 — the technique

x + 5 = 12. Subtract 5 from both sides.



Full solution

x + 5 = 12 \;\Rightarrow\; x = 12 - 5 = 7. Seven apples.



Problem 2 — Undo multiplication
You have 3x rupees. You divide them equally among three friends; each gets 24 rupees. What is x?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Each friend got \tfrac{3x}{3} = x, but actually rethink: total = 3x, three friends, so each gets x. We’re told each gets 24.



Full solution

Each friend gets x = 24. Or read it differently: 3x = 72, so x = 24. (Multiplication is undone by division.)



Problem 3 — Two operations, one variable
Solve 2x + 3 = 11.


Hint 1 — a nudge

x is wrapped first by multiplying by 2, then by adding 3. Unwrap in reverse order.



Hint 2 — the technique

First subtract 3 from both sides; then divide by 2.



Full solution

2x + 3 = 11 \Rightarrow 2x = 8 \Rightarrow x = 4.



Problem 4 — Variable on both sides
Solve 5x - 7 = 3x + 9.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Get all x-terms on one side and all numbers on the other. Subtract 3x from both sides first.



Hint 2 — the technique

5x - 3x - 7 = 9 \Rightarrow 2x - 7 = 9.



Full solution

5x - 7 = 3x + 9 \Rightarrow 2x - 7 = 9 \Rightarrow 2x = 16 \Rightarrow
x = 8. Check: 5(8)-7 = 33 and 3(8)+9 = 33. Balanced.



Problem 5 — Brackets and fractions
Solve \dfrac{x+1}{2} = \dfrac{x-3}{3}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Clear the denominators by multiplying both sides by 6.



Hint 2 — the technique

3(x+1) = 2(x-3), then expand.



Full solution

Multiply both sides by 6: 3(x+1) = 2(x-3) \Rightarrow 3x + 3 = 2x - 6 \Rightarrow x = -9. Check: LHS = \tfrac{-9+1}{2} = -4; RHS = \tfrac{-9-3}{3} = -4. ✓



Problem 6 — Word puzzle
A father is currently four times as old as his son. Twenty years from now, he will be only twice as old. Find their present ages.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let the son’s age now be x. Then the father’s age is 4x.



Hint 2 — the technique

In 20 years: son is x+20, father is 4x+20. The condition 4x+20 = 2(x+20) gives the equation.



Full solution

4x + 20 = 2(x+20) = 2x + 40 \Rightarrow 2x = 20 \Rightarrow x = 10. Son is 10, father is 40. Check 20 years later: father 60, son 30 — yes, exactly twice.

You have just solved equations by always doing the same operation on both sides until x stood alone. That recipe is the whole chapter.

The word algebra comes from the title of the 9th-century textbook al-Kitāb al-mukhtaṣar fī ḥisāb al-jabr wal-muqābala by the Persian scholar al-Khwārizmī (c. 825 CE). The phrase al-jabr literally means “the restoring” — the act of moving a term from one side of an equation to the other and reversing its sign. So when you “transpose -3 across = and write +3”, you are performing al-Khwārizmī’s al-jabr — twelve hundred years on, and still doing the same dance.


A linear equation is a balance, not a sentence to be parsed. The equals sign means the two sides weigh the same. Every step you take is allowed for one reason only: it preserves the balance. Once you see this, you stop memorising “transposition rules” and just do the same thing to both sides — and the unknown drifts towards solitude on its own.
crumbs: Ch 2 — Linear Equations in One Variable

title: Ch 2 — Linear Equations in One Variable
text:
Name

A linear equation in one variable is an equation of the form ax + b = cx + d where a, b, c, d are rational numbers and a \neq c. It has exactly one solution, found by isolating x.
The rule of balance: if A = B, then for any rational k, A + k = B + k, A - k = B - k, kA = kB, and (if k \neq 0) A/k = B/k.

A short verse, in the spirit of Bhāskara’s Līlāvatī:

Move terms across with the sign reversed; Same act on both sides keeps the truth. When at last the unknown stands alone — Read it off; that is the proof.

The standard recipe:

Clear fractions (multiply both sides by the LCM of denominators).
Open brackets (distributive law).
Move all x-terms to one side, all constants to the other.
Divide by the coefficient of x.
Always verify by substituting back.
crumbs: Ch 2 — Linear Equations in One Variable

title: Ch 2 — Linear Equations in One Variable
text:
Apply

Worked example

The sum of three consecutive integers is 72. Find them.

Competent solver’s path:

Let the smallest integer be x. The next two are x+1 and x+2.
Sum: x + (x+1) + (x+2) = 72.
Combine: 3x + 3 = 72.
Subtract 3: 3x = 69.
Divide by 3: x = 23.

The integers are 23, 24, 25. Check: 23 + 24 + 25 = 72. ✓


Practice — Warm
W1. Solve 7x = 42.


Full solution

x = 42 / 7 = 6.

W2. Solve x - 9 = -3.


Full solution

Add 9 to both sides: x = 6.

W3. Solve \tfrac{x}{4} = -2.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Multiply both sides by 4.



Full solution

x = -8.



Practice — Working
P1. Solve 4x + 9 = 25 - 2x.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Get all x on the left. Add 2x to both sides.



Full solution

6x + 9 = 25 \Rightarrow 6x = 16 \Rightarrow x = \tfrac{8}{3}.

P2. Solve 5(x-2) = 3(x+4).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Open the brackets first.



Full solution

5x - 10 = 3x + 12 \Rightarrow 2x = 22 \Rightarrow x = 11.

P3. Solve \dfrac{2x-3}{4} - \dfrac{x+1}{6} = \dfrac{1}{2}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

LCM of denominators is 12. Multiply through.



Full solution

Multiply by 12: 3(2x-3) - 2(x+1) = 6 \Rightarrow 6x - 9 - 2x - 2 = 6
\Rightarrow 4x - 11 = 6 \Rightarrow 4x = 17 \Rightarrow x = \tfrac{17}{4}.



Practice — Stretch
S1. A two-digit number has digit-sum 9. When the digits are swapped, the new number is 27 greater than the original. Find the number.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Let the tens digit be t and the units digit be u. The number is 10t + u.



Hint 2 — the technique

t + u = 9 and 10u + t = 10t + u + 27.



Full solution

From the second equation: 9u - 9t = 27 \Rightarrow u - t = 3. Combined with t + u = 9: add to get 2u = 12 \Rightarrow u = 6 and t = 3. Original number: 36. Check: swap gives 63, which is 36 + 27. ✓

S2. Distance from town A to town B is travelled by a car at 60 km/h, and back at 40 km/h. The total trip takes 5 hours. Find the one-way distance.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Time = distance ÷ speed. Let one-way distance be d km.



Hint 2 — the technique

\tfrac{d}{60} + \tfrac{d}{40} = 5.



Full solution

LCM(60, 40) = 120. Multiply through: 2d + 3d = 600 \Rightarrow 5d = 600 \Rightarrow d = 120 km. One-way distance is 120 km.



Board-Pattern Practice
1-mark (objective). The solution of 3(x-1) = 2x + 5 is (a) 5 (b) -2 (c) 8 (d) 2.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Open the bracket, collect terms.



Full solution

3x - 3 = 2x + 5 \Rightarrow x = 8. Answer: (c).

2-mark (short answer). Solve and verify: 7y + 4 = -4y - 18.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Add 4y to both sides; subtract 4.



Full solution

7y + 4y = -18 - 4 \Rightarrow 11y = -22 \Rightarrow y = -2. Check: LHS = 7(-2)+4 = -10; RHS = -4(-2) - 18 = 8 - 18 = -10. ✓

3-mark (short answer II). Solve \dfrac{3x+1}{16} + \dfrac{2x-3}{7} = \dfrac{x+3}{8} + \dfrac{3x-1}{14}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

LCM of 16, 7, 8, 14 is 112. Multiply through.



Hint 2 — the technique

After multiplying: 7(3x+1) + 16(2x-3) = 14(x+3) + 8(3x-1).



Full solution

7(3x+1) + 16(2x-3) = 14(x+3) + 8(3x-1) 21x + 7 + 32x - 48 = 14x + 42 + 24x - 8 53x - 41 = 38x + 34 15x = 75 \boxed{x = 5}.
Check: LHS = \tfrac{16}{16} + \tfrac{7}{7} = 1 + 1 = 2; RHS = \tfrac{8}{8} + \tfrac{14}{14} = 2. ✓

5-mark (long answer). Two pipes can together fill a tank in 12 minutes. Pipe A alone takes 20 minutes. (i) How long would pipe B alone take? (ii) If pipe A is shut after 5 minutes, how much longer will pipe B alone take to finish the tank?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Think in rates. A fills at \tfrac{1}{20} tank/min. Together they fill at \tfrac{1}{12} tank/min.



Hint 2 — the technique

Let B’s rate be \tfrac{1}{x}. Equation: \tfrac{1}{20} + \tfrac{1}{x} =
\tfrac{1}{12}. Solve for x.



Full solution

(i) \tfrac{1}{20} + \tfrac{1}{x} = \tfrac{1}{12} \Rightarrow \tfrac{1}{x} = \tfrac{1}{12} - \tfrac{1}{20} = \tfrac{5 - 3}{60} = \tfrac{2}{60} = \tfrac{1}{30}.
So pipe B alone takes 30 minutes.
(ii) In 5 minutes both pipes together fill 5 \times \tfrac{1}{12} =
\tfrac{5}{12} of the tank.
Remaining: 1 - \tfrac{5}{12} = \tfrac{7}{12}.
Time for B alone to finish \tfrac{7}{12} of the tank at rate \tfrac{1}{30} per min: \tfrac{7}{12} \div \tfrac{1}{30} =
\tfrac{7}{12} \times 30 = \tfrac{210}{12} = 17.5 minutes.
Pipe B needs another 17.5 minutes (= 17 min 30 sec).







NCERT Exemplar pointer: Exemplar Problem 2.2 (work-rate puzzles) is a near-twin of the 5-mark item. Previous-year pointer: CBSE 2020 Set 2, Q9 (“solve the equation with fractions on both sides”) matches the 3-mark item closely.





Common confusions

“Transposing means flipping the sign.” It means moving and reversing. When you move +3 across =, it becomes -3. The reason is that you are subtracting 3 from both sides — the sign reversal is a consequence of the balance rule, not a magic move.
“You can multiply both sides by x to clear it.” Sometimes — but if x might be 0 you can lose solutions or invent fake ones. Always pause when you multiply by something containing the unknown.
“The check is optional.” It is not. A wrong sign somewhere will pass undetected unless you substitute back and read both sides.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

A linear equation in one variable has the form ax + b = cx + d — and exactly one solution, found by isolating x.
Whatever you do to one side of an equation, do to the other. Add, subtract, multiply (by non-zero), divide (by non-zero) — the balance rule is the only rule the chapter uses.
Always substitute the answer back. The check is not optional — it is the proof that your moves preserved the balance.



Self-Assessment
Be honest with yourself.

Could you explain to a friend, without notes, why moving a term across the = sign reverses its sign?
Did you solve at least 5 of the 6 Discover problems and 5 of the 7 Practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?
Can you turn a one-paragraph word problem into a linear equation?


3 yes: move on to Understanding Quadrilaterals.
2 yes: redo Stretch S2 and the 5-mark board item, then move on.
0–1 yes: rebuild from Discover. Most stumbles here are about fraction handling — revisit Rational Numbers.
crumbs: Ch 2 — Linear Equations in One Variable

title: Ch 2 — Linear Equations in One Variable
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

Intro to Variables — letters as placeholders for unknown numbers.
Rational Numbers — solutions live in \mathbb{Q}.



What this unlocks

Algebraic Expressions and Identities — manipulating expressions before solving them.
Factorisation — equations whose left side is a product of two factors.
Class 9 — Linear Equations in Two Variables — adds a second unknown.



NCERT chapter alignment

Class 8, Chapter 2 — Linear Equations in One Variable (NCERT 2023 rationalised syllabus). Covers Sections 2.1–2.4: solving equations with the variable on either or both sides, equations reducible to linear form, word problems.
Sister synthesis lesson (different pedagogical angle): t2.linear-equations-one-var treats linear equations as an algebraic structure; this chapter emphasises the balance metaphor and word-problem translation.
Read alongside the NCERT textbook, which remains the canonical source for the board paper.



Where this matters

Research. “Solve for x” is the smallest possible mathematical problem, but the technique — apply inverse operations until the unknown is isolated — is the seed of the entire field of solving equations, from quadratics in Class 10 to differential equations in graduate analysis.


AI / ML. Training a neural network is, at its heart, solving millions of equations of the form gradient = 0 simultaneously. Gradient descent is the iterative cousin of “do the same thing to both sides”.
crumbs: Ch 2 — Linear Equations in One Variable

title: Ch 9 — Mensuration
text:
Be honest before you begin.

Do you remember the area of a rectangle (l \times b) and a triangle (\tfrac{1}{2} \times b \times h)? If not, revisit Area, Perimeter, Volume.
Can you tell a rhombus from a trapezium? If not, revisit Understanding Quadrilaterals.
Do you know that \pi \approx 3.14 and that the circumference of a circle is 2 \pi r?
Can you compute 7^2 in your head?

If three were a confident yes, read on.

title: Ch 9 — Mensuration
Ch 9 — Mensuration
text:
Wonder
Sit with these.

Why does the trapezium-pair give a parallelogram, not a rectangle?
The same trick with two triangles also gives a parallelogram. What does that say about a triangle’s area?
A circle isn’t made of straight lines. How would you go about measuring its area?
A box (a cuboid) has six rectangular faces. What is the relation between the surface area and the volume? Is there a simple one?







TipWhat you might notice (don’t peek until you’ve thought)





The chapter has three families of formulas: areas of plane figures (trapezium, rhombus, polygon), surface areas of solids (cuboid, cube, cylinder), and volumes of those solids. You will derive several from the rectangle / triangle areas you already trust.

title: Ch 9 — Mensuration
text:
Discover

Problem 1 — Area of a trapezium
A trapezium has parallel sides of 7 cm and 5 cm and the perpendicular distance between them is 4 cm. Find its area.


Hint 1 — a nudge

You just derived the formula in Touch.



Full solution

A = \tfrac{1}{2}(7 + 5) \times 4 = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 12 \cdot 4 = 24 cm^2.



Problem 2 — Area of a rhombus
A rhombus has diagonals d_1 = 10 cm and d_2 = 24 cm. Find its area.


Hint 1 — a nudge

The diagonals split the rhombus into four congruent right triangles. Add their areas.



Hint 2 — the technique

Each small triangle has legs \tfrac{d_1}{2} and \tfrac{d_2}{2}. Four of them.



Full solution

Area = 4 \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \tfrac{d_1}{2} \cdot \tfrac{d_2}{2} =
\tfrac{d_1 d_2}{2} = \tfrac{10 \cdot 24}{2} = 120 cm^2.
(General formula: area of rhombus = \tfrac{1}{2} d_1 d_2.)



Problem 3 — Surface area of a cuboid
Find the total surface area of a cuboid with l = 8, b = 5, h = 3 cm.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Six faces, three different pairs.



Full solution

Two faces of l \times b, two of b \times h, two of l \times h. \text{TSA} = 2(lb + bh + lh) = 2(40 + 15 + 24) = 2 \cdot 79 = 158 cm^2.



Problem 4 — Surface area of a cube
A cube has edge 7 cm. Find its surface area.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Six congruent square faces.



Full solution

\text{TSA} = 6 \cdot 7^2 = 6 \cdot 49 = 294 cm^2.



Problem 5 — Volume of a cuboid and a cylinder

Find the volume of a cuboid with sides 10 cm, 4 cm, 3 cm.
Find the volume of a cylinder of radius 7 cm and height 10


Take \pi = \tfrac{22}{7}.



Hint 1 — a nudge

Cuboid: V = lbh. Cylinder: V = \pi r^2 h.



Full solution

(i) V = 10 \cdot 4 \cdot 3 = 120 cm^3.
(ii) V = \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 7^2 \cdot 10 = \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 49
\cdot 10 = 22 \cdot 70 = 1540 cm^3.



Problem 6 — Curved surface of a cylinder
A cylinder has radius r = 3.5 cm and height h = 10 cm. Find its curved surface area (just the side, not the top and bottom). Take \pi = \tfrac{22}{7}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Imagine slitting the cylinder along its height and unrolling it. The unrolled side is a rectangle of width 2 \pi r (the circumference) and height h.



Full solution

CSA = 2 \pi r h = 2 \cdot \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 3.5 \cdot 10 = 2 \cdot
\tfrac{22}{7} \cdot \tfrac{35}{10} \cdot 10 = 220 cm^2.
(Total surface area of a cylinder includes the two circular ends: \text{TSA} = 2 \pi r h + 2 \pi r^2 = 2 \pi r (h + r).)

You have just measured plane areas and the surface and volume of two solids — using rectangles, circles, and the trapezium trick.

The word mensuration (“measuring”) and most of its formulas trace back to Heron of Alexandria (c. 60 CE), whose Metrica compiled the area and volume rules of antiquity into one book — including a triangle’s area from just its three sides (you meet Heron’s formula in Class 9). Two centuries earlier, Archimedes had already worked out the volume of a sphere is exactly two-thirds the volume of the cylinder it just fits inside — a result he was so proud of, he asked for the picture to be carved on his tomb.


Every solid in this chapter is a cylinder in disguise: pile up identical cross-sections of area A to height h and the volume is V = A \times h. A cuboid is the special case where the cross-section is a rectangle; a cylinder is the case where it is a circle. The same one idea covers them all.

title: Ch 9 — Mensuration
text:
Name

Plane figures.







Shape
Area




Trapezium with parallel sides a, b, height h
\tfrac{1}{2}(a+b)h


Rhombus with diagonals d_1, d_2
\tfrac{1}{2} d_1 d_2


General quadrilateral (split by diagonal of length d with offsets h_1, h_2)
\tfrac{1}{2} d (h_1 + h_2)



Solids.



Solid
Lateral / curved surface
Total surface area
Volume




Cuboid (l, b, h)
2(l + b)h
2(lb + bh + lh)
lbh


Cube (edge a)
4a^2
6a^2
a^3


Cylinder (r, h)
2 \pi r h
2 \pi r (h + r)
\pi r^2 h



One litre = 1000 cm^3 = 1000 ml.

A short verse, in the spirit of Bhāskara’s Līlāvatī:

Trapezium: parallels added, halved, times height. Rhombus: diagonals’ product, halved, comes through. Cuboid: pair-product summed, doubled, the surface gleams; Cylinder: roll it flat — a rectangle of 2 \pi r by height.

title: Ch 9 — Mensuration
text:
Apply

Worked example

A field is in the shape of a trapezium with parallel sides 25 m and 35 m, separated by 20 m. (i) What is the area of the field? (ii) If the field is to be levelled at a cost of \text{₹}12 per m^2, what is the total cost?

Competent solver’s path:

A = \tfrac{1}{2}(25 + 35) \times 20 = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 60 \cdot 20
= 600 m^2.
Cost = 600 \times 12 = \text{₹}7200.



Practice — Warm
W1. Area of a trapezium with parallel sides 9 cm and 11 cm and height 6 cm.


Full solution

\tfrac{1}{2}(9+11) \cdot 6 = 60 cm^2.

W2. Surface area of a cube of edge 5 cm.


Full solution

6 \cdot 25 = 150 cm^2.

W3. Volume of a cylinder, r = 7 cm, h = 5 cm. Use \pi =
\tfrac{22}{7}.


Full solution

V = \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 49 \cdot 5 = 22 \cdot 35 = 770 cm^3.



Practice — Working
P1. A rhombus has area 96 cm^2 and one diagonal 12 cm. Find the other diagonal.


Full solution

\tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 12 \cdot d_2 = 96 \Rightarrow d_2 = 16 cm.

P2. A closed box has dimensions 40 \times 25 \times 15 cm. Find the area of metal sheet needed to make it.


Hint 1 — a nudge

“Closed” → all six faces. Use TSA of cuboid.



Full solution

2(lb + bh + lh) = 2(40 \cdot 25 + 25 \cdot 15 + 40 \cdot 15) = 2(1000 + 375 + 600) = 2 \cdot 1975 = 3950 cm^2.

P3. An open cylindrical tank (\pi = \tfrac{22}{7}) has radius 1.4 m and height 3 m. (i) Find its capacity in litres. (ii) Find the area of metal needed to make it (open — only side and bottom).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Capacity = volume. 1 m^3 = 1000 litres.



Full solution

(i) V = \pi r^2 h = \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 1.96 \cdot 3 = \tfrac{22
\cdot 5.88}{7} = \tfrac{129.36}{7} \approx 18.48 m^3. 1 m^3 = 1000 litres, so capacity \approx 18{,}480 litres.
(ii) Metal area = CSA + bottom area = 2 \pi r h + \pi r^2 =
\pi r (2h + r). = \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 1.4 \cdot (6 + 1.4) = \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 1.4
\cdot 7.4 = \tfrac{22 \cdot 1.4 \cdot 7.4}{7} = \tfrac{227.92}{7}
\approx 32.56 m^2.



Practice — Stretch
S1. A cuboidal water tank measures 6 m \times 5 m \times 4.5 m. How many cubical buckets of edge 30 cm can be filled completely from this tank?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Volume of tank in cm^3, divided by volume of one bucket in cm^3. Mind the units.



Full solution

Tank volume = 600 \times 500 \times 450 = 1{,}35{,}000{,}000 cm^3. Bucket volume = 30^3 = 27{,}000 cm^3. Number of buckets = \tfrac{1{,}35{,}000{,}000}{27{,}000} = 5000.

S2. A solid metallic cylinder of diameter 14 cm and height 10 cm is melted and recast into smaller cubes of edge 1 cm. How many cubes are formed? (Take \pi = \tfrac{22}{7}.)


Hint 1 — a nudge

Cylinder volume / cube volume.



Full solution

V_{\text{cyl}} = \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 7^2 \cdot 10 = \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot
49 \cdot 10 = 1540 cm^3. V_{\text{cube}} = 1 cm^3. Number of cubes = 1540.



Board-Pattern Practice
1-mark (objective). Surface area of a cube of edge a is (a) a^2 (b) 3a^2 (c) 6a^2 (d) a^3.


Full solution

Six congruent square faces. Answer: (c).

2-mark (short answer). Find the area of a quadrilateral whose diagonal is 26 cm and the perpendicular offsets to the two opposite vertices are 6 cm and 9 cm.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Area of a general quadrilateral split by a diagonal: \tfrac{1}{2} d
(h_1 + h_2).



Full solution

A = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 26 \cdot (6 + 9) = 13 \cdot 15 = 195 cm^2.

3-mark (short answer II). A cylindrical pipe has inner radius 3.5 cm, outer radius 4 cm, and length 20 cm. Find the volume of the pipe material. (Take \pi = \tfrac{22}{7}.)


Hint 1 — a nudge

Volume of material = volume of outer cylinder − volume of inner cylinder.



Full solution

V = \pi (R^2 - r^2) h = \tfrac{22}{7}(16 - 12.25) \cdot 20 =
\tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 3.75 \cdot 20 = \tfrac{22 \cdot 75}{7} = \tfrac{1650}{7} \approx 235.71 cm^3.

5-mark (long answer). The water in a rectangular pool measures 80 m \times 30 m and is 5 m deep. (i) Find the volume of water in cubic metres and in litres. (ii) The pool is to be drained through a cylindrical pipe of radius 35 cm at 2 m/s. How long does it take? (Take \pi = \tfrac{22}{7}.)


Hint 1 — a nudge

Volume per second through pipe = pipe cross-section × speed. Then time = total volume / volume per second.



Full solution

(i) Volume = 80 \times 30 \times 5 = 12000 m^3 = 12000 \times
1000 = 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 litres.
(ii) Convert pipe radius: r = 35 cm = 0.35 m. Cross-section = \pi r^2 = \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 0.1225 =
\tfrac{22 \cdot 0.1225}{7} = \tfrac{2.695}{7} = 0.385 m^2.
Volume drained per second = 0.385 \times 2 = 0.77 m^3/s.
Total time = \tfrac{12000}{0.77} \approx 15{,}584 seconds \approx
4 hours 20 minutes.







NCERT Exemplar pointer: Exemplar Problem 9.5 (drain a tank through a cylindrical pipe) is a near-twin of the 5-mark item. Previous-year pointer: CBSE 2018 Set 3, Q19 (“hollow cylindrical pipe — volume of material”) matches the 3-mark item closely.





Common confusions

“1 m^2 = 100 cm^2.” No — 1 m = 100 cm, so 1 m^2 =
100 \times 100 = 10{,}000 cm^2. Watch the units.
“1 m^3 = 1000 cm^3.” No — 1 m^3 = 100^3 = 10^6 cm^3. But 1 litre = 1000 cm^3, hence 1 m^3 = 1000 litres.
“Surface area and volume scale the same way.” They don’t. Doubling the edge multiplies area by 4 and volume by 8. (The square-cube law.)



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this chapter, remember these:

The trapezium area, \tfrac{1}{2}(a+b)h, falls out of pasting two trapezia into a parallelogram — a scissors proof, not a memorised formula.
Every solid in the chapter splits into a base (area A) and a height h: volume is V = Ah, total surface area is the sum of all face areas.
Watch units. 1 m \neq 100 cm^2, and 1 m^3 = 1000 litres — the square-cube law bites here, and on every engineering exam.



Self-Assessment
Be honest with yourself.

Could you re-derive the trapezium and rhombus area formulas, with pictures, on a blank sheet?
Did you solve at least 5 of the 6 Discover problems and 5 of the 7 Practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?
Can you keep units straight (cm vs m, ml vs litres) when reading a word problem?


3 yes: move on to Exponents and Powers.
2 yes: redo Working P3 and the 5-mark board item, then move on.
0–1 yes: redo each Discover problem with the formula box from Name hidden — until each formula feels obvious.

title: Ch 9 — Mensuration
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

Area, Perimeter, Volume — basic formulas for rectangle, triangle, circle, cube.
Understanding Quadrilaterals — names and properties of trapezium, rhombus.



What this unlocks

Class 9 — Heron’s Formula — area of a triangle from its three sides.
Class 9 — Surface Areas and Volumes — adds cone, sphere, hemisphere.
Class 10 — Surface Areas and Volumes — combinations of solids.



NCERT chapter alignment

Class 8, Chapter 9 — Mensuration (NCERT 2023 rationalised syllabus). Covers Sections 9.1–9.4: area of plane figures (trapezium, general quadrilateral, polygons), surface area of cuboid, cube, and right circular cylinder, and volume of the same.
Sister synthesis lesson (different pedagogical angle): t1.area-perimeter-volume treats area and volume as foundations; this chapter pushes the formulas to the trapezium and the cylinder.
Read alongside the NCERT textbook, which remains the canonical source for the board paper.



Where this matters

Game development. Every collision detection routine for a cuboid-shaped object computes the same surface area and volume you just did. Bounding-box rendering uses l \times b \times h tens of thousands of times per second.


Research. Mensuration formulas are the finite shadow of integral calculus. Once you know how to add up n thin strips to get an area, the leap to \int is small.

title: Ch 12 — Factorisation
text:
Be honest before you begin.

Can you expand (x + 3)(x + 5)? If not, revisit Algebraic Expressions and Identities.
Do you remember the three identities (a+b)^2, (a-b)^2, and (a+b)(a-b) = a^2 - b^2?
Can you write 24 as a product of primes? If not, revisit Divisibility and Primes.
Do you know what the greatest common factor (GCF) of 12 and 18 is?

If three were a confident yes, read on.

title: Ch 12 — Factorisation
Ch 12 — Factorisation
text:
Wonder
Sit with these.

In 6x + 9, what number divides both terms? What about x^2 + 5x — is there a variable that divides both?
We discovered (a+b)(a-b) = a^2 - b^2 in the last chapter. So if you see x^2 - 25, what should you reach for?
The expression x^2 + 7x + 12 has constant 12 and middle coefficient 7. Can you guess the two numbers we’ll need?
Could every algebraic sum be factorised into two simpler pieces? Or are some sums “prime”?







TipWhat you might notice (don’t peek until you’ve thought)





Factorisation has four main techniques: pull out the common factor, group, recognise an identity (a^2 - b^2, a^2 + 2ab +
b^2, a^2 - 2ab + b^2), and split the middle term. Some expressions are irreducible — like prime numbers in algebra-land — and stay sums.

title: Ch 12 — Factorisation
text:
Discover

Problem 1 — Common factor
Factorise 6x + 9.


Hint 1 — a nudge

What is the GCF of 6 and 9?



Full solution

GCF = 3. So 6x + 9 = 3(2x + 3).
Check by expanding: 3 \cdot 2x + 3 \cdot 3 = 6x + 9. ✓



Problem 2 — Variable common factor
Factorise x^2 + 5x.


Hint 1 — a nudge

What divides both x^2 and 5x?



Full solution

Both contain x. So x^2 + 5x = x(x + 5).



Problem 3 — Group and factor
Factorise ax + ay + bx + by.


Hint 1 — a nudge

The first two terms share an a; the last two share a b. Group them.



Hint 2 — the technique

a(x + y) + b(x + y) — both pieces share (x+y).



Full solution

ax + ay + bx + by = a(x + y) + b(x + y) = (x + y)(a + b).



Problem 4 — Difference of squares
Factorise x^2 - 49.


Hint 1 — a nudge

49 = 7^2. Identity: a^2 - b^2 = (a+b)(a-b).



Full solution

x^2 - 49 = (x + 7)(x - 7).



Problem 5 — Perfect square
Factorise x^2 + 10x + 25.


Hint 1 — a nudge

25 = 5^2 and 10 = 2 \cdot 5. Identity: (a + b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2.



Full solution

x^2 + 10x + 25 = (x + 5)^2.



Problem 6 — Split the middle term
Factorise x^2 + 7x + 12.


Hint 1 — a nudge

You need two numbers whose sum is 7 and product is 12.



Hint 2 — the technique

Factors of 12: 1 \cdot 12, 2 \cdot 6, 3 \cdot 4. Of these, 3 + 4 =
7. So split: 7x = 3x + 4x.



Full solution

x^2 + 7x + 12 = x^2 + 3x + 4x + 12 = x(x + 3) + 4(x + 3) = (x + 3)(x +
4).



Problem 7 — Polynomial division
Divide 6x^3 - 8x^2 + 4x by 2x.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Pull the common factor 2x from each term.



Full solution

6x^3 - 8x^2 + 4x = 2x(3x^2 - 4x + 2). Dividing by 2x: 3x^2 - 4x +
2.
Polynomial division is just factorisation followed by cancellation when the divisor is a factor.

You have just used four techniques: common factor, grouping, identity, and middle-term split. The chapter is the choreography of switching between them.

The Greek mathematician Diophantus of Alexandria (c. 200 – c. 284 CE), in his Arithmetica, was the first to write equations in something resembling modern symbolic form — and the first to factor expressions to solve them. A millennium and a half later, his book sat on Pierre de Fermat’s desk; in its margin, next to a problem on writing a square as a sum of two squares, Fermat scribbled the conjecture that became Fermat’s Last Theorem — finally proved by Andrew Wiles in 1995. Every chain of factoring you do in this chapter is in Diophantus’ direct line.


Factoring is running multiplication backwards. The identities you proved in Chapter 8 — (a+b)^2, (a-b)^2, difference of squares — are the same equalities, read right-to-left. The chapter’s only new skill is pattern-recognition: looking at a sum and seeing the product hiding inside.

title: Ch 12 — Factorisation
text:
Name

To factorise an expression is to write it as a product of simpler expressions whose product equals the original.
The four techniques:

Common factor. Pull out the largest expression dividing every term.
Grouping. Pair terms so each pair has a common factor; pull out the shared binomial.
Identity. Recognise a^2 - b^2, (a \pm b)^2, etc., and apply.
Splitting the middle term. For x^2 + bx + c, find numbers p, q with p + q = b and pq = c, then split bx = px + qx and group.

Polynomial division is just factorisation: if g(x) divides f(x), then f(x) = g(x) \cdot q(x), and the quotient is what is left after extracting the factor.

A short verse, in the spirit of Bhāskara’s Līlāvatī:

Pull the common, group the four, spot the identity-shape; Or split the middle into two whose sum is right, whose product is too. Then the sum has become a product — And the product reveals what the sum could not.

title: Ch 12 — Factorisation
text:
Apply

Worked example

Factorise 4x^2 - 25y^2.

Competent solver’s path:

Spot the shape: difference of two squares.
4x^2 = (2x)^2 and 25y^2 = (5y)^2.
Apply a^2 - b^2 = (a+b)(a-b) with a = 2x, b = 5y.

Answer: 4x^2 - 25y^2 = (2x + 5y)(2x - 5y).


Practice — Warm
W1. Factorise 12x + 8.


Full solution

4(3x + 2).

W2. Factorise a^2 - 81.


Full solution

(a + 9)(a - 9).

W3. Factorise y^2 + 6y + 9.


Full solution

(y + 3)^2.



Practice — Working
P1. Factorise a^2 - 14a + 49.


Hint 1 — a nudge

49 = 7^2 and 14 = 2 \cdot 7. Perfect square of a difference.



Full solution

(a - 7)^2.

P2. Factorise x^2 + 9x + 18.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Two numbers with sum 9 and product 18.



Full solution

3 + 6 = 9, 3 \cdot 6 = 18. Split: 9x = 3x + 6x. x^2 + 3x + 6x + 18 = x(x + 3) + 6(x + 3) = (x + 3)(x + 6).

P3. Factorise 6x^2 + 17x + 5.


Hint 1 — a nudge

For ax^2 + bx + c, find two numbers whose product is ac and whose sum is b. Here ac = 30, b = 17.



Hint 2 — the technique

15 \cdot 2 = 30, 15 + 2 = 17. Split 17x = 15x + 2x.



Full solution

6x^2 + 17x + 5 = 6x^2 + 15x + 2x + 5 = 3x(2x + 5) + 1(2x + 5) =
(2x + 5)(3x + 1).



Practice — Stretch
S1. Factorise a^4 - b^4 completely.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Difference of squares applied twice.



Hint 2 — the technique

a^4 - b^4 = (a^2)^2 - (b^2)^2. Now factor again.



Full solution

a^4 - b^4 = (a^2 + b^2)(a^2 - b^2) = (a^2 + b^2)(a + b)(a - b).
(Note: a^2 + b^2 is not further factorisable using real-number techniques.)

S2. Factorise x^3 + 2x^2 - x - 2.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Group the four terms in pairs.



Hint 2 — the technique

Group: (x^3 + 2x^2) - (x + 2) = x^2(x + 2) - 1(x + 2).



Full solution

x^3 + 2x^2 - x - 2 = x^2(x+2) - 1(x+2) = (x+2)(x^2 - 1) = (x+2)(x+1)(x-1).



Board-Pattern Practice
1-mark (objective). x^2 - 9 factorises as (a) (x-3)(x+3) (b) (x-9)(x+9) (c) (x-3)^2 (d) (x+3)^2.


Full solution

Difference of squares. Answer: (a).

2-mark (short answer). Factorise 25x^2 - 70xy + 49y^2.


Hint 1 — a nudge

25x^2 = (5x)^2 and 49y^2 = (7y)^2 and 70xy = 2 \cdot 5x \cdot 7y. (a-b)^2 shape.



Full solution

(5x - 7y)^2.

3-mark (short answer II). Factorise x^2 + x - 132.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Two numbers with product -132 and sum 1.



Hint 2 — the technique

Try 12 and -11: product -132, sum 1. ✓



Full solution

x^2 + x - 132 = x^2 + 12x - 11x - 132 = x(x + 12) - 11(x + 12) =
(x + 12)(x - 11).
Check: (x+12)(x-11) = x^2 - 11x + 12x - 132 = x^2 + x - 132. ✓

4-mark (long answer). (i) Factorise a^2 - 2ab + b^2 - c^2. (ii) Hence find the value when a = 7, b = 4, c = 5.


Hint 1 — a nudge

The first three terms form a perfect square. Then a difference of squares appears.



Full solution

(i) a^2 - 2ab + b^2 = (a-b)^2. So a^2 - 2ab + b^2 - c^2 = (a-b)^2 - c^2 = (a - b + c)(a - b - c).
(ii) a - b = 3. So (a-b+c) = 8 and (a-b-c) = -2. Product: 8 \times (-2) = -16.
(Verify by direct substitution: 49 - 56 + 16 - 25 = -16. ✓)







NCERT Exemplar pointer: Exemplar Problem 12.6 (“factorise a^2 -
b^2 + 2bc - c^2”) is a near-twin of the 4-mark item — both use group three terms as a square, then difference-of-squares. Previous-year pointer: CBSE 2019 Set 1, Q11 (“factorise x^2 + 6x
+ 8”) matches Working P2 exactly.





Common confusions

“a^2 + b^2 = (a + b)(a - b).” No — that’s a^2 - b^2. The sum of two squares does not factor over the real numbers (in this chapter’s toolkit).
“Splitting the middle term works for any trinomial.” Only for trinomials of the form ax^2 + bx + c where the discriminant b^2 - 4ac is a perfect square. Otherwise the roots are irrational and you need the formula from Class 10.
“x^2 + 1 can be factorised.” Not over the real numbers. You meet its complex factorisation (x + i)(x - i) later, in Tier 4.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this chapter, remember these:

To factor is to write a sum as a product — the inverse of “expand”. The product reveals the structure (roots, common pieces) that the sum hid.
Four techniques cover almost everything in Class 8: common factor, grouping, identity-spot, and middle-term split. Try them in that order.
Some sums are irreducible — like primes among the integers. x^2 +
1, x^2 + x + 1, and a^2 + b^2 stay sums under real-number factoring; meeting them is normal, not failure.



Self-Assessment
Be honest with yourself.

Could you state the four techniques in order, and pick the right one for a problem you’ve never seen?
Did you solve at least 6 of the 7 Discover problems and 5 of the 7 Practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?
Given a trinomial, can you split the middle term in under thirty seconds?


3 yes: move on to Introduction to Graphs.
2 yes: redo Working P3 and the 4-mark board item, then move on.
0–1 yes: rebuild from Algebraic Expressions and Identities — factorisation runs the same identities backwards, so the forward direction must be airtight.

title: Ch 12 — Factorisation
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

Algebraic Expressions and Identities — the three standard identities, ready to be reversed.
Divisibility and Primes — finding the GCF of integer coefficients.



What this unlocks

Class 9 — Polynomials — adds Remainder and Factor theorems for cubics.
Class 10 — Polynomials — relations between roots and coefficients.
Class 10 — Quadratic Equations — solving by factorisation, the formula, completing the square.
