NCERT chapter alignment

Class 8, Chapter 12 — Factorisation (NCERT 2023 rationalised syllabus). Covers Sections 12.1–12.4: common factors, grouping, identity-based factorisation, and division of polynomials by monomials.
Sister synthesis lesson (different pedagogical angle): t2.factoring-polynomials treats factoring at the synthesis level; this chapter focuses on computational technique and the four-pattern recognition drill.
Read alongside the NCERT textbook, which remains the canonical source for the board paper.



Where this matters

Research. Factoring polynomials is the discrete cousin of factoring integers. The same algorithms — Gauss’s method, Berlekamp’s algorithm — show up in symbolic computation systems like Mathematica and SymPy.


Encryption. RSA security depends on a very specific factorisation problem being hard: factoring a 2048-bit integer that is the product of two large primes. The fact that integer factorisation is hard while polynomial factorisation (today’s lesson) is easy is the unsung asymmetry of modern cryptography.

title: Ch 8 — Algebraic Expressions and Identities
text:
Be honest before you begin.

Can you read 3x^2 - 2x + 5 as “three times an unknown squared, minus two times the unknown, plus five”? If not, revisit Intro to Variables.
Can you compute (7 + 3) \times 4 and 7 \times 4 + 3 \times 4 and confirm they’re equal? If not, revisit Multiplication as Area.
Are you comfortable with a + a = 2a and a \cdot a = a^2?
Do you know that -(-3) = 3?

If three were a confident yes, read on.

title: Ch 8 — Algebraic Expressions and Identities
Ch 8 — Algebraic Expressions and Identities
text:
Wonder
Sit with these.

The big rectangle has side (a+c) \times (b+d). Pull out the four tiles. Why do you get four pieces, not three or five?
What if c = 0? What does the picture become?
What if a and b are equal, and c and d are also equal? What is the big shape?
Could the same picture be drawn with a, c as letters (not numbers)? What would change?







TipWhat you might notice (don’t peek until you’ve thought)





The four tiles always exist: ab, ad, cb, cd. Adding them gives (a+c)(b+d). That is the distributive law, generalised. Three shortcut versions of this picture — when the two sides are the same sum, the same difference, or one of each — are the identities named in this chapter.

title: Ch 8 — Algebraic Expressions and Identities
text:
Discover

Problem 1 — Combine like terms
Simplify 4x + 7 - 2x + 3 - x.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Group x-terms together and constants together.



Full solution

x-terms: 4x - 2x - x = x. Constants: 7 + 3 = 10. Answer: x + 10.



Problem 2 — Multiply a monomial by a polynomial
Compute 3x \cdot (2x^2 + 5x - 4).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Distribute 3x to each term.



Full solution

3x \cdot 2x^2 + 3x \cdot 5x + 3x \cdot (-4) = 6x^3 + 15x^2 - 12x.



Problem 3 — Two binomials
Use the rectangle picture (or the FOIL pattern) to compute (x + 3)(x + 5).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Four tiles: x \cdot x, x \cdot 5, 3 \cdot x, 3 \cdot 5.



Full solution

(x+3)(x+5) = x^2 + 5x + 3x + 15 = x^2 + 8x + 15.
(Note the middle coefficient 8 = 3 + 5. The constant 15 = 3 \cdot 5. That is the FOIL pattern.)



Problem 4 — Square of a sum
Compute (a + b)^2 by expansion.


Hint 1 — a nudge

(a+b)^2 = (a+b)(a+b).



Hint 2 — the technique

Use FOIL.



Full solution

(a+b)(a+b) = a^2 + ab + ba + b^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2.
(Picture: a square of side a+b split into one a^2 tile, two ab tiles, one b^2 tile. The famous square of a sum identity.)



Problem 5 — Square of a difference
Compute (a - b)^2 similarly.


Hint 1 — a nudge

(a-b)(a-b). Take care with signs.



Full solution

(a-b)(a-b) = a^2 - ab - ba + b^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2.



Problem 6 — Product of sum and difference
Compute (a + b)(a - b).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Use FOIL. The two cross terms cancel.



Full solution

(a+b)(a-b) = a^2 - ab + ba - b^2 = a^2 - b^2.
(Beautiful and useful — the difference of squares identity.)



Problem 7 — Use one in the wild
Compute 103 \times 97 in your head, using one of the identities you just discovered.


Hint 1 — a nudge

103 = 100 + 3, 97 = 100 - 3.



Full solution

103 \times 97 = (100+3)(100-3) = 100^2 - 3^2 = 10000 - 9 = 9991.

You have just proved (and used) the three most important identities of school algebra: (a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2, (a-b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2, (a+b)(a-b) = a^2 - b^2.

Letters for unknowns are surprisingly recent. The French lawyer-mathematician François Viète (1591, In Artem Analyticem Isagoge) was the first to use letters — vowels for unknowns, consonants for knowns — instead of writing out res (“the thing”) in Latin. Half a century later, René Descartes (La Géométrie, 1637) settled on the convention we still use today: x, y,
z for unknowns and a, b, c for knowns. Every identity in this chapter is written in Descartes’ notation, less than four hundred years old.


A standard identity is a rectangle picture in disguise. The square of a sum is one square plus two off-diagonal tiles plus another square; the difference of squares is a rectangle with one corner removed and pasted on the other side. If you can draw the rectangle, the algebra writes itself.

title: Ch 8 — Algebraic Expressions and Identities
text:
Name

An algebraic expression is a combination of variables and constants joined by +, -, \times, \div and powers. A term is a single addend; like terms are terms differing only in their numerical coefficient.
The three standard identities: (a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2 (a-b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2 (a+b)(a-b) = a^2 - b^2
A fourth, often useful: (x+a)(x+b) = x^2 + (a+b)x + ab.
These are equalities for all values of the variables — not equations to solve.

A short verse, in the spirit of Bhāskara’s Līlāvatī:

Square the sum: each piece, then twice the cross. Square the diff: same shape, the cross sign flips. Sum times diff: the squares fall apart, the crosses cancel. FOIL all four — first, outer, inner, last.

title: Ch 8 — Algebraic Expressions and Identities
text:
Apply

Worked example

Use a standard identity to compute (2x + 3)^2 and then evaluate when x = 5.

Competent solver’s path:

Identity (a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2 with a = 2x, b = 3.
(2x+3)^2 = (2x)^2 + 2 \cdot 2x \cdot 3 + 3^2 = 4x^2 + 12x + 9.
At x = 5: 4 \cdot 25 + 12 \cdot 5 + 9 = 100 + 60 + 9 = 169.

(Double-check: 2 \cdot 5 + 3 = 13, and 13^2 = 169. ✓)


Practice — Warm
W1. Simplify 5a + 3b - 2a + b.


Full solution

3a + 4b.

W2. Expand (x - 7)(x + 7).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Difference-of-squares identity.



Full solution

x^2 - 49.

W3. Expand (p + 2)^2.


Full solution

p^2 + 4p + 4.



Practice — Working
P1. Compute (3x - 4y)^2 using the appropriate identity.


Hint 1 — a nudge

(a-b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2 with a=3x, b=4y.



Full solution

(3x)^2 - 2(3x)(4y) + (4y)^2 = 9x^2 - 24xy + 16y^2.

P2. Use (x+a)(x+b) = x^2 + (a+b)x + ab to expand (x+5)(x-3).


Hint 1 — a nudge

a = 5, b = -3.



Full solution

(x+5)(x-3) = x^2 + (5-3)x + 5 \cdot (-3) = x^2 + 2x - 15.

P3. Use the difference-of-squares identity to compute 51 \times
49 mentally.


Hint 1 — a nudge

51 = 50+1, 49 = 50-1.



Full solution

51 \times 49 = (50+1)(50-1) = 2500 - 1 = 2499.



Practice — Stretch
S1. Show that for every real a, b: (a+b)^2 - (a-b)^2 = 4ab.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Expand both squares using the identities.



Full solution

(a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2, (a-b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2. Subtract: (a^2 + 2ab + b^2) - (a^2 - 2ab + b^2) = 4ab. ✓

S2. If a + b = 7 and ab = 12, find a^2 + b^2 without solving for a and b individually.


Hint 1 — a nudge

(a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2. Rearrange to solve for a^2 + b^2.



Full solution

a^2 + b^2 = (a+b)^2 - 2ab = 49 - 24 = 25.



Board-Pattern Practice
1-mark (objective). (x + 4)(x - 4) = ? (a) x^2 - 16 (b) x^2 + 16 (c) x^2 - 8x + 16 (d) x^2 + 8x - 16.


Full solution

Difference of squares: x^2 - 16. Answer: (a).

2-mark (short answer). Find the product (2x + 3y)(2x - 3y) and state the identity used.


Full solution

(a+b)(a-b) = a^2 - b^2 with a = 2x, b = 3y. (2x)^2 - (3y)^2 = 4x^2 - 9y^2.

3-mark (short answer II). Use a suitable identity to compute (99)^2 without long multiplication.


Hint 1 — a nudge

99 = 100 - 1.



Full solution

(100 - 1)^2 = 100^2 - 2 \cdot 100 \cdot 1 + 1 = 10000 - 200 + 1 = 9801.

4-mark (long answer). (i) Expand (x + 2y - 3z)^2. (ii) If x + 2y
- 3z = 7 and x^2 + 4y^2 + 9z^2 = 49, find the value of 2xy - 6yz - 3xz.


Hint 1 — a nudge

For three terms, use (a+b+c)^2 = a^2 + b^2 + c^2 + 2(ab + bc + ca).



Full solution

(i) Let a = x, b = 2y, c = -3z. (x + 2y - 3z)^2 = x^2 + 4y^2 + 9z^2 + 2(2xy + 2y(-3z) + x(-3z)) = x^2 + 4y^2 + 9z^2 + 4xy - 12yz - 6xz.
(ii) Substitute the given values into the expansion: 49 = 49 + 2(2xy - 6yz - 3xz) 0 = 2(2xy - 6yz - 3xz) \boxed{2xy - 6yz - 3xz = 0}.







NCERT Exemplar pointer: Exemplar Problem 8.4 (using (a+b+c)^2 to extract a cross-term sum) is a near-twin of the 4-mark item. Previous-year pointer: CBSE 2019 Set 1, Q19 (“compute 103^2 using an identity”) matches the 3-mark style.





Common confusions

“(a+b)^2 = a^2 + b^2.” Almost always wrong. The cross term 2ab is missing. Picture the rectangle: it has two off-diagonal tiles, not zero.
“(a-b)^2 is negative.” No — it’s always \geq 0. The square of a real number is non-negative.
“FOIL only works on (a+b)(c+d).” It works on any two binomials. The four pieces are first, outer, inner, last. Identities are just FOIL with patterns.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this chapter, remember these:

An algebraic expression is just numbers and letters combined by the four operations and powers — and like terms live in the same column when you tidy up.
The three standard identities are the rectangle picture told algebraically: (a+b)^2, (a-b)^2, and the difference of squares.
Identities are equalities for every value — they let you transform an expression’s shape without changing what it is. That move is the whole game of algebra.



Self-Assessment
Be honest with yourself.

Could you re-derive (a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2 from a rectangle picture, on a blank sheet, without notes?
Did you solve at least 6 of the 7 Discover problems and 5 of the 7 Practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?
Given a number puzzle like 103 \times 97, can you spot which identity to deploy?


3 yes: move on to Mensuration.
2 yes: redo Stretch S2 and the 4-mark board item, then move on.
0–1 yes: rebuild from Discover Problems 4–6. The three identities are the foundation of the next chapter.

title: Ch 8 — Algebraic Expressions and Identities
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

Intro to Variables — letters as placeholders.
Multiplication as Area — the rectangle picture that proves the identities.



What this unlocks

Factorisation — running the identities backward.
Class 9 — Polynomials — adds the Remainder and Factor theorems.
Class 10 — Polynomials — relations between roots and coefficients.



NCERT chapter alignment

Class 8, Chapter 8 — Algebraic Expressions and Identities (NCERT 2023 rationalised syllabus). Covers Sections 8.1–8.5: terms, like terms, multiplication of monomials and polynomials, the standard identities.
Sister synthesis lesson (different pedagogical angle): t2.factoring-polynomials inverts these identities; this chapter establishes them by direct expansion.
Read alongside the NCERT textbook, which remains the canonical source for the board paper.



Where this matters

Research. The identity a^2 - b^2 = (a+b)(a-b) is the seed of factorisation in number theory; Fermat’s factorisation method (used by hand to break small RSA-like keys) starts from this rectangle picture.


AI / ML. The bias-variance decomposition in machine learning is a direct application of (a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2 applied to the expected-error term — the same algebra you learned today, used by practitioners every day.

title: Ch 10 — Exponents and Powers
text:
Be honest before you begin.

Can you compute 2^3, 3^2, 5^2 in your head? If not, revisit Multiplication as Area.
Are you comfortable with -3 and -5 as numbers you can do arithmetic on? If not, revisit Negative Numbers.
Do you know that a \cdot a \cdot a is written a^3?
Can you tell which is bigger, \tfrac{1}{100} or \tfrac{1}{10}?

If three were a confident yes, read on.

title: Ch 10 — Exponents and Powers
Ch 10 — Exponents and Powers
text:
Wonder
Sit with these.

After 10 folds you have 1024 layers. After 20 folds, you have one million. By how many factors does each fold multiply your stack?
If 2^{10} \approx 1000, can you guess what 2^{20} is, without computing?
We have a name for a \cdot a (“a squared”) and a \cdot a \cdot
a (“a cubed”). What might a^0 mean? Or a^{-1}?
Could you write the number 0.000\,000\,1 using a power, without spelling out the seven zeros?







TipWhat you might notice (don’t peek until you’ve thought)





The pattern 2^1 = 2, 2^2 = 4, 2^3 = 8, 2^4 = 16, \ldots has a built-in rule: going up one step multiplies by 2. Reverse the rule: going down one step divides by 2. So 2^0 should equal 1 (one step below 2^1 = 2, divide by 2). 2^{-1} = \tfrac{1}{2}. 2^{-2} =
\tfrac{1}{4}. The negative exponents fall out of the pattern.

title: Ch 10 — Exponents and Powers
text:
Discover

Problem 1 — Multiply same base
What is 2^3 \times 2^4? Can you write the answer as a single power of 2?


Hint 1 — a nudge

2^3 = 2 \cdot 2 \cdot 2 (three twos). 2^4 = 2 \cdot 2 \cdot 2 \cdot 2 (four twos). Together?



Full solution

2^3 \cdot 2^4 = 2^{3+4} = 2^7 = 128.
Rule: a^m \cdot a^n = a^{m+n}.



Problem 2 — Divide same base
What is \tfrac{2^7}{2^3} as a single power of 2?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Three of the seven 2s in the top cancel against the three 2s in the bottom.



Full solution

\tfrac{2^7}{2^3} = 2^{7-3} = 2^4 = 16.
Rule: \dfrac{a^m}{a^n} = a^{m-n}.



Problem 3 — Power of a power
What is (2^3)^4?


Hint 1 — a nudge

(2^3)^4 means four copies of 2^3 multiplied together.



Full solution

(2^3)^4 = 2^3 \cdot 2^3 \cdot 2^3 \cdot 2^3 = 2^{3 \cdot 4} = 2^{12} =
4096.
Rule: (a^m)^n = a^{mn}.



Problem 4 — Zero exponent
Use the division rule on \tfrac{2^5}{2^5}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\tfrac{2^5}{2^5} = 2^{5-5} = 2^0. But also \tfrac{2^5}{2^5} = 1.



Full solution

2^0 = 1. And in general, a^0 = 1 for any nonzero a.



Problem 5 — Negative exponent
Use the division rule on \tfrac{2^3}{2^5}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

By the rule: 2^{3-5} = 2^{-2}. By cancellation: \tfrac{1}{2^2} =
\tfrac{1}{4}.



Full solution

2^{-2} = \tfrac{1}{2^2} = \tfrac{1}{4}.
Rule: a^{-n} = \dfrac{1}{a^n}.



Problem 6 — A very small number
Express 0.000\,07 in standard form: as a \times 10^n with 1 \leq a < 10 and n an integer.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Move the decimal point so that exactly one nonzero digit is to the left of it. Count moves; each move to the right of the original is a negative power of 10.



Full solution

0.000\,07 = 7 \times 10^{-5}.



Problem 7 — A very big number
Express 24{,}50{,}00{,}00{,}000 in standard form.


Hint 1 — a nudge

The decimal sits at the end. Move it so only 2.45 is to the left.



Full solution

24{,}50{,}00{,}00{,}000 = 2.45 \times 10^{10}.

You have just discovered every law you need: multiplication adds exponents, division subtracts, powers of powers multiply, a^0 = 1, a^{-n} = 1/a^n. Standard form rides on the same rules with base 10.

Decimal exponents — and the very idea of writing fractions as a power of ten — were popularised by the Flemish engineer Simon Stevin in his 36-page pamphlet De Thiende (“The Tenth”, 1585). Stevin argued that once arithmetic was done in base 10, it could finally be performed by “every astrologer, surveyor, tapestry-maker, gauger, mintmaster, and the like”. The modern decimal point arrived a generation later; the negative exponent had to wait until John Wallis (1685) gave it the meaning a^{-n} = 1/a^n that you discovered in Discover Problem 5.


Every law of exponents falls out of one observation: a^n counts how many copies of a are stacked. Multiplying joins two stacks (add counts); dividing cancels (subtract counts); a power of a power is a stack of stacks (multiply counts); the exponent 0 is “no copies” and equals 1, the multiplicative identity.

title: Ch 10 — Exponents and Powers
text:
Name

For any nonzero base a and integers m, n:



Law
Formula




Product, same base
a^m \cdot a^n = a^{m+n}


Quotient, same base
\dfrac{a^m}{a^n} = a^{m-n}


Power of a power
(a^m)^n = a^{mn}


Power of a product
(ab)^n = a^n b^n


Zero exponent
a^0 = 1


Negative exponent
a^{-n} = \dfrac{1}{a^n}



Standard form (scientific notation): any positive number can be written as k \times 10^n, \qquad 1 \leq k < 10, \quad n \in \mathbb{Z}.

A short verse, in the spirit of Bhāskara’s Līlāvatī:

Same base meeting: add the powers; same base parting: subtract. A power of a power: multiply through. Zero on top: the answer is one; Negative exponent: a fraction is born.

title: Ch 10 — Exponents and Powers
text:
Apply

Worked example

Simplify \dfrac{(3^4)^2 \cdot 3^{-3}}{3^5}.

Competent solver’s path:

(3^4)^2 = 3^{8}.
Numerator: 3^8 \cdot 3^{-3} = 3^{8 + (-3)} = 3^5.
Whole expression: \dfrac{3^5}{3^5} = 3^{0} = 1.

Answer: 1.


Practice — Warm
W1. Simplify 5^{-2}.


Full solution

5^{-2} = \tfrac{1}{25}.

W2. Simplify (2^3)^4 \cdot 2^{-10}.


Full solution

(2^3)^4 = 2^{12}. 2^{12} \cdot 2^{-10} = 2^{2} = 4.

W3. Express 0.000\,000\,002 in standard form.


Full solution

2 \times 10^{-9}.



Practice — Working
P1. Find the value of \left(\tfrac{2}{3}\right)^{-3}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

\left(\tfrac{a}{b}\right)^{-n} = \left(\tfrac{b}{a}\right)^{n}.



Full solution

\left(\tfrac{2}{3}\right)^{-3} = \left(\tfrac{3}{2}\right)^{3} =
\tfrac{27}{8}.

P2. Simplify \dfrac{(-3)^4 \times (5)^2}{(-3)^2 \times (5)^3}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Use the same-base rules separately for (-3) and for 5.



Full solution

\dfrac{(-3)^4}{(-3)^2} = (-3)^{2} = 9. \dfrac{5^2}{5^3} = 5^{-1} = \tfrac{1}{5}. Product: 9 \cdot \tfrac{1}{5} = \tfrac{9}{5}.

P3. Express in standard form: 1{,}50{,}00{,}00{,}000 + 0.000\,000\,15. Then add them as standard-form numbers (you do not need a single common form — write each separately).


Hint 1 — a nudge

Each in standard form independently.



Full solution

1{,}50{,}00{,}00{,}000 = 1.5 \times 10^{9}. 0.000\,000\,15 = 1.5 \times 10^{-7}.
(Sum is dominated by the first: \approx 1.5 \times 10^9.)



Practice — Stretch
S1. Compare without computing each: 2^{30} versus 3^{20}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Rewrite both in a common form. 2^{30} = (2^3)^{10} = 8^{10}. 3^{20} = (3^2)^{10} = 9^{10}.



Full solution

2^{30} = 8^{10} and 3^{20} = 9^{10}. Since 9 > 8, 9^{10} >
8^{10}. So \boxed{3^{20} > 2^{30}}.

S2. A bacterium divides every 20 minutes. Starting with 1 bacterium, how many will there be after 4 hours? Express in standard form.


Hint 1 — a nudge

4 hours = 12 doubling periods.



Full solution

2^{12} = 4096. In standard form: 4.096 \times 10^3.



Board-Pattern Practice
1-mark (objective). \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^{-3} equals (a) \tfrac{1}{8} (b) -8 (c) 8 (d) \tfrac{3}{2}.


Full solution

\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^{-3} = 2^3 = 8. Answer: (c).

2-mark (short answer). Simplify (2^{-3} + 3^{-1})^{-1}.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Compute the sum inside, then take its reciprocal.



Full solution

2^{-3} + 3^{-1} = \tfrac{1}{8} + \tfrac{1}{3} = \tfrac{3 + 8}{24} =
\tfrac{11}{24}.
Reciprocal: \tfrac{24}{11}.

3-mark (short answer II). The mass of an electron is approximately 9.11 \times 10^{-31} kg. The mass of a proton is approximately 1.67 \times 10^{-27} kg. About how many times heavier than the electron is the proton?


Hint 1 — a nudge

Divide the two standard-form numbers; subtract exponents.



Full solution

Ratio = \dfrac{1.67 \times 10^{-27}}{9.11 \times 10^{-31}} =
\dfrac{1.67}{9.11} \times 10^{-27 - (-31)} = 0.1833 \times 10^4 \approx
1833.
The proton is about 1{,}833 times heavier.

4-mark (long answer). (i) Express in standard form: the diameter of a hydrogen atom, 0.000\,000\,000\,1 m. (ii) About 6.022 \times
10^{23} atoms make 1 gram of hydrogen. If each atom has the diameter from (i), find the total length of all those atoms placed end-to-end. Express in metres in standard form, and convert to kilometres.


Hint 1 — a nudge

Total length = number of atoms × diameter of one. Use the laws of exponents.



Full solution

(i) 0.000\,000\,000\,1 = 1 \times 10^{-10} m.
(ii) Total = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 1 \times 10^{-10} =
6.022 \times 10^{13} m.
In km: divide by 1000 = 10^3: 6.022 \times 10^{13} \div 10^3 = 6.022 \times 10^{10} km.
(That’s about 60 billion km — roughly 400 times the Earth–Sun distance. The point: standard form is necessary once numbers get this unwieldy.)







NCERT Exemplar pointer: Exemplar Problem 10.4 (atomic-scale ratios using standard form) is a near-twin of the 4-mark item. Previous-year pointer: CBSE 2019 Set 2, Q9 (“simplify (a^{-3})^4 \cdot a^5”) matches Working P2 in style.





Common confusions

“a^0 = 0.” No — a^0 = 1 (for nonzero a). The “zero” lives in the exponent, not in the result.
“2^{-3} = -8.” No. Negative exponent means reciprocal, not negation: 2^{-3} = \tfrac{1}{8}.
“(a + b)^n = a^n + b^n.” Almost never true (except n = 1). This is the same trap as in Algebraic Identities — distributing a power across a sum is wrong.



Takeaways
If you remember nothing else from this chapter, remember these:

The exponent counts copies. The six laws — product, quotient, power-of-power, power-of-product, zero, and negative — are six ways of saying “stack on, stack off”.
a^0 = 1 and a^{-n} = 1/a^n are not arbitrary rules. They are the only values that keep the product and quotient laws working continuously across the boundary.
Standard form, k \times 10^n, is how scientists, engineers, and computers store numbers from electron-masses to galactic distances on the same page.



Self-Assessment
Be honest with yourself.

Could you state and justify (with a quick example) all six exponent laws?
Did you solve at least 6 of the 7 Discover problems and 5 of the 7 Practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?
Given a number like 0.000\,000\,000\,42, can you write it in standard form in five seconds?


3 yes: move on to Direct and Inverse Proportions.
2 yes: redo Working P2 and the 4-mark board item, then move on.
0–1 yes: redo Discover problems 4 and 5 — until a^0 = 1 and a^{-n} = 1/a^n feel like consequences, not memorised rules.

title: Ch 10 — Exponents and Powers
text:
Connect

Prerequisites used

Multiplication as Area — repeated multiplication, the picture of a^2.
Negative Numbers — negative exponents need a number line that goes both ways.



What this unlocks

Factorisation — the laws of exponents are used at every step.
Class 9 — Number Systems — extends to rational and (formally) irrational exponents.
Class 10 — Real Numbers — the fundamental theorem in exponent form.



NCERT chapter alignment

Class 8, Chapter 10 — Exponents and Powers (NCERT 2023 rationalised syllabus). Covers Sections 10.1–10.3: laws of exponents with integer exponents, very small and very large numbers in standard form.
Sister synthesis lesson (different pedagogical angle): t2.exponents-powers treats exponents at the synthesis level; this chapter focuses on computational fluency and standard form.
Read alongside the NCERT textbook, which remains the canonical source for the board paper.



Where this matters

Systems. Computer memory is sized in powers of 2: a kilobyte is 2^{10} = 1024 bytes, a megabyte is 2^{20}, a gigabyte is 2^{30}. Every layer of a computer’s address space is a power of two.


Encryption. RSA’s “modular exponentiation” — computing a^b \bmod
n for b a 2048-bit number — is the inner loop of every TLS handshake on the internet. The laws of exponents you learnt today are literally the algorithm.


AI / ML. Floating-point numbers, the only way computers store real numbers, are standard-form numbers: \pm m \times 2^e. Every neural-net weight, every gradient, every learning rate is one such number.

title: Glossary
text:
This glossary is the canonical, plain-English reference for every term any lesson formally introduces in its ## Name beat. Each entry gives:

Plain-English one-liner. What the term means, in words you could say aloud to a friend.
First named in. The lesson where this term is first defined — your starting point if you want the full story, the worked examples, and the “where this lives in the world” connections.

This page grows with the curriculum. If a term is missing from a recently written lesson, that’s a bug — please open an issue.

title: Glossary
text:
How to use this page
You can land on this page two ways:

Navbar → Glossary. Browse alphabetically, or use the table of contents on the right.
Inline link from a lesson. Many lessons cross-link to specific entries. Clicking returns you to the same scroll position via your browser’s back button.

The glossary is not a substitute for the lessons. A definition tells you what a thing is; a lesson teaches you why it matters. Use the First named in link whenever you want the full picture.

title: Glossary
text:
A

Absolute extremum
The largest (max) or smallest (min) value of f on a closed interval [a, b]; found by comparing f at interior critical points and at the endpoints.
First named in: Class 12, Chapter 6 — Application of Derivatives (Maxima and Minima)


Addition
Combining two numbers into a total — pictured on the number line as a hop to the right by the second number’s size.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 4 — Addition and Subtraction


Addition rule (probability)
P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B). The general rule for the probability of a union; the overlap subtraction vanishes when the events are mutually exclusive.
First named in: Class 11, Chapter 14 — Probability


Adjoint of a matrix
The transpose of the cofactor matrix; satisfies A \cdot \text{adj}(A) = |A| \cdot I and gives the inverse via A^{-1} = \tfrac{1}{|A|}\text{adj}(A).
First named in: Class 12, Chapter 4 — Matrix Inverse and Cramer’s Rule


Algebraic connectivity
The second-smallest eigenvalue \lambda_2(L) of the graph Laplacian — small for graphs with bottlenecks, large for robustly connected ones (Fiedler 1973).
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 14 — Spectral Graph Theory


Algebraic expression
A recipe built from numbers, variables, and operations, like 2x + 5 or (a + b)/2.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 11 — Variables


Algebraic fraction
An expression P(x)/Q(x) where P and Q are polynomials and Q is not the zero polynomial; the domain is all x for which Q(x) \ne 0. Manipulated by the same rules as numerical fractions: factor and cancel, common-denominator addition, multiply across, divide by reciprocal.
First named in: Kiselev Algebra Part I — Chapter 4, Algebraic fractions


Analytic function
A complex function f: D \subseteq \mathbb{C} \to \mathbb{C} whose derivative f'(z) exists as a complex limit independent of the direction of approach.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 5 — Complex Functions


Angle
A measure of the turn between two lines that meet at a common point. Measured in degrees: a full turn is 360°.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 12 — Shapes and Angles


Angle of depression
The angle between the horizontal at the observer and the line of sight downward to a lower object. Equal to the angle of elevation from the lower point (alternate-interior angles).
First named in: Class 10, Chapter 9 — Some Applications of Trigonometry


Angle of elevation
The angle between the horizontal at the observer and the line of sight upward to a higher object.
First named in: Class 10, Chapter 9 — Some Applications of Trigonometry


Antiderivative
A function whose derivative gives back the original function. If F'(x) = f(x), then F is an antiderivative of f — and so is F + C for any constant.
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 9 — The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus


Area
The size of the surface inside a flat shape, measured in square units (m², cm²).
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 13 — Area, Perimeter, Volume


Arithmetic-geometric progression
A sequence whose k-th term is the term-by-term product of an AP and a GP: T_k = (a + (k-1)d)\,r^{k-1}. Summed by the “multiply by r and subtract” trick, which converts it into a plain GP plus a boundary term.
First named in: Class 11, Chapter 8 — Sequences and Series (8b)


Arithmetic sequence
A sequence in which each term is the previous term plus a fixed amount, called the common difference. Example: 3, 7, 11, 15, \ldots
First named in: Tier 2, Lesson 15 — Sequences (Intro)


Asymptote
A line that a curve approaches arbitrarily closely without ever touching. The hyperbola x^2/a^2 - y^2/b^2 = 1 has asymptotes y = \pm (b/a) x.
First named in: Class 11, Chapter 10 — Conic Sections (Ellipse and Hyperbola)


Axiom (common notion)
A general logical principle accepted without proof, applicable beyond geometry — for example, “things equal to the same thing are equal to one another.”
First named in: Class 9, Chapter 5 — Introduction to Euclid’s Geometry


Axiomatic probability
Probability defined by Kolmogorov’s three axioms — non-negativity, P(S) = 1, and additivity for mutually exclusive events — rather than by counting equally-likely outcomes.
First named in: Class 11, Chapter 14 — Probability

title: Glossary
text:
B

Base
Two related meanings, both about the number that “sets the scale”:

Base of a place-value system. The number on which a place-value system is built. Base 10 (everyday), base 2 (binary, used by computers), base 16 (hex, used by programmers), base 60 (Babylonian, still used for hours and minutes).
Base of an exponent. In an expression like a^n, the number a that is being multiplied by itself.

First named in (place-value sense): Tier 1, Lesson 3 — Place Value First named in (exponent sense): Tier 2, Lesson 7 — Exponents and Powers


Basis
A linearly independent set of vectors whose span is the whole space; equivalently, a minimal spanning set or a maximal independent set.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 3 — Vector Spaces, Linear Independence, Basis


Bayes’ theorem
P(A \mid B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) / P(B) — the rule for flipping a conditional probability. Turns “what you can measure” into “what you actually want to know.”
First named in: Tier 3, Lesson 14 — Conditional Probability and Independence


Bellman equation
The recursion V(s) = \mathbb{E}[R(s,a) + \gamma V(s')] defining the value of a state as immediate reward plus discounted future value; for the optimal value function the expectation is replaced by a max over actions, and the resulting operator is a \gamma-contraction with a unique fixed point.
First named in: Tier 7, Lesson 6 — Reinforcement Learning Theory


Bijection
A function that is both one-one (injective) and onto (surjective) — a perfect pairing of inputs with outputs that has an inverse.
First named in: Class 12, Chapter 1 — Relations and Functions


Binomial distribution
The PMF P(X = k) = \binom{n}{k} p^k (1-p)^{n-k} counting the number of successes in n independent trials, each with probability p. Mean is np.
First named in: Tier 3, Lesson 15 — Discrete Probability Distributions


Binomial theorem
The identity (a+b)^n = \sum_{r=0}^{n} \binom{n}{r} a^{n-r} b^r — every term of the expansion is a binomial coefficient times a single power product.
First named in: Tier 3, Lesson 12 — The Binomial Theorem


Borrow
The bookkeeping step in column-subtraction: when a column doesn’t have enough to subtract from, you take ten from the column to its left.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 4 — Addition and Subtraction


Burnside’s lemma
The number of orbits of a group action equals the average number of points fixed by each group element. Despite the name, due to Cauchy and Frobenius.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 3 — Group Actions

title: Glossary
text:
C

Carry
The bookkeeping step in column-addition: when a column’s total exceeds 9, the overflow moves into the column to its left.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 4 — Addition and Subtraction


Cauchy-Riemann equations
The conditions u_x = v_y and u_y = -v_x on the real and imaginary parts f = u + iv that characterise complex differentiability.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 5 — Complex Functions


Cauchy sequence
A sequence whose terms become arbitrarily close to one another: for every \epsilon > 0 there exists N with |a_m - a_n| < \epsilon for all m, n \ge N.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 15 — Continuity and Differentiability — Rigorous Foundation


Cauchy’s theorem
For an analytic f in a simply-connected region, \oint_C f(z)\, dz = 0 for every closed contour C. The keystone of complex analysis.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 5 — Complex Functions


Cavalieri’s principle
If two solids lie between the same two parallel planes and every plane parallel to those slices both solids in regions of equal area, the two solids have equal volume. The discrete ancestor of integration by slicing.
First named in: Kiselev Geometry Book II — Chapter 2, Polyhedra


Central Limit Theorem
For i.i.d. variables with finite variance, the standardised sample mean converges in distribution to a standard normal — regardless of the original distribution.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 9 — Variance, Standard Deviation, and the Central Limit Theorem


Chain rule
(f(g(x)))' = f'(g(x)) \cdot g'(x) — derivative of a composition is “outer derivative times inner derivative.”
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 4 — Differentiation Rules


Chord
A straight line segment whose two endpoints lie on a circle. The diameter is the longest chord.
First named in: Class 9, Chapter 9 — Circles


Circle
The set of points at a fixed distance (the radius) from a chosen centre.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 12 — Shapes and Angles


Class number
The size h_K = |\mathrm{Cl}(K)| of the ideal class group of a number field K — equivalently, the number of equivalence classes of fractional ideals modulo principal ideals. h_K = 1 exactly when \mathcal{O}_K is a PID (and a UFD); larger h_K measures how badly element-level unique factorisation fails.
First named in: Tier 7, Lesson 12 — Algebraic Number Theory


Combination
A selection of r items from n where order does not matter: \binom{n}{r} = n!/(r!(n-r)!). Counts the r-element subsets of an n-set.
First named in: Tier 3, Lesson 11 — Combinatorics — Counting


Common difference
The fixed step d added to each term of an arithmetic progression to get the next term; can be positive, negative, zero, or fractional.
First named in: Class 10, Chapter 5 — Arithmetic Progressions


Commutative
A property of an operation: the order of the two inputs doesn’t change the answer. Addition and multiplication are commutative; subtraction and division are not.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 5 — Multiplication as Area


Compactness
A topological tameness property: every open cover has a finite subcover. In \mathbb{R}^n this is “closed and bounded” (Heine-Borel); in general it is the property that lets continuous functions attain their max and min and turns infinite arguments into finite ones.
First named in: Tier 7, Lesson 2 — Topology


Complement of an event
For an event E in a sample space S, the complement \bar{E} is the event “E does not happen”. P(\bar{E}) = 1 - P(E).
First named in: Class 10, Chapter 14 — Probability


Complex number
A number z = a + bi with a, b \in \mathbb{R} and i^2 = -1. Lives as a point in the complex plane.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 4 — Complex Numbers


Completeness
A space is complete if every Cauchy sequence in it converges to a limit in the space; \mathbb{R} is complete, \mathbb{Q} is not.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 15 — Continuity and Differentiability — Rigorous Foundation


Composite
A whole number \ge 2 that is not prime — equivalently, has at least three divisors.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 15 — Divisibility and Primes


Compound-angle formula
The master identity \sin(A \pm B) = \sin A \cos B \pm \cos A \sin B (and its cosine and tangent siblings). Every double-angle, half-angle, triple-angle, sum-to-product, and product-to-sum formula descends from it.
First named in: Class 11, Chapter 3 — Trig Identities & Compound Angles


Compound interest
Interest computed each period on the running total, so each period’s interest itself earns interest. After n years at annual rate r: A = P(1 + r/100)^n.
First named in: Class 8, Chapter 7 — Comparing Quantities


Condition number
For a matrix, \kappa(A) = \|A\| \cdot \|A^{-1}\|. Measures how much input error a linear system Ax = b amplifies in the output. Large \kappa = ill-conditioned.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 8 — Numerical Methods


Conditional probability
P(A \mid B) = P(A \cap B) / P(B) — the probability of A once you restrict the sample space to outcomes where B has already happened.
First named in: Tier 3, Lesson 14 — Conditional Probability and Independence


Cone
A solid with a circular base and a single apex; volume \tfrac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h, slant height l = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2}.
First named in: Class 9, Chapter 11 — Surface Areas and Volumes


Confidence interval
A random interval whose construction catches the true parameter with a stated long-run frequency (e.g. 95%) under repeated sampling.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 10 — Statistical Inference


Congruence
Two figures are congruent if one can be moved to coincide with the other exactly — same shape and same size. For triangles, congruence is forced by SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, or RHS.
First named in: Class 9, Chapter 7 — Triangles


Conjugate (surd)
The expression a - \sqrt{b} is the conjugate of a + \sqrt{b}. Multiplying a binomial surd by its conjugate gives a^2 - b, a plain rational — the trick behind rationalising the denominator.
First named in: Class 9, Chapter 1 — Number Systems


Continuous function
A function whose graph can be traced through a point (or interval) without lifting the pen — formally, \lim_{x \to a} f(x) = f(a).
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 2 — Continuity


Convergence
A sequence a_n converges to L if, for every \epsilon > 0, there exists N such that n \ge N implies |a_n - L| < \epsilon.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 14 — Sequences, Series, and Convergence


Convex function
A function whose graph lies below every chord — bowl-shaped, with no internal bumps; every local minimum is automatically the global minimum.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 9 — Convex Optimisation


Convolution theorem
Convolution in the time domain becomes pointwise multiplication in the frequency domain (and vice versa) — the algorithmic miracle behind FFT-based filtering.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 13 — The Fourier Transform


Coordinate plane
Two number lines crossed at right angles, used to give every point in the plane a unique address (x, y).
First named in: Tier 2, Lesson 4 — The Coordinate Plane


Cost price
The price at which an item is bought (CP). The base for computing profit % and loss %.
First named in: Class 8, Chapter 7 — Comparing Quantities


Cramer’s rule
For AX = B with |A| \ne 0, each unknown is x_i = D_i/D, where D_i replaces column i of A with B. Elegant for small systems, exponentially slow for large.
First named in: Class 12, Chapter 4 — Matrix Inverse and Cramer’s Rule


Critical point
A point where f'(x) = 0 or f'(x) does not exist — the only places a smooth function can hide a local extremum.
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 6 — Optimisation


Cross entropy
A measure -\sum p_i \log q_i of how badly a predicted distribution q matches a true distribution p; the standard classification loss in deep learning.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 11 — Information Theory


Cross product
A 3D vector built from two vectors, perpendicular to both, with magnitude |a||b|\sin\theta (= area of the parallelogram they span). Direction follows the right-hand rule.
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 14 — Dot and Cross Product


Cube root
For a real number a, the unique real number b with b^3 = a, written \sqrt[3]{a}. Defined for negatives too: \sqrt[3]{-a} = -\sqrt[3]{a}.
First named in: Class 8, Chapter 6 — Cubes and Cube Roots


Curl
The local rotation of a vector field; \nabla \times \mathbf{F} — a scalar in 2D, a vector along the rotation axis in 3D.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 7 — Vector Fields, Divergence, Curl


Cyclic quadrilateral
A quadrilateral whose four vertices all lie on a single circle. Its opposite angles are supplementary (sum to 180°).
First named in: Class 9, Chapter 9 — Circles


Cylinder
A solid swept by translating a circle of radius r along a perpendicular axis of length h; volume \pi r^2 h, curved surface area 2 \pi r h.
First named in: Class 8, Chapter 9 — Mensuration

title: Glossary
text:
D

Decimal
A number written in base-10 place value, with columns continuing to the right of a decimal point — each step right divides the value by ten.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 8 — Decimals


Decimal point
The dot that marks the boundary between the ones column and the tenths column in a decimal number. It is purely a position marker, not an operator.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 8 — Decimals


Definite integral
The signed area between a function’s graph and the x-axis over an interval [a, b], written \int_a^b f(x)\,dx. Defined as the limit of Riemann sums; by the Fundamental Theorem, it equals F(b) - F(a) for any antiderivative F.
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 8 — The Definite Integral


Degree of a polynomial
The highest power of the variable that appears in a polynomial. The degree of 3x^2 - 4x + 7 is 2.
First named in: Tier 2, Lesson 11 — Polynomials


Derivative
The instantaneous rate of change of f at a — the limit \lim_{h \to 0} [f(a+h) - f(a)]/h, equal to the slope of the tangent line.
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 3 — The Derivative


Determinant
A scalar attached to every square matrix that equals the signed n-volume of the parallelepiped spanned by its columns; non-zero exactly when the matrix is invertible.
First named in: Class 12, Chapter 4 — Determinants


Difference of squares
The pattern x^2 - a^2 = (x + a)(x - a) — one of the most useful factoring identities in algebra.
First named in: Tier 2, Lesson 11 — Polynomials


Differentiable function
A function for which the limit \lim_{h \to 0} [f(a+h) - f(a)]/h exists at every point of its domain. Differentiable implies continuous, but not the reverse.
First named in: Class 12, Chapter 5 — Differentiability and Rules of Differentiation


Differential
The infinitesimal-language symbol df = f'(x)\,dx — the linear approximation written compactly.
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 7 — Related Rates


Differential equation
An equation relating an unknown function to its own derivatives. Its solutions are functions, not numbers; the most famous is y' = ky, whose solution is exponential.
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 12 — Differential Equations


Dihedral angle
The angle between two half-planes that share a common edge; measured by the planar angle of any cross-section perpendicular to the edge.
First named in: Kiselev Geometry Book II — Chapter 1, Lines and planes


Dimension
The size (number of vectors) of any basis of a vector space; the number of independent degrees of freedom.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 3 — Vector Spaces, Linear Independence, Basis


Direct proportion
Two quantities x and y are in direct proportion if y = kx for a fixed constant k > 0 — their ratio stays the same.
First named in: Class 8, Chapter 11 — Direct and Inverse Proportions


Directrix
A fixed line used in the locus definition of a parabola: every point on the parabola is equidistant from the focus and the directrix.
First named in: Class 11, Chapter 10 — Conic Sections (Circle and Parabola)


Discriminant
The expression b^2 - 4ac that appears under the square root in the quadratic formula. Its sign tells you how many real roots the quadratic has.
First named in: Tier 2, Lesson 14 — Quadratic Equations (Intro)


Distance formula
d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2} — the Pythagorean theorem applied to the legs |x_2 - x_1| and |y_2 - y_1| of an axis-aligned right triangle.
First named in: Class 10, Chapter 7 — Coordinate Geometry


Distributive
The rule that lets you split a multiplication across an addition: a \times (b + c) = a \times b + a \times c. Geometrically, it’s slicing a rectangle into two smaller rectangles side by side.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 5 — Multiplication as Area


Divergence
The scalar \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F} measuring how much a vector field spreads outward (positive: source) or contracts (negative: sink) at a point.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 7 — Vector Fields, Divergence, Curl


Divisibility
A whole number a is divisible by b if a \div b leaves no remainder — equivalently, if a is some whole-number multiple of b.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 15 — Divisibility and Primes


Division
The inverse of multiplication: given a total and one factor, find the other factor — equivalently, splitting a total into equal shares.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 6 — Division as Fair Share


Domain (of a function)
The set of permitted inputs to a function. For a real function given by a formula, the largest domain is the set of x for which the formula is defined (no division by zero, no even root of a negative).
First named in: Class 11, Chapter 2 — Relations and Functions


Dot product
A scalar built from two vectors, a \cdot b = a_1b_1 + a_2b_2 + \cdots = |a||b|\cos\theta. Measures alignment: positive for acute angle, zero for perpendicular, negative for obtuse.
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 14 — Dot and Cross Product

title: Glossary
text:
E

Eccentricity
A single non-negative number that classifies a conic section: e = 0 for a circle, 0 < e < 1 for an ellipse, e = 1 for a parabola, e > 1 for a hyperbola. For an ellipse e = c/a with c = \sqrt{a^2 - b^2}.
First named in: Class 11, Chapter 10 — Conic Sections (Ellipse and Hyperbola)


Eigenvalue
A scalar \lambda such that Av = \lambda v has a non-zero solution; a root of \det(A - \lambda I) = 0. The “stretch factor” the matrix applies along its preferred direction.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 4 — Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors


Eigenvector
A non-zero vector that a matrix only stretches (does not rotate); Av = \lambda v for the corresponding eigenvalue \lambda.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 4 — Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors


Elimination method
A way to solve a system of equations: scale equations so that adding or subtracting them cancels one variable, leaving a single-variable equation.
First named in: Tier 2, Lesson 6 — Systems of Linear Equations


Ellipse
A stretched-circle conic section — the set of points whose distances to two fixed foci sum to a constant. Planets orbit the Sun in ellipses.
First named in: Tier 3, Lesson 3 — Conic Sections


Entropy
The average number of bits needed to encode one outcome of a random variable: H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i. Maximised by uniform distributions, zero for certainties.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 11 — Information Theory


Epsilon-delta definition
The rigorous definition of continuity at a: for every \epsilon > 0 there exists \delta > 0 such that |x - a| < \delta implies |f(x) - f(a)| < \epsilon.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 15 — Continuity and Differentiability — Rigorous Foundation


Equation
A statement that two expressions are equal. To solve an equation is to find the value(s) of the variable that make it true.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 11 — Variables


Equivalence relation
A relation that is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive — equivalent to a partition of the set into disjoint classes.
First named in: Class 12, Chapter 1 — Relations and Functions


Equivalent fractions
Two fractions naming the same number — obtained from one another by multiplying or dividing both numerator and denominator by the same nonzero number.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 7 — Fractions


Euclid’s algorithm
A repeated-division procedure that finds the GCD of two positive integers without ever factoring them — the oldest non-trivial algorithm still in routine use.
First named in: Class 10, Chapter 1 — Real Numbers


Euler’s identity
The equation e^{i\pi} + 1 = 0, joining the five constants e, i, \pi, 1, 0 in one formula. Often called the most beautiful equation in mathematics.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 4 — Complex Numbers


Euler’s polyhedron formula
For every convex polyhedron with V vertices, E edges, and F faces, V - E + F = 2. The first theorem of topology, proved synthetically by Cauchy via flattening one face.
First named in: Kiselev Geometry Book II — Chapter 5, Regular polyhedra


Expected value
The long-run average of a random variable: E[X] = \sum_x x \cdot P(X=x). Generalises the arithmetic mean to weighted outcomes.
First named in: Tier 3, Lesson 15 — Discrete Probability Distributions


Exponent
The small raised number in an expression like a^n that says how many copies of the base are multiplied together.
First named in: Tier 2, Lesson 7 — Exponents and Powers

title: Glossary
text:
F

Factorial
The product n! = n(n-1)(n-2) \cdots 1, with 0! = 1 by convention. Counts the number of orderings of n distinct objects.
First named in: Tier 3, Lesson 11 — Combinatorics — Counting


Factoring
Writing a polynomial as a product of simpler polynomials. The reverse of expanding a product. Example: x^2 + 5x + 6 = (x + 2)(x + 3).
First named in: Tier 2, Lesson 12 — Factoring Polynomials


Factor theorem
(x - a) is a factor of a polynomial p(x) if and only if p(a) = 0. The zeros of a polynomial and its linear factors are the same fact, twice.
First named in: Class 9, Chapter 2 — Polynomials


Feasible region
The set of all points in the plane (or higher-dimensional space) that simultaneously satisfy every constraint of a system of linear inequalities. The intersection of half-planes; always convex; its corner points are where linear-programming optima are found.
First named in: Class 11, Chapter 5 — Linear Inequalities


Fermat’s Last Theorem
The statement that a^n + b^n = c^n has no positive integer solutions for any integer n > 2. Conjectured by Fermat in 1637 (“I have a truly marvellous proof which this margin is too narrow to contain”) and proven by Andrew Wiles in 1995 via the Taniyama-Shimura conjecture on elliptic curves and modular forms.
First named in: Tier 7, Lesson 13 — Elliptic Curves


Fermat’s little theorem
If p is prime and \gcd(a, p) = 1, then a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod p.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 13 — Modular Arithmetic and the RSA Algorithm


Field
A commutative ring with identity in which every non-zero element has a multiplicative inverse — so you can divide. Examples: \mathbb{Q}, \mathbb{R}, \mathbb{C}, \mathbb{F}_p for prime p.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 2 — Rings and Fields


First derivative test
Classify a critical point c by the sign change of f' across c: + \to - gives a local max, - \to + a local min, no change neither.
First named in: Class 12, Chapter 6 — Application of Derivatives (Maxima and Minima)


First principles (derivative)
Computing f'(x) directly from the limit definition \lim_{h \to 0} (f(x+h) - f(x))/h rather than by applying differentiation rules.
First named in: Class 11, Chapter 12 — Derivatives (Introduction)


Focus (of a conic)
A fixed point used in the locus definition of a conic. A parabola has one focus; an ellipse and hyperbola have two. Distance to the focus controls the curve’s defining property.
First named in: Class 11, Chapter 10 — Conic Sections (Circle and Parabola)


Fourier series
The expression of a periodic function as an infinite sum of sines and cosines, with coefficients computed by integration against the trig basis.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 12 — Fourier Series


Fourier transform
An operation \hat f(k) = \int f(x) e^{-ikx} dx that re-expresses a signal as a function of frequency rather than time.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 13 — The Fourier Transform


Fraction
A number written \tfrac{p}{q} — “p of q equal pieces of a whole”. Equivalently, the answer to the division p \div q.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 7 — Fractions


Frequency
The number of times a value (or class of values) appears in a data set.
First named in: Class 8, Chapter 4 — Data Handling


Frequency distribution
A table or chart showing how often each value (or class interval) occurs in a dataset.
First named in: Class 9, Chapter 12 — Statistics


Frustum (of a cone)
The solid that remains when a cone is sliced parallel to its base and the top tip removed — a bucket-shape with two circular faces of different radii.
First named in: Class 10, Chapter 12 — Surface Areas and Volumes


Function
A rule that assigns to each allowed input exactly one output. The same input always returns the same output.
First named in: Tier 2, Lesson 1 — Functions as Machines


Fundamental theorem of algebra
Gauss (1799): every non-constant polynomial with complex coefficients has at least one complex root. Consequence: a degree-n polynomial over \mathbb{C} factors completely into n linear factors, so it has exactly n complex roots counted with multiplicity.
First named in: Kiselev Algebra Part II — Chapter 2, Higher-degree equations


Fundamental theorem of arithmetic
Every integer greater than 1 is a prime or a product of primes, and that prime factorisation is unique apart from the order of the factors.
First named in: Class 10, Chapter 1 — Real Numbers

title: Glossary
text:
G

Gaussian elimination
The systematic algorithm of using three row operations to reduce a linear system’s augmented matrix to triangular form, then back-substituting.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 2 — Linear Systems and Gaussian Elimination


General solution (trigonometric equation)
The full family of solutions to a basic trig equation. For \sin x = \sin\alpha: x = n\pi + (-1)^n\alpha. For \cos x = \cos\alpha: x = 2n\pi \pm \alpha. For \tan x = \tan\alpha: x = n\pi + \alpha. (n \in \mathbb{Z}.)
First named in: Class 11, Chapter 3 — Trigonometric Equations


Geometric mean
For positive numbers a and b, the geometric mean is \sqrt{ab}; for n positive numbers it is the n-th root of their product. It is the “multiplicative average” — the constant ratio that, applied n times to the first term, lands on the last in a geometric progression.
First named in: Class 11, Chapter 8 — Sequences and Series (8a)


Geometric sequence
A sequence in which each term is the previous term times a fixed number, called the common ratio. Example: 2, 6, 18, 54, \ldots
First named in: Tier 2, Lesson 15 — Sequences (Intro)


Golden ratio
The number \varphi = (1 + \sqrt{5})/2 \approx 1.618, equal to the limit of the ratio of successive Fibonacci numbers and the unique positive root of x^2 = x + 1.
First named in: Kiselev Algebra Part II — Chapter 5, Sequences and Progressions


Gradient
The vector of all partial derivatives of a scalar-valued function; points in the direction of steepest ascent, with magnitude equal to that maximum rate.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 5 — Partial Derivatives and the Gradient


Gradient descent
The iterative rule w \leftarrow w - \eta\,\nabla L(w): take a small step opposite the gradient to walk downhill on a loss landscape.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 10 — Gradient Methods and SGD Variants


Graph
A pair G = (V, E) of vertices and edges modelling pairwise relationships; edges may be directed, weighted, or neither.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 12 — Graphs and Their Algorithms


Graph Laplacian
The matrix L = D - A (degree matrix minus adjacency matrix) of a graph; symmetric, positive semi-definite, and its zero eigenvalues count connected components.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 14 — Spectral Graph Theory


Greatest common divisor (GCD)
The largest whole number that divides both of two given whole numbers. Used for simplifying fractions and in the Euclidean algorithm.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 15 — Divisibility and Primes


Group
A set with a single operation satisfying four axioms — closure, associativity, identity, inverses — that abstracts every notion of symmetry, from clock arithmetic to Rubik’s cube.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 1 — Groups


Group action
A way for a group G to “move” the points of a set X, with e \cdot x = x and (gh) \cdot x = g \cdot (h \cdot x).
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 3 — Group Actions

title: Glossary
text:
H

Handshake lemma
In any graph, the sum of vertex degrees equals twice the number of edges; equivalently, the number of odd-degree vertices is even.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 12 — Graphs and Their Algorithms


Heat equation
u_t = \alpha\, u_{xx} (with multidimensional Laplacian in higher dimensions). Models diffusion of heat or particles. Time-irreversible — entropy increases.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 7 — PDEs


Heron’s formula
For a triangle with sides a, b, c and semi-perimeter s = (a+b+c)/2, the area is \sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)} — depending only on the three sides.
First named in: Class 9, Chapter 10 — Heron’s Formula


Hilbert space
A complete inner-product space — the infinite-dimensional analogue of Euclidean space; the natural setting for Fourier analysis, quantum mechanics, and modern PDE theory.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 15 — The Spectral Theorem


Histogram
A bar chart of a grouped frequency table; bars touch because the class intervals are continuous.
First named in: Class 8, Chapter 4 — Data Handling


Hyperbola
A two-branch conic section — the set of points whose distances to two foci differ by a constant. Comet flybys trace hyperbolas.
First named in: Tier 3, Lesson 3 — Conic Sections


Hypotenuse
The side of a right triangle opposite the right angle — always the longest side.
First named in: Tier 2, Lesson 10 — The Pythagorean Theorem


Hypothesis test
A procedure that rejects a null hypothesis H_0 in favour of an alternative H_1 when a test statistic falls in a region of low probability under H_0.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 10 — Statistical Inference

title: Glossary
text:
I

ILATE rule
A mnemonic for picking the u in integration by parts: prefer Inverse-trig, Logarithm, Algebraic, Trig, Exponential — earlier in the list becomes u.
First named in: Class 12, Chapter 7 — Integration Techniques


Implicit differentiation
A technique for finding dy/dx from a relation F(x, y) = 0 by differentiating both sides w.r.t. x and treating y as a function of x.
First named in: Class 12, Chapter 5 — Differentiability and Rules of Differentiation


Inclusion–exclusion
The counting rule that for two finite sets, |A \cup B| = |A| + |B| - |A \cap B|, with a three-set version that adds back the triple overlap. Generalises to any finite number of sets.
First named in: Class 11, Chapter 1 — Sets


Increasing function
A function whose output rises whenever its input does on a given interval; equivalently f'(x) \ge 0 on the interval (strictly > 0 except at isolated points for strict increase).
First named in: Class 12, Chapter 6 — Application of Derivatives (Tangents, Normals, Rates)


Indefinite integral
The family of all antiderivatives of f, written \int f(x)\,dx = F(x) + C, where F' = f and C is an arbitrary constant.
First named in: Class 12, Chapter 7 — Indefinite Integrals


Indeterminate form
A symbolic expression like 0/0 or \infty - \infty that does not by itself fix the value of a limit; it signals more work is needed.
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 1 — Limits


Infinite discontinuity
A point where f blows up to \pm\infty from at least one side; equivalent to a vertical asymptote at that point.
First named in: Class 12, Chapter 5 — Continuity


Initial condition
A specified value (and possibly derivative) at a single point that pins down one solution out of the family of solutions to a differential equation.
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 12 — Differential Equations


Inscribed angle theorem
The angle subtended by an arc of a circle at the centre is twice the angle subtended at any point on the major arc.
First named in: Class 9, Chapter 9 — Circles


Integer
A whole number — positive, negative, or zero. The set of integers is written \mathbb{Z}.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 10 — Negative Numbers


Integration by parts
The product rule run backwards: \int u\,dv = uv - \int v\,du. Useful when one factor of the integrand becomes simpler under differentiation.
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 10 — Techniques of Integration


Intermediate Value Theorem
A continuous function on [a, b] takes every value between f(a) and f(b) — the basis of every bracketed root-finder.
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 2 — Continuity


Inverse proportion
Two quantities x and y are in inverse proportion if xy = k for a fixed constant k > 0 — their product stays the same.
First named in: Class 8, Chapter 11 — Direct and Inverse Proportions


Irrational number
A real number that cannot be written as a fraction of two integers — its decimal expansion goes on forever without ever repeating. Examples: \sqrt{2}, \pi, e.
First named in: Tier 2, Lesson 9 — Square Roots and Irrationals

title: Glossary
text:
J

Jump discontinuity
A point where the one-sided limits of f both exist but disagree, so the curve “jumps” without an asymptote.
First named in: Class 12, Chapter 5 — Continuity

title: Glossary
text:
K
(No entries yet.)

title: Glossary
text:
L

Lagrange’s theorem
In a finite group G, the size of every subgroup divides |G| — and so does the order of every element.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 1 — Groups


Laplace’s equation
u_{xx} + u_{yy} = 0 (or \nabla^2 u = 0 in higher dimensions). Solutions are harmonic functions; describe steady states of heat, electrostatic potential, fluid flow potential.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 7 — PDEs


Lattice
A discrete subgroup L \subset \mathbb{R}^n of full rank — equivalently, all integer combinations \{\sum a_i b_i : a_i \in \mathbb{Z}\} of a fixed basis b_1, \ldots, b_n. The same lattice has many bases (some “good”, near-orthogonal; some “bad”, skewed), and that asymmetry is the foundation of lattice-based post-quantum cryptography.
First named in: Tier 7, Lesson 14 — Lattice-Based Post-Quantum Cryptography


Latus rectum
The chord of a conic that passes through a focus and is perpendicular to the axis. Length is |4p| for y^2 = 4px and 2b^2/a for the standard ellipse and hyperbola.
First named in: Class 11, Chapter 10 — Conic Sections (Circle and Parabola)


Learning rate
The step-size parameter \eta in a gradient-based optimiser: too small wastes compute, too large diverges.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 10 — Gradient Methods and SGD Variants


Lebesgue integral
An integral built by slicing the output axis of a function into level sets and summing each level’s measure times its value; agrees with the Riemann integral on nice functions but handles wild ones and lets you swap limits with integrals (Dominated Convergence, Monotone Convergence, Fatou’s lemma).
First named in: Tier 7, Lesson 1 — Measure Theory and Lebesgue Integration


Limit
The value f(x) approaches as x gets arbitrarily close to a, regardless of whether f(a) itself is defined.
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 1 — Limits


Line
The path traced by moving in one fixed direction; a line segment is a finite piece of a line with two endpoints.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 12 — Shapes and Angles


Linear approximation
f(x) \approx f(a) + f'(a)(x - a) — using the tangent line at a as a stand-in for f near a.
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 7 — Related Rates


Linear equation
An equation in which the unknown appears only to the first power, with no products or roots of unknowns. Its standard shape is ax + b = c.
First named in: Tier 2, Lesson 2 — Linear Equations in One Variable


Linear function
A function of the form y = mx + b whose graph is a straight line.
First named in: Tier 2, Lesson 5 — Linear Functions and Their Graphs


Linear inequality
A statement that a linear expression is less than, greater than, or equal-or- not-equal to a number. Its solution is a region of the number line, not a single value.
First named in: Tier 2, Lesson 3 — Linear Inequalities


Linear pair
Two adjacent angles whose non-shared sides form a straight line. A linear pair always sums to 180°.
First named in: Class 9, Chapter 6 — Lines and Angles


Logarithm
The exponent you would have to raise a base to in order to reach a given number. So \log_{10}(1000) = 3 because 10^3 = 1000.
First named in: Tier 3, Lesson 5 — Logarithms


Logarithmic differentiation
Take \log of both sides before differentiating; useful for y = f(x)^{g(x)} and messy products and quotients.
First named in: Class 12, Chapter 5 — Differentiability and Rules of Differentiation

title: Glossary
text:
M

Magnitude
The length of a vector, computed by Pythagoras: \|(x, y)\| = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} in 2D, \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2} in 3D.
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 13 — Vectors in 2D and 3D


Manifold hypothesis
The empirical claim that real high-dimensional data (images, text, audio) lies near a much lower-dimensional curved surface inside its ambient space; the basis for non-linear dimensionality reduction and modern generative modelling.
First named in: Tier 7, Lesson 5 — Manifold Methods


Marked price
The price displayed on an item before any discount (MP). The base for computing discount %.
First named in: Class 8, Chapter 7 — Comparing Quantities


Mathematical induction
A proof technique establishing P(n) for all n \ge n_0 by proving a base case and that P(n) implies P(n+1).
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 11 — Sets, Logic, and Proof Techniques


Matrix
A rectangular array of numbers; viewed actively, an encoded linear transformation whose columns are the images of the basis vectors.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 1 — Matrices and Matrix Operations


Mean
The “average” of a list of numbers: the sum divided by the count. The balance point of the data.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 14 — Data and the Average


Mean deviation
The average of the absolute deviations of a data set from a chosen centre (usually the mean or median): \text{MD} = (1/n) \sum |x_i - \bar{x}|.
First named in: Class 11, Chapter 13 — Statistics


Mean Value Theorem
If f is continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b), there exists c \in (a,b) with f'(c) = (f(b)-f(a))/(b-a).
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 15 — Continuity and Differentiability — Rigorous Foundation


Measure
A rule that assigns a non-negative size to subsets of a space such that the size of a countable disjoint union equals the sum of the sizes (σ-additivity); generalises length, area, volume, and probability into one axiom.
First named in: Tier 7, Lesson 1 — Measure Theory and Lebesgue Integration


Median
The middle value of a sorted dataset (or the average of the two middle values when the count is even).
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 14 — Data and the Average


Midpoint theorem
The line segment joining the midpoints of two sides of a triangle is parallel to the third side and half its length.
First named in: Class 9, Chapter 8 — Quadrilaterals


Modal class
For grouped data, the class interval with the highest frequency. The mode formula uses the modal class together with the frequencies of the preceding and succeeding classes.
First named in: Class 10, Chapter 13 — Statistics


Modular arithmetic
Arithmetic done with remainders — the system that runs clocks, days of the week, hash tables, and modern cryptography.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 6 — Division as Fair Share


Mode
The most frequently occurring value in a dataset. Useful for categorical data.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 14 — Data and the Average


Multiple integral
A definite integral over a region in two or more dimensions, computed as iterated single integrals (Fubini’s theorem) with coordinates chosen to match the region.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 6 — Multiple Integrals


Multiplication
The total count of small squares in a rectangle of given width and height — equivalently, repeated addition.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 5 — Multiplication as Area


Multiplication principle
If a process has independent steps with n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_k choices each, the total number of outcomes is n_1 \cdot n_2 \cdots n_k — the foundation of all counting.
First named in: Tier 3, Lesson 11 — Combinatorics — Counting


Mutual information
The amount I(X;Y) that knowing one random variable tells you about another; zero exactly when they are independent.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 11 — Information Theory


Mutually exclusive events
Two events A and B that cannot occur together: A \cap B = \varnothing. For such events P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B).
First named in: Class 11, Chapter 14 — Probability

title: Glossary
text:
N

Natural exponential
The function e^x, equal to its own derivative — base e \approx 2.71828.
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 5 — Derivatives of Trig, Exp, Log


Natural logarithm
The inverse of e^x, written \ln x; its derivative is 1/x.
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 5 — Derivatives of Trig, Exp, Log


Natural number
A counting number: 1, 2, 3, 4, \ldots — the answer to “how many?”. They go on without end (no largest one) and are the foundation every other kind of number is built from.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 1 — What is a Number?


Negation
The mirror of a number across zero. The negation of 5 is -5; the negation of -5 is 5.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 10 — Negative Numbers


Negative number
A number to the left of zero on the number line — used wherever a “reference zero” exists and you can be on either side of it.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 10 — Negative Numbers


Newton’s method
The root-finding iteration x_{n+1} = x_n - f(x_n)/f'(x_n). Converges quadratically near a simple root, given a good initial guess.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 8 — Numerical Methods


Normal distribution
The bell-curve distribution N(\mu, \sigma^2) with PDF \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(x-\mu)^2/(2\sigma^2)}; the universal limiting distribution of sums of many independent random variables.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 8 — Continuous Distributions and the Normal


Normal line
The line through a point on a curve that is perpendicular to the tangent line there; slope = -1/f'(a) when f'(a) \ne 0.
First named in: Class 12, Chapter 6 — Application of Derivatives (Tangents, Normals, Rates)


Normal vector
A vector perpendicular to a plane (or to a curve/surface at a point). A plane is fully described by any point on it together with its normal.
First named in: Class 12, Chapter 11 — Planes in 3D


Number
A quantity — the answer to “how many?” or “how much?”. The shapes we write on paper (3, 12, 1000, III, 三) are names for numbers, not the numbers themselves; the same number can have many names across cultures and notations.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 1 — What is a Number?


Number line
A straight line on which every number has a single home — zero in the middle, larger numbers to the right, smaller (and negative) numbers to the left.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 2 — The Number Line

title: Glossary
text:
O

Octant
One of the eight regions into which the three coordinate planes divide 3D space, named by a sign-pattern (\pm, \pm, \pm) on the coordinates.
First named in: Class 11, Chapter 11 — Introduction to Three-Dimensional Geometry


One-sided limit
The value f(x) approaches as x tends to a from one side only — written \lim_{x \to a^+} (right) or \lim_{x \to a^-} (left).
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 1 — Limits


Optimisation
The process of finding the input that maximises or minimises an objective function, typically by setting the derivative to zero and classifying the critical points.
First named in: Class 12, Chapter 6 — Application of Derivatives (Maxima and Minima)


Order of a numerical method
The exponent p such that the per-step error scales as O(h^p). Halving h multiplies accuracy by 2^p. Euler p=1, trapezoidal p=2, RK4 / Simpson p=4.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 8 — Numerical Methods


Order of magnitude
The exponent of 10 when a number is in scientific notation — how many tens it would take to multiply up to that size.
First named in: Tier 2, Lesson 8 — Scientific Notation


Ordered pair
A pair (x, y) in which the order of the entries matters: (2, 3) and (3, 2) are different ordered pairs and name different points on the plane.
First named in: Class 9, Chapter 3 — Coordinate Geometry


Origin
The point on the number line (or the coordinate plane) we choose to call zero — the starting place from which every other number is measured.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 2 — The Number Line

title: Glossary
text:
P

PAC learning
“Probably Approximately Correct”: a class is PAC learnable if some algorithm, given polynomially many i.i.d. samples in 1/\epsilon and 1/\delta, returns a hypothesis whose error is at most \epsilon with confidence at least 1 - \delta.
First named in: Tier 7, Lesson 4 — Statistical Learning Theory


Parabola
The conic section formed by slicing a cone parallel to one of its sides; the U-shaped graph of any quadratic. Equidistant from a single focus and a directrix line.
First named in: Tier 3, Lesson 2 — Parabolas — Graphing Quadratics


Parallel postulate
Euclid’s fifth postulate. Playfair’s form: through a point not on a given line, exactly one line parallel to it exists. Replacing it produces non-Euclidean geometries.
First named in: Class 9, Chapter 5 — Introduction to Euclid’s Geometry


Parallelogram
A quadrilateral with both pairs of opposite sides parallel; opposite sides are equal, opposite angles are equal, and the diagonals bisect each other.
First named in: Class 8, Chapter 3 — Understanding Quadrilaterals


Parametric curve
A curve traced by a moving point (x(t), y(t)) as a parameter t varies — capturing both the trail of points and the trip of when each point is visited.
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 15 — Parametric Equations and Curves


Parametric differentiation
For curves given by x = f(t),\ y = g(t), the slope is dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt).
First named in: Class 12, Chapter 5 — Differentiability and Rules of Differentiation


Partial derivative
The derivative of a multivariable function with respect to one variable, holding the others fixed; written \partial f / \partial x_i.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 5 — Partial Derivatives and the Gradient


Partial differential equation
An equation relating a function of several variables to its partial derivatives. Examples: heat, wave, Laplace, Schrödinger.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 7 — PDEs


Partial fractions
A technique for splitting a proper rational function P(x)/Q(x) into a sum of simpler fractions whose denominators are the irreducible factors of Q(x).
First named in: Class 12, Chapter 7 — Integration Techniques


Pascal’s triangle
The triangular array whose n-th row holds the binomial coefficients \binom{n}{0}, \binom{n}{1}, \ldots, \binom{n}{n}; each interior entry is the sum of the two entries diagonally above it. The row sums to 2^n and gives the coefficients in the expansion of (a+b)^n.
First named in: Class 11, Chapter 7 — Binomial Theorem


PDF (probability density function)
For a continuous random variable, the function whose area under the curve between a and b gives the probability of falling in that interval. Not a probability itself — a probability density.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 8 — Continuous Distributions and the Normal


Percent
A ratio out of 100. The symbol r\% stands for \tfrac{r}{100}, and “r\% of x” is \tfrac{r}{100} \cdot x.
First named in: Class 8, Chapter 7 — Comparing Quantities


Perfect cube
An integer of the form n^3 for some integer n. Equivalently, every prime in its factorisation appears with an exponent that is a multiple of three.
First named in: Class 8, Chapter 6 — Cubes and Cube Roots


Perfect square
A whole number of the form n^2 for some whole n. Equivalently, every prime in its factorisation appears an even number of times.
First named in: Class 8, Chapter 5 — Squares and Square Roots


Perimeter
The total distance around the boundary of a flat shape, measured in length units (m, cm).
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 13 — Area, Perimeter, Volume


Permutation
An arrangement of r items chosen from n where order matters: P(n,r) = n!/(n-r)!.
First named in: Tier 3, Lesson 11 — Combinatorics — Counting


Perpendicular bisector
The line through the midpoint of a segment that meets it at a right angle; also the locus of points equidistant from the segment’s two endpoints.
First named in: Kiselev Geometry Book I — Chapter 3, Perpendiculars and Obliques


Pi
The ratio of any circle’s circumference to its diameter — a constant \approx 3.14159 that is irrational and transcendental. CBSE board papers conventionally use \pi = 22/7.
First named in: Class 10, Chapter 11 — Areas Related to Circles


Pie chart
A circle divided into sectors whose central angles are proportional to category counts: angle = (\text{count} / \text{total}) \times 360°.
First named in: Class 8, Chapter 4 — Data Handling


Place value
The rule that the value of a digit depends on which column it sits in. In our base-10 system, each step left multiplies the value by ten.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 3 — Place Value


Platonic solid
A convex polyhedron whose faces are all congruent regular polygons and where the same number of faces meets at every vertex. Exactly five exist: tetrahedron, cube, octahedron, dodecahedron, icosahedron.
First named in: Kiselev Geometry Book II — Chapter 5, Regular polyhedra


PMF (probability mass function)
A function that assigns each value of a discrete random variable its probability. The probabilities must sum to 1.
First named in: Tier 3, Lesson 15 — Discrete Probability Distributions


Point
A position with no size — the smallest object in geometry.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 12 — Shapes and Angles


Polar form
Writing a complex number as z = re^{i\theta} where r = |z| and \theta = \arg(z). Multiplication then becomes “scale by r, rotate by \theta.”
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 4 — Complex Numbers


Polygon
A closed flat shape with straight sides — triangle, quadrilateral, pentagon, and so on.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 12 — Shapes and Angles


Polynomial
A sum of terms, each of the form (a number) × (a non-negative whole-number power of x). Example: 3x^2 - 4x + 7.
First named in: Tier 2, Lesson 11 — Polynomials


Post-quantum cryptography
Cryptographic schemes designed to remain secure against attackers equipped with a large-scale quantum computer. RSA, ECC, and Diffie-Hellman are all broken by Shor’s algorithm; PQC candidates rely on problems (lattice problems, code-based, hash-based, multivariate, isogeny) for which no polynomial-time quantum algorithm is known. NIST standardised CRYSTALS-Kyber (FIPS 203) and CRYSTALS-Dilithium (FIPS 204) in 2024.
First named in: Tier 7, Lesson 14 — Lattice-Based Post-Quantum Cryptography


Postulate
A geometrical statement accepted without proof as the foundation of an axiomatic geometry. Euclid stated five.
First named in: Class 9, Chapter 5 — Introduction to Euclid’s Geometry


Potential function
A scalar function f whose gradient equals a given vector field, \mathbf{F} = \nabla f; such fields are called conservative and have zero curl.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 7 — Vector Fields, Divergence, Curl


Power rule
\dfrac{d}{dx} x^n = n x^{n-1} for any real n — the workhorse derivative formula.
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 4 — Differentiation Rules


Prime
A whole number \ge 2 with exactly two divisors: 1 and itself. The first few are 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 15 — Divisibility and Primes


Prime factorisation
The unique way of writing a whole number \ge 2 as a product of primes. The Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic guarantees uniqueness.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 15 — Divisibility and Primes


Principal value branch (inverse trig)
The restricted domain on which a trigonometric function is one-one and onto its full range, making it invertible. For \sin: [-\pi/2, \pi/2]; for \cos: [0, \pi]; for \tan: (-\pi/2, \pi/2).
First named in: Class 12, Chapter 2 — Inverse Trigonometric Functions


Prism
