A polyhedron with two parallel congruent polygonal bases joined by parallelograms (rectangles in the right case). Volume = base area × height.
First named in: Kiselev Geometry Book II — Chapter 2, Polyhedra


Probability
A number between 0 and 1 measuring how likely an event is. For equally likely outcomes, P(\text{event}) = \text{favourable outcomes} / \text{total outcomes}.
First named in: Tier 3, Lesson 13 — Basic Probability


Product rule
(fg)' = f'g + fg' — never f'g'.
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 4 — Differentiation Rules


Proof
A finite chain of justified steps deriving a conclusion from accepted assumptions; the standard certificate of mathematical truth.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 11 — Sets, Logic, and Proof Techniques


Proportion
A statement that two ratios are equal; cross-multiplication finds the missing piece.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 9 — Ratio and Proportion


p-value
The probability, assuming the null hypothesis is true, of observing data at least as extreme as what was actually seen.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 10 — Statistical Inference


Pyramid
A polyhedron with one polygonal base and one apex; lateral faces are triangles. Volume = \tfrac{1}{3} × base area × height — the 3D cousin of the half-base-times-height triangle.
First named in: Kiselev Geometry Book II — Chapter 2, Polyhedra


Pythagorean identities
The three trigonometric identities — \sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1, 1 + \tan^2\theta = \sec^2\theta, 1 + \cot^2\theta = \csc^2\theta — that follow from a^2 + b^2 = c^2 by dividing by c^2, b^2, a^2 respectively.
First named in: Class 10, Chapter 8 — Trigonometric Identities


Pythagorean theorem
In a right-angled triangle with legs a, b and hypotenuse c, a^2 + b^2 = c^2. Provable by similarity, by area-rearrangement, or by coordinate algebra.
First named in: Tier 2, Lesson 10 — The Pythagorean Theorem

title: Glossary
text:
Q

Quadrant
One of the four regions into which the two coordinate axes divide the plane, numbered I–IV counter-clockwise from the upper right.
First named in: Class 9, Chapter 3 — Coordinate Geometry


Quadratic equation
An equation of the form ax^2 + bx + c = 0, with a \ne 0. Has 2, 1, or 0 real solutions depending on the discriminant.
First named in: Tier 2, Lesson 14 — Quadratic Equations (Intro)


Quadrilateral
A closed plane figure bounded by four line segments. Its four interior angles always sum to 360°.
First named in: Class 8, Chapter 3 — Understanding Quadrilaterals


Quaternion
A 4-real-number a + bi + cj + dk obeying Hamilton’s relations i^2 = j^2 = k^2 = ijk = -1; the unit quaternions form S^3 and double-cover the rotation group SO(3), giving the singularity-free representation of 3D rotation used in animation, robotics, and spacecraft attitude control.
First named in: Tier 7, Lesson 8 — Quaternions and 3D Rotations


Quotient
The result of a division — each fair share when the total is split.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 6 — Division as Fair Share


Quotient rule
(f/g)' = (f'g - fg')/g^2 — “low d-high minus high d-low, all over low squared.”
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 4 — Differentiation Rules

title: Glossary
text:
R

Radian
The angle subtended at the centre of a circle by an arc whose length equals the radius. 180° = \pi radians; calculus formulas for trig functions only work nicely in radians.
First named in: Class 11, Chapter 3 — Trigonometric Functions


Radicand
The expression sitting under a radical sign. For an even-index root over the reals, the radicand must be non-negative.
First named in: Class 11, Chapter 2 — Relations and Functions


Range
The largest value in a dataset minus the smallest — a simple measure of spread.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 14 — Data and the Average


Rate
A ratio of two quantities with different units, like km per litre or rupees per kg.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 9 — Ratio and Proportion


Ratio
A comparison of two quantities by division — the relationship that survives when both quantities are scaled by the same factor.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 9 — Ratio and Proportion


Rational number
A number that can be written \tfrac{p}{q} with p, q integers and q \ne 0. Many fractions name the same rational; between any two distinct rationals, infinitely many more lie.
First named in: Class 8, Chapter 1 — Rational Numbers


Rational-root theorem
For a polynomial with integer coefficients, every rational root p/q (in lowest terms) satisfies p \mid a_0 (constant term) and q \mid a_n (leading coefficient). The standard first attack on cubics and quartics with integer coefficients.
First named in: Kiselev Algebra Part II — Chapter 2, Higher-degree equations


Real number
Any number on the number line — every rational together with every irrational. Reals fill the line completely, leaving no gaps between neighbours.
First named in: Class 9, Chapter 1 — Number Systems


Reciprocal
The fraction obtained by flipping numerator and denominator. Dividing by a fraction is the same as multiplying by its reciprocal.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 7 — Fractions


Regular polygon
A convex polygon with all sides equal AND all interior angles equal. Inscribes in and circumscribes about a common circle; interior angle is (n-2) \cdot 180° / n.
First named in: Kiselev Geometry Book I — Chapter 8, Regular polygons and circumference


Related rates
A class of problems where two quantities are linked by an equation and you find one rate of change from the other by differentiating with respect to time.
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 7 — Related Rates


Relation
Any subset of the Cartesian product A \times B — i.e. any rule that decides which ordered pairs (a, b) “belong together.” Functions are the special relations where every input has exactly one output.
First named in: Class 11, Chapter 2 — Relations and Functions


Remainder
What’s left over after a division when the total doesn’t split exactly. The defining equation: total = divisor × quotient + remainder.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 6 — Division as Fair Share


Remainder theorem
When a polynomial p(x) is divided by (x - a), the remainder equals p(a). Plugging in a value and dividing by a linear factor are the same act.
First named in: Class 9, Chapter 2 — Polynomials


Removable discontinuity
A point where a function fails to be continuous only because of a single missing or wrong value — patchable by redefining f(a).
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 2 — Continuity


Repeating decimal
A decimal whose digits cycle forever in a fixed pattern, written with a bar over the repeating block (e.g. 0.\overline{3}). Every fraction is either terminating or repeating.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 8 — Decimals


Residue theorem
\oint_C f(z)\, dz = 2\pi i \sum_k \mathrm{Res}(f; a_k), summed over singularities inside C. Lets contour integrals (and many real integrals) be computed from local data at singular points.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 5 — Complex Functions


Riemann sum
A finite sum \sum f(x_i^*) \Delta x approximating area under a curve by rectangles; converges to the definite integral as \Delta x \to 0.
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 8 — The Definite Integral


Right angle
A quarter-turn — exactly 90°. The corner of a book, a window, or a square.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 12 — Shapes and Angles


Ring
A set with two operations (add and multiply): an abelian group under +, an associative \cdot, tied together by distributivity. Generalises the integers.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 2 — Rings and Fields


RSA
A widely used encryption scheme whose security rests on the difficulty of factoring a product of two large primes.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 15 — Divisibility and Primes


Rule of three
Given three of four quantities in a proportion a : b = c : d, find the fourth by cross-multiplication. The workhorse algorithm of applied arithmetic for two millennia, predating symbolic algebra.
First named in: Kiselev Arithmetic — Chapter 5, Ratio and proportion

title: Glossary
text:
S

Sample space
The set of every possible outcome of a random experiment. Every probability is computed relative to it.
First named in: Tier 3, Lesson 13 — Basic Probability


Scientific notation
A way of writing any number as a \times 10^n with 1 \le a < 10 and n an integer — the shape and size kept separate.
First named in: Tier 2, Lesson 8 — Scientific Notation


Secant line
A line passing through two points on a curve; its slope is the average rate of change between them.
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 3 — The Derivative


Second derivative test
At a critical point c: f''(c) < 0 → local max; f''(c) > 0 → local min; f''(c) = 0 → inconclusive.
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 6 — Optimisation


Section formula
The coordinates of a point dividing the segment from (x_1, y_1) to (x_2, y_2) in ratio m{:}n are \left(\dfrac{m x_2 + n x_1}{m + n}, \dfrac{m y_2 + n y_1}{m + n}\right).
First named in: Class 10, Chapter 7 — Coordinate Geometry


Sector
The pie-slice region of a circle bounded by two radii and the arc between them. A sector with central angle \theta has area (\theta/360°)\pi r^2 and arc length (\theta/360°)\,2\pi r.
First named in: Class 10, Chapter 11 — Areas Related to Circles


Segment (of a circle)
The 2D region of a circle bounded by a chord and one of the two arcs the chord cuts off. Minor-segment area = sector area - triangle area.
First named in: Class 10, Chapter 11 — Areas Related to Circles


Semi-perimeter
Half the perimeter of a polygon — the “s” in Heron’s formula.
First named in: Class 9, Chapter 10 — Heron’s Formula


Separation of variables
The technique of seeking PDE solutions of the product form u(x, t) = X(x)\,T(t), which (for linear PDEs on nice domains) reduces a PDE to two ODEs joined by a shared separation constant.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 7 — PDEs


Sequence
An ordered list of numbers, written a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots. Each position has its own term.
First named in: Tier 2, Lesson 15 — Sequences (Intro)


Series
The running sum of a sequence — what you get by adding the first term, then the first two, then the first three, and so on. A series can converge to a finite number or diverge to infinity.
First named in: Tier 3, Lesson 4 — Sequences and Series


Set
A collection of distinct objects, with operations union, intersection, difference, and complement; the foundational data type of modern mathematics.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 11 — Sets, Logic, and Proof Techniques


Sign chart
A diagram of the real number line with the roots of a factored polynomial (or the zeros of numerator and denominator of a rational expression) marked, recording the sign of the expression in each resulting interval. Sign flips at odd-multiplicity roots, holds at even-multiplicity roots; the standard tool for solving polynomial and rational inequalities.
First named in: Kiselev Algebra Part II — Chapter 3, Higher-degree inequalities


Similar figures
Two figures that have the same shape but possibly different size — corresponding angles equal, corresponding sides in the same ratio.
First named in: Tier 2, Lesson 13 — Similarity and Scale


Simple interest
Interest computed only on the original principal: \text{SI} = \tfrac{P \cdot R \cdot T}{100}. Each year adds the same amount.
First named in: Class 8, Chapter 7 — Comparing Quantities


Skew lines
Two lines in space that neither meet nor are parallel — the third configuration that exists in 3D but not in the plane.
First named in: Kiselev Geometry Book II — Chapter 1, Lines and planes


Slope
For a straight line, the change in y per unit change in x — its rise over run. Captures how steeply the line climbs.
First named in: Tier 2, Lesson 5 — Linear Functions and Their Graphs


Smooth manifold
A topological space that locally looks like \mathbb{R}^n, with overlapping coordinate charts whose transition maps are infinitely differentiable; the natural setting for differential calculus on curved spaces.
First named in: Tier 7, Lesson 7 — Differential Geometry


Solution of an equation
A value of the unknown that, when substituted back in, makes both sides of the equation equal.
First named in: Tier 2, Lesson 2 — Linear Equations in One Variable


Spectral theorem
Every real symmetric matrix factors as A = QDQ^T with Q orthogonal and D diagonal of real eigenvalues; an orthonormal eigenbasis always exists.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 15 — The Spectral Theorem


Sphere
The set of points in 3D space at a fixed distance (radius r) from a centre. Surface area 4\pi r^2, volume \tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3.
First named in: Class 9, Chapter 11 — Surface Areas and Volumes


Square-cube law
When you scale a figure by factor k, lengths multiply by k, areas by k^2, and volumes by k^3. The reason a giant ant could not stand and an elephant has thick legs.
First named in: Tier 2, Lesson 13 — Similarity and Scale


Square root
The non-negative number that, multiplied by itself, gives a chosen non-negative number. The square root of 9 is 3 because 3 \times 3 = 9.
First named in: Tier 2, Lesson 9 — Square Roots and Irrationals


Standard deviation
The square root of variance, \sigma = \sqrt{\text{Var}(X)} — spread in the original units of X.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 9 — Variance, Standard Deviation, and the Central Limit Theorem


Standard error
The standard deviation of a sampling distribution; for a sample mean it is \sigma/\sqrt{n}.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 9 — Variance, Standard Deviation, and the Central Limit Theorem


Substitution method
A way to solve a system of equations: isolate one variable in one equation, then plug that expression into the other equation.
First named in: Tier 2, Lesson 6 — Systems of Linear Equations


Subtraction
Taking one number away from another — pictured on the number line as a hop to the left.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 4 — Addition and Subtraction


Surd
An irrational root, especially a square or cube root that cannot be simplified to a rational. For example, \sqrt{2} and \sqrt[3]{5} are surds; \sqrt{4} = 2 is not.
First named in: Class 9, Chapter 1 — Number Systems


SVD
Singular Value Decomposition: every matrix factors as A = U\Sigma V^T with U, V orthogonal and \Sigma diagonal non-negative; the universal generalisation of the spectral theorem.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 15 — The Spectral Theorem


System of linear equations
Two or more linear equations considered together. A solution is a set of values that satisfies every equation at once.
First named in: Tier 2, Lesson 6 — Systems of Linear Equations

title: Glossary
text:
T

Tangent line
The line that just touches a curve at a single point and matches its slope there — the limit of secant lines.
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 3 — The Derivative


Tangent space
At a point p on a smooth manifold M, the vector space T_pM of velocities of curves through p; the linear approximation that lets you do calculus and host the Riemannian metric.
First named in: Tier 7, Lesson 7 — Differential Geometry


Tangent (to a circle)
A line that meets a circle at exactly one point — the point of contact. The tangent at the point of contact is perpendicular to the radius drawn to that point.
First named in: Class 10, Chapter 10 — Circles and Tangents


Terminating decimal
A decimal that ends after finitely many digits — equivalently, a fraction whose reduced denominator’s only prime factors are 2 and 5.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 8 — Decimals


Topology
A choice of which subsets of a space count as “open,” closed under arbitrary unions and finite intersections; that one choice is enough to define continuity, convergence, compactness, and connectedness without any reference to distance.
First named in: Tier 7, Lesson 2 — Topology


Transversal
A line that crosses two or more other lines. When the crossed lines are parallel, a transversal generates equal corresponding and alternate-interior angle pairs and supplementary co-interior pairs.
First named in: Class 9, Chapter 6 — Lines and Angles


Trapezium
A four-sided figure with exactly one pair of parallel sides; area \tfrac{1}{2}(a + b) h where a, b are the parallel sides and h the perpendicular distance between them.
First named in: Class 8, Chapter 9 — Mensuration


Tree
A connected graph with no cycles; on n vertices it has exactly n-1 edges.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 12 — Graphs and Their Algorithms


Triangle
A polygon with three straight sides; its three interior angles always sum to 180°.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 12 — Shapes and Angles


Trigonometric equation
An equation involving trigonometric functions of an unknown angle. Because trig functions are periodic, solutions come in infinite families, parametrised by an integer n.
First named in: Class 11, Chapter 3 — Trigonometric Equations


Trigonometric function
A function (sine, cosine, tangent, and their reciprocals) that converts an angle into a number — built from the coordinates of a point travelling around the unit circle.
First named in: Tier 3, Lesson 7 — Trig Ratios

title: Glossary
text:
U

Uncertainty principle
The Fourier-pair inequality \sigma_x \sigma_k \ge 1/2: a signal cannot be sharply localised in both time and frequency. In quantum mechanics this becomes Heisenberg’s \sigma_x \sigma_p \ge \hbar/2.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 13 — The Fourier Transform


u-substitution
An integration trick that runs the chain rule backwards: rename an inner expression as u, replace dx with du, and the integral often simplifies to a standard form.
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 10 — Techniques of Integration


Unit circle
The circle of radius 1 centred at the origin; the geometric foundation of the trigonometric functions.
First named in: Tier 3, Lesson 8 — The Unit Circle


Unit vector
A vector of magnitude 1 — pure direction with the size stripped away. Found by dividing any non-zero vector by its magnitude.
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 13 — Vectors in 2D and 3D

title: Glossary
text:
V

Variable
A letter that stands for a number — sometimes one specific unknown, sometimes any number in a range.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 11 — Variables


Variance
The expected squared deviation of a random variable from its mean: \text{Var}(X) = E[(X-\mu)^2]. Measures spread in squared units.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 9 — Variance, Standard Deviation, and the Central Limit Theorem


VC dimension
The largest number of points a hypothesis class can label every possible way (shatter); a finite VC dimension is exactly the condition for PAC learnability and controls the generalisation gap as \sqrt{d/n}.
First named in: Tier 7, Lesson 4 — Statistical Learning Theory


Vector
A quantity with both magnitude and direction, written as a tuple of coordinates such as (x, y) in 2D or (x, y, z) in 3D.
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 13 — Vectors in 2D and 3D


Vector field
A function that assigns a vector to every point of a region — a “sea of arrows” describing flow, force, or velocity.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 7 — Vector Fields, Divergence, Curl


Vector space
A set on which addition and scalar multiplication are defined and satisfy the standard eight axioms — the abstract setting that unifies arrows, polynomials, and function spaces.
First named in: Tier 5, Lesson 3 — Vector Spaces, Linear Independence, Basis


Velocity vector
For a parametric curve (x(t), y(t)), the vector (x'(t), y'(t)) — pointing in the direction of motion, with length equal to the speed.
First named in: Tier 4, Lesson 15 — Parametric Equations and Curves


Vertically opposite angles
The pair of angles directly across from each other when two lines cross. They are always equal.
First named in: Class 9, Chapter 6 — Lines and Angles


Volume
The size of the space inside a 3D shape, measured in cubic units (m³, cm³).
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 13 — Area, Perimeter, Volume

title: Glossary
text:
W

Wave equation
u_{tt} = c^2\, u_{xx}. Models vibrating strings, sound, light. d’Alembert’s solution: u(x,t) = f(x - ct) + g(x + ct) — left- and right-moving waves at speed c.
First named in: Tier 6, Lesson 7 — PDEs

title: Glossary
text:
X
(No entries yet.)

title: Glossary
text:
Y

y-intercept
The value of y where a graph crosses the y-axis — equivalently, the value of y when x = 0.
First named in: Tier 2, Lesson 5 — Linear Functions and Their Graphs

title: Glossary
text:
Z

Zero
The number meaning “nothing of this”. As a placeholder it keeps other digits in their proper columns; without it, “305” collapses into “35”.
First named in: Tier 1, Lesson 3 — Place Value


Zero-product property
If a product of two numbers is zero, then at least one of them must be zero. The hinge that turns a factored quadratic into its roots.
First named in: Tier 2, Lesson 14 — Quadratic Equations (Intro)


For authors. Every term that a lesson formally introduces in its ## Name beat must also have an entry here. See CLAUDE.md → Glossary cross-reference convention for the exact authoring rule and the entry template.

title: CBSE / NCERT Track — Class 8 to Class 12
text:
The seven tiers of Applied Maths synthesise the best of Pestalozzi, the Russian problem-first school, the Socratic tradition, and the Bhāskara sūtra style — they are the curriculum any learner would walk if they had no exam to sit and all the time in the world.
The CBSE / NCERT track is for learners who have an exam to sit, on a specific date, on a specific syllabus. It is aligned, chapter by chapter, to the rationalised NCERT 2023 syllabus for Classes 8 through 12, and every lesson includes a Board-Pattern Practice block with the question styles you will see in the actual board paper.
Use this track if any of the following describe you:

You are sitting the CBSE Class 10 or CBSE Class 12 board exam.
You are studying CBSE in Class 8, 9, or 11 and want lessons that follow your school textbook.
You are preparing for JEE / NEET and want a clean conceptual foundation that starts from the same syllabus you have already begun.
crumbs: CBSE / NCERT Track — Class 8 to Class 12

The CBSE / NCERT track is for learners who have an exam to sit, on a specific date, on a specific syllabus. title: CBSE / NCERT Track — Class 8 to Class 12
text:
How this track is organised
Class 8 → Class 9 → Class 10 (Board) → Class 11 → Class 12 (Board)
Each class is a separate tier in the curriculum manifest. Within a class, the lesson numbering matches the NCERT chapter number — Lesson 5 in Class 10 is Arithmetic Progressions because that is NCERT Class 10 Chapter 5. Where a chapter contains two distinct ideas (“one idea per lesson” — see CLAUDE.md), it is split with an a/b/c suffix (e.g. 08a-trigonometry-intro, 08b-trigonometric-identities).
crumbs: CBSE / NCERT Track — Class 8 to Class 12

title: CBSE / NCERT Track — Class 8 to Class 12
section: How a CBSE lesson is built
text:
How a CBSE lesson is built
Every CBSE lesson follows the same six-beat contract as the synthesis curriculum (Touch → Wonder → Discover → Name → Apply → Connect) and adds two CBSE-specific extras:

Apply → Board-Pattern Practice. After the Warm / Working / Stretch sets, every lesson has a block with 1-mark, 2-mark, 3-mark, and 4-or-5-mark style problems, each with full graduated hints and a worked solution. We also point you to the relevant NCERT Exemplar problem and at least one previous-year question where applicable.
Connect → NCERT chapter alignment. Every lesson tells you exactly which NCERT chapter and section it covers, plus a link to the sister synthesis lesson in the main tiers (different pedagogical angle on the same idea — useful when one explanation does not land).

Everything else — the pre-flight check, multiple representations, graduated hints, worked solutions, common confusions, self-assessment — is identical to the rest of the curriculum.
crumbs: CBSE / NCERT Track — Class 8 to Class 12

title: CBSE / NCERT Track — Class 8 to Class 12
text:
The five classes

Class 8 — 13 chapters. Rational numbers, linear equations, quadrilaterals, mensuration, exponents, factorisation, graphs.
Class 9 — 12 chapters. Number systems, polynomials, coordinate geometry, Euclid’s geometry, triangles, circles, Heron’s formula, surface areas & volumes, statistics.
Class 10 (Board) — 14 chapters. The first board year. Real numbers, polynomials, quadratics, arithmetic progressions, similarity, coordinate geometry, trigonometry, circles, mensuration, statistics, probability.
Class 11 — 14 chapters. Sets, relations and functions, trigonometry deepened, complex numbers, permutations & combinations, binomial theorem, sequences & series, straight lines, conic sections, 3D geometry, limits, derivatives, statistics, probability.
Class 12 (Board) — 13 chapters. The final board year. Inverse trig, matrices, determinants, calculus (continuity, differentiability, integrals, applications), differential equations, vectors, 3D geometry, linear programming, probability.
crumbs: CBSE / NCERT Track — Class 8 to Class 12

title: CBSE / NCERT Track — Class 8 to Class 12
text:
How to use this track

Walk it in order, one chapter at a time. Start with the first lesson of your class. Do not skip ahead — the prerequisite chain matches NCERT’s own.
Tick off the self-assessment before moving on. If two of three answers are no, redo the Stretch and Board-Pattern problems before the next lesson.
Use the synthesis lessons when something doesn’t click. The “sister synthesis lesson” link in each CBSE lesson’s Connect beat leads to a different pedagogical treatment of the same idea — often the second angle is what makes it land.
Treat the Board-Pattern Practice as the minimum, not the ceiling. The problems mirror board styles. For higher-stakes preparation (Olympiad, JEE Advanced, NEET), pair each lesson with the corresponding tier-N synthesis lesson’s Stretch problems.
crumbs: CBSE / NCERT Track — Class 8 to Class 12

title: CBSE / NCERT Track — Class 8 to Class 12
text:
Honouring NCERT, not replacing it
This curriculum supplements your NCERT textbook — it does not replace it. Every lesson points you back to the relevant NCERT sections so you read the canonical exposition alongside this one. The board exam is written from NCERT; treat that book as primary and this site as the “explain this to me again, with widgets” layer.






The lesson statuses on this page reflect a snapshot of _curriculum.yml at build time. Lessons marked planned are scheduled but may not yet have a written page; lessons marked done are complete.
crumbs: CBSE / NCERT Track — Class 8 to Class 12
